| Subnet # | Network Address | First Usable | Last Usable | Broadcast |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 192.168.50.0/26 | 192.168.50.1 | 192.168.50.62 | 192.168.50.63 |
| 2 | 192.168.50.64/26 | 192.168.50.65 | 192.168.50.126 | 192.168.50.127 |
| 3 | 192.168.50.128/26 | 192.168.50.129 | 192.168.50.190 | 192.168.50.191 |
| 4 | 192.168.50.192/26 | 192.168.50.193 | 192.168.50.254 | 192.168.50.255 |

Each sub-network has $N_{usable} = 2^{6} - 2 = 62$ usable host addresses — ready to be linked together with routers to form the internetwork.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Internetwork Architecture — Top-Level Block Diagram

The following Mermaid diagram captures the canonical *network of networks* topology, the central visual every KTU examiner expects on a 14-mark "internetwork" question.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph netA["Network 1 LAN 192.168.1.0/24"]
        hostA1["Host A 192.168.1.10"]
        hostA2["Host A2 192.168.1.11"]
        switchA["Switch L2"]
    end

    subgraph netB["Network 2 WAN 172.16.0.0/16"]
        r1["Router R1"]
        r2["Router R2"]
    end

    subgraph netC["Network 3 LAN 10.0.0.0/8"]
        hostB1["Host B 10.0.0.25"]
        hostB2["Host B2 10.0.0.26"]
        switchB["Switch L2"]
    end

    subgraph ispBlock["Network 4 ISP Backbone 203.0.113.0/24"]
        r3["ISP Edge Router R3"]
        r4["ISP Core Router R4"]
    end

    hostA1 --- switchA
    hostA2 --- switchA
    switchA --- r1
    r1 --- r2
    r2 --- r3
    r3 --- r4
    r4 --- switchB
    switchB --- hostB1
    switchB --- hostB2
```

**How to read the diagram for exam purposes:**
- Each rectangular block is a **distinct sub-network** with its own IP prefix and Layer-2 technology.
- The **routers (R1, R2, R3, R4)** are the only devices that *transcend* sub-networks — they are the internetwork's glue.
- Switches operate *within* a sub-network only; they do not participate in internetwork routing.
- The two end hosts (Host A and Host B) are in completely different LANs; they can communicate **only because of the routers** between them.

---

## 4.2 ISP Tier Hierarchy — How the Global Internet is an Internetwork

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    t1["Tier 1 ISP Global Backbone"]
    t2["Tier 1 ISP Global Backbone"]
    t3["Tier 1 ISP Global Backbone"]
    t2a["Tier 2 Regional ISP"]
    t2b["Tier 2 Regional ISP"]
    t2c["Tier 2 Regional ISP"]
    t3a["Tier 3 Local ISP"]
    t3b["Tier 3 Local ISP"]
    t3c["Tier 3 Local ISP"]
    endU["Enterprise or Home User"]

    t1 <--> t2
    t2 <--> t3
    t1 <--> t3
    t2 --> t2a
    t2 --> t2b
    t3 --> t2c
    t2a --> t3a
    t2a --> t3b
    t2c --> t3c
    t3a --> endU
    t3b --> endU
    t3c --> endU
```

**Interpretation:**
- **Tier-1 ISPs** form the *default-free* core — they own the global internetwork's trunk routes and peer with each other without paying any upstream provider.
- **Tier-2 ISPs** are regional carriers that buy transit from Tier-1s and sell to smaller ISPs.
- **Tier-3 ISPs** are the *last-mile* providers (cable, DSL, FTTH) that finally deliver the internetwork to the end user.
- Every link shown is, at the protocol level, just another **router-to-router sub-network** — proving that the global Internet itself is nothing but a gigantic internetwork of internetworks.

---

## 4.3 Functional Block Diagram — Router Internals (Why a Router Enables Internetworking)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    pIn["Incoming Physical Port"]
    pOut["Outgoing Physical Port"]
    l2In["Layer 2 De-encapsulation"]
    l3Proc["Layer 3 Processing Routing Table Lookup TTL Decrement Checksum Recompute"]
    l2Out["Layer 2 Re-encapsulation for Outgoing Link"]
    fwd["Forwarding Engine"]

    pIn --> l2In --> l3Proc --> fwd --> l2Out --> pOut
```

This is the *anatomy* of the device that makes internetworking possible. Every KTU answer on "how a router works" should reference these four blocks in order: **Receive → De-encapsulate → Route + Decrement TTL → Re-encapsulate → Transmit**.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing the router with a switch or bridge** — switches operate at Layer 2 within a network; only routers (and Layer-3 devices) create an internetwork. A 7-mark sub-part can become a 3-mark answer if you describe switching instead of routing.
> 2. **Writing "Internet" when the question says "internetwork"** — these are *not* synonymous. Examiners deduct 1 mark per instance.
> 3. **Forgetting to mention TTL decrement / header-checksum recompute** at every router hop — this is the most-missed step in 14-mark answers.
> 4. **Mixing up physical (MAC) and logical (IP) address scopes** — remember: MAC is local; IP is global within the internetwork.
> 5. **Skipping the difference between path determination and forwarding** — path determination is building the routing table; forwarding is *using* it for a single packet.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define an internetwork. How is it different from a single computer network?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An *internetwork* is a collection of two or more heterogeneous computer networks interconnected by **routing devices** (routers/gateways) operating at the network layer, providing a unified logical addressing and path-determination service. A *single network* uses one common Layer-2 protocol and one shared physical medium limited to a single broadcast domain, whereas an internetwork spans multiple such networks and presents them as **one logical network** to end-users. The router, not the switch, is the defining device of an internetwork. *(3 marks: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for heterogeneity, 1 mark for the role of the router.)*

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between an Intranet, an Extranet, and the Internet.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Property | Intranet | Extranet | Internet |
|---|---|---|---|
| Scope | Private to one organization. | Extension of intranet to selected outsiders. | Global, public. |
| Access | Employees only. | Employees + approved partners/vendors. | Anyone with a connection. |
| Security | Strict firewall. | Strong authentication (VPN, ACLs). | Best-effort, mostly open. |
| Example | College internal portal. | Supplier order-tracking portal. | Google, YouTube. |

*(3 marks — 1 mark per correct row with example.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> **ESE Module-1 Style:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each carries 14 marks and is split into (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – July 2024]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture of an internetwork** showing how multiple LANs and WANs are interconnected using routers. List the **four functions performed by a router** for every packet. (7 marks)

**(b)** Describe the **step-by-step process of packet delivery across an internetwork**, clearly bringing out the difference between **logical (IP) addressing** and **physical (MAC) addressing** at every hop. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

**(a) Architecture & Router Functions (7 marks):**

An internetwork is constructed by joining independent sub-networks (LANs/WANs) with **routers** that act as gateways between them. A typical architecture is shown below:

- **LAN 1** (e.g., 192.168.1.0/24) connects to **Router R1**.
- **WAN Link** (e.g., 172.16.0.0/16) connects **R1** to **R2**.
- **LAN 2** (e.g., 10.0.0.0/8) connects to **R2**.

Each sub-network has its own broadcast domain and Layer-2 protocol; only the router straddles them.

The **four functions a router performs per packet** are:

1. **Path Determination** — Consult the routing table (built via static routes or dynamic protocols like OSPF/BGP) to find the best next-hop interface.
2. **Forwarding (Switching)** — Move the packet from the input interface buffer to the correct output interface.
3. **Encapsulation / De-encapsulation** — Strip the incoming Layer-2 header, examine the IP header, and re-encapsulate the packet in the appropriate outgoing Layer-2 frame.
4. **Error & Diagnostic Reporting** — Decrement TTL; if TTL = 0, drop and send ICMP Time-Exceeded; respond to ICMP Echo (ping) etc.

*(4 marks for diagram + 3 marks for the four functions.)*

**(b) Packet Delivery & Address Types (7 marks):**

When Host A (192.168.1.10) sends a packet to Host B (10.0.0.25), the following sequence occurs:

1. **Host A** checks whether B is on the same subnet using the bitwise AND with its own mask. The result differs from A's own network, so A concludes B is **remote** and must be reached via its **default gateway** (Router R1, 192.168.1.1).
2. **ARP Resolution** — A broadcasts an ARP request on its LAN asking for the MAC of 192.168.1.1. R1 replies with its MAC address.
3. **Layer-2 Encapsulation on Source LAN** — A wraps the IP packet in an Ethernet frame with **Src MAC = A's MAC, Dst MAC = R1's MAC**. The **IP Src = 192.168.1.10** and **IP Dst = 10.0.0.25** remain in the payload.
4. **R1 Processing** — R1 de-encapsulates the frame, reads the IP header, **decrements TTL from 64 to 63**, recomputes the IP header checksum, and consults its routing table to find the next hop (R2).
5. **Re-encapsulation** — R1 builds a **new Ethernet frame** for the WAN link with **Src MAC = R1's WAN-side MAC, Dst MAC = R2's MAC** (resolved via ARP on the WAN). The IP header remains unchanged.
6. **R2 Processing & Final Delivery** — R2 repeats the process and forwards the frame onto LAN 2, addressed finally to **Host B's MAC**. B de-encapsulates the frame and delivers the IP packet up the stack.

**Key Distinction** *(2 marks reserved for this):*
- **IP (logical) address** is assigned administratively, identifies the *location* of the host in the internetwork's hierarchy, and remains **constant end-to-end**.
- **MAC (physical) address** is hard-burned into the NIC, identifies the *specific hardware interface*, and is **re-written at every hop**.

*(7 marks: 1 mark for subnet check, 1 for ARP, 1 for R1 processing, 1 for R2 processing, 2 for address-type distinction, 1 for concluding line.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**(a)** Explain the **five components of an internetwork** as defined in network reference models. Discuss the role of **IP as the unifying protocol**. (7 marks)

**(b)** Consider the network 192.168.40.0/24. **Divide it into four equal sub-networks** and list the network address, broadcast address, and usable host range of each. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

**(a) Five Internetwork Components (7 marks):**

According to standard internetworking models (e.g., Stallings), an internetwork is built from five components:

1. **Local Network / Sub-Network** — The underlying physical network (Ethernet LAN, Wi-Fi, leased-line WAN) that provides the link-layer transport among directly-attached hosts.
2. **Router** — The interconnecting device that links multiple sub-networks and performs path determination, packet forwarding, and address resolution across the boundaries.
3. **Internet Protocol (IP)** — The common, network-layer *glue* protocol that defines a uniform packet format and a hierarchical 32-bit (IPv4) or 128-bit (IPv6) addressing scheme, independent of the underlying link technology.
4. **Network Interface Card (NIC)** — The hardware interface that physically connects a host to its local sub-network; the NIC is the entity that holds the MAC address.
5. **Physical Transmission Medium** — Copper cable, optical fibre, or radio waves that carry the actual bits between adjacent nodes.

**Role of IP as the Unifying Protocol** *(3 marks for this part)*:
- **Common packet format** — Every sub-network, irrespective of its access method, must conform to the IP datagram structure. This is what makes heterogeneity invisible.
- **Global hierarchical addressing** — IP allows a host in a Wi-Fi LAN in Kerala to be uniquely identified from a server in Virginia, USA, with a single address.
- **Best-effort, connectionless delivery** — IP provides a simple, stateless forwarding service; reliability (when needed) is layered on top via TCP.
- **Fragmentation & Reassembly** — IP can split a packet that is too large for the next link's MTU and reassemble it later — essential when traversing heterogeneous media with different MTU limits.
- **Routing support** — IP works hand-in-hand with routing protocols (OSPF, BGP) to enable dynamic path determination across the internetwork.

*(7 marks: 1 per component + 2 marks for IP's role in unifying.)*

**(b) Subnetting Derivation (7 marks):**

Given: **192.168.40.0/24**, need 4 equal sub-networks.

**Step 1 — Bits to borrow.** $2^{s} = 4 \Rightarrow s = 2$ bits.

**Step 2 — New prefix.** /24 + 2 = **/26**.

**Step 3 — New mask.** 255.255.255.**11000000** = **255.255.255.192**.

**Step 4 — Block size.** $2^{32-26} = 2^{6} = 64$.

**Step 5 — List of subnets:**

| Subnet | Network Address | First Usable Host | Last Usable Host | Broadcast Address | Usable Count |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 192.168.40.0/26 | 192.168.40.1 | 192.168.40.62 | 192.168.40.63 | 62 |
| 2 | 192.168.40.64/26 | 192.168.40.65 | 192.168.40.126 | 192.168.40.127 | 62 |
| 3 | 192.168.40.128/26 | 192.168.40.129 | 192.168.40.190 | 192.168.40.191 | 62 |
| 4 | 192.168.40.192/26 | 192.168.40.193 | 192.168.40.254 | 192.168.40.255 | 62 |

Each sub-network can now be assigned to a separate department LAN, and the four sub-networks can be joined with routers to form the complete internetwork.

*(7 marks: 1 mark for $s=2$, 1 for new prefix/mask, 1 for block size, 3 for the four subnets in tabular form, 1 for closing remark on joining with routers.)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> Read this list **5 minutes before** the exam. Every bullet is a previously-asked KTU question or a known examiner trap.

- ✅ An **internetwork** = *network of networks* joined at **Layer 3** by **routers**.
- ✅ The **router** is the *defining device* of an internetwork — switches/bridges do not create internetworks.
- ✅ Lowercase *internetwork* ≠ uppercase *Internet*. Use them correctly.
- ✅ **IP addresses are end-to-end and constant** across the entire journey; **MAC addresses are hop-by-hop and change at every router**.
- ✅ At every router hop, **TTL is decremented by 1** and the **IP header checksum is recomputed**.
- ✅ The four router functions in order: **Path Determination → Forwarding → Encapsulation → Error Reporting**.
- ✅ ARP translates **IP → MAC** *only* on the local sub-network; remote destinations are reached via the **default gateway**.
- ✅ The global Internet itself is a **multi-tier hierarchy**: Tier-1 backbone ISPs → Tier-2 regional ISPs → Tier-3 local ISPs → end users.
- ✅ Standard metrics to memorise: IPv4 = **32 bits**; IPv6 = **128 bits**; minimum MTU = **576 bytes**; default TTL on most OSes = **64**.
- ✅ Five internetwork components: **Sub-network, Router, IP, NIC, Physical Medium**.
- ✅ Subnetting formulas: $N_{sub} = 2^{s}$, $N_{usable} = 2^{h} - 2$ where $s + h$ = host-bits originally available.
- ✅ Always pair a subnetting answer with a *routers-connecting-subnets* remark to show the examiner you understand internetworking, not just addressing.
- ✅ **Autonomous System (AS)** = a group of IP networks under a single administrative routing policy; the global Internet has **~13,000+ ASes** interconnected via **BGP**.
- ✅ **Intranet** = private organizational internetwork; **Extranet** = its controlled outside extension; **Internet** = the global public internetwork.

> **End of Module 1 Notes — Internetwork.** Best of luck on your KTU 2024 ESE!
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Network Models: Layered Tasks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT631 - NETWORK & DATA SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Network Models: Layered Tasks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are "Layered Tasks" in a Network?

**Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
> *Layered Tasks* in computer networking refer to the systematic decomposition of the complex data communication process into a hierarchical stack of independent, modular functional layers. Each layer performs a specific, well-defined set of *tasks* (services) and interacts only with the layers immediately above and below it through standardized **Service Access Points (SAPs)**. This modularity enables heterogeneous systems to communicate reliably.

In essence, instead of one monolithic program handling the entire transmission of data from a source to a destination, the **Open Systems Interconnection (OSI)** and **TCP/IP** models break this job into smaller, manageable subtasks — one per layer. Each layer handles *its own concern* and passes the result upward or downward.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** The phrase *"Layered Tasks"* in the KTU 2024 scheme for **Network & Data Security (PEMRT631)** is a direct reference to the **OSI 7-Layer Reference Model** and the **TCP/IP 4-Layer (or 5-Layer hybrid) Model**. You are expected to know: (a) the name of every layer, (b) its core function, (c) its **PDU (Protocol Data Unit)** name, and (d) the **services** it provides to the layer above.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Postal Letter" System

Imagine you want to send a handwritten letter from your house in Kerala to a friend in Germany.

- You write the letter in **English** → *Application Layer (your language/message)*
- You translate it into German using a dictionary service → *Presentation Layer (translation/format)*
- You decide whether to send a single letter or break it across multiple envelopes → *Session Layer (dialogue control)*
- You place the letter in an envelope, write the receiver's address, and a return address → *Transport Layer (end-to-end addressing + reliability)*
- You hand it to a courier → *Network Layer (routing path selection)*
- The courier loads it onto a truck that follows a specific road → *Data Link Layer (hop-to-hop framing + error detection)*
- The truck physically drives on the road → *Physical Layer (bits on wire/radio/fiber)*

At the destination, the reverse happens. **None of the layers know about the others' internal workings** — they only exchange data through a *postal interface* (the SAP).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Just as the postal worker does not need to understand German, the *Network layer does not need to understand the user's data*. This separation is what makes networks *modular, interoperable, and replaceable*.

## 1.3 The Two Reference Models at a Glance

| Aspect | OSI Model | TCP/IP Model |
|---|---|---|
| Full Name | Open Systems Interconnection | Transmission Control Protocol / Internet Protocol |
| Layers | **7** | **4** (sometimes shown as 5) |
| Origin | ISO Standard (1984) | ARPANET / IETF (1970s–80s) |
| Usage | **Theoretical/Reference** | **Practical/Internet backbone** |
| Adoption | Conceptual teaching | Real-world Internet |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard metrics** to memorize:
> - **OSI = 7 layers** (mnemonic: **"Please Do Not Throw Sausage Pizza Away"**)
> - **TCP/IP = 4 layers** (mnemonic: **"A TIN"** → Application, Transport, Internet, Network Access)
> - **PDU of each layer** (see Section 2 for full table)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Motivation Behind Layering — The "Five Tasks" Breakdown

According to the seminal **Tanenbaum** and **Forouzan** treatment (the reference texts most KTU examiners follow), every data communication job can be decomposed into **five core tasks**. The OSI model further splits these into seven layers:

1. **Task 1 — Physical Signal Transmission (Bits → Signal)**
   *How is a raw bit (0/1) represented on a medium — electrical voltage, light pulse, or radio wave?*

2. **Task 2 — Framing & Hop Reliability (Bits → Frames)**
   *How do we group bits into frames, detect errors on a single link, and access the medium?*

3. **Task 3 — End-to-End Path & Logical Addressing (Frames → Packets)**
   *How do we route the frame across multiple links to the correct destination, identified by a logical address (e.g., IP)?*

4. **Task 4 — End-to-End Reliability & Port Addressing (Packets → Segments)**
   *How do we ensure ordered, error-free delivery between the *correct processes* on the *correct hosts*, using port numbers?*

5. **Task 5–7 — User Services (Application, Presentation, Session)**
   *How do we present the data (syntax/semantics), manage dialogues (half-duplex/full-duplex), and offer meaningful services (email, web, file transfer) to the user?*

## 2.2 The OSI 7-Layer Model — Layer-by-Layer Deep Dive

### **Layer 7: Application Layer**
- **Core Function:** Provides network services directly to the **end-user applications** (browser, email client, SSH).
- **Examples:** HTTP, HTTPS, FTP, SMTP, DNS, Telnet, SNMP.
- **PDU:** **Data (Message)**
- **KTU Buzzword:** *"Closest to the user; no common OS / kernel; runs in user-space."*

### **Layer 6: Presentation Layer**
- **Core Function:** Translation, encryption/decryption, compression.
- **Examples:** SSL/TLS handshake, JPEG, MPEG, ASCII↔EBCDIC conversion.
- **PDU:** **Data (formatted)**
- **Mnemonic:** *Translation, Encryption, Compression.*

### **Layer 5: Session Layer**
- **Core Function:** Establishes, maintains, and terminates *dialogues* (sessions) between two hosts. Handles tokens, checkpoints, recovery.
- **Examples:** NetBIOS, RPC, PPTP, SIP.
- **PDU:** **Data**
- **Mnemonic:** *"Who talks when?"*

### **Layer 4: Transport Layer**
- **Core Function:** **Process-to-process delivery** using **port numbers** (SAPs). Provides **reliability** (TCP) or best-effort (UDP).
- **Examples:** **TCP** (reliable, connection-oriented), **UDP** (unreliable, connectionless).
- **PDU:** **Segment** (TCP) or **Datagram** (UDP)
- **KTU Buzzword:** *"First end-to-end layer; multiplexing/demultiplexing."*

### **Layer 3: Network Layer**
- **Core Function:** **Logical addressing (IP)** + **routing** of packets across multiple networks.
- **Examples:** IP (IPv4/IPv6), ICMP, OSPF, BGP, RIP, ARP (technically L2.5).
- **PDU:** **Packet**
- **KTU Buzzword:** *"Best-effort, connectionless packet forwarding."*

### **Layer 2: Data Link Layer**
- **Core Function:** **Node-to-node (hop-to-hop) frame delivery**, error detection/correction on a single link, MAC addressing, medium access control.
- **Sublayers:** **LLC (Logical Link Control)** + **MAC (Medium Access Control)**
- **Examples:** Ethernet (IEEE 802.3), Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11), PPP, HDLC, Switch.
- **PDU:** **Frame**
- **KTU Buzzword:** *"Reliable only on a single link — not end-to-end."*

### **Layer 1: Physical Layer**
- **Core Function:** Conversion of **bits into physical signals** (electrical, optical, RF). Defines topology, voltage levels, cable specs, connector pinouts.
- **Examples:** 1000BASE-T, Fiber-optic SMF/MMF, RJ45, Hub, Repeater.
- **PDU:** **Bit**
- **KTU Buzzword:** *"Dumbest layer — no frames, no addresses."*

## 2.3 The TCP/IP 4-Layer (5-Layer Hybrid) Model

Since the Internet does not use the OSI model practically, the **TCP/IP suite** is the de-facto standard. The hybrid 5-layer model (used by **Forouzan** and in most KTU textbooks) is:

1. **Application Layer** (≈ OSI 5+6+7 fused)
2. **Transport Layer** (≈ OSI 4)
3. **Network (Internet) Layer** (≈ OSI 3)
4. **Data Link (Network Access) Layer** (≈ OSI 2)
5. **Physical Layer** (≈ OSI 1)

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Layer (OSI) | TCP/IP Equivalent | PDU Name | Addressing Used | Service Provided |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 7 – Application | Application | Data / Message | Process Port (app-level) | User-facing services (HTTP, DNS) |
| 6 – Presentation | (merged) | Data | — | Translation, Encryption, Compression |
| 5 – Session | (merged) | Data | — | Dialogue control, Synchronization |
| 4 – Transport | Transport | Segment (TCP) / Datagram (UDP) | Port Number (16-bit) | End-to-end, Multiplexing, Reliability |
| 3 – Network | Internet / Network | Packet | Logical IP (32 / 128 bit) | Routing, Logical addressing |
| 2 – Data Link | Data Link / Network Access | Frame | MAC (48 bit) | Framing, MAC, Error detection per hop |
| 1 – Physical | Physical | Bit | None (no address) | Bit↔Signal, Topology, Cable specs |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Condition / Constraint:**
> In KTU problems, remember: **L2 = hop**, **L4 = host-to-host process**, **L3 = end-to-end host**. This distinction is the single most-tested concept in Module 1.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Cybersecurity:** Layer 2 attacks (ARP spoofing, MAC flooding) are fundamentally different from Layer 3/4 attacks (IP spoofing, SYN flood) and Layer 7 attacks (SQLi, XSS). A *Layered Tasks* model is what allows security engineers to map threats to the correct *layer* and apply the right **defense in depth** strategy.
- **Protocol Design:** When you design a new application (say, a custom IoT protocol), the layering model forces you to *reuse* the existing lower layers (TCP/IP, Ethernet) and only innovate at the application layer. This is why the Internet scales.
- **Troubleshooting:** The **OSI model is the diagnostic ladder**: if `ping` (L3) works but `HTTP` (L7) fails, you know the fault is in the upper layers — a method universally taught in Cisco's troubleshooting methodology.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The Encapsulation/Decapsulation Process — Exhaustive Walkthrough

When a user sends an **HTTP GET request** from a web browser to a server, the data passes **down** through the stack on the sender side, is transmitted bit-by-bit on the medium, and then passes **up** through the stack on the receiver side. Let's trace it step by step.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Convention used in KTU textbooks (Forouzan / Stallings):**
> - Sender side = **Encapsulation** (headers/trailers are *added*).
> - Receiver side = **Decapsulation** (headers/trailers are *removed* and acted upon).
> - Each layer communicates with its **peer** on the remote host using a **virtual/horizontal** connection. Real wires only carry L1 signals.

### Step 1 — Application Layer (Origin)
The browser generates an HTTP message. The PDU at this point is a raw **HTTP request** (no header from the network stack yet — the application simply hands the data to the OS).

$$
\text{PDU}_7 = \text{HTTP GET /index.html} \quad \text{(Data)}
$$

### Step 2 — Presentation Layer
The data is left untouched if no encryption is used. If TLS/SSL is active, the HTTP payload is encrypted. *In KTU problems, assume plaintext unless mentioned.*

### Step 3 — Session Layer
A session identifier may be added if the connection uses a session-oriented protocol (e.g., RPC). For plain HTTP over TCP, this layer is logically present but transparent.

### Step 4 — Transport Layer — **SEGMENT Formed**
The OS adds a **TCP header** to the data. This header contains the source and destination **port numbers** (e.g., source 49152, destination 80), sequence number, ACK number, flags, window size, and checksum.

$$
\text{PDU}_4 = \text{TCP Header} \; \vert \quad \text{PDU}_7 \quad \longrightarrow \quad \text{Segment}
$$

The segment's length = **TCP Header (20 bytes minimum) + HTTP payload**.

### Step 5 — Network Layer — **PACKET Formed**
The OS adds an **IP header** to the segment. This header contains the source and destination **IP addresses** (e.g., 192.168.1.5 → 93.184.216.34), TTL, protocol field, and IP header checksum.

$$
\text{PDU}_3 = \text{IP Header} \; \vert \quad \text{PDU}_4 \quad \longrightarrow \quad \text{Packet}
$$

The packet's length = **IP Header (20 bytes) + Segment**.

### Step 6 — Data Link Layer — **FRAME Formed**
The NIC (Network Interface Card) adds a **MAC header** *and* a **trailer** (for CRC error detection). The MAC header contains the source and destination **MAC addresses** of the *next hop* (which for the first hop is the default gateway's MAC, discovered via ARP).

$$
\text{PDU}_2 = \text{MAC Header} \; \vert \quad \text{PDU}_3 \; \vert \quad \text{Trailer (CRC)} \quad \longrightarrow \quad \text{Frame}
$$

The frame's length = **MAC Header (14 bytes) + Packet + Trailer (4 bytes FCS)**.

### Step 7 — Physical Layer — **BITS Encoded**
The frame is converted into a sequence of bits and modulated onto the medium (electrical voltages on copper, light pulses on fiber, RF on wireless).

$$
\text{PDU}_1 = 01010110 \, 11010001 \, \ldots \quad \longrightarrow \quad \text{Bit Stream}
$$

### Step 8 — Receiver Side Decapsulation
The receiver's NIC samples the signal, reconstructs the bit stream, then **strips** the MAC header → IP header → TCP header → delivers the HTTP payload to the browser. The browser parses it and renders the page.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Header Overhead Calculation

A common **KTU 2024 numerical** is to compute the **efficiency** (goodput ratio) of a frame. The standard formula is:

$$
\eta = \frac{\text{Payload Size}}{\text{Total Frame Size}} = \frac{L_{\text{data}}}{L_{\text{header}} + L_{\text{data}} + L_{\text{trailer}}}
$$

Where:
- $L_{\text{data}}$ = actual user data (in bytes)
- $L_{\text{header}}$ = sum of all headers added by layers (in bytes)
- $L_{\text{trailer}}$ = trailer (e.g., 4 bytes CRC at L2)

### Worked Numerical Example

> **Problem:** A user sends a 1200-byte file via HTTP over TCP/IP over Ethernet.
> - TCP header = 20 bytes
> - IP header = 20 bytes
> - Ethernet header + trailer = 14 + 4 = 18 bytes
> 
> Find the (a) total frame size on the wire, (b) efficiency.

**Solution:**

**(a) Total Frame Size:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{\text{total}} &= L_{\text{eth header}} + L_{\text{IP}} + L_{\text{TCP}} + L_{\text{data}} + L_{\text{eth trailer}} \\
&= 14 + 20 + 20 + 1200 + 4 \\
&= 1258 \; \text{bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(b) Efficiency:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta &= \frac{L_{\text{data}}}{L_{\text{total}}} \times 100\% \\
&= \frac{1200}{1258} \times 100\% \\
&= 95.389\% \approx 95.39\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Tip:** Many students forget to include the **trailer (CRC)** in the denominator, losing 2 marks. Always include it.

## 3.3 Peer-to-Peer Process Diagram (Logical View)

A **peer-to-peer process** is a *virtual* communication between the *same layer* on two different machines. For example, the **Transport layer** of Host A talks logically to the **Transport layer** of Host B using TCP — even though the actual bits travel through all lower layers.

The actual data flow is **vertical** (down the stack on the sender, up the stack on the receiver), but the *logical* flow is **horizontal** (peer-to-peer).

## 3.4 Service Access Points (SAP) — The Glue Between Layers

In the KTU scheme, you must know that each layer offers its services to the layer above through a **Service Access Point (SAP)**, identified by a **port address**.

- L1 ↔ L2: **no SAP** (electrical interface only)
- L2 ↔ L3: **SAP = IP address** (logical)
- L3 ↔ L4: **SAP = Port number** (e.g., TCP port 80)
- L4 ↔ L5: **SAP = Application identifier** (e.g., socket)

The **interface** between two layers is a *programming/library boundary* (e.g., the **Socket API** between L4 and L5). The **service** is what one layer *does for* the one above.

## 3.5 Connection-Oriented vs Connectionless Service

| Aspect | Connection-Oriented | Connectionless |
|---|---|---|
| Phases | Setup → Data Transfer → Teardown | Single shot (no setup) |
| Reliability | Acknowledged, retransmitted | No guarantee |
| Example | TCP, ATM | UDP, IP, Ethernet |
| Order | Preserved | Not guaranteed |
| Overhead | Higher (handshakes) | Lower |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — The OSI 7-Layer Model with PDUs

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    app["LAYER 7 APPLICATION\nPDU DATA\nEX HTTP FTP DNS"]
    pres["LAYER 6 PRESENTATION\nPDU DATA\nENCRYPTION COMPRESSION"]
    sess["LAYER 5 SESSION\nPDU DATA\nDIALOGUE CONTROL"]
    trans["LAYER 4 TRANSPORT\nPDU SEGMENT DATAGRAM\nTCP UDP PORT NUMBER"]
    net["LAYER 3 NETWORK\nPDU PACKET\nIP ROUTING LOGICAL ADDR"]
    dl["LAYER 2 DATA LINK\nPDU FRAME\nMAC LLC ERROR DETECT"]
    phy["LAYER 1 PHYSICAL\nPDU BIT\nVOLTAGE LIGHT RF"]

    app --> pres
    pres --> sess
    sess --> trans
    trans --> net
    net --> dl
    dl --> phy
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The data flow is **downward** (encapsulation) on the sender. The arrow direction *within* a single host's stack is always vertical between adjacent layers. **Horizontal arrows** between hosts only exist *logically* (peer-to-peer), as shown in the next diagram.

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Peer-to-Peer Logical Communication

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph HostA["HOST A STACK"]
        a1["L7 APP"] --- a2["L6 PRES"]
        a2 --- a3["L5 SESS"]
        a3 --- a4["L4 TRANS"]
        a4 --- a5["L3 NET"]
        a5 --- a6["L2 DL"]
        a6 --- a7["L1 PHY"]
    end
    subgraph HostB["HOST B STACK"]
        b1["L7 APP"] --- b2["L6 PRES"]
        b2 --- b3["L5 SESS"]
        b3 --- b4["L4 TRANS"]
        b4 --- b5["L3 NET"]
        b5 --- b6["L2 DL"]
        b6 --- b7["L1 PHY"]
    end

    a1 <-. PEER L7 .-> b1
    a2 <-. PEER L6 .-> b2
    a3 <-. PEER L5 .-> b3
    a4 <-. PEER L4 .-> b4
    a5 <-. PEER L3 .-> b5
    a6 <-. PEER L2 .-> b6
    a7 == PHYSICAL MEDIUM ==> b7
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** The **dotted** lines represent *virtual / logical* peer-to-peer communication (e.g., Host A's L4 "talks" to Host B's L4 over TCP). The **thick double-line** at the bottom is the **only real** communication — physical bits on the wire. This is the single most-asked diagram in KTU 2024.

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — TCP/IP vs OSI Layer Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    O7["OSI L7 APPLICATION"]
    O6["OSI L6 PRESENTATION"]
    O5["OSI L5 SESSION"]
    O4["OSI L4 TRANSPORT"]
    O3["OSI L3 NETWORK"]
    O2["OSI L2 DATA LINK"]
    O1["OSI L1 PHYSICAL"]

    T1["TCP/IP APPLICATION LAYER"]
    T2["TCP/IP TRANSPORT LAYER"]
    T3["TCP/IP INTERNET LAYER"]
    T4["TCP/IP NETWORK ACCESS LAYER"]

    O7 --- T1
    O6 --- T1
    O5 --- T1
    O4 --- T2
    O3 --- T3
    O2 --- T4
    O1 --- T4
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Encapsulation Data Flow (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    msg["USER DATA\nEXAMPLE 1200 BYTES"]
    tcpH["TCP HEADER\n20 BYTES"]
    ipH["IP HEADER\n20 BYTES"]
    macH["MAC HEADER\n14 BYTES"]
    crc["TRAILER CRC\n4 BYTES"]

    seg["SEGMENT\nTCP HDR + DATA"]
    pkt["PACKET\nIP HDR + SEGMENT"]
    frm["FRAME\nMAC HDR + PKT + CRC"]
    bits["BIT STREAM\n01010110 11010001"]

    msg --> seg
    tcpH --> seg
    seg --> pkt
    ipH --> pkt
    pkt --> frm
    macH --> frm
    crc --> frm
    frm --> bits
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Usage Tip:** This is the diagram to draw when solving **efficiency numericals** in KTU exams. Label each block with the byte size to make the computation auditable to the examiner.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Set B]**
**Define the term "Layered Task" in the context of computer networks. Why is the OSI model organized as seven layers instead of using a single layer?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A *layered task* refers to the systematic division of the entire data communication process into smaller, modular functional groups called *layers*, where each layer performs a specific set of operations and communicates only with its immediate neighbors through well-defined **Service Access Points (SAPs)**. The OSI model uses **seven layers** instead of one because: **(1) Modularity** — each layer can be designed, modified, and replaced independently; **(2) Interoperability** — products from different vendors can interoperate if they follow the layer's protocol; **(3) Simplicity** — a complex monolithic design is split into manageable, teachable units; and **(4) Standardization** — global protocols (TCP, IP, Ethernet) can be defined per layer. *(3 marks: definition 1m, reasons 1.5m, conclusion 0.5m)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**List the seven layers of the OSI model in order (from Layer 1 to Layer 7) and state the Protocol Data Unit (PDU) at each layer.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — table format expected):**

| Layer # | Layer Name | PDU |
|---|---|---|
| 7 | Application | Data / Message |
| 6 | Presentation | Data |
| 5 | Session | Data |
| 4 | Transport | Segment (TCP) / Datagram (UDP) |
| 3 | Network | Packet |
| 2 | Data Link | Frame |
| 1 | Physical | Bit |

*(1 mark: correct order; 1 mark: all 7 names; 1 mark: PDUs — examiner cuts 0.5 if even one is missed)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Set A]**

**(a)** Explain the functions of each layer of the OSI reference model. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** With a neat diagram, describe the process of **encapsulation** and **decapsulation** in the OSI model. Also, explain the role of **headers** and **trailers**. *(7 marks)*

#### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Functions of each OSI layer (7 marks = 7 × 1 mark per layer):**

1. **Physical Layer (L1):** Transmits raw bits as electrical, optical, or RF signals. Defines cable types, connectors, voltage levels, and topologies (bus, star, mesh). Examples: Hub, Repeater, RJ45.
2. **Data Link Layer (L2):** Groups bits into **frames**, performs error detection/correction using CRC, controls medium access (CSMA/CD in Ethernet, CSMA/CA in Wi-Fi), and uses **MAC addresses (48-bit)** for node-to-node delivery. Sub-layers: LLC + MAC.
3. **Network Layer (L3):** Provides best-effort, connectionless **packet forwarding** between hosts across multiple networks. Uses **logical IP addresses** for end-to-end identification. Handles routing (OSPF, BGP) and fragmentation.
4. **Transport Layer (L4):** Provides **end-to-end, process-to-process** delivery using **port numbers (16-bit)**. TCP offers reliable, ordered, connection-oriented service; UDP offers connectionless, best-effort service. Handles flow and congestion control.
5. **Session Layer (L5):** Establishes, manages, and terminates **sessions/dialogues** between applications. Handles tokens, checkpoints, and recovery.
6. **Presentation Layer (L6):** Handles data **syntax** — translation (ASCII↔EBCDIC), **encryption/decryption** (TLS/SSL), and **compression** (JPEG, MPEG).
7. **Application Layer (L7):** Closest to the user. Provides services like HTTP, FTP, SMTP, DNS, SNMP. The user interacts with the network through this layer.

**Part (b) — Encapsulation / Decapsulation (7 marks):**

- **Encapsulation** = the sender-side process of **adding a header (and sometimes trailer)** at each layer as the data moves **down** the stack.
- **Decapsulation** = the receiver-side process of **removing** these headers as data moves **up** the stack; each layer reads its peer's header and acts on it.
- **Headers** carry control information (addresses, sequence numbers, error codes) used by the corresponding peer layer on the other host.
- **Trailers** (e.g., CRC) are typically added only at the Data Link layer for end-of-frame error detection.
- The data unit grows in size as it descends — at the bottom it is a bit stream; at the top it is a meaningful application message. *(See Section 4.4 Mermaid diagram for the standard figure.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice): [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Set C]**

**(a)** Compare the OSI model and the TCP/IP model. List the layers of both models and explain the mapping between them. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Define the following terms with one example each: *(7 marks — 1.75 marks per term)*
   (i) Service Access Point (SAP)
   (ii) Protocol Data Unit (PDU)
   (iii) Peer-to-Peer Process
   (iv) Connection-Oriented Service

#### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — OSI vs TCP/IP (7 marks):**

| Feature | OSI | TCP/IP |
|---|---|---|
| Layers | 7 | 4 (or 5) |
| Origin | ISO | ARPANET / IETF |
| Adoption | Theoretical reference | Internet standard |
| Top layer | Application (split into 3) | Application (merged) |
| Reliability | Defined at L4 only | Defined only in TCP |
| Rigidness | Strict layered | Loose; allows cross-layer optimization |

**Mapping:**
- OSI Layers 7+6+5 → **TCP/IP Application Layer**
- OSI Layer 4 → **TCP/IP Transport Layer**
- OSI Layer 3 → **TCP/IP Internet (Network) Layer**
- OSI Layers 2+1 → **TCP/IP Network Access Layer**

*(4 marks: comparison table; 3 marks: mapping explanation with diagram)*

**Part (b) — Definitions (7 marks):**

**(i) SAP (1.75 m):** A logical interface through which one layer requests services from the layer immediately below. **Example:** Port number 80 is the SAP through which an HTTP application accesses the TCP transport service.

**(ii) PDU (1.75 m):** The unit of data passed between two peer layers at a given layer. **Example:** A *segment* is the PDU at the Transport layer; a *frame* is the PDU at the Data Link layer.

**(iii) Peer-to-Peer Process (1.75 m):** The logical communication between the *same layer* on two different machines. **Example:** Host A's Transport layer exchanging TCP segments with Host B's Transport layer.

**(iv) Connection-Oriented Service (1.75 m):** A service that establishes a logical connection (handshake) before data transfer and reliably delivers all bytes in order. **Example:** TCP — used by HTTP, FTP, SSH.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks every semester:**
> 1. **Confusing "Packet" and "Frame":** A *frame* is the L2 PDU on a single link; a *packet* is the L3 PDU across networks. Many students use these interchangeably — examiners WILL cut 1 mark each instance.
> 2. **Forgetting the trailer in efficiency numericals:** Always add 4 bytes for Ethernet CRC (FCS). A common trick: examiner may ask for "header-only" efficiency. Read the question carefully.
> 3. **Writing "OSI is the Internet protocol":** It is NOT. The Internet uses TCP/IP. OSI is a *reference* model used for teaching and conceptual mapping.
> 4. **Forgetting the Physical Layer has no address:** Students often claim "physical addressing exists" — it does NOT. Addressing starts at L2.
> 5. **Mixing up MAC vs IP addressing:** MAC = 48-bit, L2, per-hop. IP = 32-bit (IPv4) / 128-bit (IPv6), L3, end-to-end.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **The "Layered Tasks" concept** decomposes networking into **modular, hierarchical layers**, each offering a service to the one above.
- **OSI = 7 layers** (PDUs: Data → Data → Data → Segment/Datagram → Packet → Frame → Bit).
- **TCP/IP = 4 layers** (Application, Transport, Internet, Network Access).
- **Encapsulation = adding headers/trailers downward** on the sender; **Decapsulation = removing them upward** on the receiver.
- **PDU names** (in order): **Data, Data, Data, Segment, Packet, Frame, Bit** for OSI 7→1.
- **Addressing is layer-specific:** L2 uses MAC (48-bit), L3 uses IP (32/128-bit), L4 uses ports (16-bit).
- **Peer-to-peer** is *logical/horizontal* between same layers; **physical** data flow is *vertical* down/up the stack.
- **Connection-oriented (TCP)** = reliable, ordered, handshaked. **Connectionless (UDP, IP)** = best-effort, no setup.
- **Efficiency formula:** $\eta = L_{\text{data}} \,/\, (L_{\text{header}} + L_{\text{data}} + L_{\text{trailer}})$.
- **Cybersecurity context:** Attacks are *layered* (L2 ARP spoofing → L3 IP spoofing → L4 SYN flood → L7 XSS), and defenses must also be *layered* — this is the foundation of **defense in depth** and the reason Module 1 of *Network & Data Security* begins with the OSI model.
- **Key real-world protocols per layer (must memorize):** HTTP/7, TLS/6, RPC/5, TCP-UDP/4, IP-ICMP/3, Ethernet-WiFi/2, 1000BASE-T/1.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: OSI Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMRT631 - NETWORK & DATA SECURITY > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** OSI Model ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned) The **Open Systems Interconnection (OSI) Model** is a conceptual and standardized reference architecture, formally defined by the **International Organization for Standardization (ISO)** under the standard **ISO/IEC 7498-1**, which provides a **seven-layered abstract framework** for understanding and designing the communication functions of a telecommunication or computing system, independent of any specific underlying technology, vendor, or protocol implementation. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The OSI model is taught as a **reference model**, not an implementation model. Real-world networks (like the Internet) use the **TCP/IP suite**. Examiners frequently ask students to *compare* the two, so do not confuse the OSI model with the TCP/IP protocol stack. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Postal Service Imagine you are sending a **registered letter** from your home in Kerala to a friend in Delhi: - **You (Application Layer):** You write the letter in your native language (Malayalam). - **Translator (Presentation Layer):** Translates the letter into a common language (English/Hindi) and encrypts it. - **Conversation Manager (Session Layer):** Decides when the conversation starts and ends; keeps a log of letters exchanged. - **Postal Counter (Transport Layer):** Breaks the letter into envelopes (segments), assigns tracking numbers (port numbers), and ensures reliable delivery. - **Route Planner (Network Layer):** Decides the path — via Chennai, Hyderabad, or Mumbai — using postal codes (IP addresses). - **Truck Driver (Data Link Layer):** Drives the truck on the road, handles traffic signals (MAC addresses), and detects accidents (errors). - **Road & Wires (Physical Layer):** The actual road, the truck, and the fuel — the raw electrical/optical signals. Each layer **only talks to the layer directly above and below it** — just as a truck driver does not need to know what is written inside the envelope. > [!NOTE] > **Mnemonic for the 7 Layers (Top-Down):** **A**ll **P**eople **S**eem **T**o **N**eed **D**ata **P**rocessing > **Mnemonic (Bottom-Up):** **P**lease **D**o **N**ot **T**hrow **S**ausage **P**izza **A**way ### 1.3 Why the OSI Model Matters in Engineering - Provides **vendor-neutral interoperability** between heterogeneous systems. - Simplifies **troubleshooting** — network engineers can isolate faults to a specific layer. - Enables **modular design** — protocols at one layer can be upgraded without redesigning others. - Serves as a **universal vocabulary** for describing network functions in standards documents and engineering specifications. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Seven Layers — Detailed Functional Breakdown The OSI model is structured as a **strict vertical stack of 7 layers**, numbered from bottom (Layer 1) to top (Layer 7). Each layer performs a distinct set of functions and exposes a well-defined **Service Access Point (SAP)** to the layer above it. #### **Layer 1 — Physical Layer** - **Concern:** Transmission of raw unstructured bit streams over a physical medium. - **Functions:** - Bit synchronization, bit rate control, physical topology definition. - Mechanical specifications (connectors, pin layouts), electrical specifications (voltage levels, impedance), and signaling. - Multiplexing, line coding (e.g., NRZ, Manchester), and physical medium selection (copper, fiber, radio). - **PDU (Protocol Data Unit):** **Bit** - **Examples:** USB, Ethernet (100BASE-TX), Bluetooth Physical Layer, SONET/SDH, IEEE 802.11 PHY. #### **Layer 2 — Data Link Layer** - **Concern:** Reliable node-to-node data transfer across the physical link. - **Sub-layers:** - **LLC (Logical Link Control)** — IEEE 802.2 — handles error notification and flow control. - **MAC (Media Access Control)** — controls access to the shared medium and physical addressing. - **Functions:** - Framing (start/stop flags), physical (MAC) addressing. - Error detection (CRC) and error correction (ARQ mechanisms). - Flow control (stop-and-wait, sliding window). - **PDU:** **Frame** - **Examples:** Ethernet (IEEE 802.3), Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11), PPP, HDLC, Frame Relay. #### **Layer 3 — Network Layer** - **Concern:** End-to-end packet delivery across multiple interconnected networks (internets). - **Functions:** - Logical addressing (IP addresses — IPv4/IPv6). - Routing and path determination (OSPF, BGP, RIP). - Packet forwarding, fragmentation, and reassembly. - Congestion control at the network level. - **PDU:** **Packet** (or **Datagram** in connectionless service) - **Examples:** IPv4, IPv6, ICMP, IGMP, IPsec, MPLS. #### **Layer 4 — Transport Layer** - **Concern:** Reliable, transparent end-to-end data transport between host processes. - **Functions:** - **Segmentation and reassembly** of application data. - **Port addressing** (16-bit port numbers, range 0–65535). - **Connection management:** TCP (connection-oriented) vs UDP (connectionless). - **End-to-end flow control and error recovery.** - **Multiplexing/Demultiplexing** of application sessions. - **PDU:** **Segment** (TCP) or **Datagram** (UDP) - **Examples:** TCP, UDP, SCTP, DCCP. #### **Layer 5 — Session Layer** - **Concern:** Establishment, coordination, management, and termination of communication sessions between applications. - **Functions:** - **Dialog control** (half-duplex vs full-duplex). - **Synchronization points** (checkpoints) to allow resumption after a crash. - **Session identification and authentication** at the session level. - **PDU:** **Data** (or **SPDU** — Session Protocol Data Unit) - **Examples:** NetBIOS, RPC, PPTP, SIP (Session Initiation Protocol). > [!NOTE] > **KTU Examiner Tip:** The Session, Presentation, and Application layers are often *merged* in real-world protocols (e.g., HTTP handles application, presentation, and session concerns). Examiners love asking "why is this so?" — answer: **practicality and overhead reduction**. #### **Layer 6 — Presentation Layer** - **Concern:** Syntax and semantics of the information exchanged between applications. - **Functions:** - **Data translation** (EBCDIC ↔ ASCII, character encoding). - **Data formatting and syntax** (XML, JSON serialization conceptually). - **Encryption and decryption** (e.g., TLS operates here conceptually). - **Data compression** (e.g., MPEG for video, ZIP conceptually). - **PDU:** **Data** - **Examples:** SSL/TLS (partially), MIME, XDR, ASN.1, ASCII. #### **Layer 7 — Application Layer** - **Concern:** Network services directly available to end-user applications and the user. - **Functions:** - Provides the interface for applications to access network services. - **Resource sharing, file transfer, email, web browsing, network management.** - **PDU:** **Data** (often called **Message**) - **Examples:** HTTP/HTTPS, FTP, SMTP, DNS, DHCP, SNMP, POP3, IMAP, Telnet, SSH. ### 2.2 Key High-Yield Concepts (Summary Table) | **Concept** | **Definition / Value** | **Layer** | **Engineering Significance** | |---|---|---|---| | Bit | Smallest unit of data, 0 or 1 | L1 | Raw signal transmission | | Frame | L2 PDU with MAC header + trailer | L2 | Local network delivery | | Packet | L3 PDU with IP header | L3 | Routed internetwork delivery | | Segment | L4 PDU with TCP header (port + seq) | L4 | Reliable host-to-host transport | | Datagram | L4 PDU for UDP (no connection) | L4 | Fast, unreliable transport | | SAP | Service Access Point identifier | All | Logical interface between adjacent layers | | PDU | Protocol Data Unit | All | Encapsulated data unit at a given layer | | Port Number | 16-bit identifier (0–65535) | L4 | Multiplexes multiple app sessions on one host | | MAC Address | 48-bit physical address | L2 | Globally unique NIC identifier (OUI + NIC) | | IP Address | 32-bit (IPv4) / 128-bit (IPv6) | L3 | Logical hierarchical addressing for routing | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Distinction:** MAC address is **flat (non-hierarchical)** and used on a single link segment; IP address is **hierarchical** (network + host) and used for end-to-end routing across multiple networks. Examiners test this distinction repeatedly. ### 2.3 Why the Model Was Designed This Way The OSI model was engineered around three foundational design principles: - **Layering Principle:** Each layer performs a *single, well-defined* function. - **Service Abstraction:** Each layer offers services to the layer above and uses services from the layer below. - **Independence:** Changes in one layer should not require changes in adjacent layers (loose coupling). - **Peer-to-Peer Communication:** Layer *N* on one host logically communicates with Layer *N* on another host using a *peer protocol*, while the actual data travels down and up the stack. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivation: Encapsulation & Decapsulation Process ### 3.1 The Encapsulation Process (Sender Side) When a user requests a webpage, the data must travel from the Application layer (L7) all the way down to the Physical layer (L1). At each layer, the layer adds its own **header** (and sometimes **trailer**) to the PDU received from the layer above. This is called **encapsulation**. Let the application data generated at L7 be denoted as $D_{app}$. **Step 1 — Application Layer (L7):** The application (e.g., browser) generates raw application data: $$D_7 = D_{app}$$ **Step 2 — Presentation Layer (L6):** Performs encoding, encryption, or compression. Encapsulates with a presentation header $H_6$: $$D_6 = H_6 \oplus D_7$$ where $\oplus$ denotes concatenation. **Step 3 — Session Layer (L5):** Adds session identification, synchronization tokens: $$D_5 = H_5 \oplus D_6$$ **Step 4 — Transport Layer (L4):** Segments the data, adds source/destination **port numbers** and **sequence numbers**: $$D_4 = H_4 \oplus D_5 \quad \text{(Segment)}$$ **Step 5 — Network Layer (L3):** Adds source/destination **IP addresses**: $$D_3 = H_3 \oplus D_4 \quad \text{(Packet / Datagram)}$$ **Step 6 — Data Link Layer (L2):** Adds **MAC addresses** as a header $H_2$ and a trailer $T_2$ (containing CRC for error detection): $$D_2 = H_2 \oplus D_3 \oplus T_2 \quad \text{(Frame)}$$ **Step 7 — Physical Layer (L1):** Converts the frame into a stream of bits for transmission over the medium: $$D_1 = \text{encode}(D_2) \quad \text{(Bits on the wire)}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Total Overhead Calculation (Important for Exam):** If a message of size $S$ bytes is sent and the header overhead at layers L4, L3, L2 are $h_4, h_3, h_2$ bytes respectively (with L2 also adding a trailer of $t_2$ bytes), the total data transmitted on the wire is: > $$T_{wire} = S + h_4 + h_3 + h_2 + t_2 \quad \text{bytes}$$ > The **efficiency ratio** is: > $$\eta = \frac{S}{T_{wire}} = \frac{S}{S + h_4 + h_3 + h_2 + t_2}$$ **Worked Example:** Suppose $S = 1000$ bytes, $h_4 = 20$ (TCP), $h_3 = 20$ (IPv4), $h_2 = 14$ (Ethernet header), $t_2 = 4$ (Ethernet FCS). Calculate the efficiency. **Solution:** Substituting into the formula: $$T_{wire} = 1000 + 20 + 20 + 14 + 4 = 1058 \text{ bytes}$$ The efficiency is: $$\eta = \frac{1000}{1058} \approx 0.9452 = 94.52\%$$ ### 3.2 The Decapsulation Process (Receiver Side) Decapsulation is the **reverse process** performed at the receiving host. At each layer, the layer removes its corresponding header (and trailer), inspects it to determine the correct handling, and passes the remaining payload up to the next higher layer. The general flow can be represented as: $$D_{N-1} = \text{decap}(D_N) = D_N \setminus H_N$$ where $\setminus$ denotes removal of the L$N$ header. > [!NOTE] > **Critical Insight:** Only Layer 2 and Layer 3 (in practice) headers are inspected by **intermediate networking devices** (switches and routers). A switch reads L2 headers (MAC), and a router reads L3 headers (IP). Devices operating at lower layers do **not** "see" L4–L7 data — this is the basis of **network segmentation and security firewalls**. ### 3.3 Peer-to-Peer Logical Communication Although data physically flows vertically down the sender's stack, across the medium, and up the receiver's stack, the *protocols* at each layer logically communicate with their *peer* layer on the remote host. The mapping is: | **OSI Layer** | **Logical Peer Protocol** | **Unit Exchanged** | |---|---|---| | L7 — Application | Application-specific (e.g., HTTP) | Message | | L4 — Transport | TCP / UDP | Segment / Datagram | | L3 — Network | IP (IPv4 / IPv6) | Packet | | L2 — Data Link | Ethernet, Wi-Fi, PPP | Frame | | L1 — Physical | Voltage/light pulses | Bit | The header added at L$N$ on the sender is used and stripped at L$N$ on the receiver. This is known as the **peer-layer encapsulation principle**. ### 3.4 Interaction with Network Devices (Analytical Matrix) | **Device** | **Operates At** | **Reads Header Of** | **Does Not Process** | |---|---|---|---| | Hub (legacy) | L1 | None (repeats electrical signal) | All headers | | Switch / Bridge | L2 | L2 (MAC addresses) | L3, L4, L7 | | Router | L3 | L3 (IP addresses) | L4, L7 (in basic routing) | | Transport-layer Gateway | L4 | L3 and L4 headers | L7 | | Application-layer Proxy | L7 | All layers up to L7 | None (full inspection) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Security Insight (directly relevant to your course PEMRT631):** A **firewall** typically operates at L3/L4 (packet filter) or L7 (stateful inspection / application-layer gateway). A **Network Intrusion Detection System (NIDS)** like Snort operates at L2–L4, while a **Deep Packet Inspection (DPI)** engine works at L7. The OSI layer at which a security device operates determines **what attacks it can see and block**. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 The Seven-Layer OSI Architecture (Top-Down View) ```mermaid flowchart TB L7["LAYER 7 APPLICATION
HTTP FTP SMTP DNS"] L6["LAYER 6 PRESENTATION
SSL TLS MIME ENCRYPTION"] L5["LAYER 5 SESSION
RPC NETBIOS SIP PPTP"] L4["LAYER 4 TRANSPORT
TCP UDP SCTP PORTS"] L3["LAYER 3 NETWORK
IP ICMP OSPF BGP ROUTING"] L2["LAYER 2 DATA LINK
MAC LLC ETHERNET WIFI"] L1["LAYER 1 PHYSICAL
BITS VOLTAGE FIBER RADIO"] L7 --- L6 L6 --- L5 L5 --- L4 L4 --- L3 L3 --- L2 L2 --- L1 ``` ### 4.2 Data Flow with Encapsulation & Decapsulation (Sender → Receiver) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph SND["SENDER HOST"] direction TB A1["L7 APP DATA M"] A2["L6 ADD H6 ENCRYPT M"] A3["L5 ADD H5 SESSION TOKEN"] A4["L4 ADD H4 TCP PORTS SEGMENT"] A5["L3 ADD H3 IP ADDRESS PACKET"] A6["L2 ADD H2 MAC AND T2 CRC FRAME"] A7["L1 TRANSMIT BITS OVER MEDIUM"] A1 --> A2 A2 --> A3 A3 --> A4 A4 --> A5 A5 --> A6 A6 --> A7 end A7 -- "BITS TRAVEL" --> B1 subgraph RCV["RECEIVER HOST"] direction TB B1["L1 RECEIVE BITS FRAME"] B2["L2 REMOVE H2 T2 FRAME TO PACKET"] B3["L3 REMOVE H3 PACKET TO SEGMENT"] B4["L4 REMOVE H4 SEGMENT TO DATA"] B5["L5 REMOVE H5 DATA TO PRESENTATION"] B6["L6 DECRYPT DECOMPRESS DATA"] B7["L7 DELIVER TO APPLICATION"] B1 --> B2 B2 --> B3 B3 --> B4 B4 --> B5 B5 --> B6 B6 --> B7 end ``` ### 4.3 Peer-to-Peer Logical Communication Across the Stack ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph SH["SENDER"] S7["L7 APPLICATION"] S4["L4 TRANSPORT"] S3["L3 NETWORK"] S2["L2 DATA LINK"] S1["L1 PHYSICAL"] S7 --- S4 S4 --- S3 S3 --- S2 S2 --- S1 end subgraph RH["RECEIVER"] R7["L7 APPLICATION"] R4["L4 TRANSPORT"] R3["L3 NETWORK"] R2["L2 DATA LINK"] R1["L1 PHYSICAL"] R7 --- R4 R4 --- R3 R3 --- R2 R2 --- R1 end S7 -. "HTTP PROTOCOL PEER" .-> R7 S4 -. "TCP PEER SEGMENT" .-> R4 S3 -. "IP PEER PACKET" .-> R3 S2 -. "MAC PEER FRAME" .-> R2 S1 -. "RAW BITS" .-> R1 ``` ### 4.4 Functional Architecture: Where Network Devices Operate ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph OSCI["OSI LAYERS 1 TO 7"] L7N["L7 APPLICATION"] L6N["L6 PRESENTATION"] L5N["L5 SESSION"] L4N["L4 TRANSPORT"] L3N["L3 NETWORK"] L2N["L2 DATA LINK"] L1N["L1 PHYSICAL"] end HUB["HUB OP L1 REPEATER"] SW["SWITCH OP L2 MAC TABLE"] RT["ROUTER OP L3 IP ROUTING"] FW["FIREWALL OP L3 L4 L7"] PRX["PROXY OP L7 DEEP INSPECTION"] HUB --- L1N SW --- L2N RT --- L3N FW --- L4N PRX --- L7N ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Exam Paper 2024]** **Q1. Define the OSI Reference Model. List all seven layers in order from top to bottom and state the primary function of the Network Layer.** `[3 Marks]` **Model Answer:** The **OSI (Open Systems Interconnection) Reference Model** is a seven-layered abstract framework defined by ISO in standard **ISO/IEC 7498-1** that standardizes the functions of a communication system into distinct layers, enabling interoperability between heterogeneous systems. The seven layers (top to bottom) are: 1. **Application (L7)** 2. **Presentation (L6)** 3. **Session (L5)** 4. **Transport (L4)** 5. **Network (L3)** 6. **Data Link (L2)** 7. **Physical (L1)** **Primary function of the Network Layer (L3):** It is responsible for the **end-to-end delivery of packets** across multiple interconnected networks. It provides **logical addressing (IP addresses)**, **routing** (path determination), and **packet forwarding** functions. --- > **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Sessional Exam]** **Q2. Differentiate between the TCP/IP Model and the OSI Model. State any three differences.** `[3 Marks]` **Model Answer:** | **S.No.** | **OSI Model** | **TCP/IP Model** | |---|---|---| | 1 | Has **7 layers** (Application, Presentation, Session, Transport, Network, Data Link, Physical) | Has **4 layers** (Application, Transport, Internet, Network Access) | | 2 | Conceptual **reference model**; protocols rarely exist for all layers | Practical **implementation model**; protocols are widely used (TCP, IP, HTTP, FTP) | | 3 | Developed by **ISO** in 1984 | Developed by **DARPA / IETF** in the 1970s | | 4 | Strict separation of Session, Presentation, and Application concerns | Application layer **combines** Application, Presentation, and Session functions | | 5 | Transport layer reliability is generic | Defines **TCP (reliable)** and **UDP (unreliable)** explicitly | --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) > **Choose either Question A OR Question B. Both carry equal marks.** --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 1]** **(a)** Explain the functions of all **seven layers** of the OSI Reference Model with one protocol example for each layer. `[7 Marks]` **Model Solution:** | **Layer** | **Primary Functions** | **Example Protocol/Standard** | |---|---|---| | **1. Physical** | Bit transmission, voltage levels, mechanical connectors, line coding, physical topology | **Ethernet 100BASE-TX, USB, IEEE 802.11 PHY, SONET** | | **2. Data Link** | Framing, MAC addressing, error detection (CRC), flow control, medium access control | **Ethernet (IEEE 802.3), Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11), PPP, HDLC** | | **3. Network** | Logical addressing, routing, fragmentation, packet forwarding across networks | **IPv4, IPv6, ICMP, OSPF, BGP** | | **4. Transport** | End-to-end segmentation, port addressing, connection management, flow & error control | **TCP, UDP, SCTP** | | **5. Session** | Dialog control, session establishment/termination, synchronization checkpoints | **NetBIOS, RPC, SIP, PPTP** | | **6. Presentation** | Data translation, encoding, encryption/decryption, compression | **SSL/TLS, MIME, XDR, ASN.1, JPEG, MPEG** | | **7. Application** | Network services to end-user apps: file transfer, email, web, name resolution | **HTTP, FTP, SMTP, DNS, DHCP, SNMP, Telnet, SSH** | --- **(b)** With a neat diagram, describe the process of **encapsulation and decapsulation** in the OSI model. Explain how a switch and a router use OSI headers. `[7 Marks]` **Model Solution:** **Encapsulation (Sender Side, L7 → L1):** The application generates data $D_{app}$. At each layer, a header (and possibly trailer) is prepended/appended. The process is: $$D_7 = D_{app}$$ $$D_6 = H_6 \oplus D_7$$ $$D_5 = H_5 \oplus D_6$$ $$D_4 = H_4 \oplus D_5 \quad \text{(TCP Segment)}$$ $$D_3 = H_3 \oplus D_4 \quad \text{(IP Packet)}$$ $$D_2 = H_2 \oplus D_3 \oplus T_2 \quad \text{(Ethernet Frame)}$$ $$D_1 = \text{bits}(D_2) \quad \text{(Physical Signal)}$$ **Decapsulation (Receiver Side, L1 → L7):** The reverse process is performed. The receiver strips each layer's header, processes it, and passes the payload to the next higher layer. **Switch operation:** A switch operates at **L2**. It reads the **destination MAC address** in the L2 header $H_2$ and uses its **MAC address table** to forward the frame out the correct port. The switch **does not modify** the L3/L4 headers and does not inspect them. **Router operation:** A router operates at **L3**. It reads the **destination IP address** in the L3 header $H_3$, performs a **routing table lookup**, decrements the IP TTL, recomputes the L2 header (with new MAC addresses for the next hop), and forwards the packet. The router **decrements and re-encapsulates** L2, but leaves L4 and above untouched. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 1]** **(a)** Compare the **OSI model and TCP/IP model** in detail. Why is the TCP/IP model preferred in real-world Internet implementations despite the OSI model being a complete reference? `[7 Marks]` **Model Solution:** **Detailed Comparison:** | **Parameter** | **OSI Model** | **TCP/IP Model** | |---|---|---| | Full form | Open Systems Interconnection | Transmission Control Protocol / Internet Protocol | | Number of layers | 7 | 4 | | Origin | ISO (1984) | ARPA / DARPA / IETF (1970s) | | Type | Reference / Conceptual model | Practical implementation model | | Layer separation | Strict separation of concerns | Application layer merges L5/L6/L7 | | Protocol suite | Protocols were never fully built | Protocols (TCP, IP, UDP, HTTP) are in active use | | Adoption | Mostly academic; used for teaching and vendor specifications | Foundation of the modern Internet | | Dependency | Protocols can be developed independently | Protocols are tightly coupled to the model | | Transport reliability | Generic guidance | Defines TCP (reliable) and UDP (best-effort) explicitly | | Security layering | Discussed generically (e.g., encryption at L6) | Security is implemented at multiple layers (TLS at L7, IPsec at L3) | **Why TCP/IP is preferred in practice:** 1. **First-mover advantage:** TCP/IP predates OSI and was already the foundation of ARPANET and the early Internet. 2. **Simplicity:** 4 layers are easier to implement and debug than 7. 3. **Protocol maturity:** TCP/IP has decades of battle-tested, RFC-standardized protocols. 4. **Vendor and OS support:** All major operating systems, IoT devices, and routers ship native TCP/IP stacks. 5. **Performance:** Less header overhead and fewer processing stages. 6. **Interoperability:** Internet governance (IANA, ICANN) and routing (BGP) are built on TCP/IP. The OSI model, however, remains a **superior teaching and reference tool** because of its cleaner abstraction and clearer separation of session and presentation concerns. --- **(b)** A user sends an email of size **5 KB (5120 bytes)**. TCP adds a 20-byte header, IPv4 adds a 20-byte header, and Ethernet adds a 14-byte header and 4-byte trailer. Calculate: - (i) The total bytes transmitted on the wire. - (ii) The encapsulation efficiency. `[7 Marks]` **Model Solution:** **Given:** - Application payload $S = 5120$ bytes - TCP header $h_4 = 20$ bytes - IPv4 header $h_3 = 20$ bytes - Ethernet header $h_2 = 14$ bytes - Ethernet trailer $t_2 = 4$ bytes **(i) Total bytes on the wire:** $$T_{wire} = S + h_4 + h_3 + h_2 + t_2$$ $$T_{wire} = 5120 + 20 + 20 + 14 + 4$$ $$T_{wire} = 5178 \text{ bytes}$$ **(ii) Encapsulation efficiency:** $$\eta = \frac{S}{T_{wire}} \times 100\%$$ $$\eta = \frac{5120}{5178} \times 100\%$$ $$\eta \approx 98.879\%$$ **Interpretation:** About **1.12% of the bandwidth** is consumed by protocol overhead. For a small packet, this overhead is proportionally larger; for a large packet (e.g., 1500-byte MTU), the efficiency is lower (closer to ~95%) because the fixed-size headers represent a bigger fraction of the smaller frame. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:** > 1. **Confusing OSI and TCP/IP layer names.** OSI Layer 4 is "Transport," TCP/IP Layer 2 is "Internet" — never call it "Network Layer" of TCP/IP (a common error). > 2. **Wrong PDU naming.** A *bit* is for L1, a *frame* is for L2, a *packet* is for L3, a *segment* is for L4 (TCP), a *datagram* is for L4 (UDP) or L3 (IP). Examiners deduct marks for misnaming. > 3. **Confusing MAC and IP addresses.** MAC = L2, flat, 48-bit, local. IP = L3, hierarchical, 32-bit (v4) / 128-bit (v6), global. > 4. **Forgetting the L2 trailer.** Many students compute overhead using only headers and forget the Ethernet FCS trailer (4 bytes). > 5. **Saying "OSI is implemented on the Internet."** It is a reference model only; the Internet uses TCP/IP. > 6. **Skipping the diagram.** KTU examiners allocate at least **1 mark** for the encapsulation diagram in 7-mark sub-questions. Always draw it. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap — High-Density Revision Checklist - The **OSI model** is a **7-layer reference architecture** defined by **ISO/IEC 7498-1**. - The seven layers (top-down): **Application, Presentation, Session, Transport, Network, Data Link, Physical**. - **Mnemonic (top-down):** **A**ll **P**eople **S**eem **T**o **N**eed **D**ata **P**rocessing. - **PDU mapping:** Bit (L1) → Frame (L2) → Packet (L3) → Segment (L4 TCP) / Datagram (L4 UDP). - **Encapsulation** = adding headers at each layer on the **sender side** (L7 → L1). - **Decapsulation** = stripping headers at each layer on the **receiver side** (L1 → L7). - **Peer-to-peer communication:** Layer N on sender logically talks to Layer N on receiver using peer protocol. - **Address hierarchy:** MAC (48-bit, flat, L2) vs IP (32/128-bit, hierarchical, L3). - **Port numbers** are 16-bit identifiers at L4 used for application multiplexing (0–65535). - **Devices operate at specific layers:** Hub (L1), Switch (L2), Router (L3), Firewall (L3/L4/L7), Proxy (L7). - **OSI vs TCP/IP:** OSI = 7 layers (reference); TCP/IP = 4 layers (implementation). - **Security context (PEMRT631):** Encryption conceptually at L6, TLS straddles L6/L7, IPsec operates at L3. - **Efficiency formula:** $\eta = \dfrac{S}{S + h_4 + h_3 + h_2 + t_2} \times 100\%$. - **Total wire bytes:** $T_{wire} = S + \sum h_i + \sum t_i$ over all traversed layers.

topics.title: Layers in OSI Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMRT631 - NETWORK & DATA SECURITY > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Layers in OSI Model # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The **Open Systems Interconnection (OSI) Model** is a conceptual reference framework, standardized by the **International Organization for Standardization (ISO)** in **ISO/IEC 7498-1**, that characterizes and standardizes the communication functions of a telecommunication or computing system without regard to its underlying internal structure and technology. Its goal is the interoperability of diverse communication systems with standard protocols. The OSI Model partitions the abstract process of communication between two endpoints into **7 distinct layers**. Each layer communicates only with its immediate adjacent layers through well-defined **Service Access Points (SAPs)** and serves the layer above it while being served by the layer below it. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PEMRT631 - Module 1):** > Students must be able to **list, define, and explain the functions of all 7 OSI layers**, along with their associated **Protocol Data Units (PDUs)** and **standard protocols**. Expect direct 3-mark definitions and 7–14 mark descriptive questions on layer responsibilities. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Postal Mail System Imagine sending a **registered parcel** from a student in **Trivandrum** to another in **Winterset**: | OSI Layer Equivalent | Real-World Postal Action | |---|---| | **Application Layer** | You (the user) decide to send a letter and write the content | | **Presentation Layer** | You translate your message into a common language (e.g., English) and encrypt it | | **Session Layer** | You establish a unique tracking ID with the post office | | **Transport Layer** | You break a large document into multiple smaller envelopes (segments) and number them | | **Network Layer** | The postman writes the **source and destination PIN codes** (logical addressing) on each envelope | | **Data Link Layer** | The local sorting office adds a **barcode / routing slip** with the local post office address (physical/MAC addressing) | | **Physical Layer** | The actual **truck, plane, or bicycle** that physically carries the parcel | At the receiving end, the process is **reversed** (decapsulation) — each layer "opens" only the part addressed to it and passes the rest upward. > [!NOTE] > **Key Intuition:** Each layer adds its own **header (and sometimes trailer)** to the data it receives from the layer above — this is called **encapsulation**. The reverse process at the receiver is **decapsulation**. ## 1.3 The Seven Layers at a Glance $$\text{7 Layers (Top to Bottom)} = \{ \text{Application, Presentation, Session, Transport, Network, Data Link, Physical} \}$$ **Mnemonic (Top-Down):** **A**ll **P**eople **S**eem **T**o **N**eed **D**ata **P**rocessing. **Mnemonic (Bottom-Up):** **P**lease **D**o **N**ot **T**hrow **S**ausage **P**izza **A**way. --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Layer-by-Layer Functional Breakdown The OSI architecture operates on a strict **hierarchical service model**. A layer **N** entity provides services to a layer **N+1** entity, requesting services from layer **N-1**. Below is the exhaustive KTU-required analysis of each layer. ### Layer 7 — Application Layer - **Function:** Closest to the end-user. Provides network services directly to user applications (browsers, email clients, FTP). - **Protocols:** HTTP, HTTPS, FTP, SMTP, DNS, SNMP, Telnet, SSH. - **PDU:** **Data** (sometimes called APDU). - **Real-World Utility:** Every login screen, web page request, and email is processed here. ### Layer 6 — Presentation Layer - **Function:** Data translation, **syntax and semantic conversion**, **encryption/decryption**, and **compression/decompression**. - **Standards:** ASCII ↔ EBCDIC, JPEG, MPEG, TLS/SSL (encryption), GZIP. - **PDU:** **Data**. - **Real-World Utility:** Ensures a JPG image rendered on Windows is correctly interpreted on macOS. ### Layer 5 — Session Layer - **Function:** Establishes, manages, and terminates **sessions** between two communicating hosts. Handles **dialogue control** (half-duplex / full-duplex) and **synchronization** (checkpoints). - **Protocols:** NetBIOS, RPC, PPTP, SIP. - **PDU:** **Data** (sometimes **SPDU**). - **Real-World Utility:** Video conferencing apps use this layer to maintain continuous dialogue. ### Layer 4 — Transport Layer - **Function:** Provides **end-to-end** communication, **segmentation**, **flow control**, **error recovery**, and **port-based multiplexing**. Guarantees (or does not guarantee) delivery. - **Core Protocols:** - **TCP** (Transmission Control Protocol) — Connection-oriented, reliable. - **UDP** (User Datagram Protocol) — Connectionless, unreliable, fast. - **PDU:** **Segment** (TCP) or **Datagram** (UDP). - **Real-World Utility:** When you open 10 browser tabs + WhatsApp, this layer keeps them separate using **port numbers (0–65535)**. ### Layer 3 — Network Layer - **Function:** **Logical addressing** (IP addresses), **routing**, **path determination**, and **packet forwarding** across multiple networks. - **Core Protocols:** IPv4, IPv6, ICMP, OSPF, BGP, RIP, IPsec. - **PDU:** **Packet**. - **Real-World Utility:** Routers operate here. The Internet works because of Layer 3. ### Layer 2 — Data Link Layer - **Function:** **Node-to-node** data transfer, **physical (MAC) addressing**, **error detection/correction (not recovery)**, and access control to the shared medium. - **Sub-layers:** - **LLC (Logical Link Control)** — upper sub-layer, interfaces with Network Layer. - **MAC (Medium Access Control)** — lower sub-layer, controls hardware addressing. - **Protocols:** Ethernet (IEEE 802.3), Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11), PPP, HDLC, ARP. - **PDU:** **Frame**. - **Real-World Utility:** Switches operate here using MAC addresses like `00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E`. ### Layer 1 — Physical Layer - **Function:** Transmits **raw bits** over a physical medium. Defines **electrical, mechanical, procedural, and functional** specifications (voltage levels, connector types, bit rate, topology). - **Standards:** Ethernet cables (Cat5e, Cat6), fiber optics, RS-232, Bluetooth physical layer. - **PDU:** **Bits**. - **Real-World Utility:** Hubs, repeaters, network cables, and wireless radio frequencies. ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet & PDU Reference Table | Layer # | Layer Name | PDU Name | Addressing Used | Key Protocols | Device Example | |:---:|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---| | 7 | Application | Data | Message / URI | HTTP, FTP, SMTP | Gateway / Proxy | | 6 | Presentation | Data | — | SSL/TLS, JPEG | — | | 5 | Session | Data / SPDU | Session ID | RPC, NetBIOS | — | | 4 | Transport | Segment / Datagram | Port Numbers (0–65535) | TCP, UDP | Firewall | | 3 | Network | Packet | IP Address (Logical) | IP, ICMP, BGP | Router | | 2 | Data Link | Frame | MAC Address (Physical) | Ethernet, Wi-Fi | Switch / Bridge | | 1 | Physical | Bits | None (signals) | USB, DSL, 802.3 | Hub / Repeater / Cable | ## 2.3 Data Unit Size Calculation (Useful for Numericals) The **maximum theoretical efficiency** of a transmission at Layer 2 can be expressed as: $$\eta = \frac{L_{payload}}{L_{payload} + L_{header} + L_{trailer}} \times 100\%$$ Where: - $L_{payload}$ = size of the original data (bytes) - $L_{header}$ = size of the header added by the layer (bytes) - $L_{trailer}$ = size of the trailer (bytes, if any) > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Real-World Utility:** The OSI model is the **blueprint** for modern network design. **TCP/IP (Internet Protocol Suite)** is the practical implementation that maps directly onto OSI: TCP/IP has 4 layers, but its functionality aligns with all 7 OSI layers. Studying OSI helps engineers debug networks layer-by-layer (a process called the **OSI Troubleshooting Model**). --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Encapsulated Frame Size at Layer 2 This is a frequently tested numerical in KTU. Given an application-layer message of size $M$ bytes, we derive the total size of the eventual Layer 1 bit stream. ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1: Application to Transport (Segmentation)** The Transport Layer (TCP) splits the message $M$ into segments.
