# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define a network application. With a neat diagram, explain the client–server architecture used in network applications.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks, 1.5 pages):**
>
> **Definition (1 mark)**: A *network application* is a distributed application whose components execute on different end-systems and communicate over a computer network through a defined application-layer protocol. The application software runs only at the **end-systems** (hosts); intermediate devices (routers, switches) operate only at the lower layers.
>
> **Client–Server Architecture (2 marks)**:
> - The system is partitioned into **clients** (which initiate requests) and a **server** (which provides a service in response).
> - The server has a **fixed, well-known IP address** and is **always on**, listening on a designated **port** (e.g., port 80 for HTTP).
> - Clients are assigned **dynamic IP addresses** by ISPs, and they contact the server when the user invokes the service.
> - Communication is **request–reply**: client sends a request, server processes it and returns a response.
> - **Examples**: Web (HTTP), Email (SMTP), Domain Name Service (DNS), File Transfer (FTP).
> - **Diagram (1 mark)**:
>
> ```
> Client A ──┐          ┌── Client C
>            │          │
> Client B ──┼──► Server ◄── Client D
>            │ (Fixed IP,
>            │  Port 80)
>            └── Reply ──┘
> ```
>
> **Key Properties (extra credit)**: 
> - **Single point of failure** — server crash halts all clients.
> - **Scalability** — server is a bottleneck; data centers add redundancy.
> - **Administrative control** — server operator controls data and access.

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between connection-oriented (TCP) and connectionless (UDP) service. Give two examples of applications using each.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks, tabulated)**:
>
> | Parameter | **TCP (Connection-Oriented)** | **UDP (Connectionless)** |
> |---|---|---|
> | Setup | 3-way handshake before data | No setup; send immediately |
> | Reliability | ACKs, retransmission, ordering | Best-effort; no recovery |
> | Header size | 20 bytes (min) | 8 bytes |
> | Flow/Congestion | Yes (window, slow-start) | No |
> | Use cases | HTTP, FTP, SMTP, SSH, Telnet | DNS, VoIP, video streaming, gaming, SNMP |
>
> **Examples (1 mark)**:
> - **TCP apps**: HTTP web browsing, FTP file transfer, SSH remote login.
> - **UDP apps**: DNS name resolution, online multiplayer gaming.
>
> **Conclusion (½ mark)**: TCP trades latency for reliability; UDP trades reliability for speed.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout**
> - **Never confuse the OSI 7-layer model with the TCP/IP 4-layer model** in your diagrams. KTU examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this.
> - **Always state transport protocol alongside port number** ("port 80, TCP") — not just "port 80".
> - **In P2P problems, do not forget the $\dfrac{NF}{u_s + \sum u_i}$ term** — students frequently drop this third lower bound, losing 4 marks.
> - **For HTTP response time, the default formula is for persistent *non-pipelined* connections**. The 2-RTT version is *pipelined*. Read the question carefully.
> - **DNS uses port 53 over UDP** in typical operation, *not* TCP. Students who write "TCP" lose a mark.

### Question A (14 Marks): Application Architecture & P2P Distribution

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, modified for 2024 scheme]**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Compare the **client–server** and **peer-to-peer** architectures for network applications. Discuss the **advantages and limitations** of each. With a neat diagram, explain the **hybrid architecture** used in modern applications like WhatsApp and Napster.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* A file of size **F = 4 Gbits** is to be distributed to **N = 100 peers**. The server has upload rate **u_s = 50 Mbps**. Each peer has download rate **d_i = 10 Mbps** (uniform) and upload rate **u_i = 2 Mbps** (uniform). Compute:
1. The minimum distribution time for **client–server** architecture.
2. The minimum distribution time for **P2P** architecture.
3. Comment on the result — which is better and why?

> **Solution to A(a) — 7 marks**:
>
> **Comparison Table (3 marks)**:
>
> | Feature | **Client–Server** | **Peer-to-Peer** |
> |---|---|---|
> | Server requirement | Always-on dedicated server | No dedicated server |
> | Server IP | Fixed, well-known | None |
> | Scalability | Limited by server capacity | **Self-scaling** (capacity grows with peers) |
> | Single Point of Failure | **Yes** (server) | **No** (distributed) |
> | Administration | Centralized | Decentralized |
> | Security control | Easier | Harder |
> | Examples | Web, Email, DNS | BitTorrent, Blockchain |
>
> **Hybrid Architecture (2 marks)**: Hybrid uses a **central server for coordination** (e.g., user discovery, metadata, authentication) but peers exchange data **directly** with each other. *Napster* used a central directory server to locate peers holding a file, then peers transferred the file P2P. *WhatsApp* uses central servers for message relay (especially when receivers are offline) but **P2P** for voice/video calls to reduce latency and server load.
>
> ```
> Central Server
> (user discovery,
>  authentication)
>       ▲
>       │
>   ┌───┴───┐
>   │       │
> Peer A ◄─► Peer B
> (direct P2P data)
> ```
>
> **Advantages and Limitations (2 marks)**:
> - *Client–Server advantages*: centralized control, easier security, predictable performance. *Limitations*: scalability bottleneck, single point of failure, expensive.
> - *P2P advantages*: self-scaling, no infrastructure cost, fault-tolerant. *Limitations*: hard to secure, no QoS guarantee, ISP traffic shaping.
>
> **Solution to A(b) — 7 marks**:
>
> **Given**: $F = 4 \text{ Gbits} = 4 \times 10^9$ bits, $N = 100$, $u_s = 50 \text{ Mbps} = 50 \times 10^6$ bps, $d_i = 10$ Mbps, $u_i = 2$ Mbps.
>
> **1. Client–Server Distribution Time (3 marks)**:
>
> Server-upload bound:
> $$\begin{aligned}
> T_1 &= \frac{NF}{u_s} = \frac{100 \times 4 \times 10^9}{50 \times 10^6} = \frac{4 \times 10^{11}}{5 \times 10^7} = 8000 \text{ s}
> \end{aligned}$$
>
> Slowest-downloader bound:
> $$\begin{aligned}
> T_2 &= \frac{F}{\min_i d_i} = \frac{4 \times 10^9}{10 \times 10^6} = 400 \text{ s}
> \end{aligned}$$
>
> $$\begin{aligned}
> T_{CS} &= \max\{8000, 400\} = 8000 \text{ s}
> \end{aligned}$$
>
> **2. P2P Distribution Time (3 marks)**:
>
> Add the third bound — total upload capacity:
> $$\begin{aligned}
> T_3 &= \frac{NF}{u_s + \sum u_i} = \frac{100 \times 4 \times 10^9}{50 \times 10^6 + 100 \times 2 \times 10^6} \\
> &= \frac{4 \times 10^{11}}{(50 + 200) \times 10^6} = \frac{4 \times 10^{11}}{2.5 \times 10^8} = 1600 \text{ s}
> \end{aligned}$$
>
> $$\begin{aligned}
> T_{P2P} &= \max\{400, 400, 1600\} = 1600 \text{ s}
> \end{aligned}$$
>
> **3. Comment (1 mark)**:
>
> P2P is **5 times faster** (1600 s vs 8000 s). The improvement comes from the **100 peer uploads of 2 Mbps** which add 200 Mbps to the system's aggregate capacity, offloading the server. P2P wins as $N$ grows large.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): Transport Service Requirements & HTTP Performance

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023, modified for 2024 scheme]**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Explain the **four transport service requirements** of network applications: **Data Loss, Throughput, Timing, and Security**. Classify the following apps — *file transfer, video conferencing, web browsing, VoIP, email, online gaming* — based on these requirements, justifying TCP vs UDP for each.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* A web page consists of **one HTML file** and **15 image objects** referenced from it. The HTML file is 50 kbits; each image is 200 kbits. The link rate is **R = 1 Mbps** and one-way propagation delay is **d_prop = 25 ms**. Compute the total response time for:
1. **Non-persistent HTTP** with no parallel connections.
2. **Persistent HTTP with pipelining**.
3. By what factor does pipelining improve the response?

> **Solution to B(a) — 7 marks**:
>
> **The Four Service Requirements (2 marks)**:
> 1. **Data Loss** — Does the app tolerate lost data? File transfer does not; video streaming does.
> 2. **Throughput** — Does the app need a minimum bandwidth? Video streaming needs several Mbps; email is elastic.
> 3. **Timing** — Does the app need low delay? VoIP and gaming need < 150 ms; email does not.
> 4. **Security** — Does the app need confidentiality, integrity, authentication? All financial apps do.
>
> **Classification Table (4 marks)**:
>
> | Application | Loss | Throughput | Timing | Security | Transport |
> |---|---|---|---|---|---|
> | File transfer (FTP) | **No loss** | Elastic | Not critical | Optional | **TCP** |
> | Web browsing (HTTP) | **No loss** | Elastic | Not critical | Optional | **TCP** |
> | Email (SMTP/POP3) | **No loss** | Elastic | Not critical | Optional | **TCP** |
> | Video conferencing | Tolerant | ≥ 1 Mbps | **< 150 ms** | Often required | **UDP (RTP/SRTP)** |
> | VoIP | Tolerant | ≥ 32 kbps | **< 150 ms** | Often required | **UDP (RTP/SRTP)** |
> | Online gaming | Tolerant | ≥ 50 kbps | **< 50 ms** | Optional | **UDP** |
>
> **Justification Logic (1 mark)**: Apps that need **reliability** → TCP. Apps that need **low latency** and tolerate loss → UDP. If both reliability *and* low latency are needed, application-layer reliability (e.g., FEC, ARQ) is added to UDP.
>
> **Solution to B(b) — 7 marks**:
>
> **Given**:
> - 1 HTML file of size $F_h = 50$ kbits = $50 \times 10^3$ bits
> - 15 images of size $F_i = 200$ kbits each
> - Total objects $k = 1 + 15 = 16$
> - Link rate $R = 1$ Mbps = $10^6$ bps
> - $d_{prop} = 25$ ms, so $RTT = 2 \times 25 = 50$ ms = $0.05$ s
> - Total data: $F_{total} = 50 \times 10^3 + 15 \times 200 \times 10^3 = 3{,}050{,}000$ bits = $3.05$ Mbits
>
> **1. Non-Persistent HTTP, Sequential (3 marks)**:
>
> Using the formula $t_{resp} = 2k \cdot RTT + \dfrac{F_{total}}{R}$ (where $F_{total}$ is the *sum* of all object sizes):
> $$\begin{aligned}
> t_{resp}^{(a)} &= 2 \times 16 \times 0.05 + \frac{3{,}050{,}000}{1{,}000{,}000} \\
> &= 1.6 + 3.05 \\
> &= 4.65 \text{ s}
> \end{aligned}$$
>
> **2. Persistent HTTP with Pipelining (3 marks)**:
>
> Using the formula $t_{resp} = 2 \cdot RTT + \dfrac{F_{total}}{R}$:
> $$\begin{aligned}
> t_{resp}^{(b)} &= 2 \times 0.05 + \frac{3{,}050{,}000}{1{,}000{,}000} \\
> &= 0.1 + 3.05 \\
> &= 3.15 \text{ s}
> \end{aligned}$$
>
> **3. Improvement Factor (1 mark)**:
> $$\begin{aligned}
> \text{Improvement} &= \frac{4.65}{3.15} \approx 1.48 \times
> \end{aligned}$$
>
> Pipelining improves response time by a factor of **1.48** (a **32% reduction** in total response time). The saving is purely on the RTT component (1.5 s saved out of 1.6 s of RTT overhead).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print this before the exam)**
>
> **Architecture**
> - *Client–Server*: fixed-IP server, dynamic clients, always-on, SPOF risk.
> - *Peer-to-Peer*: no server, self-scalable, used by BitTorrent, blockchain.
> - *Hybrid*: server for control + peers for data (Napster, WhatsApp).
>
> **Process Communication**
> - Process identified by **IP + Port**.
> - **Socket** = software interface to the transport layer.
> - Connection identified by **5-tuple**: $\{IP_A, Port_A, IP_B, Port_B, Protocol\}$.
>
> **Transport Choice**
> - **TCP** = reliable, ordered, connection-oriented, congestion control. Used by HTTP, FTP, SMTP, SSH.
> - **UDP** = unreliable, unordered, connectionless, no congestion control. Used by DNS, VoIP, video, gaming.
> - HTTPS ≠ new transport — it is HTTP over **TLS over TCP** (or **DTLS over UDP** for QUIC/HTTP-3).
>
> **Service Requirements**
> - Four axes: *Data Loss*, *Throughput*, *Timing*, *Security*.
> - Map: Loss-intolerant + reliability → TCP. Latency-critical + loss-tolerant → UDP.
>
> **Key Formulas (must memorize)**
> - $T_{CS} \geq \max\!\left\{\dfrac{NF}{u_s},\ \dfrac{F}{\min_i d_i}\right\}$
> - $T_{P2P} \geq \max\!\left\{\dfrac{F}{u_s},\ \dfrac{F}{\min_i d_i},\ \dfrac{NF}{u_s + \sum u_i}\right\}$
> - HTTP non-persistent: $t = 2k \cdot RTT + kF/R$
> - HTTP persistent pipelined: $t = 2 \cdot RTT + kF/R$
> - $BDP = R \times RTT$
> - $d_{trans} = L/R$, $d_{prop} = D/s$
>
> **Mandatory Port Numbers** (memorize in groups of 3)
> - 20, 21 = FTP. **22 = SSH**. **23 = Telnet**. 25 = SMTP. **53 = DNS**. **80 = HTTP**. **443 = HTTPS**. 110 = POP3. 143 = IMAP.
> - 67/68 = DHCP. 161/162 = SNMP.
>
> **"Smart Edges, Dumb Network"**
> - The Application Layer exists **only at end-systems**. Routers in the core do not understand HTTP, SMTP, DNS, or any Layer-7 protocol.
>
> **Security Hooks (Module 1 → Module 2 Bridge)**
> - Application Layer is the **prime target** for phishing, XSS, SQL injection, MITM, DNS spoofing.
> - Client–Server is **easier to secure** (centralized control) but has **DDoS vulnerability**.
> - P2P is **harder to secure** (decentralized) but **resistant to single-point takedown**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Tip**: In the KTU 2024 scheme, Module 1 carries roughly **15–20% of the total marks**. Always link application-layer discussion to **security implications** — that is the unique flavour of the **PEMRT631 Network & Data Security** paper.

topics.title: Network Application Architectures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT631 - NETWORK & DATA SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Network Application Architectures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)**
> A **Network Application Architecture** is the structural blueprint that dictates how application software is logically organized across end systems and how it communicates over the network. It defines the fixed roles played by hosts (client vs. server vs. peer), the communication paradigm (sockets, message exchange), and the transport-service requirements (reliable data transfer, throughput, timing, security) required for the application to function correctly across an internetwork.

The two predominant architectural paradigms studied at the KTU level are:
1. **Client-Server Architecture**
2. **Peer-to-Peer (P2P) Architecture**
3. **Hybrid Architecture** (a practical blend of both)

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (The Restaurant Model)

Imagine a busy food court inside a Kerala shopping mall:

- **Client-Server Architecture** is exactly like a traditional *Thali Restaurant*. There is one kitchen (the **server**) and many dining tables (the **clients**). The kitchen prepares whatever the tables order. No table ever cooks food for another table — communication is always *directed toward the kitchen*. If the kitchen catches fire, the entire restaurant collapses. If one table leaves, nothing changes.

- **Peer-to-Peer (P2P) Architecture** is like a *Potluck Family Dinner*. Every guest brings a dish to share (acting as both supplier and consumer). There is no central kitchen. Each peer is simultaneously a client *and* a server. Communication is symmetric and decentralized.

- **Hybrid Architecture** is like a *Co-Operative Food Court* — there is a central *index/coordination desk* (like the registrar in BitTorrent), but actual file exchange happens directly between peers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight — Three Pillars of Application Architecture:**
> 1. **Process Addressing** — How do we identify the two communicating end processes?
>    - **IP Address** (32-bit for IPv4, 128-bit for IPv6) identifies the **host**.
>    - **Port Number** (16-bit, range **0 to 65535**) identifies the **process** on that host.
> 2. **Transport Service Demands** — What does the application need from the transport layer?
>    - Data integrity (loss tolerance), throughput, timing (delay tolerance), security.
> 3. **Application-Layer Protocol** — The standardized set of rules (e.g., HTTP, SMTP, FTP, DNS, BitTorrent) governing request/response message formats and semantics.

### Key Terminology Board (Quick Reference)

| Term | Meaning | KTU Significance |
|------|---------|------------------|
| **Process** | A running program on an end system | Communicating units of an application |
| **Socket** | The software interface (API) between application & transport layer | The doorway through which data exits/enters a process |
| **Client Process** | Initiates communication | Always opens the socket first |
| **Server Process** | Waits to be contacted | Always has a well-known/open port |
| **API** | Application Programming Interface | Used by application to invoke transport services |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metrics to Memorize for KTU Boards:**
> - Maximum Port Number = **2^16 − 1 = 65535**
> - Total usable ephemeral ports (Linux) ≈ **32768 to 60999** (about **28231** ports)
> - Reserved/well-known ports range: **0 to 1023**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Client-Server Architecture — Operational Breakdown

The client-server model is the **dominant paradigm** for network applications. Its operational logic can be broken down as follows:

- **Step 1 — Server Bootstrap:** The server process is always-on. It must be started *before* any client attempts communication. It binds to a **well-known port number** (e.g., HTTP uses port 80, HTTPS uses 443, SSH uses 22).
- **Step 2 — Passive Open:** The server calls the `listen()` system call (or equivalent). It is now in a *passive listening state*, ready to accept inbound requests.
- **Step 3 — Client Initiation:** A client process on a remote host calls the `connect()` system call, specifying the server's IP and the well-known port.
- **Step 4 — Active Open & Handshake:** A TCP three-way handshake (SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK) occurs between client and server at the transport layer.
- **Step 5 — Data Exchange:** Communication follows an **asymmetric request-response pattern** — the client always sends the request, and the server always replies.
- **Step 6 — Iterative vs. Concurrent Servers:**
  - *Iterative Server*: Handles one client at a time. Subsequent clients wait in a queue. Simple to implement, poor scalability.
  - *Concurrent Server*: Uses `fork()` (Unix/Linux) or `CreateThread()` (Windows) to spawn a child process/thread for every client. Highly scalable, used by production systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall Avoidance:** Always clearly distinguish between the *hardware* (data-center server, desktop, mobile) and the *software* (client process, server process). A single physical machine can host both client and server processes simultaneously.

### 2.2 Peer-to-Peer (P2P) Architecture — Operational Breakdown

In P2P, there is **no dedicated server**. The architecture exploits direct communication between arbitrary end systems called *peers*.

- **Step 1 — Self-Organization:** Peers discover each other dynamically, often using a *distributed hash table (DHT)*, a *centralized tracker* (in hybrid P2P), or *gossip protocols*.
- **Step 2 — Symmetric Roles:** Every peer acts simultaneously as a client (when downloading a chunk) and as a server (when uploading a chunk to another peer).
- **Step 3 — Resource Pooling:** The cumulative upload bandwidth and storage of all peers is the application's effective serving capacity. Hence, P2P scales with the user base — adding peers *adds* capacity.
- **Step 4 — Data Distribution:** Files are split into *chunks* (typically 256 KB to 4 MB). Each peer downloads chunks from multiple peers in parallel — the **tit-for-tat** mechanism (BitTorrent) incentivizes peers that upload as much as they download.

**Advantages of P2P:**
- **High Scalability:** Capacity grows with population.
- **Cost Efficiency:** No expensive server farm required.
- **Robustness:** Self-healing — failure of one peer does not collapse the system.

**Disadvantages of P2P:**
- **Security Risk:** Every peer can inject malicious chunks; needs *hash verification*.
- **Unpredictable QoS:** Performance depends on peer churn (peers joining/leaving).
- **ISP Pressure:** Heavy uplink traffic may trigger throttling.

### 2.3 Hybrid Architecture — Operational Breakdown

A practical compromise used in **modern real-world systems** like Skype, BitTorrent (with trackers), and WhatsApp (signal servers + peer messages).

- A **central server** handles **only lightweight coordination tasks** — user authentication, peer discovery, presence (online/offline status), relay under NAT conditions.
- The **bulk data transfer** (file transfer, voice/video packets) happens **directly between peers**.
- This mitigates the scalability bottleneck of pure client-server *and* the discovery problem of pure P2P.

### 2.4 Process Addressing & Socket Programming

For any two processes to communicate across the internet, four identifiers are required:

$$
\text{Process Identifier Tuple} = (\text{IP}_{\text{src}}, \text{Port}_{\text{src}},\ \text{IP}_{\text{dst}}, \text{Port}_{\text{dst}})
$$

- The **socket** on each host is uniquely identified by the local IP + local port.
- A connection between two hosts is uniquely identified by the **full 4-tuple** above.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Variables & Units |
|---|---------|----------------|-------------------|
| 1 | Port Number Range | $0 \le P \le 2^{16}-1 = 65535$ | $P$ = Port Number, integer |
| 2 | Well-Known Ports | $0 \le P \le 1023$ | Reserved for system/root services |
| 3 | Registered Ports | $1024 \le P \le 49151$ | Assigned by IANA to applications |
| 4 | Dynamic/Ephemeral Ports | $49152 \le P \le 65535$ (Linux uses $32768$–$60999$) | Assigned by OS to client sockets |
| 5 | Process Tuple | $T = (IP_s, Port_s, IP_c, Port_c)$ | Unique per network connection |
| 6 | Server Concurrent Capacity (theoretical) | $N_c = M \times P_{tcp}$ | $M$ = Memory per thread, $P_{tcp}$ = Max open TCP sockets |
| 7 | P2P Effective Upload Bandwidth | $B_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} u_i$ | $N$ = active peers, $u_i$ = upload rate of peer $i$ (Mbps) |
| 8 | P2P Minimum Distribution Time | $T_{min} = \max \left\{ \dfrac{F}{u_s},\ \dfrac{F}{d_{min}},\ \dfrac{NF}{u_s + \sum u_i} \right\}$ | $F$ = file size, $u_s$ = server upload, $d_{min}$ = slowest peer download |
| 9 | Application Throughput Demand | $R_{req} \le R_{transport}$ | $R$ in bits/second (bps) |
| 10 | Round-Trip Time (RTT) | $RTT = 2 \times T_{prop} + \dfrac{L}{R}$ | $T_{prop}$ = propagation delay (s), $L$ = packet length (bits), $R$ = link rate (bps) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formula 8 (P2P Minimum Distribution Time)** is a **favourite KTU exam question**. The three terms inside the `max{}` represent:
> 1. $\dfrac{F}{u_s}$ — server alone cannot distribute faster than this.
> 2. $\dfrac{F}{d_{min}}$ — the slowest peer cannot receive faster than this.
> 3. $\dfrac{NF}{u_s + \sum u_i}$ — total data ($N \times F$) must flow through combined uplink capacity.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Architecture | Real-World Deployment | Engineering Insight |
|--------------|----------------------|---------------------|
| Client-Server | Web (HTTP), Email (SMTP/IMAP), Banking, DNS | Predictable SLA, easier to secure centrally |
| Pure P2P | BitTorrent (older), IPFS | Maximum scalability, minimum cost |
| Hybrid | WhatsApp, Signal, Skype, modern BitTorrent | Best engineering trade-off for production |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Minimum Distribution Time in Client-Server

**Problem Setup:** A server with upload rate $u_s$ must distribute a file of size $F$ to $N$ peers. Each peer $i$ has a download rate $d_i$.

**Step-by-Step Logical Deduction:**

**Step 1 — Identify the bottleneck.**
The server must sequentially upload the file to each peer because the server's uplink is the *only* source. Even if peer $i$ has infinite download bandwidth, the server can only push one peer's stream at a time at rate $u_s$.

**Step 2 — Total data the server must transmit.**
To give the full file $F$ to each of $N$ peers, the server must upload $N \cdot F$ bits in total.

$$
\text{Total Bits Uploaded} = N \cdot F
$$

**Step 3 — Compute server-limited time.**
Using the basic rate-time-quantity relation $\text{Time} = \dfrac{\text{Quantity}}{\text{Rate}}$:

$$
T_{server} = \frac{N \cdot F}{u_s}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute per-peer download time.**
Peer $i$ can only download at rate $d_i$. The peer receives $F$ bits.

$$
T_{peer,i} = \frac{F}{d_i}
$$

**Step 5 — Overall distribution time is the slowest peer AND the server's load.**
The whole file distribution is *complete* only when:
- The server has finished uploading to all peers, AND
- The slowest peer has finished downloading.

Therefore, the minimum distribution time is the *maximum* of the two:

$$
T_{cs} = \max \left\{ \frac{N F}{u_s},\ \max_{i}\frac{F}{d_i} \right\}
$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Minimum Distribution Time in Pure P2P

**Problem Setup:** A seed peer has upload rate $u_s$. Each peer $i$ has upload rate $u_i$ and download rate $d_i$.

**Step 1 — Server contribution to aggregate bandwidth.**
Once the seed has uploaded a copy to a peer, that peer's own uplink also contributes to the aggregate pool. Total aggregate upload capacity of the system is:

$$
U_{aggregate} = u_s + \sum_{i=1}^{N} u_i
$$

**Step 2 — Aggregate bandwidth-limited time.**
Total bits to be moved across the swarm = $N \cdot F$. Using the same rate relation:

$$
T_{aggregate} = \frac{N \cdot F}{u_s + \sum_{i=1}^{N} u_i}
$$

**Step 3 — Slowest peer constraint (unchanged from client-server).**

$$
T_{peer,max} = \max_i \frac{F}{d_i}
$$

**Step 4 — Compose the final expression.**

$$
\boxed{\,T_{p2p} = \max \left\{ \frac{F}{u_s},\ \frac{F}{d_{min}},\ \frac{N F}{u_s + \sum_{i=1}^{N} u_i} \right\}\,}
$$

The first term $F/u_s$ ensures the seed has at least one full copy in the swarm.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Performance

Let us compare with concrete numbers. Assume $F = 1$ GB, $N = 10$ peers, server upload $u_s = 1$ Mbps, each peer has $u_i = 0.5$ Mbps upload and $d_i = 1$ Mbps download.

**First compute $F$ in megabits:** $F = 1 \text{ GB} = 8 \text{ Gb} = 8000 \text{ Mb}$.

**Client-Server case:**

$$
T_{cs} = \max \left\{ \frac{10 \times 8000}{1},\ \frac{8000}{1} \right\} = \max\{80000,\ 8000\} = 80000 \text{ s}
$$

**Pure P2P case:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Term 1} &= \frac{8000}{1} = 8000 \text{ s} \\
\text{Term 2} &= \frac{8000}{1} = 8000 \text{ s} \\
\text{Term 3} &= \frac{10 \times 8000}{1 + 10 \times 0.5} = \frac{80000}{6} \approx 13333 \text{ s} \\
T_{p2p} &= \max\{8000,\ 8000,\ 13333\} = 13333 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Speedup Factor:**

$$
\text{Speedup} = \frac{T_{cs}}{T_{p2p}} = \frac{80000}{13333} \approx 6.0
$$

### 3.4 Comparison Matrix

| Criterion | Client-Server | Peer-to-Peer | Hybrid |
|-----------|---------------|--------------|--------|
| Server requirement | Always-on, powerful | Optional seed | Lightweight coordinator |
| Scalability | Limited by server capacity | Scales with users | Excellent |
| Security control | Easy (centralized) | Difficult | Moderate |
| Data confidentiality | Strong | Weak (each peer sees chunks) | Moderate |
| Examples | HTTP Web, Email, Banking | IPFS, old BitTorrent | WhatsApp, Skype, modern BitTorrent |
| Single point of failure | Yes | No | Yes (coordinator) |
| Implementation complexity | Low | High | Moderate |
| Suited for | Sensitive, regulated workloads | Bulk file distribution | Real-time messaging & media |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Client-Server Architecture (Functional Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CLIENTS["CLIENT ZONE"]
        C1["CLIENT 1\nBrowser / Mobile App"]
        C2["CLIENT 2\nBrowser / Mobile App"]
        C3["CLIENT N\nBrowser / Mobile App"]
    end
    subgraph SERVER["SERVER ZONE - DATA CENTER"]
        S1["SERVER PROCESS\nalways-on daemon"]
        S2["WELL-KNOWN PORT\n80 / 443 / 25"]
        S3["REQUEST QUEUE\nconcurrent handler"]
    end
    NET["INTERNET / CLOUD\nTCP / UDP transport"]
    C1 --> NET
    C2 --> NET
    C3 --> NET
    NET --> S2
    S2 --> S1
    S1 --> S3
    S3 --> NET
    NET --> C1
    NET --> C2
    NET --> C3
```

### 4.2 Peer-to-Peer Architecture (Functional Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SWARM["PEER SWARM - DECENTRALIZED MESH"]
        P1["PEER A\nhas chunk 1 and 3"]
        P2["PEER B\nhas chunk 2 and 1"]
        P3["PEER C\nhas chunk 3 and 2"]
        P4["PEER D\nhas chunk 1 and 2"]
    end
    subgraph DHT["DISCOVERY LAYER"]
        T1["DHT OR TRACKER\npeer registry"]
    end
    P1 <--> P2
    P2 <--> P3
    P3 <--> P4
    P4 <--> P1
    P1 <--> P3
    P2 <--> P4
    P1 -.lookup.-> T1
    P2 -.lookup.-> T1
    P3 -.lookup.-> T1
    P4 -.lookup.-> T1
```

### 4.3 Hybrid Architecture (Functional Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph COORD["CENTRAL COORDINATOR SERVER"]
        AUTH["AUTHENTICATION\nlogin and identity"]
        DIR["PEER DIRECTORY\nwho is online and IP"]
        RELAY["RELAY NODE\nfor NAT traversal"]
    end
    subgraph PEERS["PEER POPULATION"]
        UA["USER DEVICE A\nclient plus mini-server"]
        UB["USER DEVICE B\nclient plus mini-server"]
        UC["USER DEVICE C\nclient plus mini-server"]
    end
    UA <--> UB
    UB <--> UC
    UA <--> UC
    UA -. signup and presence .-> AUTH
    UB -. signup and presence .-> AUTH
    UC -. signup and presence .-> AUTH
    UA -. look up peers .-> DIR
    UB -. look up peers .-> DIR
    UC -. look up peers .-> DIR
    UA -. relay if direct fails .-> RELAY
    UB -. relay if direct fails .-> RELAY
    UC -. relay if direct fails .-> RELAY
```

### 4.4 Socket Communication Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant CS as Client Socket
    participant TS as TCP/IP Transport
    participant SS as Server Socket
    CS->>TS: connect request to IP and Port
    TS->>SS: SYN packet
    SS->>TS: SYN plus ACK packet
    TS->>CS: ACK packet
    Note over CS,SS: Three-way handshake complete
    CS->>TS: HTTP GET request
    TS->>SS: deliver bytes to listening socket
    SS->>TS: HTTP 200 OK with payload
    TS->>CS: deliver bytes to client socket
    CS->>TS: close request
    TS->>SS: FIN packet
    SS->>TS: FIN plus ACK
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between Client-Server and Peer-to-Peer architectures. State two advantages of each.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Client-Server | Peer-to-Peer |
|--------|---------------|--------------|
| Server role | Always-on dedicated host | No dedicated server; peers are both client and server |
| Communication | Asymmetric request-response | Symmetric, direct peer-to-peer |
| Scalability | Limited by server capacity | Scales with peer count |
| Data confidentiality | Easier to enforce | Difficult to enforce |

*Advantages of Client-Server:* Centralized security control; predictable quality of service.
*Advantages of P2P:* High scalability; no expensive infrastructure required.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
*What is a socket? Why is the port number 16 bits long?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A **socket** is the software endpoint of a network connection — the API through which an application hands data to the transport layer (or receives data from it). It is identified locally by the pair `(IP address, Port number)`.
The port number is **16 bits** because this gives a range of $0$ to $2^{16} - 1 = 65535$, which is sufficient to uniquely identify all processes running on a host. The IANA (Internet Assigned Numbers Authority) partitions these 65536 ports into three bands: well-known (0–1023), registered (1024–49151), and dynamic/ephemeral (49152–65535).

---

### Part B Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**
*Q. A1. (a) Explain the Client-Server architecture in detail with a neat diagram. Discuss iterative and concurrent server models. (7 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

A **Client-Server architecture** is a distributed application structure that partitions tasks between *service providers* (servers) and *service requesters* (clients). The server is an **always-on host** with a **permanent IP address** that listens on a **well-known port number**. Clients may have *dynamic* IP addresses, communicate intermittently, and initiate contact with the server.

**Key Properties:**
1. **Server is always reachable** — must be started before clients connect.
2. **Clients are intermittently connected** — may disconnect and reconnect.
3. **Client does not communicate directly with other clients** — the server is the central hub.
4. **Well-known ports** — server binds to a standardized port (e.g., HTTP = 80).

**Iterative Server Model:**
- Handles **one client at a time**.
- A new request waits in a queue until the current request is fully processed.
- *Advantages:* Simple to implement, low memory footprint.
- *Disadvantages:* Long blocking; unsuitable for modern multi-user workloads.

**Concurrent Server Model:**
- Spawns a **new process or thread** for every incoming client connection (using `fork()` or `CreateThread()`).
- Multiple clients are served in parallel.
- *Advantages:* High throughput, no client has to wait.
- *Disadvantages:* Higher memory use, requires synchronization (mutexes/semaphores) for shared state.

*Diagram:* Refer to the Client-Server flowchart in Section 4.1 of these notes.

---

*(b) With suitable examples, explain the Hybrid Architecture used in modern messaging applications. Why is it preferred over pure P2P? (7 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** A **Hybrid Architecture** is a deployment model in which a *lightweight central coordinator* is combined with *direct peer-to-peer data exchange*.

**Components:**
1. **Central Coordinator Server** — performs user authentication, maintains user presence (online/offline), stores contact lists, and helps peers locate each other.
2. **Peers** — perform the *bulk* data exchange (messages, voice, video, file transfers) directly with each other once they have discovered each other.

**Real-World Examples:**
- **WhatsApp:** Signal Protocol servers handle *registration and key exchange*; messages travel peer-to-peer (or via relay if both peers are behind NAT).
- **Skype (modern):** Central servers handle login and authentication; calls flow peer-to-peer.
- **BitTorrent:** Trackers are light central servers that maintain peer lists; file chunks flow peer-to-peer.

**Why Preferred Over Pure P2P:**
1. **NAT Traversal:** Most mobile and home users are behind NAT/firewall; the central relay solves the *reachability problem*.
2. **Authentication & Anti-Spoofing:** Without a central authority, any peer can impersonate another. The coordinator issues verifiable identities.
3. **Discovery:** Searching for a peer in a fully decentralized network is expensive (DHT lookup); a directory service is fast.
4. **Compliance & Lawful Intercept:** A central coordinator allows regulatory compliance, which is impossible in pure P2P.

---

### Part B Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**
*Q. B1. (a) Explain the Peer-to-Peer (P2P) architecture. Discuss its advantages and disadvantages. (7 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

In a **Peer-to-Peer architecture**, every participating host (called a *peer*) acts simultaneously as a **client** and a **server**. There is no dedicated always-on central server. Peers form a logical overlay network and exchange data directly.

**Discovery Mechanisms in P2P:**
1. **Centralized Directory (semi-P2P):** A directory server tells peers *who has what* (e.g., Napster, modern BitTorrent trackers).
2. **Distributed Hash Table (DHT):** A fully decentralized key-value store (e.g., Kademlia used in IPFS).
3. **Gossip Protocol:** Peers randomly exchange state information periodically.
4. **Flooding Query:** Peers broadcast queries to neighbours (used in early Gnutella).

**Advantages of P2P:**
1. **Scalability** — capacity grows with user population; adding peers adds bandwidth.
2. **Cost-effective** — no expensive data center needed.
3. **Robustness** — no single point of failure; the system self-heals.
4. **Efficient bulk distribution** — parallel chunk downloading.

**Disadvantages of P2P:**
1. **Security risks** — malicious peers can inject fake chunks; needs hash-based verification.
2. **Unpredictable QoS** — performance depends on peer churn (peers joining/leaving arbitrarily).
3. **ISP pressure** — heavy uplink traffic may trigger throttling by ISPs.
4. **Hard to enforce copyright / regulation.**

---

*(b) A server with upload rate $u_s = 5$ Mbps distributes a file of size $F = 2$ GB to $N = 20$ peers. Each peer has download rate $d_i = 2$ Mbps. Compute the minimum distribution time for (i) Client-Server model and (ii) Pure P2P model where each peer additionally has upload rate $u_i = 0.5$ Mbps. (7 Marks)*

**Given Data:**
- $u_s = 5$ Mbps
- $F = 2$ GB $= 2 \times 8 = 16$ Gb $= 16000$ Mb
- $N = 20$ peers
- $d_i = 2$ Mbps (all peers)
- $u_i = 0.5$ Mbps (each peer, for P2P only)

**(i) Client-Server Minimum Distribution Time:**

**Step 1 — Server-limited time:**
The server must upload $N \cdot F$ bits at rate $u_s$.

$$
T_{server} = \frac{N \cdot F}{u_s} = \frac{20 \times 16000}{5} = \frac{320000}{5} = 64000 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 2 — Slowest-peer time:**
All peers have the same download rate, so:

$$
T_{peer} = \frac{F}{d_i} = \frac{16000}{2} = 8000 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 3 — Take the maximum:**

$$
T_{cs} = \max\{64000,\ 8000\} = 64000 \text{ s}
$$

**(ii) Pure P2P Minimum Distribution Time:**

**Step 1 — Server-must-seed term:**

$$
T_1 = \frac{F}{u_s} = \frac{16000}{5} = 3200 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 2 — Slowest-peer term:**

$$
T_2 = \frac{F}{d_{min}} = \frac{16000}{2} = 8000 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 3 — Aggregate upload term:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
U_{agg} &= u_s + N \cdot u_i = 5 + 20 \times 0.5 = 5 + 10 = 15 \text{ Mbps} \\
T_3 &= \frac{N \cdot F}{U_{agg}} = \frac{20 \times 16000}{15} = \frac{320000}{15} \approx 21333.33 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Take the maximum:**

$$
T_{p2p} = \max\{3200,\ 8000,\ 21333.33\} = 21333.33 \text{ s}
$$

**Final Comparison:**

$$
\frac{T_{cs}}{T_{p2p}} = \frac{64000}{21333.33} \approx 3.0
$$

The P2P model is approximately **3 times faster** for this scenario.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors:** Forgetting to convert GB to Mb (multiply by 8). Examiners award **1 mark deduction** for this in numericals.
> 2. **Forgetting the `max{}` Bracket:** Writing just one term instead of `max{...}` loses **2 marks** out of 7.
> 3. **Mixing up upload and download:** $u_s$ is upload, $d_i$ is download. They are NOT the same.
> 4. **In P2P, you MUST include the $F/u_s$ term** (the seed constraint) — many students drop it.
> 5. **In Hybrid, do not write "client-server" alone.** The keyword is "peer discovery + direct data exchange".
> 6. **In Client-Server diagrams, label the well-known port** — a diagram without port numbers loses at least **1 mark**.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] A **Network Application Architecture** defines how app software is structured over the network and the communication paradigm between processes.
- [ ] The **two main architectures** are **Client-Server** and **Peer-to-Peer**; a **Hybrid** of the two is dominant in practice.
- [ ] In **Client-Server**, the server has a *permanent IP*, an *always-on* status, and binds to a *well-known port* (0–1023).
- [ ] In **Peer-to-Peer**, every node acts as both *client* and *server*; the system is *self-organizing* and *decentralized*.
- [ ] In **Hybrid**, a *lightweight central coordinator* handles discovery/auth and peers exchange data *directly*.
- [ ] A **Socket** is the software endpoint (API) of a network connection — identified locally by `(IP, Port)`.
- [ ] A unique connection is identified by the **4-tuple**: `(Source IP, Source Port, Destination IP, Destination Port)`.
- [ ] Port range is **0 to 65535** ($2^{16} - 1$); 16 bits gives this range.
- [ ] **Well-known ports:** 0–1023. **Registered:** 1024–49151. **Ephemeral:** 49152–65535.
- [ ] **Iterative server** handles one client at a time; **Concurrent server** spawns threads/processes per client.
- [ ] P2P **Minimum Distribution Time** formula (must memorize): $T_{p2p} = \max\!\left\{ \dfrac{F}{u_s},\ \dfrac{F}{d_{min}},\ \dfrac{NF}{u_s + \sum u_i} \right\}$.
- [ ] Client-Server **Minimum Distribution Time**: $T_{cs} = \max\!\left\{ \dfrac{NF}{u_s},\ \dfrac{F}{d_{min}} \right\}$.
- [ ] Always convert file sizes to **bits** before plugging into the formula (1 GB = 8 Gb = 8000 Mb).
- [ ] P2P scales with users; Client-Server scales with hardware upgrade only.
- [ ] Transport service demands of apps: **reliable data transfer, throughput, timing (latency), security**.
- [ ] Examples of apps by transport: **HTTP/SMTP/FTP** use TCP (reliable); **DNS query** and **video streaming** often use UDP (loss-tolerant).
- [ ] Hybrid examples to remember for KTU: **WhatsApp, Skype, modern BitTorrent, Signal**.
- [ ] Pure P2P examples: **IPFS, early BitTorrent, Gnutella, Napster (actually semi-P2P)**.
- [ ] Always **draw a labelled diagram** in 7-mark questions — examiners actively reward neat diagrams with port numbers and IP addresses.
- [ ] In exam answers, **distinguish clearly** between *hardware* (the data center machine) and *software* (the server process).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Processes Communicating
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT631 - NETWORK & DATA SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Processes Communicating

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In any modern **Operating System** and **Networked Environment**, multiple processes must cooperate and exchange data to deliver a unified service (e.g., a web browser fetching a page from a server). The mechanisms, rules, and protocol primitives that allow two or more processes — running on the **same host** (local IPC) or on **geographically distributed hosts** (network IPC) — to transmit data and synchronize their execution are collectively defined as **Processes Communicating** or **Inter-Process Communication (IPC)**.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context for *Network & Data Security*, this topic emphasizes the **Client–Server communication model**, **Sockets**, **Ports**, and the **TCP/IP protocol handshake**, because these are the foundational abstractions upon which every secure channel (SSL/TLS, SSH, IPsec) is later constructed.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A process is an *active program*; a process becomes *communicating* when it exposes an endpoint (a **socket**) bound to a **port**, allowing remote processes to address it using the **IP address + Port number** tuple.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine two offices located in different cities that need to exchange legal documents:

- **Office A (Client)** writes a letter and hands it to a **Courier Service (Transport Layer – TCP)**.
- The courier uses a **Truck (Network Layer – IP)** driven on a **Highway (Physical Layer)** to reach **Office B (Server)**.
- Office B has a **Reception Desk (Port 80 for HTTP, Port 443 for HTTPS)** that accepts mail, sorts it, and passes it to the correct **Department (Process)**.

Key insights from this analogy:
1. The **IP address** is the *street address of the building* (locates the host).
2. The **Port number** is the *reception desk number* (locates the process within the host).
3. The **Socket** is the *mailbox* attached to that desk — the endpoint of communication.
4. **TCP** is the *registered courier with tracking* (reliable); **UDP** is the *local postal drop* (faster, unreliable).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Security Insight:** Almost every network attack (port scanning, SYN flood, session hijacking) is performed by exploiting the predictable behaviour of these communication primitives. Hence, *understanding "Processes Communicating" is the prerequisite for understanding every firewall rule and IDS signature.*

## 1.3 Standard Metrics at a Glance

- **Port Number Space:** $2^{16} = 65{,}536$ distinct ports per host.
- **MTU of standard Ethernet:** **1500 bytes**.
- **IP Header Size (IPv4):** **20 bytes** (without options).
- **TCP Header Size:** **20 bytes** (without options).
- **RTT (Round-Trip Time):** Typical Internet value **10 – 200 ms**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Fundamental Paradigms of IPC

Processes can communicate in only two mathematically distinct ways:

| # | Paradigm | Mechanism | Synchronization | Best Use Case |
|---|----------|-----------|-----------------|---------------|
| 1 | **Shared Memory** | A common region of physical memory is mapped into the address space of $\geq 2$ processes. | Provided by the programmer using semaphores/mutexes. | High-speed, same-host data exchange (e.g., database engines). |
| 2 | **Message Passing** | Explicit `send()` and `receive()` primitives exchange discrete *messages*. | Provided implicitly by the kernel (rendezvous, blocking, non-blocking). | Distributed systems, networked processes. |

## 2.2 Logical Channels for Message Passing

A *logical channel* is the abstract path that a message follows. It can be characterized by five orthogonal design decisions:

1. **Direct vs Indirect addressing**
   - *Direct*: messages name the source/destination process explicitly: `send(P, msg)` and `receive(Q, msg)`.
   - *Indirect*: messages are routed via a **mailbox** (or **port**): `send(mailbox, msg)` and `receive(mailbox, msg)`.
2. **Synchronous (Blocking) vs Asynchronous (Non-Blocking)**
   - *Synchronous send*: blocks the sender until the receiver has accepted the message.
   - *Asynchronous send*: returns control to the sender immediately; kernel buffers the message.
3. **Automatic vs Explicit buffering**
   - *Zero capacity (rendezvous)*: no buffering; sender waits for receiver.
   - *Bounded capacity (N messages)*: kernel holds up to *N*; sender blocks when full.
   - *Unbounded capacity*: sender never blocks; theoretically infinite buffer.
4. **Connection-Oriented vs Connectionless**
   - *Connection-oriented*: a logical session is established (3-way handshake); reliable, ordered byte stream (TCP).
   - *Connectionless*: each datagram is independent (UDP).
5. **Unicast vs Multicast vs Broadcast**

## 2.3 The Client–Server Model — KTU Favourite

The dominant model for *Processes Communicating* across a network is the **Client–Server Model**:

- **Server Process**: Listens on a *well-known port* (e.g., HTTP → 80, SSH → 22). Uses the `SOCKET()`, `BIND()`, `LISTEN()`, `ACCEPT()` primitives.
- **Client Process**: Initiates a connection using `SOCKET()`, `CONNECT()`. The OS dynamically allocates an *ephemeral port* (49,152 – 65,535) for the client side.
- Communication is **asymmetric**: the server is the *passive* opener, the client is the *active* opener.

## 2.4 Sockets and Ports

A **Socket** is the endpoint of a bidirectional communication flow. It is uniquely identified by the **5-tuple**:

$$
\{\text{Protocol},\ \text{SrcIP},\ \text{SrcPort},\ \text{DstIP},\ \text{DstPort}\}
$$

- **Port number** is a **16-bit unsigned integer** $\rightarrow$ range $[0,\ 65535]$.
- **Port categories** (assigned by IANA):
  - **0 – 1023** : *Well-Known Ports* — reserved for system/privileged services.
  - **1024 – 49,151** : *Registered Ports* — assigned to user-level services on request.
  - **49,152 – 65,535** : *Dynamic/Private/Ephemeral Ports* — used by clients.

## 2.5 TCP 3-Way Handshake (Connection Establishment)

This is the *single most important sub-topic* in the KTU Module-1 syllabus. A connection is established through three packets:

1. **SYN** : Client → Server (synchronizes sequence numbers; `SYN=1`, `seq=x`).
2. **SYN–ACK** : Server → Client (acknowledges client SYN and synchronizes its own; `SYN=1, ACK=1, seq=y, ack=x+1`).
3. **ACK** : Client → Server (acknowledges server SYN; `ACK=1, seq=x+1, ack=y+1`).

After these three segments, both sides enter the `ESTABLISHED` state and data transfer may begin.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Range |
|---|---------|----------------|---------------|
| 1 | Total port space | $2^{16}$ | $65{,}536$ ports |
| 2 | Well-known port count | $1024 - 0$ | $1024$ ports |
| 3 | Registered port count | $49{,}151 - 1024 + 1$ | $48{,}128$ ports |
| 4 | Ephemeral port count | $65{,}535 - 49{,}152 + 1$ | $16{,}384$ ports |
| 5 | TCP connection setup time | $T_{conn} = 1.5 \times \text{RTT}$ | seconds |
| 6 | Bandwidth–Delay Product | $\text{BDP} = \text{Bandwidth} \times \text{RTT}$ | bits |
| 7 | Optimal TCP window size | $W \geq \text{BDP}$ | bytes |
| 8 | Socket identifier | $\vert \text{IP} \vert$ + $\vert \text{Port} \vert$ | 32-bit + 16-bit |
| 9 | TCP seq-number space | $2^{32}$ | sequence numbers |
| 10 | Max TCP payload per segment | $\text{MTU} - \text{IP header} - \text{TCP header}$ | $1500 - 20 - 20 = 1460$ bytes |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** The Bandwidth–Delay Product dictates the **TCP receive window** required to fully saturate a high-bandwidth, long-distance link. For a 1 Gbps trans-Pacific link with RTT = 100 ms, $\text{BDP} = 1.25 \times 10^{8}$ bits $\approx 12.5$ MB — well above the legacy 64 KB window, which is why modern OSes implement *Window Scaling* in RFC 1323.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Total Number of Port Numbers

The port field in both TCP and UDP headers is a **16-bit unsigned integer**.

$$
\text{Total Ports} = 2^{16}
$$

**Step-by-step expansion:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
2^{16} &= 2^{10} \times 2^{6} \\
       &= 1024 \times 64 \\
       &= 65{,}536
\end{aligned}
$$

Because numbering starts at $0$, the valid range is $[0,\ 65{,}535]$.

### Breakdown of the Port Space

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Well-Known}    &= 1024 - 0      = 1{,}024 \text{ ports} \\
\text{Registered}    &= 49{,}151 - 1{,}024 + 1 = 48{,}128 \text{ ports} \\
\text{Ephemeral}     &= 65{,}535 - 49{,}152 + 1 = 16{,}384 \text{ ports} \\
\text{Total}         &= 1{,}024 + 48{,}128 + 16{,}384 = 65{,}536 \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Time Required to Establish a TCP Connection

Let $\text{RTT}$ denote the *one-way* Round-Trip Time between client and server.

**Step 1 —** Client sends **SYN** at time $t_0$.  
**Step 2 —** Server receives SYN at $t_0 + 0.5\,\text{RTT}$, then immediately transmits **SYN–ACK**.  
**Step 3 —** Client receives SYN–ACK at $t_0 + 1.5\,\text{RTT}$, then transmits the final **ACK**.  
**Step 4 —** Server receives ACK at $t_0 + 2.0\,\text{RTT}$ → connection is `ESTABLISHED`.

The *client-side* time-to-establish is therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{client}} &= 1.5 \times \text{RTT}
\end{aligned}
$$

The *server-side* time-to-establish is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{server}} &= 2.0 \times \text{RTT}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Numerical Example (Frequently Asked in KTU Exams)

> **Problem:** A client in Kerala establishes a TCP connection to a server in Singapore. If $\text{RTT} = 80\ \text{ms}$, calculate (i) the time the client waits before the connection is ready, and (ii) the time the server waits before its `accept()` returns a connected socket.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{client}} &= 1.5 \times 80\ \text{ms} \\
                  &= 120\ \text{ms} \\[4pt]
T_{\text{server}} &= 2.0 \times 80\ \text{ms} \\
                  &= 160\ \text{ms} \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Bandwidth–Delay Product and Minimum Window Size

The **BDP** is the maximum volume of data that can be *in flight* (transmitted but not yet acknowledged) on a network path.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{BDP} &= \text{Bandwidth (bps)} \times \text{RTT (s)}
\end{aligned}
$$

To **fully utilize** the link without the sender stalling on the window, the receiver must advertise a window $W$ such that:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W \geq \text{BDP}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Numerical Example

> **Problem:** A 100 Mbps link has $\text{RTT} = 60\ \text{ms}$. Calculate the minimum TCP window required to saturate the link.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{BDP} &= 100 \times 10^{6}\ \text{bits/s} \times 60 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{s} \\
          &= 6 \times 10^{6}\ \text{bits} \\
          &= \dfrac{6 \times 10^{6}}{8}\ \text{bytes} \\
          &= 750{,}000\ \text{bytes} \approx 732.4\ \text{KB} \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is why the original 16-bit TCP window field (max 65,535 bytes) was insufficient for high-BDP paths, leading to the **Window Scaling Option** (RFC 1323) that extends it to 30 bits.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Communication Paradigms at a Glance

| Attribute | Shared Memory | Message Passing (TCP) | Message Passing (UDP) |
|-----------|---------------|----------------------|------------------------|
| **Speed** | Fastest (memory bandwidth) | Moderate (kernel overhead) | Fast (no handshake) |
| **Synchronization** | Programmer's burden | Kernel-managed | Kernel-managed |
| **Reliability** | None built-in | Guaranteed (ACKs, retransmit) | Best-effort |
| **Order** | None | Preserved (FIFO) | Not guaranteed |
| **Host Scope** | Same host only | Anywhere routable | Anywhere routable |
| **Security Primitive** | `mmap` + `mlock` | TLS over TCP socket | DTLS over UDP |
| **Typical KTU Example** | Producer–Consumer | HTTP/HTTPS | DNS / VoIP |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1: High-Level Client–Server Process Communication

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    procA[Client Process A] -->|Request on Ephemeral Port| svrS[Server Process - Well Known Port 80]
    procB[Client Process B] -->|Request on Ephemeral Port| svrS
    procC[Client Process C] -->|Request on Ephemeral Port| svrS
    svrS -->|Response to A| procA
    svrS -->|Response to B| procB
    svrS -->|Response to C| procC
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Multiple clients open independent sockets to a single passive server. Each arrow represents a *separate* TCP connection identified by the 5-tuple.

## 4.2 Diagram 2: TCP 3-Way Handshake State Transition

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stp1[CLOSED] --> stp2[SYN_SENT]
    stp2[Client sends SYN seq=x] --> stp3
    stp3[SYN_RCVD - Server receives SYN] --> stp4
    stp4[Server sends SYN-ACK seq=y ack=x+1] --> stp5
    stp5[Client receives SYN-ACK] --> stp6
    stp6[ESTABLISHED - Client sends ACK ack=y+1] --> stp7
    stp7[ESTABLISHED - Server receives ACK - Data Transfer Begins]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Each oval represents a TCP FSM state; transitions occur upon transmission or reception of the labelled segment. The two sides reach `ESTABLISHED` after the third segment is *received* by the server.

## 4.3 Diagram 3: Classification of IPC Mechanisms

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    root1[Inter Process Communication] --> brn1[Shared Memory]
    root1 --> brn2[Message Passing]
    brn1 --> lf1[Memory Mapped Files]
    brn1 --> lf2[POSIX Shared Memory shm_open]
    brn2 --> lf3[Pipes - Named and Unnamed]
    brn2 --> lf4[Sockets - Stream and Datagram]
    brn2 --> lf5[Message Queues - System V and POSIX]
    brn2 --> lf6[Signals - Software Interrupts]
```

## 4.4 Diagram 4: Socket Communication Across the TCP/IP Stack

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    a1[Client Application] --> a2[Socket API - SOCKET CONNECT SEND RECV]
    a2 --> a3[Transport Layer - TCP]
    a3 --> a4[Network Layer - IP]
    a4 --> a5[Data Link Layer - Ethernet]
    a5 --> a6[Physical Medium - Cable or Wireless]
    a6 --> b5[Data Link Layer - Ethernet]
    b5 --> b4[Network Layer - IP]
    b4 --> b3[Transport Layer - TCP]
    b3 --> b2[Socket API - SOCKET BIND LISTEN ACCEPT RECV SEND]
    b2 --> b1[Server Application]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The client and server stacks are *mirror images*. Each layer adds its own header (encapsulation) on the way down, and removes it (decapsulation) on the way up. Security protocols (TLS, IPsec) typically insert themselves *between* the application and transport layers, or *between* the IP layer and data link layer, respectively.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Differentiate between connection-oriented and connectionless service in process communication. `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Connection-Oriented (TCP) | Connectionless (UDP) |
|---------|---------------------------|----------------------|
| Setup | Requires handshake (3-way) | No setup; send immediately |
| Reliability | Guaranteed delivery, ACK, retransmit | Best-effort, no ACK |
| Order | Preserved | Not guaranteed |
| Overhead | Higher (20+ bytes header, state) | Lower (8-byte header, stateless) |
| Example | HTTP, SSH, FTP | DNS query, VoIP, video streaming |

> **Marking Note:** A clean two-column table with at least *three* valid differences fetches full **3 marks**. Single-line definitions alone fetch 1 mark.

### Q2. What is a socket? List any four socket primitives used for connection-oriented client–server communication. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **socket** is the *endpoint of a bidirectional communication flow* between two processes, identified by the tuple `{IP\ \text{address},\ \text{Port\ number}}$ on a given protocol.

Four primitives (Berkeley Sockets API):

1. `SOCKET()` — Create a new communication endpoint.
2. `BIND()` — Associate the socket with a specific local IP and port.
3. `LISTEN()` — Mark socket as passive; ready to accept connections.
4. `CONNECT()` (client) / `ACCEPT()` (server) — Establish the TCP connection.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### ❑ Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the different mechanisms of **Inter-Process Communication (IPC)** with neat diagrams. Compare **shared memory** and **message passing** in detail. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Processes within an operating system must cooperate. There are two fundamental paradigms:

**1. Shared Memory:**
A region of physical memory is mapped into the address spaces of two or more cooperating processes. Writes by one process are immediately visible to the other. *Synchronization* (to avoid race conditions) is the *programmer's* responsibility, typically using semaphores or mutexes.

**Advantages:** Extremely fast (memory bus speed); suitable for high-throughput producer–consumer scenarios such as database buffer pools.
**Disadvantages:** Vulnerable to race conditions; cannot cross machine boundaries; security concerns (no message boundaries, no inherent authentication).

**2. Message Passing:**
Processes exchange discrete *messages* via the kernel using explicit `send()` and `receive()` primitives. Synchronization and data protection are handled *by the kernel*.

- *Direct* — `send(P, msg)` names the destination process.
- *Indirect* — `send(mailbox, msg)` uses a port or queue.

**Advantages:** Naturally suited for distributed systems; built-in synchronization; clearer security model.
**Disadvantages:** Slower due to kernel context switches and copying; bounded by buffering policy.

**Comparison (drawn in two columns on the answer sheet):**

| Basis | Shared Memory | Message Passing |
|-------|---------------|------------------|
| Speed | Memory-bus limited | Slower (system calls) |
| Scope | Local only | Local or remote |
| Sync | Manual | Automatic |
| Security | Weak (no boundaries) | Stronger (kernel-mediated) |

**(7-mark award criteria: 2 marks for shared memory, 2 marks for message passing, 2 marks for the comparison table, 1 mark for diagram.)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the **TCP 3-Way Handshake** for connection establishment. If the RTT between a client and a server is **120 ms**, calculate the time taken by the client and the server to complete the handshake. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — SYN:** The client sends a segment with `SYN=1` and an initial sequence number `x`. The client enters `SYN_SENT` state.

**Step 2 — SYN–ACK:** The server, upon receiving the SYN, allocates a TCB (Transmission Control Block), sends back a segment with `SYN=1, ACK=1`, its own sequence number `y`, and acknowledgement number `x+1`. The server enters `SYN_RCVD` state.

**Step 3 — ACK:** The client sends a final segment with `ACK=1, seq=x+1, ack=y+1`. The client transitions to `ESTABLISHED`. When the server receives this ACK, it also transitions to `ESTABLISHED`. Data transfer can now begin.

**Diagram (must be drawn on answer sheet):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    n1[Client] -->|SYN seq x| n2[Server]
    n2 -->|SYN-ACK seq y ack x+1| n1
    n1 -->|ACK seq x+1 ack y+1| n2
    n2 --> n3[Both sides ESTABLISHED]
```

**Calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Client side establishment time} &= 1.5 \times \text{RTT} \\
                                    &= 1.5 \times 120\ \text{ms} \\
                                    &= 180\ \text{ms} \\[4pt]
\text{Server side establishment time} &= 2.0 \times \text{RTT} \\
                                    &= 2.0 \times 120\ \text{ms} \\
                                    &= 240\ \text{ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(7-mark award criteria: 3 marks for stepwise explanation with diagram, 2 marks for the two time values, 2 marks for the formulas.)**

---

### ❑ Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **Client–Server model** of process communication. Discuss **connection-oriented** and **connectionless** service with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The **Client–Server model** is the dominant architecture for distributed process communication:

- The **Server** is a long-running *passive* process that binds to a well-known port and waits for incoming requests. It uses `SOCKET() → BIND() → LISTEN() → ACCEPT()`.
- The **Client** is a short-lived *active* process that initiates a request using `SOCKET() → CONNECT()`. The OS dynamically assigns an *ephemeral port* (49,152–65,535) for the client socket.
- A single server can serve **many clients concurrently** using either:
  - **Iterative server:** Handles one client at a time (rare in practice).
  - **Concurrent server:** Forks a child process or spawns a new thread for each `accept()`.

**Connection-Oriented Service (TCP):**

A *logical session* is established before data flows. Provides **reliable, ordered, error-checked, flow-controlled** byte stream. Used by HTTP/80, HTTPS/443, SSH/22, FTP/21.

**Connectionless Service (UDP):**

Each *datagram* is an independent, self-contained packet. **No handshake, no ACK, no retransmission, no order.** Lower overhead, lower latency, suitable for DNS/53, VoIP, online gaming, live video.

**Comparison:**

| Aspect | Connection-Oriented (TCP) | Connectionless (UDP) |
|--------|---------------------------|----------------------|
| Setup | 3-way handshake (3 packets) | None (1 packet) |
| Reliability | Guaranteed | Best-effort |
| Header Size | 20 bytes (min) | 8 bytes |
| State | Stateful in OS | Stateless |
| Flow Control | Yes (sliding window) | No |
| Use Case | File transfer, web | DNS, streaming |

**(7-mark award criteria: 2 marks for client–server roles, 2 marks for connection-oriented, 2 marks for connectionless, 1 mark for the comparison.)**

**(b)** What is a **socket**? With a diagram, explain the **socket primitives** used in client–server communication. Differentiate between **stream sockets** and **datagram sockets**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A **socket** is the endpoint of a two-way communication link between two processes running on a network. It is the *abstraction* through which the application layer accesses the transport layer (TCP/UDP). Sockets were introduced in **BSD Unix (1981)** and form the de-facto API: the **Berkeley Sockets** or **POSIX Sockets** interface.

**Socket Primitives — Server Side:**

1. `socket()` — Create an endpoint; returns a file descriptor.
2. `bind()` — Associate the socket with a specific IP and port.
3. `listen()` — Mark the socket as willing to accept connections (passive open).
4. `accept()` — Block until a client connects; return a *new* socket for data transfer.
5. `read()/write()` or `recv()/send()` — Exchange data.
6. `close()` — Terminate the connection.

**Socket Primitives — Client Side:**

1. `socket()` — Create an endpoint.
2. `connect()` — Actively attempt to reach the server's `IP:Port`.
3. `read()/write()` or `recv()/send()` — Exchange data.
4. `close()` — Terminate.

**Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    c1[Client] -->|socket| c2[connect to server IP:port]
    c2 -->|send / recv| c3[Application Data]
    c3 -->|close| c4[Connection Released]
    s1[Server] -->|socket| s2[bind to local IP:port]
    s2 -->|listen| s3[accept - block for client]
    s3 -->|send / recv| s4[Application Data]
    s4 -->|close| s5[Connection Released]
```

**Stream Sockets (`SOCK_STREAM`) vs Datagram Sockets (`SOCK_DGRAM`):**

| Feature | Stream Socket | Datagram Socket |
|---------|---------------|------------------|
| Protocol | TCP | UDP |
| Connection | Connection-oriented | Connectionless |
| Data Unit | Byte stream | Discrete datagrams |
| Boundaries | None preserved | Preserved per `send()` |
| Reliability | Guaranteed | None |
| Order | FIFO | Not guaranteed |
| Example | HTTP, Telnet | DNS, TFTP |

**(7-mark award criteria: 2 marks for socket definition + primitives list, 3 marks for diagram and step explanation, 2 marks for the stream vs datagram comparison.)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in "Processes Communicating" Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing SYN and SYN–ACK sequence numbers.** Many students write `seq = x+1` for the first SYN. The first SYN has `seq = x`; only the *acknowledgement field* in the second segment is `x+1`.
> 2. **Forgetting the asymmetry** between client and server connection-setup times: $1.5 \times \text{RTT}$ vs $2.0 \times \text{RTT}$.
> 3. **Port-range arithmetic errors:** Registered ports are **1024 to 49,151** (count = 48,128), not 49,151. Use the inclusive formula $\text{high} - \text{low} + 1$.
> 4. **Missing the diagram.** A written explanation of the handshake without the labelled three-arrow diagram loses **1–2 marks** in Part-B.
> 5. **Omitting the `IP:Port` tuple** when defining a socket. A socket is identified by *both* the IP and the port; only mentioning the port is incomplete.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **IPC** has only **two paradigms** — *Shared Memory* and *Message Passing*. All other mechanisms (pipes, sockets, queues) are specializations of message passing.
- A **process** communicates with another process via a **socket**, identified by the 5-tuple `{Protocol, SrcIP, SrcPort, DstIP, DstPort}`.
- **Port numbers** are 16-bit → **65,536 total** → split as Well-Known (0–1023), Registered (1024–49,151), Ephemeral (49,152–65,535).
- The **Client–Server model** is the canonical architecture: server binds to a well-known port, client connects from an ephemeral port.
- **TCP** is connection-oriented, reliable, ordered, byte-stream → uses the **3-way handshake** (SYN → SYN–ACK → ACK).
- TCP connection setup: client takes $1.5 \times \text{RTT}$, server takes $2.0 \times \text{RTT}$.
- **UDP** is connectionless, unreliable, datagram-oriented → no handshake, 8-byte header.
- **Stream Sockets** use TCP; **Datagram Sockets** use UDP.
- **Socket primitives** in order — Server: `socket → bind → listen → accept → recv/send → close`; Client: `socket → connect → send/recv → close`.
- **Bandwidth–Delay Product** dictates the minimum TCP window to fully utilize a link: $\text{BDP} = \text{Bandwidth} \times \text{RTT}$.
- **Security Angle:** Every secure protocol (TLS, SSH, IPsec) is a wrapper around these very primitives; understanding them is foundational for the rest of the *Network & Data Security* course.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transport Services Available to Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT631 - NETWORK & DATA SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Transport Services Available to Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

In the **TCP/IP protocol suite**, the **Transport Layer (Layer 4 of the OSI Reference Model)** provides end-to-end communication services for applications running on different hosts. The transport layer sits logically between the application layer (Layer 7) and the network layer (Layer 3), acting as the bridge that converts application-layer requests into network-routable packets.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** Transport services available to applications refer to the set of logical, end-to-end communication primitives offered by protocols such as **TCP (Transmission Control Protocol)** and **UDP (User Datagram Protocol)** to application-layer processes. These include *multiplexing/demultiplexing*, *connection-oriented reliable byte-stream delivery*, *connectionless best-effort datagram delivery*, *flow control*, *congestion control*, *timing*, and *security* (via TLS/DTLS).

The two principal transport-layer protocols of the Internet are:

| Protocol | Type | Standard | RFC |
|----------|------|----------|-----|
| **TCP** | Connection-oriented, reliable | RFC 793 / RFC 9293 | **Transmission Control Protocol** |
| **UDP** | Connectionless, unreliable | RFC 768 | **User Datagram Protocol** |

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Think of the transport layer as a **postal and courier system** that operates between two office buildings (hosts).

- The **Network Layer (IP)** is like the *road network and highways* — it knows how to get a letter from City A to City B, but does not care whether the letter is a bill, a wedding invitation, or a fragile package.
- The **Transport Layer (TCP/UDP)** is the *courier company* — it decides **how** to deliver the item: registered post with a tracking number (TCP) or a plain drop-off without tracking (UDP).
- The **Application Layer** is the *office rooms* that send and receive mail. Each room has a unique "mailbox number," which is the **port number**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** A host may run multiple applications simultaneously (e.g., a web browser, an email client, and a video call). Without transport-layer services, all this data would be merged into a single stream. Transport-layer **multiplexing** and **demultiplexing** ensure each application gets its own dedicated, isolated logical channel.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Port Identifiers

- **Well-Known Ports:** **0 – 1023** (e.g., HTTP uses **80**, HTTPS uses **443**, FTP uses **21**).
- **Registered Ports:** **1024 – 49151** (assigned by IANA for specific applications).
- **Dynamic/Private Ports:** **49152 – 65535** (allocated temporarily by the OS to clients).
- **Maximum Segment Size (MSS):** Typically **1460 bytes** for Ethernet (1500-byte MTU − 20-byte IP header − 20-byte TCP header).
- **Maximum Transmission Unit (MTU):** Default **1500 bytes** for standard Ethernet.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A transport-layer segment is identified by a **4-tuple**: `<Source IP, Source Port, Destination IP, Destination Port>`. This 4-tuple is what enables simultaneous, independent sessions between the same pair of hosts (e.g., opening two browser tabs to the same website).

### 1.4 Service Categories at a Glance

Transport-layer services are commonly grouped into **four families** that an application can request:

1. **Reliable Data Transfer** — Guarantees that every byte sent is received, in order, without errors or duplication.
2. **Throughput Guarantee** — Provides a minimum bandwidth (e.g., a video stream may demand ≥ 2 Mbps).
3. **Timing Guarantee** — Bounds end-to-end delay (e.g., VoIP requires delay < **150 ms**).
4. **Security** — Confidentiality, integrity, and authentication (provided via TLS/DTLS over TCP/UDP).

> [!WARNING]
> The Internet's transport layer (IP + TCP/UDP) is fundamentally a **best-effort** service. None of the four guarantees above is provided by the network layer. **TCP** implements reliability, flow control, and congestion control in software. **UDP** provides none of these guarantees, leaving reliability to the application itself.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two Pillars: TCP vs UDP Service Models

The transport layer exposes a small, well-defined API to applications. The two main service models are:

#### A. UDP — Connectionless, Unreliable Datagram Service
UDP provides **best-effort delivery**. There is:
- **No handshaking** before data is sent.
- **No connection state** maintained in endpoints.
- **No guarantee** of delivery, ordering, or duplication protection.
- **No flow control** or **congestion control**.

UDP simply extends the host-to-host IP service to process-to-process communication by adding **multiplexing/demultiplexing** and a lightweight **checksum**.

#### B. TCP — Connection-Oriented, Reliable Byte-Stream Service
TCP provides:
- **Connection-oriented service** — A 3-way handshake (**SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK**) establishes state at both ends before data flows.
- **Reliable data transfer** — Uses sequence numbers, acknowledgements (ACKs), retransmissions, and a sliding-window mechanism.
- **Flow control** — Sender will not overflow the receiver's buffer (via the *Receive Window* field).
- **Congestion control** — Sender throttles transmission to avoid overwhelming the network (cwnd variable, slow start, congestion avoidance, fast retransmit, fast recovery).
- **In-order byte delivery** — Segments are reassembled in correct sequence.
- **Full-duplex** — Data flows in both directions simultaneously over the same connection.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does TCP not provide timing or throughput guarantees?** Because the Internet itself does not reserve bandwidth or guarantee delay for a flow. These guarantees are end-to-end *Quality of Service* (QoS) properties that require infrastructure support (IntServ, DiffServ) which is not uniformly deployed.

### 2.2 The Transport Service Decision Matrix

A developer choosing between TCP and UDP asks: *"Does my application need reliability, or does it need speed?"*

| Application Requirement | Choose UDP | Choose TCP |
|--------------------------|------------|------------|
| Loss-tolerant, delay-sensitive (VoIP, live gaming) | ✅ | ✗ |
| Bandwidth-sensitive, elastic (file transfer, email) | ✗ | ✅ |
| Simple request-reply (DNS, SNMP, NTP) | ✅ | ✗ |
| In-order delivery required | ✗ | ✅ |
| Congestion-controlled bulk transfer | ✗ | ✅ |
| Multicast / broadcast | ✅ | ✗ |
| Small header overhead (8 bytes vs 20 bytes) | ✅ | ✗ |
| Application implements its own reliability | ✅ | — |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Meaning | Formula / Value | Units |
|--------|---------|-----------------|-------|
| $RTT$ | Round-Trip Time | $RTT = 2 \times \text{One-way delay} + \text{Processing time}$ | seconds (s) |
| $L$ | Segment size (payload) | Typically $\leq$ **1460 bytes** | bytes |
| $R$ | Transmission rate of link | $R = \text{bandwidth}$ | bits/second (bps) |
| $T_{trans}$ | Transmission delay per segment | $T_{trans} = \dfrac{L}{R}$ | seconds |
| $T_{prop}$ | Propagation delay | $T_{prop} = \dfrac{d}{s}$, where $d$ = distance, $s$ = signal speed ($\approx 2 \times 10^8$ m/s in fiber) | seconds |
| $U_{sender}$ | Sender utilization (stop-and-wait) | $U_{sender} = \dfrac{T_{trans}}{T_{trans} + RTT}$ | dimensionless (0 to 1) |
| $Throughput$ | Pipeline throughput | $Throughput = \dfrac{L \times W}{RTT}$, where $W$ = window size in segments | bytes/s |
| $cwnd$ | TCP congestion window | Initially 1 MSS, grows exponentially in slow start | MSS units |
| $ssthresh$ | Slow-start threshold | Halved on triple-duplicate-ACK | MSS units |
| $UDP_{header}$ | UDP header length | **8 bytes** (fixed) | bytes |
| $TCP_{header}$ | TCP header length | **20 bytes** (min, without options) | bytes |
| $IP_{header}$ | IP header length | **20 bytes** (IPv4, no options) | bytes |
| $MTU$ | Maximum Transmission Unit | Default **1500 bytes** (Ethernet) | bytes |
| $MSS$ | Maximum Segment Size | $MSS = MTU - IP_{header} - TCP_{header} = 1500 - 20 - 20 = 1460$ bytes | bytes |
| $\rho$ | Bit error rate of link | $\rho = \dfrac{\text{bits in error}}{\text{bits transmitted}}$ | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **stop-and-wait protocol utilization** formula $U_{sender} = \dfrac{L/R}{L/R + RTT}$ is a **KTU high-yield formula**. It explains why simple stop-and-wait is inefficient on high-bandwidth, high-latency paths (e.g., satellite links) and motivates **pipelining** (which TCP uses with a sliding window).

### 2.4 Services NOT Provided by the Internet Transport Layer

A common KTU trick question is *"Which of the following is not provided by TCP?"* The answer often includes:

- **Timing guarantees** (no hard delay bounds).
- **Throughput guarantees** (no minimum bandwidth reservation).
- **Multicast support** (TCP is strictly unicast point-to-point).
- **Security / confidentiality** (TCP itself is plaintext; security is layered above via TLS).

> [!NOTE]
> **Application-Layer Examples Mapping to Services:**
> - **HTTP/3 over QUIC** → UDP + reliability implemented in user space + TLS 1.3 built-in.
> - **DNS** → UDP port 53 (fast, small queries/responses, retransmission handled at application level).
> - **SSH (port 22)** → TCP, requires in-order byte stream.
> - **RTP (Real-time Transport Protocol)** → UDP, tolerates packet loss for low latency.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Web servers** (Nginx, Apache) listen on TCP port 80/443 because HTTP needs reliable in-order delivery.
- **Video conferencing** (Zoom, Google Meet) primarily use UDP for media streams because re-transmitting lost packets would be worse than skipping them.
- **Online multiplayer games** use UDP because the latest game state is more important than retransmitting old packets.
- **Internet of Things (IoT)** sensors often use UDP (e.g., CoAP over UDP) to minimize power and bandwidth consumption.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Why Stop-and-Wait is Inefficient — The Utilization Formula

A central analytical question in transport services is: *"Given a network's bandwidth and delay, what fraction of time is the sender actually transmitting?"*

**Step 1 — Model the timeline.**
Suppose the sender transmits one segment of $L$ bits at link rate $R$ bps, then waits for the ACK.

The **transmission delay** (time to push all bits of the segment onto the wire) is:

$$
T_{trans} = \frac{L}{R}
$$

**Step 2 — Add the round-trip propagation delay.**
