After the last bit of the segment reaches the receiver, the receiver processes it, generates an ACK, and the ACK propagates back. The total time the sender is idle is $RTT$ (round-trip time).

**Step 3 — Define sender utilization.**
Utilization $U$ is the fraction of total time the sender is busy transmitting useful data:

$$
U_{sender} = \frac{\text{Busy time}}{\text{Busy time} + \text{Idle time}} = \frac{T_{trans}}{T_{trans} + RTT}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $T_{trans} = L/R$.**

$$
U_{sender} = \frac{L/R}{L/R + RTT} = \frac{L}{L + R \cdot RTT}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation.**

- If $R \cdot RTT \gg L$ (i.e., the product of bandwidth and delay, called the **bandwidth-delay product**, is much larger than the segment size), then $U_{sender} \to 0$. The sender is mostly idle.
- If $R \cdot RTT \ll L$, then $U_{sender} \to 1$. The sender is almost always busy.

> [!NOTE]
> **Bandwidth-Delay Product (BDP):** The number of bits that can be "in flight" in the network at any time:
>
> $$
> BDP = R \times RTT \quad \text{(bits)}
> $$
>
> A sliding-window protocol of size $W$ segments achieves near-100% utilization when $W \geq \lceil BDP / L \rceil$.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU 14-Mark Style)

> **Problem:** A link has bandwidth $R = 1$ Mbps and one-way propagation delay $d = 50$ ms. The transport layer uses stop-and-wait ARQ with segment size $L = 1000$ bytes. The ACK is negligibly small. Find:
> (a) The transmission delay of a segment.
> (b) The round-trip time.
> (c) The sender utilization.
> (d) The throughput in Kbps.

**Given:**
- $R = 1$ Mbps $= 10^6$ bits/s
- One-way delay $= 50$ ms $\Rightarrow RTT = 2 \times 50 = 100$ ms $= 0.1$ s
- $L = 1000$ bytes $= 8000$ bits

**Solution:**

**(a) Transmission delay:**

$$
T_{trans} = \frac{L}{R} = \frac{8000 \text{ bits}}{10^6 \text{ bits/s}} = 8 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 8 \text{ ms}
$$

**(b) Round-trip time:**

$$
RTT = 2 \times \text{one-way delay} = 2 \times 50 \text{ ms} = 100 \text{ ms}
$$

**(c) Sender utilization:**

$$
U_{sender} = \frac{T_{trans}}{T_{trans} + RTT} = \frac{8}{8 + 100} = \frac{8}{108} \approx 0.0741
$$

$$
U_{sender} \approx 7.41\%
$$

**(d) Throughput in Kbps:**

$$
Throughput = U_{sender} \times R = 0.0741 \times 10^6 \text{ bps} = 74{,}074 \text{ bps} \approx 74.07 \text{ Kbps}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This explains why HTTP file transfers over satellite links (high RTT) were painfully slow in the 1990s. Pipelining (multiple unACKed segments) — implemented by TCP's sliding window — was the cure.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: TCP vs UDP Service Comparison

| Service Feature | Provided by UDP? | Provided by TCP? | Notes |
|------------------|------------------|------------------|-------|
| Process-to-process multiplexing | ✅ via port numbers | ✅ via port numbers | Both use 16-bit port numbers |
| Connection establishment (handshake) | ❌ | ✅ (3-way) | TCP = SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK |
| Reliable data transfer | ❌ | ✅ | TCP uses ARQ, sequence numbers, ACKs |
| In-order delivery | ❌ | ✅ | TCP reassembles byte stream |
| Flow control | ❌ | ✅ (rwnd) | Receiver advertises window size |
| Congestion control | ❌ | ✅ (cwnd) | Slow start, AIMD, fast retransmit/recovery |
| Message boundary preservation | ✅ | ❌ (byte stream) | UDP preserves datagram boundaries |
| Minimum header overhead | **8 bytes** | **20 bytes** | UDP is leaner |
| Multicast support | ✅ | ❌ | TCP is strictly unicast |
| Cryptographic security | ❌ (use DTLS) | ❌ (use TLS) | Neither provides it natively |
| Latency | **Low** (no handshake) | **Higher** (handshake + ACK delay) | UDP preferred for real-time |
| Suitability for DNS | ✅ | ✗ | DNS uses UDP port 53 |
| Suitability for HTTP/1.1, HTTP/2 | ✗ | ✅ | HTTP/3 over QUIC uses UDP |

### 3.4 The Application-to-Transport Mapping Algorithm

When a developer writes a network application, the choice of transport protocol follows this decision tree:

**Step 1** — Does the application need in-order, lossless delivery?
- If **YES** → choose **TCP**.
- If **NO** → proceed to Step 2.

**Step 2** — Is the application delay-sensitive (e.g., live audio/video/gaming)?
- If **YES** → choose **UDP** (optionally with application-level FEC/ARQ).
- If **NO** → proceed to Step 3.

**Step 3** — Is the application multicast or broadcast?
- If **YES** → choose **UDP** (TCP cannot multicast).
- If **NO** → choose **TCP** (simpler development).

**Step 4** — Does the application need to preserve message boundaries (one write = one read)?
- If **YES** → choose **UDP** (each datagram is a self-contained message).
- If **NO** → TCP is fine (it is a byte stream).

> [!NOTE]
> Modern protocols like **QUIC** (used by HTTP/3) blur this line: QUIC is built on UDP but implements reliability, congestion control, and encryption in user space, often achieving lower latency than TCP+TLS.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Transport Layer Service Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

The following Mermaid diagram depicts the functional flow of transport services from the application layer down to the link layer, and shows how multiplexing and demultiplexing work in a host.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph HL1["HOST A: SENDER SIDE"]
        APP1["App 1: Web Browser"]
        APP2["App 2: Email Client"]
        APP3["App 3: Video Call"]
        SP1["Source Port 80"]
        SP2["Source Port 25"]
        SP3["Source Port 5004"]
        MUX["Multiplexer"]
        SOCKA["TCP Socket"]
        SOCKB["UDP Socket"]
        APPDATA["Application Data"]
        TPHDR1["TCP Header + Source/Dest Ports"]
        TPHDR2["UDP Header + Source/Dest Ports"]
        SEG["Transport Segment / Datagram"]
        IPHDR["IP Header"]
        PKT["IP Packet"]
    end

    subgraph NET["INTERNET / NETWORK LAYER"]
        ROUTE["IP Forwarding and Routing"]
    end

    subgraph HL2["HOST B: RECEIVER SIDE"]
        RECVPKT["Received IP Packet"]
        DEMUX["Demultiplexer"]
        DS1["Dest Port 80"]
        DS2["Dest Port 25"]
        DS3["Dest Port 5004"]
        SKTA["TCP Socket"]
        SKTB["UDP Socket"]
        OUT1["Deliver to App 1: Web Server"]
        OUT2["Deliver to App 2: Mail Server"]
        OUT3["Deliver to App 3: Media Player"]
    end

    APP1 --> SP1
    APP2 --> SP2
    APP3 --> SP3
    SP1 --> MUX
    SP2 --> MUX
    SP3 --> MUX
    MUX -->|TCP path| SOCKA
    MUX -->|UDP path| SOCKB
    SOCKA --> APPDATA
    SOCKB --> APPDATA
    APPDATA --> TPHDR1
    APPDATA --> TPHDR2
    TPHDR1 --> SEG
    TPHDR2 --> SEG
    SEG --> IPHDR
    IPHDR --> PKT
    PKT --> ROUTE
    ROUTE --> RECVPKT
    RECVPKT --> DEMUX
    DEMUX --> DS1
    DEMUX --> DS2
    DEMUX --> DS3
    DS1 --> SKTA
    DS2 --> SKTA
    DS3 --> SKTB
    SKTA --> OUT1
    SKTA --> OUT2
    SKTB --> OUT3
```

### 4.2 TCP vs UDP Service Decision Flowchart

The following Mermaid flowchart shows how an application developer typically decides which transport service to use.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START["Application Needs Network Transport"]
    Q1{"Need In-Order, Lossless Delivery?"}
    Q2{"Latency Sensitive Real Time?"}
    Q3{"Multicast or Broadcast?"}
    Q4{"Need Message Boundary Preservation?"}
    TCP["Use TCP: Reliable Byte Stream"]
    UDP["Use UDP: Unreliable Datagram"]
    QUIC["Use QUIC over UDP: Reliable + Low Latency"]
    DTLS["Use DTLS over UDP: Secure Datagram"]

    START --> Q1
    Q1 -->|YES| TCP
    Q1 -->|NO| Q2
    Q2 -->|YES| Q4
    Q2 -->|NO| UDP
    Q4 -->|YES| UDP
    Q4 -->|NO| QUIC
    Q3 -->|YES| UDP
    Q3 -->|NO| TCP
    TCP --> TLS["Optionally wrap in TLS for security"]
    UDP --> DTLS
```

### 4.3 Transport Segment Header Anatomy (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph UDPH["UDP HEADER: 8 BYTES"]
        USP["Source Port (2 bytes)"]
        UDP["Destination Port (2 bytes)"]
        ULN["Length (2 bytes)"]
        UCS["Checksum (2 bytes)"]
    end

    subgraph TCPH["TCP HEADER: 20 BYTES MINIMUM"]
        TSP["Source Port (2 bytes)"]
        TDP["Destination Port (2 bytes)"]
        TSN["Sequence Number (4 bytes)"]
        TAK["Acknowledgement Number (4 bytes)"]
        TFL["Flags + Data Offset (2 bytes)"]
        TWN["Receive Window (2 bytes)"]
        TCH["Checksum (2 bytes)"]
        TUR["Urgent Pointer (2 bytes)"]
    end

    APPD["Application Payload (Variable)"]

    USP --- UDP
    UDP --- ULN
    ULN --- UCS
    UCS === APPD

    TSP --- TDP
    TDP --- TSN
    TSN --- TAK
    TAK --- TFL
    TFL --- TWN
    TWN --- TCH
    TCH --- TUR
    TUR === APPD
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The UDP header is **fixed at 8 bytes** (4 fields of 2 bytes each), making it ideal for small, frequent messages (e.g., DNS queries, NTP, gaming heartbeats). TCP's minimum header is **20 bytes** and can grow with options (e.g., MSS, window scaling, timestamps, SACK).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — 3 Mark Questions

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*List any three services provided by the transport layer to application-layer processes.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The transport layer provides the following services to applications:
1. **Process-to-process delivery** via multiplexing and demultiplexing using port numbers.
2. **Reliable data transfer** (in TCP) — guarantees in-order, error-free, duplicate-free delivery.
3. **Connection-oriented service with flow and congestion control** (in TCP) — ensures the sender does not overwhelm the receiver or the network.

*(For full 3 marks, students may also mention: error detection via checksum, full-duplex communication, and timing/throughput services in specialized protocols.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between connection-oriented and connectionless services with one example each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Connection-Oriented (TCP) | Connectionless (UDP) |
|-----------|--------------------------|----------------------|
| Setup | Requires handshake (SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK) | No setup; data sent immediately |
| State | Maintains connection state at both ends | Stateless |
| Reliability | Guarantees delivery, order, no duplication | Best-effort; no guarantees |
| Example | HTTP, FTP, SSH, SMTP | DNS query, SNMP, TFTP, live video streaming |

*(Award 1 mark for each of: definition of connection-oriented, definition of connectionless, one valid example each.)*

---

### PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Module 1 carries **14 marks** with internal choice. You must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the transport-layer services available to applications. List the four broad service categories and explain how TCP and UDP differ in providing them. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A stop-and-wait ARQ protocol is used over a link of bandwidth **2 Mbps** and one-way propagation delay **40 ms**. The segment size is **2000 bytes** and the ACK size is negligible. Calculate:
   (i) The transmission delay of a segment.
   (ii) The round-trip time.
   (iii) The sender utilization.
   (iv) The throughput in Kbps. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Transport-Layer Services (7 marks):**

The transport layer provides **end-to-end communication services** to application processes running on different hosts. The four broad categories are:

**1. Reliable Data Transfer:** Ensures that the data sent by the source application is delivered to the destination application **in order, without errors, and without duplication**. This is achieved through techniques such as sequence numbers, acknowledgements, retransmission timers, and checksums.
- **TCP** implements this fully.
- **UDP** does **not** implement this; the application must handle reliability itself if needed.

**2. Throughput:** A guaranteed minimum data rate (in bps) between sender and receiver. Applications like video streaming may need a sustained throughput.
- **Neither TCP nor UDP** provides hard throughput guarantees. They provide a *best-effort* service. Some applications (e.g., RTP over UDP) rely on adaptive bitrate encoding to cope with variable throughput.

**3. Timing (Delay Guarantees):** An upper bound on end-to-end delay (e.g., < **150 ms** for VoIP).
- **Neither TCP nor UDP** guarantees timing. UDP's lower per-packet latency (no handshake, no retransmission) makes it the preferred choice for delay-sensitive apps, but timing is not contractually guaranteed.

**4. Security:** Confidentiality, integrity, and authentication of transmitted data.
- **TCP and UDP** do **not** provide this natively. Security is added above the transport layer via **TLS** (for TCP) or **DTLS** (for UDP).

**TCP vs UDP Summary:**
- TCP = connection-oriented + reliable + flow control + congestion control + in-order + full-duplex.
- UDP = connectionless + unreliable + no flow/congestion control + best-effort + low overhead + preserves message boundaries + supports multicast.

*(Marking scheme: 1 mark for each service category explained, 2 marks for TCP vs UDP comparison, 1 mark for example mapping.)*

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $R = 2 \text{ Mbps} = 2 \times 10^6$ bps
- One-way delay $= 40$ ms $\Rightarrow RTT = 80$ ms $= 0.08$ s
- $L = 2000$ bytes $= 16{,}000$ bits

**(i) Transmission delay:**

$$
T_{trans} = \frac{L}{R} = \frac{16{,}000}{2 \times 10^6} = 8 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 8 \text{ ms}
$$

**(ii) Round-trip time:**

$$
RTT = 2 \times 40 = 80 \text{ ms}
$$

**(iii) Sender utilization:**

$$
U_{sender} = \frac{T_{trans}}{T_{trans} + RTT} = \frac{8}{8 + 80} = \frac{8}{88} = \frac{1}{11} \approx 0.0909
$$

$$
U_{sender} \approx 9.09\%
$$

**(iv) Throughput in Kbps:**

$$
Throughput = U_{sender} \times R = 0.0909 \times 2 \times 10^6 \text{ bps} = 181{,}818 \text{ bps} \approx 181.82 \text{ Kbps}
$$

*(Marking scheme: 1 mark each for parts (i), (ii), (iii), (iv), plus 3 marks for correct formula application and final numerical answer.)*

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **multiplexing and demultiplexing** at the transport layer. How does a host use the 4-tuple to deliver a received segment to the correct application process? Illustrate with a diagram or example. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A web server handles two simultaneous HTTP connections from the same client IP **10.0.0.5**. The client uses two browser tabs with source ports **50001** and **50002**, both destined to server port **80**. Show how the server's demultiplexer distinguishes the two connections using the 4-tuple. Discuss what happens if the client reuses the same source port for both connections. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Multiplexing and Demultiplexing (7 marks):**

- **Multiplexing** at the sender is the process of gathering data from multiple application-layer sockets, wrapping each with a transport header (containing source and destination port numbers), and passing the resulting segments to the network layer.
- **Demultiplexing** at the receiver is the reverse: the transport layer inspects the destination port (and other fields) of each incoming segment and delivers the payload to the correct application socket.

**The 4-tuple:** A unique TCP/UDP connection is identified by:
`<Source IP, Source Port, Destination IP, Destination Port>`

- For **UDP**, demultiplexing uses the **2-tuple** `<Destination IP, Destination Port>` since UDP is connectionless.
- For **TCP**, demultiplexing uses the full **4-tuple** because TCP is connection-oriented and multiple connections can share the same port pair on a server (e.g., multiple clients to port 80).

**Example:** A host with IP 192.168.1.10 runs a browser (port 49152) and an email client (port 49153), both sending to a web server (port 80) and a mail server (port 25) respectively. The transport layer adds appropriate port numbers; the receiving host uses them to dispatch data to the correct application.

*(Marking scheme: 2 marks for definitions, 2 marks for 4-tuple explanation, 2 marks for UDP vs TCP demultiplexing, 1 mark for example.)*

---

**(b) The 4-tuple in Action (7 marks):**

**Server-side demultiplexing table (conceptual):**

| Connection | Source IP | Source Port | Dest IP | Dest Port | Maps to Socket |
|------------|-----------|-------------|---------|-----------|----------------|
| 1 | 10.0.0.5 | 50001 | 203.0.113.7 | 80 | Socket A |
| 2 | 10.0.0.5 | 50002 | 203.0.113.7 | 80 | Socket B |

The server maintains a **connection control block** (or a hash table) keyed by the 4-tuple. When a segment arrives:
1. The server extracts the 4-tuple from the IP and TCP headers.
2. It searches the table for a matching 4-tuple.
3. If a match is found, the segment is delivered to the corresponding socket.
4. If no match exists, the segment is rejected (or a RST is sent).

**If the client reuses the same source port (e.g., 50001) for both connections:**
- Both connections would have the **identical 4-tuple**: `<10.0.0.5, 50001, 203.0.113.7, 80>`.
- The server's demultiplexer **cannot distinguish them** — it would deliver all segments of both connections to the same socket, **corrupting both data streams**.
- This is why the OS allocates a **unique ephemeral source port** to every new client-side TCP connection.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students write "port number identifies the connection." This is **partially correct** — for **TCP**, the **full 4-tuple** identifies the connection. For **UDP**, only the 2-tuple `<Dest IP, Dest Port>` is used. Conflating these is a common **2-mark deduction**.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes students make in this topic (and how marks are lost):**
> 1. **Conflating TCP and UDP services** — saying "TCP is unreliable" or "UDP guarantees in-order delivery." This is factually wrong and costs 2-3 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the 4-tuple** — many students say "the destination port number identifies the application." For **TCP**, the 4-tuple is required, not just the port. (See Question B solution above.)
> 3. **Confusing MSS and MTU** — MTU is the link-layer frame limit (1500 bytes for Ethernet). MSS = MTU − 40 (for IP+TCP headers) = **1460 bytes**. Mixing these up is a frequent error.
> 4. **Mixing up utilization and throughput** — Utilization is dimensionless (0 to 1 or 0% to 100%). Throughput is in **bits/second (bps)**. State units explicitly in numerical answers.
> 5. **Skipping units in the final answer** — KTU examiners often deduct 0.5 mark per missing unit. Always write **"Kbps"**, **"ms"**, **"bytes"**, etc.
> 6. **Forgetting that UDP also has a checksum** — UDP's checksum is optional in IPv4 (mandatory in IPv6), but the field still exists. Writing "UDP has no error detection" loses 1 mark.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Transport Services Available to Applications:**
> - The transport layer provides **end-to-end, process-to-process** communication.
> - **Two main protocols:** TCP (reliable, connection-oriented) and UDP (unreliable, connectionless).
> - **Multiplexing/Demultiplexing** is performed using **port numbers** (16-bit, range 0–65535).
> - **TCP 4-tuple:** `<Source IP, Source Port, Dest IP, Dest Port>` uniquely identifies a connection.
> - **UDP 2-tuple:** `<Dest IP, Dest Port>` is sufficient for demultiplexing (since UDP is stateless).
> - **TCP services:** connection setup (3-way handshake), reliable transfer (ARQ), flow control (rwnd), congestion control (cwnd), in-order delivery, full-duplex.
> - **UDP services:** best-effort delivery, low overhead (8-byte header), preserves message boundaries, supports multicast.
> - **Header sizes:** UDP = **8 bytes**, TCP = **20 bytes** (min), IP = **20 bytes** (IPv4).
> - **MTU** (Ethernet default) = **1500 bytes**; **MSS** = MTU − 40 = **1460 bytes**.
> - **Sender utilization (stop-and-wait):** $U = \dfrac{L/R}{L/R + RTT} = \dfrac{L}{L + R \cdot RTT}$.
> - **Bandwidth-Delay Product:** $BDP = R \times RTT$ — determines the number of unACKed segments needed for full utilization.
> - **Neither TCP nor UDP provides:** hard timing guarantees, hard throughput guarantees, or native security (TLS/DTLS layer is needed).
> - **Application mapping:** HTTP/SSH/FTP/email → TCP; DNS/SNMP/live video/VoIP/online gaming → UDP; HTTP/3 → QUIC (UDP-based).
> - **Well-known ports to memorize:** HTTP=**80**, HTTPS=**443**, FTP=**21**, SSH=**22**, Telnet=**23**, SMTP=**25**, DNS=**53**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transport Services Provided by the Internet
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMRT631 - NETWORK & DATA SECURITY > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Transport Services Provided by the Internet # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition The **Transport Layer** (Layer 4 of the OSI model and the Internet Protocol Suite) is the core of the entire network data-communication hierarchy. It provides **end-to-end communication services** for applications executing on different hosts, sitting directly above the network (IP) layer and below the application layer. In the context of the Internet, the two principal transport protocols are the **Transmission Control Protocol (TCP)** and the **User Datagram Protocol (UDP)**, standardized in **RFC 793** and **RFC 768** respectively. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *The transport layer is responsible for process-to-process delivery of the entire message, ensuring that data units (segments/datagrams) are delivered reliably (or unreliably, depending on the service requested) between specific application programs running on source and destination hosts.* ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The Indian Post vs. Speed Post Think of the **Internet** as a vast international postal network: - The **Network Layer (IP)** is like the **trucks, trains, and planes** that physically move a parcel from city A to city B. It only cares about getting each box to the *correct city/host*. - The **Transport Layer** is the **post office clerk** standing at the destination city. The trucks drop the parcels at the city, but the *clerk* is the one who looks at the **house number and apartment/flat number** (the **port number**) and walks it to the **right person** (the specific process/application). Now, within the transport layer, there are two famous "delivery companies": | Analogy | Internet Equivalent | Behaviour | |---|---|---| | **Registered Post with Acknowledgment** | **TCP** | Connection-oriented, reliable, ordered, slow but guaranteed. Sender waits for the receiver to sign. | | **Ordinary Letter Drop in a Post Box** | **UDP** | Connectionless, fire-and-forget, fast, no guarantee of delivery. | ## 1.3 The Core Services Provided Regardless of TCP or UDP, the transport layer provides these three fundamental services: 1. **Process-to-Process (End-to-End) Delivery:** Distinguished from host-to-host delivery of the IP layer. Uses **port numbers (0 to 65535)** to identify the specific application process. 2. **Multiplexing and Demultiplexing:** A single host with one IP address can run many applications (Chrome, WhatsApp, Zoom) simultaneously. Multiplexing combines data from multiple sockets at the sender; demultiplexing delivers incoming segments to the correct socket at the receiver. 3. **Error Control, Flow Control, and Congestion Control** (TCP only). > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Three Address Triples in Networking (KTU High-Yield):** > - **Physical / Data-Link Layer:** MAC Address (48-bit, e.g., `AA:BB:CC:DD:EE:FF`) > - **Network Layer:** IP Address (32-bit IPv4, e.g., `192.168.1.1`) > - **Transport Layer:** Port Number (16-bit, e.g., `80` for HTTP) ## 1.4 Port Number Categories A port number is a **16-bit unsigned integer**, giving a range of **0 to 65,535**. The **Internet Assigned Numbers Authority (IANA)** classifies them into three blocks: | Range | Category | Examples | |---|---|---| | **0 – 1023** | **Well-Known Ports** (require root/admin privileges) | HTTP (80), HTTPS (443), FTP (21), SSH (22), DNS (53), SMTP (25) | | **1024 – 49151** | **Registered Ports** (assigned by IANA to specific vendors) | MySQL (3306), PostgreSQL (5432), RDP (3389) | | **49152 – 65535** | **Dynamic / Ephemeral / Private Ports** (assigned by OS to client apps temporarily) | Used by your browser when connecting to Google | > [!NOTE] > **Socket = (IP Address, Port Number)** — A socket is the unique endpoint identifier of a network connection. For example, a TCP socket is the 4-tuple `(Source IP, Source Port, Destination IP, Destination Port)`. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 TCP (Transmission Control Protocol) — The "Reliable" Service TCP is a **connection-oriented, reliable, byte-stream** transport protocol. Before any data is exchanged, the sender and receiver perform a **3-way handshake** to establish a virtual connection. Every byte sent is acknowledged; lost packets are retransmitted; out-of-order packets are reordered. ### 2.1.1 Services Provided by TCP 1. **Connection-Oriented Service:** Uses the **3-Way Handshake (SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK)** before any payload transmission. 2. **Reliability:** Uses **sequence numbers**, **acknowledgements (ACKs)**, and **retransmission timers**. 3. **Ordered Data Transfer:** Each segment carries a sequence number; the receiver reassembles bytes in correct order. 4. **Flow Control:** Uses a **sliding window** mechanism. The receiver advertises its buffer size via the `rwnd` (receiver window) field in the TCP header, preventing a fast sender from overwhelming a slow receiver. Mathematically: `LastByteSent - LastByteAcked ≤ rwnd`. 5. **Congestion Control:** Uses `cwnd` (congestion window). The sender maintains `EffectiveWindow = min(cwnd, rwnd)`. Algorithms: **Slow Start, Congestion Avoidance, Fast Retransmit, Fast Recovery**. 6. **Full-Duplex Service:** Data can flow in both directions simultaneously over the same connection. 7. **Byte-Stream Service:** Application writes bytes; TCP decides segment boundaries. The application has no control over framing. ### 2.1.2 The 3-Way Handshake Logic - **Step 1:** Client → Server: `SYN` (seq = x). Opens the connection. - **Step 2:** Server → Client: `SYN + ACK` (seq = y, ack = x+1). Server agrees and synchronizes its own sequence number. - **Step 3:** Client → Server: `ACK` (seq = x+1, ack = y+1). Connection is now **ESTABLISHED**. ## 2.2 UDP (User Datagram Protocol) — The "Best-Effort" Service UDP is a **connectionless, unreliable, message-oriented** protocol. There is **no handshake**, **no acknowledgement**, **no retransmission**, and **no flow/congestion control**. The application gets the data immediately, and that's it. ### 2.2.1 Services Provided by UDP 1. **Connectionless Service:** No setup phase. The first datagram is sent immediately. 2. **Unreliable Service:** Lost datagrams are NOT retransmitted. No recovery is attempted. 3. **Message-Oriented:** UDP preserves application message boundaries. A `sendto()` of 1500 bytes produces a single datagram of 1500 bytes. 4. **No Flow / Congestion Control:** The sender can pump data as fast as it wants. Useful for real-time, loss-tolerant applications. 5. **Minimal Overhead:** Header is only **8 bytes** (vs. TCP's 20+ bytes). 6. **Optional Checksum:** A 16-bit one's complement checksum for basic error detection (corrupted datagrams are silently dropped). ### 2.2.2 Why UDP Exists (The "Why" of UDP) - **Latency over Reliability:** For VoIP, video streaming, DNS, and online gaming, retransmitting an old packet is *worse* than skipping it (the new packet is already in the user's hand). - **Stateless Nature:** Useful for protocols like **DNS** where a single query fits in one packet. - **Multicast/Broadcast:** TCP does not support multicast; UDP does. ## 2.3 Multiplexing & Demultiplexing — The Heart of Transport At the **sender**, multiple application processes (e.g., Chrome + WhatsApp) want to send data. Each creates a socket. The OS **multiplexes** data from these sockets, prepending the correct port number to each segment/datagram. At the **receiver**, the OS receives an incoming IP packet, extracts the destination port, looks it up in its **socket table**, and **demultiplexes** the segment to the correct socket (and thus the correct process). > [!NOTE] > **Connectionless (UDP) Demultiplexing:** Identified by the 2-tuple `(Dest IP, Dest Port)`. All clients using the same server port reach the same server socket. > **Connection-Oriented (TCP) Demultiplexing:** Identified by the 4-tuple `(Source IP, Source Port, Dest IP, Dest Port)`. Each unique connection gets its own socket. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield) > [!IMPORTANT] > Use `\vert` instead of `|` in tables to avoid markdown breakage. | Symbol | Formula / Concept | Meaning / Notes | |---|---|---| | **U** | $U = \dfrac{W}{1 + 2a}$ | Utilization of **Sliding Window** protocol (W = window size, a = Tp/Tt) | | **U** | $U = 1$ if $W \geq 2a + 1$ | Maximum utilization (sender never stops) | | **U** | $U = \dfrac{1}{1 + 2a}$ | Utilization of **Stop-and-Wait** protocol (W=1) | | **a** | $a = \dfrac{T_p}{T_t} = \dfrac{\text{Propagation Time}}{\text{Transmission Time}}$ | The "a" parameter — ratio of prop to trans time | | **T_p** | $T_p = \dfrac{d}{v}$ | Propagation time = distance / propagation speed (typically $v = 2 \times 10^8$ m/s in fiber) | | **T_t** | $T_t = \dfrac{L}{R}$ | Transmission time = packet length (bits) / bandwidth (bps) | | **BDP** | $BDP = R \times RTT$ (in bits) | Bandwidth-Delay Product — minimum window size for max utilization | | **RTT** | $RTT = 2 \times T_p$ | Round-Trip Time (ignoring processing delays) | | **Throughput** | $\text{Throughput} = \dfrac{L}{T_{total}} = \dfrac{L}{T_t + 2T_p}$ | Stop-and-wait effective throughput | | **TCP Header** | 20 bytes (min) | Source Port (2) + Dest Port (2) + Seq (4) + Ack (4) + Flags (2) + Window (2) + Checksum (2) + Urgent (2) | | **UDP Header** | 8 bytes | Source Port (2) + Dest Port (2) + Length (2) + Checksum (2) | | **Port Range** | $2^{16} = 65,536$ | Total possible port numbers (0 to 65,535) | ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **TCP** underpins the **World Wide Web (HTTP/HTTPS)**, **Email (SMTP, IMAP)**, **File Transfer (FTP, SFTP)**, and **Secure Shell (SSH)** — anywhere correctness matters. - **UDP** underpins **DNS lookups**, **Video Conferencing (WebRTC, Zoom, Google Meet)**, **Live Streaming (IPTV)**, **Online Gaming (multiplayer FPS)**, and **VoIP (SIP, RTP)** — anywhere freshness matters more than perfection. - **Multiplexing** is what makes a modern OS possible — without it, your laptop could only run one network app at a time. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Stop-and-Wait Protocol Utilization In the **Stop-and-Wait** protocol, the sender transmits one frame of $L$ bits and waits for an acknowledgement before sending the next. ### Step 1: Define the Transmission Time The time taken to push all $L$ bits of a frame onto the wire of bandwidth $R$ bps is: $$ T_t = \frac{L}{R} $$ ### Step 2: Define the Propagation Time The time for one bit to travel from sender to receiver across a link of distance $d$ meters at propagation speed $v$ m/s is: $$ T_p = \frac{d}{v} $$ ### Step 3: Total Cycle Time The total time from "start sending frame" to "receive ACK" is the transmission time, plus the propagation time to the receiver, plus the propagation time of the ACK back (assuming negligible ACK size), plus transmission time of the frame: $$ T_{total} = T_t + T_p + T_{ACK\_t} + T_{ACK\_p} \approx T_t + 2T_p $$ The factor of **2** appears because the signal has to traverse the link in *both* directions. ### Step 4: Utilization Definition Utilization $U$ is defined as the fraction of total time that is spent transmitting *useful* data (the original frame, not the idle waiting time): $$ U = \frac{\text{Time transmitting useful data}}{\text{Total cycle time}} = \frac{T_t}{T_t + 2T_p} $$ ### Step 5: Introduce the parameter $a$ Dividing numerator and denominator by $T_t$ and defining $a = T_p / T_t$: $$ U = \frac{1}{1 + 2a} $$ ### Step 6: Limiting Cases - If $a \to 0$ (very fast link, very small propagation): $U \to 1$ (perfect utilization). - If $a \to \infty$ (very long link, small frames): $U \to 0$ (terrible utilization — sender spends all its time waiting). ## 3.2 Derivation: Sliding Window Protocol Utilization In a **Sliding Window** protocol, the sender can transmit up to $W$ frames before needing an ACK. ### Step 1: Total Time to Send $W$ Frames The sender can continuously transmit $W$ frames back-to-back. The time to push all $W$ frames is $W \times T_t$. After the last bit of the last frame arrives at the receiver (taking $T_p$ more), the ACK comes back (another $T_p$): $$ T_{total} = W \cdot T_t + 2T_p $$ ### Step 2: Utilization Only the actual transmission of useful data matters ($W \times T_t$): $$ U = \frac{W \cdot T_t}{W \cdot T_t + 2T_p} $$ ### Step 3: Substitute $a = T_p / T_t$ Divide numerator and denominator by $T_t$: $$ U = \frac{W}{W + 2a} $$ ### Step 4: Critical Window Size If $W \geq 2a + 1$, the sender can fill the entire "pipe" of in-flight bits. The ACK arrives *before* the sender runs out of window, so: $$ U = 1 \quad \text{(if } W \geq 2a + 1 \text{)} $$ Otherwise, $U = \dfrac{W}{W + 2a}$. ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style) > **Problem:** A channel has a bandwidth $R = 1$ Mbps, a one-way propagation delay $T_p = 20$ ms, and frames of length $L = 2000$ bits. Compute utilization for: (a) Stop-and-Wait, (b) Sliding Window with $W = 7$. ### Part (a): Stop-and-Wait $$ T_t = \frac{L}{R} = \frac{2000 \text{ bits}}{10^6 \text{ bps}} = 2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 2 \text{ ms} $$ $$ a = \frac{T_p}{T_t} = \frac{20 \text{ ms}}{2 \text{ ms}} = 10 $$ $$ U_{SnW} = \frac{1}{1 + 2a} = \frac{1}{1 + 20} = \frac{1}{21} \approx 0.0476 \approx \mathbf{4.76\%} $$ **Interpretation:** 95% of the time the channel is **idle**! Stop-and-wait is catastrophic on long-fat networks. ### Part (b): Sliding Window, $W = 7$ Since $W = 7 < 2a + 1 = 21$, the window is too small to fill the pipe: $$ U_{SW} = \frac{W}{W + 2a} = \frac{7}{7 + 20} = \frac{7}{27} \approx 0.2593 \approx \mathbf{25.93\%} $$ **Better, but still not perfect.** The required window would be $W \geq 21$ to achieve $U = 100\%$. ## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: TCP vs UDP | Feature | **TCP** | **UDP** | |---|---|---| | Connection Setup | Yes (3-way handshake) | No (connectionless) | | Reliability | Guaranteed (ACKs + Retransmission) | Not guaranteed (best-effort) | | Ordering | In-order delivery | No ordering | | Flow Control | Yes (rwnd sliding window) | No | | Congestion Control | Yes (cwnd, Slow Start, AIMD) | No | | Header Size | **20 bytes** (minimum) | **8 bytes** | | Speed / Overhead | Slower, heavier | Faster, lighter | | Multicast Support | No | Yes | | Use Cases | HTTP, FTP, Email, SSH, Banking | DNS, Video, Gaming, VoIP, SNMP | | Data Unit | Segment / Byte stream | Datagram / Message | | State at Sender | Stateful (maintains connection state) | Stateless (no connection memory) | ## 3.5 Bandwidth-Delay Product (BDP) Derivation The **BDP** represents the number of bits that can be "in flight" inside the network at any moment: $$ BDP = R \times RTT $$ where $R$ is bandwidth (bps) and $RTT = 2 \times T_p$ is the round-trip time (seconds). ### Example For $R = 1$ Gbps and $RTT = 100$ ms (typical transcontinental link): $$ BDP = 10^9 \text{ bps} \times 0.1 \text{ s} = 10^8 \text{ bits} = 100 \text{ Mbits} \approx 12.5 \text{ MB} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Insight:** A TCP sender must have a window size of *at least* BDP bits to fully utilize the link. This is why the TCP window field was later expanded from 16 bits (max 64 KB) to a 32-bit scaled window in **RFC 1323** — to support high-bandwidth, long-distance links. # 4.
