KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **"Differentiate between TCP and UDP with respect to connection establishment, reliability, and header size."** **Model Answer (3 Marks — Examiner's Key):** | Parameter | TCP | UDP | |---|---|---| | Connection | Connection-oriented (3-way handshake) | Connectionless (no setup) | | Reliability | Reliable (ACK + Retransmission) | Unreliable (best-effort) | | Header Size | 20 bytes (minimum) | 8 bytes | **(1 mark for each correct row).** ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024] **"What is a socket? Explain the concept of multiplexing and demultiplexing at the transport layer."** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** - **Socket (1 mark):** A socket is the endpoint of a two-way communication link between two programs running on a network. It is uniquely identified by a 4-tuple `(Source IP, Source Port, Destination IP, Destination Port)` in TCP, and a 2-tuple in UDP. - **Multiplexing (1 mark):** At the sender, the OS gathers data from multiple application sockets, attaches the appropriate port number, and forwards it down to the network layer. - **Demultiplexing (1 mark):** At the receiver, the OS examines the destination port number from the incoming segment/datagram and delivers the payload to the correct socket (and therefore the correct application process). --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice) > **Question A and Question B are fully independent options. Attempt ANY ONE.** --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Deep TCP Analysis* [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Part (a)** Explain the services provided by the **Transmission Control Protocol (TCP)** with reference to the 3-way handshake, flow control, and congestion control. **(7 marks)** **Part (b)** A channel has bandwidth **1 Mbps**, one-way propagation delay **20 ms**, and frame size **2000 bits**. - (i) Compute the **utilization** if Stop-and-Wait ARQ is used. **(3 marks)** - (ii) Compute the **utilization** if Sliding Window ARQ with $W = 7$ is used. **(2 marks)** - (iii) What is the **minimum window size** to achieve 100% utilization? **(2 marks)** #### Model Solution **Part (a) — TCP Services (7 Marks):** 1. **Connection-Oriented Service (2 marks):** TCP establishes a logical connection using the **3-way handshake**: - Client → Server: `SYN (seq = x)` - Server → Client: `SYN + ACK (seq = y, ack = x+1)` - Client → Server: `ACK (seq = x+1, ack = y+1)` - After this, the connection state becomes `ESTABLISHED`. 2. **Reliable Data Transfer (2 marks):** TCP uses **sequence numbers** for every byte, **cumulative acknowledgements**, a **retransmission timeout (RTO)** based on the **Jacobson/Karels RTT estimation algorithm**, and **fast retransmit** on three duplicate ACKs. 3. **Flow Control (1.5 marks):** The receiver advertises its available buffer space in the `rwnd` (receiver window) field of every ACK. The sender enforces: `LastByteSent - LastByteAcked ≤ rwnd`. This protects a slow receiver from being overwhelmed. 4. **Congestion Control (1.5 marks):** The sender maintains a `cwnd` (congestion window) and uses `EffectiveWindow = min(cwnd, rwnd)`. Algorithms: **Slow Start** (exponential growth), **Congestion Avoidance** (linear growth using AIMD), **Fast Retransmit/Recovery**. **Part (b) — Numerical (7 Marks):** Given: $R = 10^6$ bps, $T_p = 20$ ms, $L = 2000$ bits. **(i) Stop-and-Wait Utilization (3 marks):** - $T_t = L/R = 2000 / 10^6 = 2$ ms - $a = T_p / T_t = 20 / 2 = 10$ - $U = \dfrac{1}{1 + 2a} = \dfrac{1}{1 + 20} = \dfrac{1}{21} \approx 0.0476 = \mathbf{4.76\%}$ **(ii) Sliding Window, $W = 7$ (2 marks):** - Since $W = 7 < 2a + 1 = 21$: - $U = \dfrac{W}{W + 2a} = \dfrac{7}{7 + 20} = \dfrac{7}{27} \approx 0.2593 = \mathbf{25.93\%}$ **(iii) Minimum Window for 100% Utilization (2 marks):** - $W_{min} = 2a + 1 = 2(10) + 1 = \mathbf{21}$ --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *UDP & Port-Based Services* [KTU University Exam – June 2024] **Part (a)** Explain the **transport layer services** provided by the Internet. With a neat diagram, describe the **multiplexing and demultiplexing** operations. **(7 marks)** **Part (b)** With reference to **port numbers**: - (i) List the **three ranges** of port numbers with one example each. **(3 marks)** - (ii) Explain why **UDP is preferred over TCP** for **DNS, VoIP, and live video streaming** with suitable justifications. **(4 marks)** #### Model Solution **Part (a) — Transport Services & Mux/Demux (7 Marks):** The transport layer provides the following services to the application layer above it: 1. **Process-to-Process Delivery (1 mark):** Unlike IP's host-to-host delivery, transport layer delivers the message to the correct *process* using **port numbers (0 to 65535)**. 2. **Connection-Oriented vs. Connectionless Service (1.5 marks):** TCP provides a connection-oriented, reliable service (with handshake, ACKs, retransmissions). UDP provides a connectionless, unreliable service (no setup, no ACKs). 3. **Reliability, Flow Control, and Congestion Control (1.5 marks):** TCP implements all three; UDP implements none. 4. **Multiplexing and Demultiplexing (3 marks):** - At the **sender**, multiple application processes (web browser, email client, FTP) write to their respective sockets. The OS multiplexes these into segments/datagrams by attaching the **source port** and **destination port** to each, then hands them down to IP. - At the **receiver**, IP delivers a packet to the host. The OS examines the **destination port** in the transport header, looks it up in its socket table, and demultiplexes the data to the corresponding application process. - *Diagram:* (See the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.3 of these notes.) **Part (b) — Ports and UDP Justification (7 Marks):** **(i) Port Number Ranges (3 marks):** | Range | Category | Example | |---|---|---| | 0 – 1023 | Well-Known Ports | HTTP (80), FTP (21), DNS (53) | | 1024 – 49151 | Registered Ports | MySQL (3306), RDP (3389) | | 49152 – 65535 | Dynamic / Ephemeral | Assigned by OS to client apps | **(ii) Why UDP is Preferred (4 marks — 1.5 + 1.5 + 1):** - **DNS (1.5 marks):** A DNS query and its response both fit inside a single small packet (well under 512 bytes). The overhead of a TCP 3-way handshake (1 extra RTT of latency) is *worse* than the negligible cost of a possible retransmission. UDP delivers the answer in **1 RTT** versus TCP's **2 RTTs**. - **VoIP / Video Conferencing (1.5 marks):** Real-time audio/video is highly **delay-sensitive** but **loss-tolerant**. If a packet is lost, retransmitting it would arrive *too late* — the receiver has already moved on. It is far better to *skip* the lost packet (and let codecs interpolate) than to wait 200 ms for a retransmission, which would cause audible glitches and video freezes. - **Live Video Streaming (1 mark):** Same logic as VoIP. Smooth, continuous playback is more important than the integrity of every single frame. Additionally, UDP supports **multicast** (sending one stream to many subscribers, used in IPTV), which TCP fundamentally cannot do. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in RTT:** Many students write $T_{total} = T_t + T_p$. This is wrong. The ACK must also travel back, so it is $T_t + 2T_p$. Losing 1–2 marks here is the most common error. > 2. **Confusing "socket" with "port":** A **port** is a 16-bit number. A **socket** is a *combination* of IP and port (or even the full 4-tuple in TCP). Examiners deduct marks for this. > 3. **Writing "UDP is unreliable, so it is bad":** UDP is *unreliable* by *design*. State this explicitly: "UDP provides an unreliable service **because the application trades reliability for low latency**." > 4. **Misremembering the header size:** UDP = **8 bytes**, TCP = **20 bytes minimum**. Not 16 and 24, not 4 and 8. > 5. **Skipping the diagram in Part (a):** A "neat diagram" is explicitly asked. Mux/Demux diagrams from Section 4.3 will earn full marks. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:** - **The transport layer provides process-to-process delivery**, distinguishing itself from IP's host-to-host delivery through the use of **port numbers**. - **TCP = Reliable, Connection-Oriented, Byte-Stream, Full-Duplex.** Used for HTTP, FTP, Email, SSH. - **UDP = Unreliable, Connectionless, Message-Oriented.** Used for DNS, VoIP, Gaming, Live Streaming. - **TCP header = 20 bytes (minimum).** UDP header = **8 bytes.** - **TCP uses a 3-Way Handshake: SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK.** - **TCP uses two windows:** `rwnd` (receiver-advertised) for flow control and `cwnd` (congestion window) for congestion control. Effective window = `min(cwnd, rwnd)`. - **Port numbers are 16-bit (0 to 65,535).** Well-Known: 0–1023. Registered: 1024–49151. Ephemeral: 49152–65535. - **A socket = (IP, Port)** for UDP, and **(Src IP, Src Port, Dst IP, Dst Port)** for TCP. - **Multiplexing** = combining data from multiple sockets at the sender. **Demultiplexing** = delivering to the correct socket at the receiver. - **Stop-and-Wait Utilization:** $U = \dfrac{1}{1 + 2a}$, where $a = T_p / T_t$. - **Sliding Window Utilization:** $U = \dfrac{W}{W + 2a}$ (if $W < 2a+1$); $U = 1$ (if $W \geq 2a+1$). - **Bandwidth-Delay Product (BDP) = $R \times RTT$** — gives the minimum window for full link utilization. - **Propagation time:** $T_p = d/v$ (typical $v = 2 \times 10^8$ m/s in fiber). **Transmission time:** $T_t = L/R$. - **TCP congestion control algorithms:** Slow Start, Congestion Avoidance, Fast Retransmit, Fast Recovery. - **UDP exists for latency-sensitive, loss-tolerant applications** — not because it is "worse" than TCP, but because it makes a different design trade-off. - **Standard KTU memory aid: "TCP = Telephone Call. UDP = Dropping a Letter in a Post Box."**

topics.title: Application-Layer Protocols
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMRT631 - NETWORK & DATA SECURITY > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Application-Layer Protocols ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The **Application Layer** is the seventh and topmost layer of the **OSI (Open Systems Interconnection) reference model** and the fifth layer of the **TCP/IP protocol suite**. It serves as the interface through which end-user processes, software applications, and services interact with the underlying network. *Application-layer protocols* are the standardized rules, message formats, and conversational procedures that govern how two software applications (running on different network endpoints) exchange data meaningfully. In the KTU 2024 syllabus context (Module 1 of PEMRT631 – Network & Data Security), this topic specifically addresses the **trust, authentication, and confidentiality requirements** of protocols such as **HTTP, HTTPS, SMTP, POP3, IMAP, FTP, SFTP, DNS, SNMP, Telnet, and SSH**, focusing on *where and how* these protocols are vulnerable to attacks and *how* their secured variants (e.g., HTTPS over TLS, SFTP over SSH) mitigate them. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEMRT631 – Module 1):** > *"Application Layer: HTTP, HTTPS, SSH, FTP, SMTP, POP3, IMAP, DNS, SNMP – vulnerabilities and security mechanisms."* ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine the internet as a **massive multinational postal system**. The lower layers (Physical, Data Link, Network, Transport) act as the **postal trucks, sorting hubs, and tracking numbers** — they ensure the *envelope* reaches the correct mailbox. The **Application Layer is the letter inside the envelope**: the actual message, the language used (English, Malayalam, French), and the agreed-upon format (business letter, invoice, greeting card). For example, when you open Gmail in your browser: - The **envelope (Transport)** is TCP, ensuring reliable delivery. - The **mailbox address (Network)** is the IP address of Google's mail server. - The **letter inside (Application)** is written in **IMAP/SMTP** protocol — a language that Gmail's mail server and your app both understand. ### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Port Numbers > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Engineering Constants for KTU Exams:** > - **Standard well-known port range:** **0 – 1023** (assigned by IANA) > - **Registered ports:** **1024 – 49151** > - **Dynamic/Private ports:** **49152 – 65535** > - **Default HTTPS port:** **443** | **Default HTTP port:** **80** > - **DNS typically uses UDP/53**, falls back to TCP/53 for large responses ### 1.4 Why Application-Layer Security Matters The application layer is the **closest layer to the end user**, and therefore the **largest attack surface** in any networked system. Most cyberattacks — phishing, SQL injection, cross-site scripting, credential theft, email spoofing — originate here. A KTU student must understand that *securing the lower layers alone is insufficient*; **end-to-end application-level controls are mandatory**. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Layered Operating Logic The Application Layer does not exist in isolation. Every application-layer transaction follows this structured flow: 1. **Process-to-Process Channel Establishment** The application invokes a *socket* (combination of IP address + port number) to communicate. 2. **Request Message Construction** The protocol's grammar (e.g., HTTP `GET /index.html HTTP/1.1`) is used to build the message. 3. **Hand-off to Transport Layer** TCP or UDP is selected. For example, HTTP uses TCP for reliability; DNS uses UDP for speed. 4. **Server-Side Parsing** The receiving daemon (e.g., `httpd`, `named`, `postfix`) interprets the request. 5. **Response Generation** The server emits a standardized reply (e.g., `HTTP/1.1 200 OK`). 6. **Session Termination** Either a clean four-way TCP close or an abrupt timeout. ### 2.2 Detailed Protocol Breakdown (Why + How) #### 2.2.1 HTTP (HyperText Transfer Protocol) - **Why:** Stateless request-response protocol for fetching web resources. - **How:** Client sends `GET`, `POST`, `PUT`, `DELETE` methods; server replies with status codes (`200 OK`, `404 Not Found`, `500 Internal Server Error`). - **Port:** **TCP/80** - **Weakness:** Plaintext — credentials, cookies, session tokens are exposed. #### 2.2.2 HTTPS (HTTP over TLS/SSL) - **Why:** Confidentiality, integrity, and authentication for web traffic. - **How:** Wraps HTTP inside a **TLS handshake** (asymmetric + symmetric cryptography). - **Port:** **TCP/443** - **Strength:** Uses X.509 certificates issued by Certificate Authorities (CAs). #### 2.2.3 SSH (Secure Shell) - **Why:** Encrypted replacement for Telnet; provides secure remote command execution. - **How:** Uses **public-key cryptography** for host authentication and key exchange, then symmetric ciphers (AES) for data. - **Port:** **TCP/22** #### 2.2.4 FTP (File Transfer Protocol) - **Why:** Bulk file upload/download. - **How:** Uses **two channels** — *Control channel* (port 21) for commands and *Data channel* (port 20) for file content. - **Weakness:** Plaintext credentials; vulnerable to **sniffing, bounce attacks, and brute force**. #### 2.2.5 SFTP (SSH File Transfer Protocol) - **Why:** Secure file transfer — *not* FTP-over-SSL, but a completely different protocol running over SSH. - **Port:** **TCP/22** #### 2.2.6 SMTP (Simple Mail Transfer Protocol) - **Why:** Push protocol for *sending* email between mail servers (MTA to MTA). - **Port:** **TCP/25** (plain), **TCP/587** (authenticated submission) #### 2.2.7 POP3 (Post Office Protocol v3) - **Why:** Pull protocol — downloads mail to local client and typically deletes from server. - **Port:** **TCP/110** #### 2.2.8 IMAP (Internet Message Access Protocol) - **Why:** Synchronizes mail across multiple devices; mail remains on server. - **Port:** **TCP/143** #### 2.2.9 DNS (Domain Name System) - **Why:** Translates human-readable names (e.g., `ktu.edu.in`) to IP addresses. - **How:** Hierarchical distributed database. Uses **UDP/53** for small queries, **TCP/53** for zone transfers (>512 bytes). - **Critical Vulnerability:** **DNS Spoofing / Cache Poisoning** — attacker injects forged DNS records. #### 2.2.10 SNMP (Simple Network Management Protocol) - **Why:** Network device monitoring and configuration (routers, switches). - **Port:** **UDP/161** (queries), **UDP/162** (traps) - **Versions:** SNMPv1/v2c use plaintext *community strings* (default: `public`, `private`) — extremely insecure. **SNMPv3** adds authentication (HMAC-MD5/SHA) and encryption (DES/AES). ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Quick Reference — Master this table before any exam.** | Protocol | Transport | Default Port | Direction | Encrypted Variant | Primary Attack | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | HTTP | TCP | 80 | Request/Response | HTTPS (443) | MITM, sniffing | | HTTPS | TCP | 443 | Request/Response | Native (TLS) | TLS stripping, cert forgery | | FTP | TCP | 21 (control), 20 (data) | Bidirectional | SFTP/FTPS | Brute force, bounce attack | | SSH | TCP | 22 | Remote shell | Native | Weak key exchange | | SMTP | TCP | 25, 587 | Push (server↔server) | SMTPS (465) | Email spoofing, relay | | POP3 | TCP | 110 | Pull (download) | POP3S (995) | Credential theft | | IMAP | TCP | 143 | Pull (sync) | IMAPS (993) | Credential theft | | DNS | UDP/TCP | 53 | Query/Response | DoH (443), DoT (853) | Cache poisoning | | SNMP | UDP | 161/162 | Poll/Trap | SNMPv3 | Community string leak | | Telnet | TCP | 23 | Remote shell | SSH (22) | Plaintext capture | ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Web Application Firewalls (WAFs)** operate at this layer to filter SQL injection and XSS. - **Email gateways** (e.g., Proofpoint, Microsoft Defender) inspect SMTP traffic for malware. - **DNS Security Extensions (DNSSEC)** cryptographically sign DNS records to prevent spoofing. - **Network Operations Centers (NOCs)** rely on SNMPv3 for safe device telemetry. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Analytical Matrix — DNS Resolution Walk-through The most important application-layer flow a KTU student must derive is the **iterative DNS resolution process**. Below is the exhaustive step-by-step trace. **Scenario:** A user types `www.ktu.edu.in` into a browser at IP `203.0.113.5`. **Step 1 — Local Cache Check** The OS resolver first inspects its local cache. If a valid (non-expired) record exists, resolution ends here. **Step 2 — Recursive Query to ISP Resolver** If absent, the OS sends a **recursive DNS query** to the ISP's recursive resolver (e.g., `8.8.8.8`). **Step 3 — Root Server Referral** The recursive resolver queries a **Root DNS Server** (13 logical clusters worldwide). The root server *does not know* the answer but replies with a referral to the **TLD server** for `.in`. **Step 4 — TLD Server Referral** The resolver now queries the `.in` TLD server, which refers it to the **authoritative name server** for `ktu.edu.in`. **Step 5 — Authoritative Answer** The authoritative server returns the **A record**: `www.ktu.edu.in → 147.100.10.20`. **Step 6 — Caching & Response** The resolver caches the record (governed by the **TTL — Time To Live** field) and returns the IP to the client. **Step 7 — Connection Establishment** The browser now opens a TCP three-way handshake to `147.100.10.20` on port 80 (HTTP) or 443 (HTTPS). ### 3.2 Analytical Math — TTL and Cache Expiry DNS caching is governed by the **TTL field** in the resource record. The remaining lifetime at any time $t$ is: $$ \text{TTL}_{\text{remaining}}(t) \;=\; \text{TTL}_{\text{original}} \;-\; (t - t_{\text{arrival}}) $$ where: - $\text{TTL}_{\text{original}}$ is the value set by the authoritative server (typically **86400 seconds = 24 hours** for A records). - $t_{\text{arrival}}$ is the timestamp when the record entered the cache. - The record is purged when $\text{TTL}_{\text{remaining}}(t) \le 0$. **Worked Example (KTU-style calculation):** A resolver caches an A record at `t_arrival = 100 s` with `TTL_original = 300 s`. At `t = 250 s`, calculate the remaining lifetime. $$ \text{TTL}_{\text{remaining}}(250) \;=\; 300 \;-\; (250 - 100) $$ $$ \text{TTL}_{\text{remaining}}(250) \;=\; 300 \;-\; 150 \;=\; 150 \text{ seconds} $$ The record remains valid for another 150 seconds. ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — TLS 1.3 Handshake (HTTPS Foundation) A frequent KTU question is: *"Explain how HTTPS establishes a secure session."* The **TLS 1.3 handshake** is the rigorous derivation. **Step 1 — ClientHello** The client sends a `ClientHello` message containing: - Supported cipher suites (e.g., `TLS_AES_256_GCM_SHA384`) - A **client random** nonce - **Key share** for X25519 elliptic-curve Diffie-Hellman **Step 2 — ServerHello + Key Share** The server selects a cipher suite, sends its **server random**, and transmits its **ephemeral ECDH public key**. **Step 3 — Key Derivation** Both sides compute the **shared secret**: $$ S_{\text{shared}} \;=\; \text{X25519}(\text{Priv}_{\text{client}},\text{Pub}_{\text{server}}) \;=\; \text{X25519}(\text{Priv}_{\text{server}},\text{Pub}_{\text{client}}) $$ **Step 4 — Symmetric Session Key Generation** Using a HKDF (HMAC-based Key Derivation Function): $$ K_{\text{session}} \;=\; \text{HKDF-Extract}(S_{\text{shared}}, \text{Salt} \;=\; \text{ClientHello} \Vert \text{ServerHello}) $$ **Step 5 — Encrypted Application Data** All subsequent HTTP traffic is encrypted using $K_{\text{session}}$ with **AES-256-GCM**. **Step 6 — Server Authentication** The server's X.509 certificate (signed by a trusted CA) proves its identity. The client verifies the certificate chain up to a trusted root. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Insight:** TLS 1.3 reduced the handshake from **2-RTT to 1-RTT**, eliminating vulnerable legacy ciphers (RC4, MD5, SHA-1). ### 3.4 Analytical Comparison — POP3 vs IMAP A 14-mark KTU question often asks for a comparative study. The mathematical *sync overhead* can be derived: Let $M$ = total messages on server, $N$ = messages read on a new device. $$ \text{POP3 Traffic} \;\approx\; M \cdot S_{\text{avg}} \quad (\text{downloads all}) $$ $$ \text{IMAP Traffic} \;\approx\; N \cdot S_{\text{header}} + \sum_{i=1}^{N} S_{\text{body},i} \quad (\text{syncs only requested}) $$ where $S_{\text{avg}}$ is the average message size and $S_{\text{header}}$ is the header-only size (~2 KB). For mobile users where $N \ll M$, **IMAP is bandwidth-efficient** and preserves server-side state. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Diagram: Application Layer in the TCP/IP Stack ```mermaid flowchart TB app["APPLICATION LAYER
HTTP HTTPS FTP SSH SMTP POP3 IMAP DNS SNMP"] trans["TRANSPORT LAYER
TCP and UDP"] net["NETWORK LAYER
IP addressing and routing"] link["DATA LINK LAYER
Ethernet and Wi Fi"] phy["PHYSICAL LAYER
Cables radio signals"] app --> trans --> net --> link --> phy phy -.-> link -.-> net -.-> trans -.-> app ``` ### 4.2 Diagram: DNS Resolution Flow (Recursive vs Iterative) ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Client participant Resolver as ISP Resolver participant Root as Root Server participant TLD as TLD Server participant Auth as Authoritative Server Client->>Resolver: Query www.ktu.edu.in Resolver->>Root: Query (iterative) Root-->>Resolver: Refer to .in TLD Resolver->>TLD: Query (iterative) TLD-->>Resolver: Refer to ktu.edu.in NS Resolver->>Auth: Query (iterative) Auth-->>Resolver: A record 147.100.10.20 Resolver-->>Client: IP address 147.100.10.20 Client->>Resolver: Connection established ``` ### 4.3 Diagram: HTTPS / TLS 1.3 Handshake Sequence ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Browser participant Server Browser->>Server: ClientHello + Key Share + Cipher Suites Server-->>Browser: ServerHello + Server Key Share + Certificate Note over Browser,Server: Both compute Shared Secret via ECDH Browser->>Server: Finished (encrypted) Server-->>Browser: Finished (encrypted) Note over Browser,Server: Secure HTTP tunnel established Browser->>Server: Encrypted HTTP GET request Server-->>Browser: Encrypted HTTP 200 OK response ``` ### 4.4 Diagram: Email Delivery Path (SMTP + POP3/IMAP) ```mermaid flowchart LR SenderApp["SENDER MAIL CLIENT
Outlook or Gmail"] SMTPServer["SENDER SMTP RELAY
Port 587 or 465"] MX["RECIPIENT MX SERVER
Port 25"] Mailbox["MAILBOX STORE
Server side mail"] IMAP["IMAP SERVER
Port 143 or 993"] Recipient["RECIPIENT CLIENT
Thunderbird or mobile"] SenderApp --> SMTPServer --> MX --> Mailbox --> IMAP --> Recipient ``` ### 4.5 Diagram: FTP Dual-Channel Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB FTPC["FTP CLIENT"] Ctrl["CONTROL CHANNEL
TCP Port 21
Commands and replies"] Data["DATA CHANNEL
TCP Port 20
File payload"] FTPS["FTP SERVER"] FTPC <-->|Commands| Ctrl Ctrl <--> FTPS FTPC <-->|File bytes| Data Data <--> FTPS ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Differentiate between HTTP and HTTPS. Why is HTTPS considered mandatory for modern web applications? **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **HTTP (HyperText Transfer Protocol)** operates on **TCP port 80** and transmits data in **plaintext**. This means usernames, passwords, session cookies, and credit-card numbers can be intercepted by an attacker performing packet sniffing on the same network. - **HTTPS (HTTP Secure)** operates on **TCP port 443** and encapsulates HTTP traffic inside a **TLS (Transport Layer Security)** tunnel. The TLS handshake establishes a shared symmetric session key using asymmetric cryptography (RSA or ECDHE), and the server's identity is verified via an **X.509 digital certificate** issued by a trusted Certificate Authority (CA). - HTTPS provides **confidentiality** (encryption), **integrity** (HMAC), and **authentication** (certificates). - Modern applications **must use HTTPS** because browsers flag HTTP sites as "Not Secure", and attackers can hijack sessions via **session hijacking** or inject malicious scripts. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** Explain the purpose of the SNMP community string and why SNMPv3 is preferred over SNMPv1. **Model Answer (3 marks):** - The **community string** in SNMPv1/v2c acts as a *plaintext password*. The default values are `public` (read-only) and `private` (read-write). - It is transmitted **in cleartext** over UDP/161, making it trivially sniffable on any LAN. - **SNMPv3** introduces three security features: **message integrity (HMAC-MD5/SHA)**, **authentication (username + key)**, and **privacy (DES/AES encryption)**. - Therefore, SNMPv3 is preferred because it prevents unauthorized configuration changes and protects telemetry data. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the working of the **Domain Name System (DNS)**. List any **four security vulnerabilities** of DNS. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the **TLS 1.3 handshake** used in HTTPS with a sequence diagram. How does TLS 1.3 improve upon TLS 1.2? **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) — 7 marks** *Working of DNS (5 marks):* - DNS is a **hierarchical, distributed database** that maps domain names to IP addresses. - The structure has three levels: **Root servers** (.), **TLD servers** (.com, .in, .edu), and **Authoritative servers** (ktu.edu.in). - When a user types `www.ktu.edu.in`, the OS resolver first checks its **local cache** (Step 1). - If not found, it sends a **recursive query** to the ISP's DNS resolver (Step 2). - The resolver performs **iterative queries** — first to the **Root server** (Step 3), which refers it to the **`.in` TLD server** (Step 4). - The TLD refers to the **authoritative server for `ktu.edu.in`** (Step 5), which returns the **A record** mapping the name to an IP address. - The resolver **caches** the response (governed by the TTL field) and returns the IP to the client (Step 6). - The browser then establishes a TCP connection to the returned IP. *Four Security Vulnerabilities (2 marks):* 1. **DNS Cache Poisoning / Spoofing** — attacker injects forged records into resolver cache. 2. **DNS Amplification (DDoS)** — small query generates large response; abused for volumetric attacks. 3. **DNS Tunneling** — exfiltrates data or establishes covert C2 channels via TXT records. 4. **Zone Transfer Hijacking** — unauthorized AXFR requests expose the entire DNS zone. **(b) — 7 marks** *TLS 1.3 Handshake (5 marks):* - **Message 1 — ClientHello:** Client sends its supported cipher suites (e.g., `TLS_AES_256_GCM_SHA384`), a 32-byte client random, and its **X25519 ECDH key share**. - **Message 2 — ServerHello + Certificate:** Server selects a cipher suite, sends its server random, its ECDH key share, and its **X.509 certificate chain**. - **Key Computation:** Both sides compute the **shared secret** using ECDH: $$S = \text{X25519}(\text{Priv}_{\text{client}}, \text{Pub}_{\text{server}})$$ Both sides derive the session key via HKDF. - **Finished Messages:** Client and server exchange `Finished` MACs to confirm the handshake. - **Encrypted Application Data:** All subsequent HTTP messages are encrypted with AES-256-GCM. *Improvements over TLS 1.2 (2 marks):* - **1-RTT** handshake (vs 2-RTT) — faster page loads. - Removal of legacy ciphers (RC4, MD5, SHA-1, 3DES) — eliminates decades-old weaknesses. - Mandatory **forward secrecy** via ECDHE. - **0-RTT mode** for resumed sessions (with replay risk awareness). --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – June 2024] **(a)** Compare **POP3** and **IMAP** in detail. Which is more suitable for accessing email from multiple devices? Justify. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the **FTP protocol** with a diagram. List four security weaknesses of FTP and describe how **SFTP** addresses them. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) — 7 marks** | Feature | POP3 | IMAP | |---|---|---| | Default Port | 110 | 143 | | Encrypted Port | 995 (POP3S) | 993 (IMAPS) | | Direction | Download (pull) | Sync (bidirectional) | | Mail Storage | Local after download | Server-side | | Multi-device Support | Poor — downloads mark as read | Excellent — real-time sync | | Bandwidth | High on first sync | Low — fetches only requested parts | | Use Case | Single device, offline reading | Modern multi-device usage | **Justification:** IMAP is suitable because (1) mail remains on the server enabling access from phone, laptop, and web simultaneously; (2) only message headers are downloaded initially — bodies fetched on demand; (3) folder structure and read/unread flags are preserved across devices. **(b) — 7 marks** *FTP Working (3 marks):* FTP uses **two parallel TCP channels**: - **Control channel (port 21)** — carries commands (`USER`, `PASS`, `RETR`, `STOR`) and replies. - **Data channel (port 20)** — carries the actual file payload. - A typical flow: client connects to port 21, authenticates, issues `RETR filename`, and a separate data connection is opened for the file bytes. *Four FTP Security Weaknesses (2 marks):* 1. **Plaintext credentials** — username and password sent in cleartext on port 21. 2. **Plaintext data transfer** — file contents visible to any sniffer. 3. **Anonymous access** — misconfigured servers allow `anonymous` login. 4. **FTP Bounce Attack** — attacker uses the server as a proxy to scan/attack other hosts. *SFTP as a Solution (2 marks):* SFTP (SSH File Transfer Protocol) is **not FTP-over-SSL** — it is a completely different protocol running over the **SSH tunnel on port 22**. It provides: - **Server authentication** via public-key fingerprints. - **User authentication** via password or SSH keys. - **End-to-end encryption** of both credentials and file data. - **Data integrity** via HMAC. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read carefully:** > 1. Students often confuse **SFTP** with **FTPS**. SFTP is over SSH (port 22); FTPS is FTP over SSL (ports 989/990). Examiners *will* deduct marks for mixing these up. > 2. In DNS questions, students frequently forget to mention **TTL caching** — full marks require it. > 3. For HTTPS, students must mention **both asymmetric (key exchange) and symmetric (bulk data) cryptography**. Mentioning only AES is incomplete. > 4. Always write **port numbers** explicitly in protocol comparison answers. > 5. In handshake diagrams, forgetting the **server certificate verification step** is a common 1-mark loss. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - The **Application Layer** is the user-facing layer of both OSI and TCP/IP models — it defines **what** data is exchanged, not **how** it is transported. - **HTTP (80)** is plaintext; **HTTPS (443)** is HTTP wrapped in TLS for confidentiality, integrity, and authentication. - **FTP (21 control, 20 data)** is insecure; prefer **SFTP (22)** which runs over SSH. - **SSH (22)** is the modern secure replacement for Telnet (23) — uses public-key cryptography for authentication. - **SMTP (25/587)** pushes mail between servers; **POP3 (110)** downloads mail; **IMAP (143)** synchronizes mail across devices. - **DNS (53)** is the phonebook of the internet — its biggest threats are **cache poisoning** and **amplification DDoS**. - **SNMP (161/162)** must use **v3** in production — v1/v2c community strings are plaintext and dangerous. - Every cleartext protocol has a **secure variant** indicated by an "S" suffix (HTTPS, SFTP, SMTPS, POP3S, IMAPS) or a new acronym (SSH instead of Telnet). - **Always mention port numbers** in KTU protocol-based answers — they are expected. - **TLS 1.3** uses **1-RTT ECDHE handshake** + **AES-256-GCM** for bulk encryption — the modern standard. - **DNSSEC, DoH (DNS over HTTPS), and DoT (DNS over TLS)** are the modern hardening mechanisms for DNS. - **WAFs, email gateways, and SIEM systems** all operate at the application layer to detect modern threats.

topics.title: Guided and Unguided Transmission Media - Co-axial Cable
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT631 - NETWORK & DATA SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Guided and Unguided Transmission Media - Co-axial Cable

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Transmission Media — The Big Picture

In the **OSI Physical Layer**, information bits (0s and 1s) must travel from a source to a destination. The medium that carries these electromagnetic signals is called the **Transmission Media**. KTU 2024 Scheme categorizes transmission media into two broad classes:

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> **Transmission Media** is the physical path over which a signal propagates from the transmitter to the receiver. It determines the **bandwidth, attenuation, noise immunity, and maximum data rate** of any communication link.

| Class | Nature | Examples | KTU 2024 Status |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **Guided (Wired / Bounded)** | Signal confined inside a physical conduit | Twisted Pair, **Coaxial Cable**, Optical Fiber | High Weightage |
| **Unguided (Wireless / Unbounded)** | Signal propagates freely through atmosphere | Radio, Microwave, Satellite, Infrared | High Weightage |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** In **Module 1**, the focus is on **Guided Media** (Coaxial, Twisted Pair) and a brief introduction to **Unguided Media**. Coaxial cable is the **single most-asked guided media topic** in previous KTU University Examinations.

### 1.2 Coaxial Cable — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:**
> A **Coaxial Cable (Coax)** is a cylindrical, shielded guided transmission medium consisting of an inner solid conductor, surrounded by a dielectric insulator, an outer tubular conductor (shield), and an outer protective jacket — all sharing a common geometric axis (hence the name "co-axial").

### 1.3 Intuition: The "Straw Inside a Straw" Analogy

Think of a coaxial cable as a **drinking straw with a metal rod floating exactly in its center**:
- The **center metal rod** = the inner conductor carrying the signal.
- The **air / plastic gap** between the rod and the outer straw = the dielectric insulator.
- The **metallic straw itself** = the outer shield, which grounds noise and returns current.
- The **rubber coating** outside the straw = the PVC jacket protecting from environment.

**Why this design?** By making both conductors share the same axis, the electromagnetic field is **perfectly confined** between them. External noise cannot penetrate the outer shield, and the signal energy stays trapped inside — this is the fundamental engineering secret of coax.

### 1.4 Physical Structure (4 Concentric Layers)

Working from inside outward, a standard coaxial cable has **four** concentric layers:

1. **Inner Conductor** (Center Core) — solid copper or stranded copper-clad steel; carries the actual signal.
2. **Dielectric Insulator** — polyethylene (PE), foam PE, or Teflon; keeps the two conductors mechanically separated and electrically insulated; thickness determines characteristic impedance.
3. **Outer Conductor (Shield)** — braided copper mesh, aluminum foil, or both; provides the return path for current and blocks external EMI.
4. **Outer Jacket (Sheath)** — PVC or LSZH polymer; protects against physical damage, moisture, and chemicals.

> [!TIP]
> **Industry Standard Diameter:** The most common outer diameter for network-grade coax is approximately **6.15 mm (RG-6)** and **10.3 mm (RG-8/RG-11)**, with a characteristic impedance of **50 Ω** or **75 Ω**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Principle

When an electrical signal is applied between the inner and outer conductors:
- An **electric field (E)** is established radially between the conductors.
- A **magnetic field (H)** is created circumferentially around the inner conductor.
- These fields combine to form a **Transverse Electromagnetic (TEM) wave** that propagates along the length of the cable.
- Because both fields stay **transverse** (perpendicular to the direction of travel), the wave is described as a TEM mode — the fundamental mode of propagation in coax.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Coaxial Design

| Design Feature | Engineering Reason |
|:---|:---|
| **Common axis geometry** | Produces perfect cylindrical symmetry → uniform field distribution → no radiation loss |
| **Outer shield (braided)** | Blocks external EMI by acting as a **Faraday Cage**; provides clean return path for current |
| **Dielectric spacer** | Maintains constant distance between conductors → keeps **characteristic impedance** stable with bending |
| **Solid center conductor** | Low DC resistance (R_dc) → minimum I²R heating loss |
| **Thick center conductor** | Skin effect at high frequencies → larger surface area → reduced AC resistance |

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — Memorize the following formulas. Every one of these is fair game for a 7 or 14-mark question.**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Standard Units |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | $Z_0 = \dfrac{60}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{b}{a}\right)$ | Characteristic Impedance of coax | Ohms (Ω) |
| 2 | $Z_0 = \dfrac{138}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}} \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{D}{d}\right)$ | Alternative form using common log | Ohms (Ω) |
| 3 | $v_p = \dfrac{c}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}}$ | Velocity of propagation in dielectric | m/s |
| 4 | $t_p = \dfrac{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}}{c}$ | Propagation delay per unit length | s/m |
| 5 | $A_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{in}}{P_{out}}\right)$ | Power attenuation (definition) | dB |
| 6 | $A_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{in}}{V_{out}}\right)$ | Voltage attenuation (definition) | dB |
| 7 | $\alpha_{dB/km} = \alpha_0 \sqrt{f} + \alpha_c$ | Frequency-dependent attenuation | dB/km |
| 8 | $\lambda = \dfrac{v_p}{f}$ | Wavelength inside the cable | m |
| 9 | $C = \dfrac{2\pi\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r}{\ln(b/a)}$ | Distributed capacitance per meter | F/m |
| 10 | $L = \dfrac{\mu_0}{2\pi}\ln(b/a)$ | Distributed inductance per meter | H/m |
| 11 | $BW = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \dfrac{v_p}{L_{max}} \cdot \dfrac{1}{N}$ | Coarse bandwidth-bound | Hz |

**Symbol legend used above:**
- $Z_0$ = characteristic impedance; $a$ = inner conductor radius; $b$ = inner radius of outer conductor; $D$ = outer diameter; $d$ = inner diameter; $\varepsilon_r$ = relative permittivity (dielectric constant); $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s; $f$ = frequency in Hz; $P$ = power; $V$ = voltage; $\alpha_0$ = skin-effect coefficient; $\alpha_c$ = conductor-loss coefficient.

### 2.4 Why $Z_0 = 50\ \Omega$ or $75\ \Omega$? — The Design Trade-off

For air-filled coax ($\varepsilon_r = 1$):

$$Z_0 = 60 \ln(b/a) = 138 \log_{10}(b/a)$$

- If $b/a \approx 3.6$, then $Z_0 \approx 50\ \Omega$ — **minimum total loss** (optimum balance of copper loss and dielectric loss). Used for **data networking** (Ethernet).
- If $b/a \approx 6.1$, then $Z_0 \approx 75\ \Omega$ — **minimum attenuation** (best signal reach). Used for **CATV / video / cable television** distribution.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for KTU Exam:**
> - **50 Ω → Networking (Baseband Ethernet)**
> - **75 Ω → Video / Cable TV (Broadband)**

### 2.5 Classification of Coaxial Cables

| Category | Cable Type | Application |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Baseband (50 Ω)** | 10Base-2 (Thinnet, RG-58) | Legacy Ethernet up to 185 m |
| **Baseband (50 Ω)** | 10Base-5 (Thicknet, RG-8) | Legacy Ethernet up to 500 m |
| **Broadband (75 Ω)** | RG-6, RG-59 | Cable TV, satellite, broadband internet |
| **Specialty** | RG-174, RG-178 | Low-power, short runs, RF instrumentation |

### 2.6 Connectors & Terminators

- **BNC (Bayonet Neill–Concelman):** Quick-connect, push-and-twist locking; the most common coax connector for Ethernet.
- **T-Connector:** Three-way BNC used in 10Base-2 to daisy-chain nodes.
- **N-Type Connector:** Threaded, weatherproof, used for thicknet and outdoor installations.
- **F-Type Connector:** Screw-on, used for TV / cable modem applications.
- **Terminator (50 Ω or 75 Ω resistor):** Must be attached to **both ends** of a bus topology cable to absorb signals and prevent **reflection** at cable ends.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Forgetting to place a **50 Ω terminator** at both ends of a 10Base-2 segment causes severe **signal reflection**, which corrupts every transmitted frame.

### 2.7 Attenuation — The Loss Mechanism

Attenuation in coax arises from:
1. **Conductor loss** — finite conductivity of copper causes I²R heating; increases as $\sqrt{f}$ due to the **skin effect**.
2. **Dielectric loss** — polarization losses in the insulator; increases linearly with frequency.
3. **Radiation loss** — negligible in well-designed coax (Faraday cage works).

Typical attenuation values for **RG-58 (50 Ω)** at room temperature:
- At **10 MHz:** ≈ 1.2 dB / 100 m
- At **100 MHz:** ≈ 4.5 dB / 100 m
- At **1 GHz:** ≈ 17 dB / 100 m

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

Coaxial cable is the backbone of:
- **Cable television (CATV) distribution networks** — uses 75 Ω broadband coax with multiple channels multiplexed using FDM.
- **Cellular base station antenna feeds** — connects radio unit to the antenna on the tower.
- **High-speed cable internet (DOCSIS 3.0/3.1)** — gigabit data rates over existing HFC (Hybrid Fiber-Coax) plants.
- **Test & measurement** — oscilloscope probes, RF signal generators, spectrum analyzers.
- **Aerospace and military** — radar, avionics, secure communications.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Characteristic Impedance of Coaxial Cable ($Z_0$)

We model the coax as a distributed network of $L$ and $C$ per unit length. For TEM mode propagation:

**Step 1 — Electric Field between the conductors (Gauss's Law):**
At radius $r$ between $a$ and $b$, the electric field is purely radial:

$$E_r = \dfrac{V}{r \ln(b/a)}$$

**Step 2 — Distributed Capacitance per unit length (C):**
By integrating the displacement current over the cylindrical surface:

$$C = \dfrac{2\pi\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r}{\ln(b/a)}\quad \text{F/m}$$

**Step 3 — Distributed Inductance per unit length (L):**
Using the magnetic field of a long straight conductor with return path at radius $b$:

$$L = \dfrac{\mu_0}{2\pi}\ln(b/a)\quad \text{H/m}$$

**Step 4 — Characteristic Impedance definition for lossless line:**

$$Z_0 = \sqrt{\dfrac{L}{C}}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute L and C into the expression:**

$$Z_0 = \sqrt{\dfrac{\mu_0 \ln(b/a)}{2\pi} \cdot \dfrac{\ln(b/a)}{2\pi\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r}}$$

$$Z_0 = \dfrac{\ln(b/a)}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{\mu_0}{\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r}}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m and $1/(2\pi)\sqrt{\mu_0/\varepsilon_0} = 60$:**

$$Z_0 = \dfrac{60}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}}\,\ln\!\left(\dfrac{b}{a}\right)\ \Omega$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **master formula** for coax impedance. A $Z_0$ of exactly $50\ \Omega$ is obtained by choosing $b/a = e^{50\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}/60}$. For polyethylene ($\varepsilon_r = 2.25$), this gives $b/a \approx 3.5$.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Velocity of Propagation ($v_p$)

For a lossless TEM line, the phase velocity is:

$$v_p = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}$$

Substituting L and C from above:

$$v_p = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\dfrac{\mu_0}{2\pi}\ln(b/a) \cdot \dfrac{2\pi\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r}{\ln(b/a)}}}$$

$$v_p = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\mu_0\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r}} = \dfrac{c}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}}$$

where $c = 1/\sqrt{\mu_0\varepsilon_0} = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s is the speed of light in vacuum.

For polyethylene dielectric with $\varepsilon_r = 2.25$:

$$v_p = \dfrac{3 \times 10^8}{\sqrt{2.25}} = \dfrac{3 \times 10^8}{1.5} = 2 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$$

This is exactly **66.6 % of the speed of light** — and is why KTU numericals always quote coax delay as roughly **5 ns/m**.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Propagation Delay per Unit Length ($t_p$)

$$t_p = \dfrac{1}{v_p} = \dfrac{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}}{c} \text{ seconds per meter}$$

For polyethylene:

$$t_p = \dfrac{1.5}{3 \times 10^8} = 5 \text{ ns/m}$$

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Attenuation of a Coaxial Segment (Worked Numerical)

> **Problem (typical KTU 3-mark question):**
> A 200 m run of RG-58 coaxial cable has an attenuation of **4.5 dB per 100 m at 100 MHz**. A 100 MHz signal is injected with a power of **10 mW**. Find:
> (i) the output power at the end of the run,
> (ii) the output voltage if the input is 5 V RMS into a 50 Ω load.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Total attenuation in dB:**

$$A_{dB} = 4.5 \times 2 = 9 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 2 — Convert dB to power ratio:**

$$\dfrac{P_{in}}{P_{out}} = 10^{9/10} = 10^{0.9} = 7.943$$

**Step 3 — Output power:**

$$P_{out} = \dfrac{P_{in}}{7.943} = \dfrac{10 \text{ mW}}{7.943} \approx 1.26 \text{ mW}$$

**Step 4 — Output voltage (using $P = V^2 / R$):**

$$V_{out} = \sqrt{P_{out} \times R} = \sqrt{1.26 \times 10^{-3} \times 50} \approx 0.251 \text{ V RMS}$$

**Verification via voltage formula:**

$$\dfrac{V_{in}}{V_{out}} = 10^{9/20} = 10^{0.45} = 2.818$$

$$V_{out} = \dfrac{5}{2.818} \approx 1.77 \text{ V} \quad \text{(apparent mismatch!)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> The discrepancy arises because the **9 dB** attenuation refers to **matched power**, where $P_{in} = V_{in}^2/Z_0$ and the ratio $V_{in}/V_{out}$ corresponds to the **square root** of the power ratio when converted via 20log. The first method (using $P_{out}$ and $V_{out} = \sqrt{P_{out} R}$) is the **KTU-recommended** approach. Always show both steps for full marks.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Coax vs Other Guided Media (Common KTU Comparison Question)

| Parameter | UTP (Twisted Pair) | Coaxial Cable | Optical Fiber |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **Max Bandwidth** | 500 MHz (Cat-6a) | 1–3 GHz | > 50 THz |
| **Attenuation (dB/km)** | 20–30 (Cat-5e) | 5–20 | < 0.2 |
| **EMI Immunity** | Poor | Excellent (shielded) | Immune |
| **Security** | Low (radiates) | Moderate | Very high |
| **Cost / meter** | Low | Moderate | High |
| **Bend Radius** | Tight (4 × OD) | Moderate (10 × OD) | Large (30 × OD) |
| **Connectors** | RJ-45 | BNC, F-Type, N-Type | SC, ST, LC |
| **Typical Distance** | 100 m | 185–500 m (baseband) | 100s of km |
| **Installation Skill** | Easy | Moderate | Skilled |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Classification Tree — Transmission Media

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[TRANSMISSION MEDIA] --> B[GUIDED MEDIA]
    A[TRANSMISSION MEDIA] --> C[UNGUIDED MEDIA]
    B --> B1[Twisted Pair Cable]
    B --> B2[Coaxial Cable]
    B --> B3[Optical Fiber Cable]
    B1 --> B1a[UTP Unshielded]
    B1 --> B1b[STP Shielded]
    B2 --> B2a[Baseband 50 Ohm]
    B2 --> B2b[Broadband 75 Ohm]
    B2a --> B2a1[10Base2 RG58]
    B2a --> B2a2[10Base5 RG8]
    B2b --> B2b1[RG6 CATV]
    B2b --> B2b2[RG59 Video]
    C --> C1[Radio Waves]
    C --> C2[Microwave]
    C --> C3[Infrared]
    C --> C4[Satellite]
    C1 --> C1a[LF MF HF VHF UHF]
    C2 --> C2a[Terrestrial]
    C2 --> C2b[Line of Sight]
```

### 4.2 Cross-Sectional Architecture of Coaxial Cable (Block-Level Functional View)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[CENTER CONDUCTOR] --> B[DIELECTRIC INSULATOR]
    B --> C[OUTER CONDUCTOR SHIELD]
    C --> D[OUTER PVC JACKET]
    A --> A1[Function 1: Carries Signal Current]
    B --> B1[Function 2: Maintains Constant Impedance]
    C --> C1[Function 3: Faraday Cage Blocks EMI]
    D --> D1[Function 4: Environmental Protection]
    A1 --> E[TEM WAVE PROPAGATION]
    B1 --> E[TEM WAVE PROPAGATION]
    C1 --> E[TEM WAVE PROPAGATION]
    D1 --> E[TEM WAVE PROPAGATION]
    E --> F[OUTPUT TO RECEIVER]
```

### 4.3 Functional Signal Flow in a 10Base-2 Ethernet Segment

```mermaid
graph LR
    S[PC1 with NIC] --> T1[BNC T Connector]
    T1 --> SEG1[Coax Segment 1 RG58]
    SEG1 --> T2[BNC T Connector]
    T2 --> N2[PC2 with NIC]
    T2 --> SEG2[Coax Segment 2 RG58]
    SEG2 --> T3[BNC T Connector]
    T3 --> N3[PC3 with NIC]
    T3 --> SEG3[Coax Segment 3 RG58]
    SEG3 --> TERM1[50 Ohm Terminator End A]
    S --> T0[BNC T Connector]
    T0 --> TERM0[50 Ohm Terminator End B]
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram — End-to-End Coaxial Communication Link

```mermaid
graph LR
    TX[Digital Source] --> ENC[Line Encoder NRZ Manchester]
    ENC --> MOD[RF Modulator Optional]
    MOD --> AMP[Line Driver Amplifier]
    AMP --> COAX[Coaxial Cable Channel]
    COAX --> ATT[Attenuation Noise Distortion]
    ATT --> RXAMP[Receiver Amplifier Equalizer]
    RXAMP --> DEM[Demodulator Optional]
    DEM --> DEC[Line Decoder]
    DEC --> DST[Digital Destination]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Differentiate between **guided** and **unguided** transmission media. Give **two examples** of each.

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Guided Media | Unguided Media |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Path of signal | Confined within a physical medium | Propagates freely through space |
| Signal type | Electrical or optical | Electromagnetic waves |
| Security | More secure (contained) | Less secure (over-the-air) |
| Attenuation | Lower | Higher (atmospheric) |
| Installation | Requires cabling | No cabling required |
| **Examples** | Coaxial, Twisted Pair, Fiber Optic | Radio, Microwave, Satellite, Infrared |

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
List the **four layers** of a coaxial cable in order from inside to outside and state the **function** of each.

**Model Answer (4 × 0.5 mark for layer + 4 × 0.5 mark for function):**

1. **Inner Conductor (Center Core)** — Solid copper; carries the actual signal current.
2. **Dielectric Insulator** — Polyethylene; keeps inner and outer conductors separated and maintains a constant characteristic impedance.
3. **Outer Conductor (Braided Shield)** — Copper braid / aluminum foil; provides return current path and acts as a Faraday cage against EMI.
4. **Outer Jacket (PVC Sheath)** — PVC polymer; protects against physical, chemical, and moisture damage.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, every Part-B question has an **internal choice** between two sub-questions (Q-A or Q-B), and each sub-question carries 7 + 7 = 14 marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q-A(a) [7 Marks]: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
With a neat cross-sectional diagram, explain the **construction and working of a coaxial cable**. Why is its characteristic impedance typically chosen as **50 Ω for data networks** and **75 Ω for cable TV**?

**Model Answer:**

**Construction (3 marks):**
A coaxial cable consists of four concentric layers:
1. A solid or stranded copper **inner conductor** at the center.
2. A **dielectric insulator** (typically polyethylene, εᵣ = 2.25) surrounding it.
3. A braided copper **outer conductor / shield**.
4. A **PVC outer jacket** for mechanical and environmental protection.

**Working (2 marks):**
The signal is applied between the inner and outer conductors. Due to the cylindrical symmetry, an electromagnetic wave is launched in **TEM (Transverse Electromagnetic) mode** and propagates along the length of the cable. The outer shield acts as a Faraday cage, preventing radiation losses and rejecting external electromagnetic interference (EMI).

**Characteristic Impedance Choice (2 marks):**
The characteristic impedance is given by:

$$Z_0 = \dfrac{60}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}}\,\ln\!\left(\dfrac{b}{a}\right)$$

- At $b/a \approx 3.6$ → $Z_0 = 50\ \Omega$. This ratio **minimizes total loss** (balanced copper loss + dielectric loss), making it ideal for **digital data networks** (10Base-2, 10Base-5 Ethernet).
- At $b/a \approx 6.1$ → $Z_0 = 75\ \Omega$. This ratio **minimizes attenuation** (lowest signal loss per meter), making it ideal for **broadband video / cable TV distribution**.

**Cross-Sectional Diagram (to be drawn in exam):**

```mermaid
graph TD
    CL[COAXIAL CABLE CROSS SECTION] --> L1[INNER CONDUCTOR Solid Copper]
    CL --> L2[DIELECTRIC INSULATOR PE Foam]
    CL --> L3[OUTER SHIELD Copper Braid]
    CL --> L4[OUTER JACKET PVC Black]
```

---

**Q-A(b) [7 Marks]: [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**
A **200 m long RG-58 coaxial cable** has an attenuation of **5 dB per 100 m at 50 MHz**. If the input signal power is **20 mW**, calculate:
(i) the total attenuation in dB,
(ii) the output power,
(iii) the output voltage assuming a 50 Ω matched system.

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Total attenuation (1 mark):**

$$A_{dB} = 5 \text{ dB}/100\text{m} \times 2 = 10 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 2 — Output power (3 marks):**

Using the definition of decibel:

$$A_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{in}}{P_{out}}\right)$$

$$10 = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{20 \text{ mW}}{P_{out}}\right)$$

$$1 = \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{20}{P_{out}}\right)$$

$$\dfrac{20}{P_{out}} = 10$$

$$P_{out} = 2 \text{ mW}$$

**Step 3 — Output voltage (3 marks):**

For a matched 50 Ω system:

$$P_{out} = \dfrac{V_{out}^2}{R} = \dfrac{V_{out}^2}{50}$$

$$V_{out} = \sqrt{2 \times 10^{-3} \times 50} = \sqrt{0.1} \approx 0.316 \text{ V RMS}$$

**Final Answers:** (i) **10 dB**, (ii) **2 mW**, (iii) **0.316 V RMS**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**Q-B(a) [7 Marks]: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Explain the **classification of coaxial cables** based on impedance and application. Compare **baseband coaxial (50 Ω)** with **broadband coaxial (75 Ω)** systems in tabular form. Mention typical cable types used in each.

**Model Answer:**

**Classification (2 marks):**
Coaxial cables are classified by their characteristic impedance, which is determined by the ratio of inner-to-outer conductor radii and the dielectric constant.

- **Baseband Coax (50 Ω):** Designed for **digital data transmission** in computer networks. Optimized for minimum total loss.
- **Broadband Coax (75 Ω):** Designed for **analog video / RF signal distribution** in cable TV. Optimized for minimum attenuation over long distances.

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Baseband (50 Ω) | Broadband (75 Ω) |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Impedance** | 50 Ω | 75 Ω |
| **Signal type** | Digital (Manchester / NRZ) | Analog (FDM channels) |
| **b/a ratio** | ≈ 3.6 | ≈ 6.1 |
| **Optimization** | Minimum total loss | Minimum attenuation |
| **Topology** | Bus | Tree / Branching |
| **Bandwidth** | Single baseband channel | Multiple FDM channels (up to 1 GHz) |
| **Typical cables** | RG-58 (10Base-2), RG-8 (10Base-5) | RG-6, RG-59, RG-11 |
| **Connectors** | BNC, T-Connector | F-Type, N-Type |
| **Applications** | Legacy Ethernet | Cable TV, DOCSIS internet, satellite |

**Examples (2 marks):**
- **Baseband:** RG-58 (Thinnet, 10Base-2, max 185 m), RG-8 (Thicknet, 10Base-5, max 500 m).
- **Broadband:** RG-6 (most common CATV drop cable), RG-59 (short video runs), RG-11 (long trunk runs).

---

**Q-B(b) [7 Marks]: [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
Derive an expression for the **characteristic impedance of a coaxial cable** in terms of the inner conductor radius $a$, outer conductor inner radius $b$, and the relative permittivity of the dielectric $\varepsilon_r$. Hence, calculate the characteristic impedance of a coax with $a = 1$ mm, $b = 4$ mm, and $\varepsilon_r = 2.25$.

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Per-unit-length capacitance (2 marks):**
By Gauss's law, the capacitance per meter between concentric cylinders is:

$$C = \dfrac{2\pi\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r}{\ln(b/a)}\ \text{F/m}$$

**Step 2 — Per-unit-length inductance (2 marks):**
By Ampere's law, the inductance per meter is:

$$L = \dfrac{\mu_0}{2\pi}\ln(b/a)\ \text{H/m}$$

**Step 3 — Characteristic impedance (2 marks):**

$$Z_0 = \sqrt{\dfrac{L}{C}} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{\mu_0}{\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r}} \cdot \ln(b/a)$$

$$Z_0 = \dfrac{60}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}}\,\ln\!\left(\dfrac{b}{a}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Numerical calculation (1 mark):**

$$Z_0 = \dfrac{60}{\sqrt{2.25}} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{4}{1}\right) = \dfrac{60}{1.5} \times 1.386 = 40 \times 1.386 = 55.45\ \Omega$$

This is close to the **standard 50 Ω** used in Ethernet cables.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Mixing $\log$ and $\ln$** — Always use the natural logarithm ($\ln$) in the master formula; convert to common log only when explicitly asked. A common student error is to write $60\log_{10}(b/a)$ — that is **wrong** (the constant changes to $138$).
> 2. **Confusing radius vs diameter** — Some students substitute $D$ (diameter) into the formula meant for $a$ and $b$ (radii). The $b/a$ ratio works only for radii; if you use diameters, you get the same result because the ratio is dimensionless, but the constant is different.
> 3. **Forgetting $\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}$** — In exams, students often drop the dielectric constant and write $Z_0 = 60 \ln(b/a)$, giving a wrong answer for non-air dielectrics.
> 4. **Wrong units for attenuation** — Coax attenuation is given in **dB per 100 m** or **dB per km**. Read the problem carefully; many KTU numericals use 100 m, but students assume km.
> 5. **No terminators in 10Base-2** — Drawing a bus topology without the 50 Ω terminator at both ends costs a full mark in drawing questions.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize Before KTU Exam**

- [x] **Coaxial cable** has **4 layers**: inner conductor → dielectric → outer shield → jacket.
- [x] **Characteristic Impedance Master Formula:** $Z_0 = \dfrac{60}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}}\ln(b/a)$ Ω (use $\ln$, not $\log_{10}$).
- [x] **50 Ω** → optimized for **minimum total loss** → used in **Ethernet / data networks**.
- [x] **75 Ω** → optimized for **minimum attenuation** → used in **CATV / video distribution**.
- [x] **Velocity of propagation:** $v_p = c / \sqrt{\varepsilon_r}$ — always less than speed of light.
- [x] **Propagation delay:** $t_p = \sqrt{\varepsilon_r} / c$ — for PE cable ≈ **5 ns/m**.
- [x] **10Base-2 (Thinnet)** uses RG-58, max segment length **185 m**, 50 Ω, BNC T-connectors, 50 Ω terminators at both ends.
- [x] **10Base-5 (Thicknet)** uses RG-8, max segment length **500 m**, 50 Ω, vampire-tap connectors.
- [x] **Attenuation formula:** $A_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(P_{in}/P_{out}) = 20 \log_{10}(V_{in}/V_{out})$.
- [x] **Skin effect** causes conductor loss to increase as $\sqrt{f}$, hence high-frequency signals attenuate more.
- [x] **Faraday cage** principle: outer shield rejects external EMI → excellent noise immunity.
- [x] **TEM mode** is the fundamental propagation mode in coax (fields are transverse to direction of travel).
- [x] **Connectors:** BNC (Ethernet), F-Type (TV), N-Type (outdoor / thicknet), T-Connector (bus topology), Terminator (50/75 Ω).
- [x] **Why coax still matters:** DOCSIS 3.1 cable internet, cellular base-station antenna feeders, CATV distribution, RF test equipment.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fiber Optic Cable (FOC):** A guided transmission medium composed of one or more thin cylindrical strands of ultra-pure glass (silica, $\text{SiO}_2$) or plastic, known as **optical fibers**, which transmit information in the form of **pulses of light** (photons) generated by an LED or a laser diode. The propagation is governed by the optical phenomenon of **Total Internal Reflection (TIR)** between a higher-refractive-index *core* and a lower-refractive-index *cladding*.

In the context of **KTU PEMRT631 (Network & Data Security)**, the fiber optic cable is studied as the **physical Layer-1 transmission backbone** of secure modern networks because it is inherently immune to electromagnetic interference (EMI), extremely difficult to tap without detection, and offers virtually unlimited bandwidth — properties critical for **secure data transmission**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Light Pipe" Mental Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy:** Imagine shining a flashlight down a long, mirrored hallway. The light bounces off the walls and travels the entire length of the hallway, even if the hallway bends. The **walls of the hallway are the cladding**, the **air inside is the core**, and the **light beam is your data**. Whatever you flash at the entrance appears at the exit — that's exactly how fiber optics transmit data using light pulses.

A more technical analogy: Think of the fiber as a **"river of photons"** confined inside a glass pipe. Because the cladding has a lower refractive index than the core, any light ray striking the core-cladding boundary at an angle steeper than the **critical angle** is reflected back into the core — never escaping. This is **Total Internal Reflection (TIR)**, the single most important principle underlying optical networking.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> - **Speed of light in vacuum:** $c = 3 \times 10^8$ **m/s** (**299,792,458 m/s** exactly).
> - **Speed of light in silica core:** $v \approx 2 \times 10^8$ **m/s** (≈ 67% of $c$).
> - **Standard optical transmission windows:** **850 nm**, **1300 nm**, **1310 nm**, **1550 nm**.
> - **Refractive Index of pure silica core:** $n_1 \approx 1.48$.
> - **Refractive Index of typical cladding:** $n_2 \approx 1.46$.
> - **Attenuation target of modern SMF:** < **0.2 dB/km** at **1550 nm**.
> - **Standard bandwidth-distance product of MMF (OM3):** **2000 MHz·km** at 850 nm.

These metrics are the **gold-standard benchmarks** examiners expect you to recall for KTU 2024 scheme assessments.

## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of an Optical Fiber — Layer-by-Layer Breakdown

A fiber optic cable is a **multi-layered cylindrical structure**. Each layer has a specific physical purpose:

1. **Core** ($n_1$): The innermost glass cylinder (typical diameter **8–62.5 μm**) that carries the light signal.
2. **Cladding** ($n_2$): A glass layer with a *lower* refractive index surrounding the core (typical diameter **125 μm**). Its job is to keep light inside the core via TIR.
3. **Buffer / Coating** (acrylic polymer): A protective layer (~250 μm diameter) shielding the glass from moisture and mechanical shock.
4. **Strength Members** (Kevlar / aramid yarn): Provide tensile strength so the cable can be pulled through conduits without snapping.
5. **Outer Jacket** (PVC / LSZH): The visible outermost black or colored sheath protecting the entire bundle from the environment.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does cladding have a *lower* refractive index?** Because TIR can **only** occur when light moves from a **denser (higher-$n$)** medium to a **less dense (lower-$n$)** medium. If $n_2 > n_1$, the light would leak out — defeating the purpose of the fiber entirely.

### 2.2 Optical Principles — Snell's Law and Total Internal Reflection

**Snell's Law** governs how light bends when crossing a boundary:

$$n_1 \sin(\theta_1) = n_2 \sin(\theta_2)$$

where:
- $n_1, n_2$ = refractive indices of the two media
- $\theta_1$ = angle of incidence (measured from the **normal**)
- $\theta_2$ = angle of refraction

**Critical Angle ($\theta_c$):** The smallest angle of incidence at which a light ray traveling from a denser medium to a less dense medium is *completely* reflected (no refraction). Setting $\theta_2 = 90^\circ$:

$$\sin(\theta_c) = \frac{n_2}{n_1}$$

**Total Internal Reflection (TIR):** Occurs when $\theta_1 > \theta_c$. Since $\sin(\theta_2) > 1$ has no real solution, *no light escapes* — all energy is reflected back into the core.

### 2.3 Acceptance Angle and Numerical Aperture (NA)

The **acceptance angle ($\theta_a$)** is the maximum angle (with respect to the fiber axis) at which an entering light ray will still undergo TIR. Any ray entering at a steeper angle simply refracts out through the cladding and is lost.

$$\sin(\theta_a) = \text{NA} = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}$$

The **Numerical Aperture (NA)** is a dimensionless figure of merit that quantifies the light-gathering ability of the fiber:
- **Higher NA → more light collected → easier coupling → more modal dispersion.**
- **Lower NA → less light collected → harder coupling → better bandwidth.**

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All key formulas for Fiber Optic Cable — exam-ready.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Speed of light in medium | $v = \dfrac{c}{n}$ | m/s | $c = 3\times 10^8$ m/s |
| 2 | Snell's Law | $n_1 \sin(\theta_1) = n_2 \sin(\theta_2)$ | — | Boundary refraction |
| 3 | Critical Angle | $\sin(\theta_c) = \dfrac{n_2}{n_1}$ | radians / degrees | Required $n_1 > n_2$ |
| 4 | Numerical Aperture | $\text{NA} = \sin(\theta_a) = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}$ | dimensionless | $0 < \text{NA} < 1$ |
| 5 | Acceptance Cone Half-Angle | $\theta_a = \sin^{-1}(\text{NA})$ | degrees | Light acceptance limit |
| 6 | Attenuation (Power Loss) | $\alpha = \dfrac{10}{L} \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{\text{in}}}{P_{\text{out}}}\right)$ | dB/km | $L$ in km |
| 7 | Power at distance $L$ | $P_{\text{out}} = P_{\text{in}} \cdot 10^{-\alpha L / 10}$ | Watts or dBm | After $L$ km |
| 8 | Refractive Index Profile (Graded Index) | $n(r) = n_1 \sqrt{1 - 2\Delta (r/a)^\alpha}$ | — | $\Delta = (n_1^2 - n_2^2)/(2 n_1^2)$ |
| 9 | Bandwidth-Distance Product | $B \cdot L$ | MHz·km | Higher = better fiber |
| 10 | Pulse Broadening (Modal) | $\Delta\tau_{\text{modal}} = \dfrac{n_1 L}{c}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{\cos\theta_a} - 1\right)$ | s | Step-index MMF only |
| 11 | Pulse Broadening (Chromatic) | $\Delta\tau_{\text{chroma}} = L \cdot D \cdot \Delta\lambda$ | s | $D$ = dispersion coefficient (ps/(nm·km)) |
| 12 | Maximum Bit Rate (Nyquist) | $R_b \le \dfrac{B}{2}$ | bits/s | $B$ = channel bandwidth (Hz) |
| 13 | Number of Modes (MMF) | $N \approx \dfrac{2}{\alpha + 2}\!\left(\dfrac{\pi d \cdot \text{NA}}{\lambda}\right)^{\!2}$ | integer | $\alpha$ = profile parameter |
| 14 | Bending Loss Condition | $R_b < \dfrac{3 n_1^2 \lambda}{4\pi (n_1^2 - n_2^2)}$ | meters | Below this radius → loss |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary conditions / validity rules:**
> - TIR requires $n_1 > n_2$ **strictly**.
> - $\text{NA} \le 1$ always (since it is the sine of a real angle).
> - Attenuation $\alpha$ is **always positive** when $P_{\text{out}} < P_{\text{in}}$.
> - Number of modes $N$ is **meaningless for SMF** ($N \to 0$ by design).

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In practice, fiber optic cables are the **physical backbone of the global internet**. Submarine cables laid across ocean floors carry ~**95% of all intercontinental data traffic**. In **data centers**, **OM3/OM4 multimode fiber** runs server-to-rack links at **10 Gbps, 40 Gbps, 100 Gbps**, and emerging **400 Gbps** speeds. In **secure military and financial networks**, fiber is favored because it emits **no electromagnetic radiation (EMI-free)**, making eavesdropping via induction practically impossible — directly aligning fiber with the *security* objectives of **PEMRT631**.

## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Numerical Aperture from Snell's Law

We derive the NA expression rigorously because it is the **most commonly asked derivation** in KTU university exams for this module.

**Starting point:** A light ray entering the flat input face of the fiber from air ($n_{\text{air}} = 1$) at angle $\theta_0$ to the fiber **axis**. Inside the fiber, it refracts to angle $\theta_1$ relative to the axis. The same ray strikes the core-cladding boundary at angle $\phi_1$ to the **normal** of that boundary.

**Step 1 — Apply Snell's Law at the air-core entry face:**

$$n_{\text{air}} \sin(\theta_0) = n_1 \sin(\theta_1)$$

**Step 2 — Recognize geometric complement:**

From the geometry of a straight cylinder, the ray hitting the core-cladding boundary makes an angle $\phi_1$ with the **normal** to that boundary, and $\phi_1 = 90^\circ - \theta_1$.

Therefore $\theta_1 = 90^\circ - \phi_1$, and so $\sin(\theta_1) = \cos(\phi_1)$.

Substituting:

$$\sin(\theta_0) = n_1 \cos(\phi_1)$$

**Step 3 — Apply the TIR condition at the core-cladding boundary:**

For TIR to just *begin*, $\phi_1$ must equal the critical angle $\phi_c$:

$$\sin(\phi_c) = \frac{n_2}{n_1}$$

This gives $\cos(\phi_c) = \sqrt{1 - \sin^2(\phi_c)} = \sqrt{1 - n_2^2/n_1^2} = \dfrac{\sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}}{n_1}$.

**Step 4 — Substitute $\cos(\phi_c)$ back:**

$$\sin(\theta_a) = n_1 \cdot \frac{\sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}}{n_1}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\;\text{NA} = \sin(\theta_a) = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}\;}$$

> **Why it matters:** Every time you see a numerical aperture value on a fiber datasheet (e.g., OM3 MMF: NA = 0.20, SMF: NA ≈ 0.14), this derivation explains where the number came from.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Attenuation Coefficient from Power Measurements

Given: $P_{\text{in}}$ launched into a fiber of length $L$ km, and $P_{\text{out}}$ measured at the far end. Find the attenuation coefficient $\alpha$ in dB/km.

**Step 1 — Define attenuation per unit length in linear (non-log) form:**

Let the power ratio per km be $k$. After $L$ km:

$$\frac{P_{\text{out}}}{P_{\text{in}}} = k^L$$

Taking the logarithm:

$$k = \left(\frac{P_{\text{out}}}{P_{\text{in}}}\right)^{\!1/L}$$

**Step 2 — Convert to decibels:**

By definition, attenuation in dB over length $L$ is:

$$\text{Loss (dB)} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_{\text{in}}}{P_{\text{out}}}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Express attenuation per km:**

Dividing both sides by $L$:

$$\alpha \;=\; \frac{10}{L} \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_{\text{in}}}{P_{\text{out}}}\right) \;\; \text{dB/km}$$

**Worked Numerical Example (KTU-typical 7-mark problem):**

> A **10 km** fiber link launches **1 mW** into the fiber and **40 μW** is measured at the receiver. Calculate $\alpha$.

**Step 1 — Compute power ratio:**

$$\frac{P_{\text{in}}}{P_{\text{out}}} = \frac{1 \times 10^{-3}}{40 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{1000}{40} = 25$$

**Step 2 — Take log:**

$$\log_{10}(25) = 1.39794$$

**Step 3 — Multiply by 10/L:**

$$\alpha = \frac{10}{10} \times 1.39794 = 1.398 \; \text{dB/km}$$

> **Final answer:** $\alpha \approx$ **1.40 dB/km**. Such a high value would be unacceptable for a long-haul link (modern SMF achieves < 0.2 dB/km) — this would be typical of a *low-grade plastic optical fiber* (POF) or a damaged cable.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Bandwidth Limit from Pulse Broadening

Modal dispersion $\Delta\tau$ determines the minimum pulse spacing and hence maximum bit rate.

**Step 1 — Time taken by the highest-order mode (longest path):**

$$t_{\max} = \frac{L}{v \cos\theta_a} = \frac{n_1 L}{c \cos\theta_a}$$

**Step 2 — Time taken by the lowest-order mode (axial ray, $\theta = 0$):**

$$t_{\min} = \frac{L}{v} = \frac{n_1 L}{c}$$

**Step 3 — Pulse broadening:**

$$\Delta\tau = t_{\max} - t_{\min} = \frac{n_1 L}{c}\!\left(\frac{1}{\cos\theta_a} - 1\right)$$

**Step 4 — Apply Nyquist's criterion for binary signaling:**

For a binary (2-level) signal, the maximum bit rate $R_b$ must satisfy:

$$R_b \le \frac{1}{2 \Delta\tau}$$

This is the **fundamental bandwidth limit** imposed by modal dispersion in step-index multimode fiber.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Fiber Types

| Parameter | Single-Mode Fiber (SMF) | Multi-Mode Fiber (MMF) Step-Index | Multi-Mode Fiber (MMF) Graded-Index |
|---|---|---|---|
| Core diameter | **8–10 μm** | **50 / 62.5 / 100 μm** | **50 / 62.5 μm** |
| Cladding diameter | **125 μm** | **125 μm** | **125 μm** |
| $n_1$ profile | Uniform step | Uniform step | Parabolic |
| Light source | **Laser diode** | LED | LED / VCSEL |
| Wavelength | **1310 / 1550 nm** | **850 / 1300 nm** | **850 / 1300 nm** |
| Number of modes $N$ | **1 (single mode)** | Hundreds–thousands | Hundreds–thousands |
| Modal dispersion | None | **Severe** | Reduced (parabolic profile) |
| Bandwidth–Distance | **> 1000 GHz·km** | **10–100 MHz·km** | **200–2000 MHz·km** |
| Maximum link length (1 Gbps) | **> 100 km** | **< 2 km** | **< 2 km (OM3)** |
| Typical cost | Higher | Low | Moderate |
| Security application | **Long-haul, submarine, secure backbone** | LAN, data-center rack | LAN, data-center spine |

> [!NOTE]
> **For PEMRT631 (Network & Data Security) exam emphasis:** SMF is the secure long-haul choice because (a) it cannot be tapped easily due to the small core, (b) it does not leak modal information that can be intercepted, and (c) its single-mode operation makes crosstalk-based eavesdropping impossible.

## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Optical Communication Link

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **end-to-end signal flow** of a fiber optic communication system — a high-yield visualization for KTU 14-mark theory questions.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srcA[Data Source] --> srcB[Electrical Signal Generator]
    srcB --> srcC[E O Converter LED or LASER Diode]
    srcC --> srcD[Optical Transmitter Modulator]
    srcD --> srcE[Fiber Optic Cable Core and Cladding TIR]
    srcE --> srcF[Optical Amplifier EDFA optional Repeater]
    srcF --> srcG[Photodetector PIN or APD]
    srcG --> srcH[O E Converter Amplifier and Filter]
    srcH --> srcI[Digital Receiver Decoder]
    srcI --> srcJ[Data Sink Destination]

    subgraph TX[Transmitter End]
      srcA
      srcB
      srcC
      srcD
    end

    subgraph CHANNEL[Optical Channel Physical Layer]
      srcE
      srcF
    end

    subgraph RX[Receiver End]
      srcG
      srcH
      srcI
      srcJ
    end
```

### 4.2 Cross-Sectional Layer Diagram of Fiber Optic Cable

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1["Outer Jacket PVC or LSZH Polymer"]
    L2["Strength Members Kevlar Aramid Yarn"]
    L3["Buffer Coating 250 microns Acrylate"]
    L4["Cladding 125 microns Lower Refractive Index n2"]
    L5["Core 8 to 62.5 microns Higher Refractive Index n1"]
    L6["Light Signal Photons Propagating via Total Internal Reflection"]

    L1 --> L2 --> L3 --> L4 --> L5
    L5 -. guides .-> L6
```

### 4.3 Ray Propagation — Step-Index vs Graded-Index Fiber

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SI[Step Index Fiber Sharp Boundaries]
      s1[Input Ray] --> s2[Core Cladding Boundary]
      s2 -- TIR zigzag path --> s3[Output Ray]
    end

    subgraph GI[Graded Index Fiber Parabolic n profile]
      g1[Input Ray] --> g2[Continuous Refraction]
      g2 -- sinusoidal curved path --> g3[Output Ray]
    end
```

### 4.4 Total Internal Reflection — Acceptance Cone Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    cone["Acceptance Cone Half Angle theta_a = sin inverse NA"]
    axis["Fiber Axis Normal to Entry Face"]
    boundary["Critical Ray Just Suffers TIR"]
    outside["Rejected Ray Escapes Cladding Loss"]

    cone --> axis
    cone --> boundary
    cone --> outside
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Visualization Tip:** In your answer sheet, always draw the **acceptance cone** with two boundary rays when explaining NA — it earns easy visualization marks and demonstrates clarity of thought to the examiner.

## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]**
**Define Numerical Aperture (NA) of an optical fiber. What is its significance?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Numerical Aperture (NA)** of an optical fiber is a dimensionless quantity that measures the light-gathering capability of the fiber. It is defined as the sine of the maximum acceptance angle $\theta_a$.

$$\text{NA} = \sin(\theta_a) = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}$$

**Significance:**
1. It determines the **acceptance cone** within which incoming light rays will be guided by total internal reflection.
2. A higher NA means **easier coupling** of light from the source into the fiber, at the cost of higher modal dispersion and lower bandwidth.
3. It dictates the **maximum possible data rate** achievable in a multimode fiber link.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Expected]**
**Differentiate between Single-Mode Fiber (SMF) and Multi-Mode Fiber (MMF).**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | SMF | MMF |
|---|---|---|
| Core diameter | 8–10 μm (small) | 50/62.5 μm (large) |
| Light source | Laser diode | LED |
| Propagation | Single mode (axial ray) | Many modes (zigzag paths) |
| Modal dispersion | Negligible | Significant |
| Bandwidth | Very high (>100 GHz·km) | Moderate (10–2000 MHz·km) |
| Cost & coupling | Higher cost, precise alignment | Lower cost, easy coupling |
| Typical use | Long-haul telecom, submarine cables | LANs, data centers, short links |
