### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for **Numerical Aperture (NA)** of a step-index optical fiber in terms of the refractive indices of core and cladding. Explain the significance of the **acceptance angle**. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A step-index fiber has a core refractive index $n_1 = 1.50$ and a cladding refractive index $n_2 = 1.45$. Calculate: **(i)** the Numerical Aperture, **(ii)** the acceptance angle $\theta_a$, and **(iii)** the critical angle $\theta_c$ at the core-cladding interface. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation of NA — Full Step-by-Step (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** Apply Snell's Law at the air-core boundary:
$$n_{\text{air}} \sin(\theta_0) = n_1 \sin(\theta_1)$$
where $n_{\text{air}} = 1$, $\theta_0$ = external angle, $\theta_1$ = internal angle.

**Step 2:** Geometry gives $\theta_1 + \phi_1 = 90^\circ$, so $\sin(\theta_1) = \cos(\phi_1)$:
$$\sin(\theta_0) = n_1 \cos(\phi_1)$$

**Step 3:** Apply Snell's Law at the core-cladding boundary for the limiting (critical) case:
$$n_1 \sin(\phi_c) = n_2 \sin(90^\circ) \;\Rightarrow\; \sin(\phi_c) = \dfrac{n_2}{n_1}$$

**Step 4:** For the critical ray, $\phi_1 = \phi_c$. Use $\cos(\phi_c) = \sqrt{1 - n_2^2/n_1^2}$:
$$\sin(\theta_a) = n_1 \cdot \frac{\sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}}{n_1}$$

**Step 5:** Therefore:
$$\boxed{\;\text{NA} = \sin(\theta_a) = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}\;}$$

**Significance of Acceptance Angle (extra credit):**
The acceptance angle $\theta_a$ defines the cone of light within which any incident ray will undergo total internal reflection and be guided through the fiber. Rays entering at angles greater than $\theta_a$ escape through the cladding and are lost. The corresponding solid cone has half-angle $\theta_a$ and the fiber can only accept light through the "acceptance cone" of this geometry.

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

Given: $n_1 = 1.50$, $n_2 = 1.45$.

**(i) Numerical Aperture:**

$$\text{NA} = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2} = \sqrt{(1.50)^2 - (1.45)^2}$$

$$= \sqrt{2.2500 - 2.1025} = \sqrt{0.1475}$$

$$\boxed{\text{NA} \approx 0.384}$$

**(ii) Acceptance Angle:**

$$\theta_a = \sin^{-1}(\text{NA}) = \sin^{-1}(0.384)$$

$$\boxed{\theta_a \approx 22.6^\circ}$$

**(iii) Critical Angle at Core-Cladding Interface:**

$$\sin(\theta_c) = \frac{n_2}{n_1} = \frac{1.45}{1.50} = 0.9667$$

$$\boxed{\theta_c \approx 75.2^\circ}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - Students often confuse **acceptance angle $\theta_a$** (measured from fiber *axis*) with **critical angle $\theta_c$** (measured from *normal to boundary*). Mixing these loses 2 marks instantly.
> - Always state $n_{\text{air}} = 1$ explicitly in Step 1 of the derivation; examiners deduct 0.5 marks if omitted.
> - In numericals, **do not truncate** — carry at least 4 decimal places in intermediate steps to avoid rounding-error penalties.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Expected]`

**(a)** Explain the **principle of total internal reflection** in optical fibers with a neat diagram. State the conditions for TIR to occur. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A 15 km optical fiber link has an input power of **2 mW** and an output power of **50 μW**. Determine: **(i)** the total attenuation in dB, **(ii)** the attenuation coefficient in dB/km, and **(iii)** the output power after 25 km if the same input power is used (assuming linear attenuation). **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Total Internal Reflection — Theory + Diagram (7 marks):**

When a light ray travels from a **denser medium** (higher refractive index $n_1$) to a **rarer medium** (lower refractive index $n_2$), at angles of incidence larger than the **critical angle $\theta_c$**, the light ray is **completely reflected** back into the denser medium without any refraction into the rarer medium. This phenomenon is called **Total Internal Reflection (TIR)**.

**Conditions for TIR:**
1. Light must travel from a **denser to a rarer** medium: $n_1 > n_2$.
2. Angle of incidence must exceed the **critical angle**: $\theta_i > \theta_c$, where $\sin(\theta_c) = n_2 / n_1$.

**Diagram (describe in answer):**
A horizontal core-cladding boundary. Draw a normal perpendicular to the boundary. Show three rays from the same point inside the core:
- Ray 1: $\theta_i < \theta_c$ → refracts out (lost).
- Ray 2: $\theta_i = \theta_c$ → grazes the boundary.
- Ray 3: $\theta_i > \theta_c$ → reflects back (TIR).

In a fiber, the same ray bounces repeatedly inside the core, traveling in a **zigzag path** with no loss of energy (ideally).

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

Given: $L = 15$ km, $P_{\text{in}} = 2$ mW $= 2 \times 10^{-3}$ W, $P_{\text{out}} = 50$ μW $= 50 \times 10^{-6}$ W.

**(i) Total attenuation in dB:**

$$A = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_{\text{in}}}{P_{\text{out}}}\right) = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{2 \times 10^{-3}}{50 \times 10^{-6}}\right)$$

$$= 10 \log_{10}(40) = 10 \times 1.60206$$

$$\boxed{A \approx 16.02 \text{ dB}}$$

**(ii) Attenuation coefficient:**

$$\alpha = \frac{A}{L} = \frac{16.02}{15}$$

$$\boxed{\alpha \approx 1.068 \text{ dB/km}}$$

**(iii) Output power after 25 km:**

$$P_{\text{out}}(25) = P_{\text{in}} \cdot 10^{-\alpha L / 10} = 2 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 10^{-1.068 \times 25 / 10}$$

$$= 2 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 10^{-2.67}$$

$$10^{-2.67} = 10^{-3} \cdot 10^{0.33} = 0.001 \times 2.138 = 2.138 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$P_{\text{out}}(25) = 2 \times 10^{-3} \times 2.138 \times 10^{-3} = 4.276 \times 10^{-6} \text{ W}$$

$$\boxed{P_{\text{out}}(25) \approx 4.28 \text{ μW}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - **Unit conversion mistakes** ($mW \leftrightarrow \mu W$) are the #1 reason students lose full marks on attenuation problems. Always convert to the same unit before computing the ratio.
> - In part (iii), do **not** assume $\alpha$ remains constant if the problem hints at non-linear effects — but if the problem explicitly states "linear attenuation," proceed as shown.
> - For total attenuation, do not multiply by $L$ — that is the attenuation coefficient. Mislabeling these loses 1 mark.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Fiber Optic Cable**

- [ ] **Fiber optic cable = light-guided glass strand** using **Total Internal Reflection (TIR)**.
- [ ] **Core ($n_1$) > Cladding ($n_2$)** — strict requirement for TIR.
- [ ] **Snell's Law:** $n_1 \sin\theta_1 = n_2 \sin\theta_2$ — foundation formula.
- [ ] **Critical angle:** $\sin(\theta_c) = n_2 / n_1$.
- [ ] **Numerical Aperture (NA):** $\text{NA} = \sin(\theta_a) = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}$.
- [ ] **Acceptance cone** has half-angle $\theta_a = \sin^{-1}(\text{NA})$.
- [ ] **Attenuation formula:** $\alpha = \dfrac{10}{L} \log_{10}(P_{\text{in}}/P_{\text{out}})$ in **dB/km**.
- [ ] **Output power formula:** $P_{\text{out}} = P_{\text{in}} \cdot 10^{-\alpha L / 10}$.
- [ ] **SMF** = 8–10 μm core, laser source, single mode, long-haul, secure.
- [ ] **MMF** = 50/62.5 μm core, LED source, many modes, LANs, lower cost.
- [ ] **Modal dispersion** dominates in MMF; **chromatic dispersion** dominates in SMF.
- [ ] **Bending loss** occurs when bend radius < minimum safe radius $R_b < 3 n_1^2 \lambda / [4\pi(n_1^2 - n_2^2)]$.
- [ ] **Security advantage:** No EMI emission → very hard to tap → ideal for **secure PEMRT631 networks**.
- [ ] **Optical windows:** **850 nm, 1310 nm, 1550 nm** — lowest attenuation at 1550 nm.
- [ ] **Numerical aperture NA is dimensionless** and always $\le 1$.
- [ ] **Critical angle is measured from the normal**, **acceptance angle from the fiber axis** — never confuse them.
- [ ] **Submarine cables carry ~95% of intercontinental traffic** — real-world significance.
- [ ] **Bandwidth–distance product (MHz·km)** is the standard figure of merit for fiber capacity.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction and Transport-Layer Services: Relationship Between Transport and Network Layers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT631 - NETWORK & DATA SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Introduction and Transport-Layer Services: Relationship Between Transport and Network Layers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Transport Layer — Formal Definition

The **Transport Layer** is **Layer 4** of the **OSI (Open Systems Interconnection)** reference model and the **TCP/IP protocol suite**. It is responsible for providing **process-to-process (end-to-end) delivery** of an entire message from a process running on a source host to a corresponding process running on a destination host.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus context, the transport layer sits directly above the **Network Layer (Layer 3)** and offers its services to the upper **Session, Presentation, and Application Layers (Layers 5–7)**. While the network layer provides *host-to-host* logical communication, the transport layer refines this into *process-to-process* logical communication by adding **port addressing, multiplexing/demultiplexing, flow control, error control, and connection management**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The transport layer is *not* a single protocol — it is a **service layer** that can be implemented by different protocols (most notably **TCP** and **UDP** in the Internet protocol suite).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Postal Service Inside a Building"

Imagine a large office building with hundreds of employees. The **Network Layer** is like the building's **mailroom** that receives a package from outside and knows *which building* (host) it belongs to. However, the mailroom does not know *which employee* (process) inside the building should receive the package.

The **Transport Layer** acts like the **internal courier desk** that takes the package from the mailroom, reads the **employee ID number** (port number) written on it, and delivers it to the correct desk. Without this internal courier, the mailroom would dump every package in the lobby — chaos!

**Key Real-World Insight:** Every day, your browser (a process) opens a connection to a web server (another process). Millions of such processes run concurrently on the internet. The transport layer is what keeps all these "conversations" from getting tangled up.

### 1.3 Position in the Protocol Stack

$$
\boxed{\text{Application Layer (HTTP, FTP, DNS, SMTP)} \rightarrow \text{Transport Layer (TCP, UDP)} \rightarrow \text{Network Layer (IP)} \rightarrow \text{Link Layer} \rightarrow \text{Physical Layer}}
$$

The transport layer is the **lowest layer** that deals with the **end-to-end meaning of communication**, and the **highest layer** that deals with the **end-to-end transmission strategy**.

### 1.4 Core Terminology in Tabular Form

| Term | Definition | KTU Significance |
|------|------------|------------------|
| **Process-to-Process Delivery** | Delivery of data between specific application processes on different hosts | Central purpose of the transport layer |
| **Multiplexing** | Combining multiple process data streams into one network-layer connection | Improves bandwidth efficiency |
| **Demultiplexing** | Separating one network stream into multiple process-specific streams | Required for parallel sessions |
| **Port Number** | A 16-bit logical address (0–65535) identifying a specific process | The "apartment number" analogy |
| **Socket** | The combination of (IP address + Port number) — uniquely identifies one end of a connection | Endpoint of communication |
| **Segment / Datagram** | The PDU (Protocol Data Unit) of the transport layer | Encapsulated into IP packets at Layer 3 |

> [!NOTE]
> The transport layer PDU is called a **Segment** in TCP and a **Datagram** (or User Datagram) in UDP. At the network layer, both are encapsulated inside an **IP Packet**.

### 1.5 Why Do We Need a Transport Layer When the Network Layer Already Exists?

This is a classic **KTU 2-mark question**. The answer lies in the **limitations of the Network Layer**:

- The **Network Layer (IP)** provides only **best-effort, unreliable, connectionless** service — it makes *no guarantee* of delivery, ordering, or duplication control.
- The **Transport Layer** adds **reliability, ordering, flow control, and congestion control** on top of IP — turning a "best-effort" postal system into a "registered post with tracking" system.
- The network layer routes packets to *hosts*; the transport layer delivers data to *processes* within those hosts.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fundamental Distinction:** *Network Layer = Host-to-Host* | *Transport Layer = Process-to-Process*. The transport layer is the **service-enhancement layer** of the TCP/IP model.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Detailed Services Offered by the Transport Layer

The transport layer provides **four major categories of services** as defined in the KTU syllabus (aligned with Forouzan and Kurose-Ross textbooks):

#### 2.1.1 Process-to-Process Addressing (Port Addressing)
- Each application process is identified by a **16-bit port number** ranging from **0 to 65535**.
- **Port Number Categories:**
  - **Well-Known Ports (0–1023):** Reserved for standard services (e.g., HTTP = 80, HTTPS = 443, FTP = 21, SSH = 22, DNS = 53, SMTP = 25).
  - **Registered Ports (1024–49151):** Assigned to user applications upon request.
  - **Dynamic / Ephemeral Ports (49152–65535):** Assigned temporarily by the OS to client processes.

> [!NOTE]
> The IANA (Internet Assigned Numbers Authority) governs port number assignments. KTU students should memorize the well-known ports for HTTP, HTTPS, FTP, SSH, DNS, and SMTP.

#### 2.1.2 Multiplexing and Demiplexing

- **Multiplexing (at sender side):** Multiple processes on the source host generate data. The transport layer collects data from all these sockets, adds the appropriate transport header (with source/destination port), and **passes the resulting segments to the network layer using a single network-layer service**.
- **Demultiplexing (at receiver side):** The transport layer receives segments from the network layer, examines the **destination port number** in each segment header, and delivers the data to the correct application process (socket).

The transport layer performs two types of demultiplexing:

$$
\boxed{\text{Connectionless Demultiplexing (UDP) vs. Connection-Oriented Demultiplexing (TCP)}}
$$

- **UDP Demultiplexing:** Uses only the **destination port number** from the segment header. All UDP segments destined for the same destination IP + port are delivered to the same socket, regardless of the source.
- **TCP Demultiplexing:** Uses a **4-tuple** `(Source IP, Source Port, Destination IP, Destination Port)` to identify the connection. Each unique 4-tuple corresponds to a different socket. This is what allows a single web server to serve thousands of clients simultaneously.

#### 2.1.3 Connection-Oriented vs. Connectionless Service

This is the **most important transport-layer service distinction** for KTU exams:

| Feature | **TCP (Connection-Oriented)** | **UDP (Connectionless)** |
|---|---|---|
| Acronym Expansion | Transmission Control Protocol | User Datagram Protocol |
| Connection Setup | Three-way handshake (SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK) | None — send and forget |
| Reliability | Guaranteed delivery using ACK + retransmission | Best-effort, no ACK |
| Ordering | Sequence numbers ensure in-order delivery | No ordering guarantee |
| Flow Control | Yes (sliding window protocol) | No |
| Congestion Control | Yes (cwnd, AIMD) | No |
| Header Size | **20 bytes** (minimum, can go up to 60) | **8 bytes** (fixed) |
| Speed | Slower due to overhead | Faster, low latency |
| Use Cases | HTTP/HTTPS, FTP, SSH, Email (SMTP) | DNS, VoIP, Video streaming, Gaming, SNMP |
| PDU Name | **Segment** | **Datagram** |
| Multiplexing Basis | 4-tuple (IP + Port both sides) | 2-tuple (Destination IP + Port) |

#### 2.1.4 Error Control, Flow Control, and Congestion Control

- **Error Control (TCP only):** Uses **checksums**, **acknowledgments (ACKs)**, and **timeouts** to detect and retransmit lost or corrupted segments.
- **Flow Control (TCP only):** Prevents the sender from overwhelming the receiver. Uses the **receive window (rwnd)** field in the TCP header — the receiver advertises how much buffer space it has available.
- **Congestion Control (TCP only):** Prevents the sender from overwhelming the network. Uses the **congestion window (cwnd)** and algorithms like **Slow Start, Congestion Avoidance, Fast Retransmit, Fast Recovery**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **UDP provides NONE of these three services.** UDP is intentionally minimal — it is the "fire-and-forget" protocol of the internet.

### 2.2 Relationship Between Transport and Network Layers

The relationship between the transport and network layers is **hierarchical, complementary, and inseparable** — but the two layers operate at fundamentally different scopes.

#### 2.2.1 The Hierarchical Relationship

$$
\boxed{\text{Transport Layer} = \text{End-to-End enhancement of Network Layer services}}
$$

The transport layer **sits on top** of the network layer and **uses** the network layer's services. The network layer does not know that the transport layer exists. From the network layer's perspective, it is simply carrying an IP packet whose payload happens to be a TCP segment or UDP datagram.

This creates a **clear separation of concerns:**

| Concern | Handled By | Scope |
|---|---|---|
| Bit transmission over a wire | Physical Layer | Local |
| Frame delivery between adjacent nodes | Data Link Layer | One hop |
| **Packet routing across networks** | **Network Layer** | **Host-to-Host** |
| **Process-to-process data delivery** | **Transport Layer** | **End-to-End** |
| User-facing services | Application Layer | End-to-End (logical) |

#### 2.2.2 The Complementary Relationship

The network layer provides **logical communication between hosts**, while the transport layer extends this to **logical communication between processes**. The transport layer can be thought of as a **"refinement service"** that takes the raw, unreliable host-to-host delivery of IP and adds the missing pieces:

- **Process identification** (port numbers)
- **Reliability** (if using TCP)
- **Ordering** (if using TCP)
- **Flow regulation** (if using TCP)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** A common 3-mark question is *"Why can't the network layer provide process-to-process delivery?"* The answer is: **the network layer deals with packets (routing), not processes (applications). IP addresses identify machines, not programs. Port numbers exist only at the transport layer.**

#### 2.2.3 Analogy: Postal System

| Real-World Component | Network/Transport Layer |
|---|---|
| Postal address (house number + street) | IP address (Network Layer) |
| Recipient's name (Mr. X, Apartment 4B) | Port number (Transport Layer) |
| Mailman delivers to the house | Router forwards the IP packet |
| Apartment's intercom delivers to the person | Transport layer delivers to the socket |

#### 2.2.4 The "Service" Relationship — A Formal View

In the **OSI service model**:

- The **Network Layer** provides a *service* to the **Transport Layer** at the **Network Service Access Point (NSAP)**.
- The **Transport Layer** provides a *service* to the **Session/Application Layer** at the **Transport Service Access Point (TSAP)**.

The TSAP is realized as a **port number** in the TCP/IP model.

$$
\boxed{\text{NSAP} = \text{IP Address}, \quad \text{TSAP} = \text{Port Number}}
$$

A socket in the TCP/IP model is the **concatenation of NSAP and TSAP**:

$$
\text{Socket} = (\text{IP Address}, \text{Port Number})
$$

#### 2.2.5 Key Differences and Similarities

| Aspect | Network Layer | Transport Layer |
|---|---|---|
| OSI Layer | 3 | 4 |
| PDU | Packet | Segment (TCP) / Datagram (UDP) |
| Addressing | IP Address (32-bit / 128-bit) | Port Number (16-bit) |
| Scope of Addressing | Host | Process (application) |
| Core Function | Routing (forwarding packets) | Multiplexing/demultiplexing + QoS enhancement |
| Header Length | IP: 20 bytes, IPv6: 40 bytes | TCP: 20–60 bytes, UDP: 8 bytes |
| Reliability | Best-effort (no guarantee) | TCP: Reliable; UDP: Best-effort |
| Implemented In | Routers + end hosts | **Only** end hosts (not in routers) |
| Protocol Examples | IPv4, IPv6, OSPF, BGP | TCP, UDP, SCTP, DCCP |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crucial KTU Point:** Transport-layer functions are implemented **only in the end systems** (source and destination hosts). The **routers in the middle** do **not** implement TCP/UDP — they only look at the IP header. This is why transport-layer reliability is "end-to-end."

#### 2.2.6 The "End-to-End Argument" (Saltzer-Reed-Kaashoek, 1984)

A foundational networking principle (often tested in KTU):

> *"Intelligence (reliability, ordering, error control) should be placed at the edges of the network, not in the core. The network core should remain simple and fast; only the endpoints should handle complex reliability mechanisms."*

This is why **the Internet's network layer (IP) is intentionally simple and unreliable, while the transport layer (TCP) is complex and reliable**. Contrast this with the old telephone network (ATM), which had a complex network layer and a simple transport layer.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

$$
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
\text{Port Number Range} &= 0 \text{ to } 2^{16} - 1 = 0 \text{ to } 65535 \\
\text{Well-Known Ports} &= 0 \text{ to } 1023 \\
\text{Registered Ports} &= 1024 \text{ to } 49151 \\
\text{Dynamic Ports} &= 49152 \text{ to } 65535 \\
\text{UDP Header Size} &= 8 \text{ bytes (fixed)} \\
\text{TCP Header Size} &= 20 \text{ bytes (minimum)} \leq \text{ Header} \leq 60 \text{ bytes (with options)} \\
\text{Socket} &= (\text{IP Address}, \text{Port Number}) \\
\text{TCP Connection ID} &= (\text{Source IP}, \text{Src Port}, \text{Dest IP}, \text{Dest Port}) \\
\text{UDP Socket ID} &= (\text{Dest IP}, \text{Dest Port})
\end{aligned}
}
$$

| Concept | Key Value / Formula | KTU Importance |
|---|---|---|
| Maximum Port Number | $2^{16} - 1 = 65535$ | 2-mark direct question |
| UDP Header Size | 8 bytes (4 fields × 2 bytes each) | 2-mark direct question |
| TCP Header Size (min) | 20 bytes | 2-mark direct question |
| TCP 3-Way Handshake Steps | SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK | 5-mark question |
| Multiplexing | Sender side — many-to-one | Conceptual |
| Demultiplexing | Receiver side — one-to-many | Conceptual |
| TSAP | Port Number | Conceptual |
| NSAP | IP Address | Conceptual |
| End-to-End Argument | Reliability at edges, not core | 5-mark essay |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Web Browsing (HTTP/HTTPS):** Uses TCP port 80/443 for reliable, ordered page delivery.
- **Video Conferencing (Zoom, Teams):** Uses UDP for low-latency, real-time audio/video where some loss is acceptable.
- **Online Gaming:** Uses UDP for rapid position updates; a delayed packet is worse than a lost one.
- **DNS Lookups:** Uses UDP port 53 for fast query-response (typically < 512 bytes).
- **SSH Remote Login:** Uses TCP port 22 for secure, reliable terminal sessions.
- **IoT / Sensor Networks:** Use lightweight UDP variants (CoAP) to conserve battery and bandwidth.

The transport layer is where the **"Quality of Service"** decision is made in software architecture — choosing TCP vs. UDP is one of the most consequential decisions a network engineer makes.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Why $2^{16} - 1 = 65535$ is the Maximum Port Number

The transport layer port number is encoded in the **Source Port** and **Destination Port** fields of the TCP and UDP headers. Each of these fields has a width of exactly **16 bits**.

**Step 1: Identify the number of bits allocated for the port number field.**

$$
\text{Field Width} = 16 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate the total number of distinct values representable by a 16-bit field.**

For an $n$-bit binary field, the number of unique values ranges from $0$ to $2^n - 1$. Substituting $n = 16$:

$$
\text{Number of Unique Values} = 2^{16} - 1
$$

**Step 3: Compute the numerical value of $2^{16}$.**

$$
2^{16} = 2^{10} \times 2^{6} = 1024 \times 64 = 65536
$$

**Step 4: Subtract 1 to get the maximum port number.**

$$
\text{Maximum Port Number} = 2^{16} - 1 = 65536 - 1 = 65535
$$

**Step 5: Define the range.**

$$
\text{Port Number Range} = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, 65535
$$

**Step 6: Compute the total count of available port numbers.**

$$
\text{Total Available Ports} = 65535 - 0 + 1 = 65536
$$

This matches the expected total of $2^{16} = 65536$ possible port assignments, confirming the derivation.

$$
\boxed{\text{Total Port Numbers} = 2^{16} = 65536 \quad \text{; Maximum Port Number} = 65535}
$$

### 3.2 Derivation: UDP Header Size = 8 Bytes (Fixed)

The UDP header contains **four mandatory fields**, each of size **2 bytes (16 bits)**.

**Step 1: List the four fields of the UDP header.**

| Field | Size (bytes) |
|---|---|
| Source Port Number | 2 |
| Destination Port Number | 2 |
| Length (UDP header + data) | 2 |
| Checksum | 2 |

**Step 2: Sum the field sizes.**

$$
\text{UDP Header Size} = 2 + 2 + 2 + 2 = 8 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 3: Verify using the binary representation.**

$$
8 \text{ bytes} = 8 \times 8 \text{ bits} = 64 \text{ bits} = 2^6 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 4: Confirm that UDP has no optional fields.**

Unlike TCP, UDP does **not** support header options. Therefore, the header is **always exactly 8 bytes**, regardless of payload size.

$$
\boxed{\text{UDP Header} = 8 \text{ bytes (fixed)}}
$$

### 3.3 Derivation: TCP Header Size — Minimum 20 Bytes, Maximum 60 Bytes

**Step 1: List the 10 mandatory (fixed) fields of the TCP header.**

| Field | Size (bytes) |
|---|---|
| Source Port | 2 |
| Destination Port | 2 |
| Sequence Number | 4 |
| Acknowledgment Number | 4 |
| Data Offset (Header Length) | 0.5 (4 bits) |
| Reserved / Flags (URG, ACK, PSH, RST, SYN, FIN) | 1.5 (12 bits) |
| Window Size | 2 |
| Checksum | 2 |
| Urgent Pointer | 2 |
| **Subtotal (Fixed)** | **20 bytes** |

**Step 2: Sum the fixed fields.**

$$
\text{Minimum TCP Header Size} = 2 + 2 + 4 + 4 + 1 + 1 + 2 + 2 + 2 = 20 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 3: Account for optional fields.**

The TCP header may include **Options + Padding** of size $N$ bytes, where:

$$
0 \text{ bytes} \leq N \leq 40 \text{ bytes}
$$

This is because the **Data Offset** field is 4 bits wide, which encodes the header length in units of 32-bit (4-byte) words. The Data Offset can represent values from **5 to 15** (in 32-bit words), which corresponds to:

$$
\text{Minimum Header} = 5 \times 4 = 20 \text{ bytes}
$$
$$
\text{Maximum Header} = 15 \times 4 = 60 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 4: Final range of TCP header size.**

$$
\boxed{20 \text{ bytes} \leq \text{TCP Header Size} \leq 60 \text{ bytes}}
$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: TCP vs. UDP vs. IP — Layered Comparison

| Layer | Protocol | Service Type | Reliability | PDU Name | Addressing |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Transport (4) | **TCP** | Connection-oriented | **Yes** (reliable) | Segment | Port (16-bit) |
| Transport (4) | **UDP** | Connectionless | **No** (best-effort) | Datagram | Port (16-bit) |
| Network (3) | **IP (IPv4/IPv6)** | Connectionless | **No** (best-effort) | Packet | IP Address (32/128-bit) |

### 3.5 Analytical Derivation: How Multiplexing/Demultiplexing Works

**Scenario:** Host A (IP = 192.168.1.10) has two processes — Process P1 using port 5000 and Process P2 using port 5001 — both sending data to Host B (IP = 10.0.0.5, Port 80).

**Step 1: Multiplexing at Host A (Sender Side).**

- P1 generates data → Transport layer adds header with `Source Port = 5000`, `Dest Port = 80`.
- P2 generates data → Transport layer adds header with `Source Port = 5001`, `Dest Port = 80`.
- Both segments are handed to the **Network Layer** and transmitted using a single IP connection.

**Step 2: Network Layer routing.**

- Routers in the network forward the IP packets to Host B based on the destination IP address (10.0.0.5).
- **Routers do NOT inspect transport-layer headers.** They only look at the IP address.

**Step 3: Demultiplexing at Host B (Receiver Side).**

- Transport layer at Host B examines each segment's **Destination Port = 80**.
- All segments with `Dest Port = 80` are delivered to the **web server socket** (e.g., Apache/Nginx on port 80).
- The web server uses the **4-tuple (Source IP, Source Port, Dest IP, Dest Port)** to distinguish between the two client connections.

**Step 4: Resulting Socket Table at Host B.**

| Client IP | Client Port | Server IP | Server Port | Socket FD |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 192.168.1.10 | 5000 | 10.0.0.5 | 80 | 1 |
| 192.168.1.10 | 5001 | 10.0.0.5 | 80 | 2 |

This is the **core of TCP demultiplexing** — the 4-tuple uniqueness ensures that even if the same client opens multiple connections, the server can keep them separate.

### 3.6 Analytical Proof: Why Network Layer Alone Cannot Provide Process-to-Process Delivery

**Hypothesis:** The network layer (IP) can deliver packets host-to-host, but cannot distinguish between processes on the same host.

**Step 1: Consider a single host with three active applications:**

- Web browser (wants to access http://example.com)
- Email client (sending mail via SMTP)
- Video call (using UDP on some port)

All three are running on the **same host** with the **same IP address** (e.g., 192.168.1.10). They simultaneously exchange data with **different remote servers**.

**Step 2: When a packet arrives at the host, the network layer consults the IP address only.**

- The IP address matches — the packet is delivered to the host.
- **But the network layer has no way to know** whether the packet belongs to the browser, the email client, or the video call.

**Step 3: The transport layer resolves this ambiguity by adding a port number.**

- The browser uses port 80 (HTTP) or 443 (HTTPS).
- The email client uses port 25 (SMTP) or 587 (SMTP submission).
- The video call uses an ephemeral port (e.g., 50000).

**Step 4: Conclusion.**

$$
\boxed{\text{IP address} = \text{Host identity} \quad ; \quad \text{Port number} = \text{Process identity}}
$$

Without port numbers, multiple processes on a single host cannot be uniquely identified. Therefore, **process-to-process delivery is impossible without the transport layer**.

### 3.7 Quantitative Comparison of TCP vs. UDP Header Overhead

**Given:** A payload of 1000 bytes.

**Step 1: Calculate the total bytes on the wire for UDP.**

$$
\text{Total UDP Bytes} = \text{UDP Header} + \text{Payload} = 8 + 1000 = 1008 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate the total bytes on the wire for TCP (minimum header).**

$$
\text{Total TCP Bytes} = \text{TCP Header} + \text{Payload} = 20 + 1000 = 1020 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate overhead percentage.**

For UDP:

$$
\text{UDP Overhead \%} = \frac{8}{1008} \times 100\% \approx 0.794\%
$$

For TCP:

$$
\text{TCP Overhead \%} = \frac{20}{1020} \times 100\% \approx 1.961\%
$$

**Step 4: Ratio of overheads.**

$$
\frac{\text{TCP Overhead}}{\text{UDP Overhead}} = \frac{20}{8} = 2.5
$$

**Step 5: Interpretation.**

TCP incurs **2.5 times more header overhead** than UDP. For small payloads (e.g., 10 bytes of DNS data), the overhead becomes significant:

$$
\text{UDP Overhead (10B payload)} = \frac{8}{8+10} \times 100\% \approx 44.4\%
$$
$$
\text{TCP Overhead (10B payload)} = \frac{20}{20+10} \times 100\% \approx 66.7\%
$$

This quantifies **why UDP is preferred for small, latency-sensitive messages**.

$$
\boxed{\text{UDP} = \text{Lower latency, lower overhead, no reliability} \quad ; \quad \text{TCP} = \text{Higher reliability, higher overhead}}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1: Layered Position of the Transport Layer in the TCP/IP Model

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Application Layer<br>HTTP, FTP, DNS, SMTP, SSH]:::app
    B[Transport Layer<br>TCP, UDP - Port Addressing]:::trans
    C[Network Layer<br>IPv4, IPv6 - Logical Addressing]:::net
    D[Data Link Layer<br>Ethernet, Wi-Fi - MAC Addressing]:::link
    E[Physical Layer<br>Cables, Fiber, Radio Waves]:::phy

    A -->|Data + Header| B
    B -->|Segment or Datagram| C
    C -->|IP Packet| D
    D -->|Frame| E
    E -->|Bits| F((Transmission Medium)):::medium

    B -.->|Provides service to| A
    C -.->|Provides service to| B
    D -.->|Provides service to| C
    E -.->|Provides service to| D

    classDef app fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef trans fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFF
    classDef net fill:#4682B4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    classDef link fill:#32CD32,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    classDef phy fill:#9370DB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    classDef medium fill:#FFA500,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

**Interpretation:** Notice that the **Transport Layer (highlighted in red)** is the **lowest layer that deals with end-to-end meaning** and the **highest layer that deals with end-to-end transmission strategy**. It bridges the application-oriented upper layers with the network-oriented lower layers.

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2: Multiplexing and Demultiplexing Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph SRC["Sender Host - 192.168.1.10"]
        P1[Process 1<br>Browser<br>Port 5000]:::p
        P2[Process 2<br>Email Client<br>Port 5001]:::p
        P3[Process 3<br>Video App<br>Port 5002]:::p
        MUX{Transport Layer<br>Multiplexer}:::mux
    end

    MUX -->|IP Packet| NET[Network Layer<br>IP Routing]:::net

    NET -->|IP Packet| DMUX{Transport Layer<br>Demultiplexer}:::dmux

    subgraph DST["Receiver Host - 10.0.0.5"]
        Q1[Web Server<br>Port 80]:::q
        Q2[Mail Server<br>Port 25]:::q
        Q3[Video Server<br>Port 5060]:::q
    end

    DMUX -->|Dest Port 80| Q1
    DMUX -->|Dest Port 25| Q2
    DMUX -->|Dest Port 5060| Q3

    classDef p fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef q fill:#FFE4E1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef mux fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFF
    classDef dmux fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFF
    classDef net fill:#4682B4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
```

**Interpretation:** This diagram visualizes the **multiplexing/demultiplexing process**. On the sender side, three application processes (with different source ports) are merged into a single network-layer stream. On the receiver side, the single network-layer stream is split into three distinct process deliveries based on the **destination port number**.

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3: Relationship Between Transport and Network Layers

```mermaid
graph TD
    APP[Application Layer<br>Needs: Reliable, Ordered Data]:::app
    TL[Transport Layer<br>TCP or UDP<br>Provides: Process-to-Process Delivery]:::trans
    NL[Network Layer<br>IPv4/IPv6<br>Provides: Host-to-Host Delivery]:::net
    LL[Data Link Layer<br>Ethernet<br>Provides: Node-to-Node Delivery]:::link

    APP -->|"Asks for service<br>via port number"| TL
    TL -->|"Asks for service<br>via IP address"| NL
    NL -->|"Asks for service<br>via MAC address"| LL

    APP -.->|"Service Provided:<br>Data to the correct process"| APP1[End-User]:::user
    TL -.->|"Service Provided:<br>Process-to-Process Channel"| APP
    NL -.->|"Service Provided:<br>Host-to-Host Channel"| TL
    LL -.->|"Service Provided:<br>Node-to-Node Channel"| NL

    classDef app fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef trans fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFF
    classDef net fill:#4682B4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    classDef link fill:#32CD32,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    classDef user fill:#FFA500,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

**Interpretation:** This diagram emphasizes the **service-stacking relationship**. Each layer **uses the services of the layer below** and **provides enhanced services to the layer above**. The transport layer is the **first layer that offers end-to-end (host-to-host) service enhancement**.

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4: TCP 3-Way Handshake (Connection Establishment)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Client
    participant Server

    Note over Client,Server: Connection Establishment Phase
    Client->>Server: SYN (Seq = x, SYN bit = 1)
    Server-->>Client: SYN-ACK (Seq = y, Ack = x+1, SYN & ACK bits = 1)
    Client->>Server: ACK (Seq = x+1, Ack = y+1, ACK bit = 1)

    Note over Client,Server: Connection Established - Data Transfer Begins
    Client->>Server: Data Segment (Seq = x+1)
    Server-->>Client: ACK (Ack = x+data_length)

    Note over Client,Server: Connection Termination Phase
    Client->>Server: FIN (FIN bit = 1)
    Server-->>Client: ACK
    Server-->>Client: FIN
    Client->>Server: ACK
```

**Interpretation:** The **3-way handshake** is the canonical example of how the transport layer (TCP) sets up a connection before any data flows. The **SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK** sequence synchronizes sequence numbers and establishes state on both sides — a feature absent in UDP.

### 4.5 Mermaid Diagram 5: PDU Encapsulation Across Layers

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Application Data<br>Hello World]:::data
    B[TCP Segment<br>TCP Header + Data<br>20-60 bytes header]:::trans
    C[IP Packet<br>IP Header + TCP Segment<br>20 bytes IP header]:::net
    D[Ethernet Frame<br>MAC Header + IP Packet + MAC Trailer<br>14+ bytes header]:::link
    E[Physical Bits<br>Transmitted over cable/wireless]:::phy

    A -->|Add TCP header| B
    B -->|Add IP header| C
    C -->|Add MAC header + trailer| D
    D -->|Encode as bits| E

    classDef data fill:#FFFFFF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef trans fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    classDef net fill:#4682B4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    classDef link fill:#32CD32,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    classDef phy fill:#9370DB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
```

**Interpretation:** This **encapsulation flow** shows how application data progressively gains headers at each layer. The transport layer's **TCP/UDP header** is the **first header that contains port numbers**, enabling process-to-process delivery.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Q1. Define the Transport Layer. What are its main responsibilities?**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

The **Transport Layer** is Layer 4 of the OSI reference model and the TCP/IP protocol suite. It is responsible for **process-to-process (end-to-end) delivery** of data between application programs running on different hosts connected to a network.

**Main Responsibilities:**

1. **Process-to-Process Addressing:** Uses 16-bit port numbers (0–65535) to identify specific application processes.
2. **Multiplexing and Demultiplexing:** Combines multiple application data streams into one network-layer connection (multiplexing) and separates incoming data to the correct application (demultiplexing).
3. **Connection Management:** TCP establishes, maintains, and terminates connections using a 3-way handshake.
4. **Error Control, Flow Control, and Congestion Control:** Provided by TCP through acknowledgments, sequence numbers, and window-based mechanisms.
5. **Reliability Services:** TCP guarantees ordered, error-free delivery; UDP provides best-effort, unreliable service.

---

#### **Q2. Differentiate between TCP and UDP. Mention two applications of each.**
`[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | TCP | UDP |
|---|---|---|
| Type | Connection-oriented | Connectionless |
| Reliability | Reliable (ACK + retransmission) | Unreliable (no ACK) |
| Header Size | 20–60 bytes | 8 bytes (fixed) |
| Speed | Slower | Faster |
| Flow Control | Yes | No |
| Congestion Control | Yes | No |
| PDU Name | Segment | Datagram |
| Ordering | Guaranteed | Not guaranteed |

**TCP Applications:** HTTP, HTTPS, FTP, SSH, SMTP (Email), Telnet.

**UDP Applications:** DNS, VoIP, Video Streaming (live), Online Gaming, SNMP, TFTP.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the relationship between the Transport Layer and the Network Layer with a suitable diagram. How does the transport layer enhance the network layer's services?

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the process of multiplexing and demultiplexing in the transport layer. Distinguish between TCP and UDP demultiplexing.

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

**Step 1: Position in the Protocol Stack (2 marks)**

The Transport Layer (Layer 4) sits above the Network Layer (Layer 3) in both the OSI and TCP/IP models. It provides its services to the upper layers (Session, Presentation, Application) while using the services of the Network Layer below it.

**Step 2: Service Relationship (2 marks)**

- The **Network Layer** provides **host-to-host logical communication** using **IP addresses**. It guarantees neither delivery nor ordering — its service model is **best-effort**.
- The **Transport Layer** enhances this by providing **process-to-process logical communication** using **port numbers** (in addition to IP addresses). It adds **reliability, ordering, flow control, and congestion control** (in the case of TCP).

**Step 3: Diagrammatic Representation (1 mark)**

```
[ Application Layer ]
        |  (Port-based service)
[ Transport Layer (TCP/UDP) ]
        |  (IP-based service)
[ Network Layer (IP) ]
        |
[ Data Link Layer ]
        |
[ Physical Layer ]
```

**Step 4: Key Enhancements Provided by the Transport Layer (2 marks)**

| Enhancement | Description |
|---|---|
| Process Addressing | Identifies specific application using 16-bit port numbers |
| Multiplexing | Allows multiple applications to use a single network connection |
| Reliability (TCP) | Guarantees delivery via ACKs and retransmission |
| Ordering (TCP) | Uses sequence numbers to reorder segments |
| Flow Control (TCP) | Receiver window prevents fast sender from overwhelming slow receiver |
| Congestion Control (TCP) | Slow Start, Congestion Avoidance protect the network from collapse |

**Step 5: The End-to-End Argument (Bonus 1 mark)**

The Internet is designed on the principle that **complex functions belong at the edges (end systems), not in the core (routers)**. Hence, the Network Layer (IP) is kept intentionally simple, while the Transport Layer (TCP) carries the burden of reliability. This is the **end-to-end argument** (Saltzer, Reed, Kaashoek — 1984).

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Step 1: Definition of Multiplexing and Demultiplexing (2 marks)**

- **Multiplexing (Sender Side):** The transport layer accepts data from multiple application sockets, attaches the appropriate transport header (with source and destination port numbers), and passes the resulting segments to the network layer using a single network-layer service.
- **Demultiplexing (Receiver Side):** The transport layer receives segments from the network layer, examines the header fields, and delivers each segment's payload to the correct application socket.

**Step 2: Diagram (3 marks)**

```
[Sender Host]                               [Receiver Host]
                                              
[App1 - Port 5000] --\                        /-- [Web Server - Port 80]
                     \                      /
[App2 - Port 5001] -- Multiplexer --> IP -- Demultiplexer --> [Mail Server - Port 25]
                     /                      \
[App3 - Port 5002] --/                        \-- [DNS Server - Port 53]
                                              
   (Single Network Connection)         (Multiple Application Deliveries)
```

**Step 3: UDP Demultiplexing (1 mark)**

UDP demultiplexing uses only the **2-tuple (Destination IP Address, Destination Port Number)**. All UDP segments arriving at the same destination IP + port are delivered to the same socket, regardless of the source IP or source port.

**Step 4: TCP Demultiplexing (1 mark)**

TCP demultiplexing uses the **4-tuple (Source IP, Source Port, Destination IP, Destination Port)**. Each unique 4-tuple corresponds to a distinct socket. This allows a single web server (e.g., on port 80) to maintain **thousands of simultaneous client connections** without confusion.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the various services provided by the Transport Layer. Explain why these services cannot be provided by the Network Layer alone.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With neat sketches, explain the TCP 3-way handshake and 4-way termination procedures. What is the role of sequence numbers in this process?

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

**Step 1: List of Transport Layer Services (3 marks)**

1. **Process-to-Process Delivery** — Identifies specific application via port numbers.
2. **Multiplexing and Demultiplexing** — Many-to-one and one-to-many data delivery.
3. **Connection-Oriented Service (TCP)** — 3-way handshake establishes a virtual connection.
4. **Reliability** — Error detection, acknowledgment, and retransmission.
5. **Sequencing** — Reorders out-of-order segments using sequence numbers.
6. **Flow Control** — Sliding window prevents receiver buffer overflow.
7. **Congestion Control** — Prevents network collapse using cwnd management.

**Step 2: Why Network Layer Cannot Provide These (2 marks)**

The Network Layer (IP):
- Knows only **hosts** (via IP addresses), not **processes**.
- Provides **best-effort, unreliable, connectionless** service.
- Does **not maintain state** between packets.
- Is **implemented in routers**, which must process packets at high speed — adding reliability at the network layer would slow down the entire Internet.
- **Routers do not implement TCP/UDP** — they discard the transport header at every hop.

**Step 3: The Role of Port Numbers (1 mark)**

A single host can run hundreds of processes. IP addresses alone cannot distinguish them. Port numbers (16 bits, 0–65535) provide the second level of addressing required for process-to-process delivery.

**Step 4: Practical Justification (1 mark)**

If the network layer tried to provide reliability, every router would need to maintain connection state — making routing tables astronomically large and routers impossibly slow. The **end-to-end principle** solves this elegantly.

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Step 1: 3-Way Handshake — Diagram (3 marks)**

```
        Client                           Server
          |                                |
          |-------- SYN (Seq=x) --------->|   (1)
          |                                |
          |<--- SYN-ACK (Seq=y, Ack=x+1) -|   (2)
          |                                |
          |-------- ACK (Seq=x+1, Ack=y+1) ->|   (3)
          |                                |
          |<====== Connection Established ====>|
          |                                |
```

**Step 2: Explanation of Each Step (2 marks)**

- **Step 1 (SYN):** The client sends a segment with the **SYN (Synchronize) flag = 1** and an **initial sequence number (ISN = x)**. This signals the intent to open a connection.
- **Step 2 (SYN-ACK):** The server responds with **SYN = 1, ACK = 1**, acknowledges the client's sequence number (`Ack = x+1`), and provides its own ISN (`y`).
- **Step 3 (ACK):** The client acknowledges the server's ISN with `Ack = y+1`. The connection is now **fully established** and data transfer can begin.

**Step 3: 4-Way Termination — Diagram (1 mark)**

```
        Client                           Server
          |                                |
          |-------- FIN (FIN=1) --------->|   (1)
          |                                |
          |<----------- ACK --------------|   (2)
          |                                |
          |<----------- FIN --------------|   (3)
          |                                |
          |----------- ACK -------------->|   (4)
          |                                |
          |<====== Connection Closed ====>|
```

**Step 4: Role of Sequence Numbers (1 mark)**

Sequence numbers (32-bit fields) serve three critical purposes:
1. **Initial Synchronization:** The ISNs (x, y) synchronize both sides during the handshake.
2. **Ordering:** Each segment is tagged with a sequence number, allowing the receiver to reassemble data in the correct order even if segments arrive out of order.
3. **Loss Detection & Retransmission:** If an ACK is not received within a timeout, the sender retransmits the unacknowledged segment using the sequence number as a reference.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Network and Transport Layer addresses:** Students often write "port IP address" or "IP port number." The correct terminology is **"IP address" (Network Layer)** and **"port number" (Transport Layer)**.
> 2. **Forgetting that routers don't implement TCP/UDP:** A frequent error is to claim that "routers use transport-layer reliability." This is **false** — routers only implement up to Layer 3.
> 3. **Stating TCP header is "20 bytes" without mentioning the 60-byte maximum:** Examiners expect the full range (20–60 bytes) due to options.
> 4. **Confusing multiplexing direction:** Students sometimes write "demultiplexing happens at the sender." Remember: **multiplexing = sender side; demultiplexing = receiver side**.
> 5. **Missing the End-to-End Argument:** This is a frequent bonus point worth 1–2 marks. Always mention it in any question on transport/network layer relationship.
> 6. **Drawing TCP handshake with 2 steps instead of 3:** A common error. There are **exactly 3 steps**: SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- 📌 The **Transport Layer** is the **4th layer** in the OSI model and provides **process-to-process** delivery.
- 📌 **Port numbers are 16 bits** (0 to **65535**); **2^16 = 65536** total possible values.
- 📌 **UDP header = 8 bytes (fixed)**, **TCP header = 20–60 bytes**.
- 📌 **TCP = connection-oriented, reliable, ordered, with flow and congestion control.**
- 📌 **UDP = connectionless, unreliable, unordered, minimal overhead, low latency.**
- 📌 **Network Layer = host-to-host** (uses **IP addresses**); **Transport Layer = process-to-process** (uses **port numbers**).
- 📌 A **socket = (IP address, port number)** — the TCP/IP endpoint.
- 📌 **TCP demultiplexing** uses a **4-tuple**; **UDP demultiplexing** uses a **2-tuple** (Destination IP + Port).
- 📌 **Multiplexing** happens at the **sender**; **Demultiplexing** happens at the **receiver**.
- 📌 **Routers do NOT implement TCP/UDP** — only end systems do (end-to-end argument).
- 📌 **Well-known ports: 0–1023.** IANA governs port assignments.
- 📌 TCP uses a **3-way handshake** (SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK) and **4-way termination** (FIN → ACK → FIN → ACK).
- 📌 The **End-to-End Argument** is the foundational design principle: **intelligence at the edges, simplicity in the core**.
- 📌 Common application mappings: **HTTP→80, HTTPS→443, FTP→21, SSH→22, DNS→53, SMTP→25, Telnet→23**.
- 📌 Transport layer **enhances** the network layer — it does not replace it.
- 📌 The **Transport Layer is implemented ONLY in end systems** (source and destination hosts), not in the network core.
- 📌 TCP provides **reliability on top of an unreliable IP** — IP is best-effort; TCP makes it look reliable to applications.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Overview of the Transport Layer in the Internet
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT631 - NETWORK & DATA SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Overview of the Transport Layer in the Internet

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Transport Layer** is **Layer 4** of both the **OSI (Open Systems Interconnection) Reference Model** and the **TCP/IP Protocol Suite**. It is the conceptual heart of end-to-end communication, providing **host-to-host** (process-to-process) data delivery services to applications running on networked devices.

In the **TCP/IP architecture**, the transport layer sits directly above the **Internet (Network) Layer** and below the **Application Layer**. Its primary role is to offer a logical, reliable (or best-effort) conduit between **application processes** executing on different hosts, abstracting away the underlying physical and logical topology of the network.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:**
> The transport layer is responsible for **process-to-process delivery** — extending the network layer's host-to-host service up to the application's doorsteps. The two dominant Internet transport protocols are **TCP (Transmission Control Protocol)** — connection-oriented and reliable — and **UDP (User Datagram Protocol)** — connectionless and best-effort.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Postal Service

Imagine the **Internet** as a vast global postal system:

- The **Network Layer (IP)** is like the **truck and highway system** that moves envelopes between cities (hosts). It only knows the destination **city address (IP address)**, not the **person** inside the house.
- The **Transport Layer** is the **letter carrier at the receiving end**, who looks at the apartment number / recipient name on the envelope and delivers the mail to the **correct person inside the building**.

So while the IP layer moves data between **two computers**, the transport layer ensures the data reaches the **correct running program** (a web browser, an email client, a video stream) on that computer. The "apartment number" in this analogy is the **Port Number**, and the "person" is the **application process / socket**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for Exams:**
> - **Network Layer → Host-to-Host delivery** (IP addresses identify machines)
> - **Transport Layer → Process-to-Process delivery** (Port numbers identify applications)

## 1.3 Primary Services Offered by the Transport Layer

The transport layer offers the following core services to the upper layers:

| Service | Description |
|---|---|
| **Process-to-Process Addressing** | Uses **port numbers** (16-bit, range **0–65535**) to identify specific applications/sockets. |
| **Multiplexing / Demultiplexing** | Allows multiple applications on one host to simultaneously use the network through port differentiation. |
| **Connection Management** | Establishes, maintains, and terminates logical connections (TCP's 3-way handshake / 4-way termination). |
| **Reliable Data Transfer** | Guarantees in-order, error-free delivery using acknowledgements, sequence numbers, and retransmissions (TCP only). |
| **Flow Control** | Prevents a fast sender from overwhelming a slow receiver using a **sliding window** mechanism. |
| **Congestion Control** | Prevents network collapse by throttling senders when routers are overloaded (TCP Reno, Cubic, BBR). |
| **Error Control** | Detects lost or corrupted segments via **checksums**, **ACKs**, and **timeouts**. |

## 1.4 Position of the Transport Layer in the TCP/IP Stack

The transport layer is **sandwiched** between the application layer above and the internet layer below. It receives data from applications, breaks it into **segments** (TCP) or **datagrams** (UDP), and hands them to IP for transmission.

> [!NOTE]
> The unit of data at the transport layer is called a **Segment** (in TCP) or a **User Datagram** (in UDP). After the network layer encapsulates this, it becomes a **Packet** or **Datagram** (at IP), and at the link layer it is called a **Frame**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Do We Need a Separate Transport Layer?

The network layer (IP) is fundamentally **unreliable, connectionless, and best-effort** — it makes no delivery guarantees. IP packets can be:
- Lost
- Duplicated
- Reordered
- Corrupted

Yet applications demand varied qualities of service:
- A **file transfer** needs 100% reliability.
- A **live video call** needs low latency more than reliability.
- A **DNS query** needs a single quick response.

The transport layer bridges this gap by providing **differentiated service semantics** to applications. It is the **only layer** in the TCP/IP model that truly talks to applications.

## 2.2 The Two Principal Protocols — TCP vs UDP

| Property | **TCP (Transmission Control Protocol)** | **UDP (User Datagram Protocol)** |
|---|---|---|
| Connection Type | **Connection-oriented** | **Connectionless** |
| Reliability | **Reliable** (ACKs, retransmission) | **Unreliable** (best-effort) |
| Ordering | In-order delivery guaranteed | No ordering guarantee |
| Flow Control | Yes (sliding window) | No |
| Congestion Control | Yes (cwnd-based) | No |
| Header Size | **20 bytes** (min) — up to 60 bytes | **8 bytes** (fixed) |
| Speed | Slower (overhead) | Faster (low overhead) |
| Use Cases | HTTP/HTTPS, FTP, SSH, Email | DNS, VoIP, Video streaming, TFTP, SNMP |
| Multiplexing | Port-based | Port-based |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favourite Question:** "Differentiate between TCP and UDP." This is asked in nearly every semester — be ready with at least 6–8 distinguishing points.

## 2.3 Port Numbers — The Address of the Transport Layer

A **port number** is a **16-bit unsigned integer**, theoretically ranging from **0 to 65535** ($2^{16} - 1$). They are divided into three ranges by IANA:

| Range | Decimal Span | Purpose | Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Well-Known Ports** | **0 – 1023** | Reserved for privileged system services | HTTP = **80**, HTTPS = **443**, FTP = **21**, SSH = **22**, DNS = **53**, SMTP = **25** |
| **Registered Ports** | **1024 – 49151** | Assigned to user-level processes / apps | MySQL = 3306, PostgreSQL = 5432 |
| **Dynamic / Ephemeral Ports** | **49152 – 65535** | Assigned temporarily by OS to client sockets | Allocated when a client initiates an outgoing connection |

> [!NOTE]
> A **socket** = **(IP Address, Port Number, Protocol)** — the unique endpoint of a network conversation. In programming, sockets are the API used by application developers to access transport services.

## 2.4 Multiplexing and Demultiplexing

These are arguably the **most fundamental transport layer functions**:

- **Multiplexing at Sender:** Multiple application processes on the source host generate data. The transport layer collects data from all these sockets and forwards it down to the network layer, tagged with appropriate **source port numbers** so the receiver can route replies.
- **Demultiplexing at Receiver:** When segments arrive, the transport layer examines the **destination port number** in the header and delivers each segment to the correct application socket.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** Multiplexing happens at the **sender**, demultiplexing at the **receiver**. The destination port number is the demultiplexing key.

There are two flavours:
1. **Connectionless Demultiplexing (UDP):** Demultiplexing is based purely on the **destination port number** only. Each socket is identified by a 2-tuple `(destination IP, destination port)`.
2. **Connection-Oriented Demultiplexing (TCP):** Demultiplexing is based on the **4-tuple** `(source IP, source port, destination IP, destination port)`. This allows multiple simultaneous TCP connections from the same host to the same server (e.g., a browser opening 6 tabs to the same website).

## 2.5 Connection-Oriented Service — The TCP Handshakes

### 2.5.1 Three-Way Handshake (Connection Establishment)
To prevent delayed duplicate packets from corrupting a new connection, TCP uses a **3-step synchronization**:

1. **SYN:** Client sends a segment with `SYN=1`, initial sequence number `SEQ = x`.
2. **SYN-ACK:** Server responds with `SYN=1, ACK=1`, sequence `SEQ = y`, acknowledgement `ACK = x+1`.
3. **ACK:** Client sends `ACK=1`, `ACK = y+1`. Connection is now **ESTABLISHED**.

### 2.5.2 Four-Way Handshake (Connection Termination)
TCP uses a modified 3-way handshake to gracefully close a connection, because TCP is **full-duplex** (both sides send FIN independently):

1. **FIN:** Client → Server (`FIN=1, SEQ=u`)
2. **ACK:** Server → Client (`ACK=1, ACK=u+1`)
3. **FIN:** Server → Client (`FIN=1, SEQ=v`)
4. **ACK:** Client → Server (`ACK=1, ACK=v+1`) → **TIME_WAIT** state.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Value | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Port Number Range | $0 \leq P \leq 2^{16}-1 = 65535$ | **16-bit** unsigned integer |
| Total number of ports | $2^{16} = 65536$ | Excludes the "all zeros" conceptual range |
| Well-known ports | $0 \text{ to } 1023$ | **1024** ports |
| Registered ports | $1024 \text{ to } 49151$ | $49151 - 1024 + 1 = 48128$ ports |
| Ephemeral ports | $49152 \text{ to } 65535$ | $65535 - 49152 + 1 = 16384$ ports |
| TCP Header (min) | **20 bytes** | Without options |
| UDP Header (fixed) | **8 bytes** | Source port(2)+Dest port(2)+Length(2)+Checksum(2) |
| TCP Sequence Number | 32-bit | Wraps at $2^{32}$ |
| TCP Window Size | 16-bit (header) — scaled | Max ~ **64 KB** (or 1 GB with window scaling) |
| Checksum | 16-bit one's complement sum | Same algorithm for TCP, UDP, IP |
| Socket Address | $(\text{IP}, \text{Port}, \text{Protocol})$ | 3-tuple |
| TCP 4-tuple | $(\text{SrcIP}, \text{SrcPort}, \text{DstIP}, \text{DstPort})$ | Uniquely identifies a connection |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Software Engineering:** Every network application programmer uses the **Berkeley Sockets API** (`socket()`, `bind()`, `listen()`, `accept()`, `connect()`) which directly interfaces with the transport layer.
- **Cybersecurity:** Knowledge of port numbers is the bedrock of **firewall rule design**, **intrusion detection**, and **port scanning (Nmap)**.
- **Performance Engineering:** Understanding TCP's **congestion window (cwnd)** is essential for tuning data centre and cloud workloads.
- **5G/IoT:** UDP variants like **QUIC** (over UDP) are now used in HTTP/3 to reduce handshake latency on mobile networks.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Calculating Total Number of Available Ports

A port number is stored in a **16-bit field**. The number of distinct values representable in $n$ bits is $2^n$.

$$
N_{\text{ports}} = 2^{16}
$$

Since port numbering starts from **0**, the maximum port value is $2^{16} - 1$.

$$
P_{\max} = 2^{16} - 1 = 65536 - 1 = 65535
$$

**Total distinct port numbers** = Maximum value $-$ Minimum value $+$ 1.

$$
N_{\text{total}} = 65535 - 0 + 1 = 65536
$$

This matches $2^{16}$, confirming the derivation.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Size of Each Port Range

### Step 1 — Well-Known Ports

$$
N_{\text{well-known}} = 1023 - 0 + 1 = 1024 \text{ ports}
$$

### Step 2 — Registered Ports

$$
N_{\text{registered}} = 49151 - 1024 + 1 = 48128 \text{ ports}
$$

### Step 3 — Dynamic / Ephemeral Ports

$$
N_{\text{ephemeral}} = 65535 - 49152 + 1 = 16384 \text{ ports}
$$

### Step 4 — Verification

$$
N_{\text{total}} = 1024 + 48128 + 16384
$$

$$
N_{\text{total}} = 65536
$$

$$
\checkmark \; \text{Consistent with } 2^{16}.
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: TCP Header Size

The TCP header has the following fixed fields (each field width in bits):

| Field | Width (bits) |
|---|---|
| Source Port | 16 |
| Destination Port | 16 |
| Sequence Number | 32 |
| Acknowledgement Number | 32 |
| Data Offset + Reserved + Flags | 16 |
| Window Size | 16 |
| Checksum | 16 |
| Urgent Pointer | 16 |
| **Total** | **160 bits** |

Converting to bytes:

$$
\text{Header Size} = \frac{160 \text{ bits}}{8 \text{ bits/byte}} = 20 \text{ bytes}
$$

If **options** are present, an additional $N \times 4$ bytes are appended (up to 40 bytes). Therefore:

$$
\text{TCP Header Size} = 20 + 4N \text{ bytes}, \quad 0 \leq N \leq 10
$$

$$
\text{Maximum TCP Header} = 20 + 40 = 60 \text{ bytes}
$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation: UDP Header Size

The UDP header is fixed with four fields, each 16 bits:

$$
\text{UDP Header} = 4 \text{ fields} \times 16 \text{ bits} = 64 \text{ bits}
$$

$$
\text{UDP Header Size} = \frac{64 \text{ bits}}{8} = 8 \text{ bytes}
$$

The UDP **total length field** (header + data) has a minimum value of 8 (header alone), making the smallest possible UDP datagram exactly **8 bytes**.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — TCP vs UDP Header Comparison

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta H_{\text{bytes}} &= H_{\text{TCP,min}} - H_{\text{UDP}} \\
&= 20 - 8 \\
&= 12 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** A minimal TCP segment carries **12 bytes more overhead** than a UDP datagram of the same payload. For a tiny payload of, say, 1 byte:
- UDP datagram = $1 + 8 = 9$ bytes (overhead = **88.9%**)
- TCP segment = $1 + 20 = 21$ bytes (overhead = **95.2%**)

This is why UDP is preferred for small, latency-sensitive messages (e.g., DNS queries, gaming packets).

---

## 3.6 Step-by-Step: TCP Three-Way Handshake Sequence Numbers

Let the client's initial sequence number be $x$ and the server's initial sequence number be $y$.

### Step 1 — SYN (Client → Server)
$$
\text{Flag set: } SYN = 1
$$
$$
\text{SEQ} = x, \quad \text{ACK} = \text{(not sent)}
$$

### Step 2 — SYN-ACK (Server → Client)
$$
\text{Flag set: } SYN = 1, \quad ACK = 1
$$
$$
\text{SEQ} = y, \quad \text{ACK} = x + 1
$$

The server acknowledges the client's SYN by setting `ACK = x + 1` (next expected byte).

### Step 3 — ACK (Client → Server)
$$
\text{Flag set: } ACK = 1
$$
$$
\text{SEQ} = x + 1, \quad \text{ACK} = y + 1
$$

The connection state now transitions to **ESTABLISHED** on both sides.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Both sides consume **one sequence number** for the SYN (and one for the FIN), even though no data is carried. This is why the acknowledgement is `$x+1$` and not `$x$`.

---

## 3.7 Worked Example: Identifying a Socket

A user opens `https://www.ktu.edu.in` in Chrome. The browser opens **5 simultaneous TCP connections** to fetch different sub-resources.

- Server IP: `103.42.156.7`
- Server Port: `443` (HTTPS)
- Client IP: `10.0.0.55`
- Client Ephemeral Ports: `49152, 49153, 49154, 49155, 49156`

**Question:** How does the server demultiplex incoming segments back to the correct browser tab?

**Solution:** Each of the 5 connections has a **distinct 4-tuple**:

$$
(\text{SrcIP}, \text{SrcPort}, \text{DstIP}, \text{DstPort})
$$

| Tab | Source Port |
|---|---|
| Tab 1 | 49152 |
| Tab 2 | 49153 |
| Tab 3 | 49154 |
| Tab 4 | 49155 |
| Tab 5 | 49156 |

Since each connection has a unique ephemeral source port, the server can route incoming HTTP responses correctly using the source port as a discriminator. This is the essence of **connection-oriented demultiplexing**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Transport Layer Position in the TCP/IP Stack

This diagram maps the transport layer's hierarchical position between the application and internet layers, with examples of protocols.

```mermaid

    tp --> a2
    net --> a3
    lnk --> a4

    a1 -->|"Sockets API"| a2

```

    end

        s3["Video Server\nPort 554"]:::srv
    end

    a1 --> M
    a2 --> M
    a3 --> M

---

## 4.3 TCP Three-Way Handshake — State Diagram

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant C as CLIENT
    participant S as SERVER

    Note over C,S: Initial State: CLOSED
    C->>S: SYN (SEQ=x)
    Note over S: State: LISTEN -> SYN_RCVD
    S->>C: SYN+ACK (SEQ=y, ACK=x+1)
    Note over C: State: SYN_SENT -> ESTABLISHED
    C->>S: ACK (SEQ=x+1, ACK=y+1)
    Note over S: State: SYN_RCVD -> ESTABLISHED
    Note over C,S: BOTH SIDES ESTABLISHED
```

---

## 4.4 TCP Four-Way Connection Termination

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant C as CLIENT
    participant S as SERVER

    Note over C,S: BOTH IN ESTABLISHED STATE
    C->>S: FIN (SEQ=u)
    Note over S: State: ESTABLISHED -> CLOSE_WAIT
    S->>C: ACK (ACK=u+1)
    Note over C: State: ESTABLISHED -> FIN_WAIT_1
    C->>S: FIN (SEQ=v)
    Note over S: State: CLOSE_WAIT -> LAST_ACK
    S->>C: ACK (ACK=v+1)
    Note over C: State: FIN_WAIT_1 -> TIME_WAIT
    Note over S: State: LAST_ACK -> CLOSED
    Note over C: After 2MSL timeout -> CLOSED
```

---

## 4.5 Functional Architecture of Transport Layer Services

```mermaid

        P2P["Process-to-Process Addressing\nvia Port Numbers"]:::core
        MUX["Multiplexing"]:::core

        CM["Connection Management\n3-Way / 4-Way Handshakes"]:::core
        FC["Flow Control\nSliding Window"]:::core
        CC["Congestion Control\ncwnd, AIMD, Slow Start"]:::core
        EC["Error Control\nChecksums, ACKs, Retransmissions"]:::core
    end

    UP["Application Layer\nSockets, HTTP, FTP, DNS, SMTP"]:::up
    DOWN["Network Layer\nIP Best-Effort Delivery"]:::down

    UP -->|"API: socket / connect / send / recv"| TL
    TL -->|"Segments / Datagrams"| DOWN

    classDef core fill:#fefae0,stroke:#dda15e,color:#000,stroke-width:2px

```
