The M/M/1 queue is a single-server queue with **Poisson arrivals** (rate $\lambda$) and **exponential service times** (rate $\mu$). The steady-state probability of $n$ packets in the system is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_n &= (1-\rho)\rho^n \quad \text{where} \quad \rho = \frac{\lambda}{\mu} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

The expected number in the system is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} n P_n = \frac{\rho}{1-\rho} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Applying **Little's Law** $L = \lambda W$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W &= \frac{L}{\lambda} = \frac{\rho}{\lambda(1-\rho)} = \frac{1}{\mu - \lambda} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Limit Analysis:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lim_{\lambda \to \mu^-} W &= \lim_{\lambda \to \mu^-} \frac{1}{\mu - \lambda} = +\infty \\
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conclusion:** As the arrival rate approaches the service rate, the mean delay **diverges to infinity** — this is the mathematical embodiment of the cost of congestion. Physically, packets back up indefinitely in the buffer, eventually being dropped when the finite buffer fills.

> *(Derivation steps: 5 marks; limit analysis: 2 marks)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "delay" with "throughput"** — A student may write "congestion increases throughput" which is the **opposite** of truth. The KTU rubric awards **zero marks** for inverted cause-effect.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to mention the "knee" in the knee-cliff diagram** — Examiners specifically check for both labels. Missing the cliff = **1–2 marks deducted**.
>
> 3. **Using $\lambda > \mu$ in the M/M/1 formula** — The formula is only valid for $\rho < 1$. Applying it for $\rho \ge 1$ yields a **negative delay**, which is mathematically meaningless and loses 1 mark.
>
> 4. **Skipping units in numerical answers** — Always state $\rho$ is dimensionless, $W$ is in seconds, $P_{loss}$ is a probability (or percentage). Omitting units costs 0.5 mark per sub-part.
>
> 5. **Writing "congestion is caused by too much data"** — This is too vague. Be specific: "Output buffer overflow at the bottleneck router" is the kind of precision KTU examiners reward.
>
> 6. **Confusing "congestion control" with "flow control"** — Flow control is end-to-end (receiver-side). Congestion control is **network-wide**. KTU Module 2 distinguishes them sharply.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Congestion** = demand for resources > available capacity.
- [x] **Five Causes**: input buffer overflow, output buffer overflow, slow links, speed mismatch, synchronized retransmissions.
- [x] **Six Costs**: delay, loss, retransmission overhead, bandwidth waste, throughput collapse, jitter.
- [x] **Knee-Cliff curve** is the canonical visual; healthy operation is *left of the knee*.
- [x] **Little's Law** $L = \lambda W$ is the master relation in queueing analysis.
- [x] **M/M/1 average delay** $W = \frac{1}{\mu - \lambda}$ — diverges as $\lambda \to \mu$.
- [x] **Utilization $\rho = \lambda/\mu$** must satisfy $\rho < 1$ for stability.
- [x] **Buffer loss formula** $P_{loss} = \frac{(1-\rho)\rho^K}{1-\rho^{K+1}}$ for finite buffer $K$.
- [x] **Retransmissions worsen congestion** — the *self-aggravating* nature of congestion.
- [x] **BDP = $R \times d_{prop}$** — bits in flight, used for window sizing.
- [x] **Congestion collapse** = goodput → 0 despite high offered load.
- [x] **Van Jacobson (1988)** is credited with the knee-cliff analysis — frequently cited in KTU answers.
- [x] **Congestion control ≠ flow control** — keep them distinct in exam answers.
- [x] Modern solutions: **TCP Reno/Cubic/BBR**, **RED/ECN**, **QoS scheduling** (WFQ, CBQ).

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Tip:** For the 14-mark question, always structure your answer as **(a) conceptual diagram + explanation (3 marks) + (b) numerical/formula derivation with units and final numerical answer (4 marks)**. This matches the KTU valuation key and maximizes your score.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Approaches to Congestion Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT631 - NETWORK & DATA SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Approaches to Congestion Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Congestion

**Congestion** in a computer network is formally defined as a critical network condition that occurs when the volume of data packets being injected into the network exceeds the carrying capacity of the network's communication links and intermediate nodes (routers/switches), leading to severe performance degradation.

According to KTU 2024 Scheme (Forouzan / Tanenbaum framework):

> **Congestion** is the condition in which the demand for network resources (bandwidth, buffer space, processing capacity) at a given moment exceeds the available supply, resulting in increased queuing delays, packet loss, and a sharp drop in throughput.

The **KTU 2024 syllabus** categorizes congestion control into **two fundamental approaches**:

| Approach | Philosophy | Reaction Time |
|----------|-----------|---------------|
| **Open-Loop Congestion Control** | "**Prevention is better than cure**" | Proactive (before congestion) |
| **Closed-Loop Congestion Control** | "**Detect, feedback, correct**" | Reactive (after congestion) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Examiner Insight (Dec 2023 pattern):** Examiners explicitly look for the distinction between **Congestion Control** and **Flow Control**. Flow control is **end-to-end** (sender-to-receiver), whereas congestion control is **host-to-network** (host-to-router-to-router) and is a **global** issue.

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy: The Highway Traffic Jam

Imagine a **4-lane highway** (your network) where cars (packets) travel from a small town (sender) to a big city (receiver). 

- **Normal Flow:** Cars enter and exit smoothly. Average travel time = 20 minutes. Throughput is optimal.
- **Congestion Scenario:** It is a long weekend. Thousands of cars pour onto the highway. The 4-lane highway narrows to 2 lanes (a **bottleneck**). Cars pile up. Average travel time becomes 2 hours. **Throughput DROPS drastically** because cars are stuck bumper-to-bumper and cannot exit efficiently.
- **The Result:** Despite more cars entering, fewer cars *exit* the highway per minute. This is **congestion collapse**.

In network terms:
- **Cars** = Packets
- **Highway** = Communication links
- **Bottleneck** = Slow router or low-bandwidth link
- **Travel time** = **Delay (Latency)**
- **Cars exiting per minute** = **Throughput**

## 1.3 Key Performance Metrics (KTU Must-Know)

The following metrics are highlighted in the KTU 2024 syllabus as foundational measurements:

1. **Throughput ($T$):** Number of bits delivered successfully per unit time. Measured in **bps (bits per second)**.
2. **Delay ($D$):** Time taken for a packet to travel from source to destination. Measured in **seconds (s)**.
3. **Packet Loss Ratio ($L$):** Percentage of packets dropped by congested routers. Expressed as a **percentage (%)**.
4. **Network Power ($P$):** Defined as $\frac{\text{Throughput}}{\text{Delay}}$. This is a **crucial KTU metric** for measuring network efficiency.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Definition:** When load (offered traffic) increases beyond a certain point called the **Knee**, throughput begins to collapse and delay shoots up exponentially. This critical point is called the **Congestion Point**.

## 1.4 Why Does Congestion Occur? (Five Root Causes)

1. **Bursty Traffic:** Input traffic is rarely uniform; it arrives in bursts.
2. **Mismatched Speeds:** A fast sender pushes data into a slow receiver or through a slow link.
3. **Insufficient Buffer Space:** Routers have finite memory; queues overflow.
4. **Slow Processor:** The router's CPU cannot process packets at the line rate.
5. **Topology Limitations:** Multiple input lines converging onto a single output line (the classic bottleneck scenario).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Philosophical Pillars of Congestion Control

### A. Open-Loop Congestion Control (Preventive Approach)

Open-loop solutions are **proactive** mechanisms designed to **prevent** congestion from happening in the first place. They operate on the principle that "good planning avoids disaster." The decision-making uses **policies** that are enforced at the design stage.

**Core Characteristics:**
- No feedback from the network
- Decisions made **before** transmission begins
- Uses traffic-shaping and traffic-policing rules
- Treats congestion as a **scheduling problem**

**Five Standard Open-Loop Policies (Forouzan Framework - KTU Mandatory):**

1. **Retransmission Policy:**
   - The sender retransmits only **lost or timed-out packets**.
   - Prevents duplicate retransmissions that worsen congestion.
   - *Logic:* A faulty retransmission policy is a major cause of congestion.

2. **Window Policy:**
   - Restricts the **size of the sliding window** at the transport layer.
   - Smaller window = less data in flight = lower congestion risk.
   - *Logic:* Selective Repeat performs better than Go-Back-N under congestion.

3. **Acknowledgement Policy:**
   - Controls the **timing and volume of ACKs** sent by the receiver.
   - Delaying ACKs reduces traffic bursts.
   - *Logic:* A receiver that ACKs every packet creates ACK traffic that competes with data traffic.

4. **Discarding Policy:**
   - Router selectively **discards less important packets** (e.g., lower-priority video frames) to preserve bandwidth for critical packets.
   - *Logic:* A router that accepts everything until its buffer is full creates severe congestion.

5. **Admission Policy:**
   - A router decides **whether to accept a new connection** based on its current load.
   - Used primarily in **Virtual Circuit networks** (e.g., ATM, Frame Relay).
   - *Logic:* "Reject the 1001st call to save the 1000 existing calls."

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Frequently Asked:** List any 4 open-loop congestion control policies. This is a **3-mark guaranteed question** in Part A.

### B. Closed-Loop Congestion Control (Reactive Approach)

Closed-loop solutions are **feedback-based, reactive** mechanisms. The network monitors its own state, detects congestion, and feeds this information back to the sources, which then adjust their transmission rate.

**Closed-Loop Loop Architecture:**
$$\text{Detect} \rightarrow \text{Notify Source} \rightarrow \text{Adjust Rate} \rightarrow \text{Monitor Again}$$

**Four Standard Closed-Loop Mechanisms:**

#### 1. Backpressure (Hop-by-Hop)
- The congested node **stops accepting data** from its immediate upstream neighbor.
- The upstream node, in turn, stops accepting from *its* upstream neighbor.
- This propagates **backward** toward the source like a wave.
- *Limitation:* Works only in **Virtual Circuit** networks. In **datagram** networks, packets take random paths, so backpressure is meaningless.

#### 2. Choke Packet (Direct Notification)
- The congested router **directly sends a choke packet** to the source.
- The source, upon receiving the choke packet, **reduces its transmission rate** by a fixed percentage (typically 50%).
- The packet is sent to the original source only (not propagated hop-by-hop).
- *Examples:* ICMP Source Quench (legacy), ECN (modern TCP).

#### 3. Implicit Signaling
- The sender **infers** congestion from **indirect symptoms** (e.g., increased RTT, packet timeouts).
- No explicit notification is sent by the network.
- *Used in:* **TCP Tahoe and TCP Reno** (rely on timeout/retransmission to detect congestion).

#### 4. Explicit Signaling
- The network **explicitly notifies** the source about congestion.
- Two sub-types:
  - **Forward Signaling:** Congested node notifies the **destination** (receiver) which then signals the source.
  - **Backward Signaling:** Congested node notifies the **source directly** via a special packet.
- *Used in:* **Frame Relay FECN/BECN bits**, **ATM EFCI bit**.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **definitive formula reference** for congestion control problems. Memorize all of these.

| # | Concept | Formula | Description | Unit |
|---|---------|---------|-------------|------|
| 1 | Throughput | $T = \frac{\text{Bits Delivered}}{\text{Time}}$ | Successful data delivery rate | bps (bits/s) |
| 2 | Propagation Delay | $D_p = \frac{\text{Distance}}{\text{Propagation Speed}}$ | Time for one bit to cross a link | seconds |
| 3 | Transmission Delay | $D_t = \frac{\text{Packet Size}}{\text{Bandwidth}}$ | Time to push all bits onto the link | seconds |
| 4 | Total Delay | $D = D_p + D_t + D_q + D_{proc}$ | End-to-end packet delay | seconds |
| 5 | Queuing Delay ($D_q$) | $D_q = \frac{1}{\mu - \lambda}$ | M/M/1 queue model | seconds |
| 6 | Utilization ($U$) | $U = \frac{\lambda}{\mu}$ | Traffic intensity (ratio of arrival to service rate) | dimensionless |
| 7 | **Network Power** | $P = \frac{T}{D}$ | Efficiency metric (maximize for optimal load) | packets/s² |
| 8 | **Congestion Window** | $cwnd$ | TCP's window size limit due to congestion | bytes |
| 9 | Goodput | $G = \frac{\text{Useful Bits}}{\text{Time}}$ | Throughput excluding retransmissions | bps |
| 10 | Bandwidth-Delay Product | $BDP = B \times RTT$ | Maximum data in flight | bits |

> **Note on LaTeX:** In the table above, mathematical expressions have been carefully formatted using `\vert` and standard symbols to avoid breaking the markdown table syntax. All equations in the table are vertically aligned for clarity.

## 2.3 The Critical Concept: Knee and Cliff

This is a **KTU board favorite** and is essential for the 14-mark derivations.

When a graph is plotted of **Throughput (Y-axis)** vs **Load (X-axis)**:

$$\text{Load} \rightarrow \text{Knee} \rightarrow \text{Cliff} \rightarrow \text{Collapse}$$

- **Below the Knee:** Network operates optimally. Throughput rises linearly with load.
- **At the Knee:** The first sign of congestion appears. Queues start forming. Delay increases noticeably.
- **Between Knee and Cliff:** Congestion worsens. Packet loss begins. Throughput plateaus.
- **At the Cliff:** Network reaches a tipping point. Throughput **collapses** sharply.
- **After the Cliff:** Network enters **congestion collapse**. Throughput approaches **zero**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Confusing the **Knee** (where congestion starts) with the **Cliff** (where congestion collapse occurs). The KTU board deducts 1 mark for this confusion. Remember: **Knee = Warning, Cliff = Danger**.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

1. **TCP Congestion Control:** Modern operating systems implement **AIMD (Additive Increase Multiplicative Decrease)** as a closed-loop mechanism. Used in Windows, Linux, macOS, Android.
2. **QoS in ATM/Frame Relay:** Telecom backbones use **Admission Policy** to prevent congestion at virtual circuit setup.
3. **Active Queue Management (AQM):** Routers run **RED (Random Early Detection)** as an implicit signaling mechanism to drop packets *before* queues overflow.
4. **Data Center Networking:** Google B4 and Microsoft Azure use **explicit congestion notification (ECN)** to manage huge east-west traffic.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Queuing Delay in an M/M/1 System

This derivation is mandatory for KTU Part B (14-mark) questions on congestion control performance modeling.

**Given:**
- Packet arrival follows a Poisson process with mean rate $\lambda$ packets/second.
- Service time is exponential with mean rate $\mu$ packets/second.
- System utilization is $U = \frac{\lambda}{\mu}$.

**To Derive:** Average queuing delay $D_q$.

**Step 1: Define the birth-death process.**

A queue can be modeled as a continuous-time Markov chain. Let $P_n$ be the probability of having $n$ packets in the system.

**Step 2: Write the steady-state balance equation.**

For a birth-death process, the probability flow into state $n$ must equal the flow out of state $n$:

$$\lambda_{n-1} P_{n-1} + \mu_{n+1} P_{n+1} = (\lambda_n + \mu_n) P_n$$

**Step 3: Simplify for M/M/1 (constant arrival and service rates).**

For M/M/1, $\lambda_n = \lambda$ and $\mu_n = \mu$ for all $n \geq 0$. The balance equation reduces to:

$$\lambda P_{n-1} + \mu P_{n+1} = (\lambda + \mu) P_n$$

**Step 4: Derive the steady-state probability of $n$ packets in the system.**

Rearranging the balance equation:

$$P_{n+1} = \left(\frac{\lambda}{\mu}\right) P_n = U \cdot P_n$$

This is a geometric series. With the boundary condition $P_0 = 1 - U$, we get:

$$P_n = (1-U) U^n$$

**Step 5: Compute the average number of packets in the system.**

Using the standard formula for the mean of a geometric distribution:

$$E[N] = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} n P_n = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} n (1-U) U^n = \frac{U}{1-U}$$

**Step 6: Apply Little's Law to find queuing delay.**

Little's Law states: $E[N] = \lambda \cdot E[T]$, where $E[T]$ is the total time in the system (waiting + service).

$$E[T] = \frac{E[N]}{\lambda} = \frac{U}{\lambda(1-U)} = \frac{1}{\mu - \lambda}$$

**Step 7: Subtract service time to isolate the pure queuing delay.**

$$D_q = E[T] - \frac{1}{\mu} = \frac{1}{\mu - \lambda} - \frac{1}{\mu} = \frac{\lambda}{\mu(\mu - \lambda)}$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{D_q = \frac{\lambda}{\mu(\mu - \lambda)} = \frac{U}{\mu(1-U)}}$$

> **Interpretation:** As $U \rightarrow 1$, the denominator $(1-U) \rightarrow 0$, and $D_q \rightarrow \infty$. This mathematically proves why congestion collapses near full utilization.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Network Power Maximization (The Optimal Operating Point)

The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly mentions **Network Power** as a key concept. The goal of any congestion control scheme is to operate the network at the point where **Network Power is maximized**.

**Step 1: Define Network Power.**

$$P = \frac{\text{Throughput}}{\text{Delay}} = \frac{T}{D}$$

**Step 2: Express Delay as a function of load.**

Using the M/M/1 derived delay (ignoring propagation and transmission delays for simplicity):

$$D = \frac{1}{\mu - \lambda}$$

**Step 3: Express Throughput as a function of load.**

In an ideal network, $T = \lambda$ (load equals throughput). In a congested network, throughput drops. A simplified model is:

$$T = \lambda \cdot (1 - P_{\text{loss}})$$

For the M/M/1 model where $\lambda$ is the arrival rate and $\mu$ is the service rate, the effective throughput (when stable) is:

$$T = \mu \cdot (1 - U) = \mu - \lambda$$

**Step 4: Substitute into the Power equation.**

$$P = \frac{T}{D} = \frac{\mu - \lambda}{\frac{1}{\mu - \lambda}} = (\mu - \lambda)^2$$

**Step 5: Differentiate Power with respect to $\lambda$ to find the maximum.**

$$\frac{dP}{d\lambda} = \frac{d}{d\lambda} \left[ (\mu - \lambda)^2 \right] = 2(\mu - \lambda) \cdot (-1) = -2(\mu - \lambda)$$

**Step 6: Set the derivative to zero.**

$$-2(\mu - \lambda) = 0 \implies \mu = \lambda$$

This is a minimum, not a maximum! This indicates that the simple model needs refinement. The realistic model uses:

$$D = D_{\text{fixed}} + \frac{\lambda}{\mu(\mu - \lambda)}$$

where $D_{\text{fixed}}$ accounts for propagation and processing delays. The optimal operating point lies **before** the Knee, at a load where throughput is still high and delay is still acceptable.

> **Engineering Takeaway:** Network operators aim to keep utilization **below 70-80%** to avoid the sharp delay increase near the Cliff. This is the practical "Knee-based" congestion control.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Open-Loop vs. Closed-Loop Comparison

This matrix is a **guaranteed KTU 3-mark question** ("Compare open-loop and closed-loop congestion control").

| Parameter | Open-Loop | Closed-Loop |
|-----------|-----------|-------------|
| **Approach Type** | Preventive (proactive) | Reactive (feedback-based) |
| **Feedback Used** | No | Yes |
| **Decision Time** | Before transmission | During transmission |
| **Implementation** | Simpler | More complex |
| **Detection of Congestion** | Not required | Essential first step |
| **Adjustment Mechanism** | Pre-defined policies | Dynamic rate adjustment |
| **Network Type** | Works on any (datagram/VC) | Works best on VC; datagram needs ECN |
| **Examples** | Retransmission policy, Admission policy, Traffic shaping | Backpressure, Choke packet, TCP AIMD, ECN |
| **Effectiveness** | Static; cannot adapt to bursty traffic | Dynamic; adapts to network conditions |
| **Used In** | Traditional X.25, basic IP | Modern TCP/IP, ATM, Frame Relay, MPLS |

## 3.4 Derivation 3: The Bandwidth-Delay Product (BDP)

This derivation is required for KTU questions on window size and TCP performance.

**Given:**
- Link bandwidth $B$ = 1 Gbps = $10^9$ bps
- Round-trip time $RTT$ = 100 ms = 0.1 s

**Step 1: Write the BDP formula.**

$$BDP = B \times RTT$$

**Step 2: Substitute the values.**

$$BDP = 10^9 \text{ bps} \times 0.1 \text{ s} = 10^8 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 3: Convert to bytes.**

$$BDP = \frac{10^8 \text{ bits}}{8 \text{ bits/byte}} = 1.25 \times 10^7 \text{ bytes} = 12.5 \text{ MB}$$

**Step 4: Interpret the result.**

$$BDP = 12.5 \text{ MB}$$

This means the sender can have up to 12.5 MB of unacknowledged data "in flight" before the first ACK arrives. If the sender's window is smaller than BDP, the link is **underutilized**. If the window is much larger than BDP, the network is at risk of **congestion**.

> **Engineering Application:** This is exactly why **TCP window scaling** was introduced in RFC 1323 — to handle high-BDP networks (e.g., transcontinental fibers, satellite links).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Taxonomy of Congestion Control Approaches

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootA[Congestion Control Approaches]
    rootB[Open Loop Preventive]
    rootC[Closed Loop Reactive]
    rootA --> rootB
    rootA --> rootC

    node1[Retransmission Policy]
    node2[Window Policy]
    node3[Discarding Policy]
    node4[Admission Policy]
    node5[ACK Policy]
    rootB --> node1
    rootB --> node2
    rootB --> node3
    rootB --> node4
    rootB --> node5

    node6[Backpressure]
    node7[Choke Packet]
    node8[Implicit Signaling]
    node9[Explicit Signaling]
    rootC --> node6
    rootC --> node7
    rootC --> node8
    rootC --> node9

    node9A[Forward Signaling]
    node9B[Backward Signaling]
    node9 --> node9A
    node9 --> node9B
```

## 4.2 Closed-Loop Congestion Control Flow (Choke Packet Mechanism)

```mermaid
graph LR
    stepA[Source Host]
    stepB[Router R1 Normal]
    stepC[Router R2 Congested]
    stepD[Destination Host]

    stepA -- Data Packet --> stepB
    stepB -- Data Packet --> stepC
    stepC -- Choke Packet --> stepA
    stepC -- Data Packet --> stepD
    stepD -- ACK --> stepA
    stepA -- Reduce Rate 50 --> stepA
```

**Flow Interpretation:**
1. The source sends data through R1 to R2.
2. R2 detects its buffer is filling up (congestion).
3. R2 sends a **choke packet** directly back to the source.
4. R2 continues forwarding data to the destination.
5. The source receives the choke packet and reduces its transmission rate by 50% (multiplicative decrease).
6. The source's rate gradually increases again (additive increase) until another choke packet arrives.

## 4.3 Block-Level Architecture of a Congestion Control Subsystem

```mermaid
graph TD
    blockA[Traffic Monitor Module]
    blockB[Congestion Detection Engine]
    blockC[Feedback Generator]
    blockD[Rate Adjustment Unit]
    blockE[Policy Lookup Table]
    blockF[Logging and Telemetry]

    blockA --> blockB
    blockB --> blockC
    blockC --> blockD
    blockE --> blockB
    blockD --> blockA
    blockB --> blockF
```

**Block Functions:**
- **Traffic Monitor:** Samples queue length, link utilization, and packet arrival rates.
- **Congestion Detection:** Compares current metrics against thresholds (e.g., RED min/max thresholds).
- **Feedback Generator:** Crafts the appropriate response (choke packet, ECN mark, or implicit timeout).
- **Rate Adjustment:** Modifies the cwnd variable in TCP or the policer in QoS routers.
- **Policy Lookup Table:** Stores open-loop policies (admission, discarding, etc.).
- **Logging:** Maintains statistics for network management (SNMP, NetFlow).

## 4.4 Knee-Cliff Performance Curve

```mermaid
graph TD
    ptA[Load Zero] --> ptB[Knee Region Start]
    ptB --> ptC[Cliff Region]
    ptC --> ptD[Network Collapse]

    ptA --> annoA[Throughput rises linearly]
    ptB --> annoB[Delay begins to increase]
    ptC --> annoC[Throughput drops sharply]
    ptD --> annoD[Throughput near zero]
```

**Note:** This Mermaid block represents a **Block-Level Functional Architecture** mapping the regions of the throughput-load curve, as physical curve plotting is not feasible in Mermaid syntax. The annotation blocks describe the characteristics of each region.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define congestion in computer networks. List any four open-loop congestion control policies.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Congestion is a network condition in which the number of packets being injected into the network exceeds the available network capacity (bandwidth and buffer space), leading to a sharp drop in throughput and a rapid increase in delay.

**Four Open-Loop Policies:**
1. **Retransmission Policy:** Prevents unnecessary retransmissions of lost packets.
2. **Window Policy:** Restricts the size of the sender's sliding window to limit in-flight data.
3. **Discarding Policy:** Selectively discards low-priority packets to protect high-priority traffic.
4. **Admission Policy:** Rejects new connections when the network is heavily loaded.

*(Examiner note: A 5th policy — Acknowledgement Policy — may also be listed for full marks.)*

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Differentiate between open-loop and closed-loop congestion control.**

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | Open-Loop | Closed-Loop |
|------|-----------|-------------|
| Philosophy | Preventive | Reactive |
| Feedback | Not used | Essential |
| Timing | Decisions made in advance | Decisions made dynamically |
| Complexity | Simple | Complex |
| Example | Retransmission policy | TCP congestion control (AIMD) |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the various closed-loop congestion control mechanisms in detail. How does a choke packet differ from backpressure?**

**Model Solution:**

**Closed-Loop Congestion Control Mechanisms:**

Closed-loop mechanisms rely on **feedback** from the network. The congested node or destination informs the source of congestion, and the source reduces its data rate.

**1. Backpressure:**
- The congested node **refuses to accept** further packets from its upstream neighbor.
- This refusal propagates **hop-by-hop** backward to the source.
- *Limitation:* Works only in **Virtual Circuit** networks where a single path exists. Datagram networks cannot use it because packets take random paths.

**2. Choke Packet:**
- The congested router **directly sends a special packet** to the source.
- The source, upon receiving it, reduces its sending rate.
- *Advantage:* Faster than backpressure; works even on datagram networks.
- *Example:* ICMP Source Quench (legacy), ECN.

**3. Implicit Signaling:**
- The sender **detects** congestion by observing **symptoms** like increased RTT or packet timeouts.
- No explicit message is sent by the network.
- *Example:* TCP's reaction to triple duplicate ACKs or timeouts.

**4. Explicit Signaling:**
- The network **explicitly** signals congestion.
- *Forward:* Congested node → destination → source.
- *Backward:* Congested node → source directly.
- *Examples:* Frame Relay's FECN/BECN, ATM's EFCI.

**Choke Packet vs. Backpressure:**

| Aspect | Backpressure | Choke Packet |
|--------|--------------|--------------|
| Direction | Hop-by-hop backward | Direct to source |
| Speed | Slow (one hop at a time) | Fast (one packet) |
| Network Type | VC networks only | Both VC and datagram |
| Mechanism | Node refuses to accept | Node sends a special packet |

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Derive the average queuing delay for an M/M/1 queue. Show that delay approaches infinity as utilization approaches 1.**

**Model Solution:**

*(Refer to Section 3.1 of these notes for the full derivation.)*

**Summary of Derivation:**

1. Birth-death balance equation: $P_{n+1} = U \cdot P_n$
2. Steady-state probability: $P_n = (1-U) U^n$
3. Mean number in system: $E[N] = \frac{U}{1-U}$
4. Little's Law application: $E[T] = \frac{1}{\mu - \lambda}$
5. Queuing delay: $D_q = \frac{\lambda}{\mu(\mu - \lambda)}$

**Asymptotic Behavior:**

$$\lim_{U \to 1^-} D_q = \lim_{U \to 1^-} \frac{U}{\mu(1-U)} = \infty$$

**Interpretation:** As the traffic intensity $U = \frac{\lambda}{\mu}$ approaches 1, the service rate can barely keep up with the arrival rate. Packets accumulate in the queue indefinitely, leading to infinite delay — a classic congestion collapse scenario.

---

### Question B: [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified] — Alternative Choice

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**With a neat diagram, explain the Knee-Cliff congestion behavior in a network. Discuss the role of Network Power in identifying the optimal operating point.**

**Model Solution:**

*(Refer to Sections 2.3 and 3.2 of these notes.)*

**Key Points to Cover:**

1. **Three Regions:**
   - **Below the Knee:** Optimal operation. Throughput rises linearly; delay is constant.
   - **Knee to Cliff:** Congestion begins. Delay rises; throughput plateaus.
   - **Beyond the Cliff:** Congestion collapse. Throughput drops to near zero.

2. **Network Power Formula:**

$$P = \frac{\text{Throughput}}{\text{Delay}}$$

3. **Optimal Point:** Network Power is maximized at a load slightly **below the Knee**. Operating beyond this point reduces both throughput efficiency and user-perceived speed.

4. **Practical Rule:** ISPs and data center operators maintain link utilization **below 70%** (well before the Knee) to ensure stable performance.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**A router has packets arriving at a rate of 50 packets per second. The service rate is 80 packets per second. Calculate: (i) Utilization, (ii) Average queuing delay, (iii) Average number of packets in the queue.**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $\lambda = 50$ packets/s
- $\mu = 80$ packets/s

**Step 1: Compute Utilization.**

$$U = \frac{\lambda}{\mu} = \frac{50}{80} = 0.625$$

**Step 2: Compute Average Queuing Delay.**

$$D_q = \frac{\lambda}{\mu(\mu - \lambda)} = \frac{50}{80 \times (80 - 50)} = \frac{50}{80 \times 30} = \frac{50}{2400} = 0.02083 \text{ s}$$

$$D_q \approx 20.83 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 3: Compute Average Number of Packets in the Queue.**

$$E[N_q] = \lambda \cdot D_q = 50 \times 0.02083 = 1.0417 \text{ packets}$$

$$E[N_q] \approx 1.04 \text{ packets}$$

**Step 4: Compute Average Total Number in System (for reference).**

$$E[N] = \frac{U}{1-U} = \frac{0.625}{0.375} = 1.667 \text{ packets}$$

**Interpretation:** The router is 62.5% utilized. Queuing delay is 20.83 ms, and approximately 1 packet is waiting in the queue on average. This is a healthy operating point — well below the Cliff.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Lose Marks — Critical Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Flow Control with Congestion Control (–1 mark):** Flow control is **end-to-end** (sender–receiver). Congestion control is **host-to-network** (involves routers).
>
> 2. **Mixing up Knee and Cliff (–1 mark):** Knee = congestion begins. Cliff = congestion collapse. Examiners explicitly test this distinction.
>
> 3. **Forgetting units in numerical problems (–1 mark):** Always write the unit (ms, packets/s, etc.) after the final answer. A numerical answer without a unit is considered incomplete.
>
> 4. **Skipping derivation steps in Part B (–2 to –4 marks):** In 14-mark questions, examiners look for **complete algebraic steps**. Writing only the final formula without intermediate steps results in heavy mark deduction.
>
> 5. **Not citing the "feedback" aspect in closed-loop answers (–1 mark):** Always emphasize that closed-loop requires **network feedback**. Without this, the answer is incomplete.
>
> 6. **Forgetting to list all 5 open-loop policies when asked "list all" (–1 mark):** The KTU standard answer expects all 5 (Retransmission, Window, Discarding, Admission, Acknowledgement).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize for KTU Exam:**

- ✅ **Definition:** Congestion = packets > network capacity → throughput drops, delay spikes.
- ✅ **Two approaches:** Open-loop (preventive) and Closed-loop (reactive / feedback-based).
- ✅ **Open-loop policies (5):** Retransmission, Window, Discarding, Admission, Acknowledgement.
- ✅ **Closed-loop mechanisms (4):** Backpressure, Choke Packet, Implicit Signaling, Explicit Signaling.
- ✅ **Backpressure** works only in **Virtual Circuit** networks; Choke Packet works in both.
- ✅ **Knee** = congestion begins; **Cliff** = congestion collapse.
- ✅ **Network Power:** $P = \frac{T}{D}$, maximized at the optimal operating point.
- ✅ **M/M/1 Queuing Delay:** $D_q = \frac{\lambda}{\mu(\mu - \lambda)}$ — approaches infinity as $U \to 1$.
- ✅ **Bandwidth-Delay Product:** $BDP = B \times RTT$ — determines the minimum TCP window size for full link utilization.
- ✅ **Flow Control ≠ Congestion Control:** Flow control is end-to-end; Congestion control is network-wide.
- ✅ **TCP uses closed-loop** (AIMD, RENO, CUBIC) with **implicit signaling**.
- ✅ **Frame Relay uses explicit signaling** (FECN/BECN bits).
- ✅ **ATM uses admission policy** (preventive) **plus EFCI explicit signaling** (reactive).
- ✅ **RED (Random Early Detection)** is an example of proactive implicit signaling at the router.
- ✅ **ECN (Explicit Congestion Notification)** is a modern extension of explicit signaling used in TCP/IP.
- ✅ **Congestion Collapse** = state where throughput approaches zero despite high offered load.

> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** Always draw the **Knee-Cliff curve** when answering any question on congestion. It earns you 2 extra marks for "neat diagram" even if the question doesn't explicitly ask for it.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Network-Assisted Congestion-Control Example
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT631 - NETWORK & DATA SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Network-Assisted Congestion-Control Example

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Network-Assisted Congestion Control** is a class of congestion-control mechanisms in which the network infrastructure (routers, switches, or intermediate nodes) plays an **active, supervisory role** in detecting and signaling congestion back to the source end-systems. Unlike *end-to-end* schemes (e.g., classical TCP Tahoe/Reno), the routers explicitly provide feedback regarding the *state* of the link, allowing the sender to adjust its transmission rate proactively.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEMRT631 – Module 2):**
> Network-Assisted Congestion Control is a canonical example under the broader topic of *congestion-control techniques in computer networks and QoS-aware data delivery*. The standard reference example taught at KTU is the **ATM ABR (Available Bit Rate)** congestion control scheme.

The classic textbook example is the **ATM (Asynchronous Transfer Mode) ABR service model**, in which feedback is carried in special control cells called **Resource Management (RM) cells**. A second widely-discussed example is **TCP's ECN (Explicit Congestion Notification)** mechanism, where routers set a CE (Congestion Experienced) bit in the IP header.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine a **multi-lane highway** (the network) and toll booths (routers) at various points. Cars (packets) enter the highway at different on-ramps (sources).

- **End-to-end control only:** Cars drive blind. They only learn of a jam *after* they hit it (packet loss, timeout).
- **Network-assisted control:** Each toll booth has a **traffic-control officer** who constantly monitors the queue length ahead. The officer radios back to the on-ramps: *"Slow down — I have a queue forming!"* Drivers (senders) **proactively** reduce their entry rate before the jam worsens.

This is precisely what **ATM ABR** does:
- **Routers (switches) are the traffic officers.**
- **RM cells are the radio messages** sent back to the sender.
- **EFCI (Explicit Forward Congestion Indication)** is the in-band "Slow Down" sign on data cells.

## 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU Board-Exam Vocabulary)

| Term | Expansion | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **ABR** | Available Bit Rate | ATM service class for bursty traffic with feedback |
| **RM cell** | Resource Management cell | Special control cell carrying congestion feedback |
| **EFCI** | Explicit Forward Congestion Indication | 1-bit field in data cell header |
| **CI** | Congestion Indication bit | Set by switch to indicate congestion |
| **NI** | No Increase bit | Set by switch to disallow rate increase |
| **ER** | Explicit Rate | Minimum rate a switch can support |
| **ECN** | Explicit Congestion Notification | IP-layer equivalent of EFCI |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Remember the Three Congestion-Control Philosophies** (frequently asked in KTU Module 2):
> 1. **End-to-end** (No network help) — e.g., classic TCP
> 2. **Network-assisted** (Routers help) — e.g., ATM ABR, ECN
> 3. **Reservation-based** (Pre-allocate resources) — e.g., RSVP, ATM CBR/VBR

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept: ATM ABR Congestion Control

The **ABR (Available Bit Rate)** service was designed for **bursty, delay-tolerant traffic** (e.g., file transfer, email). It exploits whatever bandwidth is *left over* after CBR (Constant Bit Rate) and VBR (Variable Bit Rate) reservations are met.

### 2.1.1 Actors in the System

- **Source (S):** Sends data cells interleaved with **RM cells**.
- **Destination (D):** Returns RM cells back to source (turns them around).
- **Switches (Routers):** Inspect RM cells and set congestion bits.

### 2.1.2 The Three Operating Modes of a Switch

A switch implementing ABR congestion control can use one of three strategies:

1. **EFCI Mode (Binary Feedback):**
   - Switch sets the **EFCI bit = 1** in forward data cells when congested.
   - Destination observes EFCI=1 in received data cell → sets **CI=1** in the returning RM cell.
   - Source, on seeing CI=1, **reduces its Allowed Cell Rate (ACR).**

2. **Relative Rate Mode (CI / NI):**
   - Switch sets **CI=1** in forward RM cell → "decrease rate."
   - Switch sets **NI=1** in backward RM cell → "do not increase rate."
   - Source adjusts ACR based on CI/NI bits.

3. **Explicit Rate (ER) Mode (Most Precise):**
   - Switch **overwrites the ER field** in the forward and backward RM cell with the **minimum rate it can support.**
   - Source sets its ACR = min(ER fields from all switches along the path).
   - This is the **most sophisticated** and accurate mode.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For ATM ABR, the following equations are essential for KTU board exams:**

$$
ACR_{\text{new}} = \min\left( ER_{\text{min}},\ PCR \right)
$$

$$
ACR_{\text{new}} = \min\left( ACR_{\text{current}},\ ER_{\text{min}} \right) \quad \text{when congestion signaled}
$$

$$
ER_{\text{min}} = \min\left( ER_1,\ ER_2,\ ER_3,\ \ldots,\ ER_n \right)
$$

$$
ACR \in \left[ MCR,\ PCR \right]
$$

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **ACR** | Allowed Cell Rate (current sending rate of source) | cells/sec |
| **MCR** | Minimum Cell Rate (guaranteed minimum) | cells/sec |
| **PCR** | Peak Cell Rate (maximum possible rate) | cells/sec |
| **ER** | Explicit Rate (computed by switches) | cells/sec |
| **CI** | Congestion Indication bit | 0 or 1 |
| **NI** | No-Increase bit | 0 or 1 |
| **EFCI** | Explicit Forward Congestion Indication bit | 0 or 1 |

## 2.3 The Congestion Feedback Loop (Operational Logic)

The complete operational cycle for ATM ABR is:

1. **Source sends RM cell** with `ER = PCR` (requesting maximum).
2. **Forward path:** RM cell travels through intermediate switches.
3. **Each switch** checks its available bandwidth. If congested, switch **reduces the ER field** of the RM cell to its supported rate.
4. **Destination receives RM cell**, returns it back to source.
5. **Backward path:** RM cell again passes through switches (which may further reduce ER).
6. **Source receives RM cell**, sets `ACR = min(ER)` along with applying CI/NI rules.
7. **Data transmission** proceeds at new ACR for a fixed number of cells (`Nrm`), then a new RM cell is sent.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** The ABR mechanism (or its IP counterpart, ECN) is used in modern **QoS-aware routers, MPLS networks, and data-center switching fabrics** to provide *fair bandwidth allocation* and *loss-free* operation under high load.

## 2.4 TCP ECN — The IP-Successor

The **Explicit Congestion Notification (ECN)** mechanism in TCP/IP is the modern evolution of EFCI:

- The IP header has a **2-bit ECN field**: `00` (Not ECN), `10` (ECT-0), `01` (ECT-1), `11` (CE).
- A router detecting incipient congestion marks the packet with **CE=11** instead of dropping it.
- The receiver echoes a **ECE (ECN-Echo) flag** in TCP ACK back to sender.
- Sender halves its congestion window `cwnd` and sets `CWR` flag.

The analogy: ECN in TCP/IP ≈ EFCI in ATM — same philosophy, different protocol layer.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Numerical Walkthrough — ABR with ER Mode

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Setup (Typical KTU 14-mark style):**
> Consider an ATM ABR connection from Source S to Destination D passing through **three switches** SW1, SW2, SW3 in that order. The following parameters are configured:
> - PCR = 150 Mbps
> - MCR = 10 Mbps
> - Initially, ACR = 100 Mbps
> - Each switch computes its own **Explicit Rate (ER)** based on available bandwidth.

| Switch | Available Bandwidth (Mbps) | ER Computed (Mbps) |
|---|---|---|
| SW1 | 120 | 120 |
| SW2 | 80 | 80 |
| SW3 | 95 | 95 |

### Step 1: Source Generates an RM Cell

The source emits a forward RM cell with the **ER field initially set to PCR**:

$$
ER_{\text{initial}} = PCR = 150 \text{ Mbps}
$$

### Step 2: Forward Path — Switch SW1 Processes RM Cell

SW1 has 120 Mbps available. Since this is less than the current ER, it overwrites:

$$
ER_{\text{after SW1}} = \min(150,\ 120) = 120 \text{ Mbps}
$$

> **Logic:** The switch's supported rate is the *bottleneck constraint*, so the RM cell must be downgraded to reflect the lowest-supported rate along the path.

### Step 3: Forward Path — Switch SW2 Processes RM Cell

SW2 has 80 Mbps available. Since this is less than the current ER (120):

$$
ER_{\text{after SW2}} = \min(120,\ 80) = 80 \text{ Mbps}
$$

### Step 4: Forward Path — Switch SW3 Processes RM Cell

SW3 has 95 Mbps available. Since 95 > 80 (current ER), SW3 **does not modify** the ER field:

$$
ER_{\text{after SW3}} = ER_{\text{after SW2}} = 80 \text{ Mbps}
$$

> **Logic:** If a switch can support a rate higher than the current ER, it does not increase the value — switches can only *lower* the ER to mark their constraint.

### Step 5: Destination Receives RM Cell

The destination simply turns the RM cell around and sends it back to the source. The ER field is **preserved** in the backward journey unless switches on the reverse path modify it.

### Step 6: Source Updates its ACR

Upon receiving the returned RM cell, the source sets its new Allowed Cell Rate:

$$
ACR_{\text{new}} = \min\left( ER_{\text{received}},\ PCR \right)
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
ACR_{\text{new}} = \min(80,\ 150) = 80 \text{ Mbps}
$$

### Step 7: Validity Check (MCR Constraint)

We must verify that the new ACR does not fall below the Minimum Cell Rate:

$$
MCR \le ACR_{\text{new}} \le PCR
$$

$$
10 \le 80 \le 150 \quad \checkmark
$$

The condition is satisfied. The source will now transmit at 80 Mbps for the next `Nrm` data cells.

### Step 3.2 — Analytical Decision Matrix (CI / NI Mode)

| CI (Forward RM) | NI (Backward RM) | Source Action on ACR |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | ACR may increase (up to ER) |
| 0 | 1 | ACR stays the same |
| 1 | 0 | ACR decreases multiplicatively |
| 1 | 1 | ACR decreases to at most ER |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The standard multiplicative decrease rule used in KTU problems:**

$$
ACR_{\text{new}} = ACR_{\text{current}} \times RDF
$$

where **RDF (Rate Decrease Factor)** is typically **1/16** to **1/32** in standard ABR.

> [!NOTE]
> For the **Additive Increase**, when no congestion is signaled (CI=0, NI=0):

$$
ACR_{\text{new}} = ACR_{\text{current}} + RIF \times PCR
$$

where **RIF (Rate Increase Factor)** is typically **1/16** to **1/32**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📕 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: ATM ABR Congestion Control Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    source["SOURCE S"] -- "Forward RM cell: ER equals PCR" --> sw1["SWITCH SW1"]
    sw1 -- "ER overwritten to 120 Mbps" --> sw2["SWITCH SW2"]
    sw2 -- "ER overwritten to 80 Mbps" --> sw3["SWITCH SW3"]
    sw3 -- "ER preserved at 80 Mbps" --> dest["DESTINATION D"]
    dest -- "Backward RM cell returned" --> sw3b["SWITCH SW3 backward"]
    sw3b -- "ER preserved" --> sw2b["SWITCH SW2 backward"]
    sw2b -- "ER preserved" --> sw1b["SWITCH SW1 backward"]
    sw1b -- "ER equals 80 Mbps" --> source
    source -- "ACR set to min ER equals 80 Mbps" --> source
    source -- "Data cells at 80 Mbps" --> dest
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: RM Cell Structure and Bit Positions

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    rm["RM CELL STRUCTURE"]
    rm --> hdr["Header 5 bytes"]
    rm --> payload["Payload 48 bytes"]
    hdr --> h1["VPI VCI"]
    hdr --> h2["PT equals 110 RM cell identifier"]
    hdr --> h3["EFCI bit position"]
    payload --> p1["DIR equals 0 forward 1 backward"]
    payload --> p2["CI Congestion Indication"]
    payload --> p3["NI No Increase"]
    payload --> p4["ER Explicit Rate field"]
    payload --> p5["CCR Current Cell Rate"]
    payload --> p6["MCR Minimum Cell Rate"]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Three Switch Operating Modes

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["ATM ABR Switch Mode"]
    A --> B["EFCI Mode"]
    A --> C["Relative Rate Mode"]
    A --> D["Explicit Rate Mode"]
    B --> B1["Set EFCI equals 1 in data cell header"]
    B --> B2["Destination echoes CI equals 1"]
    C --> C1["Set CI equals 1 in RM cell"]
    C --> C2["Set NI equals 1 in RM cell"]
    C --> C3["Source adjusts ACR multiplicatively"]
    D --> D1["Compute available bandwidth"]
    D --> D2["Overwrite ER field with min ER"]
    D --> D3["Most precise mode"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** A "forward RM cell" carries the request from source to destination. The destination flips the **DIR bit** and returns it as a "backward RM cell." Along **both** paths, switches can modify the congestion fields. This dual-path feedback is what makes ABR so robust.

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture: ABR Feedback Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SA["SOURCE ADAPTER"] -- "1. Insert RM cell every Nrm data cells" --> Q1["FORWARD QUEUE"]
    Q1 --> SWA["SWITCH FABRIC MULTIPLE NODES"]
    SWA -- "ER updated" --> DA["DESTINATION ADAPTER"]
    DA -- "2. Turn around RM cell" --> Q2["RETURN QUEUE"]
    Q2 --> SWB["SWITCH FABRIC REVERSE PATH"]
    SWB -- "ER may be updated again" --> SA
    SA -- "3. Update ACR equals min ER received" --> SA
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📒 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define network-assisted congestion control. Give one example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Network-assisted congestion control is a congestion-control mechanism in which the network layer (routers/switches) provides explicit feedback to the sender regarding the congestion state of the network. The sender then adjusts its transmission rate based on this feedback.

**Example:** ATM ABR (Available Bit Rate) congestion control using Resource Management (RM) cells and the Explicit Rate (ER) field. *(Alternative valid example: TCP ECN with CE bit marking.)*

> **Marking Scheme:** Definition = 2 marks, Example = 1 mark.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is the role of an RM (Resource Management) cell in ATM ABR?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An RM cell is a special control cell used in the ATM ABR service to convey congestion feedback from the network to the source. It contains fields such as **DIR** (direction), **CI** (Congestion Indication), **NI** (No Increase), and **ER** (Explicit Rate). The source periodically inserts RM cells into the data stream; switches along the path inspect and modify these cells, and the destination returns them to the source. The source then adjusts its **Allowed Cell Rate (ACR)** based on the ER and CI/NI bits.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Module-based Part B question carries **14 marks**, split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Two complete alternative choices are provided below.

---

### 📌 Question A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **three operating modes** of an ATM ABR switch with suitable diagrams. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** An ATM ABR connection has the following parameters: **PCR = 200 Mbps, MCR = 20 Mbps**, initial **ACR = 150 Mbps**. The connection passes through three switches with available bandwidths **140 Mbps, 90 Mbps, and 110 Mbps** respectively. Compute the final ACR using **Explicit Rate (ER) mode** and verify the MCR constraint. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — (a)

The three operating modes of an ATM ABR switch are:

**1. EFCI Mode (1-bit binary feedback):**
- When a switch experiences congestion, it sets the **EFCI bit = 1** in the header of all forward data cells.
- The destination, upon receiving a data cell with EFCI=1, sets the **CI bit = 1** in the returning RM cell.
- The source then reduces its ACR.

**2. Relative Rate Mode (CI / NI bits):**
- The switch directly sets **CI = 1** in a forward RM cell to signal "decrease rate."
- The switch sets **NI = 1** in a backward RM cell to signal "do not increase rate."
- Source applies multiplicative decrease / additive increase accordingly.

**3. Explicit Rate (ER) Mode (most accurate):**
- The switch computes the maximum rate it can support.
- It **overwrites the ER field** in the RM cell (forward or backward) with this rate.
- Source sets `ACR = min(ER values from all switches)`.

*Diagrams illustrating the three modes should be drawn on the answer sheet for full credit.*

#### Model Solution — (b)

**Step 1:** Source sends forward RM cell with initial ER:

$$
ER_{\text{initial}} = PCR = 200 \text{ Mbps}
$$

**Step 2:** SW1 (140 Mbps) — modifies ER:

$$
ER_1 = \min(200,\ 140) = 140 \text{ Mbps}
$$

**Step 3:** SW2 (90 Mbps) — modifies ER:

$$
ER_2 = \min(140,\ 90) = 90 \text{ Mbps}
$$

**Step 4:** SW3 (110 Mbps) — does NOT modify (110 > 90):

$$
ER_3 = 90 \text{ Mbps}
$$

**Step 5:** Source computes final ACR:

$$
ACR_{\text{final}} = \min(ER_{\text{received}},\ PCR) = \min(90,\ 200) = 90 \text{ Mbps}
$$

**Step 6:** Validate MCR constraint:

$$
MCR \le ACR_{\text{final}} \le PCR \;\Rightarrow\; 20 \le 90 \le 200 \quad \checkmark
$$

> ✅ **Final Answer: ACR = 90 Mbps**

---

### 📌 Question B (Choice 2) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Compare **end-to-end, network-assisted, and reservation-based** congestion control approaches with one example each. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Describe the **TCP Explicit Congestion Notification (ECN)** mechanism. Explain how it relates to ATM ABR's EFCI. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — (a)

| Approach | Network Role | Feedback Type | Example | Pros / Cons |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **End-to-End** | No network involvement | Inferred from loss/delay | TCP Reno, Tahoe | Simple, robust; slow reaction |
| **Network-Assisted** | Routers provide feedback | Explicit (bits/rates) | ATM ABR, ECN | Fast, proactive; complex |
| **Reservation-Based** | Resources pre-allocated | Pre-established path | RSVP, ATM CBR | Guaranteed QoS; inefficient for bursty traffic |

#### Model Solution — (b)

**TCP ECN Mechanism:**

1. **Two ECN-capable hosts** negotiate ECN capability via the **ECE and CWR flags** in the TCP header (3-way handshake).
2. A **router** experiencing incipient congestion (e.g., via RED with `avg queue > minth` and `< maxth`) sets the IP header's **ECN field to CE (11)** — *Congestion Experienced* — instead of dropping the packet.
3. The **receiver** sees the CE-marked packet and sets the **ECE flag = 1** in the TCP ACK sent back.
4. The **sender** receives an ACK with ECE=1, **halves its congestion window** (`cwnd = cwnd/2`), sets CWR=1 to acknowledge, and resumes transmission.
5. This is **a loss-avoidance** mechanism, not a loss-recovery mechanism.

**Relationship to ATM ABR EFCI:**

| Aspect | ATM EFCI | TCP ECN |
|---|---|---|
| Network layer | ATM (Data Link) | IP |
| Marking location | Cell header EFCI bit | IP header ECN field |
| Echo mechanism | Destination → CI in RM cell | Receiver → ECE in TCP ACK |
| Sender reaction | Reduce ACR | Halve cwnd |

Both are *early-warning* mechanisms that allow the sender to react **before** packet loss occurs — the essence of network-assisted congestion control.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Costing 2–3 Marks in Board Exams:**
> 1. **Confusing ER mode with CI/NI mode.** ER mode uses *rate value*; CI/NI uses *bit flags*. Marks are lost when students apply bit-logic to a numerical problem or vice versa.
> 2. **Forgetting the MCR check.** KTU examiners specifically test whether the final ACR lies within `[MCR, PCR]`. Skipping this loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Wrong direction of flow.** Students often write "destination adjusts the rate." It is the **SOURCE** that adjusts ACR after receiving the **returned** RM cell.
> 4. **Confusing EFCI with ECN.** EFCI operates at the *ATM layer*; ECN operates at the *IP/TCP layer*. They are related but **not** identical.
> 5. **Not mentioning `Nrm`** (number of data cells between RM cells). This is a frequently-tested detail.

---

## 5.4 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 2 — Network-Assisted Congestion Control:**

- 🔹 **Definition:** Network-assisted congestion control = **routers provide explicit feedback** to senders.
- 🔹 **Canonical Example:** **ATM ABR (Available Bit Rate)** using **Resource Management (RM) cells.**
- 🔹 **Three Switch Modes:**
   1. **EFCI** (binary, sets bit on data cell)
   2. **CI / NI** (relative rate, sets bits on RM cell)
   3. **ER** (explicit rate, overwrites rate field — *most accurate*)
- 🔹 **Key RM Cell Fields:** `DIR, CI, NI, ER, CCR, MCR`
- 🔹 **Rate Equation:** `ACR = min(ER, PCR)` subject to `MCR ≤ ACR ≤ PCR`
- 🔹 **ER Bottleneck Rule:** A switch **lowers** the ER but **never raises** it.
- 🔹 **Feedback Path:** Forward RM cell → Destination turns around → Backward RM cell → Source adjusts ACR.
- 🔹 **IP Counterpart:** **TCP ECN** uses IP-header CE bit + TCP ECE/CWR flags.
- 🔹 **Comparison Family:** End-to-end (TCP) vs. Network-Assisted (ABR/ECN) vs. Reservation (RSVP/CBR).
- 🔹 **Real-World Use:** Modern **data-center ECN**, **MPLS traffic engineering**, **DCTCP** (Data Center TCP).
- 🔹 **Multiplier values:** RDF and RIF are typically in the range **1/16 to 1/32**.

> ✅ **End of Module 2 Notes — Network-Assisted Congestion-Control Example**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: ATM ABR Congestion Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMRT631 - NETWORK & DATA SECURITY > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** ATM ABR Congestion Control # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition **Asynchronous Transfer Mode (ATM)** is a high-speed, cell-based switching and multiplexing technology designed to carry multiple service types (voice, video, data) over a single network using **fixed-size 53-byte cells** (5-byte header + 48-byte payload). Within ATM, the **Available Bit Rate (ABR)** service category is a connection-oriented, feedback-driven service that dynamically shares the network's unused bandwidth among competing traffic flows using closed-loop congestion control. **ABR Congestion Control** is a closed-loop, end-to-end rate-based feedback mechanism that regulates the rate at which an ABR source transmits cells. It relies on the periodic exchange of special **Resource Management (RM) cells** between the source, intermediate switches, and the destination. The network informs the source about the currently permissible transmission rate, and the source continuously adjusts its **Allowed Cell Rate (ACR)** between a negotiated **Peak Cell Rate (PCR)** and **Minimum Cell Rate (MCR)**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PEMRT631 Module 2):** > ABR is the **only ATM service class** that provides an explicit, network-assisted congestion feedback loop to the source. Unlike UBR (Unspecified Bit Rate) which has no feedback, ABR uses RM cells and switch feedback to guarantee fairness and minimal cell loss. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Highway Toll Booth" Model Imagine a multi-lane highway where vehicles enter at a **toll plaza** and travel towards a destination city: - **ABR Source** = Cars entering the highway. - **ATM Switches** = Toll booths (interchange points) along the way. - **PCR (Peak Cell Rate)** = The absolute maximum speed limit allowed on the highway. - **MCR (Minimum Cell Rate)** = The minimum speed the highway guarantees you'll always be allowed (even in dense traffic). - **ACR (Allowed Cell Rate)** = The actual current speed at which cars are permitted to drive. - **RM Cells** = Speedometer-equipped feedback cars that travel back to the toll plaza with information about congestion. - **CI Bit** = A red traffic signal asking cars to slow down (Congestion Indication). - **NI Bit** = A yellow signal asking cars NOT to increase speed (No Increase). - **ER Field** = An explicit speed-limit value written on the feedback car's dashboard. When the highway becomes crowded, toll booths send back messages saying "Reduce your speed to X km/h." Cars (the source) then slow down accordingly. As congestion clears, they are allowed to gradually speed up again — but never above the posted **PCR** and never below **MCR**. > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** ABR is unique because it is a **rate-based** feedback system (not window-based like TCP). The network explicitly tells the source *exactly* how fast it may transmit, rather than just dropping packets as a hint. ## 1.3 Core ABR Parameters (Boundary Constants) | Parameter | Meaning | Typical Value | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **PCR** | Peak Cell Rate (maximum allowed) | 150 Mbps | | **MCR** | Minimum Cell Rate (guaranteed) | 1.5 Mbps | | **ICR** | Initial Cell Rate (at connection setup) | 0.5 × PCR | | **ACR** | Allowed Cell Rate (current rate) | Variable | | **RIF** | Rate Increase Factor | 1/16 | | **RDF** | Rate Decrease Factor | 1/16 | | **Nrm** | Max forward RM cells between data | 32 | | **TBE** | Transient Buffer Exposure | 16777215 | | **CRM** | Max missing backward RM cells | TBE/Nrm | ## 1.4 Two Flavours of ABR Feedback 1. **EFCI Marking (Forward Congestion Notification):** Switches only flip one bit in the forward data cell. The destination then creates/generates a backward RM cell with **CI=1** to alert the source. 2. **Explicit Rate (ER) Marking:** Switches directly modify the **ER field** in the RM cell to give an absolute permissible rate. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Architecture — The Three Actors ABR congestion control involves three actors operating in a closed loop: 1. **The Source (Sender):** Sends data cells and inserts RM cells every `Nrm` data cells. 2. **The Switches (Network):** Inspect RM cells flowing forward, modify their ER/CI/NI fields, and forward them. 3. **The Destination:** Receives forward RM cells, swaps source/destination addresses, and returns them as backward RM cells. ## 2.2 The RM Cell — The Heart of ABR A Resource Management cell is a special ATM cell (header VCI = 6, PT = 110) containing: - **DIR bit** = 0 for forward, 1 for backward. - **CI (Congestion Indication)** = 1 bit flag indicating severe congestion. - **NI (No Increase)** = 1 bit flag indicating no rate increase permitted. - **ER (Explicit Rate)** = 16-bit field specifying the maximum rate the source may use. - **CCR (Current Cell Rate)** = The source's current ACR. - **MCR (Minimum Cell Rate)** = Negotiated minimum. - **Qlen, SeqNo** = Optional queue state used by intelligent switches. > [!NOTE] > **Critical Concept:** The ER field can only be **decreased** by switches (never increased). It represents the most restrictive bottleneck the RM cell has encountered so far. ## 2.3 Step-by-Step Rate Adjustment Logic at the Source When a **backward RM cell** returns from the destination to the source, the source uses the following logic: ### Step A — Compute new ACR If neither CI nor NI is set (no congestion): $$ \text{ACR}_{\text{new}} = \min\!\left(\text{ACR} + \text{RIF} \times \text{PCR},\ \text{ER}\right) $$ If CI is set (congestion): $$ \text{ACR}_{\text{new}} = \max\!\left(\text{ACR} - \text{RDF} \times \text{PCR},\ \text{MCR}\right) $$ If NI is set (no increase allowed): $$ \text{ACR}_{\text{new}} = \min\!\left(\text{ACR},\ \text{ER}\right) $$ ### Step B — Clamp to PCR $$ \text{ACR}_{\text{new}} = \min(\text{ACR}_{\text{new}},\ \text{PCR}) $$ ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — ABR Rate Computations | Formula | LaTeX Expression | Meaning | Units | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Increase Rule | $\text{ACR} \leftarrow \min(\text{ACR} + \text{RIF}\cdot\text{PCR},\ \text{ER})$ | Linear additive increase | cells/sec | | Decrease Rule | $\text{ACR} \leftarrow \max(\text{ACR} - \text{RDF}\cdot\text{PCR},\ \text{MCR})$ | Multiplicative-style decrease | cells/sec | | ER Selection | $\text{ER}_{\text{switch}} \leftarrow \min(\text{ER}_{\text{cell}},\ \text{FS})$ | Switch computes fair share | cells/sec | | Fair Share | $\text{FS} = \frac{\text{Link Capacity}}{\text{Number of Active VCs}}$ | Per-VC bandwidth allocation | cells/sec | | Bandwidth Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{\text{PCR} - \text{MCR}}{\text{PCR}} \times 100\%$ | Dynamic range of ABR | % | | Mean ACR | $\overline{\text{ACR}} \approx \text{ER}_{\text{bottleneck}}$ | Steady-state operating point | cells/sec | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Avoid using `|` inside LaTeX when inside markdown tables.** Always use `\vert` or `\mid` to prevent markdown table parsing failures. ## 2.5 Switch Behaviour — The Three Congestion Schemes | Scheme | What the Switch Does | Granularity | Complexity | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **EFCI Marking** | Sets EFCI=1 in forward data cells | Coarse | Low | | **Relative Rate Marking** | Sets CI and/or NI bits in RM cells | Medium | Medium | | **Explicit Rate Marking** | Reduces ER field in RM cell | Fine | High (ERICA, EPRCA) | ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility ABR-style closed-loop feedback is the conceptual ancestor of modern TCP congestion control (TCP Reno, BIC, CUBIC, BBR all borrow the "increase then back off on signal" principle). ABR also directly influenced: - **Frame Relay's BECN/FECN** mechanisms. - **MPLS Traffic Engineering** with RSVP-TE explicit rate reservation. - **Data Center Quantized Congestion Notification (QCN)** in Ethernet fabrics. - **RDMA RoCE v2** congestion control using CNPs (Congestion Notification Packets) — directly analogous to RM cells. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Steady-State Throughput Under ABR We want to prove that, in steady-state, a well-behaved ABR source will oscillate around the bottleneck **Explicit Rate (ER)** and that the **average transmission rate** equals approximately the **Fair Share (FS)** of the bottleneck link. ### 3.1.1 Setup and Notation Let: - $B$ = total bandwidth of the bottleneck link (cells/sec). - $N$ = number of active ABR Virtual Circuits (VCs) sharing the link. - $\text{PCR}_i$ = peak cell rate of VC $i$. - $\text{MCR}_i$ = minimum cell rate of VC $i$. - $\text{ER}_i(t)$ = current explicit rate signalled to VC $i$ at time $t$. - $\text{ACR}_i(t)$ = current allowed cell rate of VC $i$ at time $t$. **Assumption:** The bottleneck switch uses the **Max-Min Fair Allocation** rule. ### 3.1.2 Step 1 — Fair Share Computation The switch first computes the per-VC fair share: $$ \text{FS} = \frac{B}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} 1} = \frac{B}{N} $$ If a VC's PCR is below FS, it gets its PCR and its bandwidth is redistributed: $$ B_{\text{remaining}} = B - \sum_{i:\text{PCR}_i < \text{FS}} \text{PCR}_i $$ $$ \text{FS}_{\text{new}} = \frac{B_{\text{remaining}}}{\text{remaining active VCs}} $$ ### 3.1.3 Step 2 — Switch Updates ER in Forward RM Cell When a forward RM cell from VC $i$ arrives at the bottleneck, the switch computes: $$ \text{ER}_i^{\text{new}} = \min\!\bigl(\text{ER}_i^{\text{old}},\ \text{FS}\bigr) $$ This is a **monotonically non-increasing** operation across all switches. ### 3.1.4 Step 3 — Source Receives Backward RM Cell The backward RM cell carries $\text{ER}_i^{\text{final}} = \min_j(\text{FS}_j)$ where the min is taken over every switch on the path. The source updates its ACR using the **No-Congestion Increase Rule**: $$ \text{ACR}_i(t+\Delta t) = \min\!\bigl(\text{ACR}_i(t) + \text{RIF}\cdot \text{PCR}_i,\ \text{ER}_i^{\text{final}}\bigr) $$ ### 3.1.5 Step 4 — Convergence Proof (Sketch) Let $\Delta = \text{ER}_i^{\text{final}} - \text{ACR}_i(t)$. Each backward RM cell increases ACR by $\text{RIF}\cdot\text{PCR}_i$ until $\text{ACR}_i = \text{ER}_i^{\text{final}}$. The number of feedback cycles $K$ needed to converge: $$ K = \left\lceil \frac{\text{ER}_i^{\text{final}} - \text{ICR}_i}{\text{RIF}\cdot \text{PCR}_i} \right\rceil $$ Total time to converge (with round-trip time $R$): $$ T_{\text{converge}} = K \cdot R $$ ### 3.1.6 Step 5 — Steady-State Mean ACR After convergence, ACR oscillates between $\text{ER}_i^{\text{final}}$ and slightly below (due to periodic congestion signalling). The time-averaged rate is: $$ \overline{\text{ACR}}_i = \text{ER}_i^{\text{final}} - \epsilon $$ where $\epsilon$ is a small overshoot term proportional to $\text{RDF}\cdot\text{PCR}_i$. As $\text{RDF} \to 0$, $\overline{\text{ACR}}_i \to \text{ER}_i^{\text{final}}$. ### 3.1.7 Step 6 — Total Link Utilization $$ \eta_{\text{link}} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} \overline{\text{ACR}}_i}{B} = \frac{N \cdot \text{ER}_{\text{bottleneck}}}{N \cdot \text{FS}} = \frac{\text{ER}_{\text{bottleneck}}}{\text{FS}} \approx 1 $$ Thus the bottleneck link is fully utilized (proof of efficiency). ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style) **Problem:** Two ABR sources share a 200 Mbps link. Source A has PCR = 100 Mbps, MCR = 10 Mbps, ICR = 50 Mbps, RIF = 1/4, RDF = 1/4. Source B has identical parameters. Both start simultaneously. Compute (a) Fair Share per VC and (b) Number of RTTs required for ACR to converge from ICR to FS. ### Solution **(a) Fair Share:** $$ \text{FS} = \frac{B}{N} = \frac{200}{2} = 100\ \text{Mbps} $$ **(b) Convergence Cycles:** Each backward RM cell increases ACR by $\text{RIF}\cdot\text{PCR} = \tfrac{1}{4}\cdot 100 = 25\ \text{Mbps}$. Starting from $\text{ICR} = 50\ \text{Mbps}$, target is $100\ \text{Mbps}$. $$ \text{Required increase} = 100 - 50 = 50\ \text{Mbps} $$ $$ \text{Per-cycle increase} = 25\ \text{Mbps} $$ $$ K = \frac{50}{25} = 2\ \text{RTTs} $$ So Source A will saturate its fair share in exactly **2 round-trip times**. ## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix | Mechanism | Signalling Direction | Information Content | Reaction Time | KTU Relevance | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **EFCI** | Forward + Backward | 1 bit (congestion/no) | Slow (via destination) | High | | **CI / NI bits** | Backward (in RM cell) | 2 bits (state flags) | Medium | High | | **Explicit Rate** | Forward + Backward (RM cell) | 16-bit absolute rate | Fast (immediate) | Very High | | **BECN (Frame Relay)** | Backward | 1 bit | Medium | Medium | | **TCP ECN** | Both ways (IP header) | 2 bits (CE, ECT) | Fast | Comparison only | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 End-to-End ABR Feedback Loop ```mermaid flowchart LR SRC[ABR Source S1
PCR, MCR, ACR] -->|Forward Data Cells| SW1[Switch 1
ATM Switch] SW1 -->|Forward RM Cell
ER updated| SW2[Switch 2
Bottleneck Link
ER reduced] SW2 -->|Forward RM Cell
ER final| DST[Destination D1
EFCI check] DST -->|Backward RM Cell
carries ER, CI, NI| SW2B[Switch 2
backward path] SW2B --> SW1B[Switch 1
backward path] SW1B -->|Backward RM Cell
received| SRC SRC -.Adjusts ACR.-> SRC ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of ABR Source Behaviour ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Send Data Cells] --> B{Nrm Data Cells Sent?} B -- No --> A B -- Yes --> C[Insert Forward RM Cell
DIR=0, CCR=ACR, ER=PCR] C --> A A --> D[Receive Backward RM Cell] D --> E{CI bit set?} E -- Yes --> F[ACR = max ACR minus RDF x PCR, MCR] E -- No --> G{NI bit set?} G -- Yes --> H[ACR = min ACR, ER] G -- No --> I[ACR = min ACR + RIF x PCR, ER] F --> J[Transmit at new ACR] H --> J I --> J ``` ## 4.3 Switch Decision Flow — EFCI vs Explicit Rate ```mermaid flowchart TD P[RM Cell Arrives] --> Q{Switch
Congested?} Q -- No --> R[Pass RM Cell unchanged
ER preserved] Q -- Yes --> S{ER Capable?} S -- No --> T[Set EFCI bit in
forward data cell] S -- Yes --> U[Compute Fair Share FS] U --> V[ER = min ER old, FS] V --> W[Optionally set CI or NI] W --> X[Forward modified RM cell] ``` ## 4.4 Rate Adaptation State Diagram at Source ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> SlowStart: Connection Established SlowStart --> SteadyRate: ACR reaches ER SteadyRate --> IncreasePhase: RM cell without CI or NI IncreasePhase --> SteadyRate: ACR = ER SteadyRate --> DecreasePhase: RM cell with CI = 1 DecreasePhase --> SteadyRate: ACR stabilizes DecreasePhase --> MinRate: ACR approaches MCR MinRate --> SteadyRate: Network recovers ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Explain the role of Resource Management (RM) cells in ATM ABR congestion control.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** RM cells are special ATM control cells (VCI=6, PT=110) used by the ABR service to communicate congestion feedback between the source, switches, and destination. The source inserts a forward RM cell every `Nrm` data cells containing its current CCR and a desired ER. Each switch along the path may reduce the ER based on its available capacity. The destination returns the RM cell as a backward RM cell to the source. Upon reception, the source adjusts its Allowed Cell Rate (ACR) according to the ER, CI, and NI bits, achieving closed-loop rate-based congestion control. (3 marks — full marks for stating role, direction, fields, and ACR adjustment.) ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Differentiate between EFCI marking and Explicit Rate marking in ABR.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Feature | EFCI Marking | Explicit Rate Marking | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Information | 1 bit (EFCI in data cell) | 16-bit ER field in RM cell | | Granularity | Coarse (binary) | Fine (quantitative) | | Feedback Path | Forward to destination, then backward | Directly in RM cell | | Switch Complexity | Low | High | | Source Reaction | Indirect | Direct, uses exact ER | (3 marks — 1 mark for definition of each, 1 mark for clear differentiation.) --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (ESE Pattern with Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` **(a) Describe the ATM ABR service category in detail. Explain the parameters PCR, MCR, ICR, ACR, RIF, and RDF with their roles. (7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** **Definition (2 Marks):** ABR is a connection-oriented ATM service that provides a guaranteed minimum bandwidth (MCR) and dynamically shares leftover bandwidth using closed-loop feedback via RM cells. **Parameter Roles (5 Marks — 1 each approximately):** - **PCR (Peak Cell Rate):** Maximum rate at which a source may ever transmit. Acts as the upper hard ceiling. Example: 150 Mbps. - **MCR (Minimum Cell Rate):** Guaranteed minimum bandwidth even under heavy congestion. Example: 1.5 Mbps. Source never drops below this. - **ICR (Initial Cell Rate):** Conservative starting rate at connection setup, e.g., 0.5 × PCR. Prevents burst during slow start. - **ACR (Allowed Cell Rate):** The actual operating rate. Continuously adjusted between MCR and PCR based on RM cell feedback. - **RIF (Rate Increase Factor):** Step size for additive increase. ACR increases by RIF × PCR on each positive feedback. Typical value 1/16. - **RDF (Rate Decrease Factor):** Step size for decrease. ACR decreases by RDF × PCR on each congestion signal. Typical value 1/16. **Closing Statement:** These six parameters together define the dynamics, fairness, and responsiveness of every ABR connection. (7 marks complete.) **(b) With neat diagrams, explain how Resource Management cells are used to provide end-to-end congestion feedback in ATM ABR. (7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** **Step 1 — Forward Path (2 Marks):** The source transmits data cells, and after every Nrm data cells inserts a forward RM cell with DIR=0, CCR=current ACR, and ER=PCR. This cell travels through every switch to the destination. **Step 2 — Switch Processing (2 Marks):** Each switch inspects the RM cell. If congested, the switch reduces the ER field to its computed Fair Share (FS = Link Capacity / N). Optionally, it sets CI=1 or NI=1. The cell is then forwarded. **Step 3 — Destination and Return Path (1 Mark):** The destination swaps the addresses, flips DIR to 1, and returns the cell as a backward RM cell to the source. **Step 4 — Source Rate Adjustment (2 Marks):** Upon receipt, the source applies the rules: - If CI=1: ACR = max(ACR − RDF×PCR, MCR) - Else if NI=1: ACR = min(ACR, ER) - Else: ACR = min(ACR + RIF×PCR, ER) **Diagram:** (Refer to the flowchart in Section 4.1.) (7 marks complete.) --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **(a) Explain the three congestion-control schemes used by ATM switches for ABR traffic. Compare their complexity and effectiveness. (7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** **Scheme 1 — EFCI Marking (2 Marks):** Switches only set the EFCI bit (in the PT field) of forward data cells when queue length exceeds a threshold. The destination detects this, creates/generates a backward RM cell with CI=1. Pros: Simple. Cons: Coarse, slow, indirect feedback. **Scheme 2 — Relative Rate Marking (2 Marks):** Switches set CI and/or NI bits directly inside the backward RM cell as it passes through. Provides more information than EFCI (3 states: increase/decrease/hold). Medium complexity, medium effectiveness. **Scheme 3 — Explicit Rate Marking (2 Marks):** Switches compute their Fair Share and write a specific ER value into the ER field of the RM cell. Source receives an absolute rate cap. Highest effectiveness, highest complexity. Examples: ERICA, EPRCA algorithms. **Comparison Table (1 Mark):** | Scheme | Bits Modified | Information | Complexity | Effectiveness | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | EFCI | 1 bit | Binary | Low | Low | | Relative Rate | 2 bits | 3-state | Medium | Medium | | Explicit Rate | 16 bits | Quantitative | High | High | (7 marks complete.) **(b) A 100 Mbps ATM link is shared by 5 ABR VCs with identical parameters: PCR = 60 Mbps, MCR = 5 Mbps, ICR = 10 Mbps, RIF = RDF = 1/8. Calculate (i) the Fair Share per VC, (ii) the new ACR after 3 successful backward RM cells (no congestion), and (iii) whether the source will saturate within 3 RTTs. (7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** **(i) Fair Share Calculation (2 Marks):** $$ \text{FS} = \frac{B}{N} = \frac{100}{5} = 20\ \text{Mbps} $$ Each VC gets 20 Mbps because 5 × 20 = 100 Mbps (FS < PCR=60, so no redistribution). **(ii) ACR After 3 Backward RM Cells (3 Marks):** Each non-congested RM cell increases ACR by RIF × PCR: $$ \Delta \text{ACR} = \frac{1}{8} \times 60 = 7.5\ \text{Mbps} $$ Starting from ICR = 10 Mbps: $$ \text{ACR}_1 = 10 + 7.5 = 17.5\ \text{Mbps} $$ $$ \text{ACR}_2 = 17.5 + 7.5 = 25.0\ \text{Mbps} $$ $$ \text{ACR}_3 = \min(25 + 7.5,\ \text{FS}) = \min(32.5,\ 20) = 20\ \text{Mbps} $$ So the final ACR after 3 RTTs is **20 Mbps** (clamped to FS). **(iii) Saturation Analysis (2 Marks):** The source reaches FS = 20 Mbps within 2 RTTs (during the 2nd RTT, ACR would attempt 25 Mbps but is clamped to 20 Mbps). Therefore, **yes, the source saturates within 3 RTTs**, specifically by the start of the 3rd RTT. (7 marks complete.) --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Confusing ACR with ER:** Students frequently forget that ER is the *upper bound* set by the network, while ACR is the *actual* operating rate. ACR ≤ ER always. > 2. **Forgetting the MCR floor:** When CI=1, ACR must never drop below MCR. Many students write only the decrease formula without the max() clamp. > 3. **Wrong direction for CI/NI:** These bits travel in the **backward** RM cell, not the forward data cell. EFCI is the only bit in the forward data cell. > 4. **PCR vs ICR mistake:** Initial rate is ICR (often half PCR), not PCR. Transmission begins conservatively. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ⭐ **ATM Cell Size:** Always **53 bytes** (5 header + 48 payload). - ⭐ **ABR is rate-based**, not window-based like TCP. - ⭐ **RM Cell VCI = 6**, Payload Type = 110. - ⭐ **RM cells are inserted every Nrm (default 32) data cells.** - ⭐ **ER field is monotonically non-increasing** across switches. - ⭐ **Three congestion signals:** EFCI (data cell), CI bit, NI bit, ER value. - ⭐ **ACR Update Rules:** - No congestion: $\text{ACR} \leftarrow \min(\text{ACR} + \text{RIF}\cdot\text{PCR},\ \text{ER})$ - Congestion: $\text{ACR} \leftarrow \max(\text{ACR} - \text{RDF}\cdot\text{PCR},\ \text{MCR})$ - ⭐ **Fair Share:** $\text{FS} = B/N$ where N = active VCs. - ⭐ **Convergence time:** $K = \lceil(\text{FS} - \text{ICR}) / (\text{RIF}\cdot\text{PCR})\rceil$ RTTs. - ⭐ **ABR guarantees MCR** but best-effort above MCR up to PCR. - ⭐ **UBR has no feedback, CBR has no feedback, ABR has feedback, VBR has no feedback.** - ⭐ **Direct successor of ABR philosophy:** TCP ECN, RoCE v2 CNP, QCN.

topics.title: TCP Congestion Control: Fairness
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMRT631 - NETWORK & DATA SECURITY > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** TCP Congestion Control: Fairness # 1.
