> **Pitfall:** The `|` character is used in the table above for $\vert L \vert$ and $\mid$ — these are rendered as LaTeX delimiters and **do not break the table syntax**. Do not paste the `|` character raw inside a cell.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Corporate Site-to-Site VPN:** Two branch office routers (e.g., Cisco ASA, Juniper SRX) establish an IKEv2 IPSec Tunnel Mode VPN across the public Internet, providing a private WAN at a fraction of MPLS cost.
- **Remote-Access VPN:** A mobile employee connects from an untrusted Wi-Fi to the company gateway; the OS automatically enters a Tunnel Mode ESP session (e.g., L2TP/IPSec, IKEv2 in macOS, Windows, and Android).
- **Routing Protocol Authentication:** OSPF, BGP, and RIPng can use AH to authenticate neighbour adjacencies.
- **5G Core & Telecom Backhaul:** IPSec ESP is the de-facto security for N2, N4, and N3 reference points in 5G NSA/SA deployments (3GPP TS 33.210).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — ESP Cipher Block Alignment Padding

When ESP encrypts the payload, the cipher (e.g., AES in CBC mode) requires the plaintext to be a **multiple of the cipher's block size** $B_c$. We must compute the exact number of pad bytes $P_{length}$ to insert.

**Step 1 — Identify the required plaintext input to the cipher.**

In ESP, the encryption input is the concatenation:

$$E_{in} = \text{Payload} \;\Vert\; \text{Pad} \;\Vert\; \text{Pad-Length} \;\Vert\; \text{Next-Header}$$

The three trailing fields have a **fixed total length of 2 bytes** (1 byte pad-length + 1 byte next-header).

**Step 2 — Express the total length of $E_{in}$.**

Let $L_{payload}$ be the payload length in bytes. Then:

$$L_{E_{in}} = L_{payload} + P_{length} + 2$$

**Step 3 — Apply the block-alignment constraint.**

For a block cipher, the total length $L_{E_{in}}$ must be an exact multiple of $B_c$:

$$L_{E_{in}} \equiv 0 \pmod{B_c}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the smallest non-negative $P_{length}$.**

We require:

$$L_{payload} + P_{length} + 2 \equiv 0 \pmod{B_c}$$

Rearranging for $P_{length}$:

$$P_{length} \equiv -\left(L_{payload} + 2\right) \pmod{B_c}$$

**Step 5 — Choose the canonical representative in $[0, B_c - 1]$.**

$$P_{length} = \left( B_c - \left[ \left(L_{payload} + 2 \right) \bmod B_c \right] \right) \bmod B_c$$

This is precisely the **Killer Formula** item #3 in the KTU formula sheet.

> This is a **two-step modular arithmetic expression** that appears verbatim in *Cryptography and Network Security* by Stallings and is reproducible here without any external reference.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Padding Calculation

> **KTU Style Question:** An ESP packet uses **AES-CBC** (block size $B_c = 16$ bytes). The plaintext payload is **31 bytes** long. Compute the exact value of the **Pad-Length** field.

**Step-by-step solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{payload} &= 31 \text{ bytes} \\
B_c &= 16 \text{ bytes} \\
\text{Compute } \left(L_{payload} + 2\right) \bmod B_c &= (31 + 2) \bmod 16 \\
&= 33 \bmod 16 \\
&= 1 \\
\text{Then } P_{length} &= \left(B_c - 1\right) \bmod B_c \\
&= (16 - 1) \bmod 16 \\
&= 15 \bmod 16 \\
&= 15 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification** (re-check the alignment):

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{E_{in}} &= L_{payload} + P_{length} + 2 \\
&= 31 + 15 + 2 \\
&= 48 \text{ bytes} \\
\text{Is } 48 \text{ a multiple of } 16? &\quad 48 / 16 = 3 \; \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** 15 pad bytes; the *Pad-Length* field will contain the integer **15**.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — AH vs ESP

| Property | **AH (RFC 4302)** | **ESP (RFC 4303)** |
|---|---|---|
| Integrity & Authentication | **Yes** (covers IP header + payload) | **Yes** (covers payload only, not outer IP header) |
| Confidentiality (Encryption) | **No** | **Yes** |
| Anti-Replay Service | **Yes** (sequence number) | **Yes** (sequence number) |
| Protects Outer IP Header Fields | **Yes** (mutable fields excluded) | **No** |
| Mandatory with NAT Traversal? | No (breaks with NAT) | Yes (with UDP encapsulation) |
| Protocol Number in IP Header | **51** | **50** |
| Typical Use | Authenticate routing protocols | Build commercial VPNs |
| Combined Use? | Can be used **simultaneously with ESP** in a single packet (rare) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** When asked *"Can AH and ESP be used together?"* — the answer is **YES**, but in practice this is rarely done because ESP already provides authentication. Combining them doubles the cryptographic overhead.

### 3.4 Logical Flow — How an IPSec Packet Is Constructed (Transport Mode ESP)

1. **Application data** arrives from upper layers (TCP, UDP, …).
2. IPSec policy is consulted via the **SPD (Security Policy Database)**.
3. The outbound **SA** is looked up in the **SAD** using the **selector** (src/dst IP, protocol, port).
4. The payload is **padded** using the formula in Section 3.1.
5. **ESP Header** (SPI + Sequence Number) is prepended.
6. **Encryption** is performed on: `Payload ∥ Pad ∥ Pad-Length ∥ Next-Header`.
7. The **IV (Initialization Vector)** is prepended (un-encrypted) for block ciphers.
8. **ICV (Integrity Check Value)** is computed over `ESP Header ∥ IV ∥ Ciphertext` and appended.
9. The result is passed to IP, which prepends the **original IP header** (Transport Mode).
10. The packet is transmitted. The receiver **reverses** these steps.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Architecture — IPSec Functional Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    appLayer["APPLICATION LAYER - HTTP FTP SMTP DNS"]
    transLayer["TRANSPORT LAYER - TCP UDP ICMP"]
    netLayer["IP LAYER - Routing Fragmentation"]
    ipsecBlock["IPSEC SUBSYSTEM"]
    saDb["SECURITY ASSOCIATION DATABASE - SAD"]
    spDb["SECURITY POLICY DATABASE - SPD"]
    ikeModule["IKE DAEMON - IKEv2"]
    cryptoEngine["CRYPTO ENGINE - AES SHA HMAC"]
    isakmp["ISAKMP MESSAGE HANDLER"]

    appLayer --> transLayer --> netLayer --> ipsecBlock
    ipsecBlock --> saDb
    ipsecBlock --> spDb
    ipsecBlock --> ikeModule
    ikeModule --> isakmp
    ipsecBlock --> cryptoEngine
```

### 4.2 Transport Mode vs Tunnel Mode — Packet Encapsulation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TM["TRANSPORT MODE - Host to Host"]
        tmA["Original IP Header"]
        tmB["ESP or AH Header"]
        tmC["TCP or UDP Header"]
        tmD["Application Data"]
        tmE["ESP Trailer - Pad Length Next Header"]
        tmF["ESP ICV"]
        tmA --> tmB --> tmC --> tmD --> tmE --> tmF
    end

    subgraph TuM["TUNNEL MODE - Gateway to Gateway VPN"]
        tumA["NEW Outer IP Header"]
        tumB["ESP or AH Header"]
        tumC["Original IP Header"]
        tumD["TCP or UDP Header"]
        tumE["Application Data"]
        tumF["ESP Trailer - Pad Length Next Header"]
        tumG["ESP ICV"]
        tumA --> tumB --> tumC --> tumD --> tumE --> tumF --> tumG
    end
```

### 4.3 IKEv2 — Four-Message Handshake

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Initiator
    participant Responder

    Note over Initiator,Responder: IKE_SA_INIT exchange - establishes IKE SA and keys
    Initiator->>Responder: HDR 1 SA1 KE1 NONCE1
    Responder->>Initiator: HDR 2 SA2 KE2 NONCE2

    Note over Initiator,Responder: IKE_AUTH exchange - authenticates peers and creates Child SA
    Initiator->>Responder: HDR 3 IDi AUTH ESP Proposal TS
    Responder->>Initiator: HDR 4 IDr AUTH ESP Accepted TS

    Note over Initiator,Responder: IPSec Child SA is now established - data flows
```

### 4.4 ESP Packet Format (Bit-Level Layout)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ESPacket["ESP PACKET - LAYOUT FROM LEFT TO RIGHT"]
        e1["0 to 31 - Security Parameters Index SPI - 32 bits"]
        e2["32 to 63 - Sequence Number - 32 bits"]
        e3["64 to 95+ - IV - 32 to 128 bits unencrypted"]
        e4["Payload Data - variable length - ENCRYPTED"]
        e5["Padding 0 to 255 bytes - ENCRYPTED"]
        e6["Pad Length 8 bits - ENCRYPTED"]
        e7["Next Header 8 bits - ENCRYPTED"]
        e8["ICV 96 to 256 bits - AUTHENTICATED"]
        e1 --> e2 --> e3 --> e4 --> e5 --> e6 --> e7 --> e8
    end
```

### 4.5 AH Packet Format (Bit-Level Layout)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph AhPacket["AH PACKET - LAYOUT FROM LEFT TO RIGHT"]
        a1["0 to 7 - Next Header - 8 bits"]
        a2["8 to 15 - Payload Length - 8 bits"]
        a3["16 to 31 - Reserved - 16 bits"]
        a4["32 to 63 - SPI - 32 bits"]
        a5["64 to 95 - Sequence Number - 32 bits"]
        a6["96 to 127+ - Authentication Data ICV - variable 96 128 256 bits"]
        a1 --> a2 --> a3 --> a4 --> a5 --> a6
    end
```

### 4.6 Security Association Lifecycle (SAD State Machine)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> Negotiating: IKE_SA_INIT exchange begins
    Negotiating -> Authenticated: IKE_AUTH messages exchanged
    Authenticated --> Mature: Child SA keys derived
    Mature --> Rekeying: Soft lifetime reached
    Rekeying --> Mature: New Child SA created via CREATE_CHILD_SA
    Mature --> Dying: Hard lifetime reached
    Dying --> [*]: SA deleted from SAD
    Mature --> [*]: Manual delete or peer DPD failure
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

#### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between the Authentication Header (AH) and the Encapsulating Security Payload (ESP) protocols of IPSec. Mention any two points of difference.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Confidentiality:** ESP provides **encryption** of the payload (confidentiality), whereas AH does **not** encrypt data — it only authenticates it.
2. **Coverage of IP Header:** AH authenticates the **entire IP packet** including most of the outer IP header, while ESP authenticates and encrypts **only the payload** (and in Tunnel Mode, the inner IP header); the outer IP header remains **unprotected by ESP**.

*(Any other valid point — protocol numbers AH=51, ESP=50; AH not compatible with NAT; ESP supports combined integrity + encryption — also earns full marks.)*

---

#### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**What is a Security Association (SA) in IPSec? Why is an SA considered unidirectional?**

**Model Answer:**

A **Security Association (SA)** is a logical, one-way (simplex) connection between two IPSec endpoints that defines the **security services, cryptographic algorithms, keys, lifetimes, and operational mode** (transport or tunnel) applied to the traffic that matches a given selector.

An SA is **unidirectional** because each direction of a conversation can use *different* cryptographic parameters — for example, the sender may use AES-256-GCM for confidentiality, while the receiver may use AES-128-CBC. The SA only protects traffic flowing in **one direction**, so a full bidirectional IPSec session always requires **two SAs** — one inbound, one outbound. This is also why the triple $(SPI, \text{Destination IP}, \text{Protocol})$ uniquely identifies *one* SA.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (Choice Provided)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **IPSec architecture** as defined in RFC 4301. Describe the roles of the **SAD**, **SPD**, and **IKE** in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain **Transport Mode** and **Tunnel Mode** of ESP. Give **one real-world example** of each. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) IPSec Architecture (RFC 4301) — 7 marks**

The IPSec architecture, defined in RFC 4301, has three core subsystems:

1. **Security Protocols (AH and ESP):** Carry the actual security services on each packet. AH is protocol number 51, ESP is protocol number 50.
2. **Security Association Database (SAD):** A kernel-resident table holding the state of every active SA, indexed by the triple $(SPI, \text{Destination IP}, \text{Protocol})$. Each SAD entry contains: SPI, anti-replay sequence number, cryptographic algorithm IDs, keys, SA lifetime, and mode (transport/tunnel).
3. **Security Policy Database (SPD):** Stores **outbound and inbound processing rules**. For every packet, the SPD is consulted to determine whether the packet should be: *protected* (use IPSec), *bypassed* (sent in cleartext), or *discarded*. The selection is based on *selectors* — source/destination IP, port, protocol, user identity.
4. **Internet Key Exchange (IKE):** Automates the negotiation, creation, and deletion of SAs. IKEv2 (RFC 7296) uses a 4-message handshake: IKE_SA_INIT establishes the IKE SA, IKE_AUTH authenticates the peers and creates the first Child SA. IKE handles key derivation via **Diffie–Hellman**, peer authentication via **PSK / certificates / EAP**, and SA lifetime management.

**(b) ESP Modes — 7 marks**

Refer to **Section 4.2 diagram**. Textual elaboration:

- **Transport Mode ESP:** The **original IP header** is preserved and the ESP header is inserted *between* the IP header and the upper-layer (TCP/UDP) header. Only the **payload** (and ESP trailer) is encrypted. Authentication covers the ESP header, payload, and ESP trailer. **Example:** Host-to-host IPSec between two servers in the same administrative domain.
- **Tunnel Mode ESP:** The **entire original IP packet** is encapsulated as the new payload of an ESP packet, with a **brand-new outer IP header** prepended. Both the original IP header and the payload are encrypted. **Example:** A branch-office VPN gateway sending all employee traffic through a tunnel to the corporate HQ firewall.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **IKEv2 four-message handshake** with a sequence diagram. How is it different from IKEv1? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the **ESP packet format**. Why is the *Next-Header* field placed inside the encrypted portion? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) IKEv2 Handshake — 7 marks**

IKEv2 simplifies IKEv1 by collapsing the eight-message Main Mode / Quick Mode dance into **four messages**, divided into two exchanges:

1. **IKE_SA_INIT (Messages 1 & 2):** Negotiates the cryptographic suite for the IKE SA, performs a **Diffie–Hellman key exchange**, and exchanges nonces. The result is the *SKEYSEED* from which all subsequent keys are derived.
2. **IKE_AUTH (Messages 3 & 4):** Authenticates the peers (using PSK, digital signature, or EAP), exchanges identities, and negotiates the first **Child SA** (the actual IPSec SA protecting user data).

**Differences from IKEv1:**

| Feature | IKEv1 | IKEv2 |
|---|---|---|
| Total messages for first SA | 6 (Main) or 3 (Aggressive) | 4 (fixed) |
| Modes | Main + Aggressive | Single unified mode |
| NAT Traversal | Bolt-on | Built-in |
| Reliability | UDP, no retransmission | UDP with built-in retransmit & ack |
| Authentication options | Limited | PSK, certificates, EAP |

**(b) ESP Packet Format — 7 marks**

Refer to **Section 4.4 diagram**. The packet from left to right is:

`SPI (32 bits) ∥ Sequence Number (32 bits) ∥ IV (variable, 32–128 bits) ∥ Ciphertext (Payload + Pad + Pad-Length + Next-Header) ∥ ICV (96–256 bits)`

**Why the *Next-Header* field is encrypted:**
The *Next-Header* field is placed at the **end of the encrypted payload** to ensure that an attacker performing **traffic analysis** cannot determine which upper-layer protocol (TCP, UDP, ICMP, etc.) is in use. Hiding the next-header is part of the *Limited Traffic-Flow Confidentiality* service defined in RFC 4301. The receiver, who holds the decryption key, simply reads the last decrypted byte to identify the next layer. If *Next-Header* were placed in the clear (outside encryption), it would leak protocol information to eavesdroppers.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing AH and ESP coverage.** Many students write *"ESP encrypts the IP header"*. This is wrong. ESP **leaves the outer IP header in the clear**. Only in *Tunnel Mode* does the **inner** IP header get encrypted.
> 2. **Forgetting that an SA is unidirectional.** Marks are lost when students describe a single SA as covering *both* directions. The correct answer is **two SAs per session**.
> 3. **Incorrect protocol numbers.** AH = 51, ESP = 50. Swapping these is a classic one-mark loss.
> 4. **Forgetting the ICV.** ESP's authentication covers the **ESP header, IV, and ciphertext — not the outer IP header**. AH's authentication **does** cover the outer IP header (except for mutable fields like TTL).
> 5. **Mixing up IKEv1 and IKEv2 message counts.** IKEv1 Main Mode = 6 messages, Aggressive Mode = 3 messages. IKEv2 = **4 messages** total for the first SA.
> 6. **Wrong Pad-Length computation.** Students often forget the "+2" (for Pad-Length + Next-Header fields) when computing cipher block alignment.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **IPSec** secures traffic at the **Network Layer (Layer 3)** of the OSI model, transparently protecting **all** upper-layer protocols.
- The two security protocols are **AH (Authentication Header, IP protocol 51)** and **ESP (Encapsulating Security Payload, IP protocol 50)**.
- **AH** provides integrity + authentication only; **ESP** provides integrity + authentication + confidentiality.
- AH covers the **outer IP header**; ESP does **not**.
- A **Security Association (SA)** is a **unidirectional** logical state object, identified by the triple **$(SPI, \text{Destination IP}, \text{Protocol})$**.
- **Transport Mode** is used for host-to-host; **Tunnel Mode** is used for VPNs (gateway-to-gateway).
- **IKEv2** is the modern key-exchange protocol, with a clean **4-message** handshake (IKE_SA_INIT + IKE_AUTH).
- ESP packet ends with the **ICV (Integrity Check Value)** — typically 96 bits (HMAC-MD5), 128 bits (HMAC-SHA-1), or 256 bits (HMAC-SHA-256).
- The **anti-replay window** uses a **32-bit sequence number** (or 64-bit with ESN) — a packet with a sequence number below the window's left edge is dropped.
- **Padding rule for block ciphers in ESP:** $P_{length} = \left(B_c - \left[(L_{payload} + 2) \bmod B_c\right]\right) \bmod B_c$.
- **RFC 4301** is the umbrella architecture document; **RFC 4302** (AH), **RFC 4303** (ESP), and **RFC 7296** (IKEv2) are the protocol specifics.
- IPSec is the **de-facto security layer for enterprise VPNs, 5G core networks, SD-WAN overlays, and IP routing protocol authentication**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Routing Algorithms: The Link-State (LS) Routing Algorithm
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT631 - NETWORK & DATA SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Routing Algorithms: The Link-State (LS) Routing Algorithm

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Link-State (LS) Routing Algorithm** is a *global* and *topology-aware* shortest-path routing algorithm in which every router maintains an **identical Link-State Database (LSDB)** representing the complete map of the network. Each router independently runs **Dijkstra's Shortest-Path-First (SPF) algorithm** on this map to compute the minimum-cost path to every other router, and derives its own forwarding table.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, LS routing falls under the category of **Intra-AS (Autonomous System) Routing Protocols**, and is the algorithmic basis of **OSPF (Open Shortest Path First)** — standardized in **RFC 2328** for IPv4 and **RFC 5340** for IPv6 (OSPFv3).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Google Maps Analogy"

Imagine every household in Kerala is a **router**. Instead of each person asking only their immediate neighbors for directions (this is what **Distance-Vector** does), the Kerala government provides **every household with a complete, updated road map of the entire state**. Now:

1. Every house can **independently compute** the shortest route from its own location to any other house.
2. Whenever a road is closed or a new bridge opens, the government issues an **update notice** to every household (this is *flooding*).
3. Each household then **re-plans** its routes on the new map.

This is exactly what Link-State Routing does — but with routers, links, and IP prefixes.

| **LS Analogy Element** | **Real Network Element** |
| :--- | :--- |
| Household | Router |
| Complete state road map | Link-State Database (LSDB) |
| Road closure notice | Link-State Packet (LSP / LSA) |
| Re-planning the route | Re-running Dijkstra's algorithm |
| Drawing your own route | Building the Forwarding Table |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (KTU Favourite Question):** Distance-Vector uses **local information + neighbors' information** (Bellman-Ford), while Link-State uses **global information** of the entire network (Dijkstra).

## 1.3 Fundamental Metrics

* **Cost Metric $c(u,v)$** — Non-negative administrative weight of the link between router $u$ and router $v$. In OSPF, cost is typically inversely proportional to link bandwidth: 
$$c(u,v) = \dfrac{\text{Reference Bandwidth}}{\text{Link Bandwidth}}$$

* **Shortest-Path Distance $D(v)$** — The minimum accumulated cost from source router $u$ to destination router $v$.

* **Number of Routers $N$** — Total routers in the AS.
* **Number of Links $E$** — Total edges in the topology graph.

---

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Operational Phases of Link-State Routing

The LS algorithm executes in **five distinct phases**. KTU examiners frequently test these phases as short notes or 7-mark questions.

### **Phase 1 — Neighbor Discovery**
Each router sends **HELLO packets** out every interface (in OSPF, this uses a dedicated multicast address **224.0.0.5**). Neighbors respond, and the discovering router learns:
* The **Router ID** of each neighbor.
* The **IP address** of the corresponding interface.
* The **subnet** to which that interface is connected.

### **Phase 2 — Link-Cost Measurement**
Each router measures the cost to each of its directly connected neighbors. Cost may be:
* Statically configured (administrative weight).
* Dynamically measured (delay, packet loss, throughput).
* The most common real-world formula is bandwidth-based (see Formula Sheet below).

### **Phase 3 — Link-State Packet (LSP / LSA) Construction**
The router constructs a small packet containing:
* **Sender's Router ID**
* **Sequence Number** (to identify freshness)
* **Age** (for expiry)
* **List of (neighbor ID, cost)** pairs — one for each directly connected link.

### **Phase 4 — Reliable Flooding**
The LSP is **flooded to ALL routers in the AS** (not just neighbors) using a controlled, reliable flooding algorithm:
* Each router forwards the LSP out every interface **except the one it arrived on**.
* Sequence numbers prevent **duplicate forwarding** and **loops**.
* Old or duplicate LSPs are discarded.

> [!NOTE]
> *Reliable flooding* ensures that, eventually, **every router has an identical LSDB**. This is what makes the algorithm *global*.

### **Phase 5 — Shortest-Path Computation (Dijkstra's Algorithm)**
Each router now runs **Dijkstra's SPF algorithm** on its LSDB to compute the shortest path from itself (the source) to every other router. The output is a tree called the **Shortest-Path Tree (SPT)** rooted at the local router. The forwarding table is then built by reading the SPT.

## 2.2 Properties of Link-State Routing

| **Property** | **Link-State Behavior** |
| :--- | :--- |
| Information Scope | **Global** — full topology |
| Algorithm | **Dijkstra (Link-State / SPF)** |
| Convergence Speed | **Fast** (typically seconds) |
| Loop Formation | **Loop-free** at convergence (uses SPF tree) |
| CPU / Memory Cost | **High** ($O(N \log N)$ per computation) |
| Bandwidth Cost | **Higher** (flooding of LSPs) |
| Robustness | **High** (each router computes independently) |
| Real-World Protocol | **OSPF, IS-IS** |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Formula / Concept** | **Expression** | **Units / Notes** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| OSPF Link Cost (Cisco) | $c(u,v) = \dfrac{\text{Reference BW}}{\text{Interface BW}}$ | Reference BW = **100 Mbps** (default Cisco) |
| Dijkstra Update Rule | $D(v) = \min\{\,D(v),\; D(w) + c(w,v)\,\}$ | For all $v \notin N'$, $w \in N'$ |
| Dijkstra Termination | $N' = V$ (all vertices added) | Algorithm stops when all $N$ nodes finalized |
| Message Complexity (LSP Flooding) | $O(N \cdot E)$ LSPs sent | Each of $N$ routers sends $E$ LSPs |
| Storage Complexity | $O(N \cdot E)$ per router | LSDB size |
| Time Complexity — Naive | $O(N^2)$ | Linear scan for min |
| Time Complexity — Heap (Fibonacci) | $O(E + N \log N)$ | Production-grade OSPF |
| LSP Freshness | Accept if $\text{Seq}_{new} > \text{Seq}_{old}$ | Wraps around with care |
| Max-Age (LSP TTL) | $\text{MaxAge} = \mathbf{1 \text{ hour}}$ | After which LSP is re-flooded (OSPF) |
| Bandwidth Examples | 10 Mbps → cost **10**; 100 Mbps → cost **1**; 1 Gbps → cost **1** (with $BW_{ref}=1000$ Mbps) | Inverse relationship |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students write Dijkstra's update as $D(v) = D(w) + c(w,v)$ instead of the **min** form. The min is essential — it represents the relaxation step that prevents the cost from ever increasing.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Link-State routing underpins virtually every large enterprise and ISP network. The algorithm's predictable convergence, loop-freedom at steady state, and ability to engineer traffic using **OSPF areas** (Area 0 backbone + non-backbone areas with ABRs) make it the de-facto standard for:
* **Enterprise campus networks** (multi-area OSPF).
* **ISP interior routing** (alongside IS-IS).
* **Data center fabrics** (with OSPF or IS-IS underlay).
* **SDN underlays** (BGP-LS exports link-state to controllers).

In contrast, **Distance-Vector** (RIP, IGRP) is used only in small, simple networks today — making **OSPF/IS-IS the only LS protocol candidates in modern KTU networking electives**.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivation — Dijkstra's Algorithm Trace

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example (Standard KTU 6-Node Topology)**
> Consider the following abstract network. Each edge label $c(u,v)$ represents the link cost. Router $u$ is the source.

## 3.1 Network Topology (Reference Graph)

We use the canonical 6-node example $\{u, v, w, x, y, z\}$ with the following link costs:

| **Edge** | **Cost** | **Edge** | **Cost** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $(u,v)$ | **1** | $(w,y)$ | **1** |
| $(u,x)$ | **2** | $(w,z)$ | **2** |
| $(v,w)$ | **5** | $(x,y)$ | **4** |
| $(v,x)$ | **3** | $(y,z)$ | **2** |
| $(w,x)$ | **4** | | |

## 3.2 Dijkstra's Algorithm — Pseudocode (Conceptual)

> Mathematical, not Python — strictly per the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE non-programming constraint for this analytical topic.

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{Initialization:} \\
&\quad N' = \{u\} \\
&\quad \forall v \neq u:\; D(v) = c(u,v) \text{ if } (u,v) \in E,\; \text{else } \infty \\
&\quad p(v) = u \text{ for adjacent } v,\; \text{else undefined} \\[4pt]
&\textbf{Loop:} \\
&\quad \text{Find } w \notin N' \text{ minimizing } D(w) \\
&\quad N' = N' \cup \{w\} \\
&\quad \forall v \notin N' \text{ adjacent to } w: \\
&\qquad D(v) = \min\{\,D(v),\; D(w) + c(w,v)\,\} \\
&\qquad p(v) = w \text{ if relaxation improved } D(v) \\[4pt]
&\textbf{Until } N' = V
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.3 Iterative Trace (Step-by-Step Deduction)

### **Step $k=0$ — Initialization**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N' &= \{u\} \\
D(v) &= c(u,v) = 1 \\
D(x) &= c(u,x) = 2 \\
D(w) &= D(y) = D(z) = \infty
\end{aligned}
$$

Predecessors: $p(v) = p(x) = u$. Minimum unvisited node = $v$ (cost **1**).

### **Step $k=1$ — Add $v$ to $N'$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N' &= \{u,\, v\} \\
\text{Relax via } v:\quad D(w) &= \min\{\infty,\; 1 + 5\} = 6,\;\; p(w) = v \\
D(x) &= \min\{2,\; 1 + 3\} = 2 \quad (\text{no change, keep } p(x)=u)
\end{aligned}
$$

Minimum unvisited = $x$ (cost **2**).

### **Step $k=2$ — Add $x$ to $N'$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N' &= \{u,\, v,\, x\} \\
\text{Relax via } x:\quad D(w) &= \min\{6,\; 2 + 4\} = 6 \quad (\text{tie, keep } p(w)=v) \\
D(y) &= \min\{\infty,\; 2 + 4\} = 6,\;\; p(y) = x
\end{aligned}
$$

Minimum unvisited = $w$ (cost **6**) — tie with $y$, but we add $w$ first by convention.

### **Step $k=3$ — Add $w$ to $N'$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N' &= \{u,\, v,\, x,\, w\} \\
\text{Relax via } w:\quad D(y) &= \min\{6,\; 6 + 1\} = 6 \quad (\text{no change, keep } p(y)=x) \\
D(z) &= \min\{\infty,\; 6 + 2\} = 8,\;\; p(z) = w
\end{aligned}
$$

Minimum unvisited = $y$ (cost **6**).

### **Step $k=4$ — Add $y$ to $N'$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N' &= \{u,\, v,\, x,\, w,\, y\} \\
\text{Relax via } y:\quad D(z) &= \min\{8,\; 6 + 2\} = 8 \quad (\text{no change, keep } p(z)=w)
\end{aligned}
$$

Minimum unvisited = $z$ (cost **8**).

### **Step $k=5$ — Add $z$ to $N'$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N' &= \{u,\, v,\, x,\, w,\, y,\, z\} = V \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \textbf{TERMINATE}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.4 Resulting Shortest-Path Tree (SPT) from $u$

| **Destination** | **Shortest Cost $D(v)$** | **Predecessor $p(v)$** | **Shortest Path (Reverse)** |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| $v$ | **1** | $u$ | $u \to v$ |
| $x$ | **2** | $u$ | $u \to x$ |
| $w$ | **6** | $v$ | $u \to v \to w$ |
| $y$ | **6** | $x$ | $u \to x \to y$ |
| $z$ | **8** | $w$ | $u \to v \to w \to z$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **forwarding table** of router $u$ is derived by reading the **first hop** on each reverse path. For example, for destination $z$, the first hop is $v$ (since $u \to v$ is the first edge on the reverse path $u \to v \to w \to z$).

## 3.5 Forwarding Table of Router $u$

| **Destination Subnet** | **Next Hop** | **Total Cost** |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| $v$ | $v$ | 1 |
| $x$ | $x$ | 2 |
| $w$ | $v$ | 6 |
| $y$ | $x$ | 6 |
| $z$ | $v$ | 8 |

## 3.6 Verification — Why this is Optimal

By Dijkstra's correctness theorem, once a node is added to $N'$, its shortest distance is **final** because all future paths must pass through nodes whose distances are **at least as large** as the current node's distance — hence cannot yield a smaller total.

Formally:

$$
\forall w \in N',\; \nexists \text{ path } u \rightsquigarrow w \text{ with cost } < D(w)
$$

This is the **greedy-choice property** that Dijkstra's algorithm exploits — guaranteed by the assumption that all link costs are **non-negative**.

# 4. Link-State Routing — Algorithmic & Network Properties

## 4.1 Mathematical Correctness of Dijkstra

The proof relies on the invariant that, at every step, the node $w$ chosen with minimum $D(w)$ has its final shortest-path distance. The relaxation formula

$$
D(v) = \min\{\,D(v),\; D(w) + c(w,v)\,\}
$$

ensures that the distance vector $D$ is **monotonically non-increasing** for any newly considered path through $w$. Combined with the fact that all costs are $\geq 0$, no future path can improve on a node already finalized.

## 4.2 Comparison Matrix — Link-State vs. Distance-Vector

| **Criterion** | **Link-State (OSPF)** | **Distance-Vector (RIP)** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Information Used | **Full topology** | Only neighbors' distances |
| Algorithm | **Dijkstra** | **Bellman-Ford** |
| Convergence | Fast (seconds) | Slow (count-to-infinity) |
| Loops at Convergence | None | Possible (temporary) |
| CPU / Memory | High | Low |
| Bandwidth (Updates) | Event-driven, small LSPs | Periodic full table |
| Scalability | Hierarchical (Areas) | Poor for large nets |
| Metric | Cost (typically bandwidth) | Hop count |
| Max Hops / Routers | Unlimited (with areas) | Max 15 hops |
| Real Protocol | **OSPF, IS-IS** | **RIP v1/v2, IGRP** |

## 4.3 Complexity Analysis (Big-O Summary)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Message Complexity} &= O(N \cdot E) \\
\text{LSDB Storage} &= O(N \cdot E) \\
\text{Dijkstra (Naive)} &= O(N^2) \\
\text{Dijkstra (Binary Heap)} &= O((E + N) \log N) \\
\text{Dijkstra (Fibonacci Heap)} &= O(E + N \log N)
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> In OSPF, the trigger to re-run Dijkstra is the **arrival of a new LSP** (not periodic). This is the **event-driven** nature that makes OSPF bandwidth-efficient compared to RIP's every-30-seconds broadcast.

## 4.4 Engineering Reality — OSPF Areas (Hierarchical LS)

To scale beyond ~100 routers, OSPF introduces a **two-level hierarchy**:

* **Area 0 (Backbone)** — All non-backbone areas must connect to Area 0.
* **Non-Backbone Area** — Internal topology is hidden from other areas.
* **Area Border Router (ABR)** — Summarizes routes between areas.
* **Backbone Router** — Has interfaces in Area 0.
* **Internal Router** — All interfaces in a single area.

This keeps each router's LSDB bounded to its own area, drastically reducing memory and CPU load.
