# 6. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Confusing LS with DV** — Many students describe LS as "routers exchange tables with neighbors" — this describes **Distance-Vector**, not Link-State. LS floods *topology* (LSPs), not routing tables.
> 2. **Forgetting the $\min$ in relaxation** — Writing $D(v) = D(w) + c(w,v)$ without $\min$ loses 1–2 marks instantly.
> 3. **Not updating the predecessor** — When relaxation improves $D(v)$, the predecessor $p(v)$ MUST be set to $w$. Examiners deduct marks for missing this.
> 4. **Confusing $N'$ (finalized set) with $V$ (all nodes)** — $N'$ grows; $V$ is fixed.
> 5. **Ignoring sequence numbers in flooding** — A common 3-mark question asks "how are loops avoided in flooding?" Answer: **sequence numbers** reject duplicates.

---

## 6.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — Jul 2023]`
**List the five steps involved in the Link-State Routing Algorithm.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Neighbor Discovery** — Each router discovers its directly connected neighbors and learns their addresses (via HELLO packets).
2. **Link-Cost Measurement** — Each router determines the cost (metric) to each of its neighbors.
3. **LSP Construction** — Each router builds a Link-State Packet containing the list of (neighbor, cost) pairs, its own Router ID, a sequence number, and an age field.
4. **Reliable Flooding** — Each router floods its LSP to **all other routers** in the AS; sequence numbers prevent duplicates and loops.
5. **Dijkstra's Algorithm** — Each router runs the SPF algorithm on its LSDB to compute the shortest path to every destination and builds its forwarding table.

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`
**Differentiate between Link-State and Distance-Vector routing algorithms (any four points).**

**Model Answer (3 marks — any 4 of the following):**

| **Sl.** | **Link-State** | **Distance-Vector** |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Uses **global** topology information | Uses only **neighbors'** information |
| 2 | Algorithm: **Dijkstra (SPF)** | Algorithm: **Bellman-Ford** |
| 3 | **Fast convergence**, loop-free at steady state | **Slow convergence**, susceptible to count-to-infinity |
| 4 | **High** memory and CPU cost | **Low** memory and CPU cost |
| 5 | Used in **OSPF, IS-IS** | Used in **RIP, IGRP** |
| 6 | Event-driven updates; bandwidth-efficient | Periodic full-table updates; bandwidth-wasteful |
| 7 | Scalable via **hierarchical areas** | Limited scalability (RIP: max 15 hops) |

---

## 6.2 Part B — ESE Module Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> *Strictly per KTU 2024 ESE pattern: each Part B question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.*

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the Link-State Routing Algorithm in detail. Describe the role of **Link-State Packets (LSPs)** and **reliable flooding** in achieving loop-free routing. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using Dijkstra's algorithm, compute the shortest path from source node **$u$** to all other nodes in the network given below. Construct the forwarding table for $u$. **(7 marks)**

> Topology for (b):

| **Edge** | **Cost** | **Edge** | **Cost** |
| :--- | :---: | :--- | :---: |
| $(u, v)$ | 2 | $(w, z)$ | 3 |
| $(u, w)$ | 5 | $(x, y)$ | 2 |
| $(u, x)$ | 1 | $(y, z)$ | 4 |
| $(v, w)$ | 3 | $(w, y)$ | 2 |
| $(v, x)$ | 4 | | |

### **Model Solution — Question A**

#### **Part (a) — Detailed Explanation of Link-State Routing (7 marks)**

**LS Algorithm — Definition (1 mark):**
A global, topology-aware algorithm in which every router maintains an identical **Link-State Database (LSDB)** and runs **Dijkstra's SPF algorithm** to compute shortest paths.

**Five Phases (3 marks):**
1. Neighbor discovery using HELLO.
2. Link-cost measurement.
3. LSP construction.
4. Reliable flooding.
5. Dijkstra's SPF + forwarding table.

**Role of LSPs (1.5 marks):**
Each LSP contains the router's ID, sequence number, age, and a list of (neighbor, cost) pairs. LSPs carry **raw topology**, not routing tables — hence the term *link-state*. Sequence numbers ensure **freshness** and prevent duplicate forwarding.

**Role of Reliable Flooding (1.5 marks):**
Flooding delivers every router's LSP to **all other routers**, ensuring identical LSDBs. The algorithm:
* Forwards LSPs out **all interfaces except the one received on**.
* Uses **sequence numbers** to discard duplicates.
* Uses **acknowledgments** and **retransmissions** to ensure reliability.
* Guarantees convergence to a **loop-free** state.

#### **Part (b) — Dijkstra Trace (7 marks)**

**Step 0 — Initialize** ($N'=\{u\}$): 
$$D(v)=2,\; D(w)=5,\; D(x)=1,\; D(y)=\infty,\; D(z)=\infty$$

**Step 1 — Add $x$** (min = 1): 
$$D(y)=\min\{\infty, 1+2\}=3 \text{ via }x; \; D(v)=\min\{2, 1+4\}=2$$

**Step 2 — Add $v$** (min = 2): 
$$D(w)=\min\{5, 2+3\}=5 \text{ (no change)}; \; D(y)=\min\{3, 2+\infty\}=3$$

**Step 3 — Add $y$** (min = 3): 
$$D(w)=\min\{5, 3+2\}=5 \text{ (no change)}; \; D(z)=\min\{\infty, 3+4\}=7 \text{ via }y$$

**Step 4 — Add $w$** (min = 5): 
$$D(z)=\min\{7, 5+3\}=7 \text{ (no change)}$$

**Step 5 — Add $z$** (min = 7): Algorithm terminates.

**Final Shortest Paths from $u$:**

| **Dest** | **Cost $D$** | **Path** |
| :---: | :---: | :--- |
| $v$ | 2 | $u \to v$ |
| $w$ | 5 | $u \to w$ |
| $x$ | 1 | $u \to x$ |
| $y$ | 3 | $u \to x \to y$ |
| $z$ | 7 | $u \to x \to y \to z$ |

**Forwarding Table for $u$ (1 mark):**

| **Destination** | **Next Hop** | **Cost** |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $v$ | $v$ | 2 |
| $w$ | $w$ | 5 |
| $x$ | $x$ | 1 |
| $y$ | $x$ | 3 |
| $z$ | $x$ | 7 |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With a suitable example, explain **Dijkstra's shortest-path algorithm** step by step. How is it used in Link-State Routing? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the network given below, find the shortest paths from node **$A$** to all other nodes using Dijkstra's algorithm. Also draw the resulting **Shortest-Path Tree (SPT)**. **(7 marks)**

> Topology for (b):

| **Edge** | **Cost** | **Edge** | **Cost** |
| :--- | :---: | :--- | :---: |
| $(A, B)$ | 4 | $(C, D)$ | 1 |
| $(A, C)$ | 2 | $(D, E)$ | 3 |
| $(B, C)$ | 1 | $(B, D)$ | 5 |
| $(C, E)$ | 6 | $(D, F)$ | 2 |
| $(E, F)$ | 2 | | |

### **Model Solution — Question B**

#### **Part (a) — Dijkstra's Algorithm with Example (7 marks)**

**Concept (1.5 marks):** Dijkstra's algorithm computes the shortest path from a single source to all other vertices in a graph with **non-negative edge weights**. It maintains:
* A set $N'$ of finalized nodes.
* A tentative distance $D(v)$ for every node $v$.
* A predecessor $p(v)$ for path reconstruction.

**Algorithm Steps with Toy Example (3.5 marks):**
Consider a triangle $u\text{-}v\text{-}w$ with $c(u,v)=1, c(v,w)=2, c(u,w)=4$.

| **Step** | **$N'$** | **$D(v), p(v)$** | **$D(w), p(w)$** | **Action** |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| 0 | $\{u\}$ | $1, u$ | $4, u$ | Min = $v$ |
| 1 | $\{u, v\}$ | — | $\min\{4, 1+2\}=3, v$ | Min = $w$ |
| 2 | $\{u, v, w\}$ | — | — | **Done** |

Result: $D(v)=1$ via $u$, $D(w)=3$ via $v$. Path $u \to v \to w$ (cost 3) beats direct $u \to w$ (cost 4).

**Use in Link-State Routing (2 marks):** In OSPF, every router runs Dijkstra **locally** on its identical LSDB. The source is the local router; "edges" are the links in the LSDB. The output SPT directly gives the forwarding table.

#### **Part (b) — Dijkstra Trace from Node $A$ (7 marks)**

**Step 0 — Initialize** ($N'=\{A\}$): 
$$D(B)=4,\; D(C)=2,\; D(D)=D(E)=D(F)=\infty$$

**Step 1 — Add $C$** (min = 2): 
$$D(B)=\min\{4, 2+1\}=3 \text{ via }C$$
$$D(E)=\min\{\infty, 2+6\}=8 \text{ via }C$$

**Step 2 — Add $B$** (min = 3): 
$$D(D)=\min\{\infty, 3+5\}=8 \text{ via }B$$

**Step 3 — Add $D$** (min = 8, tie with $E$, add $D$ first): 
$$D(E)=\min\{8, 8+3\}=8 \text{ (no change, keep }C)$$
$$D(F)=\min\{\infty, 8+2\}=10 \text{ via }D$$

**Step 4 — Add $E$** (min = 8): 
$$D(F)=\min\{10, 8+2\}=10 \text{ (no change)}$$

**Step 5 — Add $F$** (min = 10): Algorithm terminates.

**Final SPT from $A$:**

| **Dest** | **Cost** | **Path** | **First Hop** |
| :---: | :---: | :--- | :---: |
| $B$ | 3 | $A \to C \to B$ | $C$ |
| $C$ | 2 | $A \to C$ | $C$ |
| $D$ | 8 | $A \to C \to B \to D$ | $C$ |
| $E$ | 8 | $A \to C \to E$ | $C$ |
| $F$ | 10 | $A \to C \to B \to D \to F$ | $C$ |

**SPT Diagram (for 7 marks completion):**
$$A \xrightarrow{2} C \xrightarrow{1} B \xrightarrow{5} D \xrightarrow{2} F$$
$$A \xrightarrow{2} C \xrightarrow{6} E$$

---

## 6.3 Topic Recap — High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision (Memorize These 12 Points):**

1. **LS = Global topology** + **Dijkstra's algorithm** at every router.
2. **DV = Local info + Bellman-Ford** (RIP uses DV — don't confuse).
3. Five phases: **Discovery → Measure → LSP → Flood → Dijkstra**.
4. **Reliable flooding** uses **sequence numbers** + acknowledgments to prevent loops and duplicates.
5. **LSP contents:** Router ID + Seq Num + Age + list of (neighbor, cost).
6. **Dijkstra update rule:** $D(v) = \min\{D(v), D(w) + c(w,v)\}$.
7. **Greedy-choice property** holds because link costs are **non-negative**.
8. **OSPF cost formula (Cisco):** $c = BW_{ref} / BW_{interface}$ (default $BW_{ref} = 100$ Mbps).
9. **Time complexity:** $O(N^2)$ naive, $O(E + N \log N)$ with Fibonacci heap.
10. **Forwarding table** is derived from the **first hop** of the SPT.
11. **OSPF hierarchy** = Area 0 backbone + non-backbone areas + ABRs (scales LS to large networks).
12. **Convergence:** LS is fast (seconds); DV is slow (count-to-infinity possible).

> **All the best for your KTU ESE! Maintain the algorithm trace systematically — examiners reward clarity and correct predecessor updates.**
---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The Distance-Vector (DV) Routing Algorithm
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT631 - NETWORK & DATA SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** The Distance-Vector (DV) Routing Algorithm

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Distance-Vector (DV) Routing Algorithm** is a decentralized, iterative, and asynchronous routing protocol in which each node (router) in a network maintains a **distance vector** — a data structure containing, for every known destination, the estimated *shortest distance* (cost) and the *next-hop* identifier along the path that achieves that minimum. Routers periodically exchange these vectors with their **directly connected neighbors only**, and each router independently recomputes its own table using the classic iterative-relaxation rule.

Mathematically, the algorithm is grounded in the **Bellman–Ford equation**, which for a destination $y$ viewed from node $x$ through an intermediate neighbor $v$ is written as:

$$D_x(y) = \min_{v \in N(x)} \left\{ c(x,v) + D_v(y) \right\}$$

where $N(x)$ is the set of neighbors of $x$, and $c(x,v)$ is the cost of the direct link from $x$ to $v$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Under the 2024 NEP-aligned scheme, DV is treated as a *deterministic, decentralized shortest-path method* and forms the theoretical basis for protocols like **RIP (Routing Information Protocol)**. It is contrasted with the **Link-State (LS) routing** approach in the same module.

---

## 1.2 Intuition Through a Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **tourist visiting a new city without a smartphone map**. Each tourist stands at a street corner and only knows the *distances to his immediate neighbours* (the streets branching directly away). To find the shortest route to a museum across the city, the tourist **asks every neighbour**: *"How far is the museum from you?"* Each neighbour answers with *their* best estimate, and the tourist adds the street length to that neighbour.

The tourist then chooses the neighbour whose **total estimated distance** (street + neighbour's distance) is smallest, and that neighbour becomes the *first step* of his journey. After a few rounds of asking — and after each neighbour has had a chance to improve *their* estimates by asking *their* neighbours — every tourist in the city converges on the truly shortest routes.

That iterative "ask neighbours, sum, pick minimum, repeat" process **is the DV algorithm**.

| KTU Term | Tourist Analogy |
|---|---|
| Router / Node | Tourist at an intersection |
| Distance Vector $D_x$ | Tourist's mental list of "best-known distances to landmarks" |
| Direct link cost $c(x,v)$ | Physical street length to a neighbouring intersection |
| Bellman-Ford update | "Total = street-to-neighbour + neighbour's reported distance" |
| Iteration | One round of asking-and-answering among all tourists |
| Convergence | When no tourist changes his chosen next-hop anymore |

---

## 1.3 Key Characteristics at a Glance

> [!NOTE]
> **Five-Finger Summary of DV's Identity (high-yield for KTU 2-markers):**
> 1. **Iterative** — the algorithm repeats until no router updates its table.
> 2. **Asynchronous** — routers need not synchronize their updates; any local change triggers a recomputation.
> 3. **Distributed** — every node computes *only* from information received from its immediate neighbours; no node has global knowledge.
> 4. **Self-terminating** — the algorithm halts automatically when convergence is reached.
> 5. **Bellman–Ford based** — the per-node update equation is mathematically identical to the Bellman–Ford shortest-path recurrence.

The standard cost metric in KTU-style textbook examples is **hop count = 1** per link, or **link delay / administrative cost**, expressed in **milliseconds (ms)** or **hop units**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — The "Why" Behind Every Step

Each router $x$ in the network stores a row vector $D_x = [D_x(y) \text{ for every destination } y]$, and a corresponding next-hop vector $NH_x$. The algorithm executes the following loop indefinitely:

- **Step 1 — Wait for Trigger:** A router either receives a distance-vector update from a neighbour, detects a link-cost change, or experiences a periodic timer expiry.
- **Step 2 — Recompute (Bellman–Ford Relaxation):** For every destination $y$ and every neighbour $v$, the router evaluates the candidate cost $c(x,v) + D_v(y)$ and compares it with its current $D_x(y)$.
- **Step 3 — Update or Stay:** If a strictly smaller candidate is found, $D_x(y)$ is replaced and $NH_x(y)$ is set to $v$. Otherwise, no change.
- **Step 4 — Announce:** The router advertises its (possibly new) vector to all neighbours.
- **Step 5 — Repeat:** Each neighbouring router treats the announcement as a new trigger and re-enters Step 1.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this converges:** Because link costs are non-negative integers (or finite non-negative reals) and the network is finite, the longest possible simple path has at most $N-1$ edges (where $N$ is the number of nodes). Each iteration can only *decrease* a distance estimate — never increase it — and there are only finitely many such decreases. Hence, the process is **monotonically non-increasing** and is bounded below by **0**, guaranteeing termination in at most $(N-1)$ iterations.

---

## 2.2 The Bellman–Ford Equation (Expanded)

For a node $x$ with neighbour set $N(x) = \{v_1, v_2, \ldots, v_k\}$ and destination $y$:

$$D_x(y) = \min \{ c(x,v_1) + D_{v_1}(y),\; c(x,v_2) + D_{v_2}(y),\; \ldots,\; c(x,v_k) + D_{v_k}(y) \}$$

**Boundary conditions** (KTU frequently tests these):

- $D_x(x) = 0$ for every node $x$ (distance to self is always **zero**).
- $D_x(y) = \infty$ initially if $y$ is unreachable or unknown.
- $c(x,v) = \infty$ if no direct link exists between $x$ and $v$.

**Special routing case (intra-domain hop-count metric):**

$$D_x(y) = \min_{v \in N(x)} \left\{ \text{cost}(x \to v) + D_v(y) \right\}$$

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Formula Reference Table — memorize before any KTU ESE attempt on DV.**

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Conditions / Units | Practical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Bellman–Ford core equation | $D_x(y) = \min_v \{\, c(x,v) + D_v(y) \,\}$ | $v \in N(x)$; cost $\geq 0$ | Per-iteration router recomputation |
| 2 | Self-distance boundary | $D_x(x) = 0$ | Always | Initial table seeding |
| 3 | Unknown initial cost | $D_x(y) = \infty$ | Before first update | Marks unknown destinations |
| 4 | Maximum iterations to converge | $N - 1$ | $N$ = number of nodes | Convergence time bound |
| 5 | Good-news propagation speed | $h$ iterations | $h$ = hop count of best path | Time taken to detect improvement |
| 6 | Bad-news propagation speed (basic DV) | $h \times \text{iter}$ up to $N-1$ | Path length $h$ | Count-to-infinity timing |
| 7 | Link-cost change detection | $\Delta c \neq 0 \Rightarrow \text{trigger}$ | Continuous / discrete | Reactivity to topology events |
| 8 | RIP update interval | $\mathbf{30 \; s}$ | Default | Real protocol timing |
| 9 | RIP max hop count | $\mathbf{15}$ | Topology limit | Anti-count-to-infinity clamp |
| 10 | Distance-vector message size | $O(\vert V \vert)$ entries | $\vert V \vert$ destinations per neighbour | Bandwidth scaling |

**Real-world engineering utility:**
- **Internet Service Providers (ISPs)** historically used DV-based protocols (RIP, IGRP) inside small autonomous systems where simplicity outweighed convergence speed.
- **Military / tactical mesh networks** still use DV variants (e.g., B.A.T.M.A.N.-adv) for their self-healing, distributed nature.
- **Embedded / IoT routing** (e.g., RPL for Low-Power and Lossy Networks in IEEE 802.15.4) is essentially a DV descendant, tuned for energy-constrained nodes.

---

## 2.4 Distinguishing DV from Link-State (LS) — KTU's Favourite Comparison

| Property | Distance-Vector | Link-State |
|---|---|---|
| Knowledge scope | Local — only neighbours | Global — full topology map |
| Information exchanged | Whole distance vector | Only link-cost changes (LSAs) |
| Algorithm basis | Bellman–Ford | Dijkstra |
| Convergence speed | Slow (count-to-infinity risk) | Fast (after LSP flooding) |
| Message size | $O(\vert V \vert)$ per update | $O(\vert E \vert)$ per LSA |
| Memory requirement | Low per node | High (full graph kept) |
| Robustness | Lower — neighbour trust | Higher — every node computes independently |
| Example protocols | **RIP, IGRP, RPL** | **OSPF, IS-IS** |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Walkthrough

## 3.1 Canonical KTU Textbook Topology (3-Node & 5-Node Worked Examples)

The KTU examination board frequently uses a small network with **three to five nodes** to test the iterative update of distance vectors. We will work through both an abstract derivation and a numerical worked example.

### 3.1.1 Abstract Derivation of the Update Rule

Suppose node $x$ has just received a fresh distance-vector advertisement from neighbour $v$, announcing $D_v(y)$ for every destination $y$.

The new candidate cost for $x \to y$ via $v$ is:

$$\text{Candidate}_v(y) \;=\; c(x,v) \;+\; D_v(y)$$

Compare with the existing best:

$$D_x^{\text{new}}(y) \;=\; \min \left\{ D_x^{\text{old}}(y),\; \text{Candidate}_v(y) \right\}$$

The corresponding next-hop:

$$NH_x^{\text{new}}(y) \;=\;
\begin{cases}
v & \text{if } \text{Candidate}_v(y) < D_x^{\text{old}}(y) \\
NH_x^{\text{old}}(y) & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}$$

Because $c(x,v) \geq 0$, the candidate is monotonically non-decreasing with respect to the neighbour's table, which is what makes DV's iterative relaxation provably convergent.

---

### 3.1.2 Numerical Worked Example — Five-Node Topology

Consider the topology below (link cost = hop count, i.e., $\mathbf{1}$ per edge):

| Direct Link | Cost |
|---|---|
| u — v | **1** |
| v — x | **1** |
| u — x | **2** |
| u — w | **5** |
| x — w | **3** |
| w — y | **1** |
| x — y | **2** |

All cost values are in **hop units**.

#### Iteration 0 — Initial Tables

Each node knows its own distance (**0**), and has no other information yet.

$$D_u^{(0)} = [0,\; \infty,\; \infty,\; \infty,\; \infty] \quad \text{order: } (u, v, w, x, y)$$
$$D_v^{(0)} = [\infty,\; 0,\; \infty,\; \infty,\; \infty]$$
$$D_w^{(0)} = [\infty,\; \infty,\; 0,\; \infty,\; \infty]$$
$$D_x^{(0)} = [\infty,\; \infty,\; \infty,\; 0,\; \infty]$$
$$D_y^{(0)} = [\infty,\; \infty,\; \infty,\; \infty,\; 0]$$

#### Iteration 1 — First Round of Advertisements

- $u$ advertises to $v$, $x$, $w$.
- $v$ advertises to $u$, $x$.
- $x$ advertises to $u$, $v$, $w$, $y$.
- $w$ advertises to $u$, $x$, $y$.
- $y$ advertises to $w$, $x$.

**Recomputing $u$'s table:**

$$D_u^{(1)}(v) = \min \{ c(u,v) + D_v^{(0)}(v),\; c(u,x) + D_x^{(0)}(v),\; c(u,w) + D_w^{(0)}(v) \}$$

$$D_u^{(1)}(v) = \min \{ 1 + 0,\; 2 + \infty,\; 5 + \infty \} = \min \{1,\; \infty,\; \infty\} = 1$$

$$D_u^{(1)}(x) = \min \{ 1 + D_v^{(0)}(x),\; 2 + 0,\; 5 + D_w^{(0)}(x) \} = \min \{1 + \infty,\; 2,\; 5 + \infty\} = 2$$

$$D_u^{(1)}(w) = \min \{ 1 + D_v^{(0)}(w),\; 2 + D_x^{(0)}(w),\; 5 + 0 \} = \min \{\infty,\; \infty,\; 5\} = 5$$

$$D_u^{(1)}(y) = \min \{ 1 + D_v^{(0)}(y),\; 2 + D_x^{(0)}(y),\; 5 + D_w^{(0)}(y) \} = \min \{\infty,\; \infty,\; \infty\} = \infty$$

After Iteration 1:

$$D_u^{(1)} = [0,\; 1,\; 5,\; 2,\; \infty]$$

By symmetric computation:

$$D_v^{(1)} = [1,\; 0,\; \infty,\; 1,\; \infty]$$
$$D_w^{(1)} = [5,\; \infty,\; 0,\; 3,\; 1]$$
$$D_x^{(1)} = [2,\; 1,\; 3,\; 0,\; 2]$$
$$D_y^{(1)} = [\infty,\; \infty,\; 1,\; 2,\; 0]$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** Already the *direct* 1-hop links are discovered, and the 2-hop link $u \to x$ is correctly set to **2** at node $u$.

#### Iteration 2 — Second Round

Now nodes use Iteration-1 tables to refine. Focus on $u \to y$:

$$D_u^{(2)}(y) = \min \{ c(u,v) + D_v^{(1)}(y),\; c(u,x) + D_x^{(1)}(y),\; c(u,w) + D_w^{(1)}(y) \}$$

$$D_u^{(2)}(y) = \min \{ 1 + \infty,\; 2 + 2,\; 5 + 1 \} = \min \{\infty,\; 4,\; 6\} = 4$$

So $u$ now knows that the path $u \to x \to y$ yields a cost of **4**. Continuing this logic across all destinations and nodes, we arrive at the converged tables after at most $N - 1 = 4$ iterations.

#### Final Converged State

$$D_u^{\text{final}} = [0,\; 1,\; 4,\; 2,\; 4] \quad \text{via: } (-,\; v,\; x,\; x,\; x)$$
$$D_v^{\text{final}} = [1,\; 0,\; 4,\; 1,\; 3] \quad \text{via: } (u,\; -,\; x,\; x,\; x)$$
$$D_w^{\text{final}} = [4,\; 4,\; 0,\; 3,\; 1] \quad \text{via: } (x,\; x,\; -,\; x,\; y)$$
$$D_x^{\text{final}} = [2,\; 1,\; 3,\; 0,\; 2] \quad \text{via: } (u,\; v,\; w,\; -,\; y)$$
$$D_y^{\text{final}} = [4,\; 3,\; 1,\; 2,\; 0] \quad \text{via: } (x,\; x,\; w,\; x,\; -)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification using Dijkstra (single-source from $u$):** The shortest paths from $u$ are $u$ (0), $u\to v$ (1), $u\to x \to w$ (4), $u\to x$ (2), $u\to x \to y$ (4). The DV result $D_u^{\text{final}} = [0, 1, 4, 2, 4]$ matches exactly.

---

## 3.2 Mathematical Convergence Proof (Sketch)

Define $d_x^{(k)}(y)$ as node $x$'s estimate of distance to $y$ after $k$ iterations. We claim:

$$d_x^{(k)}(y) \;\leq\; d_x^{(k+1)}(y)$$

is **not** always true; instead the correct invariant is:

$$\text{(i)}\; d_x^{(0)}(y) = \infty \text{ if } y \neq x,\; 0 \text{ otherwise}$$
$$\text{(ii)}\; d_x^{(k+1)}(y) = \min_v \{ c(x,v) + d_v^{(k)}(y) \}$$

By induction on $k$, the estimate after $k$ iterations equals the length of the shortest path from $x$ to $y$ constrained to use at most $k$ links. Since any simple path in a graph of $N$ nodes has at most $N-1$ edges:

$$d_x^{(k)}(y) = \delta(x,y) \quad \text{for all } k \geq N-1$$

where $\delta(x,y)$ is the true shortest-path cost. This proves both termination (after $N-1$ iterations) and correctness of the final tables.

---

## 3.3 The Count-to-Infinity Problem (Analytical Treatment)

When a link cost *increases* (or a link fails), bad news propagates slowly through DV. The classic KTU illustration uses a 3-node line: $x \to y \to z$, with costs initially $c(x,y) = 4$, $c(y,z) = 1$, and destination $z$.

Converged initial state (hop count):

$$D_y(z) = 1,\quad D_x(z) = 5\;\text{(via } y\text{)}$$

Now suppose the link $y \to z$ fails. Before $y$ can detect this, it may still believe $D_y(z) = 1$ for one round. Meanwhile, if $z$ advertises $D_z(z) = 0$ (it still exists), and $y$ uses $z$ as a *neighbour*, it might think $z$ is reachable via $x$ at cost $c(y,z) + D_z(z) = 1 + 0 = 1$ — except $z$ is not a neighbour in this topology. The bad-news cycle proceeds until $D_x(z)$ *increments by 1* per iteration until it reaches $\infty$. With $\mathbf{N}$ nodes along the line, this takes up to $\mathbf{N - 1}$ rounds — the **count-to-infinity pathology**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mitigation strategies taught at KTU level:**
> - **Split Horizon:** Never advertise a route back to the neighbour from which you learned it.
> - **Poisoned Reverse:** Advertise that route back with cost **$\infty$** instead of just omitting it.
> - **Hold-Down Timers:** Refuse to accept updates about a recently-failed route for a bounded period.
> - **Triggered Updates:** Immediately propagate a cost increase rather than waiting for the next periodic tick.
> - **Path-Vector Protocols (BGP):** Carry the full AS-path, eliminating the cycle entirely.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Distance-Vector Routing — Functional Block Architecture

The diagram below depicts the **per-node functional architecture** of the DV algorithm: which internal module performs which role, and how information flows between a node and its immediate neighbours.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Link Cost Table c x v] --> B1[Neighbour Interface RX]
    A2[Periodic Timer 30 s] --> B1
    A3[Topology Change Detector] --> B1
    B1 --> C1[D_x Table Read]
    B2[D_v Table Incoming From Neighbour v] --> C1
    C1 --> D1[Bellman Ford Engine Min over v of c plus Dv]
    D1 --> E1{New Cost Smaller than D_x old}
    E1 -- Yes --> F1[Update D_x y and Next Hop x to v]
    E1 -- No --> F2[Keep D_x y unchanged]
    F1 --> G1[Mark Table Dirty Flag]
    F2 --> G1
    G1 --> H1[D_x Table Write Back]
    H1 --> I1[Neighbour Interface TX Announce Vector]
    I1 --> J1[Neighbour v Receives Update]
    J1 --> B2
    I1 --> J2[Neighbour w Receives Update]
    J2 --> B2
```

**Reading guide:**
- The three inputs to the *Neighbour Interface RX* are: direct link costs, periodic-timer ticks, and link-failure events.
- The *Bellman–Ford Engine* is the pure computational core, fed both the local $D_x$ and the incoming $D_v$.
- The decision diamond **E1** is the *relaxation* step — only strictly smaller candidates trigger a table mutation.
- The TX path re-broadcasts the modified vector, which is the *advertisement* that restarts the loop in each neighbour.

---

## 4.2 Iteration Sequence Diagram — Three Nodes $u$, $v$, $w$

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    iter0["Iteration 0 Each node D x x equals 0 others equals infinity"]
    iter1["Iteration 1 Each node discovers 1 hop neighbours"]
    iter2["Iteration 2 Best 2 hop paths discovered"]
    iter3["Iteration 3 Best 3 hop paths discovered"]
    conv["Convergence No more table updates"]
    iter0 --> iter1 --> iter2 --> iter3 --> conv
```

In general, after exactly **$h$** iterations, every node knows the shortest path to destinations within **$h$ hops**. Convergence is therefore guaranteed in at most **$N-1$** iterations for a network of $N$ nodes.

---

## 4.3 Good-News vs. Bad-News Propagation — Side-by-Side Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph GN["Good News Propagation Fast"]
        G1[Link u to v becomes cost 1] --> G2[Iteration 1 v advertises 1]
        G2 --> G3[Iteration 2 u updates to 2 via v]
        G3 --> G4[Iteration 3 w updates to 3 via u]
    end
    subgraph BN["Bad News Propagation Slow Count to Infinity"]
        B1[Link v to w fails] --> B2[Iteration 1 v still believes old cost]
        B2 --> B3[Iteration 2 v increments estimate]
        B3 --> B4[Iteration 3 v increments again]
        B4 --> B5[Iteration k v finally reaches infinity]
    end
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Visual take-away for KTU:** Good news travels at the speed of the **path length in hops**, while bad news travels at the speed of **iteration count times hop length**, often spanning the entire diameter of the network. This is the single most-tested intuition question in KTU Part-A on DV.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** — *Define the Distance-Vector routing algorithm. State two key properties that distinguish it from Link-State routing.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 each for two properties):**

Distance-Vector routing is a decentralized shortest-path algorithm in which every router maintains a table of estimated distances to all destinations and updates that table using only the information advertised by its directly connected neighbours. The update is governed by the Bellman–Ford equation:

$$D_x(y) = \min_v \{ c(x,v) + D_v(y) \}$$

Two distinguishing properties:

1. **Local knowledge only** — each node bases decisions purely on neighbour advertisements, with no global topology map. (Link-State, by contrast, requires every node to hold a complete LSDB.)
2. **Whole-vector advertisement** — every update carries the entire distance vector, not just the link change. (Link-State advertises only modified LSAs.)

*(1 + 1 + 1 = 3 marks)*

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** — *What is the "count-to-infinity" problem in DV routing? Name one technique to mitigate it.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 for cause, 1 for example, 1 for mitigation):**

Count-to-infinity is a pathological behaviour in which, after a link-cost increase or failure, routers iteratively *increase* their distance estimates by small increments (often **1 per round**) until they realize the destination is truly unreachable. For a path of $h$ hops, the bad news can take up to $N-1$ iterations to propagate, during which incorrect routing loops can form.

**Mitigation example:** *Split Horizon with Poisoned Reverse* — a router advertises the cost of a route back to the neighbour from which it learned it as **$\infty$**, preventing the neighbour from choosing the failing path as a fallback.

*(1 + 1 + 1 = 3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module-Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part-B question carries **14 marks**, split typically as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Students answer **either** the entire Question A **or** the entire Question B. Both choices below are framed for the *same DV topic* with different sub-cognitive demands.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Computation-Heavy

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 3]** — *Consider the 4-node network shown below. Link costs (in hop units) are: $c(A,B) = 1$, $c(A,C) = 4$, $c(B,C) = 2$, $c(B,D) = 5$, $c(C,D) = 1$.*

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
*Using the Distance-Vector algorithm, compute the converged distance tables at all four nodes. Show at least two full iterations of the Bellman–Ford update at node A and node B.*

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
*If the link $B \to D$ fails, trace how node A's distance to D evolves across the next three iterations. Explain whether the network would ever converge to a finite cost for $A \to D$ in this topology, and justify.*

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — Solution:**

**Iteration 0** — Each node knows only its own distance:

$$D_A^{(0)} = [0,\; \infty,\; \infty,\; \infty] \quad \text{order: } (A,B,C,D)$$

**Iteration 1 — Node A receives from neighbours $B$ and $C$:**

- $D_B^{(0)}(B) = 0,\; D_B^{(0)}(C) = \infty,\; D_B^{(0)}(D) = \infty$
- $D_C^{(0)}(C) = 0,\; D_C^{(0)}(B) = \infty,\; D_C^{(0)}(D) = \infty$

Update at $A$:

$$D_A^{(1)}(B) = \min \{ c(A,B) + 0,\; c(A,C) + D_C^{(0)}(B) \} = \min\{1,\; 4 + \infty\} = 1 \quad \text{via } B$$

$$D_A^{(1)}(C) = \min \{ 1 + \infty,\; 4 + 0 \} = 4 \quad \text{via } C$$

$$D_A^{(1)}(D) = \min \{ 1 + \infty,\; 4 + 1 \} = 5 \quad \text{via } C$$

Result: $D_A^{(1)} = [0,\; 1,\; 4,\; 5]$.

**Iteration 2 — After symmetric updates from all nodes:**

- $B$'s update: $D_B^{(2)}(A) = 1$ via $A$, $D_B^{(2)}(C) = 2$ via $A$ or $C$ direct, $D_B^{(2)}(D) = 3$ via $C$.
- $A$'s update using $B$'s new vector: $D_A^{(2)}(D) = \min\{1 + 3,\; 4 + 1\} = \min\{4,\; 5\} = 4$ via $B$.

After convergence:

| Node | $D(\cdot, A)$ | $D(\cdot, B)$ | $D(\cdot, C)$ | $D(\cdot, D)$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **A** | 0 | 1 (via B) | 3 (via B) | **4 (via B)** |
| **B** | 1 (via A) | 0 | 2 (via C) | **3 (via C)** |
| **C** | 3 (via B) | 2 (via B) | 0 | 1 (via D) |
| **D** | 4 (via C) | 3 (via C) | 1 (via C) | 0 |

**Verification (Dijkstra from A):** $A\to B\to C\to D = 1+2+1 = 4$. **Matches.** *(7 marks: 2 for Iter-1, 2 for Iter-2, 3 for final tables and verification.)*

---

**Part (b) — Solution:**

After $B \to D$ fails:

- $D_B(D)$ must be recomputed. $B$ still receives $D_C(D) = 1$, so candidate via $C$ is $c(B,C) + 1 = 2 + 1 = 3$. So $D_B^{(1)}(D) = 3$ via $C$. (Cost rises from **3** correctly, no count-to-infinity yet — $C \to D$ still valid.)
- $D_A(D)$ now becomes $c(A,B) + D_B(D) = 1 + 3 = 4$ via $B$. **Same cost as before**, but path is now $A \to B \to C \to D$.

**Iteration 1 after failure:** $D_A(D) = 4$, $D_B(D) = 3$.

**Iteration 2:** No further change (steady state) because $B \to C \to D$ is still alive.

**Conclusion:** In this topology, the failure does **not** trigger count-to-infinity because an alternate valid path $A \to B \to C \to D$ exists with finite cost. The network converges immediately to the new shortest path.

> Count-to-infinity would occur only if *all* paths from $A$ to $D$ went through the failed link — a single alternate route is enough to break the loop. *(7 marks: 2 for recomputation, 3 for tracing, 2 for conclusion.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Theory-Heavy Alternative

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 3]** — *Answer the following sub-parts on the DV algorithm:*

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
*(i) State and explain the Bellman–Ford equation as used in the DV algorithm. Derive the per-iteration update rule for a node with $k$ neighbours.* **(4 marks)**
*(ii) Prove that the algorithm terminates in at most $N-1$ iterations for a network of $N$ nodes.* **(3 marks)**

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
*(i) Discuss the count-to-infinity problem in detail with a 3-node illustrative example.* **(4 marks)**
*(ii) Compare and contrast the Split Horizon and Poisoned Reverse techniques as solutions, with their limitations.* **(3 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — Solution:**

**(i) Bellman–Ford Equation** *(4 marks)*

The Bellman–Ford equation for DV expresses the shortest distance from node $x$ to destination $y$ as the minimum, over all neighbours $v$, of the cost to reach $v$ plus the shortest distance from $v$ to $y$:

$$D_x(y) = \min_{v \in N(x)} \{ c(x,v) + D_v(y) \}$$

**Derivation of per-iteration update rule:**

Let $D_x^{(k)}(y)$ denote node $x$'s estimate of distance to $y$ after $k$ iterations. The update rule is:

$$D_x^{(k+1)}(y) = \min_{v \in N(x)} \{ c(x,v) + D_v^{(k)}(y) \}$$

with the boundary conditions:

$$D_x^{(0)}(x) = 0, \quad D_x^{(0)}(y) = \infty \;\text{ for } y \neq x$$

For a node with $k$ neighbours $\{v_1, v_2, \ldots, v_k\}$, this expands to:

$$D_x^{(k+1)}(y) = \min \{ c(x,v_1) + D_{v_1}^{(k)}(y),\; c(x,v_2) + D_{v_2}^{(k)}(y),\; \ldots,\; c(x,v_k) + D_{v_k}^{(k)}(y) \}$$

**Why this works:** Each iteration restricts paths to those of length at most one more than the previous iteration. The minimum over neighbours aggregates the best known extensions.

---

**(ii) Termination proof** *(3 marks)*

**Claim:** After $N-1$ iterations, no $D_x(y)$ will change further.

**Proof sketch:**

- *Base case:* After 0 iterations, each node knows only paths of length **0** (i.e., itself), giving $D_x^{(0)}(y) = 0$ iff $y = x$.
- *Inductive step:* Assume $D_x^{(k)}(y)$ equals the cost of the shortest path from $x$ to $y$ that uses at most $k$ links. Then $D_x^{(k+1)}(y)$ extends each such path by exactly one extra hop, taking the minimum over all neighbour-extensions — hence equals the cost of the shortest path using at most $k+1$ links.
- *Termination bound:* Any simple path in a graph of $N$ nodes contains at most $N-1$ edges. Therefore, for all $k \geq N-1$, the shortest $k$-link path equals the true shortest path, and no further update can reduce any $D_x(y)$. The algorithm is thus guaranteed to terminate by iteration $N-1$. $\blacksquare$

---

**Part (b) — Solution:**

**(i) Count-to-infinity — 3-node illustration** *(4 marks)*

Consider $x \leftrightarrow y \leftrightarrow z$ in a line, with $c(x,y) = c(y,z) = 1$ and destination $z$. Initially:

$$D_y(z) = 1, \quad D_x(z) = 2\;\text{(via } y\text{)}$$

Suppose the link $y \to z$ fails. Before $y$ detects it, $y$ may have received $D_x(z) = 2$. Using $x$ as a neighbour, $y$ computes candidate $= c(y,x) + D_x(z) = 1 + 2 = 3$. So $D_y(z)$ "increments" to **3**.

Next round: $x$ recomputes via $y$, getting $1 + 3 = 4$. So $D_x(z) = 4$.

The estimates grow **1 per iteration** until they reach $\infty$, a phenomenon called count-to-infinity. During the slow climb, packets may loop between $x$ and $y$, increasing network load and degrading service.

---

**(ii) Split Horizon vs. Poisoned Reverse** *(3 marks)*

| Aspect | Split Horizon | Poisoned Reverse |
|---|---|---|
| Mechanism | *Omit* a route from advertisements back to its origin neighbour. | *Advertise* the route back with cost **$\infty$**. |
| Strength | Simple, low overhead. | Stronger guarantee that the neighbour will not use the failing path. |
| Weakness | Fails when **3 or more** routers form a loop (information must transitively return). | Also fails in loops of length $\geq 3$; still vulnerable to count-to-infinity on longer cycles. |
| KTU exam tip | "Never tell A about a route learned from A" | "Tell A the route is $\infty$" |

**Common limitation:** Both techniques solve only **2-node loops**. For longer cycles, **triggered updates** and **hold-down timers** are needed as additional defences. Path-vector protocols (BGP) sidestep the entire problem by carrying explicit path information.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students typically lose marks on DV questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the boundary condition** $D_x(x) = 0$. Examiners explicitly check this — missing it costs 1 mark.
> 2. **Showing only the final table without iterations.** A 7-mark question demands **at least two iterations** of visible computation. Jumping straight to the answer forfeits process marks.
> 3. **Mixing up next-hop and distance.** The table has *two* columns; examiners often verify the next-hop consistency by re-running Bellman–Ford — mismatch loses 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Ignoring $\infty$ for unreachable nodes.** Initial tables must show $\infty$ for non-self destinations; convergence tables must show **0** for self.
> 5. **Confusing DV with LS.** Using "floods LSAs to all nodes" when describing DV is a serious conceptual error costing 1–2 marks.
> 6. **Stating only the algorithm name as a mitigation.** The examiner wants the *specific technique* (split horizon, hold-down, poisoned reverse), not a vague "use timers."

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary — re-read this list 10 minutes before the KTU ESE:**

- **DV = decentralized + iterative + asynchronous + Bellman–Ford-based.**
- Update rule: $D_x(y) = \min_v \{ c(x,v) + D_v(y) \}$.
- Boundary conditions: $D_x(x) = 0$, $D_x(y) = \infty$ initially.
- **Convergence bound:** $N - 1$ iterations for $N$ nodes.
- Each iteration discovers shortest paths of one additional hop.
- Routers exchange vectors only with **direct neighbours**, never with everyone.
- **Good news** propagates in **$h$ iterations** where $h$ = hop count of new path.
- **Bad news** propagates in **up to $N - 1$ iterations** — count-to-infinity pathology.
- **Split Horizon:** don't advertise a route back to the source.
- **Poisoned Reverse:** advertise that route as $\infty$ to the source.
- **Hold-Down Timers:** delay acceptance of route updates after a failure.
- **Triggered Updates:** send updates immediately, not waiting for the 30 s timer.
- **RIP** is the canonical real-world DV protocol: updates every **30 s**, max hops **15**, hop count as metric.
- **Link-State vs. DV:** LS uses global topology + Dijkstra; DV uses local info + Bellman–Ford.
- **Message size in DV:** $O(\vert V \vert)$ entries per advertisement, $O(\vert E \vert)$ for the whole update cycle.
- **Robustness caveat:** DV trusts neighbour tables blindly; a malicious neighbour can poison routes (hence secured variants like RIPng with authentication).
- **Time complexity** per iteration per node: $O(k \cdot \vert V \vert)$ where $k = \vert N(x) \vert$.
- **Path-Vector (BGP)** is a DV descendant that carries the full AS-path, eliminating count-to-infinity.
- **RPL** in IoT is a modern DV variant using rank instead of hop count.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hierarchical Routing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT631 - NETWORK & DATA SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Hierarchical Routing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Hierarchical Routing** is a scalable routing architecture in which the entire network (or internetwork) is partitioned into a logical multi-level hierarchy of **routing domains**, **clusters**, **regions**, or **areas**, and routers are organized into a corresponding hierarchy of responsibility. Instead of every router maintaining a complete topological view of the entire network, a router at a given level of the hierarchy maintains detailed routing information only for its own local group and aggregated (summary) routing information for all other groups above it.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** Hierarchical Routing is a routing strategy in which routers are organized into a multi-level tree-like structure such that each router knows the complete topology of its own cluster and the direction (gateway) to reach remote clusters, but does **not** know the internal topology of those remote clusters.

The concept was formally introduced by **Kleinrock and Kamoun (1977)** as a solution to the "network explosion" problem — the exponential growth of routing table size and routing traffic as the number of nodes $N$ increases in *flat* (non-hierarchical) routing schemes.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The Postal System

Imagine you want to send a letter to someone in **Kerala, India** from your home in **Kochi**.

- You do **not** need to know the **exact house number, street, and lane** of the destination in Trivandrum. You only need to know: "Write **Trivandrum → Kerala → India** on the envelope and drop it in the local postbox."
- The local postman knows every street in Kochi (his **local cluster**).
- The Trivandrum post office knows every street in Trivandrum (its **local cluster**).
- A central sorting hub in Delhi knows the direction to reach each **state** but not every street inside the state.

This is **exactly** how hierarchical routing works:

- **Cluster** = Local post office
- **Gateway router** = The mail truck that takes letters out of the cluster
- **Summary route** = "Direction to Trivandrum"
- **Full route** = "Exact house number inside Trivandrum"

## 1.3 Why Was Hierarchical Routing Invented? — The Scalability Crisis

In a **flat routing** scheme, every router must maintain a routing table with **one entry per destination**. If the network has $N$ nodes, every router has $N$ entries, and total memory used across the network is $N \times N = N^2$.

- For $N = 1000$ nodes → $10^6$ entries across the network.
- For $N = 1,000,000$ nodes (modern Internet) → $10^{12}$ entries — completely infeasible.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Hierarchical routing reduces the routing table size from **$O(N)$** (linear) to roughly **$O(\log N)$** or **$O(\sqrt{N})$** depending on the number of hierarchy levels — a massive scalability gain.

## 1.4 Standard Terminology Used by KTU Examiners

| Term | Meaning |
|------|---------|
| **Cluster / Region / Area** | A group of routers that share full topological knowledge among themselves |
| **Gateway Router** | A router that belongs to more than one cluster and summarises routes between them |
| **Intra-cluster Routing** | Routing performed *inside* a single cluster (e.g., OSPF intra-area) |
| **Inter-cluster Routing** | Routing performed *between* clusters (e.g., OSPF inter-area) |
| **L-level Hierarchy** | Hierarchy with $L$ layers; at level $L$ the entire network is one summary |
| **Aggregation / Summarization** | Combining many specific routes into one summary route advertised upward |

## 1.5 Hierarchy vs. Real-World Protocol Mapping

| KTU Concept | Real Protocol Implementation |
|-------------|----------------------------|
| 2-level hierarchy | **OSPF Areas** (intra-area + inter-area), **IS-IS Levels** |
| 3-level hierarchy | **BGP** (Autonomous System → Region → Router) |
| Global Internet | **Intra-AS** (OSPF, IS-IS) + **Inter-AS** (BGP) = effectively 2-level |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two-Level Hierarchical Routing Model (Kamoun-Kleinrock)

In a **2-level hierarchy**, the $N$ routers of the network are grouped into $K$ clusters, where each cluster contains approximately $N/K$ routers.

### Step-by-Step Logic of the Model

1. **Step 1 — Cluster Formation:** Divide the $N$ routers into $K$ balanced clusters such that every cluster has approximately $N/K$ routers.
2. **Step 2 — Intra-Cluster Knowledge:** Every router maintains a complete routing table of size $(N/K - 1)$ for all *other* routers within its own cluster.
3. **Step 3 — Inter-Cluster Knowledge:** Every router maintains **only one entry per remote cluster** (treated as a single logical destination). There are $K - 1$ other clusters.
4. **Step 4 — Total Table Size:** A router's routing table contains entries for intra-cluster destinations + one entry per remote cluster gateway.

## 2.2 Derivation of the Optimal Number of Clusters $K^*$

### Step A — Total Table Size Function

Let $T$ be the total number of entries in a single router's routing table:

$$
T = \underbrace{\left(\frac{N}{K} - 1\right)}_{\text{intra-cluster routes}} + \underbrace{(K - 1)}_{\text{remote cluster gateway routes}}
$$

For large $N$ and $K$, the "$-1$" terms are negligible, so we approximate:

$$
T \approx \frac{N}{K} + K
$$

### Step B — Optimization Using Calculus

To find the optimal $K$ that **minimizes** $T$, take the derivative with respect to $K$ and set it to zero:

$$
\frac{dT}{dK} = -\frac{N}{K^2} + 1 = 0
$$

$$
1 = \frac{N}{K^2} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad K^2 = N \quad \Longrightarrow \quad K^{*} = \sqrt{N}
$$

### Step C — Minimum Possible Table Size

Substitute $K^* = \sqrt{N}$ back into the table-size formula:

$$
T_{\min} = \frac{N}{\sqrt{N}} + \sqrt{N} = 2\sqrt{N}
$$

### Step D — Interpretation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> With optimal 2-level hierarchical routing, each router needs only **$2\sqrt{N}$** entries instead of $N$ entries (flat routing). For $N = 10^6$ nodes, this is **2000 entries vs 1,000,000 entries** — a **500×** reduction.

## 2.3 The L-Level Hierarchy (Generalization)

For an $L$-level hierarchy with $N$ total routers:

### Step-by-Step Design Procedure

1. **Step 1:** Choose $L$ levels of hierarchy.
2. **Step 2:** Choose the cluster sizes $N_1, N_2, N_3, \ldots, N_L$ such that $N_1 \cdot N_2 \cdot N_3 \cdots N_L = N$.
3. **Step 3:** At the **lowest level (level 1)**, the router knows all $N_1$ routers in its local group.
4. **Step 4:** At the next level up, it knows $N_2$ entries summarising the next larger region.
5. **Step 5:** Continue up to level $L$, where it knows $N_L$ entries (often $N_L = N$, i.e., the whole network).

### Kamoun-Kleinrock Optimal Design (1977)

For the **optimal** multi-level hierarchy, the cluster sizes form a geometric progression:

$$
N_i = e^{i+1} \quad \text{for } i = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, L-1
$$

The **optimal number of levels** is:

$$
L^{*} = \lceil \ln N \rceil
$$

where $\ln$ denotes the natural logarithm. Equivalently, $L^* = \log_e N$.

### Resulting Optimal Table Size

For the optimal L-level design, the table size at any single router is approximately:

$$
T_{\text{opt}} \approx 2 \cdot (e - 1) \cdot L^{*} \approx 3.44 \cdot \lceil \ln N \rceil
$$

This is a **logarithmic** function of $N$, giving massive scalability.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** Memorise every formula in this table — Kamoun-Kleinrock problems appear frequently in KTU Module 3 questions.

| # | Concept | Formula | Description |
|---|---------|---------|-------------|
| 1 | Flat routing table size | $T_{\text{flat}} = N$ | One entry per destination |
| 2 | 2-level table size | $T = \dfrac{N}{K} + K$ | $K$ clusters of size $N/K$ |
| 3 | Optimal number of clusters (2-level) | $K^{*} = \sqrt{N}$ | Derivative of $T$ w.r.t. $K$ = 0 |
| 4 | Minimum table size (2-level) | $T_{\min} = 2\sqrt{N}$ | Substitute $K^*$ back |
| 5 | Memory saving ratio | $\dfrac{T_{\text{flat}}}{T_{\text{hier}}} = \dfrac{N}{2\sqrt{N}} = \dfrac{\sqrt{N}}{2}$ | |
| 6 | Total network memory (flat) | $M_{\text{flat}} = N^2$ entries | $N$ routers × $N$ entries |
| 7 | Total network memory (2-level) | $M_{\text{hier}} = N \cdot 2\sqrt{N} = 2N^{3/2}$ | |
| 8 | Optimal levels (L-level) | $L^{*} = \lceil \ln N \rceil$ | Natural log of $N$ |
| 9 | Cluster size at level $i$ | $N_i = e^{i+1}$ | Geometric progression |
| 10 | Optimal L-level table size | $T_{\text{opt}} \approx 2(e-1)L^{*}$ | Logarithmic in $N$ |
| 11 | Path stretch factor | $\rho = \dfrac{\text{Hierarchical path length}}{\text{Optimal (shortest) path length}}$ | Usually $1 < \rho \leq 2$ |
| 12 | Sub-optimality of hierarchical paths | $L_{\text{hier}} \leq 2 \cdot L_{\text{opt}} + 1$ | Worst-case bound (Kleinrock-Kamoun) |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** In your answer script, write absolute value as $\vert x \vert$ or $\mid x \mid$ — **never** use the vertical bar `|` inside a sentence that could be confused with markdown table syntax.

## 2.5 Engineering Utility in the Real World

| Field | Application |
|-------|-------------|
| **Internet Backbone** | **BGP** uses inter-domain hierarchy (Autonomous Systems) |
| **Enterprise Networks** | **OSPF Areas** (Area 0 backbone + non-backbone areas) |
| **Mobile Ad-hoc Networks (MANET)** | Zone-based routing protocols like **ZRP** (Zone Routing Protocol) |
| **Sensor Networks (IoT)** | LEACH, PEGASIS — cluster-head election then aggregated routing |
| **Telecom (5G/6G)** | Hierarchical SDN controllers (Local → Regional → Global) |
| **Data Center Networking** | Fat-tree, Clos networks use hierarchical addressing |

## 2.6 Trade-offs of Hierarchical Routing

### Advantages
1. **Dramatic reduction in table size** — from $O(N)$ to $O(\sqrt{N})$ or $O(\log N)$.
2. **Reduced routing update traffic** — only summary LSAs are sent between clusters.
3. **Faster convergence** — changes inside a cluster are hidden from the rest of the network.
4. **Administrative isolation** — each cluster can run different internal routing policies.

### Disadvantages
1. **Sub-optimal paths** — packets may travel longer routes because the gateway summary hides "shortcuts" between clusters.
2. **Path stretch** — the worst-case path length is approximately $2 \times$ the optimal path (a theorem by Kleinrock-Kamoun).
3. **Single point of failure** — if a gateway router crashes, the cluster becomes isolated.
4. **Configuration complexity** — addressing plans must be designed hierarchically (e.g., CIDR blocks in BGP).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Path Sub-Optimality Bound:** In an L-level hierarchy, if $L_{\text{opt}}$ is the optimal shortest path length, then the path chosen by hierarchical routing satisfies: $L_{\text{hierarchical}} \leq 2 \cdot L_{\text{opt}} + 1$.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Full Derivation: Optimal Cluster Count for 2-Level Hierarchy

### Starting Premise

We have a network of $N$ routers. We divide them into $K$ clusters, each containing $N/K$ routers (assuming $N$ is divisible by $K$).

### Line 1 — Routing Table Contents

A router needs to forward packets to:
- Every other router **inside its own cluster** (intra-cluster).
- The **gateway** of every other cluster (inter-cluster).

Therefore, the number of entries in one router's table is:

$$
T(K) = \underbrace{\left( \frac{N}{K} - 1 \right)}_{\text{Other routers in same cluster}} + \underbrace{(K - 1)}_{\text{Gateways to other clusters}}
$$

### Line 2 — Simplification

Since $N$ and $K$ are large (we are dealing with real networks), the $-1$ terms are negligible compared to the main terms. We approximate:

$$
T(K) \approx \frac{N}{K} + K
$$

### Line 3 — Differentiation to Find the Minimum

Differentiate $T(K)$ with respect to $K$:

$$
\frac{dT}{dK} = \frac{d}{dK}\left( \frac{N}{K} \right) + \frac{d}{dK}(K)
$$

$$
\frac{dT}{dK} = -\frac{N}{K^2} + 1
$$

### Line 4 — Setting the Derivative to Zero

For the optimal (minimum) value, we set $\dfrac{dT}{dK} = 0$:

$$
-\frac{N}{K^2} + 1 = 0
$$

### Line 5 — Solving for $K$

$$
1 = \frac{N}{K^2}
$$

$$
K^2 = N
$$

$$
K = \pm \sqrt{N}
$$

Since $K$ must be a positive integer, we take:

$$
K^{*} = \sqrt{N}
$$

### Line 6 — Second Derivative Test (Confirming Minimum)

$$
\frac{d^2 T}{dK^2} = \frac{2N}{K^3} > 0 \quad \text{for } K > 0
$$

The second derivative is positive, confirming a **minimum** at $K = K^*$.

### Line 7 — Substituting Back to Get Minimum Table Size

$$
T_{\min} = \frac{N}{K^{*}} + K^{*}
$$

$$
T_{\min} = \frac{N}{\sqrt{N}} + \sqrt{N}
$$

$$
T_{\min} = \sqrt{N} + \sqrt{N}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{\min} = 2\sqrt{N}}
$$

### Line 8 — Numerical Example

Suppose $N = 10000$ routers.

$$
K^{*} = \sqrt{10000} = 100 \text{ clusters}
$$

$$
T_{\min} = 2\sqrt{10000} = 2 \times 100 = 200 \text{ entries}
$$

Compare with flat routing:

$$
T_{\text{flat}} = 10000 \text{ entries}
$$

Memory saved per router:

$$
\frac{10000 - 200}{10000} = 98\%
$$

## 3.2 Full Derivation: L-Level Optimal Hierarchy

### Starting Premise

Suppose we design an $L$-level hierarchy with cluster sizes $N_1, N_2, N_3, \ldots, N_L$, where:

$$
N_1 \cdot N_2 \cdot N_3 \cdots N_L = N
$$

### Line 1 — Total Table Size for L Levels

At the lowest level, a router has $N_1$ entries for its own group. At each higher level, it has additional summary entries.

$$
T = (N_1 - 1) + (N_2 - 1) + (N_3 - 1) + \cdots + (N_L - 1)
$$

For large clusters, neglecting the $-1$ terms:

$$
T \approx N_1 + N_2 + N_3 + \cdots + N_L
$$

### Line 2 — Constraint Equation

$$
N_1 \cdot N_2 \cdot N_3 \cdots N_L = N
$$

### Line 3 — Geometric-Progress Optimisation

We want to **minimise** $T = \sum_{i=1}^{L} N_i$ subject to $\prod_{i=1}^{L} N_i = N$.

By the **AM-GM inequality**:

$$
\frac{N_1 + N_2 + \cdots + N_L}{L} \geq (N_1 \cdot N_2 \cdots N_L)^{1/L} = N^{1/L}
$$

$$
T \geq L \cdot N^{1/L}
$$

### Line 4 — Equality Condition

AM-GM holds with equality **if and only if** all the $N_i$ are equal. However, the **Kamoun-Kleinrock optimal design** chooses:

$$
N_i = e^{i+1}
$$

This is a geometric progression that minimises the **maximum** table size at any single router (rather than the average), which is more realistic for engineering.

### Line 5 — Optimal Number of Levels

With the constraint $N_1 \cdot N_2 \cdots N_L = N$ and the chosen geometric progression:

$$
\prod_{i=0}^{L-1} e^{i+1} = e^{\sum_{i=0}^{L-1}(i+1)} = e^{L(L+1)/2}
$$

Setting $e^{L(L+1)/2} = N$ and solving for $L$:

$$
L(L+1)/2 = \ln N
$$

For large $L$:

$$
L^2 / 2 \approx \ln N
$$

$$
\boxed{L^{*} = \sqrt{2 \ln N}}
$$

(Kamoun-Kleinrock's exact asymptotic result; many textbooks approximate this as $L^* = \lceil \ln N \rceil$.)

### Line 6 — Resulting Table Size

With $N_i = e^{i+1}$ and $L = L^* = \sqrt{2 \ln N}$:

$$
T_{\text{opt}} = \sum_{i=0}^{L-1} e^{i+1} = e + e^2 + \cdots + e^{L}
$$

This is a geometric series with first term $e$, ratio $e$, and $L$ terms:

$$
T_{\text{opt}} = e \cdot \frac{e^L - 1}{e - 1} = \frac{e(e^L - 1)}{e - 1}
$$

Since $e^L = e^{\sqrt{2 \ln N}} \approx N^{\sqrt{2/\ln N}}$, for large $N$, $T_{\text{opt}}$ grows **very slowly** — approximately logarithmic in $N$.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam-Style)

> **Problem:** A network has $N = 900$ routers. Design an optimal 2-level hierarchical routing scheme.

**Step 1 — Compute Optimal Number of Clusters:**

$$
K^{*} = \sqrt{N} = \sqrt{900} = 30 \text{ clusters}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Routers per Cluster:**

$$
\frac{N}{K^{*}} = \frac{900}{30} = 30 \text{ routers per cluster}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Routing Table Size per Router:**

$$
T_{\min} = \frac{N}{K^{*}} + K^{*} = 30 + 30 = 60 \text{ entries}
$$

**Step 4 — Compare with Flat Routing:**

$$
T_{\text{flat}} = N = 900 \text{ entries}
$$

**Step 5 — Memory Reduction:**

$$
\text{Reduction} = 1 - \frac{60}{900} = 1 - 0.0667 = 93.33\%
$$

## 3.4 Comparison Matrix: Flat vs. Hierarchical Routing

| Metric | Flat Routing | 2-Level Hierarchical | L-Level Hierarchical |
|--------|-------------|---------------------|---------------------|
| Table size per router | $N$ | $2\sqrt{N}$ | $O(\ln N)$ |
| Total network memory | $N^2$ | $2N^{3/2}$ | $O(N \ln N)$ |
| Convergence speed | Slow (entire network flooded) | Fast (intra-cluster only) | Fastest (level-by-level) |
| Path optimality | Optimal (shortest) | Up to 2× optimal | Up to 2× optimal |
| Routing update traffic | $O(N^2)$ LSAs | $O(N)$ per cluster | $O(N)$ per cluster |
| Configuration complexity | Simple | Moderate (needs addressing plan) | High |
| Scalability | Poor | Good | Excellent |

## 3.5 Why Hierarchical Routing Costs Path Optimality — Mathematical Proof Sketch

**Theorem (Kleinrock-Kamoun 1977):** *For an L-level hierarchy, the path length produced by hierarchical routing is at most $2 \cdot L_{\text{opt}} + 1$ where $L_{\text{opt}}$ is the true shortest path.*

### Proof Sketch (Line by Line)

1. Consider any source router $S$ in cluster $C_S$ and destination $D$ in cluster $C_D$.
2. The shortest path between $S$ and $D$ in the **flat** network is at most $L_{\text{opt}}$ hops.
3. This path must cross cluster boundaries a certain number of times.
4. When the path crosses a boundary, the **gateway** used is **fixed** for that source-destination pair (no choice — it is the summarised route).
5. Because the gateway is fixed and may not lie on the optimal path, the path is **detoured**.
6. The maximum detour occurs when the optimal path crosses through *interior* of many clusters; the hierarchical path is forced to detour to the *boundary gateways* of those clusters.
7. This detour adds at most $L_{\text{opt}} + 1$ extra hops, giving the bound.

$$
\boxed{L_{\text{hier}} \leq 2 \cdot L_{\text{opt}} + 1}
$$

This bound is **tight** — there exist worst-case topologies for which hierarchical routing produces exactly this sub-optimality.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Block Diagram: Two-Level Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef cluster fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#60a5fa,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef gw fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#fb923c,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef router fill:#064e3b,stroke:#34d399,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff
    classDef net fill:#312e81,stroke:#a78bfa,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff

    subgraph CL1["CLUSTER 1 — Region: North"]
        A1[Router A1]:::router
        A2[Router A2]:::router
        A3[Router A3]:::router
        GW1[Gateway Router G1]:::gw
        A1 --- A2
        A2 --- A3
        A3 --- GW1
    end

    subgraph CL2["CLUSTER 2 — Region: South"]
        B1[Router B1]:::router
        B2[Router B2]:::router
        B3[Router B3]:::router
        GW2[Gateway Router G2]:::gw
        B1 --- B2
        B2 --- B3
        B3 --- GW2
    end

    subgraph CL3["CLUSTER 3 — Region: East"]
        C1[Router C1]:::router
        C2[Router C2]:::router
        C3[Router C3]:::router
        GW3[Gateway Router G3]:::gw
        C1 --- C2
        C2 --- C3
        C3 --- GW3
    end

    GW1 <--> GW2
    GW2 <--> GW3
    GW1 <--> GW3
```

### How to Read This Diagram

- **Green boxes** (A1, A2, B1, B2…) are ordinary internal routers. They know every detail about their own cluster only.
- **Orange boxes** (G1, G2, G3) are **gateway routers** — they sit on the border of two or more clusters and summarise inter-cluster traffic.
- **Blue rounded boxes** are the clusters themselves.
- Notice the symmetry: gateways are fully meshed, but internal routers have **no** knowledge of foreign clusters.

## 4.2 Information-Scope Diagram (What Each Router Knows)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef know fill:#166534,stroke:#86efac,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef dont fill:#7f1d1d,stroke:#fca5a5,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff
    classDef router fill:#1e40af,stroke:#93c5fd,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff

    R1[Router A2 inside Cluster 1]:::router
    K1[Full topology of Cluster 1]:::know
    K2[Gateway G1 address only]:::know
    K3[Number of remote clusters: 2]:::know
    D1[Internal topology of Cluster 2]:::dont
    D2[Internal topology of Cluster 3]:::dont
    D3[Addresses of routers B1, C2 etc]:::dont

    R1 --> K1
    R1 --> K2
    R1 --> K3
    R1 --> D1
    R1 --> D2
    R1 --> D3
```

## 4.3 Block Architecture: Packet Forwarding Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    classDef input fill:#0c4a6e,stroke:#38bdf8,color:#ffffff
    classDef proc fill:#581c87,stroke:#c084fc,color:#ffffff
    classDef out fill:#831843,stroke:#fb7185,color:#ffffff

    PKT[Incoming IP Packet with Dest IP]:::input
    CHK1{Destination in same cluster?}:::proc
    LOOK1[Look up exact intra-cluster route]:::proc
    FWD1[Forward via intra-cluster table]:::out
    CHK2{Destination cluster known?}:::proc
    LOOK2[Look up gateway for that cluster]:::proc
    FWD2[Forward to Gateway Router]:::out
    DROP[Drop or send to default route]:::out

    PKT --> CHK1
    CHK1 -- Yes --> LOOK1 --> FWD1
    CHK1 -- No --> CHK2
    CHK2 -- Yes --> LOOK2 --> FWD2
    CHK2 -- No --> DROP
```

## 4.4 L-Level Hierarchy (Tree) Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef root fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#fb923c,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff
    classDef mid fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#60a5fa,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef leaf fill:#064e3b,stroke:#34d399,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff
    classDef router fill:#374151,stroke:#d1d5db,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff

    ROOT[LEVEL 3 — Global Summary]:::root
    M1[LEVEL 2 — Continent A]:::mid
    M2[LEVEL 2 — Continent B]:::mid
    C1[LEVEL 1 — Country X]:::mid
    C2[LEVEL 1 — Country Y]:::mid
    C3[LEVEL 1 — Country Z]:::mid
    C4[LEVEL 1 — Country W]:::mid
    R1[R1]:::router
    R2[R2]:::router
    R3[R3]:::router
    R4[R4]:::router
    R5[R5]:::router
    R6[R6]:::router

    ROOT --> M1
    ROOT --> M2
    M1 --> C1
    M1 --> C2
    M2 --> C3
    M2 --> C4
    C1 --> R1
    C1 --> R2
    C2 --> R3
    C3 --> R4
    C3 --> R5
    C4 --> R6
```

### Reading the Tree

- **Root (Level 3)** — the entire network seen as one logical destination. Used by global routers only.
- **Branches (Level 2)** — continents or major regions. Each level-2 router knows summary routes to every continent.
- **Sub-branches (Level 1)** — countries inside each continent. Each level-1 router knows its country's internal topology in full.
- **Leaves** — the actual end-routers sending/receiving user traffic.

### Mapping to KTU Concepts

| OSPF Concept | Hierarchical Routing Term |
|-------------|--------------------------|
| Area 0 backbone | Level-2 cluster (inter-cluster domain) |
| Area 1, Area 2 | Level-1 clusters |
| Area Border Router (ABR) | **Gateway router** |
| Type 1 LSA (Router LSA) | Intra-cluster advertisement |
| Type 3 LSA (Summary LSA) | Inter-cluster **summary** route |
| Intra-area route | $N/K$ entries per router |
| Inter-area route | One per area ($K$ entries) |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define hierarchical routing. Why is it preferred over flat routing in large networks?**

**Model Answer:**

Hierarchical routing is a routing strategy in which routers are organised into a tree-like structure of clusters or regions. Each router maintains complete topology information only for its own local cluster, and a single summary route for every other cluster via a **gateway router**.

It is preferred over flat routing in large networks for the following reasons:

1. **Reduction in routing table size:** In flat routing, each router has $O(N)$ entries. With optimal 2-level hierarchy, this drops to $O(\sqrt{N})$, and with $L$-level hierarchy, to $O(\ln N)$.
2. **Reduced routing overhead:** Link-state advertisements (LSAs) are confined within a cluster, drastically reducing the bandwidth consumed by routing updates.
3. **Faster convergence:** Topology changes inside a cluster do not propagate to the rest of the network, so convergence is faster.
4. **Administrative scalability:** Each cluster can be managed by a different administrative authority (e.g., OSPF Areas, BGP Autonomous Systems).

**Keywords for full marks:** scalability, aggregation, gateway, $O(\sqrt{N})$, $O(\ln N)$, summary route.

---

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is a gateway router? Explain its role in hierarchical routing with an example.**

**Model Answer:**

A **gateway router** is a router that belongs to two or more clusters in a hierarchical routing hierarchy. It is responsible for **summarising** the routing information of its own cluster and advertising a single aggregated route for that cluster to routers in other clusters.

**Example:** Consider a college network divided into three clusters — CSE Department, ECE Department, and Admin Block. The router **G1** at the boundary of the CSE cluster connects to the central backbone and acts as the gateway. Internal CSE routers (e.g., C1, C2, C3) advertise their routes to G1. G1 then advertises a **single summary route** "10.1.0.0/16" to all routers outside the CSE cluster, hiding the internal details of CSE.

**Keywords for full marks:** border router, summary advertisement, aggregation, inter-cluster traffic, encapsulation of topology.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 3]

**(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the concept of hierarchical routing. Differentiate between intra-cluster and inter-cluster routing. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A network consists of $N = 6400$ routers. Design an **optimal 2-level hierarchical routing** scheme. Compute (i) the optimal number of clusters, (ii) the size of each cluster, (iii) the minimum routing table size per router, and (iv) the percentage memory saved compared to flat routing. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a)

1. **Diagram** — Draw a 2-level hierarchy with 3 clusters, each containing 3–4 internal routers, and gateway routers connecting the clusters (similar to the diagram in Section 4.1 above).

2. **Intra-cluster routing:** Routers within a single cluster use detailed routing tables that include every other router in that cluster. They run a link-state protocol (e.g., OSPF) locally.

3. **Inter-cluster routing:** When the destination is in a different cluster, the source router does not know the exact path. It simply forwards the packet to the **gateway router** of its own cluster, which then forwards it through the inter-cluster backbone to the gateway of the destination cluster, and finally to the destination.

4. **Example:** Source = A2 in Cluster 1, Destination = B2 in Cluster 2. Path = A2 → G1 → G2 → B2 (only 4 hops, with no knowledge of internal topology of Cluster 2).

#### Model Solution for (b)

**(i) Optimal number of clusters:**

$$
K^{*} = \sqrt{N} = \sqrt{6400} = 80 \text{ clusters}
$$

**(ii) Size of each cluster:**

$$
\frac{N}{K^{*}} = \frac{6400}{80} = 80 \text{ routers per cluster}
$$

**(iii) Minimum routing table size per router:**

$$
T_{\min} = \frac{N}{K^{*}} + K^{*} = 80 + 80 = 160 \text{ entries}
$$

(Using the simplified Kamoun-Kleinrock formula $T_{\min} = 2\sqrt{N} = 2 \times 80 = 160$ — same answer.)

**(iv) Memory saved compared to flat routing:**

Flat routing table size: $T_{\text{flat}} = N = 6400$ entries.

$$
\text{Memory saved} = \frac{T_{\text{flat}} - T_{\min}}{T_{\text{flat}}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
= \frac{6400 - 160}{6400} \times 100\% = \frac{6240}{6400} \times 100\% = 97.5\%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students often forget to use the **simplified formula** $T_{\min} = 2\sqrt{N}$ after computing $K^*$, and they write $T = (N/K - 1) + (K - 1)$, which gives the **same answer** only if you remember the $-1$ terms. For $N = 6400$, both give 160. But for small $N$, the $-1$ terms matter. **Always state which formula you used.**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 3]

**(a)** Discuss the limitations of flat routing. Explain with a suitable example how hierarchical routing solves the scalability problem. Compare 2-level and L-level hierarchies. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a network with $N = 100{,}000$ routers, compute (i) the optimal number of clusters for 2-level hierarchy, (ii) the minimum table size per router, and (iii) the optimal number of levels $L^*$ for an L-level hierarchy. Comment on which scheme is more scalable. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Limitations of Flat Routing:**

1. **Routing table size grows linearly with $N$:** Every router needs an entry for every other router → $N$ entries per router, $N^2$ total network memory.
2. **Routing update traffic grows as $O(N^2)$:** When a link fails, the link-state advertisement (LSA) must be flooded to **all** $N$ routers in the network.
3. **Slow convergence:** With $N^2$ LSAs in the network, recomputing shortest paths (Dijkstra) is slow.
4. **No administrative boundaries:** Hard to manage in networks owned by multiple organisations.

**Example of Scalability Solution:**

The Internet uses a **2-level hierarchy**:
- **Level 1 (Intra-AS):** Inside each Autonomous System (AS), routers use OSPF or IS-IS to find optimal paths among a few thousand routers.
- **Level 2 (Inter-AS):** Between ASes, the Border Gateway Protocol (BGP) exchanges only **summary reachability information**, not full topology.

**Comparison of 2-level and L-level Hierarchies:**

| Aspect | 2-Level | L-Level |
|--------|---------|---------|
| Table size | $2\sqrt{N}$ | $O(\ln N)$ |
| Number of levels | 2 | $L^* = \lceil \ln N \rceil$ |
| Configuration | Simpler | More complex |
| Path optimality | Slightly sub-optimal | Same upper bound (≤ 2× optimal) |
| Scalability | Good | Excellent |

#### Model Solution for (b)

**(i) Optimal number of clusters (2-level):**

$$
K^{*} = \sqrt{N} = \sqrt{100000} = \sqrt{10^5} \approx 316.23
$$

Since $K$ must be an integer, $K^* = 316$ (or 317 for perfect divisibility).

**(ii) Minimum table size (2-level):**

$$
T_{\min} = 2\sqrt{N} = 2 \times 316.23 \approx 632.46
$$

Approximately **633 entries per router**.

**(iii) Optimal number of levels (L-level):**

$$
L^{*} = \lceil \ln N \rceil = \lceil \ln 100000 \rceil = \lceil 11.513 \rceil = 12 \text{ levels}
$$

**Comment on Scalability:**

For $N = 100{,}000$:

- 2-level scheme: ~633 entries per router.
- L-level scheme: approximately $2(e-1)L^* \approx 3.44 \times 12 \approx 41$ entries per router.

The **L-level hierarchy is far more scalable** — it reduces entries per router by an additional factor of **~15×** compared to the 2-level scheme, at the cost of more complex configuration.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** In (iii), students often confuse natural log ($\ln$) with $\log_{10}$. Remember: $\ln 100000 = 11.51$, but $\log_{10} 100000 = 5$. **The Kamoun-Kleinrock formula uses the natural logarithm.** A common 1-mark deduction occurs for this mistake.

---

## 5.3 Common KTU Mark-Loss Pitfalls (Examiner's Voice)

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Mistakes in Hierarchical Routing Answers:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the "+ K" term** — Students write $T = N/K$ only, forgetting the gateway entries. Always state the full formula $T = N/K + K$.
> 2. **Using $\log_{10}$ instead of $\ln$** — The Kamoun-Kleinrock formula $L^* = \ln N$ uses the **natural logarithm**. Base 10 gives a different (wrong) answer.
> 3. **Confusing clustering with subnetting** — Subnetting is **address aggregation**; clustering is **routing aggregation**. They are related but not identical.
> 4. **Not drawing the diagram in (a) parts** — Even in 14-mark questions, examiners expect a **neat labelled diagram** of the hierarchy for full marks. 2–3 marks are reserved for diagram quality.
> 5. **Skipping the optimisation calculus** — When asked for optimal $K$, students sometimes just state $K = \sqrt{N}$ without showing the differentiation step. Always show $\frac{dT}{dK} = 0$ for full marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### 🎯 High-Density Revision Checklist

- [ ] **Definition:** Hierarchical routing organises routers into a tree of clusters; each router knows its own cluster's topology in full and other clusters only via summary gateway routes.
- [ ] **Inventors:** **Kleinrock and Kamoun (1977)** — "Hierarchical Routing for Large Networks" — performance evaluation paper.
- [ ] **2-Level Table Size Formula:** $T = \dfrac{N}{K} + K$
- [ ] **Optimal Cluster Count (2-Level):** $K^{*} = \sqrt{N}$
- [ ] **Minimum Table Size (2-Level):** $T_{\min} = 2\sqrt{N}$
- [ ] **Optimal Levels (L-Level):** $L^{*} = \lceil \ln N \rceil$ (Kamoun-Kleinrock asymptotic: $L^* = \sqrt{2 \ln N}$)
- [ ] **Cluster Size at Level $i$:** $N_i = e^{i+1}$ (geometric progression)
- [ ] **Memory Complexity:** Flat = $O(N^2)$, 2-level = $O(N^{3/2})$, L-level = $O(N \ln N)$
- [ ] **Path Sub-Optimality Bound:** $L_{\text{hier}} \leq 2 \cdot L_{\text{opt}} + 1$
- [ ] **Gateway Router:** Border router summarising intra-cluster routes for inter-cluster advertisement.
- [ ] **Real-World Mapping:** OSPF Areas (intra-area + inter-area), BGP Autonomous Systems, Zone Routing Protocol (MANET), SDN hierarchical controllers.
- [ ] **Trade-offs:** Reduced memory and update traffic ↔ sub-optimal paths and configuration complexity.
- [ ] **Key Logarithm Rule:** $\ln 10 \approx 2.303$, $\log_{10} e \approx 0.434$.
- [ ] **Memory Reduction Formula:** $\text{Saving}\% = \left(1 - \dfrac{2\sqrt{N}}{N}\right) \times 100\% = \left(1 - \dfrac{2}{\sqrt{N}}\right) \times 100\%$.

### 🧠 Mnemonics for the Exam

- **"$K = \sqrt{N}$, $T = 2\sqrt{N}$"** → Think "**S**quare **R**oot and **T**wice it" (Square root = K, Twice that = T).
- **"L = ln N"** → "**L**ong networks need **ln** levels" (L and ln rhyme).
- **"Path ≤ 2·Optimal + 1"** → "**D**etour is at most **double**".

### 📌 Quick Numerical Reference Table

| $N$ (routers) | $K^* = \sqrt{N}$ | $T_{\min} = 2\sqrt{N}$ | Saving vs Flat |
|-------------|------------------|-----------------------|----------------|
| 100 | 10 | 20 | 80% |
| 1,000 | 32 | 63 | 93.7% |
| 10,000 | 100 | 200 | 98% |
| 100,000 | 316 | 632 | 99.37% |
| 1,000,000 | 1,000 | 2,000 | 99.8% |

---

**End of Notes — Hierarchical Routing, Module 3, PEMRT631 (KTU 2024 Scheme)**
