topics.title: Routing in the Internet
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMRT631 - NETWORK & DATA SECURITY > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Routing in the Internet # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is Internet Routing? **Routing in the Internet** is the process by which data packets are forwarded from a source host to a destination host across multiple interconnected networks, using routing protocols that dynamically discover, maintain, and select optimal paths through the Internet's decentralized topology. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** > Routing in the Internet refers to the hierarchical, distributed mechanism that operates at Layer-3 (Network Layer) of the TCP/IP model. It involves the cooperation of millions of routers organized into **Autonomous Systems (AS)**, each running internal routing protocols (IGPs) and communicating externally via inter-domain protocols (EGPs), with **BGP (Border Gateway Protocol version 4)** being the de-facto standard backbone protocol. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The Postal System of a Country Imagine the Internet as a massive country where every home (host) needs to send letters (packets) to other homes. | Internet Concept | Postal Analogy | |---|---| | **Host** | A home/household | | **Router** | A local post office | | **Autonomous System (AS)** | A state or province (administered independently) | | **Network Address (IP)** | The city and PIN code | | **Routing Table** | The delivery route book held by each post office | | **BGP** | The national postal coordination office | | **OSPF / RIP** | The internal courier routing rules within a state | > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** Just as PIN codes allow postal workers to quickly sort and route mail regionally before forwarding it nationally, **IP prefixes** allow routers to aggregate forwarding decisions efficiently. A local postmaster (intra-AS router) does not need to know every house in the country; they only need to know which **state (AS)** to forward the letter to. --- ## 1.3 The Two-Tier Routing Hierarchy The Internet does **NOT** use a single, monolithic routing protocol. Instead, it employs a **two-tier hierarchical architecture**: 1. **Intra-AS (Interior Gateway Protocol - IGP):** Routing *within* a single administrative domain (e.g., a university's network, an ISP's internal network). Examples: **RIP, OSPF, IS-IS, EIGRP**. 2. **Inter-AS (Exterior Gateway Protocol - EGP):** Routing *between* autonomous systems. The de-facto standard is **BGP-4 (Border Gateway Protocol version 4)**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why a Two-Tier System?** > - **Scalability:** A single global routing table currently holds ~900,000+ IPv4 prefixes. If every router knew every prefix in detail, memory and CPU would collapse. > - **Administrative Autonomy:** Each AS (organization) wants **policy control** — it decides *which* neighboring AS to send traffic through, *which* prefixes to advertise, and *which* to reject. > - **Aggregation:** Hierarchical routing drastically reduces the size of routing tables. --- ## 1.4 Autonomous Systems (AS) — The Building Blocks An **Autonomous System (AS)** is a collection of IP networks and routers under the control of a single organization (e.g., an ISP, a university, a government body) that presents a **common routing policy** to the Internet. - Each AS is assigned a globally unique **16-bit (or 32-bit in ASN extension) Autonomous System Number (ASN)**. - ASNs are allocated by **IANA** (Internet Assigned Numbers Authority) via Regional Internet Registries (RIRs) like APNIC, ARIN, RIPE NCC. - **ASN Range:** 1 – 65,534 (16-bit public), 64,512 – 65,534 (private), 32-bit extension from 131,072 onwards. ### Classification of Autonomous Systems: | AS Type | Description | Example | |---|---|---| | **Stub AS** | Connects to only ONE other AS; carries only local traffic. | Small corporate network | | **Multihomed AS** | Connects to multiple ASes but refuses to carry transit traffic. | Large enterprise | | **Transit AS** | Carries traffic that neither originates nor terminates in itself. | Tier-1 ISP (e.g., AT&T, Lumen) | --- ## 1.5 Core Routing Metrics Used in the Internet The Internet uses several metrics to evaluate a "best" path. Unlike simple textbook networks, the real Internet often mixes these metrics: - **Hop Count:** Number of routers a packet traverses. - **Cost / Administrative Distance:** Locally assigned weight by the network administrator. - **Bandwidth / Delay / Load:** Used in link-state and advanced distance-vector protocols. - **Policy:** Used primarily in **BGP** — NOT a pure technical metric, but a business/political one (e.g., "prefer route through peer AS rather than transit AS to save money"). > [!NOTE] > **Core Takeaway:** At the IGP level, routing is **technical and algorithmic** (shortest path). At the BGP level, routing is **political and economic** (policy-driven path vector). # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Intra-AS Routing Protocols (IGPs) ### 2.1.1 Routing Information Protocol (RIP) RIP is one of the oldest distance-vector protocols, documented in **RFC 2453**. **Operational Characteristics:** - Uses **Hop Count** as its sole metric (maximum 15 hops; 16 = infinity/unreachable). - Updates the routing table every **30 seconds** using UDP port 520. - Implements **split horizon, route poisoning, and hold-down timers** to prevent loops. - Converges slowly on large networks. **Bellman-Ford Equation used by RIP:** For each destination $x$ and each neighbor $v$, the router maintains: $$ D_x(v) = \min_u \left\{ c(v,u) + D_x(u) \} $$ where $c(v,u)$ is the cost from $v$ to neighbor $u$, and $D_x(u)$ is the neighbor's distance to $x$. --- ### 2.1.2 Open Shortest Path First (OSPF) OSPF is a **link-state protocol** standardized by the IETF in **RFC 2328** (OSPFv2 for IPv4) and **RFC 5340** (OSPFv3 for IPv6). **Operational Characteristics:** - Uses **Dijkstra's shortest path first (SPF) algorithm** to compute least-cost paths. - Cost metric is typically the **inverse of link bandwidth**: $$ \text{Cost} = \frac{\text{Reference Bandwidth}}{\text{Interface Bandwidth}} $$ - Default reference bandwidth = **100 Mbps**. (Hence, Fast Ethernet = 1, Gigabit = 1 → modifications required for 10 Gbps links.) - Supports **Type of Service (TOS)**-based routing and **equal-cost multi-path (ECMP)**. - Uses **LSA (Link State Advertisements)** flooded via reliable flooding. - Hierarchical design within a single AS via **Areas** (Area 0 is the backbone). **Dijkstra's Algorithm Formula (used by OSPF):** Let $N$ = set of nodes whose shortest path from source $s$ is finalized. $$ D(v) = \min_{u \in N} \left\{ D(u) + c(u,v) \right\} $$ --- ## 2.2 Inter-AS Routing Protocol: BGP The **Border Gateway Protocol version 4 (BGP-4)** is the **sole inter-AS routing protocol** of the modern Internet. Documented in **RFC 4271**. ### 2.2.1 BGP Classifications | BGP Type | Description | |---|---| | **eBGP (external BGP)** | Runs between routers in **different** ASes | | **iBGP (internal BGP)** | Runs between routers **within the same** AS | ### 2.2.2 BGP Path Attributes (Critical for KTU Exams) BGP routers advertise routes as **prefixes + path attributes**. The KTU-highlighted attributes are: | Attribute | Type | Description | |---|---|---| | **AS-PATH** | Well-known mandatory | Sequence of ASes the route has traversed | | **NEXT-HOP** | Well-known mandatory | IP address of the next-hop router | | **ORIGIN** | Well-known mandatory | IGP, EGP, or INCOMPLETE | | **LOCAL-PREF** | Well-known discretionary | Local preference (higher = preferred) | | **MED (MULTI_EXIT_DISC)** | Optional non-transitive | Hint to neighboring AS on preferred entry point | | **COMMUNITY** | Optional transitive | Tag for applying routing policies | | **ORIGINATOR_ID** | Optional non-transitive | Router ID that originated the route (used in route reflection) | ### 2.2.3 BGP Path Selection Algorithm (HIGH-YIELD for KTU!) When multiple BGP routes exist for the same prefix, the router applies the following decision process **in strict order**: $$ \text{Highest LOCAL\_PREF} \rightarrow \text{Shortest AS\_PATH} \rightarrow \text{Lowest ORIGIN} \rightarrow \text{Lowest MED} \rightarrow \text{eBGP over iBGP} \rightarrow \text{Lowest IGP cost to NEXT\_HOP} \rightarrow \text{Oldest route} \rightarrow \text{Lowest Router ID} \rightarrow \text{Lowest Neighbor IP} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse the order. Remember: **LOCAL_PREF beats AS_PATH**. A route with a longer AS_PATH can still be preferred if its LOCAL_PREF is higher! --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Formula / Concept | Expression | Notes | |---|---|---| | RIP metric | $\text{Hops} \leq 15$ | 16 = unreachable | | OSPF cost | $\text{Cost} = \frac{100 \text{ Mbps}}{\text{Link BW}}$ | Configurable; default ref = 100 Mbps | | Bellman-Ford (DV) | $D_x(v) = \min_u \left\{ c(v,u) + D_x(u) \right\}$ | Distance-Vector update | | Dijkstra (LS) | $D(v) = \min_{u \in N} \left\{ D(u) + c(u,v) \right\}$ | Link-State shortest path | | BGP LOCAL_PREF | $\max$ preferred | Local scope only | | BGP AS_PATH | $\min$ length preferred | Tie-breaker | | BGP MED | $\min$ preferred | Sent to neighbor AS | | RIP update timer | $30 \text{ s}$ | Periodic | | OSPF LSA refresh | $30 \text{ min}$ | Link-state flooding | | BGP keepalive | $60 \text{ s}$ (default) | TCP port 179 | | BGP hold timer | $180 \text{ s}$ | Triggers session reset | --- ## 2.4 Real-World Utility of Internet Routing - **ISP Traffic Engineering:** Tier-1 ISPs use **BGP communities** and **LOCAL_PREF** to balance traffic across expensive trans-oceanic links. - **Anycast DNS:** Services like Cloudflare DNS (1.1.1.1) advertise the same prefix from hundreds of locations; BGP naturally routes users to the **topologically nearest** instance. - **DDoS Mitigation:** BGP **blackholing** and **RTBH (Remotely Triggered Black Hole)** use BGP communities to drop attack traffic upstream. - **SD-WAN / SASE:** Modern enterprise networks overlay policy-based routing over BGP to optimize SaaS application access. - **Multi-cloud networking:** Cloud providers interconnect via BGP, allowing enterprises to peer their on-premise networks with AWS, Azure, and GCP seamlessly. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example: Distance-Vector Update (RIP-style) **Problem Setup:** Consider three routers $A$, $B$, $C$ as shown below. Compute the new distance vector at router $A$ after one Bellman-Ford update. $$ \text{Costs: } c(A,B) = 2, \quad c(A,C) = 5, \quad c(B,C) = 1, \quad c(B,D) = 4 $$ $$ \text{Known distances to destination } D: \\ D_D(B) = 4, \quad D_D(C) = 3 \text{ (via } B\text{)} $$ --- ### Step 1 — Identify all possible next-hops from $A$ Router $A$ has two neighbors: $B$ and $C$. --- ### Step 2 — Compute the cost via neighbor $B$ $$ D_D(B \text{ from } A) = c(A,B) + D_D(B) $$ Substituting values: $$ D_D(B \text{ from } A) = 2 + 4 = 6 $$ --- ### Step 3 — Compute the cost via neighbor $C$ $$ D_D(C \text{ from } A) = c(A,C) + D_D(C) $$ Substituting values: $$ D_D(C \text{ from } A) = 5 + 3 = 8 $$ --- ### Step 4 — Apply the Bellman-Ford minimum $$ D_D(A) = \min \{ 6,\ 8 \} = 6 $$ --- ### Step 5 — Final Routing Table at Router $A$ | Destination | Next-Hop | Cost | |---|---|---| | $D$ | $B$ | **6** | > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** Even though going via $C$ requires only 3 hops to reach $D$, the cumulative cost $A \to C \to B \to D$ = $5 + 1 + 4 = 10$ is more expensive than the direct path $A \to B \to D = 2 + 4 = 6$. RIP, however, does **not** see this — RIP only counts hops, so it would choose the 3-hop path. This illustrates a key limitation of pure hop-count distance-vector protocols. --- ## 3.2 Worked Example: Dijkstra's Algorithm (OSPF-style) **Problem:** Compute shortest paths from source $S$ to all nodes in the following graph: | Edge | Cost | |---|---| | $S \to A$ | 7 | | $S \to B$ | 2 | | $A \to B$ | 3 | | $A \to D$ | 4 | | $B \to D$ | 4 | | $B \to C$ | 1 | | $C \to D$ | 2 | | $C \to E$ | 5 | | $D \to E$ | 1 | --- ### Iteration 0 — Initialization $$ N = \{S\}, \quad D(S)=0,\ D(A)=7,\ D(B)=2,\ D(C)=\infty,\ D(D)=\infty,\ D(E)=\infty $$ --- ### Iteration 1 — Add $B$ (lowest tentative cost among neighbors of $N$) Update all neighbors of $B$: $$ D(C) = D(B) + c(B,C) = 2 + 1 = 3 $$ $$ D(D) = D(B) + c(B,D) = 2 + 4 = 6 $$ New tentative costs: $A=7,\ C=3,\ D=6,\ E=\infty$. Pick $C$. --- ### Iteration 2 — Add $C$ Update neighbors of $C$: $$ D(D) = \min\{6,\ D(C)+c(C,D)\} = \min\{6,\ 3+2\} = 5 $$ $$ D(E) = D(C) + c(C,E) = 3 + 5 = 8 $$ Tentative: $A=7,\ D=5,\ E=8$. Pick $D$. --- ### Iteration 3 — Add $D$ $$ D(E) = \min\{8,\ D(D)+c(D,E)\} = \min\{8,\ 5+1\} = 6 $$ Tentative: $A=7,\ E=6$. Pick $E$. --- ### Iteration 4 — Add $E$ No improvement possible for $A$. Tentative: $A=7$. Pick $A$. --- ### Iteration 5 — Add $A$ All nodes finalized. --- ### Final Shortest Paths | Destination | Shortest Cost | Path | |---|---|---| | $S$ | $0$ | $S$ | | $B$ | $2$ | $S \to B$ | | $C$ | $3$ | $S \to B \to C$ | | $D$ | $5$ | $S \to B \to C \to D$ | | $E$ | $6$ | $S \to B \to C \to D \to E$ | | $A$ | $7$ | $S \to A$ | --- ## 3.3 Worked Example: BGP Path Selection **Problem:** Router $R1$ in **AS 100** receives four BGP advertisements for prefix **200.50.0.0/16**: | Route | LOCAL_PREF | AS_PATH | ORIGIN | MED | |---|---|---|---|---| | Route 1 | 150 | `300 400` | IGP | 50 | | Route 2 | 200 | `500 600 700` | EGP | 20 | | Route 3 | 200 | `800` | IGP | 30 | | Route 4 | 100 | `900` | INCOMPLETE | 10 | **Apply BGP decision process in strict order:** --- ### Step 1 — Highest LOCAL_PREF $$ \text{Route 2} = \text{Route 3} = 200 \quad > \quad \text{Route 1} = 150 \quad > \quad \text{Route 4} = 100 $$ Eliminate Routes 1 and 4. --- ### Step 2 — Shortest AS_PATH $$ \text{Route 3: AS\_PATH length} = 1 \quad < \quad \text{Route 2: AS\_PATH length} = 3 $$ **Selected: Route 3** (`AS_PATH = 800`, ORIGIN = IGP). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final answer:** Router $R1$ installs **Route 3** as the best path for prefix 200.50.0.0/16, even though Route 2 had a more attractive MED. This is because **MED is checked AFTER LOCAL_PREF and AS_PATH**. --- ## 3.4 Comparison Matrix: Distance-Vector vs Link-State vs Path-Vector | Property | RIP (DV) | OSPF (LS) | BGP (Path-Vector) | |---|---|---|---| | **Algorithm** | Bellman-Ford | Dijkstra | Best-path + policy | | **Metric** | Hop count | Cost (bandwidth-based) | Policy + path attributes | | **Convergence** | Slow | Fast | Moderate | | **Scalability** | Poor (≤15 hops) | Excellent (hierarchical areas) | Excellent (global Internet) | | **Loop Prevention** | Split horizon, poison reverse | SPF tree, no loops | AS_PATH loop detection | | **Update Trigger** | Periodic (30s) | Event-driven (LSA) | Incremental updates | | **Scope** | Intra-AS | Intra-AS | Inter-AS | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Two-Tier Internet Routing Architecture ```mermaid graph LR subgraph AS1["AS 100 - Tier1 ISP North"] R1A["R1A - Edge Router"] R1B["R1B - Core Router"] R1C["R1C - Edge Router"] end subgraph AS2["AS 200 - Tier1 ISP South"] R2A["R2A - Edge Router"] R2B["R2B - Core Router"] R2C["R2C - Edge Router"] end subgraph AS3["AS 300 - Stub Customer Network"] R3A["R3A - Edge Router"] R3B["R3B - Internal Router"] end R1A -- eBGP Session AS100-AS300 --> R3A R2A -- eBGP Session AS200-AS300 --> R3A R1A -- eBGP Session AS100-AS200 --> R2A R1B -. iBGP .-> R1A R1B -. iBGP .-> R1C R2B -. iBGP .-> R2A R2B -. iBGP .-> R2C R3A -- OSPF Internal --> R3B style AS1 fill:#e6f2ff,stroke:#0066cc style AS2 fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#cc0000 style AS3 fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#009900 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** > - **Solid lines** = eBGP sessions (between different ASes). > - **Dotted lines** = iBGP sessions (within the same AS). > - **OSPF** runs internally inside AS 300. > - Each AS makes independent policy decisions about which neighboring AS to peer with. --- ## 4.2 BGP Best-Path Selection Decision Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Receive Multiple BGP Routes for Prefix X"] --> B{"Step 1: Highest LOCAL_PREF?"} B -- "Unique Winner" --> Z["Install Route in RIB"] B -- "Tie" --> C{"Step 2: Shortest AS_PATH?"} C -- "Unique Winner" --> Z C -- "Tie" --> D{"Step 3: Lowest ORIGIN?"} D -- "Unique Winner" --> Z D -- "Tie" --> E{"Step 4: Lowest MED?"} E -- "Unique Winner" --> Z E -- "Tie" --> F{"Step 5: eBGP over iBGP?"} F -- "Unique Winner" --> Z F -- "Tie" --> G{"Step 6: Lowest IGP Cost to NEXT_HOP?"} G -- "Unique Winner" --> Z G -- "Tie" --> H{"Step 7: Oldest Route?"} H -- "Unique Winner" --> Z H -- "Tie" --> I{"Step 8: Lowest Router ID"} I --> Z style A fill:#fff4cc style Z fill:#ccffcc ``` --- ## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Internet Router ```mermaid flowchart LR IN["Incoming Packets
from Interfaces"] --> FWD["Forwarding Engine
Hardware-based Lookup"] RIB["Routing Information Base
RIB - Full Routing Table"] --> FWD FWD --> OUT["Outgoing Packets
to Egress Interfaces"] PROTOCOLS["Routing Protocols"] --> PROC["Route Processor
CPU"] PROC --> RIB PROC --> ADJ["Adjacency Tables"] PROC --> LSDB["Link-State Database
OSPF Only"] BGP["BGP-4 eBGP / iBGP"] --> PROC OSPF["OSPF"] --> PROC RIP["RIP v2"] --> PROC STATIC["Static Routes"] --> PROC POLICY["Policy Engine
Route Maps, Communities"] --> PROC style FWD fill:#ffcccc style RIB fill:#cce5ff style PROC fill:#ccffcc ``` > [!NOTE] > **Architecture Insight:** > - The **control plane** (protocols + route processor) computes paths and fills the **RIB**. > - The **data plane** (forwarding engine) uses a compressed version of the RIB called the **FIB (Forwarding Information Base)** for wire-speed packet forwarding. > - This separation — known as **control-plane / data-plane separation** — is the basis of modern SDN (Software-Defined Networking) and routers like Cisco's **ASIC-based** IOS-XR platforms. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📘 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Explain the difference between intra-AS and inter-AS routing. Give one example protocol for each.** **Model Answer:** Intra-AS routing (also called **Interior Gateway Protocol - IGP**) operates **within a single Autonomous System**. It is concerned with optimal packet forwarding among routers that share a common administrative policy. IGPs focus on **technical metrics** such as hop count, bandwidth, or delay, and typically converge quickly. Example: **OSPF (Open Shortest Path First)**. Inter-AS routing (also called **Exterior Gateway Protocol - EGP**) operates **between different Autonomous Systems**. It is concerned with **policy-driven** path selection across administrative boundaries. EGP metrics include not only technical efficiency but also business/political considerations (e.g., peering agreements, transit costs). The dominant protocol is **BGP-4 (Border Gateway Protocol version 4)**, RFC 4271. | Property | Intra-AS (IGP) | Inter-AS (EGP) | |---|---|---| | Scope | Within single AS | Between ASes | | Example | OSPF, RIP | BGP-4 | | Metric | Technical (cost, hops) | Policy + path attributes | | Scalability Need | Limited | Massive (global) | --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **What is an Autonomous System (AS)? Differentiate between stub, multihomed, and transit AS.** **Model Answer:** An **Autonomous System (AS)** is a group of IP networks and routers under the control of a single administrative entity, presenting a unified routing policy to the Internet. Each AS is identified by a unique **Autonomous System Number (ASN)** assigned by IANA via Regional Internet Registries. | AS Type | Description | Carries Transit Traffic? | |---|---|---| | **Stub AS** | Connected to only **one** other AS | No | | **Multihomed AS** | Connected to **multiple** ASes, but does NOT allow transit | No | | **Transit AS** | Allows other ASes to route traffic through it | Yes | Examples: A small office network = **Stub**; a university with two ISP links = **Multihomed**; a Tier-1 ISP like Level-3 = **Transit**. --- ## 📕 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Choose ONE > **Note:** KTU allows Module Internal Choice. Solve **either** Question A **or** Question B. --- ### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **BGP path selection algorithm**. Discuss the role of **LOCAL_PREF** and **AS_PATH** attributes. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Compare **RIP** and **OSPF** routing protocols with respect to algorithm, metric, convergence, and scalability. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) **BGP Path Selection Algorithm:** BGP is a **path-vector protocol**. When a BGP router receives multiple advertisements for the same prefix, it must select ONE best path to install in its routing table (RIB). The decision is made by applying the following steps in **strict order**: $$ \text{(1) Highest LOCAL\_PREF} \rightarrow \text{(2) Shortest AS\_PATH} \rightarrow \text{(3) Lowest ORIGIN} \rightarrow \text{(4) Lowest MED} $$ $$ \rightarrow \text{(5) eBGP over iBGP} \rightarrow \text{(6) Lowest IGP cost to NEXT\_HOP} $$ $$ \rightarrow \text{(7) Oldest route} \rightarrow \text{(8) Lowest Router ID} \rightarrow \text{(9) Lowest Neighbor IP} $$ **Role of LOCAL_PREF:** - LOCAL_PREF is the **first** attribute checked. - It is a **32-bit value** (default = 100). - **Higher** LOCAL_PREF is preferred. - It is exchanged **only between iBGP peers** within the same AS — never sent to eBGP neighbors. - Used to influence outbound traffic engineering (e.g., "prefer exit via ISP-A over ISP-B"). **Role of AS_PATH:** - AS_PATH is the **second** attribute checked (after LOCAL_PREF). - It is a sequence of AS numbers that the route has traversed. - **Shorter** AS_PATH is preferred. - When a route crosses an AS boundary, the advertising AS prepends its own ASN to the AS_PATH. - Crucially, AS_PATH provides **loop detection**: if a router sees its own ASN in the AS_PATH, it rejects the route. **Diagram (refer Section 4.2 above):** Decision flowchart. --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) | Parameter | RIP | OSPF | |---|---|---| | **Algorithm** | Distance-Vector (Bellman-Ford) | Link-State (Dijkstra SPF) | | **Metric** | Hop count (max 15) | Cost = Reference BW / Link BW | | **Convergence** | Slow (periodic 30s updates) | Fast (event-driven LSA flooding) | | **Scalability** | Poor; small networks only | Excellent; supports hierarchical Areas | | **Loop Prevention** | Split horizon, route poisoning | Loop-free SPF tree by design | | **Updates** | Full periodic table | Incremental LSA | | **Transport** | UDP port 520 | IP protocol 89 (direct) | | **Standard** | RFC 2453 | RFC 2328 | **Conclusion:** OSPF is the preferred choice for modern enterprise and ISP networks due to its scalability, fast convergence, and hierarchical design. RIP is largely legacy and used only in very small networks. --- ### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a)** Describe the operation of **OSPF** with hierarchical area design. Explain the role of **Area 0**, **Area Border Routers (ABR)**, and **Backbone**. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A router receives four BGP routes for prefix **10.20.0.0/16** with the following attributes. Determine the best route using the BGP decision process: | Route | LOCAL_PREF | AS_PATH | ORIGIN | MED | |---|---|---|---|---| | R1 | 100 | `200 300` | IGP | 50 | | R2 | 150 | `400 500 600` | EGP | 20 | | R3 | 150 | `700` | IGP | 30 | | R4 | 150 | `800 900` | INCOMPLETE | 10 | **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) **OSPF Hierarchical Design:** In large networks, OSPF uses a **two-level hierarchy** to scale: 1. **Backbone Area (Area 0):** - The central area to which **all other areas must connect**. - Responsible for distributing routing information between non-backbone areas. - All Area Border Routers (ABRs) belong to Area 0 and one other area. 2. **Regular Areas (e.g., Area 1, Area 2):** - Connected to the backbone via at least one ABR. - Internal topology is hidden from other areas — only **summary LSAs (Type 3)** are injected into the backbone. **Router Types in OSPF:** - **Internal Router:** All interfaces belong to the same area. - **Area Border Router (ABR):** Connects two or more areas; one must be Area 0. Generates summary LSAs. - **Backbone Router:** Has at least one interface in Area 0. - **Autonomous System Boundary Router (ASBR):** Injects external routes (e.g., from BGP) into OSPF via Type 5 LSAs. **LSA Types Recap:** - Type 1: Router LSA - Type 2: Network LSA - Type 3: Summary LSA (ABR-generated) - Type 4: ASBR Summary LSA - Type 5: External LSA (ASBR-generated) **Advantages:** Reduced SPF computation per area, smaller LSDB, controlled summarization, fault isolation. --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Apply BGP Path Selection:** **Step 1 — Highest LOCAL_PREF:** - R1 = 100 - R2 = R3 = R4 = 150 ✓ Eliminate **R1**. Remaining: R2, R3, R4. **Step 2 — Shortest AS_PATH (after LOCAL_PREF tie):** - R2: length 3 (`400 500 600`) - R3: length 1 (`700`) ✓ - R4: length 2 (`800 900`) Eliminate **R2 and R4**. **Selected: R3** with AS_PATH = `700`, ORIGIN = IGP, MED = 30. > [!NOTE] > **Answer:** Router installs **R3** as the best path. Even though R4 has a lower MED (10), MED is checked only AFTER LOCAL_PREF and AS_PATH. This is the classic BGP decision order. --- ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common places where students LOSE marks in this topic:** > 1. **Order of BGP attributes:** Listing MED before AS_PATH — WRONG. AS_PATH comes BEFORE MED. > 2. **LOCAL_PREF scope:** Writing that LOCAL_PREF is exchanged between eBGP peers — WRONG. It propagates only via iBGP. > 3. **OSPF cost formula:** Writing cost = bandwidth / 100 Mbps — WRONG sign. Correct is **Reference BW / Interface BW**. For 10 Gbps links, the default reference gives cost = 1, so administrators must **raise the reference bandwidth**. > 4. **Dijkstra vs Bellman-Ford:** Confusing which algorithm belongs to OSPF vs RIP. OSPF = Dijkstra (Link-State); RIP = Bellman-Ford (Distance-Vector). > 5. **Hierarchical OSPF areas:** Forgetting that **all non-backbone areas MUST connect to Area 0**. A "floating area" with no Area 0 attachment is a configuration error. --- ## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ The Internet routing system is **two-tiered**: Intra-AS (IGP) and Inter-AS (BGP). - ✅ **Autonomous Systems (AS)** are the fundamental units of Internet routing policy. - ✅ **RIP** is a distance-vector protocol limited to **15 hops**; uses **Bellman-Ford**. - ✅ **OSPF** is a link-state protocol using **Dijkstra's SPF**; supports hierarchical **Areas** with **Area 0** as the backbone. - ✅ **BGP-4** is the **only** inter-AS routing protocol of the global Internet (RFC 4271). - ✅ BGP is a **path-vector** protocol using attributes, NOT a pure metric-based protocol. - ✅ **BGP decision order** (HIGH-YIELD): `LOCAL_PREF → AS_PATH → ORIGIN → MED → eBGP/iBGP → IGP cost → Age → Router ID`. - ✅ **AS_PATH** serves a **dual purpose**: path selection metric AND loop detection. - ✅ **NEXT_HOP** attribute: in eBGP, set to the advertising router; in iBGP, unchanged from eBGP advertisement. - ✅ Default **BGP timers**: keepalive = **60 s**, hold = **180 s**, session over **TCP port 179**. - ✅ OSPF uses **IP protocol number 89** (not TCP/UDP), RIP uses **UDP 520**. - ✅ Internet routing at the BGP level is fundamentally **policy-driven** rather than purely performance-driven. - ✅ **Route aggregation / CIDR** is critical for keeping the global routing table scalable (~900K IPv4 prefixes). - ✅ **iBGP full mesh** requirement: within an AS, all BGP speakers must be fully meshed (or use route reflectors / confederations for scalability). > **🎯 KTU 2024 Success Tip:** Memorize the **BGP decision process order** and the **OSPF cost formula**. These two items appear in nearly every KTU paper on this topic.

topics.title: Intra-AS Routing in the Internet: RIP
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMRT631 - NETWORK & DATA SECURITY > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Intra-AS Routing in the Internet: RIP # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The **Routing Information Protocol (RIP)** is one of the oldest **Interior Gateway Protocols (IGP)** used within an Autonomous System (AS) for intra-domain routing. It is a classic **distance-vector routing protocol** in which each router maintains a routing table (a *distance vector*) containing an entry for every reachable destination, indexed by the destination network/IP prefix, the next-hop router, the outgoing interface, and the **metric (hop count)** required to reach that destination. RIP is formally standardized in: - **RFC 1058** (RIPv1, 1988) — classful version - **RFC 2453** (RIPv2, 1998) — classless version with authentication and subnet support - **RFC 2080** (RIPng, 1997) — IPv6 extension RIP uses the **Bellman–Ford algorithm** as its underlying computational engine and operates directly over **UDP port 520** (RIPv2) at the transport layer. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEMRT631 – Module 3):** > Intra-AS routing handles packet forwarding *inside* a single administrative domain (e.g., inside a campus, an enterprise, or an ISP). RIP, OSPF, and IS-IS are the three classical IGPs examined in this module. ## 1.2 Key Terminology (Exact KTU Terminology) | Term | Definition | |------|-----------| | **Autonomous System (AS)** | A collection of routers and networks under a single administrative control using a common routing policy. | | **Intra-AS Routing** | Routing of datagrams *within* a single AS. | | **Distance-Vector Protocol** | A protocol where each router advertises its *entire* routing table to directly connected neighbors only. | | **Hop Count** | The number of routers a packet must traverse to reach a destination. | | **Infinity Metric** | A hop count of **16** in RIP (i.e., destination is declared *unreachable*). | | **Bellman–Ford Equation** | The recursive update rule: $D_x(y) = \min_v \{ c(x,v) + D_v(y) \}$. | ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **"The Boarding-Pass Counter" Analogy:** > Imagine you are at a busy airport (your source router) and you want to reach a remote city (the destination). You can only ask the staff at the *next counter* (your directly connected neighbor) how many *layovers* (hops) are needed. That counter person only knows their **own personal list** of routes (their distance vector) — they do not know the global network map. You keep asking counters along the way, and the **shortest total layovers** wins. After 30 seconds, every counter *rebroadcasts* its updated list to its immediate neighbors — this is exactly how RIP operates! - The **maximum allowed hop count is 15**. The 16th hop is considered "unreachable" (Infinity). This is a hard limit designed to prevent **count-to-infinity** problems. - Routers exchange their **entire routing table** with neighbors every **30 seconds**. - Convergence time is slow compared to link-state protocols (typically 30–180 seconds under fault conditions). # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Mechanism The life-cycle of a RIP router can be broken down into five phases: 1. **Initialization**: On boot-up, the router creates a routing table with entries only for its *directly connected* networks, each with a metric of **0** (or **1** depending on counting convention). 2. **Periodic Update (Every 30 s)**: The router broadcasts (or multicasts for RIPv2 at **224.0.0.9**) its *entire* routing table out every active interface. 3. **Receiving Updates**: When a RIP message arrives from a neighbor, the router processes each route entry and runs the **Bellman–Ford update** on it. 4. **Route Selection**: If the received route offers a *better* metric than the existing route (and the new metric is not Infinity), the route is installed/updated. 5. **Route Expiration**: If a route is not refreshed within **180 seconds**, its metric is set to **Infinity (16)**. After a further **120-second hold-down/flush timer**, the route is finally deleted from the table. ## 2.2 The Core Equation — Bellman–Ford Update Rule The mathematical heart of RIP is the **Bellman–Ford equation**: $$ D_x(y) = \min_{v \in N(x)} \left\{ c(x,v) + D_v(y) \right\} $$ Where: - $D_x(y)$ = the shortest distance from router $x$ to destination $y$ - $N(x)$ = the set of all neighbors of router $x$ - $c(x,v)$ = the cost of the direct link from $x$ to neighbor $v$ (in RIP, this is always **1** per hop) - $D_v(y)$ = the distance advertised by neighbor $v$ to reach $y$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Fact:** In RIP, $c(x,v) = 1$ for every *up* link. Therefore, $D_x(y) = 1 + D_v(y)$ when choosing the *shortest* path. ## 2.3 RIP Timers — The Master Cheat Sheet | Timer | Default Value | Purpose | |-------|---------------|---------| | **Update Timer** | **30 s** | Periodic interval at which the full routing table is advertised. | | **Invalid Timer** | **180 s** | After this period without a refresh, the route is marked invalid (metric = 16). | | **Hold-Down Timer** | **180 s** | During this period, the router suppresses updates about a route that has gone down (prevents ping-pong). | | **Flush Timer** | **240 s** | After this, the route is finally removed from the routing table. | ## 2.4 Loop-Prevention Mechanisms (Anti-Loop Strategies) RIP suffers from the **count-to-infinity problem** when a link fails. Several heuristics mitigate (but do not fully eliminate) this: | Mechanism | Description | Why It Helps | |-----------|-------------|--------------| | **Split Horizon** | A router never advertises a route back to the neighbor from which it learned the route. | Prevents two-node routing loops. | | **Poison Reverse** | A router advertises the learned route back to the neighbor with metric = **16 (Infinity)**. | Aggressively informs the neighbor that the route is unusable via that path. | | **Hold-Down Timers** | Once a route is marked invalid, the router ignores updates about it for a fixed period. | Stabilizes the network during a topology change. | | **Triggered Updates** | When a route metric changes, the update is sent *immediately* (not waiting for the 30-s cycle). | Accelerates convergence. | | **Maximum Hop Count = 15** | Bounds the metric to 15. | Caps the worst-case count-to-infinity duration to 15 × 30 s = **450 s** in the pathological case. | ## 2.5 RIPv1 vs RIPv2 vs RIPng | Feature | RIPv1 | RIPv2 | RIPng | |---------|-------|-------|-------| | RFC | 1058 | 2453 | 2080 | | Class Support | Classful | Classless (VLSM/CIDR) | Classless (IPv6) | | Subnet Mask in Update | No | Yes | Yes | | Authentication | None | Plaintext / MD5 | None (relies on IPsec) | | Transport | UDP 520 | UDP 520 | UDP 521 | | Multicast Address | Broadcast (255.255.255.255) | 224.0.0.9 | FF02::9 | | Next-Hop Field | No | Yes | Yes | ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Small Enterprise LANs**: RIP is still configured on legacy Cisco/Juniper devices for small branch-office networks because of its simplicity. - **Teaching Tool**: RIP is the canonical example used in KTU/computer-networks curricula to introduce the *Bellman–Ford algorithm* and the *count-to-infinity* problem before progressing to OSPF. - **Convergence Demonstration**: RIP is used in **GNS3 / Cisco Packet Tracer** labs to demonstrate routing convergence behavior. - **Embedded Networking**: Some IoT firmware uses stripped-down RIP-like protocols because they require negligible memory and CPU resources. > [!NOTE] > **Industry Note:** Modern enterprise networks have largely migrated to **OSPF** or **IS-IS** for IPv4 and **OSPFv3** or **BGP** for IPv6, owing to RIP's slow convergence and 15-hop limit. However, RIP remains a *mandatory conceptual topic* in the KTU 2024 PEMRT631 syllabus. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example: Bellman–Ford RIP Distance-Vector Convergence ### 3.1.1 Network Topology Consider a simple 4-router linear topology: **R1 ↔ R2 ↔ R3 ↔ R4**, all links cost 1 hop. Initial Routing Table of R1 (only direct network known): - Destination N1: Cost = 0 (directly connected) ### 3.1.2 Convergence via Periodic Updates We trace the routing table of **R1** (in terms of cost to each destination) over four update cycles. **Notation:** $D(R1, Nx) = $ current cost from R1 to network Nx. #### Step 1 — Initialization (t = 0 s) R1's initial vector: $$ D(R1, N1) = 0, \quad D(R1, N2) = \infty, \quad D(R1, N3) = \infty, \quad D(R1, N4) = \infty $$ R1 advertises: `(N1, 0)` to R2. #### Step 2 — First Update (t = 30 s) R2 sends its vector to R1. R2 currently knows N1=1, N2=0, others = ∞. R1 applies Bellman–Ford: $$ \begin{aligned} D(R1, N1) &= \min\{0, 1 + D(R2,N1)\} = \min\{0, 1+1\} = 0 \\ D(R1, N2) &= \min\{\infty, 1 + D(R2,N2)\} = \min\{\infty, 1+0\} = 1 \\ D(R1, N3) &= \min\{\infty, 1 + D(R2,N3)\} = \min\{\infty, 1+\infty\} = \infty \\ D(R1, N4) &= \min\{\infty, 1 + D(R2,N4)\} = \min\{\infty, 1+\infty\} = \infty \end{aligned} $$ #### Step 3 — Second Update (t = 60 s) By now, R2 has learned N3=1 from R3. R2 advertises `(N1,1), (N2,0), (N3,1)`. R1 recomputes: $$ \begin{aligned} D(R1, N1) &= 0 \quad \text{(unchanged, no improvement)} \\ D(R1, N2) &= \min\{1, 1+0\} = 1 \quad \text{(unchanged)} \\ D(R1, N3) &= \min\{\infty, 1+1\} = 2 \\ D(R1, N4) &= \min\{\infty, 1+\infty\} = \infty \end{aligned} $$ #### Step 4 — Third Update (t = 90 s) R2 has now learned N4=2 (via R3 → R4). R1 recomputes: $$ D(R1, N4) = \min\{\infty, 1+2\} = 3 $$ #### Step 5 — Convergence Achieved The final R1 distance vector is: $$ D(R1) = \{N1{:}\,0,\ N2{:}\,1,\ N3{:}\,2,\ N4{:}\,3\} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Convergence Time:** It took **3 update cycles × 30 s = 90 seconds** to learn a destination 3 hops away. This empirical result illustrates why RIP is considered **slow-converging**. ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — RIP Update Decision Logic The following decision table governs how a RIP router handles a *received* route entry. This is the heart of any KTU exam question on RIP. | Condition | Action Taken | |-----------|--------------| | Received route is **not** in local table | **Install** the route with next-hop = sender, metric = received metric + 1. | | Received route is in local table, but with a **larger metric** than (received + 1) | **Update** the route (better path found). | | Received route is in local table, with **same** metric as (received + 1) | **No action** (already optimal via another equal-cost path). | | Received route is in local table, with a **smaller** metric than (received + 1) | **No action** (current path is better). | | Received metric = **16 (Infinity)** | If route is via that sender, mark the route as **invalid** (metric = 16) and start invalid timer. | ## 3.3 The Count-to-Infinity Problem (Walkthrough) Suppose the link **R1–R2** in the topology above suddenly fails. **Step A (t = 0):** R1 detects failure. R1's entry for N2 becomes 16 (Infinity). **Step B (t = 30 s, before R1's bad news reaches others):** R3 sends its vector `(N2, 1)` to R1. (R3 still thinks it can reach N2 via R2 in 1 hop.) **Step C:** R1 applies Bellman–Ford: $$ D(R1, N2) = \min\{16, 1 + D(R3,N2)\} = \min\{16, 1+1\} = 2 $$ R1 incorrectly believes it can reach N2 via R3 in 2 hops. **Step D (t = 60 s):** R1 advertises `(N2, 2)` to R2 (and R3). R3, in turn, recomputes: $$ D(R3, N2) = \min\{1, 1 + D(R1,N2)\} = \min\{1, 1+2\} = 1 $$ R3 is unaffected; R1's belief creeps up: 2 → 3 → 4 … **Step E:** The metric increments by 1 every 30 s. After 15 such increments (≈ **450 s**), the metric hits **16 = Infinity**, and the route is finally declared unreachable. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students incorrectly state that count-to-infinity takes "16 × 30 = 480 s". The correct answer is **15 × 30 = 450 s** because RIP stops incrementing at 16 and immediately invalidates the route. ## 3.4 RIP Packet Format (RIPv2) The RIPv2 message structure (as per RFC 2453) is essential KTU knowledge: | Field | Size (bytes) | Description | |-------|--------------|-------------| | Command | 1 | 1 = Request, 2 = Response | | Version | 1 | Always **2** for RIPv2 | | Unused | 2 | Must be zero | | Address Family Identifier (AFI) | 2 | **2** for IP | | Route Tag | 2 | Used to carry AS path info (EIGRP-style) | | IP Address | 4 | Destination network address | | Subnet Mask | 4 | Subnet mask (new in RIPv2) | | Next Hop | 4 | Next-hop router IP (new in RIPv2) | | Metric | 4 | Hop count (1–16) | | **Maximum Entries per Packet** | — | Up to **25** route entries per UDP datagram | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a RIP Router This Mermaid diagram illustrates how a RIP-enabled router processes incoming UDP datagrams on port 520, applies the Bellman-Ford update, and forwards updates to its neighbors. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Physical Interface Card] --> B[IP Layer Forwarding Engine] B --> C{UDP Destination Port} C -- Port 520 --> D[RIP Process Demultiplexer] C -- Other Ports --> E[Forward to Upper Layer Apps] D --> F[Parse RIPv2 Message Header] F --> G[Extract Route Entries: AFI, IP, Mask, Metric] G --> H{Bellman-Ford Update Module} H --> I{Compare with Local Routing Table} I -- Better Metric Found --> J[Update Routing Table Entry] I -- No Improvement --> K[Discard Update] I -- New Route --> L[Install New Route Entry] J --> M[Mark Route for Next Periodic Update] L --> M M --> N[Update Timer: 30s Countdown] N -- Timer Expires --> O[Build RIPv2 Response Packet] O --> P[Encapsulate in UDP Port 520] P --> Q[Send to Multicast 224.0.0.9] K --> N style H fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style O fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px styleQ fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.2 Distance-Vector Convergence Sequence (4-Router Linear Topology) ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant R1 participant R2 participant R3 participant R4 Note over R1,R4: t = 0s: All routers have only directly-connected networks R2->>R1: Update(N1=1, N2=0) R2->>R3: Update(N2=0, N3=1) R3->>R2: Update(N2=1, N3=0) R3->>R4: Update(N3=1, N4=0) Note over R1,R4: t = 30s: R1 learns N2; R4 learns N3 R1->>R2: Update(N1=0, N2=1) R3->>R2: Update(N2=2, N3=0, N4=1) R4->>R3: Update(N3=2, N4=0) Note over R1,R4: t = 60s: R1 learns N3 via R2; R4 learns N2 via R3 R2->>R1: Update(N1=1, N2=0, N3=1) R2->>R3: Update(N2=0, N3=1, N4=2) Note over R1,R4: t = 90s: R1 learns N4 via R2 -> R3 R1->>R2: Update(N1=0, N2=1, N3=2, N4=3) Note over R1,R4: t = 120s: Network Fully Converged ``` ## 4.3 RIP Packet Header Structure (RIPv2) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Command: 1B
1=Req 2=Resp] --> B[Version: 1B
Value=2] B --> C[Unused: 2B
Must be 0] C --> D[Route Entry 1
20 Bytes] D --> E[Route Entry 2
20 Bytes] E --> F[... up to 25 Entries ...] F --> G[Authentication: 20B
Optional] style A fill:#FFB6C1 style B fill:#FFB6C1 style C fill:#D3D3D3 style D fill:#98FB98 style E fill:#98FB98 style F fill:#98FB98 style G fill:#FFD700 ``` > [!NOTE] > Each route entry is exactly 20 bytes: AFI(2) + Route Tag(2) + IP(4) + Subnet Mask(4) + Next Hop(4) + Metric(4) = 20 bytes. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Explain the meaning of "distance-vector" in the context of RIP. Why is RIP classified as a distance-vector protocol?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - A *distance vector* is a routing table that lists for every destination network the **distance (hop count)** and the **direction (next-hop router)**. - RIP is called a *distance-vector* protocol because **(i)** each router maintains a distance vector, **(ii)** it exchanges the entire vector with only its **directly connected neighbors**, and **(iii)** it has **no knowledge of the global topology** — only the distances advertised by neighbors. - This is in contrast to *link-state* protocols (like OSPF) where every router builds a complete map of the network. ### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **What is the maximum hop count in RIP? What happens when a hop count reaches 16?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - The maximum allowed hop count in RIP is **15**. - A hop count of **16** is treated as **Infinity**, meaning the destination is declared **unreachable**. - This limit exists to **bound the count-to-infinity problem** and to prevent routing loops from propagating indefinitely. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mark Losers:** > - Forgetting to mention that RIP uses **UDP port 520** (cost: -1 mark) > - Confusing RIPv1 (classful, broadcast) with RIPv2 (classless, multicast 224.0.0.9) (cost: -2 marks) > - Using "16" as the maximum hop count instead of "15" (cost: -2 marks) > - Not drawing the routing table evolution in convergence questions (cost: -3 marks) > - Forgetting the four RIP timers (Update, Invalid, Hold-Down, Flush) (cost: -2 marks) ### Question A: `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]` **(a) Explain the operation of the RIP routing algorithm with a suitable example. How does a router maintain and update its distance-vector table using the Bellman–Ford equation? (7 marks)** **(b) With reference to RIP, explain the count-to-infinity problem. Describe how split horizon and poison reverse help mitigate this problem. (7 marks)** #### Model Solution ### Part (a) — Operation of RIP (7 marks) **Step 1 — Initialization (1 mark):** When a RIP router boots, it creates a routing table containing only its directly connected networks. Each entry has a metric of **0** (or **1**, depending on counting convention; KTU accepts either with justification). The next-hop is the destination network itself, and the outgoing interface is the local interface on that subnet. **Step 2 — Periodic Update (1 mark):** Every **30 seconds**, the router broadcasts (RIPv1) or multicasts to **224.0.0.9** (RIPv2) its *entire* routing table out every active RIP-enabled interface. The update is carried inside a UDP datagram with **destination port 520**. **Step 3 — Receiving & Processing Updates (2 marks):** When a router receives a RIP response, it processes each route entry. For each entry, it adds **1** to the received metric (because the route is one more hop away via the sender). It then compares this value with the existing entry in the local table. If the new path is better (smaller metric), the local table is updated. **Step 4 — Bellman–Ford Update Rule (2 marks):** The decision is governed by: $$ D_x(y) = \min_{v \in N(x)} \left\{ 1 + D_v(y) \right\} $$ The router picks the *neighbor* $v$ that minimizes the total cost to destination $y$. **Step 5 — Worked Example (1 mark):** For the 4-router linear topology R1–R2–R3–R4 above, R1 converges to `D(R1) = {N1:0, N2:1, N3:2, N4:3}` in 90 s. --- ### Part (b) — Count-to-Infinity and Mitigation (7 marks) **Step 1 — Problem Definition (2 marks):** When a link fails, neighboring routers may not learn of the failure immediately. In the time gap, they may continue to advertise stale routes. This causes the metric to slowly increment by 1 every update cycle — a phenomenon called **count-to-infinity**. In the worst case, RIP takes up to **15 × 30 = 450 seconds** to declare a destination unreachable. **Step 2 — Split Horizon (2 marks):** The *split horizon* rule states: **"Never advertise a route back to the neighbor from which you learned it."** This breaks the simplest two-node loops. Example: If R2 learned N4 from R3, it will *not* advertise N4 back to R3. **Step 3 — Poison Reverse (2 marks):** A more aggressive variant: instead of *silently* not advertising the route back, the router advertises the route back with metric = **16 (Infinity)**. This explicitly tells the neighbor *"this route is dead via me, do not use me as a path."* This is also called *route poisoning* combined with *poison reverse*. **Step 4 — Conclusion (1 mark):** Together, split horizon and poison reverse convert a slow count-to-infinity into a *fast* failure detection. They do not, however, eliminate the problem completely in larger topologies — hence the need for the 15-hop cap and hold-down timers. --- ### Question B: `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]` **(a) List and explain the four timers used in RIP. Why is the value of 30 seconds chosen for the update timer? (7 marks)** **(b) Compare RIPv1 and RIPv2 with respect to classful/classless support, authentication, multicast/broadcast behavior, and VLSM support. State the role of RIPng. (7 marks)** #### Model Solution ### Part (a) — RIP Timers (7 marks) | Timer | Default | Purpose | |-------|---------|---------| | **Update Timer** | 30 s | Periodicity of full-table advertisements. | | **Invalid Timer** | 180 s | Without refresh, route metric is set to 16 (invalid). | | **Hold-Down Timer** | 180 s | Suppresses acceptance of inferior updates for a route that has just gone down. | | **Flush Timer** | 240 s | The route is finally *deleted* from the routing table. | **Why 30 seconds? (2 marks):** - Short enough for reasonably fast convergence (a route propagates across the network in $n \times 30$ s for an $n$-hop path). - Long enough to avoid flooding the network with overhead (RIP updates are small, but a 1 s timer would generate 86,400 packets/day per neighbor). - Empirically tuned by Xerox PARC in the original 1980s design to balance convergence speed vs. bandwidth consumption. **Justification of other timer values (1 mark):** The 180 s invalid/hold-down and 240 s flush values give a *safety window* of 60 s during which the router refuses to accept destabilizing updates. --- ### Part (b) — RIPv1 vs RIPv2 vs RIPng (7 marks) | Parameter | RIPv1 | RIPv2 | RIPng | |-----------|-------|-------|-------| | **Class Support** | Classful (A/B/C inferred from first octet) | Classless (carries subnet mask) | Classless (IPv6) | | **VLSM / CIDR** | Not supported | Supported | Supported | | **Authentication** | None | Plaintext / MD5 (RFC 2453) | None (relies on IPsec at network layer) | | **Update Delivery** | **Broadcast** to 255.255.255.255 | **Multicast** to 224.0.0.9 | **Multicast** to FF02::9 | | **Subnet Mask Field** | Absent | Present (4 bytes) | Present (prefix length) | | **Next-Hop Field** | Absent | Present (4 bytes) | Present (16 bytes IPv6) | | **Transport** | UDP 520 | UDP 520 | **UDP 521** | | **RFC** | 1058 | 2453 | 2080 | **Role of RIPng (1 mark):** RIPng (RIP next generation) is the IPv6 adaptation of RIP. It uses the same distance-vector algorithm and Bellman–Ford logic, but with IPv6 addresses (128-bit), 16-byte next-hop fields, prefix-length encoding, and operates on UDP port **521**. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — RIP (Module 3, PEMRT631)** - ✅ RIP is a **distance-vector, intra-AS (IGP)** protocol using **hop count** as its metric. - ✅ Operates over **UDP port 520** (RIPv1, RIPv2) / **UDP 521** (RIPng). - ✅ Maximum hop count = **15**; **16 = Infinity** = unreachable. - ✅ Update Timer = **30 s**; Invalid = **180 s**; Hold-down = **180 s**; Flush = **240 s**. - ✅ Core algorithm = **Bellman–Ford**: $D_x(y) = \min_v \{1 + D_v(y)\}$. - ✅ Count-to-infinity worst case = **15 × 30 s = 450 s** (NOT 480 s). - ✅ RIPv1 = classful + broadcast; **RIPv2 = classless + multicast 224.0.0.9**; RIPng = IPv6 + UDP 521. - ✅ RIPv2 added: **Subnet mask, Next-hop, Authentication, Route tag**. - ✅ Anti-loop mechanisms: **Split horizon, Poison reverse, Hold-down, Triggered updates, 15-hop cap**. - ✅ Each RIP packet carries up to **25 route entries** × 20 bytes each = 500 bytes + 4-byte header. - ✅ RIP is **slow to converge**; not suitable for large networks; modern networks use **OSPF/IS-IS** instead. - ✅ RIP exchanges the **entire routing table** every 30 s (no incremental updates except *triggered updates* on change).

topics.title: Intra-AS Routing in the Internet: OSPF
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT631 - NETWORK & DATA SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Intra-AS Routing in the Internet: OSPF

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**OSPF (Open Shortest Path First)** is a **link-state routing protocol** standardized as **RFC 2328** (OSPF Version 2) by the IETF. It is the most widely deployed **intra-Autonomous System (intra-AS)** routing protocol, used to dynamically compute the shortest path between any two routers within a single Autonomous System. OSPF uses **Dijkstra's Shortest Path First (SPF) algorithm** on a synchronized link-state database (LSDB) to build a loop-free routing topology.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** OSPF is classified as an **intra-domain (interior gateway) protocol**, meaning it operates *inside* a single Autonomous System, in contrast to inter-domain protocols like **BGP**.

## 1.2 The Five Defining Characteristics of OSPF

1. **Open Standard** – publicly available, vendor-neutral (non-proprietary, unlike Cisco's EIGRP).
2. **Link-State Paradigm** – every router maintains a complete map (graph) of the network topology.
3. **Classless Protocol** – supports **VLSM (Variable Length Subnet Masking)** and **CIDR**.
4. **Hierarchical Design** – the AS is divided into numbered **Areas** to scale to large networks.
5. **Cost-Based Metric** – path cost is determined by cumulative **interface cost** (inversely proportional to bandwidth).

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Google Maps of a University Campus"

Imagine your university campus as an Autonomous System:

- Every **department building** is a **router**.
- Every **road connecting two buildings** is a **link** with a known *traffic congestion* (cost).
- Every building puts up a **public noticeboard** showing *exactly which roads are open and how congested each one is*. This is the **LSA (Link State Advertisement)**.
- Periodically, the **security office** collects all noticeboards and gives every building a **complete, identical campus map** — this is the **Link-State Database (LSDB)**.
- Using this identical map, every building independently runs the **Dijkstra algorithm** to find the **shortest, least-congested path** to every other building — this is the **routing table**.

In this analogy:
- The **noticeboard** is the **LSA**.
- The **campus map copy** is the **LSDB**.
- The **shortest path calculation** is the **Dijkstra SPF algorithm**.
- A **cluster of buildings** managed under one administrative zone is an **OSPF Area**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Open"?** OSPF is "open" because its specification is published as a public IETF RFC, allowing any vendor to implement it (unlike Cisco's EIGRP which was, for many years, a proprietary Cisco-only protocol).

## 1.4 Distinguishing OSPF from Distance-Vector Protocols (RIP)

| Aspect | RIP (Distance-Vector) | OSPF (Link-State) |
|---|---|---|
| Algorithm | Bellman-Ford | Dijkstra (SPF) |
| Network Knowledge | Neighbor only | Entire topology |
| Convergence | Slow (count-to-infinity) | Fast (seconds) |
| Metric | Hop count (max 15) | **Cost** (bandwidth-based) |
| Hierarchy | Flat | **Hierarchical (Areas)** |
| Updates | Periodic (30 s) | Event-triggered LSAs |

This rapid convergence and global topology view is precisely why OSPF is preferred for large enterprise networks and ISP backbones.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 OSPF Operational Workflow (Five Logical Phases)

### Phase 1 — Neighbor Discovery (Hello Protocol)
- Routers send **OSPF Hello packets** every **10 seconds** on broadcast networks (30 s on NBMA/point-to-point).
- A Hello packet contains: **Router ID, Area ID, Hello/Dead intervals, Neighbor list, Subnet mask, DR/BDR priority**.
- If Router A's Hello lists Router B, and vice-versa, the routers transition to the **2-Way state** → they are *neighbors*.

### Phase 2 — Adjacency Formation & Database Synchronization
- Neighbors elect a **Designated Router (DR)** and **Backup DR (BDR)** on multi-access segments to reduce LSA flooding complexity from $O(n^2)$ to $O(n)$.
- Adjacencies form only between a router and the DR/BDR (and between DR and BDR).
- Routers exchange **Database Description (DBD) packets**, then **Link-State Requests (LSR)**, then **Link-State Updates (LSU)**, then **Link-State Acknowledgments (LSAck)** — synchronizing their LSDBs to **identical** copies.

### Phase 3 — SPF Computation (Dijkstra's Algorithm)
- Each router runs Dijkstra's algorithm on its LSDB with itself as the **root**, computing the **shortest path tree (SPT)**.
- From the SPT, the router generates entries in its **routing table (RIB)** for every destination network.

### Phase 4 — LSA Flooding (Reacting to Change)
- When a link changes, the detecting router generates a new **LSA**, floods it via **LSU packets** to all neighbors, which in turn flood to their neighbors — ensuring rapid, reliable dissemination.
- Every LSA is **acknowledged (LSAck)** to guarantee delivery.

### Phase 5 — LSA Aging and Refresh
- LSAs have a **MaxAge = 3600 seconds (1 hour)**.
- Every **30 minutes (LSRefreshTime)**, the originating router refreshes its own LSA to prevent expiration.

## 2.2 OSPF Cost Metric — The Central Equation

OSPF assigns a **cost** to each outgoing interface. The path cost is the **sum of outgoing interface costs** along the path. The default formula standardized by Cisco (and the KTU-expected convention) is:

$$\text{Cost} = \frac{\text{Reference Bandwidth}}{\text{Interface Bandwidth}}$$

The default **Reference Bandwidth** is **100 Mbps**. Any interface faster than 100 Mbps (e.g., 1 Gbps, 10 Gbps) will have a cost of 1, which is why modern deployments often raise the reference bandwidth to **100 Gbps** to retain path differentiation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Cases:**
> - If interface bandwidth = Reference bandwidth → Cost = **1**
> - Cost is an **integer** (rounded down to nearest whole number)
> - The **path cost** is the **sum of outgoing interface costs**

## 2.3 OSPF Hierarchical Architecture — Area Structure

To scale to thousands of routers, OSPF supports a **two-level hierarchy**:

- **Backbone Area (Area 0 / Transit Area):** The core area to which all other areas must connect. All inter-area traffic must traverse Area 0.
- **Non-Backbone Areas:** Connected to Area 0 via **Area Border Routers (ABRs)**. Each area maintains its own **separate LSDB**, reducing SPF computation overhead and LSA flooding scope.
- **Stub Area / Totally Stubby Area:** Areas that do **not** receive external (Type 5) LSAs — they use a **default route (0.0.0.0/0)** toward the ABR.
- **Not-So-Stubby Area (NSSA):** Allows redistribution of external routes (as Type 7 LSAs) which are converted to Type 5 at the ABR.

## 2.4 Router Roles in an OSPF Hierarchy

| Router Type | Definition | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Internal Router** | All interfaces in one area | Runs one SPF calculation |
| **Backbone Router** | At least one interface in Area 0 | Part of the core backbone |
| **Area Border Router (ABR)** | Interfaces in ≥ 2 areas, one being Area 0 | Summarizes & forwards inter-area LSAs (Type 3) |
| **Autonomous System Boundary Router (ASBR)** | Redistributes routes from another AS or protocol (e.g., BGP, static) | Injects External LSAs (Type 5) into OSPF |
| **Designated Router (DR)** | Elected on multi-access networks | Reduces LSA flooding adjacencies |
| **Backup DR (BDR)** | Hot-standby for DR | Takes over if DR fails |

## 2.5 OSPF Packet Types — The Five OSPF Message Types

| Type Code | Packet Name | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **1** | **Hello** | Discover / maintain neighbor relationships |
| **2** | **Database Description (DBD)** | Summarize LSDB contents for synchronization |
| **3** | **Link-State Request (LSR)** | Request specific LSAs from neighbor |
| **4** | **Link-State Update (LSU)** | Carry actual LSAs in response |
| **5** | **Link-State Acknowledgment (LSAck)** | Acknowledge receipt of LSUs |

> [!NOTE]
> An **LSU packet** can carry **multiple LSA instances**. The LSAck confirms reliable flooding.

## 2.6 LSA Types — The Eleven LSA Varieties

| Type | LSA Name | Generated By | Scope | Carries |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | Router LSA | Every router | Within area | Router's links and states |
| **2** | Network LSA | DR | Within area | Routers attached to the multi-access network |
| **3** | Summary LSA | ABR | Between areas | Inter-area network routes |
| **4** | ASBR Summary LSA | ABR | Between areas | Location of the ASBR |
| **5** | AS External LSA | ASBR | Throughout AS (except stub areas) | Routes external to the OSPF AS |
| **6** | Group Membership LSA | MOSPF | Within area | Multicast group membership |
| **7** | NSSA External LSA | ASBR (in NSSA) | Within NSSA | External routes in NSSA, converted to Type 5 at ABR |

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet — Complete Quick Reference

| Concept | Formula / Constant | Default Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Interface Cost | $\text{Cost} = \frac{\text{RefBW}}{\text{InterfaceBW}}$ | RefBW = 100 Mbps | dimensionless |
| Path Cost | $\text{PathCost} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{Cost}(E_i)$ | — | dimensionless |
| Hello Interval (Broadcast) | — | **10 s** | seconds |
| Dead Interval (Broadcast) | — | **40 s** (4 × Hello) | seconds |
| Hello Interval (NBMA/Point-to-Point) | — | **30 s** | seconds |
| Dead Interval (NBMA/Point-to-Point) | — | **120 s** | seconds |
| LSA MaxAge | — | **3600 s** (1 hour) | seconds |
| LSA RefreshTime | — | **1800 s** (30 min) | seconds |
| LSA MaxAge Diff | — | **900 s** (15 min) | seconds |
| Router ID Selection | Highest active IP, then highest loopback, then manual `router-id` | — | 32-bit |
| Dijkstra Relaxation | $d[v] = \min(d[v],\ d[u] + w(u,v))$ | — | weight |

## 2.8 Why OSPF Matters in Real Engineering

- **ISP Backbones:** OSPF runs as the **IGP of choice** for many Tier-1/Tier-2 ISPs due to its fast convergence and hierarchical scaling.
- **Enterprise Networks:** Multi-area OSPF enables thousands of routers across geographically distributed campuses.
- **Data Centers:** With appropriate tuning (e.g., reference-bandwidth, BFD, OSPFv3 for IPv6), OSPF supports modern DC fabrics.
- **Military / Government Networks:** Hierarchical OSPF areas map naturally to security and administrative zones.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example — Dijkstra's Algorithm on an OSPF LSDB

Consider a small OSPF topology with 4 routers (A, B, C, D) and the following link costs (derived from interface bandwidths):

| Link | Cost |
|---|---|
| A — B | 2 |
| A — C | 4 |
| B — C | 1 |
| B — D | 5 |
| C — D | 3 |

**Question:** Compute the shortest path from **A** to all other nodes using Dijkstra's algorithm (OSPF's SPF computation).

### Step 1 — Initialize the Data Structures

We maintain two sets:
- **S (visited/settled nodes):** nodes whose shortest path from A is finalized.
- **Q (unvisited/queue):** nodes whose tentative distance is known but not yet finalized.

We also maintain the array **d[v] = tentative shortest distance from A to v**, and **π[v] = predecessor of v on the shortest path**.

Initial state:
- $S = \emptyset$
- $Q = \{A, B, C, D\}$
- $d[A] = 0$, $d[B] = \infty$, $d[C] = \infty$, $d[D] = \infty$
- $\pi[A] = \text{NIL}$, $\pi[B] = \pi[C] = \pi[D] = \text{NIL}$

### Step 2 — Iteration 1: Settle Node A

Pick the node in $Q$ with the minimum tentative distance → **A** ($d[A] = 0$).
Move A from $Q$ to $S$. $S = \{A\}$.

Examine all neighbors of A: **B (cost 2)** and **C (cost 4)**.

For neighbor B:

$$
d[B] = \min(\infty,\ d[A] + w(A,B)) = \min(\infty,\ 0 + 2) = 2
$$

$$
\pi[B] = A
$$

For neighbor C:

$$
d[C] = \min(\infty,\ d[A] + w(A,C)) = \min(\infty,\ 0 + 4) = 4
$$

$$
\pi[C] = A
$$

Updated state: $d = [A:0,\ B:2,\ C:4,\ D:\infty]$.

### Step 3 — Iteration 2: Settle Node B

Pick minimum in $Q$ → **B** ($d[B] = 2$).
Move B to $S$. $S = \{A, B\}$.

Examine neighbors of B: **A (already settled, skip)**, **C (cost 1)**, **D (cost 5)**.

For neighbor C (relaxation):

$$
d[C] = \min(d[C],\ d[B] + w(B,C)) = \min(4,\ 2 + 1) = 3
$$

$$
\pi[C] = B \quad \text{(since 3 < 4, we update the predecessor)}
$$

For neighbor D:

$$
d[D] = \min(\infty,\ d[B] + w(B,D)) = \min(\infty,\ 2 + 5) = 7
$$

$$
\pi[D] = B
$$

Updated state: $d = [A:0,\ B:2,\ C:3,\ D:7]$.

### Step 4 — Iteration 3: Settle Node C

Pick minimum in $Q$ → **C** ($d[C] = 3$).
Move C to $S$. $S = \{A, B, C\}$.

Examine neighbors of C: **A (settled)**, **B (settled)**, **D (cost 3)**.

For neighbor D (relaxation):

$$
d[D] = \min(d[D],\ d[C] + w(C,D)) = \min(7,\ 3 + 3) = 6
$$

$$
\pi[D] = C \quad \text{(since 6 < 7, we update the predecessor)}
$$

Updated state: $d = [A:0,\ B:2,\ C:3,\ D:6]$.

### Step 5 — Iteration 4: Settle Node D

Pick minimum in $Q$ → **D** ($d[D] = 6$).
Move D to $S$. $S = \{A, B, C, D\}$. **Algorithm terminates** (all nodes settled).

### Step 6 — Construct Shortest Path Tree (SPT) and Routing Table

Final shortest path tree rooted at A:

| Destination | Shortest Path | Total Cost |
|---|---|---|
| B | A → B | **2** |
| C | A → B → C | **3** |
| D | A → B → C → D | **6** |

Routing table at Router A (intra-area, OSPF Type 1 + Type 2 LSAs):

| Destination Network | Next Hop | Outgoing Interface | Cost |
|---|---|---|---|
| B's network | B | Link A-B | 2 |
| C's network | B | Link A-B (then B-C) | 3 |
| D's network | B | Link A-B (then B-C, C-D) | 6 |

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — OSPF Neighbor State Machine Transitions

The OSPF neighbor adjacency progresses through **8 states**. The analytical matrix below maps each transition:

| From State | Event | To State | Packet Triggered |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Down** | Interface comes up; Hello sent | **Init** | Hello (out) |
| **Init** | Hello received listing own Router ID | **2-Way** | — |
| **2-Way** | Start adjacency (DR/BDR election complete) | **Exstart** | DBD (empty) |
| **Exstart** | Master/Slave elected, DBD sequence | **Exchange** | DBD (LSA headers) |
| **Exchange** | All DBDs exchanged; need specific LSAs | **Loading** | LSR |
| **Loading** | All LSRs satisfied; LSDBs identical | **Full** | LSU + LSAck |
| **Full** | — | **Full** (steady state) | — |
| **2-Way** | On point-to-point / DR-BDR link | **Exstart** (skip) | — |

> [!NOTE]
> On **point-to-point** links, the 2-Way state transitions directly to **Exstart** without waiting for DR/BDR election, since there is no multi-access contention.

## 3.3 Cost Computation Table (Reference Bandwidth = 100 Mbps)

| Interface Type | Bandwidth | Computed Cost = 100 / BW | Actual Cost Used |
|---|---|---|---|
| Serial | 1.544 Mbps | 100 / 1.544 ≈ 64.76 | **64** |
| Ethernet | 10 Mbps | 100 / 10 = 10 | **10** |
| Fast Ethernet | 100 Mbps | 100 / 100 = 1 | **1** |
| Gigabit Ethernet | 1000 Mbps | 100 / 1000 = 0.1 | **1** (minimum) |
| 10 Gigabit Ethernet | 10000 Mbps | 100 / 10000 = 0.01 | **1** (minimum) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The minimum cost is **1**. This is why modern OSPF deployments re-tune `auto-cost reference-bandwidth` to **100000** (100 Gbps) to restore proportional cost scaling on high-speed links.

## 3.4 Decision Logic — When Does a Router Recompute SPF?

A router **does not** recompute SPF on every Hello. SPF recomputation is triggered only when:
1. A **new LSA** arrives (Type 1 or Type 2) that changes the LSDB.
2. The LSA's age reaches **MaxAge** (3600 s) without refresh.
3. The router itself generates a new LSA (link up/down).

To prevent CPU thrashing, two timers throttle SPF:
- **SPF delay timer** (typically **5 seconds**): wait time after a topology change before running SPF.
- **SPF hold timer** (typically **10 seconds**): minimum gap between two consecutive SPF runs.

## 3.5 Reliability Analysis of LSA Flooding

Flooding reliability is achieved through:
- **Explicit Acknowledgment** — every LSU must be LSAck'd.
- **Retransmission** — if no LSAck within **RxmtInterval (5 s)**, retransmit the LSU.
- **Sequence Numbers** — each LSA has a 32-bit sequence number starting at 0x80000001, incremented on each new instance. Receivers accept only the highest sequence number for a given LSA type, Link-State ID, and Advertising Router.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Believing that OSPF uses periodic "full table" updates like RIP. **OSPF does NOT send periodic full routing updates.** It sends **event-triggered LSA refreshes every 30 minutes** plus **immediate LSAs on topology change**.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 OSPF Hierarchical Area Topology (Two-Level Hierarchy)

The diagram below shows a multi-area OSPF Autonomous System with the backbone (Area 0) at the core and three attached non-backbone areas (Area 1, Area 2, Area 3), interconnected via ABRs. An ASBR injects external (BGP/static) routes.

```mermaid
graph TD
    ASBR1[ASBR - Autonomous System Boundary Router]
    ABR12[ABR - Area Border Router 1-2]
    ABR23[ABR - Area Border Router 0-2]
    ABR01[ABR - Area Border Router 0-1]
    R0A[Backbone Router R0A]
    R0B[Backbone Router R0B]
    R1A[Internal Router R1A]
    R1B[Internal Router R1B]
    R2A[Internal Router R2A]
    R2B[Internal Router R2B]
    R3A[Internal Router R3A]
    EXT[External AS - BGP / Static Routes]

    subgraph Area0 [AREA 0 - Backbone Transit Area]
        ABR12
        ABR23
        ABR01
        R0A
        R0B
    end

    subgraph Area1 [AREA 1 - Standard Area]
        R1A
        R1B
        ABR01
    end

    subgraph Area2 [AREA 2 - Stub Area]
        R2A
        R2B
        ABR23
    end

    subgraph Area3 [AREA 3 - NSSA]
        R3A
        ASBR1
    end

    ASBR1 --- EXT
    ABR12 --- Area0
    ABR01 --- R0A
    R0A --- R0B
    R0B --- ABR23
    R1A --- ABR01
    R1B --- R1A
    R2A --- ABR23
    R2B --- R2A
    R3A --- ASBR1
    ABR01 --- ABR12
    ABR12 --- ABR23
```

## 4.2 OSPF Neighbor Adjacency State Machine

The state machine below shows all 8 OSPF neighbor states and the valid transitions triggered by Hello / DBD / LSR / LSU / LSAck packets.

```mermaid
graph TD
    S0[State Down - No Hellos received]
    S1[State Attempt - NBMA only - Unicast Hello sent]
    S2[State Init - Hello received without own RID]
    S3[State 2-Way - Bidirectional Hello confirmed]
    S4[State Exstart - Master-Slave negotiation]
    S5[State Exchange - DBD packets exchanged]
    S6[State Loading - LSR and LSU in progress]
    S7[State Full - LSDBs fully synchronized]

    S0 -->|Hello received| S2
    S1 -->|Hello received| S2
    S2 -->|Hello lists own RID| S3
    S3 -->|Adjacency start - point to point| S4
    S3 -->|DR-BDR or P2P link| S4
    S4 -->|Master-Slave elected| S5
    S5 -->|DBDs complete| S6
    S6 -->|LSR satisfied| S7
    S3 -->|On broadcast - not DR-BDR| S3
```

## 4.3 OSPF Packet Format — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The OSPF protocol uses a common **24-byte OSPF header** followed by a type-specific payload. The block diagram below illustrates the structure:

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph HDR [OSPF Header - 24 bytes]
        VER[Version - 1 byte - Version 2]
        TYPE[Type - 1 byte - 1 to 5]
        PKTLEN[Packet Length - 2 bytes]
        RID[Router ID - 4 bytes]
        AID[Area ID - 4 bytes]
        CHK[Checksum - 2 bytes]
        AUTHTYPE[Auth Type - 2 bytes]
        AUTH[Authentication - 8 bytes]
    end

    VER --> TYPE --> PKTLEN --> RID --> AID --> CHK --> AUTHTYPE --> AUTH

    subgraph PAY [Type-Specific Payload]
        HELLO[Hello - Type 1 - Network mask Hello Dead Options NBMA neighbors]
        DBD[DBD - Type 2 - LSA headers summary]
        LSR[LSR - Type 3 - Request specific LSAs]
        LSU[LSU - Type 4 - Full LSA instances]
        LSAck[LSAck - Type 5 - List of LSA headers]
    end

    AUTH --> PAY
```

## 4.4 LSA Flooding — Sequence of Events (Detailed)

The block diagram below captures the step-by-step LSA flooding protocol used in OSPF for topology-change propagation.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A1[Step 1 - Topology change detected - e.g. link down]
    A2[Step 2 - Detecting router generates new LSA - increments sequence number]
    A3[Step 3 - LSA flooded to all adjacent neighbors via LSU]
    A4[Step 4 - Each neighbor acknowledges with LSAck]
    A5[Step 5 - Neighbors install LSA into LSDB and re-flood]
    A6[Step 6 - All routers in area converge to identical LSDB]
    A7[Step 7 - Each router runs Dijkstra SPF on updated LSDB]
    A8[Step 8 - Updated routing table installed in RIB]
    A9[Step 9 - LSA aged - 30 min - refresh by originator]

    A1 --> A2 --> A3 --> A4 --> A5 --> A6 --> A7 --> A8 --> A9
```

## 4.5 DR / BDR Election — Block Diagram (Multi-Access Network)

```mermaid
graph TD
    E1[All OSPF routers on multi-access segment boot]
    E2[Each router sets DR priority from interface config - default 1 - range 0 to 255]
    E3[Routers with priority 0 never become DR-BDR]
    E4[Wait for OSPF Hellos to learn existing DR-BDR]
    E5[If no DR present - wait OSPF Wait Timer - 40 s]
    E6[Election - Highest priority wins - tie broken by highest Router ID]
    E7[Winners - Highest priority = DR - Second highest = BDR]
    E8[All other routers form Full adjacency only with DR and BDR]
    E9[All routers remain in 2-Way with each other - not Full]

    E1 --> E2 --> E3 --> E4 --> E5 --> E6 --> E7 --> E8 --> E9
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **DR/BDR election is non-preemptive.** Once a DR is elected, it remains the DR even if a higher-priority router comes online later. The DR only changes if it fails.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Explain why OSPF is preferred over RIP in modern enterprise networks. List any three advantages.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
OSPF is preferred over RIP due to:

1. **Faster Convergence:** OSPF uses link-state updates and Dijkstra's algorithm, converging in seconds, while RIP (distance-vector) suffers from slow convergence and count-to-infinity problems.
2. **No Hop Count Limitation:** RIP is limited to 15 hops, making it unusable for large networks. OSPF has no such artificial limit and uses a **cost metric** based on interface bandwidth.
3. **Hierarchical Scalability:** OSPF supports a **multi-area hierarchy** (Area 0 backbone + non-backbone areas), which limits LSA flooding scope and reduces routing overhead, allowing it to scale to thousands of routers.
4. **Classless & VLSM Support:** OSPF supports VLSM and CIDR natively, whereas older RIP versions do not.

*(Examiner's tip: 1 mark per advantage, max 3.)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Paper]**
**What is the role of the Designated Router (DR) in an OSPF multi-access network? Why is it needed?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
In multi-access networks (e.g., Ethernet), the **Designated Router (DR)** acts as a central point for LSA flooding and adjacency formation.

**Role:**
- The DR collects and distributes LSAs on behalf of all routers on the segment.
- All other routers (DROthers) form **Full adjacencies only with the DR and BDR**, and remain in **2-Way state** with each other.

**Why needed:**
- On a segment with $n$ routers, full-mesh adjacencies would be $O(n^2)$ — a heavy overhead. With a DR, only $O(n)$ adjacencies are needed, dramatically reducing LSA replication, bandwidth, and CPU usage.

*(Examiner's tip: Clearly state the $O(n^2)$ vs $O(n)$ distinction for full marks.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the different **OSPF packet types** with their purposes. Describe the **OSPF neighbor adjacency state machine** in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a suitable diagram, explain the **OSPF hierarchical area architecture**. Differentiate between **ABR**, **ASBR**, and **Internal Router**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part A(a) [7 marks]

**OSPF Packet Types (Table format, 3 marks):**

| Type | Packet | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Hello** | Discover and maintain neighbor relationships (sent every 10 s on broadcast). |
| 2 | **DBD (Database Description)** | Summarize the LSDB contents during initial synchronization. |
| 3 | **LSR (Link-State Request)** | Request specific LSAs the router is missing. |
| 4 | **LSU (Link-State Update)** | Carry the actual LSA instances in response. |
| 5 | **LSAck (LSAck)** | Acknowledge receipt of LSUs to ensure reliable flooding. |

**Neighbor State Machine (4 marks):**

1. **Down:** No Hellos received from neighbor. Initial state.
2. **Attempt (NBMA only):** Sending Hellos but no response.
3. **Init:** Hello received from neighbor, but neighbor's Hello does **not** list our Router ID.
4. **2-Way:** Bidirectional communication established — each side sees the other's Router ID in its Hello. On multi-access, this is the highest state for non-DR/BDR pairs.
5. **Exstart:** Master/Slave relationship negotiated; first empty DBD exchanged.
6. **Exchange:** DBDs exchanged containing LSA headers; each side builds a link-state request list.
7. **Loading:** LSR / LSU / LSAck exchange in progress to fill in missing LSAs.
8. **Full:** LSDBs fully synchronized; steady-state adjacency.

*(Examiner's tip: 3 marks for packet table, 4 marks for state machine with transitions.)*

#### Model Solution — Part A(b) [7 marks]

**Hierarchical Area Architecture (4 marks):**

OSPF supports a **two-level hierarchy**:
- **Area 0 (Backbone / Transit Area):** The central area. All inter-area traffic must pass through Area 0.
- **Non-Backbone Areas:** Areas 1, 2, 3, … connected to Area 0 via ABRs. Each area has its own LSDB, reducing LSA flooding and SPF computation overhead.
- **Stub Area:** Does not receive Type 5 (External) LSAs. Uses a default route to the ABR.
- **Totally Stubby Area (Cisco):** No Type 3, 4, or 5 LSAs. Only intra-area routes + default route.
- **NSSA (Not-So-Stubby Area):** Allows ASBR within the area, but external routes enter as Type 7 LSAs and are converted to Type 5 at the ABR.

**Router Roles (3 marks):**

| Router | Interfaces | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Internal Router** | All within one area | Single LSDB, single SPF run |
| **ABR** | ≥ 2 areas, one being Area 0 | Generates Type 3 Summary LSAs, performs inter-area summarization |
| **ASBR** | At least one, redistributes external routes | Generates Type 5 External LSAs |

*(Examiner's tip: Drawing the diagram with Area 0 at center and 2–3 attached areas earns 1 extra mark.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam - June 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain **link-state routing** with a comparison to **distance-vector routing**. Describe **Dijkstra's algorithm** step-by-step with a worked example. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compute the **OSPF shortest-path tree** for the following network using Router A as the root. **(7 marks)**

| Link | Cost |
|---|---|
| A — B | 4 |
| A — D | 2 |
| B — C | 3 |
| B — E | 5 |
| C — D | 1 |
| C — E | 2 |
| D — E | 6 |

#### Model Solution — Question B(a) [7 marks]

**Link-State vs Distance-Vector (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Link-State (OSPF) | Distance-Vector (RIP) |
|---|---|---|
| Network View | Complete topology (LSDB) | "Rumor" of distances to destinations |
| Algorithm | Dijkstra (SPF) | Bellman-Ford |
| Update Trigger | Topology change (LSA flooding) | Periodic (every 30 s) |
| Metric | Cost (bandwidth) | Hop count |
| Convergence | Fast (seconds) | Slow (minutes) |
| Loops | Loop-free after SPF run | Vulnerable to count-to-infinity |
