| CPU/Memory | Higher (LSDB + SPT) | Lower |

**Dijkstra's Algorithm (4 marks):**

**Step 1:** Initialize $d[v] = \infty$ for all $v \neq s$, $d[s] = 0$.

**Step 2:** Create two sets — $S$ (settled) and $Q$ (unsettled, $Q = V - S$).

**Step 3:** Repeat:
1. Pick $u \in Q$ with the smallest $d[u]$.
2. Move $u$ to $S$.
3. For every neighbor $v$ of $u$, apply **relaxation**:

$$d[v] = \min(d[v],\ d[u] + w(u,v))$$

and update $\pi[v] = u$ if the path improves.

**Step 4:** Until $Q$ is empty.

**Result:** A shortest-path tree rooted at $s$, giving the minimum cost to every node.

*(Examiner's tip: Mention the relaxation equation $d[v] = \min(d[v],\ d[u] + w(u,v))$ for full marks.)*

#### Model Solution — Question B(b) [7 marks]

| Step | Settle | Update d[B] | Update d[C] | Update d[D] | Update d[E] |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Init | — | ∞ | ∞ | ∞ | ∞ |
| 1 | **A** (d=0) | 4 (via A) | ∞ | 2 (via A) | ∞ |
| 2 | **D** (d=2) | 4 | 2+1=**3** (via D) | settled | 2+6=8 (via D) |
| 3 | **C** (d=3) | min(4, 3+3)=4 | settled | settled | min(8, 3+2)=**5** (via C) |
| 4 | **B** (d=4) | settled | settled | settled | min(5, 4+5)=5 |
| 5 | **E** (d=5) | settled | settled | settled | settled |

**Final Shortest-Path Tree from A:**

| Destination | Shortest Path | Total Cost |
|---|---|---|
| B | A → B | **4** |
| C | A → D → C | **3** |
| D | A → D | **2** |
| E | A → D → C → E | **5** |

**Routing Table at Router A:**

| Destination | Next Hop | Outgoing Interface | Cost |
|---|---|---|---|
| B's network | B | A-B | 4 |
| C's network | D | A-D | 3 |
| D's network | D | A-D | 2 |
| E's network | D | A-D | 3 |

*(Examiner's tip: For full marks, show the predecessor $\pi$ for each node: $\pi[B]=A,\ \pi[D]=A,\ \pi[C]=D,\ \pi[E]=C$.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in OSPF Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing LSAs and LSU:** Students often interchange "LSA" and "LSU". Remember: **LSA = the routing data; LSU = the packet that transports LSAs.** A single LSU packet can carry multiple LSAs.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the OSPF Hierarchy rule:** Inter-area traffic **MUST** pass through Area 0. A common mistake is drawing a direct link between Area 1 and Area 2, which is **not allowed** in OSPF (use a virtual link in exceptional cases).
>
> 3. **Cost formula confusion:** Writing $\frac{\text{Bandwidth}}{\text{Reference Bandwidth}}$ instead of the correct $\frac{\text{Reference Bandwidth}}{\text{Bandwidth}}$. The cost is **inversely** proportional to bandwidth — higher bandwidth = lower cost.
>
> 4. **Mixing up LSA Types:** Type 1 = Router LSA (intra-area), Type 2 = Network LSA (DR-generated), Type 3 = Summary (ABR-generated), Type 5 = External (ASBR-generated). Examiners frequently test this distinction.
>
> 5. **Dijkstra on wrong root:** The "shortest path" answer must be from the **given root router's** perspective. Computing shortest paths from an arbitrary node loses marks.
>
> 6. **Believing DR election is preemptive:** It is **NOT** — once elected, a DR remains DR until it fails. New routers with higher priority do not displace an existing DR.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **OSPF = Open Shortest Path First** — a **link-state**, **classless**, **hierarchical**, **open-standard** intra-AS routing protocol standardized in **RFC 2328**.
- [x] Uses **Dijkstra's SPF algorithm** on a synchronized **Link-State Database (LSDB)** to build a loop-free routing table.
- [x] **Metric:** $\text{Cost} = \frac{\text{Reference Bandwidth (100 Mbps default)}}{\text{Interface Bandwidth}}$. Minimum cost = 1.
- [x] **Hierarchy:** All non-backbone areas must connect to **Area 0 (backbone)** via an **ABR**.
- [x] **Five Router Roles:** Internal, Backbone, **ABR**, **ASBR**, **DR/BDR**.
- [x] **Five OSPF Packet Types:** Hello, DBD, LSR, LSU, LSAck.
- [x] **Key LSA Types:** Type 1 (Router), Type 2 (Network), Type 3 (Summary), Type 4 (ASBR Summary), Type 5 (External), Type 7 (NSSA External).
- [x] **Eight Neighbor States:** Down → Attempt → Init → 2-Way → Exstart → Exchange → Loading → Full.
- [x] **DR/BDR Election** is **non-preemptive**, based on **highest priority** (then highest Router ID).
- [x] **Hello timer:** 10 s (broadcast), 30 s (NBMA). **Dead timer:** 4 × Hello.
- [x] **LSA MaxAge:** 3600 s. **LSA Refresh:** every 1800 s (30 min).
- [x] **OSPF supports authentication** (Type 1 = Null, Type 2 = Plain text, MD5 / SHA — see RFC 5709 / RFC 7474).
- [x] **OSPFv3 (RFC 5340)** is the IPv6 version — it operates over IPv6 link-local addresses and uses a separate protocol for IPv4 (OSPFv2).
- [x] **Flooding is reliable** — every LSU must be explicitly LSAck'd; retransmission occurs after RxmtInterval (5 s) if unacknowledged.
- [x] **Hierarchical design benefits:** Limits LSA scope, reduces SPF computation, enables route summarization at ABRs, and isolates topology changes within an area.
- [x] **Real-world deployment:** OSPF is the most widely used IGP in enterprise networks and many ISP backbones, often paired with **BGP** at the AS boundary (BGP handles inter-AS, OSPF handles intra-AS).
- [x] **KTU keyword memory hook:** **"O.S.P.F. = Open, Shortest, Path, First"** → Open standard, Shortest path (Dijkstra), Path computed per link, First-choice IGP.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inter-AS Routing: BGP
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT631 - NETWORK & DATA SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Inter-AS Routing: BGP

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Border Gateway Protocol (BGP)** is the de facto **inter-domain (inter-AS) routing protocol** of the global Internet, standardized in **RFC 4271**. It is classified as a **Path Vector Protocol** (an extension of the Distance Vector paradigm) and operates over **TCP port 179**. Each BGP advertisement carries a complete list of Autonomous System (AS) numbers representing the path a route has traversed, which is the central mechanism preventing routing loops at the inter-AS scale.

BGP is a **policy-based routing protocol**, meaning reachability decisions are driven by commercial relationships, security policies, and administrative preferences rather than purely by shortest-path metrics.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The International Postal Union"** 📮
>
> Imagine every country as an Autonomous System (AS). Each country has its own internal postal rules (intra-domain protocols like **OSPF** or **RIP**). When you send a letter from Kerala (India) to Ontario (Canada), the local post office does *not* know the entire global road map — it just hands the letter to the next trusted national carrier. Each carrier stamps the letter with its country code before forwarding. The receiving end can read the **stamp trail** to:
> - Detect loops (a country will refuse to forward a letter it has already seen).
> - Enforce **policy** (e.g., "I will not forward mail to countries on my sanctions list").
>
> **BGP is the global stamping & forwarding system of the Internet.**

## 1.3 Why BGP Exists: The Need for Inter-AS Routing

The Internet is a **network of networks**. No single router knows the full topology. We need a protocol to:
1. Exchange reachability information between independently administered ASes.
2. Enforce **policy** (who is allowed to transit through whom, who is preferred).
3. Guarantee **scalability** for a graph of **>70,000 ASes** and **>900,000** advertised prefixes.
4. Provide **loop-free** paths via the AS_PATH attribute.

## 1.4 Key Terminology at a Glance

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **AS (Autonomous System)** | A collection of IP networks under a single administrative domain, identified by a globally unique **16-bit (extended to 32-bit) ASN**. |
| **eBGP (External BGP)** | BGP session between routers in **different** ASes. |
| **iBGP (Internal BGP)** | BGP session between routers **within the same** AS. |
| **BGP Speaker** | Any router running BGP. |
| **Peers / Neighbors** | Two BGP speakers exchanging routes over a TCP session. |
| **NLRI** | Network Layer Reachability Information (prefix + length). |
| **RIB** | Routing Information Base (Adj-RIBs-In, Loc-RIB, Adj-RIBs-Out). |
| **Prefix** | An IP address block (e.g., `192.168.0.0/16`). |
| **Route Reflector (RR)** | An iBGP optimization device that reduces the full-mesh requirement. |
| **Confederation** | An AS split into sub-ASes to reduce iBGP mesh size. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Architecture

BGP uses **TCP (port 179)** to establish a reliable, connection-oriented session between peers. This is fundamentally different from OSPF/RIP which run directly over IP.

### 2.1.1 The Two Logical Planes of BGP

1. **Control Plane** — Exchange of routing information (UPDATE messages).
2. **Data Plane** — Forwarding of actual user traffic (handled by the underlying FIB, populated via the Loc-RIB).

## 2.2 Types of BGP Sessions

### 2.2.1 eBGP (External BGP)
- Runs between routers in **different** ASes.
- Routers are usually **directly connected** (peering link).
- The **AS_PATH** is **prepended** (the local AS is added to the left) on every eBGP advertisement.
- **NEXT_HOP** is set to the IP of the advertising eBGP peer.

### 2.2.2 iBGP (Internal BGP)
- Runs between routers in the **same** AS.
- Used to redistribute eBGP-learned routes inside the AS so that every internal router can make consistent external forwarding decisions.
- **AS_PATH is NOT modified** when propagating via iBGP.
- A core rule: a route learned via iBGP **must NOT** be re-advertised to another iBGP peer (to prevent loops). Hence the **full-mesh requirement**: with $n$ iBGP speakers, you need $\binom{n}{2} = \frac{n(n-1)}{2}$ sessions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** Failure to maintain a full iBGP mesh inside a transit AS leads to **routing black-holes** and **sub-optimal hot-potato routing** — a frequent exam question.

## 2.3 BGP Path Attributes (The "DNA" of a Route)

BGP uses **path attributes** attached to each route. They are categorized as:

### 2.3.1 Well-Known Mandatory (must be recognized & sent by every implementation)
- **ORIGIN** — IGP, EGP, or INCOMPLETE.
- **AS_PATH** — Sequence of ASNs traversed. **Loop detection + path preference**.
- **NEXT_HOP** — IP address of the next-hop router.

### 2.3.2 Well-Known Discretionary (recognized, but optional to send)
- **LOCAL_PREF** — Local preference, used **only inside an AS**. Higher is preferred. Default = **100**.
- **ATOMIC_AGGREGATE** — Indicates that an aggregation has been performed.

### 2.3.3 Optional Transitive (may be passed even if unrecognized)
- **COMMUNITY** — Tag for shared policy decisions.
- **AGGREGATOR** — AS + Router ID of the router that performed aggregation.
- **MULTI_EXIT_DISC (MED)** — Hint to neighbors about preferred entry point. Lower is preferred.

## 2.4 BGP Path Selection Algorithm (The "Decision Process")

When multiple paths to the same prefix exist, BGP installs **exactly one best path** using this strict order:

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board-Favorite Question** ⭐
> Memorize this sequence — it appears almost every semester.

1. **Highest Weight** (Cisco proprietary, local to router). Default = 0.
2. **Highest LOCAL_PREF**. Default = 100.
3. **Locally originated** route (next hop = 0.0.0.0) wins.
4. **Shortest AS_PATH**.
5. **Lowest ORIGIN** code (IGP < EGP < INCOMPLETE).
6. **Lowest MED** (only compared between routes from the same neighbor AS).
7. **eBGP over iBGP** (prefer external path).
8. **Lowest IGP cost to NEXT_HOP**.
9. **Oldest path** (for stability).
10. **Lowest Router ID**, then **lowest neighbor IP**.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet

| Symbol | Formula / Definition | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| $n(n-1)/2$ | iBGP full-mesh sessions for $n$ speakers | Scalability check |
| $\vert \text{AS\_PATH} \vert$ | Number of ASNs in path | Loop detection / length |
| $LP \in [0, 4{,}294{,}967{,}295]$ | 32-bit unsigned Local Preference | Outbound traffic engineering |
| $MED \in [0, 4{,}294{,}967{,}295]$ | 32-bit unsigned (default = 0) | Inbound traffic engineering hint |
| $\text{AS\_PATH prepended}$ | $\text{AS\_PATH} = [AS_{\text{local}}] \cup \text{AS\_PATH}_{\text{old}}$ | Route de-preferencing (lengthen path) |
| $R = 1 - \frac{M}{M + N}$ | Route Reflector redundancy ratio (M mirrors of N clients) | RR design |
| $\text{Update}_{i+1} \le T_{\text{KEEPALIVE}} \cdot k$ | BGP session holds active with $k$ keepalives skipped | Stale-session detection |

> **Note on pipes in tables:** Per the markdown safeguard, the absolute value / cardinality is written using `\vert` (rendered as $\vert \cdot \vert$) to avoid breaking the table grid.

## 2.6 Real-World Utility

BGP powers **every inter-provider connection on the Internet**. ISPs, CDNs (Cloudflare, Akamai), universities, and enterprises all run BGP. Notable real-world incidents like the **2017 Google BGP hijack** (leaked prefix caused global service disruption) and the **2008 Pakistan Telecom YouTube blackhole** underscore BGP's **operational fragility** and the importance of **RPKI (Resource Public Key Infrastructure)** as a security overlay.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — iBGP Full-Mesh Session Count

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (commonly asked for 7 marks):** "An ISP has deployed 12 internal BGP routers. How many iBGP sessions are required in a full-mesh deployment? If they migrate to a Route Reflector architecture with 2 RRs and 10 clients, how many sessions are needed?"

### Step 1: Recall the constraint
A full-mesh iBGP topology is required to prevent the rule "**iBGP-learned routes must not be re-advertised to iBGP peers**" from blocking route propagation.

### Step 2: General formula
For $n$ speakers, the number of unordered pairs is given by the combination:

$$
S_{\text{mesh}}(n) = \binom{n}{2} = \frac{n(n-1)}{2}
$$

### Step 3: Substitute $n = 12$

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{mesh}}(12) &= \frac{12 \cdot (12 - 1)}{2} \\
&= \frac{12 \cdot 11}{2} \\
&= \frac{132}{2} \\
&= 66 \text{ sessions}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** A pure full-mesh requires **66 TCP sessions** — a heavy burden on router CPU/memory.

### Step 4: Route Reflector design
A Route Reflector (RR) re-adverts iBGP-learned routes among its clients, breaking the loop-prevention rule. With **2 RRs** (for redundancy) and **10 clients**:
- Each client peers with **both RRs** → $10 \times 2 = 20$ sessions.
- The two RRs peer with **each other** → $1$ session.
- (We assume RRs are not clients of each other to avoid single point of failure — a common best practice).

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{RR}} &= (N_{\text{clients}} \times N_{\text{RRs}}) + S_{\text{amongRRs}} \\
&= (10 \times 2) + \binom{2}{2} \\
&= 20 + 1 \\
&= 21 \text{ sessions}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5: Reduction analysis

$$
\Delta S = 66 - 21 = 45 \text{ sessions saved} \quad\Rightarrow\quad \text{Reduction} = \frac{45}{66} \times 100\% \approx 68.2\%
$$

> **Engineering Insight:** RR design trades **full-mesh complexity** for a **single point of failure** (mitigated by using dual RRs per cluster).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — AS_PATH Loop Detection

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** "AS 65010 receives a BGP UPDATE from AS 65020 advertising prefix `203.0.113.0/24`. The AS_PATH attribute reads `65020 65030 65040 65010 65050`. Should AS 65010 accept the route? Justify using the loop-prevention rule."

### Step 1: Rule statement
On receiving an UPDATE, the BGP speaker scans the **AS_PATH**. If the **local ASN** appears anywhere in the path, the route is **rejected** as a loop.

### Step 2: Apply the rule

$$
\text{AS\_PATH} = [65020 \to 65030 \to 65040 \to 65010 \to 65050]
$$

### Step 3: Membership test
Let $L = \{65010\}$ be the set of local ASNs. The condition for rejection is:

$$
L \cap \text{AS\_PATH} \neq \emptyset
$$

### Step 4: Evaluate

$$
\begin{aligned}
L \cap \text{AS\_PATH} &= \{65010\} \cap \{65020, 65030, 65040, 65010, 65050\} \\
&= \{65010\} \neq \emptyset
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5: Conclusion
Since the local ASN **65010 is present** in the AS_PATH, the route **must be rejected**. The AS_PATH vector has effectively come back to its origin, indicating a routing loop.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — BGP Best-Path Selection Walkthrough

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A router in AS 100 has three candidate paths to `198.51.100.0/24`. Apply the decision process and identify the winner.

| Candidate | LOCAL_PREF | AS_PATH | ORIGIN | MED | Type |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Path A | 150 | `100 200 300` | IGP | — | iBGP |
| Path B | 150 | `100 400` | EGP | 50 | eBGP |
| Path C | 200 | `100 500 600 700` | INCOMPLETE | — | iBGP |

### Step-by-step elimination

**Step 1 — Weight:** Assume all weights = 0 (default). **Tie — proceed.**

**Step 2 — LOCAL_PREF:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Path A}: LP &= 150 \\
\text{Path B}: LP &= 150 \\
\text{Path C}: LP &= 200
\end{aligned}
$$

Highest LOCAL_PREF is **Path C (200)**. Path C is the best path. (Steps 3-10 are bypassed.)

### Verification of Path C's full attribute set

$$
\text{Path C} = \left\{ LP=200,\; \text{AS\_PATH}=[100\ 500\ 600\ 700],\; \text{ORIGIN}=\text{INCOMPLETE} \right\}
$$

> **Selected Path: C** — driven entirely by the highest LOCAL_PREF. The longer AS_PATH and weaker ORIGIN are irrelevant because the decision was finalized at Step 2.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Each Attribute

| Engineering Goal | Attribute to Manipulate | Direction | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| Influence **outbound** traffic from your AS | `LOCAL_PREF` | Within iBGP, higher wins | Prefer ISP-A over ISP-B for all internal users |
| Influence **inbound** traffic into your AS | `AS_PATH prepending` | Lengthen path on outbound eBGP | Make your route less attractive to neighbor |
| Influence **inbound** traffic (subtle) | `MED` | Sent to single neighbor AS | Tell AS 200 to use AS 100 as entry point |
| Aggregate / summarize routes | `AGGREGATE` + `ATOMIC_AGGREGATE` | Reduce routing table size | Announce `10.0.0.0/8` instead of /16s |
| Tag routes for shared policy | `COMMUNITY` | Cross-vendor policy signaling | NO_EXPORT, NO_ADVERTISE, custom values |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 BGP Finite State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> idle
    idle --> connect : manualStart / TCP attempt
    connect --> active : TCP failure
    connect --> openSent : TCP success + OPEN sent
    active --> openSent : TCP established
    active --> idle : retry timeout exceeded
    openSent --> openConfirm : OPEN received + KEEPALIVE sent
    openConfirm --> established : KEEPALIVE received
    established --> idle : NOTIFICATION or fatal error
    established --> established : UPDATE / KEEPALIVE exchange
```

**Interpretation of States:**

| State | Meaning |
|---|---|
| `idle` | BGP is disabled; no resources allocated. |
| `connect` | Waiting for TCP connection to complete. |
| `active` | TCP retry; trying to re-establish session. |
| `openSent` | OPEN message sent, awaiting peer's OPEN. |
| `openConfirm` | OPEN received; awaiting peer's first KEEPALIVE. |
| `established` | Peering up; UPDATE/KEEPALIVE/NOTIFICATION exchange is live. |

---

## 4.2 eBGP vs iBGP Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph as1["AS 100 (ISP-North)"]
        r1["R1 (eBGP to AS200)"]
        r2["R2 (iBGP)"]
        r3["R3 (iBGP)"]
    end
    subgraph as2["AS 200 (Customer)"]
        r4["R4 (eBGP to AS100)"]
    end

    r1 -. "eBGP (TCP 179)" .-> r4
    r1 --- r2
    r1 --- r3
    r2 --- r3
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **solid lines** inside AS 100 are **iBGP sessions** (full mesh among R1, R2, R3).
- The **dashed line** between R1 and R4 is the **eBGP session** crossing the AS boundary.

---

## 4.3 BGP UPDATE Message Processing Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    recv["Receive UPDATE over TCP port 179"] --> parse["Parse NLRI and Path Attributes"]
    parse --> loopchk{"Local ASN in AS_PATH?"}
    loopchk -- "YES" --> drop1["Reject Route / Log Loop"]
    loopchk -- "NO" --> policyin["Apply Import Policy (filter lists, route-maps)"]
    policyin --> ribin["Store in Adj-RIBs-In"]
    ribin --> decision["Run Decision Process (Best Path Algorithm)"]
    decision --> locrib["Install in Loc-RIB"]
    locrib --> ibgpspread{"iBGP Peer?"}
    ibgpspread -- "YES" --> ibgpprop["Propagate to other iBGP speakers (no AS_PATH change)"]
    ibgpspread -- "NO" --> policyout["Apply Export Policy"]
    policyout --> ebgpprop["Prepend local ASN, then advertise to eBGP peer"]
```

---

## 4.4 BGP Best-Path Decision Funnel

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Candidate Paths to Prefix P"] --> B{"Step 1: Weight"}
    B -- "Highest wins" --> Z["BEST PATH"]
    B -- "Tie" --> C{"Step 2: LOCAL_PREF"}
    C -- "Highest wins" --> Z
    C -- "Tie" --> D{"Step 3: Locally Originated"}
    D -- "Yes wins" --> Z
    D -- "Tie" --> E{"Step 4: Shortest AS_PATH"}
    E -- "Shortest wins" --> Z
    E -- "Tie" --> F{"Step 5: Lowest ORIGIN"}
    F -- "IGP preferred" --> Z
    F -- "Tie" --> G["Continue to MED, eBGP/iBGP, IGP cost..."]
    G --> Z
```

---

## 4.5 BGP Message Types (Summary Table)

| Message Type | Code | Purpose | Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|
| **OPEN** | 1 | Establishes BGP session; negotiates hold-time, ASN, BGP-ID. | Once at start |
| **UPDATE** | 2 | Advertises new / withdrawn routes and their attributes. | On change |
| **KEEPALIVE** | 3 | Confirms session liveness. | Every **60s** (default) |
| **NOTIFICATION** | 4 | Reports an error and tears down session. | On error |
| **ROUTE-REFRESH** *(optional)* | 5 | Requests re-advertisement of Adj-RIBs-Out. | On policy change |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between eBGP and iBGP. State at least three points.**

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | eBGP | iBGP |
|---|---|---|
| **Scope** | Between different ASes. | Within the same AS. |
| **AS_PATH handling** | Local ASN is prepended. | AS_PATH is unchanged. |
| **Topology** | Peering is usually direct (single hop). | Requires full-mesh (or RR / confederation). |
| **NEXT_HOP** | Set to peer's interface IP. | Preserved from original eBGP advertisement. |
| **Loop prevention** | Uses AS_PATH inspection. | Uses split-horizon + full-mesh. |
| **Default Admin Distance** | **20** (Cisco). | **200** (Cisco). |

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**What is the role of the AS_PATH attribute in BGP? How does it prevent routing loops?**

**Model Answer:**

The **AS_PATH** is a well-known mandatory BGP attribute that records the **sequence of Autonomous System numbers** a route advertisement has traversed, with the **most recent AS prepended to the left**.

**Roles:**
1. **Loop Prevention:** When a BGP speaker receives an UPDATE, it scans the AS_PATH. If its **own ASN** is present, the route is rejected, since it would mean the route has already visited this AS — a routing loop.
2. **Path Selection (Step 4 of the decision process):** Routes with **shorter AS_PATHs** are preferred, allowing ASes to influence inbound traffic by artificially lengthening (prepending) the path.
3. **Policy Tool:** ASes can filter routes based on AS_PATH regular expressions (e.g., reject routes traversing a specific transit provider).

**Example:** If AS 65010 sees an UPDATE with `AS_PATH = 65020 65030 65010`, it will **drop the route** because 65010 already appears in the path.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall** ⚠️
> When drawing the BGP state machine, students often **forget to label the trigger events** (manualStart, TCP failure, etc.) and lose 2 marks. Always show **inputs** above the arrows. Also, when writing the path-selection algorithm, do **not** skip steps — order matters; mixing up "shortest AS_PATH" with "lowest MED" will be penalized.

---

### 🔹 Question A (14 Marks)

**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3]`**
**(a)** Explain the architecture of BGP. Describe the **four BGP message types** in detail. *(7 marks)*
**(b)** Illustrate and explain the **BGP finite state machine**. Mention all six states and the messages exchanged between them. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — (a) BGP Architecture & Message Types

**BGP Architecture:**
BGP is a **path-vector protocol** that runs over **TCP port 179**, providing reliable, connection-oriented transport. Each AS contains one or more **BGP speakers** that:
1. Establish peerings with BGP speakers in other ASes (**eBGP**).
2. Distribute externally-learned routes to internal BGP speakers (**iBGP**).
3. Maintain three RIBs:
   - **Adj-RIBs-In** — received, unprocessed routes.
   - **Loc-RIB** — best routes selected by the decision process.
   - **Adj-RIBs-Out** — routes to advertise to peers.

**BGP Message Types:**

1. **OPEN Message (Code 1):** Sent after the TCP connection is established. Carries:
   - BGP Version (currently 4).
   - Local AS Number.
   - Hold Time (default **180 s**).
   - BGP Identifier (Router ID).
   - Optional Parameters (e.g., multiprotocol extensions).

2. **UPDATE Message (Code 2):** Carries:
   - **Withdrawn Routes** — prefixes being withdrawn.
   - **Path Attributes** — ORIGIN, AS_PATH, NEXT_HOP, LOCAL_PREF, MED, etc.
   - **NLRI** — new reachable prefixes.
   A single UPDATE can advertise one feasible path to multiple prefixes, but it should contain prefixes sharing the same path attributes.

3. **KEEPALIVE Message (Code 3):** A 19-byte header-only message. Sent every **60 s** (one-third of the default hold time) to confirm session liveness. If **three consecutive KEEPALIVE messages** are missed, the peer is considered down.

4. **NOTIFICATION Message (Code 4):** Signals an error and **immediately closes** the session. Contains:
   - **Error Code** (e.g., Message Header Error, OPEN Message Error, UPDATE Message Error, Hold Timer Expired, Finite State Machine Error, Cease).
   - **Error Subcode.**
   - **Data field** for diagnostics.

> **Note:** A fifth optional message, **ROUTE-REFRESH (Code 5)**, allows a peer to request re-advertisement of all routes after a policy change.

#### Model Solution — (b) BGP Finite State Machine

The BGP FSM has **six states**:

1. **Idle** — Initial state. BGP waits for a `Start` event (administrative or peer restart). No resources allocated; rejects all incoming connections.

2. **Connect** — Waits for TCP connection to complete. If TCP fails, transitions to **Active** state. If TCP succeeds, sends OPEN → **OpenSent**.

3. **Active** — Indicates a retry scenario. BGP continuously attempts to establish TCP. On success, sends OPEN. Persistent failure to connect (after retries) returns BGP to **Idle**.

4. **OpenSent** — OPEN message has been sent. Awaits OPEN from peer. On receiving valid OPEN, sends KEEPALIVE and transitions to **OpenConfirm**. Errors trigger NOTIFICATION.

5. **OpenConfirm** — Awaits peer's KEEPALIVE. Once received, moves to **Established**. If the hold timer expires, NOTIFICATION is sent.

6. **Established** — Peering is live. UPDATEs are exchanged; KEEPALIVE messages maintain liveness. Any NOTIFICATION or fatal error tears the session down to **Idle**.

**Key events triggering transitions:**
- `manualStart` / `automaticStart` — Idle → Connect.
- `TcpConnectionConfirmed` — Connect/OpenSent progression.
- `TcpCRefused`, `TcpError` — back to Connect / Active.
- `BGPOpen` / `KeepAliveMsg` / `NotifyMsg` — drive the state machine.

---

### 🔹 Question B (14 Marks)

**`[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3]`**
**(a)** List and explain the **BGP path attributes** with their categories. Why is AS_PATH considered mandatory? *(7 marks)*
**(b)** Describe the **BGP path selection algorithm** in detail. Apply it to determine the best path for the following candidates to prefix `192.0.2.0/24`: *(7 marks)*

| Path | Weight | LOCAL_PREF | AS_PATH | ORIGIN | MED | Type |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| P1 | 0 | 100 | `100 200 300 400` | IGP | 50 | eBGP |
| P2 | 100 | 100 | `100 500 600` | EGP | 20 | eBGP |
| P3 | 0 | 150 | `100 700 800 900 1000` | IGP | — | iBGP |

#### Model Solution — (a) BGP Path Attributes

BGP path attributes are categorized into four classes:

**1. Well-Known Mandatory** — must be present in every UPDATE:
- **ORIGIN** — `IGP` (route originated via network statement), `EGP` (legacy), `INCOMPLETE` (redistributed). Lower origin code is preferred.
- **AS_PATH** — sequence of ASNs traversed.
- **NEXT_HOP** — IP of next-hop router.

**2. Well-Known Discretionary** — must be recognized, but may be omitted:
- **LOCAL_PREF** — propagated iBGP-wide; used to choose outbound exit point.
- **ATOMIC_AGGREGATE** — flags that an aggregate was performed.

**3. Optional Transitive** — passed on even if the receiving router doesn't understand:
- **COMMUNITY** — multi-purpose tag.
- **AGGREGATOR** — origin of aggregation.
- **MULTI_EXIT_DISC (MED)** — non-transitive in practice, optional transitive by spec.

**4. Optional Non-Transitive** — discarded if not understood.

**Why AS_PATH is mandatory:**
BGP is the protocol that glues together **independently administered ASes**. Without the AS_PATH:
- **Loop detection would be impossible** at the inter-AS scale, since distance-vector poison reverse cannot work across administrative boundaries.
- **Path-preference signaling (Step 4 of decision process)** would not exist, leaving no clean way for an AS to indicate a longer / less-preferred route.
- **Policy expression via AS-path filters and regular expressions** would not work.

Hence the AS_PATH is **non-negotiable and must be present in every BGP UPDATE.**

#### Model Solution — (b) Path Selection Walk-through

We apply the BGP decision process step by step:

**Step 1 — Weight:** $\text{P1}=0,\ \text{P2}=100,\ \text{P3}=0$. **P2 wins (100).**

Once the highest-weight path is identified, the algorithm **terminates** with **P2 as the best path.** The remaining steps are not evaluated for this question's purpose.

**Final Best Path: P2** — driven entirely by the locally configured Weight (Cisco proprietary). The longer AS_PATH of P2 (3 ASNs vs. 4 for P1) is irrelevant because Step 1 short-circuits all subsequent comparisons.

> **Engineering Note:** In multi-vendor networks (Juniper, Nokia), there is no Weight. The equivalent is a **"primary"** or local-preference manipulation, but Weight is the canonical first filter in Cisco IOS.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ✅

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist**

- **BGP = Path Vector Protocol** operating over **TCP port 179**.
- **eBGP** is for inter-AS, **iBGP** is for intra-AS redistribution.
- **AS_PATH** is the cornerstone: provides **loop prevention** + **path preference**.
- **NEXT_HOP** attribute differs in eBGP vs iBGP — this is a **top exam trap**.
- **Full-mesh iBGP** requires $n(n-1)/2$ sessions; mitigate with **Route Reflectors** or **Confederations**.
- **LOCAL_PREF** influences **outbound** traffic; **AS_PATH prepending** and **MED** influence **inbound** traffic.
- **BGP path selection order:** Weight → LOCAL_PREF → Locally Originated → Shortest AS_PATH → Lowest ORIGIN → Lowest MED → eBGP over iBGP → Lowest IGP cost → Oldest → Lowest Router-ID.
- **Four message types:** OPEN, UPDATE, KEEPALIVE (every 60s), NOTIFICATION.
- **Six FSM states:** Idle, Connect, Active, OpenSent, OpenConfirm, Established.
- **Hold Time default = 180 s**; KEEPALIVE default = 60 s (one-third of hold time).
- **Route Aggregation** reduces table size; **ATOMIC_AGGREGATE** flag indicates loss of path information.
- **Security:** BGP has **no built-in authentication** of UPDATE contents — that's why **RPKI / ROA** and **BGPsec** are the modern security overlays.
- **Real-world incidents** (Pakistan/YouTube 2008, Google 2017) make BGP security a **favourite viva question**.

> 🎯 **Pro Tip for KTU Exam:** Always draw the **state machine** with **triggering events labeled on arrows**. Always write the **path-selection algorithm as a numbered list** — partial credit is preserved if you forget a later step.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Broadcast Routing Algorithms and Multicast
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT631 - NETWORK & DATA SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Broadcast Routing Algorithms and Multicast

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Broadcasting in a Network?

In the context of computer networks, **broadcasting** is the process of delivering a single packet from a source node to **every other node** in the network. A broadcast packet is typically addressed to a special *broadcast address* (e.g., `255.255.255.255` in IPv4 or `ff02::1` in IPv6).

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Forouzan / Tanenbaum Standard):**
> *Broadcast Routing* is the delivery of a message (or packet) from a single source to **all possible destinations** in the network simultaneously, using efficient forwarding strategies so as to minimise duplicate copies and bandwidth wastage.

### 🌟 Real-World Analogy (Intuition)

Imagine you are standing in the centre of a large **classroom** and the teacher shouts a single announcement:

| Communication Type | Real-World Analogy | Network Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| **Unicast** | Whispering a secret to one friend | 1 sender → 1 receiver |
| **Broadcast** | Teacher shouting an announcement to the whole class | 1 sender → ALL nodes |
| **Multicast** | Teacher announcing "All CSE students, submit your assignments" | 1 sender → SUBSET (Group) |

The teacher doesn't run around the class speaking to each student one-by-one — that would be inefficient **unicast replication**. Instead, one announcement reaches everyone through *controlled propagation*.

## 1.2 What is Multicasting?

**Multicasting** is a more selective form of communication where the source sends **a single copy** of the packet, and the network is responsible for **duplicating it only at branch points** so that it reaches only the members of a specific *multicast group*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Multicast Group:** A logical collection of hosts (identified by a Class D IPv4 address in the range **224.0.0.0 – 239.255.255.255**, or an IPv6 `ff00::/8` prefix) that have expressed interest in receiving a particular data stream.

## 1.3 Why Not Just Flood or Repeat Unicast?

Three naive approaches exist, each with severe limitations:

1. **Repeat Unicast:** Source sends N copies — wastes **N× bandwidth** on the first link alone.
2. **Uncontrolled Flooding:** Every node re-broadcasts every packet — causes **broadcast storms**, infinite loops, and **duplicate packet explosions**.
3. **Multihop Broadcast without control:** Generates **2^(n-1)** duplicates in the worst case for *n* nodes.

> [!WARNING]
> KTU frequently asks: *"Why is uncontrolled flooding unacceptable in modern IP networks?"* — The answer must highlight **loops, congestion, and exponential duplicate generation**.

## 1.4 The Three Categories of Broadcast Routing Algorithms

| Category | Key Idea | Major Algorithms |
|---|---|---|
| **Naïve / Flooding-based** | Forward everywhere with minimal control | Simple Flooding |
| **Controlled Flooding** | Suppress duplicates using TTL / Sequence Numbers | TTL-based, Sequence-number controlled |
| **Tree-based (Efficient)** | Pre-compute a *Spanning Tree* and forward only along it | Spanning Tree Broadcast, RPF, RPB, TRPB, RPM |

These will be analysed exhaustively in SECTION 2.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ⚙️ SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Algorithm 1 — Simple Flooding

**Operational Logic:**
- Source transmits the packet to **every directly-connected neighbour**.
- Every receiving node, upon receiving a packet for the first time, **re-broadcasts** it to all its other neighbours.
- If a node receives a duplicate, it **discards** it.

**Why it fails (the 'How' & 'Why' behind the failure):**
- **Loop Problem:** A → B → A → B → ∞ (packets keep circulating forever).
- **Bandwidth Collapse:** Generates massive duplicate traffic.
- **No Quality of Service (QoS)** guarantee.

## 2.2 Algorithm 2 — Controlled Flooding (Two Sub-Variants)

### Variant A: Hop-Count / TTL Controlled Flooding

**Logic Steps:**
1. The source sets the **Time-To-Live (TTL)** field in the IP header to a small value (typically the **diameter** of the network).
2. Each router decrements TTL by **1** on every hop.
3. If TTL reaches **0**, the router **drops the packet**.
4. Otherwise, it forwards the packet to all neighbours.

### Variant B: Sequence-Number Controlled Flooding

**Logic Steps:**
1. Source assigns a unique **sequence number** to each broadcast packet.
2. Every node maintains a list `Seen[Source, SequenceNo]`.
3. If `(Source, SeqNo)` already exists in `Seen` → **DISCARD**.
4. Otherwise, store it and re-broadcast.

> [!NOTE]
> **Limitation of Controlled Flooding:** Both variants still produce **far more packets than necessary** because the underlying topology may contain many cycles.

## 2.3 Algorithm 3 — Spanning Tree Broadcast

**Core Principle:**
A *Spanning Tree* is a subset of network links that:
- Connects **all** nodes,
- Contains **no cycles**,
- Has exactly **(N − 1)** edges for N nodes.

**Logic:**
1. Run a distributed algorithm (e.g., **BFS from source**) to construct a spanning tree rooted at the source.
2. Forward the broadcast packet **only** along the tree edges.

**Advantage:** Each node receives the packet exactly **once**, along a single path → optimal duplicate count.

**Disadvantage:** All routers must maintain the spanning tree; not scalable for dynamic topologies without re-computation.

## 2.4 Algorithm 4 — Reverse Path Forwarding (RPF) — *Most Important for KTU*

**Intuition:** "A packet is accepted *only* if it arrives on the link that is the **shortest path back to the source**."

**Logic Steps:**
1. Every router knows its **shortest path to the source** (via unicast routing table — e.g., Dijkstra/Link State).
2. When a packet arrives on interface `I`:
   - If `I` is the **same interface** the router would use to send to the source → **ACCEPT & FORWARD**.
   - Otherwise → **DISCARD** (it is treated as a duplicate).
3. The accepted packet is forwarded on **all other interfaces** except the one it arrived on.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Result:** RPF eliminates the *loop problem*. However, it is *not* optimal — a router may still forward to non-tree links, generating some duplicates.

## 2.5 Algorithm 5 — Reverse Path Broadcasting (RPB)

RPB is an **enhancement of RPF** where duplicates are avoided by ensuring only the **designated parent** of each LAN forwards the broadcast.

**Logic:**
- For each LAN/broadcast link, only the router that has the **shortest path to the source** (the *parent*) actually transmits.
- Other routers with equal distance remain silent (using tie-breaking rules).

This produces a true broadcast tree (effectively a shortest-path tree).

## 2.6 Algorithm 6 — Truncated Reverse Path Broadcasting (TRPB)

**Logic:**
- Some hosts do **not** need the broadcast (e.g., they are not part of the target group).
- Hosts that are **not members** send an **IGMP (Internet Group Management Protocol)** "leave" signal.
- Routers keep track of group membership and **prune** (silently drop) subtrees with no interested receivers.

## 2.7 Algorithm 7 — Reverse Path Multicasting (RPM)

RPM is the **fully optimised** version:
- Start with RPB flooding.
- Routers with **no interested downstream receivers** send **prune messages** back up the tree.
- Pruned branches are remembered via **soft-state timers**; if a receiver later joins, the branch is **grafted** back.

RPM evolves into modern protocols: **DVMRP**, **PIM-DM**, etc.

## 2.8 🌳 Multicast Routing — Source-Based vs Shared Trees

| Property | Source-Based Tree (SPT) | Group-Shared Tree (RPT/CBT) |
|---|---|---|
| **Number of trees** | One per (Source, Group) pair | One per Group |
| **Root** | Source node | A pre-designated **Core / Rendezvous Point (RP)** |
| **Optimality** | Optimal paths from source | Sub-optimal (longer paths possible) |
| **Scalability** | Poor for many senders | Excellent for many senders / few groups |
| **Example Protocols** | DVMRP, PIM-DM, PIM-SSM | PIM-SM, CBT |
| **State stored** | O(S × G) per router | O(G) per router |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Favourite Question:** *"Differentiate Source-Specific Tree and Shared Tree with examples."* — Use the table above as your answer skeleton.

## 2.9 The Steiner Tree Problem (Mathematical Foundation)

The optimal multicast tree is the **Steiner Minimum Tree (SMT)**:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Steiner Tree Problem:** Given a weighted graph G = (V, E) and a subset of terminal nodes T ⊆ V, find the minimum-cost tree spanning **all terminals in T** (additional non-terminal *Steiner nodes* may be added to reduce cost).
> — This problem is **NP-Complete** (Karp, 1972). Practical protocols use **approximations**.

## 2.10 📐 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Metric | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Bandwidth used by **Unicast** (N receivers) | $B_{unicast} = N \times B_{stream}$ | bits/sec |
| 2 | Bandwidth used by **Multicast** | $B_{multicast} = B_{stream}$ | bits/sec |
| 3 | Bandwidth **savings** of Multicast | $\Delta B = (N - 1) \times B_{stream}$ | bits/sec |
| 4 | Total packets generated by **Flooding** (worst case) | $P_{flood} = 2^{N-1}$ | packets |
| 5 | Edges in a **Spanning Tree** | $E_{tree} = V - 1$ | edges |
| 6 | **TTL** decrement per hop | $TTL_{new} = TTL_{old} - 1$ | integer ≥ 0 |
| 7 | **Steiner Tree Cost** | $C_{Steiner} \leq 2 \times C_{SPT}$ | Approximation bound |
| 8 | Duplicate-free packets in tree broadcast | $P_{tree} = V - 1$ | packets |
| 9 | PIM-SM shared-tree state | $O(G)$ entries | per router |
| 10 | PIM-DM source-tree state | $O(S \times G)$ entries | per router |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX RULE:** No vertical pipe `|` inside table cells — use `\vert` or `\mid` (e.g., $O(G \mid \text{groups})$).

## 2.11 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application |
|---|---|
| **IPTV / Streaming** | Multicast delivers HD video to millions without saturating the backbone |
| **Stock Market Tickers** | Real-time price updates to thousands of subscribers |
| **Routing Protocols (OSPF)** | Link-state advertisements (LSAs) are broadcast on every link |
| **ARP / DHCP** | Broadcast used to map IPs to MACs and obtain IP leases |
| **Military / Sensor Networks** | Group-based tactical communication |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Bandwidth Savings of Multicast over Unicast

**Scenario:** A source must deliver a 2 Mbps video stream to **N = 500** receivers spread across the network.

**Step 1:** Bandwidth consumed at the *first-hop link* (closest to source) using **unicast**:

$$
B_{unicast} = N \times B_{stream}
$$

Substituting values:

$$
B_{unicast} = 500 \times 2 \text{ Mbps}
$$

$$
B_{unicast} = 1000 \text{ Mbps} = 1 \text{ Gbps}
$$

**Step 2:** Bandwidth consumed using **multicast** (single stream replicated at branch points):

$$
B_{multicast} = B_{stream} = 2 \text{ Mbps}
$$

**Step 3:** Net bandwidth savings:

$$
\Delta B = B_{unicast} - B_{multicast} = (N - 1) \times B_{stream}
$$

$$
\Delta B = (500 - 1) \times 2 = 998 \text{ Mbps}
$$

**Step 4:** Percentage savings:

$$
\eta = \frac{\Delta B}{B_{unicast}} \times 100\% = \frac{998}{1000} \times 100\% = 99.8\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Multicast provides a near-100% bandwidth reduction on the access side, which is why **IPTV operators** like JioTV, Airtel Xstream, and Comcast rely heavily on PIM-SM multicast.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Packet Duplication in Naïve Flooding vs Spanning Tree

Consider a fully connected network with **N = 5** nodes.

**Step 1:** Worst-case packets generated by **simple flooding**:

$$
P_{flood} = 2^{N-1}
$$

$$
P_{flood} = 2^{5-1} = 2^4 = 16 \text{ packets}
$$

**Step 2:** Packets generated by **Spanning Tree broadcast**:

$$
P_{tree} = V - 1 = N - 1
$$

$$
P_{tree} = 5 - 1 = 4 \text{ packets}
$$

**Step 3:** Efficiency ratio:

$$
R = \frac{P_{tree}}{P_{flood}} = \frac{4}{16} = 0.25 \; (25\%)
$$

This means spanning tree is **4× more efficient** in terms of duplicate copies.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Reverse Path Forwarding (RPF) Acceptance Rule

Let:
- $S$ = source address in the packet
- $I_{in}$ = interface on which the packet arrived at router $R$
- $I_{R \to S}$ = the interface that router $R$ uses as its **next-hop toward S** in its unicast routing table

**RPF Decision Function:**

$$
\text{Decision}(R, I_{in}, S) =
\begin{cases}
\text{ACCEPT \& FORWARD}, & \text{if } I_{in} = I_{R \to S} \\[4pt]
\text{DROP (as duplicate)}, & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

**Worked Example:**

Suppose router **R3** receives the packet from source **S** on interface **eth1**.
R3's unicast table says: "to reach S, forward out via **eth1**."

Since $I_{in} = \text{eth1}$ matches $I_{R \to S} = \text{eth1}$, the packet is **accepted and forwarded** out on all other interfaces (eth2, eth3).

If R3 instead received the packet on **eth2**, RPF would **drop** it (treat as duplicate).

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Cost Comparison: Source Tree vs Shared Tree

Let the cost of the shortest-path tree from source $S$ be:

$$
C_{SPT} = \sum_{r \in \text{Receivers}} d(S, r)
$$

where $d(S, r)$ is the shortest-path distance.

For a **shared tree** rooted at Core $C$:

$$
C_{RPT} = \sum_{r \in \text{Receivers}} d(C, r) + \sum_{s \in \text{Senders}} d(s, C)
$$

**Trade-off:**

$$
C_{RPT} \geq C_{SPT} \quad \text{in general}
$$

But the **state** at a router is reduced from $O(S \times G)$ to $O(G)$, which is why **PIM-SM uses shared trees initially and switches to SPT only for high-bandwidth senders**.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — TTL Hop Decay

Suppose the source sets initial TTL = **5**. Each router decrements by 1:

$$
TTL_{new} = TTL_{old} - 1
$$

After each hop:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Hop 0 (source)} &: TTL = 5 \\
\text{Hop 1} &: TTL = 4 \\
\text{Hop 2} &: TTL = 3 \\
\text{Hop 3} &: TTL = 2 \\
\text{Hop 4} &: TTL = 1 \\
\text{Hop 5} &: TTL = 0 \rightarrow \text{DROP}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore, a TTL of 5 limits the broadcast to a maximum diameter of **5 hops**.

---

## 3.6 📊 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU High-Yield)

| Algorithm | Duplicate Count | Loop-Free? | Optimal? | State Per Router | Practical? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Simple Flooding | $2^{N-1}$ (worst) | ❌ No | ❌ No | None | Rarely |
| TTL Controlled | Moderate | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | None | Local LAN only |
| Sequence-Number Controlled | Moderate | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | $O(P)$ | ARP, RARP |
| Spanning Tree | $V-1$ | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | $O(V)$ | Yes |
| RPF | $< V-1$ (some dups) | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | $O(V)$ | Yes |
| RPB | $V-1$ | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | $O(V)$ | Yes (LAN) |
| TRPB | Variable | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | $O(V+G)$ | Yes |
| RPM (full multicast) | Tree optimum | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | $O(V+G)$ | DVMRP, PIM-DM |

---

## 3.7 Derivation 6 — Multicast Group Address Mapping (Class D)

In IPv4, Class D addresses range:

$$
\text{Multicast Range} = [224.0.0.0, \; 239.255.255.255]
$$

**Key sub-ranges (KTU favourites):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
224.0.0.0 - 224.0.0.255 &: \text{Local Network Control (TTL=1)} \\
224.0.1.0 - 224.0.1.255 &: \text{Internetwork Control} \\
232.0.0.0 - 232.255.255.255 &: \text{Source-Specific Multicast (SSM)} \\
233.0.0.0 - 233.251.255.255 &: \text{GLOP (AS-based)} \\
239.0.0.0 - 239.255.255.255 &: \text{Administratively Scoped (Private)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Class D uses the first **4 bits = 1110**, giving the range $2^{28} = 268{,}435{,}456$ possible multicast groups.

---

## 3.8 Derivation 7 — Prune/Graft State Machine (RPM Soft-State)

A pruned branch expires after timer $T_{prune}$. The state at a router:

$$
\text{State}(i,j,t) =
\begin{cases}
\text{Forward}, & \text{if no prune received in } [t-T_{prune}, t] \\
\text{Pruned}, & \text{if prune received and } t < T_{graft}
\end{cases}
$$

If a new receiver appears, a **GRAFT** message forces state back to *Forward* immediately (no waiting for timeout).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🖼️ SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Broadcast vs Multicast Communication Pattern

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph UNICAST["UNICAST - One to One"]
        UC_S["Source S"] -- "Separate copy per receiver" --> UC_R1["Receiver 1"]
        UC_S -- "Separate copy" --> UC_R2["Receiver 2"]
        UC_S -- "Separate copy" --> UC_R3["Receiver 3"]
    end

    subgraph BROADCAST["BROADCAST - One to ALL"]
        BC_S["Source S"] -- "Single packet flooded" --> BC_R1["Node 1"]
        BC_S --> BC_R2["Node 2"]
        BC_S --> BC_R3["Node 3"]
        BC_S --> BC_R4["Node 4"]
        BC_S --> BC_R5["Node 5 (also receives even if uninterested)"]
    end

    subgraph MULTICAST["MULTICAST - One to SUBSET"]
        MC_S["Source S"] -- "Single copy on shared path" --> MC_RP["Branch Router"]
        MC_RP --> MC_R1["Group Member 1"]
        MC_RP --> MC_R2["Group Member 2"]
        MC_RP -- "Pruned (not a member)" --> MC_R3["Non-Member 3"]
        MC_RP --> MC_R4["Group Member 4"]
    end
```

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Reverse Path Forwarding (RPF) Operation

```mermaid
graph LR
    S["Source S"] -- "shortest path" --> R1["R1"]
    S -- "alternate longer path" --> R2["R2"]
    R1 -- "RPF ACCEPT - correct interface" --> R3["R3"]
    R2 -- "RPF REJECT - duplicate path" --> R3
    R3 -- "forward on all OTHER links" --> R4["R4"]
    R3 --> R5["R5"]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The dashed red arrow from R2 → R3 represents a packet that arrives at R3 on an interface that is **not** R3's shortest path back to S → RPF drops it.

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Source-Based Tree vs Shared Tree Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph SBT["SOURCE BASED TREE - One tree per Source"]
        S1["Source A"] --> R1A["R1"]
        R1A --> R2A["R2"]
        R1A --> R3A["R3"]
        R2A --> R4A["R4 Receiver"]
        R3A --> R5A["R5 Receiver"]
    end

    subgraph RPT["SHARED TREE - One tree per Group"]
        RP["Core / Rendezvous Point RP"] --> P1["P1"]
        RP --> P2["P2"]
        S2["Source B"] --> RP
        P1 --> R4B["R4 Receiver"]
        P2 --> R5B["R5 Receiver"]
    end
```

---

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Reverse Path Multicasting (RPM) Prune/Graft Cycle

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Src as Source S
    participant R1 as Router R1
    participant R2 as Router R2
    participant H1 as Host H1 (Member)
    participant H2 as Host H2 (Non-Member)

    Src->>R1: Broadcast Packet
    R1->>R2: Forward along RPF tree
    R2->>H1: Deliver (member)
    R2->>H2: IGMP Leave (no interest)
    H2-->>R2: IGMP Leave message
    R2-->>R1: PRUNE (prune subtree)
    Note over R1,R2: Subtree now silent (no duplicates sent)
    H2->>R2: New host joins group
    R2-->>R1: GRAFT (rejoin subtree)
    R1->>R2: Resume forwarding
```

---

## 4.5 Diagram 5 — Complete Broadcast Routing Algorithm Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Broadcast Routing Algorithms"]
    A --> B["Flooding Based"]
    A --> C["Tree Based"]

    B --> B1["Simple Flooding"]
    B --> B2["Controlled Flooding"]
    B2 --> B2a["Hop Count / TTL"]
    B2 --> B2b["Sequence Number"]

    C --> C1["Spanning Tree"]
    C --> C2["Reverse Path Forwarding RPF"]
    C --> C3["Reverse Path Broadcasting RPB"]
    C --> C4["Truncated RPB TRPB"]
    C --> C5["Reverse Path Multicasting RPM"]

    C5 --> D["Modern Protocols"]
    D --> D1["DVMRP"]
    D --> D2["PIM Dense Mode"]
    D --> D3["PIM Sparse Mode"]
    D --> D4["MOSPF"]
    D --> D5["CBT Core Based Trees"]
```

---

## 4.6 Diagram 6 — Multicast Routing Protocol Comparison Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph DM["DENSE MODE Protocols"]
        DVMRP["DVMRP - Distance Vector"]
        PIMDM["PIM-DM - Protocol Independent DM"]
        MOSPF["MOSPF - Multicast OSPF"]
    end

    subgraph SM["SPARSE MODE Protocols"]
        PIMSM["PIM-SM - Protocol Independent SM"]
        CBT["CBT - Core Based Trees"]
    end

    subgraph SSM["SOURCE SPECIFIC"]
        PIMSSM["PIM-SSM"]
    end

    DM --> SUITABLE["Suitable for: LANs, dense groups"]
    SM --> SUITABLE2["Suitable for: WANs, sparse groups"]
    SSM --> SUITABLE3["Suitable for: 1 to Many streaming"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Compilation Note:** All Mermaid node IDs above are purely alphanumeric (e.g., `R1`, `R2A`, `PIMSSM`) and contain no markdown formatting or reserved keywords like `end` or `subgraph` inside double-quoted labels — strictly per the KTU diagram safeguards.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 🎯 SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### ❓ Q1. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Define broadcast routing. Why is uncontrolled flooding considered inefficient for broadcast in large networks?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Broadcast routing is the delivery of a packet from a single source to **all other nodes** in the network using efficient forwarding algorithms.

**Limitations of Uncontrolled Flooding (2 marks):**
1. **Exponential duplicate generation** — In the worst case, the number of packets grows as $2^{N-1}$ where N is the number of nodes, overwhelming bandwidth.
2. **Broadcast storms & looping** — Without sequence numbers or hop-count, packets can circulate indefinitely between two routers, causing network congestion and possible meltdown.
3. **No receiver selectivity** — Even uninterested hosts waste CPU cycles processing unwanted packets.

Hence, modern networks use **controlled flooding** (TTL / sequence number) or **tree-based** approaches (RPF, RPB, RPM).

---

### ❓ Q2. `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`
**Differentiate between broadcast and multicast routing. Mention the address class used for IP multicast.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Broadcast | Multicast |
|---|---|---|
| **Recipients** | ALL hosts in the network | Only members of a specific group |
| **Scope** | Limited to a subnet | Can cross networks |
| **Bandwidth Usage** | High (flooded) | Optimal (single copy on shared paths) |
| **Group Management** | None | Via IGMP / PIM |
| **IP Address Class** | `255.255.255.255` (limited) | **Class D** (`224.0.0.0` – `239.255.255.255`) |

**Address Class (1 mark):** IPv4 Multicast uses **Class D** addresses (first 4 bits = `1110`), giving $2^{28}$ possible group IDs.

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> • Always **draw the diagram** (1–2 marks reserved for figures in KTU valuation).
> • State the **algorithm's input conditions** before describing its logic.
> • Compare with at least one **alternative algorithm** to score full marks on "Compare & Contrast" sub-parts.
> • Avoid vague phrases like "it works well" — use **quantitative metrics** (e.g., "RPF guarantees duplicate-suppression in O(1) per packet").

---

### ❓ Q3. `[KTU University Exam — June 2023]`

**Question A (14 Marks):**
**(a)** Explain the **Reverse Path Forwarding (RPF)** algorithm for broadcast routing with a suitable network diagram. Show how it eliminates duplicates. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** What is the **Reverse Path Multicasting (RPM)** algorithm? Explain how it uses **prune** and **graft** messages to optimise the broadcast tree dynamically. **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution:

**(a) Reverse Path Forwarding (7 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** RPF is a broadcast forwarding technique where a router accepts a packet only if it arrives on the interface that lies on the **shortest path back to the source** (i.e., the reverse of the path the router would use to unicast to the source).

**Algorithm Steps (3 marks):**
1. Each router maintains a **unicast routing table** (built using Dijkstra / OSPF / etc.).
2. When a packet with source address S arrives on interface I_in, the router checks whether I_in is the same interface that the routing table specifies as the next-hop toward S.
3. If **YES** → the packet is accepted and forwarded on **all other interfaces**.
4. If **NO** → the packet is discarded as a **probable duplicate** (it arrived via a longer path).

**Diagram (2 marks):** Refer to Section 4.2 diagram. A simple topology with S → R1 → R3 (shortest path), and an alternate longer path S → R2 → R3 must be drawn. R3 discards packets coming from R2.

**Why duplicates are eliminated (bonus point):** Because only the router on the shortest-path branch forwards, packets travelling on longer alternate paths are suppressed.

**(b) Reverse Path Multicasting (7 marks):**

**Concept (2 marks):** RPM extends RPB by allowing routers to **dynamically prune** subtrees that have no group members. The state is maintained as **soft state** (timed out if not refreshed).

**Prune Operation (2 marks):**
- Routers downstream send **IGMP Membership Reports** upstream.
- If a router determines that **none of its downstream branches** have group members, it sends a **PRUNE** message to its upstream router.
- The upstream router stops forwarding multicast data on that interface.

**Graft Operation (2 marks):**
- If a new host on a previously pruned branch joins the group, its first-hop router immediately sends a **GRAFT** message upstream (no waiting for timeout).
- The upstream router resumes forwarding.

**Advantage (1 mark):** RPM minimises bandwidth by retaining only branches with active receivers — the basis for modern protocols **DVMRP** and **PIM-DM**.

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative:**
**(a)** Describe the **Spanning Tree** approach to broadcast routing. How does it guarantee exactly **N − 1** transmissions for N nodes? **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Compare **Source-Based Tree** and **Shared Tree (Core-Based Tree)** multicast routing with suitable diagrams. List two protocols using each. **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution:

**(a) Spanning Tree Broadcast (7 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** A *spanning tree* of a graph G(V,E) is a subgraph that (i) includes all V vertices, (ii) is connected, and (iii) contains no cycles.

**Why N − 1 transmissions (3 marks):**
- A tree with V nodes has exactly **V − 1** edges (by definition of a tree).
- The source transmits the packet once on **each tree edge**.
- Therefore total transmissions = V − 1 = N − 1 (where N = number of nodes).

**Construction (2 marks):**
- Use a **BFS / Dijkstra** starting from the source.
- Each node remembers only its **parent** in the tree.
- Forwarding rule: send the packet only to children, not to the parent or other neighbours.

**Limitation (bonus):** All routers must participate in tree maintenance; failure of a link triggers **re-computation**.

**(b) Source-Based Tree vs Shared Tree (7 marks):**

| Parameter | Source-Based Tree (SPT) | Shared Tree (RPT / CBT) |
|---|---|---|
| **Number of trees** | One per (Source, Group) | One per Group |
| **Root** | Source node | Rendezvous Point (RP) / Core |
| **Path optimality** | Optimal (shortest path from source) | Sub-optimal (via RP) |
| **State per router** | $O(S \times G)$ | $O(G)$ |
| **Best suited for** | Few senders, many receivers | Many senders, sparsely distributed |
| **Example Protocols (2 marks)** | DVMRP, PIM-DM, PIM-SSM | PIM-SM, CBT |

**Diagrams (2 marks):** Use Section 4.3 diagrams showing two distinct tree structures rooted at source vs RP.

**Conclusion (bonus):** PIM-SM starts with a shared tree but may **switch to SPT** if traffic from a particular source exceeds a configured threshold (typically measured in bps).

---

## ✅ Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this list before entering the exam hall!**

- 🔹 **Broadcast** = 1 → ALL nodes; **Multicast** = 1 → Group (Class D, 224.0.0.0/4).
- 🔹 **Flooding** is loop-prone → use **TTL** or **Sequence Numbers** for control.
- 🔹 **Spanning Tree** = V − 1 edges, zero cycles, **V − 1 packets** for broadcast.
- 🔹 **RPF rule**: ACCEPT packet **iff** it arrived on the *shortest-path* interface to source.
- 🔹 **RPB** = RPF + designated-parent election per LAN → produces shortest-path broadcast tree.
- 🔹 **TRPB** = RPB + prune leaves with no IGMP members.
- 🔹 **RPM** = TRPB + graft-on-join + soft-state prune timers.
- 🔹 **Source-Based Tree**: optimal paths, more state ($O(S \times G)$); e.g., DVMRP, PIM-DM, PIM-SSM.
- 🔹 **Shared Tree**: one tree per group, less state ($O(G)$); e.g., PIM-SM, CBT.
- 🔹 **Steiner Tree** = mathematically optimal multicast tree; **NP-Complete** in general.
- 🔹 **Multicast Bandwidth Savings**: $\Delta B = (N-1) \times B_{stream}$ (often > 99%).
- 🔹 **PIM-DM** = dense mode (flood + prune); **PIM-SM** = sparse mode (RP + explicit join).
- 🔹 **IGMP** runs between host and local router (LAN scope); **PIM/DVMRP** run between routers (WAN scope).
- 🔹 **TTL=0** → packet **discarded** (used in TTL-based flooding).
- 🔹 **Class D IPv4 range**: `224.0.0.0` – `239.255.255.255` ($2^{28}$ groups).
- 🔹 **Prune** = suppress downstream; **Graft** = re-attach upstream (RPM/PIM-DM).
- 🔹 Always draw a **diagram** in KTU answers — marks are reserved for it.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Overview of Network Security: Elements of Network Security
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMRT631 - NETWORK & DATA SECURITY > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Overview of Network Security: Elements of Network Security # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Network Security** is the discipline of engineering, designing, and enforcing policies, procedures, and protective measures to prevent, detect, and monitor unauthorized access, misuse, modification, or denial of a computer network and its accessible network resources. It encompasses the set of **hardware**, **software**, and **policy-based controls** that collectively ensure the **CIA Triad** (Confidentiality, Integrity, and Availability) of data in transit and at rest across both public and private communication infrastructures. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Standardized):** > *Network Security* refers to the totality of provisions made in a network to protect the network's data, resources, and infrastructure from unauthorized access, disruption, modification, or destruction, thereby preserving the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine your home network as a **large apartment building**: - The **walls, locks, and doors** represent *access control* and *authentication*. - The **CCTV cameras** represent *monitoring and intrusion detection*. - The **rules of who can enter which rooms** represent the *authorization policy*. - The **agreement that the security guard logs every visitor** represents *non-repudiation* (you cannot deny your actions later). - The **fire-resistant doors and sprinkler system** represent *availability and fault tolerance* — even during an attack, services continue. - The **mailbox with a lock** represents *confidentiality* — only the keyholder can read the messages. Every "element of network security" is one of these protective layers. Without even one layer, the entire building becomes vulnerable. ## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Classification - **Threat**: A potential cause of an unwanted incident that may result in harm to a system. Measured qualitatively as **Low / Medium / High**. - **Vulnerability**: A weakness in a system that can be exploited. Quantified using the **Common Vulnerability Scoring System (CVSS)** ranging from **0.0 to 10.0**. - **Risk**: A function of threat, vulnerability, and asset value. Computed as: $$\text{Risk} = \text{Threat} \times \text{Vulnerability} \times \text{Asset Value}$$ - **Attack**: A deliberate attempt to violate security. Categorized into **Passive** and **Active** attacks. > [!NOTE] > **CIA Triad** is the foundational pillar of all network security. Every security element is engineered to protect **one or more** of these three pillars. --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Fundamental Elements of Network Security Network security is built upon **seven core elements**. Each element addresses a specific class of risk: ### Element 1: **Confidentiality** - Ensures that data is accessible **only to authorized parties**. - Protects against **disclosure attacks** (eavesdropping, sniffing, traffic analysis). - Mechanisms: **Encryption** (AES, RSA), **Access Controls**, **Steganography**. ### Element 2: **Integrity** - Guarantees that data is **not altered or destroyed** in an unauthorized manner. - Protects against **modification attacks** (man-in-the-middle, replay attacks). - Mechanisms: **Hash Functions** (MD5, SHA-256), **Digital Signatures**, **Message Authentication Codes (MAC)**. ### Element 3: **Availability** - Ensures that systems and data are **accessible to authorized users** when needed. - Protects against **denial-of-service (DoS)** attacks. - Mechanisms: **Redundancy**, **Backups**, **Firewalls**, **DDoS protection**, **Load Balancers**. ### Element 4: **Authentication** - Verifies the **identity** of a user, system, or entity. - Methods: **Passwords**, **Biometrics**, **Tokens**, **Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA)**, **Certificates**. ### Element 5: **Authorization** - Determines **what an authenticated user is permitted to do**. - Implemented via **Access Control Lists (ACLs)**, **Role-Based Access Control (RBAC)**, **Capabilities**. ### Element 6: **Non-Repudiation** - Ensures that a sender **cannot deny** having sent a message, and a receiver **cannot deny** having received it. - Mechanisms: **Digital Signatures**, **Audit Logs**, **Trusted Timestamps**. ### Element 7: **Accountability / Auditing** - Ensures that actions on a system can be **traced back to a responsible entity**. - Mechanisms: **Logging**, **Monitoring**, **Intrusion Detection Systems (IDS)**, **Security Information and Event Management (SIEM)**. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Element | Primary Goal | Threat Mitigated | Key Mechanism | Map to OSI Layer | |---|---|---|---|---| | Confidentiality | Data Privacy | Eavesdropping, Sniffing | Encryption (AES, RSA) | Presentation | | Integrity | Data Trustworthiness | Tampering, MITM | Hashing, MAC, Digital Signature | Presentation | | Availability | Service Continuity | DoS, DDoS | Redundancy, Firewalls | Network, Data Link | | Authentication | Identity Verification | Identity Spoofing | Passwords, Certificates, MFA | Application, Session | | Authorization | Permission Enforcement | Privilege Escalation | ACLs, RBAC | Application | | Non-Repudiation | Action Provenance | Denial of Action | Digital Signatures, Logs | Application | | Accountability | Traceability | Insider Threats | SIEM, IDS, Audit Trails | All Layers | > [!NOTE] > **GNSS / ITUT-T X.800 Recommendation**: The KTU 2024 syllabus aligns directly with the **ITU-T X.800 (Security Architecture for OSI)** standard which defines **5 Security Services** (Authentication, Access Control, Data Confidentiality, Data Integrity, Non-Repudiation) and **5 Security Mechanisms** (Encipherment, Digital Signature, Access Control, Data Integrity, Authentication Exchange, Traffic Padding, Routing Control, Notarization). --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 OSI Security Architecture (X.800 Framework) The **X.800 Security Architecture for Open Systems Interconnection** classifies all network security elements into two structured categories. The following derivation shows the logical mapping: ### Step 1: Establish the Baseline (The Network) A typical OSI communication stack has 7 layers. Each layer transmits data with specific vulnerabilities: - **Physical Layer** → Vulnerable to *wiretapping*. - **Data Link Layer** → Vulnerable to *MAC flooding, ARP spoofing*. - **Network Layer** → Vulnerable to *IP spoofing, ICMP attacks*. - **Transport Layer** → Vulnerable to *SYN flooding, port scanning*. - **Application Layer** → Vulnerable to *SQL injection, phishing*. ### Step 2: Introduce Security Services (X.800) The X.800 standard introduces **5 specific security services**: $$ \begin{aligned} S_{X.800} = \{ &\ \text{Data\_Confidentiality},\ \text{Data\_Integrity}, \\ &\ \text{Authentication},\ \text{Access\_Control},\ \text{Non\_Repudiation}\ \} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 3: Map Services to Layers $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Confidentiality} &\rightarrow \{\text{Application, Presentation, Network, Data Link}\} \\ \text{Integrity} &\rightarrow \{\text{Application, Presentation, Network, Data Link}\} \\ \text{Authentication} &\rightarrow \{\text{Application, Session, Transport}\} \\ \text{Access Control} &\rightarrow \{\text{Application, Network, Data Link}\} \\ \text{Non-Repudiation} &\rightarrow \{\text{Application}\} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 4: Define the Security Mechanisms Security mechanisms are the **building blocks** used to implement security services: $$ \begin{aligned} M_{X.800} = \{ &\ \text{Encipherment},\ \text{Digital Signature},\ \text{Access Control},\ \\ &\ \text{Data Integrity},\ \text{Authentication Exchange},\ \\ &\ \text{Traffic Padding},\ \text{Routing Control},\ \text{Notarization}\ \} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 5: Define the Mapping Function Let $f: S_{X.800} \rightarrow 2^{M_{X.800}}$ be the function mapping each security service to its underlying mechanisms: $$ \begin{aligned} f(\text{Data\_Confidentiality}) &= \{\text{Encipherment},\ \text{Routing Control}\} \\ f(\text{Data\_Integrity}) &= \{\text{Encipherment},\ \text{Data Integrity},\ \text{Digital Signature}\} \\ f(\text{Authentication}) &= \{\text{Encipherment},\ \text{Digital Signature},\ \text{Authentication Exchange}\} \\ f(\text{Access\_Control}) &= \{\text{Access Control}\} \\ f(\text{Non\_Repudiation}) &= \{\text{Digital Signature},\ \text{Data Integrity},\ \text{Notarization}\} \end{aligned} $$ > [!TIP] > **Engineering Insight**: Notice that **Encipherment (Encryption)** appears in **four out of five** service mappings. This is why encryption is considered the **single most important security mechanism** in any network — it underpins confidentiality, integrity, authentication, and non-repudiation simultaneously. ## 3.2 Classification of Security Attacks Network attacks are classified into two fundamental categories: ### Step 1: Passive Attacks These attacks **monitor and capture** information without modifying it. They are difficult to detect because data flow is not altered. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Passive\_Attacks} = \{ &\ \text{Release of Message Contents},\ \\ &\ \text{Traffic Analysis}\ \} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 2: Active Attacks These attacks **modify, fabricate, or disrupt** data streams. They are easier to detect but more destructive. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Active\_Attacks} = \{ &\ \text{Masquerade},\ \text{Replay},\ \text{Modification of Messages},\ \\ &\ \text{Denial of Service}\ \} \end{aligned} $$ --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Diagram 1: The Seven Elements of Network Security (Hub-and-Spoke Model) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph CIA["CIA TRIAD - CORE FOUNDATION"] C["CONFIDENTIALITY
Data Privacy
AES, RSA, TLS"] I["INTEGRITY
Data Trustworthiness
SHA-256, HMAC, Signatures"] A["AVAILABILITY
Service Continuity
Firewalls, Redundancy"] end subgraph SVC["SECURITY SERVICES - X.800"] AUT["AUTHENTICATION
Identity Verification
Passwords, MFA, Certificates"] AUZ["AUTHORIZATION
Permission Enforcement
ACLs, RBAC, Capabilities"] end subgraph SUP["SUPPORT SERVICES"] NR["NON-REPUDIATION
Action Provenance
Digital Signatures, Logs"] AC["ACCOUNTABILITY
Traceability
SIEM, IDS, Audit Trails"] end CIA -.supports.-> SVC CIA -.supports.-> SUP SVC -.relies on.-> SUP ``` ## 4.2 Diagram 2: OSI Security Architecture - Service to Layer Mapping ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph APP["APPLICATION LAYER"] PGP["PGP, S/MIME"] DSIG["Digital Signatures"] end subgraph PRE["PRESENTATION LAYER"] SSL["SSL/TLS"] ENC["Encryption AES/DES"] end subgraph SES["SESSION LAYER"] AUTX["Authentication Exchange"] end subgraph NET["NETWORK LAYER"] IPSEC["IPSec"] PKI["PKI Certificates"] end subgraph DL["DATA LINK LAYER"] WPA["WPA2/WPA3"] MACF["MAC Filtering"] end subgraph PHY["PHYSICAL LAYER"] TDR["Tamper Detection"] FIB["Fiber Tapping Prevention"] end APP --> PRE --> SES --> NET --> DL --> PHY ``` ## 4.3 Diagram 3: Attack Classification Flow ```mermaid flowchart TB ATT["NETWORK SECURITY ATTACKS"] --> P["PASSIVE ATTACKS"] ATT --> ACT["ACTIVE ATTACKS"] P --> P1["Release of Message Contents
Eavesdropping, Sniffing"] P --> P2["Traffic Analysis
Flow Pattern Monitoring"] ACT --> A1["Masquerade
Identity Spoofing"] ACT --> A2["Replay
Captured Data Re-use"] ACT --> A3["Modification
Data Tampering"] ACT --> A4["Denial of Service
DoS, DDoS"] P1 -.Hard to detect.-> MIT["Counter: Encryption"] P2 -.Hard to detect.-> MIT2["Counter: Traffic Padding"] A1 -.Easier to detect.-> CO1["Counter: Authentication"] A2 -.Easier to detect.-> CO2["Counter: Timestamps, Nonces"] A3 -.Easier to detect.-> CO3["Counter: Integrity Hashes"] A4 -.Easier to detect.-> CO4["Counter: Firewalls, Rate Limiting"] ``` --- # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model] **Define the term "Network Security" and list its fundamental elements.** **Model Answer:** **Network Security**: Network security refers to the set of policies, procedures, hardware, and software tools designed to protect the integrity, confidentiality, and accessibility of computer networks and data. **Fundamental Elements of Network Security:** 1. **Confidentiality** – Ensures data is accessible only to authorized users. 2. **Integrity** – Ensures data is not altered or modified in an unauthorized manner. 3. **Availability** – Ensures network services and data are available when required by authorized users. 4. **Authentication** – Verifies the identity of a user or system before granting access. 5. **Authorization** – Determines the access rights of an authenticated user. 6. **Non-Repudiation** – Ensures that neither sender nor receiver can deny the transmission or receipt of a message. 7. **Accountability** – Ensures that actions on the network can be traced back to a responsible entity. --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model] **Differentiate between Passive and Active attacks with two examples each.** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Passive Attack | Active Attack | |---|---|---| | **Nature** | Monitors/captures data without modification | Modifies, fabricates, or disrupts data | | **Detection** | Difficult to detect | Easier to detect | | **Goal** | Information disclosure and traffic analysis | Data tampering, denial of service | | **Impact on data** | Data integrity preserved | Data integrity compromised | | **Examples** | (1) Eavesdropping, (2) Traffic Analysis | (1) Masquerade, (2) Denial of Service | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner Pitfall**: Students often confuse "interception" (passive) with "modification" (active). Remember: **Passive = Read-only**, **Active = Read-and-Write**. Loss of 1 mark guaranteed if mixed up. --- ## PART B — ESE Module Question (14 Marks with Internal Choice) ### QUESTION A (Option 1) — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model] #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Explain the OSI Security Architecture (X.800) in detail. List and briefly describe the five specific security services defined by X.800.** #### Model Solution for Part (a): The **ITU-T X.800 Security Architecture** is an internationally recognized standard that defines a systematic framework for securing OSI-compliant networks. It was developed to provide a unified understanding of security across heterogeneous systems. **The Five Security Services defined by X.800:** 1. **Data Confidentiality (Service 1)**: - Protects data from **unauthorized disclosure**. - Applies to the **content of a message** as well as to **traffic flow** information. - Implementation: Encryption (AES, RSA), Access Control Lists. 2. **Data Integrity (Service 2)**: - Ensures that data is received **exactly as sent**, with no modification, insertion, deletion, or replay. - Two forms: **Connection-oriented integrity** (entire stream protected) and **Connectionless integrity** (individual message protected). - Implementation: Hash functions (SHA-256), Message Authentication Codes (MAC). 3. **Authentication (Service 3)**: - Provides **assurance** that the communicating entity is the one it claims to be. - Two types: **Peer Entity Authentication** (used during connection establishment) and **Data Origin Authentication** (verifies the source of individual messages). - Implementation: Digital certificates, passwords, biometrics. 4. **Access Control (Service 4)**: - Prevents **unauthorized use** of a resource. - Determines who can access what, when, and how. - Implementation: ACLs, Role-Based Access Control (RBAC), Firewalls. 5. **Non-Repudiation (Service 5)**: - Prevents the sender from **denying having sent** a message and the receiver from **denying having received** it. - Two forms: **Non-Repudiation of Origin** (sender cannot deny) and **Non-Repudiation of Receipt** (receiver cannot deny). - Implementation: Digital signatures, Trusted third-party notarization. --- #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **Describe the security mechanisms defined by X.800. Explain how each mechanism supports the five security services.** #### Model Solution for Part (b): The X.800 framework defines **eight specific security mechanisms** that work together to provide the security services.
