These mechanisms are: 1. **Encipherment (Encryption)**: - Uses mathematical algorithms to **transform plaintext into ciphertext**. - Supports: Confidentiality (primary), Integrity, Authentication. 2. **Digital Signature**: - Attaches a cryptographic code to data to **verify authenticity and integrity**. - Supports: Authentication, Integrity, Non-Repudiation. 3. **Access Control**: - Enforces rules about **who can access what** resources. - Supports: Access Control service (primary). 4. **Data Integrity**: - Uses checksums, hashes, and MACs to **detect alterations**. - Supports: Data Integrity service. 5. **Authentication Exchange**: - Mechanisms to **verify identity** through passwords, tokens, or challenge-response protocols. - Supports: Authentication service. 6. **Traffic Padding**: - Inserts **dummy traffic** into a data stream to frustrate traffic analysis attempts. - Supports: Confidentiality (against traffic analysis). 7. **Routing Control**: - Selects **physically secure routes** for sensitive data and allows route changes to avoid compromised nodes. - Supports: Confidentiality, Availability. 8. **Notarization**: - Uses a **trusted third party** to certify the properties of data exchange (e.g., timestamps, signatures). - Supports: Non-Repudiation, Data Integrity. **Mapping Summary Table:** | Security Service | Primary Mechanisms | |---|---| | Confidentiality | Encipherment, Routing Control | | Integrity | Encipherment, Data Integrity, Digital Signature | | Authentication | Encipherment, Authentication Exchange, Digital Signature | | Access Control | Access Control Mechanism | | Non-Repudiation | Digital Signature, Data Integrity, Notarization | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner Pitfall**: Many students write **"five mechanisms"** instead of **eight**. The complete list is: Encipherment, Digital Signature, Access Control, Data Integrity, Authentication Exchange, Traffic Padding, Routing Control, Notarization. Missing any of these will cost **at least 1 mark**. --- ### QUESTION B (Option 2 — Alternative Choice) — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model] #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Explain in detail the CIA Triad. How do the elements of Confidentiality, Integrity, and Availability map to real-world network security mechanisms?** #### Model Solution for Part (a): The **CIA Triad** is the foundational model of information security that forms the basis for designing and evaluating all network security controls. Each pillar addresses a unique class of threats: **1. Confidentiality:** - **Definition**: The property that information is not made available or disclosed to unauthorized individuals, entities, or processes. - **Threats addressed**: Eavesdropping, sniffing, shoulder surfing, social engineering. - **Mechanisms**: - **Encryption** (AES-256, RSA, ECC) — scrambles data so only keyholders can read it. - **Access Control Lists (ACLs)** — restrict who can view specific resources. - **Steganography** — hides the existence of data within other data. **2. Integrity:** - **Definition**: The property whereby data has not been altered or destroyed in an unauthorized manner. - **Threats addressed**: Tampering, man-in-the-middle attacks, malware injection. - **Mechanisms**: - **Hash Functions** (SHA-256, SHA-3) — produce a fixed-size digest to detect any change in data. - **Message Authentication Codes (MAC)** — combine hashing with a secret key. - **Digital Signatures** — provide cryptographic integrity with non-repudiation. **3. Availability:** - **Definition**: The property of being accessible and usable on demand by an authorized entity. - **Threats addressed**: DoS, DDoS, ransomware, hardware failures, natural disasters. - **Mechanisms**: - **Redundancy** (RAID, Failover clusters, Geographically distributed data centers). - **Firewalls and DDoS mitigation appliances**. - **Regular Backups** (3-2-1 rule: 3 copies, 2 media, 1 offsite). - **Load Balancers** to distribute traffic and prevent overload. --- #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **Discuss the various types of active attacks in detail. For each, propose a specific countermeasure.** #### Model Solution for Part (b): Active attacks involve the **modification, fabrication, or disruption** of data streams and are generally easier to detect than passive attacks. The four main types are: **1. Masquerade:** - **Description**: An attacker pretends to be an authorized user to gain access to a system or send fraudulent messages. - **Examples**: IP spoofing, MAC spoofing, email spoofing, session hijacking. - **Countermeasure**: Strong authentication mechanisms (multi-factor authentication, digital certificates), biometric verification, and Public Key Infrastructure (PKI). **2. Replay:** - **Description**: An attacker captures a valid data transmission and **replays it later** to perform an unauthorized action (e.g., re-sending a money transfer request). - **Examples**: Replaying an authentication token, replaying a transaction request. - **Countermeasure**: Use of **nonces (numbers used once)**, **timestamps**, and **sequence numbers** in protocols. Examples: Kerberos authentication, TLS session tokens with expiry. **3. Modification of Messages:** - **Description**: An attacker **alters portions of a legitimate message** in transit to change its meaning or effect. - **Examples**: Changing the recipient of a wire transfer, modifying routing tables, altering DNS responses (DNS poisoning). - **Countermeasure**: **Message integrity checks** using cryptographic hash functions (SHA-256, HMAC), digital signatures, and authenticated routing protocols (BGPsec). **4. Denial of Service (DoS) / Distributed Denial of Service (DDoS):** - **Description**: An attacker **overwhelms a system** with traffic or requests, rendering it unavailable to legitimate users. - **Examples**: SYN flood, UDP flood, HTTP flood, Smurf attack, botnet-driven DDoS. - **Countermeasure**: **Firewalls, Intrusion Prevention Systems (IPS), rate limiting, traffic scrubbing services** (e.g., Cloudflare, AWS Shield), and **anycast network architectures** for traffic dispersion. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner Pitfall**: For active attacks, students often forget to mention **specific attack names** (e.g., SYN flood, IP spoofing). Generic statements like "use authentication" will fetch only partial marks — always pair a threat with a **named specific attack** and a **named specific countermeasure**. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ### Rapid Revision Checklist - **CIA Triad**: Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability — the foundational model. - **X.800 Security Services**: Five services — Confidentiality, Integrity, Authentication, Access Control, Non-Repudiation. - **X.800 Security Mechanisms**: Eight mechanisms — Encipherment, Digital Signature, Access Control, Data Integrity, Authentication Exchange, Traffic Padding, Routing Control, Notarization. - **Passive Attacks**: Two types — Release of Message Contents, Traffic Analysis. - **Active Attacks**: Four types — Masquerade, Replay, Modification, Denial of Service. - **Authentication vs Authorization**: Authentication = *Who are you?* | Authorization = *What can you do?* - **Non-Repudiation Tools**: Digital Signatures, Trusted Timestamps, Audit Logs. - **Confidentiality Tool**: Encryption (AES, RSA, ECC). - **Integrity Tools**: Hashing (SHA-256), MAC, HMAC, Digital Signatures. - **Availability Tools**: Redundancy, Backups (3-2-1 Rule), DDoS mitigation, Load Balancers. ### Memory Tricks for KTU Exams - **CIA + 2 = 7 elements**: Add **Authentication** and **Authorization** to get the 5th and 6th. **Non-Repudiation** is the 7th. - **Eight mechanisms start with**: E (Encipherment), D (Digital Signature), A (Access Control), D (Data Integrity), A (Authentication Exchange), T (Traffic Padding), R (Routing Control), N (Notarization) → **"EDAD-ATRN"** mnemonic. - **Active attacks = MRMD**: **M**asquerade, **R**eplay, **M**odification, **D**enial of Service. - **Passive attacks = "Read-only"**: Always remember passive attackers **never modify** data. ### Critical Definitions to Memorize (Board-Examiner Favorites) | Term | Definition | |---|---| | **Threat** | A potential cause of an unwanted incident. | | **Vulnerability** | A weakness in a system that can be exploited. | | **Risk** | The probability that a threat will exploit a vulnerability. | | **Attack** | A deliberate attempt to violate security policy. | | **Asset** | Anything of value to the organization (data, hardware, software). | | **Security Policy** | A set of rules and practices that specify how an organization protects its assets. |

topics.title: Classification of Network Attacks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT631 - NETWORK & DATA SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Classification of Network Attacks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme** for **Network & Data Security**, a **Network Attack** is formally defined as any deliberate, unauthorized, and malicious attempt to compromise the **Confidentiality, Integrity, or Availability (CIA Triad)** of a networked computing resource, the data transmitted across it, or the underlying communication infrastructure.

The **Classification of Network Attacks** refers to the systematic taxonomy used to categorize these threats based on multiple orthogonal dimensions: the **attacker's intent**, the **mechanism of operation**, the **OSI layer targeted**, and the **security service violated**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):**
> According to the **2024 Scheme syllabus**, students are expected to master the taxonomy of attacks — distinguishing between **Passive** and **Active** attacks, understanding the **X.800 Security Threats** classification (Interruption, Interception, Modification, Fabrication), and identifying attack vectors like **DoS, DDoS, Sniffing, Spoofing, and Malware**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Bank Heist

Imagine a bank's secure vault (your network):

- A **Passive Attack** is like a spy with binoculars sitting across the street, silently watching how much money each customer carries in, when guards change shifts, and which alarm boxes are connected. The vault itself is never touched — but secret knowledge leaks out. This compromises **Confidentiality**.
- An **Active Attack** is like a thief who walks up, drills the lock, swaps a real customer's deposit slip with a forged one, or simply jams the bank's front door so customers can't enter. The vault is physically altered or denied. This compromises **Integrity** and **Availability**.

The **Classification System** is the police department's *crime taxonomy* — grouping offenses by *what* was harmed, *how* it was done, and *which layer of defense* was breached.

## 1.3 Why Classification Matters in Engineering

| Reason | Engineering Implication |
|---|---|
| **Defense-in-Depth Design** | Each attack class demands a *different* countermeasure (encryption ≠ firewall ≠ IDS) |
| **Risk Assessment** | Engineers prioritize defenses based on attack probability × business impact |
| **Incident Response** | Correct classification dictates the runbook (e.g., DDoS mitigation ≠ malware quarantine) |
| **Standards Compliance** | ISO 27001, NIST SP 800-53, and ITU-T X.800 mandate threat categorization |

> [!NOTE]
> **Foundation Standard:** The foundational taxonomy referenced worldwide (and implicitly tested in KTU exams) is the **ITU-T X.800 Recommendation**, which defines security attacks in terms of **threats to information and communication networks**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Classification Sheet

## 2.1 Primary Classification Dimensions

Network attacks are classified along **four orthogonal axes** that KTU examiners frequently combine in a single question:

1. **By Behavior / Activity Pattern** → Passive vs. Active
2. **By Security Service Violated** → Confidentiality / Integrity / Availability / Authenticity / Non-repudiation
3. **By Target / OSI Layer** → Application, Transport, Network, Data Link, Physical
4. **By Specific Attack Vector** → Malware, DoS, Injection, Spoofing, Sniffing, MITM, etc.

## 2.2 Dimension 1 — Passive vs. Active Attacks (The Core Divide)

### 2.2.1 Passive Attacks — The Silent Observer

A passive attack attempts to **learn or make use of information** from the system but **does not modify the system's resources or alter the operation**.

**Operational Logic Steps:**

- The attacker monitors the transmission channel (wired, wireless, or fiber).
- Packets are captured using tools such as **Wireshark**, **tcpdump**, or **Kismet**.
- The captured traffic is analyzed to extract credentials, session tokens, or business intelligence.
- The victim remains unaware because data flow is *not* disrupted.

**Sub-Categories:**

- **Release of Message Contents:** Reading the actual content of a message (e.g., reading an unencrypted email).
- **Traffic Analysis:** Even if content is encrypted, the *pattern, frequency, length, and origin-destination pairs* of encrypted traffic leak metadata.

### 2.2.2 Active Attacks — The Intruder

An active attack attempts to **alter system resources, modify data, or affect the normal operation** of the network.

**Operational Logic Steps:**

- The attacker injects, deletes, alters, or replays legitimate traffic.
- Detection by the victim is *probable* because observable side-effects occur.
- Damage scope: data corruption, identity theft, service outage, financial fraud.

**Sub-Categories (the four canonical X.800 threats):**

- **Masquerade:** An entity pretends to be a different entity (e.g., IP spoofing, email spoofing).
- **Replay:** Captured valid data is maliciously retransmitted to produce an unauthorized effect.
- **Modification of Messages:** Part of a legitimate message is altered, delayed, or reordered.
- **Denial of Service (DoS):** Prevents or impairs the authorized use of information or network resources.

## 2.3 Dimension 2 — Classification by X.800 Threat Model

The **ITU-T X.800 standard** provides a four-quadrant threat classification that KTU examiners love:

| X.800 Threat | Definition | Security Service Breached | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Interruption** | An asset of the system becomes unavailable or unusable | **Availability** | DoS attack, hardware sabotage |
| **Interception** | An unauthorized party gains access to an asset | **Confidentiality** | Wiretapping, packet sniffing |
| **Modification** | An unauthorized party tampers with an asset | **Integrity** | Man-in-the-Middle data alteration |
| **Fabrication** | An unauthorized party inserts counterfeit objects into the system | **Authenticity** | Forged email, fake routing updates |

## 2.4 Dimension 3 — Classification by OSI Layer

| OSI Layer | Common Attacks | Countermeasure Family |
|---|---|---|
| **Application (L7)** | SQL Injection, XSS, Phishing, Ransomware, Malware | WAF, Input Validation, EDR |
| **Presentation (L6)** | SSL Stripping, Certificate Spoofing | TLS, Certificate Pinning |
| **Session (L5)** | Session Hijacking, Cookie Theft | Session Tokens, Secure Flags |
| **Transport (L4)** | SYN Flood, UDP Flood, Port Scanning | Stateful Firewalls, Rate Limiting |
| **Network (L3)** | IP Spoofing, Smurf Attack, ICMP Flood | ACLs, uRPF, BCP-38 |
| **Data Link (L2)** | ARP Spoofing, MAC Flooding, VLAN Hopping | Port Security, DHCP Snooping, 802.1X |
| **Physical (L1)** | Cable Tapping, Electromagnetic Eavesdropping | Faraday Cages, Fiber Optics |

## 2.5 Dimension 4 — Classification by Attack Vector (The Modern Industry View)

Modern cybersecurity frameworks (NIST, MITRE ATT&CK) classify attacks into **seven major families**:

1. **Malware-based:** Virus, Worm, Trojan Horse, Ransomware, Spyware, Rootkit, Botnet
2. **Denial-of-Service (DoS / DDoS):** Volumetric, Protocol, Application-Layer
3. **Eavesdropping / Sniffing:** Packet capture, keylogging, electromagnetic interception
4. **Spoofing / Identity Attacks:** IP, ARP, DNS, Email, Caller ID spoofing
5. **Man-in-the-Middle (MITM):** Session hijacking, SSL stripping, evil twin Wi-Fi
6. **Injection / Code Execution:** SQL injection, XSS, Command injection, Buffer Overflow
7. **Social Engineering:** Phishing, Vishing, Smishing, Pretexting, Baiting

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Formula / Heuristic for KTU:**
> $$\text{Total Attack Surface} = \bigcup_{i=1}^{7} \text{Vulnerability}_i \times \text{Exposure Probability}_i$$
> In exam answers, always map an attack to **at least one** of the four X.800 threats AND one of the seven vector families — this fetches full marks.

## 2.6 Engineering Utility

This classification is the **backbone of every Security Policy Document**, **Penetration Test Report**, and **Network Architecture Diagram** used by:

- **SOC Analysts** (Tier-1 alert triage)
- **Penetration Testers** (reconnaissance → exploitation → reporting)
- **Network Architects** (designing segmented, zero-trust topologies)
- **Forensic Investigators** (root-cause analysis post-breach)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Decision Logic

Because *Classification of Network Attacks* is a **taxonomic / conceptual** topic rather than a numerical derivation, the rigorous analytical exercise here is to **build a decision tree** that lets a student classify *any* given attack into the correct category — a common 7–14 mark KTU exam pattern.

## 3.1 Analytical Exercise — Classifying a Given Attack

**Scenario:** A student reads about an attack where the attacker floods a web server with millions of HTTP GET requests from a botnet, causing the server's CPU to spike to 100% and genuine users to receive timeout errors.

### Step 1 — Identify Observable Symptoms

| Symptom | Observed? | Implication |
|---|---|---|
| Data content was read? | ❌ No | Not primarily a confidentiality breach |
| Data was altered? | ❌ No | Not primarily an integrity breach |
| Service is unavailable? | ✅ Yes | **Availability** is violated |
| Attacker injected traffic? | ✅ Yes (flood) | **Active** attack |

### Step 2 — Map to X.800 Threat

The service has been made unavailable → this maps directly to **X.800 Interruption**, which violates **Availability**.

### Step 3 — Map to Active Sub-Category

The attacker is actively disrupting service → this is **Denial of Service (DoS)**. The use of a *botnet* (multiple distributed sources) elevates it to **Distributed DoS (DDoS)**.

### Step 4 — Map to OSI Layer

The flood is at the **HTTP (Application Layer, L7)** level, so it is specifically an **Application-Layer DDoS** (e.g., HTTP Flood, Slowloris).

### Step 5 — Map to Attack Vector Family

This belongs to the **DoS / DDoS family** — specifically the **Application-Layer / Volumetric** subtype.

### Final Classification

$$\text{HTTP Flood from Botnet} \equiv \begin{cases} \text{Behavior: Active} \\ \text{X.800 Threat: Interruption} \\ \text{Security Service: Availability} \\ \text{OSI Layer: L7 (Application)} \\ \text{Vector Family: DoS/DDoS} \end{cases}$$

## 3.2 Generalized Decision Algorithm (For Exam Use)

When asked *"Classify the following attack"*, follow this deterministic 5-step algorithm:

**Step 1 — Was data observed without modification?**
- Yes → **Passive** (go to Step 2a)
- No, system state was altered → **Active** (go to Step 2b)

**Step 2a — Passive Sub-classification:**
- Was content read? → **Release of Message Contents**
- Only metadata extracted? → **Traffic Analysis**

**Step 2b — Active Sub-classification:**
- Identity was faked? → **Masquerade**
- Captured traffic was replayed? → **Replay**
- Message content was altered? → **Modification of Messages**
- Service was disrupted? → **Denial of Service**

**Step 3 — X.800 Mapping:**
- Passive → generally **Interception**
- DoS → **Interruption**
- Masquerade/Fabrication → **Fabrication**
- Replay/Modification → **Modification**

**Step 4 — Identify the Vector Family** (Malware, DoS, Sniffing, Spoofing, MITM, Injection, Social Engineering).

**Step 5 — Identify the OSI Layer** using the table in §2.4.

## 3.3 Worked Example #2 — ARP Spoofing

**Attack:** Attacker sends forged ARP replies on a LAN, associating their MAC address with the default gateway's IP. Victim's traffic is now forwarded through the attacker.

**Step-by-Step Classification:**

1. **Behavior:** The attacker *injects* packets into the network → **Active**.
2. **X.800 Threat:** The attacker is *masquerading* as another entity → **Masquerade → Fabrication**.
3. **Security Service:** **Authenticity** is violated (no proof the gateway is genuine).
4. **OSI Layer:** ARP operates at **Layer 2 (Data Link)** with cross-over into Layer 3.
5. **Vector Family:** **Spoofing / Identity Attack**, often enabling **MITM** (Man-in-the-Middle).
6. **Countermeasure:** **Dynamic ARP Inspection (DAI)**, **port security**, static ARP entries.

## 3.4 Worked Example #3 — Replay Attack

**Attack:** Attacker captures an authenticated $100 bank transfer request and replays it 10 times.

1. **Behavior:** Original traffic was *passively* captured, then *actively* retransmitted → **Hybrid**, but classified as **Active** because it has a modifying system effect.
2. **X.800 Threat:** **Replay** (sub-class of active).
3. **Security Service:** **Integrity** (a transaction is duplicated, distorting the system state).
4. **Countermeasure:** **Nonces**, **timestamps**, **sequence numbers**, **TOTP/HOTP**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Taxonomy — Classification of Network Attacks

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootA[Network Attacks]
    rootB[By X.800 Threat]
    rootC[By OSI Layer]
    rootD[By Vector Family]

    rootA --> passA[Passive Attacks]
    rootA --> actA[Active Attacks]

    passA --> subP1[Release of Message Contents]
    passA --> subP2[Traffic Analysis]

    actA --> subA1[Masquerade]
    actA --> subA2[Replay]
    actA --> subA3[Modification of Messages]
    actA --> subA4[Denial of Service]

    rootB --> x800_1[Interruption - Availability]
    rootB --> x800_2[Interception - Confidentiality]
    rootB --> x800_3[Modification - Integrity]
    rootB --> x800_4[Fabrication - Authenticity]

    rootC --> L1[Layer 1 Physical]
    rootC --> L2[Layer 2 Data Link]
    rootC --> L3[Layer 3 Network]
    rootC --> L4[Layer 4 Transport]
    rootC --> L7[Layer 7 Application]

    rootD --> fam1[Malware]
    rootD --> fam2[DoS or DDoS]
    rootD --> fam3[Spoofing or MITM]
    rootD --> fam4[Sniffing or Eavesdropping]
    rootD --> fam5[Injection Attacks]
    rootD --> fam6[Social Engineering]

    subP1 --> x800_2
    subP2 --> x800_2
    subA1 --> x800_4
    subA2 --> x800_3
    subA3 --> x800_3
    subA4 --> x800_1
```

## 4.2 Attack Vector Family — Detailed Sub-Taxonomy

```mermaid
graph TD
    vectorRoot[Attack Vector Families]

    vectorRoot --> mal[Malware Family]
    vectorRoot --> dos[DoS or DDoS Family]
    vectorRoot --> spo[Spoofing or MITM Family]
    vectorRoot --> inj[Injection Family]
    vectorRoot --> soc[Social Engineering Family]

    mal --> mal1[Virus]
    mal --> mal2[Worm]
    mal --> mal3[Trojan Horse]
    mal --> mal4[Ransomware]
    mal --> mal5[Rootkit]
    mal --> mal6[Botnet]

    dos --> dos1[Volumetric Flood]
    dos --> dos2[Protocol Attack SYN Flood]
    dos --> dos3[Application Layer Slowloris]

    spo --> spo1[IP Spoofing]
    spo --> spo2[ARP Spoofing]
    spo --> spo3[DNS Spoofing]
    spo --> spo4[MITM]

    inj --> inj1[SQL Injection]
    inj --> inj2[Cross Site Scripting XSS]
    inj --> inj3[Buffer Overflow]
    inj --> inj4[Command Injection]

    soc --> soc1[Phishing]
    soc --> soc2[Vishing]
    soc --> soc3[Smishing]
    soc --> soc4[Pretexting]
```

## 4.3 OSI-Layer Attack Mapping (Functional Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    OSI[OSI 7 Layer Stack and Common Attacks]

    OSI --> L7Block[Application Layer L7]
    OSI --> L4Block[Transport Layer L4]
    OSI --> L3Block[Network Layer L3]
    OSI --> L2Block[Data Link Layer L2]
    OSI --> L1Block[Physical Layer L1]

    L7Block --> A1[SQL Injection and XSS and Phishing]
    L7Block --> C1[Defense: WAF and Input Validation]

    L4Block --> A2[SYN Flood and UDP Flood]
    L4Block --> C2[Defense: Stateful Firewall]

    L3Block --> A3[IP Spoofing and ICMP Flood]
    L3Block --> C3[Defense: ACL and uRPF]

    L2Block --> A4[ARP Spoofing and MAC Flooding]
    L2Block --> C4[Defense: Port Security and 802.1X]

    L1Block --> A5[Cable Tapping]
    L1Block --> C5[Defense: Fiber and Faraday Cage]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between active and passive attacks with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

A **passive attack** attempts to *learn or read* information without altering system resources. It is difficult to detect because data flow is not changed. Example: an attacker using **Wireshark** to capture unencrypted HTTP credentials on a Wi-Fi network — this is a passive attack because no packet is modified.

An **active attack** attempts to *modify* system resources or affect normal operation. It is detectable but harder to prevent. Example: a **SYN Flood attack** that overwhelms a server's connection table, making it unreachable — the attacker actively injects malicious traffic.

| Parameter | Passive | Active |
|---|---|---|
| Goal | Read / Learn | Modify / Disrupt |
| Detection | Hard | Easier |
| Prevention | Easier (encryption) | Harder |
| X.800 Mapping | Interception | Modification / Interruption / Fabrication |

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List the four security threats defined by the ITU-T X.800 standard and identify which security service is violated by each.**

**Model Answer:**

The ITU-T X.800 Recommendation defines four canonical threats:

1. **Interruption** — an asset becomes unavailable → violates **Availability**.
2. **Interception** — an unauthorized entity gains access → violates **Confidentiality**.
3. **Modification** — unauthorized tampering with data → violates **Integrity**.
4. **Fabrication** — insertion of counterfeit objects → violates **Authenticity** (and often Non-Repudiation).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Every Module carries at least one 14-mark question. The examiner will offer you a choice between **Question A** and **Question B** — both from Module 4. Each question has two sub-parts of 7 marks each. Your answer should be approximately **6–8 pages** long for full marks.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **classification of network attacks based on the X.800 threat model**. With a neat diagram, describe how each threat maps to a fundamental security service.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A company's web server has been receiving **10 million HTTP requests per second** from over 50,000 unique IP addresses worldwide, causing a complete outage. Classify this attack using **all four classification dimensions** (Behavior, X.800, OSI Layer, Vector Family) and recommend **three specific countermeasures**.

**Model Answer — Part (a):**

The **ITU-T X.800 standard** provides the internationally accepted taxonomy for network security threats. It classifies threats into four mutually exclusive categories based on the **effect on the system's CIA Triad**:

**Step 1 — Interruption (Threat to Availability)**

The system asset is destroyed or becomes unavailable. Example: a disk failure or a DoS attack. Countermeasure: **redundancy**, **backup**, **DDoS mitigation appliances**.

**Step 2 — Interception (Threat to Confidentiality)**

An unauthorized party gains access to an asset. This is the classic **passive attack**. Examples: wiretapping, illicit file copying. Countermeasure: **encryption**, **access control**.

**Step 3 — Modification (Threat to Integrity)**

An unauthorized party not only accesses but *tamper with* an asset. Examples: altering a program, modifying the contents of a message in transit. Countermeasure: **message authentication codes (MAC)**, **digital signatures**.

**Step 4 — Fabrication (Threat to Authenticity)**

An unauthorized party inserts counterfeit objects into the system. Examples: insertion of spoofed packets, fake ARP replies. Countermeasure: **digital certificates**, **PKI**, **mutual authentication**.

**Diagram:**

```
                X.800 Threats
                      |
   -----------------------------------------
   |              |              |          |
Interruption  Interception   Modification  Fabrication
   |              |              |          |
Availability  Confidentiality  Integrity  Authenticity
```

**Model Answer — Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Classification:**

- **Behavior:** **Active** (the attacker is injecting traffic into the network).
- **X.800 Threat:** **Interruption** → violates **Availability**.
- **OSI Layer:** **Layer 7 — Application Layer** (HTTP GET flood).
- **Vector Family:** **Distributed Denial-of-Service (DDoS)** of the *Application-Layer / Volumetric* subtype.

**Step 2 — Three Recommended Countermeasures:**

1. **Rate Limiting / Web Application Firewall (WAF):** Configure a WAF (e.g., AWS Shield, Cloudflare) to filter malicious GET requests based on rate, geo-blocking, and signature heuristics.
2. **Anycast Network Scrubbing:** Route incoming traffic through a DDoS-scrubbing CDN that absorbs volumetric floods closer to the source, far from the origin server.
3. **CAPTCHA and Bot-Behavior Profiling:** Deploy CAPTCHA challenges for suspicious clients and use behavioral analytics to distinguish browsers from bots (e.g., TLS fingerprinting, JS-challenge puzzles).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**(a) [7 Marks]** With suitable examples, classify network attacks based on **(i) passive vs active behavior** and **(ii) OSI layer targeted**. Present the answer in tabular form.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain **ARP Spoofing**, **Replay Attack**, and **SQL Injection** with their target OSI layers, the X.800 threat each represents, and **two countermeasures per attack**.

**Model Answer — Part (a):**

| Classification Axis | Category 1 | Category 2 |
|---|---|---|
| **Behavior** | Passive (e.g., Eavesdropping, Traffic Analysis) | Active (e.g., DoS, Masquerade, Replay) |
| **OSI Layer — L7** | Application (SQL Injection, XSS, Phishing) | — |
| **OSI Layer — L4** | Transport (SYN Flood, UDP Flood) | — |
| **OSI Layer — L3** | Network (IP Spoofing, Smurf) | — |
| **OSI Layer — L2** | Data Link (ARP Spoofing, MAC Flooding) | — |
| **OSI Layer — L1** | Physical (Cable Tap, EM Eavesdropping) | — |

**Passive Attack Examples:**
- **Eavesdropping:** Attacker listens to unencrypted Wi-Fi traffic.
- **Traffic Analysis:** Observing encrypted packet sizes and timings to deduce organizational activity.

**Active Attack Examples:**
- **DoS:** SYN Flood exhausts connection table.
- **Masquerade:** IP Spoofing fakes source identity.

**Model Answer — Part (b):**

**1. ARP Spoofing**
- **Target OSI Layer:** Layer 2 (Data Link), crossing into Layer 3.
- **X.800 Threat:** **Fabrication / Masquerade** — the attacker forges ARP replies impersonating the gateway.
- **Countermeasures:** (i) **Dynamic ARP Inspection (DAI)** on managed switches, (ii) **Static ARP entries** for critical hosts.

**2. Replay Attack**
- **Target OSI Layer:** Layer 7 (Application), often Layer 4 during capture.
- **X.800 Threat:** **Modification** — the system state is altered by re-execution of a valid transaction.
- **Countermeasures:** (i) **Nonces** (one-time random numbers), (ii) **Timestamps + Sequence Numbers**.

**3. SQL Injection**
- **Target OSI Layer:** Layer 7 (Application).
- **X.800 Threat:** **Modification / Fabrication** — attacker forges queries that alter the database.
- **Countermeasures:** (i) **Parameterized Queries / Prepared Statements**, (ii) **Input Validation** and **WAF rules**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Lose Marks on this Topic:**
> 1. **Confusing "Threat" with "Attack":** A *threat* is the potential; an *attack* is the realization. Examiners deduct marks if these are used interchangeably.
> 2. **Forgetting the 4th Dimension:** Students often describe only Passive vs. Active but skip the X.800 mapping. Always state **which X.800 threat** your classified attack represents.
> 3. **OSI Layer Mistakes:** SQL Injection is *Application Layer (L7)*, not "Database layer" (databases are not an OSI layer). ARP Spoofing is **L2**, not L3.
> 4. **Mixing Passive and Active:** Replay Attack involves a *passive capture* followed by an *active retransmission* — but it is classified as **Active** because the *system effect* is modification.
> 5. **No Countermeasure Listed:** A 14-mark answer without recommended defenses is considered incomplete. Always close with **at least 2 countermeasures**.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- **Definition:** A network attack is any deliberate attempt to breach CIA of a system.
- **Four Classification Axes:** (1) Passive vs Active, (2) X.800 Threats, (3) OSI Layer, (4) Vector Family.
- **Passive Attacks:** Do not modify system state; aim to read or analyze. Hard to detect; countered by **encryption**.
- **Active Attacks:** Modify system state; aim to disrupt, inject, or forge. Easier to detect; countered by **integrity checks + authentication + monitoring**.
- **X.800 Four Threats:** Interruption (Availability), Interception (Confidentiality), Modification (Integrity), Fabrication (Authenticity).
- **Seven Major Vector Families:** Malware, DoS/DDoS, Sniffing, Spoofing, MITM, Injection, Social Engineering.
- **OSI Attack Hotspots:** L1 (physical tap), L2 (ARP), L3 (IP spoofing), L4 (SYN flood), L7 (SQLi/XSS/phishing).
- **Decision Algorithm:** Symptom → Behavior → X.800 → Layer → Vector → Countermeasure.
- **Golden Rule for KTU Answers:** Map every attack to all four dimensions in a single table.
- **Countermeasure Mapping:** Passive → encryption; Active DoS → firewalls/CDN; Injection → WAF/input validation; Spoofing → authentication/PKI; Malware → EDR/antivirus; Social Engineering → user training.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Security Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT631 - NETWORK & DATA SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Security Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are Security Methods?

In the context of **Network and Data Security**, **Security Methods** refer to the comprehensive set of mechanisms, protocols, tools, and procedural controls employed to protect data, systems, networks, and digital assets from unauthorized access, modification, disruption, or destruction. These methods collectively enforce the core pillars of information security — **Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability (CIA Triad)** — along with **Authentication, Authorization, Non-Repudiation, and Accountability**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
> Security Methods are the structured technical and administrative controls that safeguard information assets against threats and vulnerabilities. They include authentication mechanisms, access control models, firewall architectures, intrusion detection/prevention systems, virtual private networks, and public key infrastructure frameworks.

## 1.2 The Three Pillars of Information Security (CIA Triad)

| Pillar | Definition | Example Method |
|--------|------------|----------------|
| **Confidentiality** | Ensures that data is accessible only to authorized users | Encryption (AES, RSA) |
| **Integrity** | Ensures that data is not altered or tampered with during transit or storage | Hashing (SHA-256), Digital Signatures |
| **Availability** | Ensures that systems and data are accessible when needed by authorized users | Redundancy, Firewalls, DDoS protection |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bank Vault" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine a bank's vault as your computer network.
> - **Authentication** is like showing your **ID card** at the entrance.
> - **Access Control** decides which **locker** you can open.
> - **Firewalls** act as the **security guard** checking everyone at the door.
> - **Encryption** is like putting your money in a **sealed, coded box** that only the receiver can open.
> - **Intrusion Detection Systems (IDS)** are the **CCTV cameras** that record suspicious activity.
>
> Just as a bank uses multiple layers of security (guards, vaults, alarms, cameras), modern networks use a **defense-in-depth** strategy combining several security methods.

## 1.4 Categories of Security Methods

The KTU 2024 syllabus classifies Security Methods into the following primary categories:

1. **Authentication Methods** — Verifying the identity of a user, device, or entity.
2. **Access Control Mechanisms** — Determining what an authenticated user is allowed to do.
3. **Perimeter Security (Firewalls)** — Filtering traffic between networks.
4. **Intrusion Detection and Prevention Systems (IDS/IPS)** — Monitoring and responding to threats.
5. **Virtual Private Networks (VPNs)** — Securing communication over public networks.
6. **Public Key Infrastructure (PKI)** — Managing digital certificates and keys.
7. **Biometric Security** — Using physiological/behavioral traits for authentication.

## 1.5 Key Terminology

> [!NOTE]
> **Must-Know Terms for KTU Exam:**
> - **Subject:** The entity (user, process) requesting access.
> - **Object:** The resource (file, database, server) being accessed.
> - **Principal:** The identity associated with a subject.
> - **Threat:** A potential cause of an unwanted incident.
> - **Vulnerability:** A weakness that can be exploited.
> - **Risk:** The probability of a threat exploiting a vulnerability.
> - **Defense-in-Depth:** A layered security approach using multiple methods.
> - **Trust Boundary:** The point where data or execution changes its level of trust.

## 1.6 Why Study Security Methods?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per the **KTU 2024 Scheme Outcome-Based Education (OBE)** framework, understanding Security Methods enables the engineer to:
> - Design secure network architectures (**CO4** — Design/Develop).
> - Analyze vulnerabilities in existing systems (**CO3** — Apply/Analyze).
> - Implement appropriate countermeasures (**CO5** — Implement/Evaluate).
> - Comply with cybersecurity standards and legal frameworks.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Authentication Methods

Authentication is the process of **verifying the claimed identity** of a user, system, or entity. It is the **first line of defense** in any security architecture.

### 2.1.1 Three Factors of Authentication

| Factor | Description | Examples |
|--------|-------------|----------|
| **Knowledge Factor (Something you know)** | Information only the user knows | Passwords, PINs, Security Questions |
| **Possession Factor (Something you have)** | Physical or digital token in user's possession | Smart Cards, OTP tokens, Mobile Phones |
| **Biometric Factor (Something you are)** | Inherent physical or behavioral traits | Fingerprint, Iris, Voice, Face |

> [!NOTE]
> **Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA)** combines two or more factors. For example, an ATM requires both a **card** (possession) and a **PIN** (knowledge).

### 2.1.2 Password-Based Authentication

Despite its weaknesses, passwords remain the most common authentication method. Security is measured by **entropy**.

$$ H = L \times \log_2(N) $$

Where:
- $H$ = Entropy in bits
- $L$ = Length of the password
- $N$ = Size of the character set (e.g., 26 for lowercase, 95 for full ASCII)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** A password with $L = 8$ characters from $N = 95$ ASCII characters has $H = 8 \times \log_2(95) \approx 52.4$ bits of entropy. Higher entropy means higher security.

### 2.1.3 Biometric Authentication

Biometrics uses unique physiological or behavioral characteristics. Key metrics for evaluating biometric systems are:

**False Acceptance Rate (FAR):**
$$ \text{FAR} = \frac{\text{Number of false acceptances}}{\text{Total number of impostor attempts}} $$

**False Rejection Rate (FRR):**
$$ \text{FRR} = \frac{\text{Number of false rejections}}{\text{Total number of genuine attempts}} $$

**Equal Error Rate (EER):** The point where FAR = FRR. A **lower EER** indicates a **better biometric system**.

### 2.1.4 Challenge-Response Authentication

In this method, the server sends a **random challenge** that the user must answer using a shared secret or private key.

$$ \text{Response} = f(\text{Challenge}, \text{Secret}) $$

## 2.2 Access Control Models

Access Control determines **what an authenticated user is allowed to do**. There are three primary models:

### 2.2.1 Discretionary Access Control (DAC)

- The **owner** of the resource decides who can access it.
- Implemented via **Access Control Lists (ACLs)**.
- Example: File permissions in Linux (`rwx` for owner, group, others).

### 2.2.2 Mandatory Access Control (MAC)

- Access is based on **security labels** and **clearance levels**.
- The system enforces policies; users cannot change them.
- Example: Military classification (Top Secret, Secret, Confidential, Unclassified).

### 2.2.3 Role-Based Access Control (RBAC)

- Access is based on the **role** of the user within an organization.
- Users are assigned roles; roles are assigned permissions.
- Example: A "Doctor" role can access patient records; a "Receptionist" role cannot.

| Feature | DAC | MAC | RBAC |
|---------|-----|-----|------|
| **Policy Set By** | Resource Owner | System Administrator | System Administrator |
| **Flexibility** | High | Low | Medium |
| **Security Level** | Medium | Very High | High |
| **Ease of Administration** | Difficult | Difficult | Easy |
| **Example** | Linux file permissions | Military systems | Enterprise systems |

### 2.2.4 Attribute-Based Access Control (ABAC)

A more recent model where access decisions are based on **attributes** of the user, resource, and environment.

$$ \text{Access Decision} = f(\text{User Attributes}, \text{Resource Attributes}, \text{Environment Attributes}) $$

## 2.3 Firewall Architectures

A **firewall** is a network security device that monitors and filters incoming and outgoing network traffic based on predefined security policies.

### 2.3.1 Types of Firewalls

| Type | OSI Layer | Key Feature | Limitation |
|------|-----------|-------------|------------|
| **Packet Filtering Firewall** | Network (L3) | Inspects packet headers (IP, Port) | Cannot inspect payload |
| **Stateful Inspection Firewall** | Network-Transport (L3-L4) | Tracks connection state | Resource-intensive |
| **Application-Level Gateway (Proxy)** | Application (L7) | Inspects application-layer data | Slow, protocol-specific |
| **Circuit-Level Gateway** | Session (L5) | Validates TCP handshakes | Limited content inspection |
| **Next-Generation Firewall (NGFW)** | L3-L7 | Deep Packet Inspection + IDS + App awareness | Expensive, complex |

### 2.3.2 Firewall Rule Processing

When a packet arrives, the firewall evaluates it against a **rule base** in **top-to-bottom order**. The first matching rule determines the action:

$$ \text{Action} = \begin{cases} \text{ACCEPT} & \text{if rule matches and action = allow} \\ \text{DROP} & \text{if rule matches and action = deny} \\ \text{Default Policy} & \text{if no rule matches} \end{cases} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Default Deny (Implicit Deny):** The recommended best practice is to set the default policy to **DROP**, so any unmatched traffic is blocked.

## 2.4 Intrusion Detection and Prevention Systems (IDS/IPS)

### 2.4.1 Intrusion Detection System (IDS)

An IDS monitors network traffic or system activities for **malicious activity** and generates alerts. It is **passive** — it does not block traffic.

**Types of IDS:**
- **NIDS (Network-based IDS):** Monitors traffic on an entire network segment.
- **HIDS (Host-based IDS):** Monitors activities on a single host.

### 2.4.2 Detection Methods

| Method | Technique | Advantage | Disadvantage |
|--------|-----------|-----------|--------------|
| **Signature-Based Detection** | Compares traffic to known attack patterns (signatures) | Low false positives | Cannot detect zero-day attacks |
| **Anomaly-Based Detection** | Establishes a baseline of normal behavior; flags deviations | Detects unknown attacks | High false positive rate |
| **Stateful Protocol Analysis** | Tracks protocol state to identify deviations | Protocol-aware | Resource-intensive |

### 2.4.3 Intrusion Prevention System (IPS)

An IPS is an **active** device that not only detects but also **blocks** malicious traffic. It sits **inline** in the network path.

## 2.5 Virtual Private Networks (VPNs)

A VPN creates an **encrypted tunnel** over a public network to ensure secure communication.

### 2.5.1 VPN Tunneling Protocols

| Protocol | Encryption | Port | Use Case |
|----------|-----------|------|----------|
| **PPTP** | MPPE (weak) | 1723 | Legacy, insecure |
| **L2TP/IPSec** | IPSec | 1701 | Standard, secure |
| **OpenVPN** | SSL/TLS | 1194 | Open-source, flexible |
| **IKEv2/IPSec** | IPSec | 500/4500 | Mobile, fast reconnection |
| **WireGuard** | ChaCha20 | 51820 | Modern, lightweight |

## 2.6 Public Key Infrastructure (PKI)

PKI is a framework for managing **digital certificates** and **public-key encryption**. Key components include:

- **Certificate Authority (CA):** Trusted entity that issues digital certificates.
- **Registration Authority (RA):** Verifies the identity of certificate applicants.
- **Digital Certificate:** Binds a public key to an identity.
- **Certificate Revocation List (CRL):** List of revoked certificates.
- **OCSP (Online Certificate Status Protocol):** Real-time certificate status checking.

### 2.6.1 Digital Certificate Structure (X.509)

A standard X.509 certificate contains:
- **Version** (e.g., v3)
- **Serial Number** (unique CA-assigned ID)
- **Issuer** (CA's Distinguished Name)
- **Validity Period** (Not Before, Not After)
- **Subject** (Owner's Distinguished Name)
- **Public Key** (Subject's public key + algorithm)
- **Signature** (CA's digital signature over all fields)

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Key Point |
|---------|----------------|-----------|
| Password Entropy | $H = L \times \log_2(N)$ | Higher $L$ and $N$ = stronger password |
| Biometric FAR | $\text{FAR} = \frac{FA}{FA + \text{Rejected Impostors}}$ | Lower is better |
| Biometric FRR | $\text{FRR} = \frac{FR}{FR + \text{Accepted Genuine}}$ | Lower is better |
| Firewall Rule Order | First match wins (top-down) | Place specific rules first |
| RSA Key Size | Security $\propto$ key length | Min **2048 bits** recommended |
| AES Key Sizes | 128, 192, or 256 bits | 256-bit is military-grade |
| Hash Output Size | SHA-256 → 256 bits, SHA-512 → 512 bits | Collision resistance |
| VPN Tunnel | $\text{Data} = E_{K}(\text{Plaintext} \vert \text{Header})$ | Encapsulation |
| RBAC | User $\to$ Role $\to$ Permission | Separation of duties |
| Bell-LaPadula | No Read Up, No Write Down | Confidentiality model |
| Biba Model | No Read Down, No Write Up | Integrity model |

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Security methods are applied in:
> - **Banking:** MFA, encryption, firewalls for online transactions.
> - **Healthcare:** RBAC for patient records, HIPAA compliance.
> - **Cloud Computing:** PKI, VPNs, IAM (Identity and Access Management).
> - **IoT:** Lightweight authentication and encryption.
> - **Government/Military:** MAC, classified network architectures.
> - **E-commerce:** SSL/TLS certificates, digital signatures for trust.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Password Entropy Calculation

**Problem:** Calculate the entropy of an 8-character password using only lowercase English letters ($N = 26$). How does the entropy change if we include uppercase letters, digits, and special characters ($N = 95$)?

### Case 1: Lowercase Only

$$ H_1 = L \times \log_2(N) $$

Substituting $L = 8$ and $N = 26$:

$$ H_1 = 8 \times \log_2(26) $$

We compute $\log_2(26)$:

$$ \log_2(26) = \frac{\log_{10}(26)}{\log_{10}(2)} = \frac{1.41497}{0.30103} \approx 4.7004 $$

Therefore:

$$ H_1 = 8 \times 4.7004 \approx 37.60 \text{ bits} $$

### Case 2: Full ASCII Character Set

Substituting $L = 8$ and $N = 95$:

$$ H_2 = 8 \times \log_2(95) $$

We compute $\log_2(95)$:

$$ \log_2(95) = \frac{\log_{10}(95)}{\log_{10}(2)} = \frac{1.97772}{0.30103} \approx 6.5699 $$

Therefore:

$$ H_2 = 8 \times 6.5699 \approx 52.56 \text{ bits} $$

### Analysis

The entropy **increases** by approximately $14.96$ bits when expanding from lowercase-only to the full ASCII character set. This is because each character now carries more information (more possible values per position).

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** For a fixed length, **increasing the character set size** is an effective way to strengthen passwords. However, **increasing length** is generally more effective than increasing character set diversity.

## 3.2 Derivation: Brute-Force Time Estimation

**Problem:** How long does it take to brute-force a password with $H = 52.56$ bits of entropy, assuming an attacker can test $10^9$ (1 billion) passwords per second?

### Step 1: Total Search Space

The total number of possible passwords is:

$$ S = 2^H $$

$$ S = 2^{52.56} $$

$$ \log_{10}(S) = 52.56 \times \log_{10}(2) = 52.56 \times 0.30103 \approx 15.82 $$

$$ S = 10^{15.82} \approx 6.61 \times 10^{15} $$

### Step 2: Average Time to Crack

On average, the attacker tests half the search space:

$$ S_{\text{avg}} = \frac{S}{2} = \frac{6.61 \times 10^{15}}{2} = 3.305 \times 10^{15} $$

### Step 3: Time Calculation

With a rate of $R = 10^9$ guesses/second:

$$ T = \frac{S_{\text{avg}}}{R} = \frac{3.305 \times 10^{15}}{10^9} = 3.305 \times 10^6 \text{ seconds} $$

Converting to days:

$$ T = \frac{3.305 \times 10^6}{86400} \approx 38.25 \text{ days} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** A password with 52.56 bits of entropy can withstand a brute-force attack at 1 billion guesses/second for approximately **38 days**. This demonstrates why strong passwords and rate-limiting are critical.

## 3.3 Derivation: Biometric EER Calculation

**Problem:** Given the following test results for a fingerprint biometric system:
- Total genuine attempts: 1000
- Total impostor attempts: 1000
- False Rejections (FR): 20
- False Acceptances (FA): 5

Calculate FAR, FRR, and determine the EER (assume EER is approximately at the crossover point).

### Step 1: Calculate FAR

$$ \text{FAR} = \frac{\text{Number of false acceptances}}{\text{Total number of impostor attempts}} $$

$$ \text{FAR} = \frac{5}{1000} = 0.005 = 0.5\% $$

### Step 2: Calculate FRR

$$ \text{FRR} = \frac{\text{Number of false rejections}}{\text{Total number of genuine attempts}} $$

$$ \text{FRR} = \frac{20}{1000} = 0.020 = 2.0\% $$

### Step 3: Interpretation

The system has a **lower FAR (0.5%)** than **FRR (2.0%)**, meaning it is more secure against impostors but less convenient for legitimate users. The EER would be at the point where FAR = FRR, which is not reached in this single operating point.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** A security system with a very low FAR is suitable for **high-security environments** (e.g., nuclear facilities), while a system with a low FRR is better for **user-friendly applications** (e.g., smartphone unlock).

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Security Methods

| Criterion | Passwords | Biometrics | Smart Cards | PKI/Digital Certificates |
|-----------|-----------|------------|-------------|--------------------------|
| **Security Level** | Low-Medium | High | High | Very High |
| **User Convenience** | High | High | Medium | Medium |
| **Cost** | Low | High | Medium | Medium |
| **Revocability** | Easy (change password) | Impossible | Possible | Possible (revoke cert) |
| **Forgery Resistance** | Low | Very High | High | Very High |
| **Privacy Concerns** | Low | High | Low | Low |
| **Scalability** | High | Medium | Medium | High |
| **Best Use Case** | General login | Physical access | Banking, gov IDs | Web security (HTTPS) |

## 3.5 Derivation: RSA Key Strength Estimation

**Problem:** Estimate the number of operations required to factor an RSA modulus $n$ using the General Number Field Sieve (GNFS), the fastest known factoring algorithm.

The complexity of GNFS is approximately:

$$ L_n = e^{c \cdot (\ln n)^{1/3} \cdot (\ln \ln n)^{2/3}} $$

For a 2048-bit RSA key ($n \approx 2^{2048}$), the security level is approximately **112 bits**, meaning it would take $2^{112}$ operations to factor.

$$ \text{Time to break} = \frac{2^{112}}{10^9} \text{ seconds} \approx 1.6 \times 10^{17} \text{ years} $$

> [!NOTE]
> This demonstrates why 2048-bit RSA is considered secure against classical computing attacks, but vulnerable to **future quantum computers** running Shor's algorithm.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Defense-in-Depth Architecture

This diagram illustrates the **layered security model** where multiple security methods work together to protect a network.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA["External Threats and Attackers"] --> nodeB["Perimeter Layer: Firewall and IDS/IPS"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Network Layer: VPN and Network Segmentation"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Host Layer: HIDS and Antivirus"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Application Layer: Authentication and RBAC"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["Data Layer: Encryption and Hashing"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["Secure Data Center"]
    nodeB -.Alerts.-> nodeH["SIEM and Monitoring"]
    nodeD -.Logs.-> nodeH
    nodeE -.Audit Trails.-> nodeH
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Explanation:** Each layer provides an independent barrier. If one layer is breached, the next layer still protects the system. The SIEM (Security Information and Event Management) collects alerts from all layers for centralized monitoring.

## 4.2 Authentication Workflow

This diagram shows the step-by-step process of **multi-factor authentication**.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["User Initiates Login"] --> nodeB["Step 1: Enter Username and Password"]
    nodeB --> nodeC{"Password Valid?"}
    nodeC -- No --> nodeD["Access Denied - Log Failure"]
    nodeC -- Yes --> nodeE["Step 2: Send OTP to Registered Device"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["User Enters OTP"]
    nodeF --> nodeG{"OTP Valid?"}
    nodeG -- No --> nodeD
    nodeG -- Yes --> nodeH["Step 3: Biometric Verification"]
    nodeH --> nodeI{"Biometric Match?"}
    nodeI -- No --> nodeD
    nodeI -- Yes --> nodeJ["Access Granted - Create Session"]
    nodeJ --> nodeK["Log Audit Trail to SIEM"]
```

## 4.3 PKI Certificate Verification Flow

This diagram illustrates how a **digital certificate** is verified in a PKI hierarchy.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA["Client Receives Server Certificate"] --> nodeB["Step 1: Check Certificate Validity Period"]
    nodeB --> nodeC{"Within Validity?"}
    nodeC -- No --> nodeD["Reject: Expired Certificate"]
    nodeC -- Yes --> nodeE["Step 2: Check Issuer against Trusted CA Store"]
    nodeE --> nodeF{"Trusted CA?"}
    nodeF -- No --> nodeG["Reject: Untrusted Issuer"]
    nodeF -- Yes --> nodeH["Step 3: Verify CA Digital Signature"]
    nodeH --> nodeI{"Signature Valid?"}
    nodeI -- No --> nodeJ["Reject: Tampered Certificate"]
    nodeI -- Yes --> nodeK["Step 4: Check CRL or OCSP for Revocation"]
    nodeK --> nodeL{"Revoked?"}
    nodeL -- Yes --> nodeM["Reject: Certificate Revoked"]
    nodeL -- No --> nodeN["Accept: Establish Secure TLS Session"]
```

## 4.4 Firewall Rule Processing Flow

This diagram shows how a firewall processes incoming packets against its rule base.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Incoming Packet Arrives"] --> nodeB["Extract Header: Source IP, Dest IP, Port, Protocol"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Compare with Rule 1"]
    nodeC --> nodeD{"Rule 1 Matches?"}
    nodeD -- Yes --> nodeE["Apply Action: Allow or Deny"]
    nodeD -- No --> nodeF["Compare with Rule 2"]
    nodeF --> nodeG{"Rule 2 Matches?"}
    nodeG -- Yes --> nodeE
    nodeG -- No --> nodeH["Compare with Subsequent Rules"]
    nodeH --> nodeI{"Any Rule Matches?"}
    nodeI -- Yes --> nodeE
    nodeI -- No --> nodeJ["Apply Default Policy: Implicit Deny"]
    nodeE --> nodeK["Log Decision"]
    nodeJ --> nodeK
```

## 4.5 Access Control Decision Model

This block diagram maps the **ABAC (Attribute-Based Access Control)** decision process.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["User Attributes: Role, Department, Clearance"] --> nodeD["Policy Decision Point PDP"]
    nodeB["Resource Attributes: Type, Sensitivity, Owner"] --> nodeD
    nodeC["Environment Attributes: Time, Location, Device"] --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE{"Policy Evaluation"}
    nodeE -- Permit --> nodeF["Policy Enforcement Point PEP: Grant Access"]
    nodeE -- Deny --> nodeG["PEP: Block Access and Log Event"]
```

---
