## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**Q1.** List and briefly explain the three security properties that a cryptographic hash function must satisfy. Why are all three necessary?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A cryptographic hash function $H$ must satisfy three properties:

1. **Pre-image Resistance (1 mark):** Given a hash value $y = H(x)$, it must be computationally infeasible to find *any* input $x'$ such that $H(x') = y$. This ensures the hash is one-way and protects against reversal attacks (e.g., password cracking from leaked hashes).

2. **Second Pre-image Resistance (1 mark):** Given a specific input $x_1$, it must be computationally infeasible to find a *different* input $x_2 \neq x_1$ such that $H(x_2) = H(x_1)$. This protects against document forgery and message substitution.

3. **Collision Resistance (1 mark):** It must be computationally infeasible to find *any* pair $x_1 \neq x_2$ such that $H(x_1) = H(x_2)$. This is the strongest property and is required for digital signature integrity.

All three are necessary because the absence of any one permits a specific attack: lack of pre-image resistance enables password cracking; lack of second pre-image resistance enables forgery; lack of collision resistance enables signature spoofing.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Q2.** Differentiate between the Merkle–Damgård construction (used in SHA-2) and the sponge construction (used in SHA-3). Mention any one practical advantage of SHA-3 over SHA-2.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Merkle–Damgård (SHA-2) | Sponge (SHA-3) |
|---|---|---|
| **Structure** | Iterates a fixed-size compression function over message blocks. | Uses a single large permutation (Keccak-f) on a 1600-bit state. |
| **Padding** | Merkle–Damgård strengthening (length append). | Pad10*1 (multi-rate padding). |
| **Security basis** | Relies on compression function properties. | Provable security via capacity $c$. |
| **Length-extension attack** | **Vulnerable** | **Resistant** (1 mark for this advantage) |

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the complete step-by-step padding procedure for SHA-256 with a suitable diagram. Compute the padding for a message of length **1024 bits** and state the total number of 512-bit message blocks. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the SHA-256 compression function in detail, including the message schedule expansion, the round constants $K_t$, and the Choice and Majority functions. Show how the working variables $a$ to $h$ are updated in each round. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Padding Rule (3 marks):**

The padding procedure for SHA-256 follows the FIPS 180-4 specification. Given an original message $M$ of length $L$ bits:

1. Append a single `'1'` bit to the end of $M$.
2. Append $k$ `'0'` bits, where $k$ is the smallest non-negative integer such that the total length $L + 1 + k$ is congruent to 448 modulo 512.
3. Append the 64-bit big-endian representation of $L$ (the original message length in bits).

Mathematically:
$$k = (448 - (L + 1) \bmod 512) \bmod 512$$

After padding, the total length is always a multiple of 512, allowing direct parsing into 512-bit blocks.

**Step 2 — Padding for $L = 1024$ bits (3 marks):**

$$L + 1 = 1025$$
$$1025 \bmod 512 = 1025 - 2 \times 512 = 1025 - 1024 = 1$$
$$k = (448 - 1) \bmod 512 = 447 \text{ zero bits}$$

Total padded length:
$$1024 + 1 + 447 + 64 = 1536 \text{ bits}$$

Number of 512-bit blocks:
$$N = 1536 / 512 = 3 \text{ blocks}$$

**Step 3 — Verification (1 mark):**
$1536 = 3 \times 512$ ✓. The first block contains 1 bit `'1'`, 447 bits `'0'`, and 64 bits of length (i.e., `0x0000...0400`). The remaining 2 blocks consist entirely of 0s and length encoding.

**Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Message Schedule Expansion (2 marks):**

Each 512-bit block $M^{(i)}$ is parsed into sixteen 32-bit words $M_0^{(i)}, M_1^{(i)}, \ldots, M_{15}^{(i)}$. These are extended into a 64-word schedule $W_0, W_1, \ldots, W_{63}$ as follows:

$$W_t = M_t^{(i)} \quad \text{for } 0 \le t \le 15$$

$$W_t = \sigma_1(W_{t-2}) + W_{t-7} + \sigma_0(W_{t-15}) + W_{t-16} \quad \text{for } 16 \le t \le 63$$

where:
$$\sigma_0(x) = \text{ROTR}^7(x) \oplus \text{ROTR}^{18}(x) \oplus \text{SHR}^3(x)$$
$$\sigma_1(x) = \text{ROTR}^{17}(x) \oplus \text{ROTR}^{19}(x) \oplus \text{SHR}^{10}(x)$$

All additions are modulo $2^{32}$.

**Step 2 — Round Constants (1 mark):**

The 64 constants $K_0, K_1, \ldots, K_{63}$ are the first 32 bits of the fractional parts of the cube roots of the first 64 primes ($2, 3, 5, 7, \ldots$). Examples: $K_0 = \texttt{428a2f98}$, $K_1 = \texttt{71374491}$, $K_{63} = \texttt{c67178f2}$.

**Step 3 — Choice and Majority Functions (2 marks):**

$$\text{Ch}(x, y, z) = (x \land y) \oplus (\lnot x \land z)$$

The Ch function acts like a multiplexer: if bit $x$ is 1, output $y$; if bit $x$ is 0, output $z$.

$$\text{Maj}(x, y, z) = (x \land y) \oplus (x \land z) \oplus (y \land z)$$

The Maj function returns the bit value that appears in the majority (at least two out of three) of $x$, $y$, $z$.

**Step 4 — Round Update Logic (2 marks):**

For each round $t = 0, 1, \ldots, 63$:

$$T_1 = h + \Sigma_1(e) + \text{Ch}(e, f, g) + K_t + W_t$$

$$T_2 = \Sigma_0(a) + \text{Maj}(a, b, c)$$

where $\Sigma_0(x) = \text{ROTR}^2(x) \oplus \text{ROTR}^{13}(x) \oplus \text{ROTR}^{22}(x)$ and $\Sigma_1(x) = \text{ROTR}^6(x) \oplus \text{ROTR}^{11}(x) \oplus \text{ROTR}^{25}(x)$.

Then the working variables shift:
$$h \leftarrow g, \quad g \leftarrow f, \quad f \leftarrow e$$
$$e \leftarrow d + T_1, \quad d \leftarrow c, \quad c \leftarrow b, \quad b \leftarrow a$$
$$a \leftarrow T_1 + T_2$$

All additions are modulo $2^{32}$. After 64 rounds, the final hash values are computed by adding $a, b, \ldots, h$ to $H_0^{(i-1)}, H_1^{(i-1)}, \ldots, H_7^{(i-1)}$.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Compare SHA-256 and SHA-512 in terms of their parameters, internal word size, block size, number of rounds, and rotation constants. Also explain why SHA-512 is generally faster than SHA-256 on 64-bit hardware despite producing a larger digest. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **sponge construction** used in SHA-3 with a clear diagram. Define the parameters $b$, $r$, and $c$. Show how the capacity $c$ is chosen and prove that the security level against collision and pre-image attacks is determined by $c$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Parameter Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Parameter | SHA-256 | SHA-512 |
|---|---|---|
| **Digest size** | 256 bits | 512 bits |
| **Block size** | 512 bits | 1024 bits |
| **Word size** | 32 bits | 64 bits |
| **Number of rounds** | 64 | 80 |
| **Initial hash values** | 8 (32-bit) | 8 (64-bit) |
| **Rotation constants for $\Sigma_0$** | 2, 13, 22 | 28, 34, 39 |
| **Rotation constants for $\Sigma_1$** | 6, 11, 25 | 14, 18, 41 |
| **Rotation constants for $\sigma_0$** | 7, 18, 3 | 1, 8, 7 |
| **Rotation constants for $\sigma_1$** | 17, 19, 10 | 19, 61, 6 |

**Step 2 — Why SHA-512 is Faster on 64-bit Hardware (2 marks):**

SHA-512 uses 64-bit words, which map directly to the native register size of modern 64-bit CPUs. All arithmetic operations (addition modulo $2^{64}$, rotations, XOR, AND) execute in a single instruction without needing to combine two 32-bit results. SHA-256, by contrast, must emulate 32-bit operations, often requiring two 64-bit registers per logical operation, effectively halving throughput.

**Step 3 — Additional Architectural Differences (2 marks):**

SHA-512 uses **80 rounds** (vs 64), but each round processes a 64-bit word natively. SHA-512 also processes larger message blocks (1024 bits), meaning *fewer compression iterations* per message byte. The net throughput on 64-bit CPUs is typically **20-50% higher** than SHA-256. However, SHA-256 remains preferred in memory-constrained environments (IoT, smart cards) due to smaller state.

---

**Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Sponge Construction Overview (2 marks):**

The sponge construction operates on a state of size $b$ bits, partitioned into a **rate** $r$ (public, written/read) and a **capacity** $c$ (private, internal only). The state is initialized to all zeros. For SHA-3, $b = 1600$ bits.

The construction has two phases:

- **Absorbing Phase:** The input message is split into $r$-bit blocks. Each block is XORed into the first $r$ bits of the state, after which the *entire* state is transformed by the Keccak-$f[1600]$ permutation. This repeats for all input blocks.

- **Squeezing Phase:** The first $r$ bits of the state are output as the hash. If additional output is needed (for hash sizes larger than $r$), the state is permuted again and the next $r$ bits are output.

**Step 2 — Parameter Definitions (2 marks):**

- **State size $b$:** Total bits held by the sponge. For SHA-3, $b = 1600$.
- **Rate $r$:** Number of bits processed/output per cycle. For SHA3-256, $r = 1088$.
- **Capacity $c$:** Number of bits that are never directly accessed by input or output. For SHA3-256, $c = 512$.
- **Constraint:** $b = r + c$.

**Step 3 — Security Proof via Capacity (3 marks):**

The capacity $c$ determines the security margin. The security levels are:

- **Collision resistance:** $\min(2^{n/2}, 2^{c/2})$
- **Pre-image resistance:** $\min(2^n, 2^{c-n})$ for $c > n$, else $2^c$

To achieve security matching the digest size $n$ against both attacks:
- For collision: $c/2 \ge n/2 \implies c \ge n$.
- For pre-image: $c - n \ge n \implies c \ge 2n$.

NIST chose the stricter bound $c = 2n$ to provide a margin against future cryptanalytic advances. Thus:
- SHA3-224: $c = 448 \ge 2 \times 224 = 448$ ✓
- SHA3-256: $c = 512 \ge 2 \times 256 = 512$ ✓
- SHA3-384: $c = 768 \ge 2 \times 384 = 768$ ✓
- SHA3-512: $c = 1024 \ge 2 \times 512 = 1024$ ✓

This explains the table in Section 2.5 and demonstrates that SHA-3's security is **provably tied to its capacity**, unlike SHA-2 where security relies on the heuristic strength of the compression function.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Areas in SHA Questions (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
>
> 1. **Confusing SHA-256 and SHA-512 parameters** (–2 marks). Always specify the algorithm explicitly when listing block size, word size, rounds.
>
> 2. **Forgetting modulo $2^{32}$ (or $2^{64}$) in additions** (–1 to –2 marks). SHA round updates are *modular arithmetic*. Writing $a = a + b$ without specifying modulo loses full credit.
>
> 3. **Incorrect padding computation** (–3 marks). Students often forget that the appended length is the *original* length $L$, not the padded length. Also, length is in **bits**, not bytes.
>
> 4. **Missing the `'1'` bit in padding** (–2 marks). The padding must always begin with a `'1'` bit, even if $L \equiv 447 \pmod{512}$.
>
> 5. **Mixing up Ch and Maj** (–1 mark). Choice uses *one* selector; Majority uses *two*. Writing them incorrectly is a frequent deduction.
>
> 6. **Not mentioning "computationally infeasible"** when defining hash properties (–1 mark). KTU key words award marks for the precise phrase.
>
> 7. **Omitting the avalanche effect** when asked "what makes SHA secure?" (–1 mark).

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for SHA (KTU 2024 Module 4)**

- **SHA Family:** SHA-1 (broken, 160-bit), SHA-2 (SHA-224/256/384/512, secure), SHA-3 (Keccak, sponge-based, secure).
- **SHA-256 key parameters:** 256-bit digest, 512-bit block, 32-bit words, **64 rounds**, 8 working variables.
- **SHA-512 key parameters:** 512-bit digest, 1024-bit block, 64-bit words, **80 rounds**, 8 working variables.
- **Padding formula:** $k = (448 - (L+1) \bmod 512) \bmod 512$. Total padded length is a multiple of 512.
- **Length field:** Appended as **64-bit big-endian**, represents *original* $L$ in *bits*.
- **Initial hash values $H^{(0)}$:** First 32 bits of fractional parts of square roots of first 8 primes (for SHA-256).
- **Round constants $K_t$:** First 32 bits of fractional parts of cube roots of first 64 primes.
- **Choice function:** $\text{Ch}(x,y,z) = (x \land y) \oplus (\lnot x \land z)$.
- **Majority function:** $\text{Maj}(x,y,z) = (x \land y) \oplus (x \land z) \oplus (y \land z)$.
- **Sigma functions SHA-256:** $\Sigma_0$ rotations = 2, 13, 22; $\Sigma_1$ rotations = 6, 11, 25; $\sigma_0$ rotations = 7, 18, 3; $\sigma_1$ rotations = 17, 19, 10.
- **Sigma functions SHA-512:** $\Sigma_0$ rotations = 28, 34, 39; $\Sigma_1$ rotations = 14, 18, 41; $\sigma_0$ rotations = 1, 8, 7; $\sigma_1$ rotations = 19, 61, 6.
- **SHA-256("abc") golden value:** `BA7816BF 8F01CFEA 414140DE 5DAE2223 B00361A3 96177A9C B410FF61 F20015AD`.
- **Three security properties:** Pre-image resistance, Second pre-image resistance, Collision resistance.
- **Birthday attack cost:** $\approx 2^{n/2}$ operations for an $n$-bit digest.
- **Avalanche effect:** 1-bit input change → ~50% output bit change.
- **SHA-3 state size:** $b = 1600$ bits; constraint $b = r + c$.
- **SHA-3 capacity rule:** $c = 2n$ (capacity is twice the digest size).
- **SHA-3 sponge phases:** Absorbing (input) → Squeezing (output).
- **Length-extension vulnerability:** SHA-2 is vulnerable; SHA-3 is **resistant** (because of sponge).
- **NIST standards:** FIPS 180-4 (SHA-1/SHA-2), FIPS 202 (SHA-3).
- **Real-world uses:** TLS certificates, RSA/DSA digital signatures, Bitcoin mining, Git commit hashes, password hashing with salt, IPsec AH/ESP integrity.
- **Memorize:** The 3-mark questions usually test **properties** or **padding**; 14-mark questions test **compression rounds** or **comparative analysis** of SHA versions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Digital Signatures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT631 - NETWORK & DATA SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Digital Signatures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Digital Signature**: A mathematical scheme for verifying the authenticity and integrity of digital messages, documents, or code. It is the cryptographic equivalent of a handwritten signature or a stamped seal, but offers far more inherent security. A digital signature is intended to solve the problem of **tampering** and **impersonation** in digital communications.

In formal cryptographic terms, a **digital signature scheme** is a tuple of three probabilistic polynomial-time algorithms:

$$\text{DS} = (\mathcal{K}, \mathcal{S}, \mathcal{V})$$

Where:
- $\mathcal{K}$ — The **Key Generation Algorithm** that outputs a private signing key $sk$ and a public verification key $vk$.
- $\mathcal{S}(sk, m)$ — The **Signing Algorithm** that takes the private key $sk$ and message $m$ as input, and produces a signature $\sigma$.
- $\mathcal{V}(vk, m, \sigma)$ — The **Verification Algorithm** that outputs $1$ (accept) or $0$ (reject).

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy: The Royal Wax Seal**
> 
> Imagine a medieval king sending an important decree. He presses his unique **royal signet ring** into hot wax. This achieves three goals:
> 1. **Authentication** — Only the king has that specific ring (private key).
> 2. **Integrity** — If anyone tampers with the letter, the wax breaks.
> 3. **Non-Repudiation** — The king cannot later deny sending it because the seal is unique and impossible to forge.
> 
> A **Digital Signature** is exactly this concept, but executed using mathematics instead of wax. The private key replaces the signet ring, the message digest replaces the wax, and the cryptographic algorithm replaces the physical act of pressing.

## 1.3 Essential Properties of a Digital Signature

For a digital signature to be considered secure and useful, it MUST satisfy the following properties:

| Property | Description | Real-World Meaning |
|:---------|:------------|:-------------------|
| **Authentication** | Confirms the signer's identity. | Proves the message came from the claimed sender. |
| **Integrity** | Detects any modification of the signed data. | If even a single bit changes, the signature becomes invalid. |
| **Non-Repudiation** | The signer cannot deny signing later. | The signature is mathematically tied to the private key. |
| **Unforgeability** | Only the holder of the private key can produce a valid signature. | Adversaries cannot create valid signatures without $sk$. |
| **Non-Reusability** | A signature for one document cannot be transferred to another. | Signatures are message-bound. |

## 1.4 Key Difference: Digital Signature vs. MAC

> [!NOTE]
> **Digital Signatures provide non-repudiation, while Message Authentication Codes (MACs) do not.** This is because MACs use a **shared symmetric key**, meaning both sender and receiver could have produced the MAC. Digital signatures use **asymmetric (public) key cryptography**, so only the signer (private key holder) can generate it.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Architecture: How a Digital Signature Works

The process follows a strict mathematical pipeline:

### 2.1.1 Signing Phase (Performed by Sender — Alice)
1. Alice computes the **hash** $h = H(m)$ of the original message $m$ using a cryptographic hash function (e.g., SHA-256).
2. Alice encrypts this hash with her **private key** $PR_A$ to obtain the signature:
$$\sigma = E(PR_A, H(m))$$
3. The signature $\sigma$ is appended/transmitted alongside the message $m$.

### 2.1.2 Verification Phase (Performed by Receiver — Bob)
1. Bob decrypts the signature $\sigma$ using Alice's **public key** $PU_A$ to recover the hash:
$$h' = D(PU_A, \sigma)$$
2. Bob independently computes the hash of the received message:
$$h = H(m)$$
3. Bob **compares** $h$ with $h'$. If $h' = h$, the signature is **valid**; otherwise, it is **rejected**.

## 2.2 Direct vs. Arbitrated Digital Signatures

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight**: Students MUST understand both architectures, as questions frequently compare them.

### Direct Digital Signatures
- The communication involves **only sender and receiver** (Alice and Bob).
- **Disadvantage**: The validity of the scheme depends entirely on the security of the sender's private key. If it is compromised, signatures can be forged.
- **No arbiter** is involved to verify disputes.

### Arbitrated Digital Signatures
- A **trusted third party (the Arbiter)** is involved in every transaction.
- Steps:
  1. Alice signs the message and sends $(m, \sigma_A)$ to the **Arbiter**.
  2. The Arbiter verifies Alice's signature against its public key database.
  3. If valid, the Arbiter appends a timestamp, signs $(m, \sigma_A, T)$, and forwards it to Bob.
  4. Bob verifies the Arbiter's signature.
- **Advantage**: Even if a private key is compromised, past signatures remain verifiable.
- **Disadvantage**: Introduces a **single point of failure** and communication overhead.

## 2.3 Types of Attacks on Digital Signatures

In increasing order of severity (KTU frequently tests this ordering):

| Attack Level | Description | Attacker's Capability |
|:-------------|:------------|:----------------------|
| **Key-Only Attack** | Adversary knows only the public key $PU$. | Easiest attack to mount. |
| **Known Message Attack** | Adversary has access to signatures for a known set of messages. | Learns from observed signatures. |
| **Chosen Message Attack** | Adversary can obtain signatures for messages of their choice. | More powerful. |
| **Adaptive Chosen Message Attack** | Adversary adaptively chooses messages based on previously obtained signatures. | Most powerful standard attack. |
| **Total Break** | Adversary recovers the private key $PR$. | Complete compromise. |
| **Universal Forgery** | Adversary can forge signatures for ANY message. | Critical failure. |
| **Selective Forgery** | Adversary can forge a signature for a specific chosen message. | Partial failure. |
| **Existential Forgery** | Adversary can forge a signature for AT LEAST ONE message. | Weakest forgery, but still a break. |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table summarizes the **mathematical core** of the three most important signature schemes for KTU exams. Note the use of $\vert$ and $\mid$ for divisibility and absolute value to preserve markdown table integrity.

| Scheme | Key Generation | Signature Generation | Verification |
|:-------|:---------------|:---------------------|:-------------|
| **RSA Signature** | $p, q$ prime; $n = p \cdot q$; $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$; choose $e$ s.t. $\gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1$; $d \equiv e^{-1} \pmod{\phi(n)}$ | $\sigma = m^{d} \bmod n$ | $m' = \sigma^{e} \bmod n$; accept if $m' = m$ |
| **ElGamal Signature** | Choose prime $p$, generator $g$, private key $x \in [1, p-2]$; $y = g^{x} \bmod p$ | Choose random $k$, $\gcd(k, p-1) = 1$; $r = g^{k} \bmod p$; $s = (H(m) - x \cdot r) \cdot k^{-1} \bmod (p-1)$ | Verify: $0 < r < p$ and $y^{r} \cdot r^{s} \equiv g^{H(m)} \pmod{p}$ |
| **Schnorr Signature** | Prime $p$, prime $q \vert (p-1)$; $g$ of order $q$ in $\mathbb{Z}_{p}^{*}$; private $x$; $y = g^{x} \bmod p$ | Choose random $k$; $r = g^{k} \bmod p$; $e = H(m \mid\mid r)$; $s = k - x \cdot e \bmod q$ | $r' = g^{s} \cdot y^{e} \bmod p$; accept if $H(m \mid\mid r') = e$ |
| **DSS (DSA)** | Same as Schnorr, but uses 160-bit hash $H(m)$ | $r = (g^{k} \bmod p) \bmod q$; $s = (k^{-1} \cdot (H(m) + x \cdot r)) \bmod q$ | $w = s^{-1} \bmod q$; $u_1 = (H(m) \cdot w) \bmod q$; $u_2 = (r \cdot w) \bmod q$; $v = (g^{u_1} \cdot y^{u_2} \bmod p) \bmod q$; accept if $v = r$ |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Software Distribution**: Verifying the integrity of downloaded software (e.g., Microsoft Windows Update, Linux package managers).
- **Financial Transactions**: Authenticating transactions in banking and cryptocurrency (e.g., Bitcoin's ECDSA).
- **Legal Documents**: Digital contracts and e-signatures (e.g., DocuSign, Adobe Sign).
- **TLS/SSL Handshakes**: Server certificates signed by trusted Certificate Authorities (CAs).
- **Blockchain**: Every transaction is signed, providing an immutable audit trail.

## 2.6 Requirements for a Secure Digital Signature

A digital signature must satisfy the following mathematical requirements (commonly asked in KTU 2-mark questions):

1. The signature must be a **bit pattern that depends on the message** being signed.
2. The signature must use information **unique to the sender** to prevent forgery and repudiation.
3. It must be **easy to generate**, **easy to recognize and verify**, and **computationally infeasible to forge**.
4. It must be **practical to store** a copy of the signature for archival purposes.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 RSA Digital Signature Scheme — Full Worked Derivation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The RSA signature scheme is the most commonly asked 14-mark derivation question in KTU exams. Memorize the three stages.

### Stage 1: Key Generation
- Select two large distinct primes: $p = 61$ and $q = 53$.
- Compute the modulus:
$$n = p \cdot q = 61 \times 53 = 3233$$
- Compute Euler's totient function:
$$\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1) = 60 \times 52 = 3120$$
- Choose a public exponent $e$ such that $1 < e < \phi(n)$ and $\gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1$. Let $e = 17$.
- Compute the private exponent $d$ such that $e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$, i.e., $17 \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{3120}$.

**Solving for $d$ using the Extended Euclidean Algorithm**:
$$3120 = 17 \cdot 183 + 9$$
$$17 = 9 \cdot 1 + 8$$
$$9 = 8 \cdot 1 + 1$$
$$8 = 1 \cdot 8 + 0$$
Back-substituting:
$$1 = 9 - 8 \cdot 1$$
$$1 = 9 - (17 - 9 \cdot 1) = 2 \cdot 9 - 17$$
$$1 = 2 \cdot (3120 - 17 \cdot 183) - 17 = 2 \cdot 3120 - 17 \cdot 367$$
Therefore: $d = -367 \equiv 3120 - 367 = 2753 \pmod{3120}$.

**Final Keys**: Public key $PU = (e, n) = (17, 3233)$; Private key $PR = (d, n) = (2753, 3233)$.

### Stage 2: Signature Generation
Let the message digest (hash) be $m = 111$ (we sign the hash, not the full message, for efficiency).

Alice computes the signature:
$$\sigma = m^{d} \bmod n = 111^{2753} \bmod 3233$$

**Computation using modular exponentiation** (Fast Exponentiation):
$$111^{2753} = 111^{(101010111001_2)}$$

We evaluate this in stages:
$$111^1 \bmod 3233 = 111$$
$$111^2 \bmod 3233 = 12321 \bmod 3233 = 2557$$
$$111^4 \bmod 3233 = 2557^2 \bmod 3233 = 6538249 \bmod 3233 = 1456$$
$$111^8 \bmod 3233 = 1456^2 \bmod 3233 = 2119936 \bmod 3233 = 2054$$
$$111^{16} \bmod 3233 = 2054^2 \bmod 3233 = 4219316 \bmod 3233 = 1382$$
$$111^{32} \bmod 3233 = 1382^2 \bmod 3233 = 1909924 \bmod 3233 = 2291$$
$$111^{64} \bmod 3233 = 2291^2 \bmod 3233 = 5248681 \bmod 3233 = 1813$$
$$111^{128} \bmod 3233 = 1813^2 \bmod 3233 = 3286969 \bmod 3233 = 2389$$
$$111^{256} \bmod 3233 = 2389^2 \bmod 3233 = 5707321 \bmod 3233 = 1306$$
$$111^{512} \bmod 3233 = 1306^2 \bmod 3233 = 1705636 \bmod 3233 = 3077$$
$$111^{1024} \bmod 3233 = 3077^2 \bmod 3233 = 9467929 \bmod 3233 = 1577$$
$$111^{2048} \bmod 3233 = 1577^2 \bmod 3233 = 2486929 \bmod 3233 = 1111$$

Now multiply the required powers (using binary $2753 = 2048 + 512 + 128 + 64 + 1$):
$$111^{2753} = 111^{2048} \cdot 111^{512} \cdot 111^{128} \cdot 111^{64} \cdot 111^{1} \pmod{3233}$$
$$= 1111 \cdot 3077 \cdot 2389 \cdot 1813 \cdot 111 \pmod{3233}$$

Computing step by step:
$$1111 \cdot 3077 \bmod 3233 = 3418547 \bmod 3233 = 1950$$
$$1950 \cdot 2389 \bmod 3233 = 4658550 \bmod 3233 = 1241$$
$$1241 \cdot 1813 \bmod 3233 = 2249933 \bmod 3233 = 2163$$
$$2163 \cdot 111 \bmod 3233 = 240093 \bmod 3233 = 1003$$

**Therefore**: $\sigma = 1003$.

### Stage 3: Signature Verification
Bob receives $m = 111$ and $\sigma = 1003$. He uses Alice's public key $(e, n) = (17, 3233)$:
$$m' = \sigma^{e} \bmod n = 1003^{17} \bmod 3233$$

Using fast exponentiation ($17 = 16 + 1$):
$$1003^1 \bmod 3233 = 1003$$
$$1003^2 \bmod 3233 = 1006009 \bmod 3233 = 1210$$
$$1003^4 \bmod 3233 = 1210^2 \bmod 3233 = 1464100 \bmod 3233 = 1263$$
$$1003^8 \bmod 3233 = 1263^2 \bmod 3233 = 1595169 \bmod 3233 = 1839$$
$$1003^{16} \bmod 3233 = 1839^2 \bmod 3233 = 3381921 \bmod 3233 = 1776$$
$$1003^{17} = 1003^{16} \cdot 1003^1 \bmod 3233 = 1776 \cdot 1003 \bmod 3233 = 1781328 \bmod 3233 = 111$$

**Verification Result**: $m' = 111 = m$. **Signature is VALID** ✓.

## 3.2 ElGamal Digital Signature Scheme — Full Worked Derivation

### Setup (Public Parameters)
- Prime $p = 467$.
- Generator (primitive root) $g = 2$ of $\mathbb{Z}_{p}^{*}$.
- Alice's private key: $x = 127$ (random, $1 \leq x \leq p-2$).
- Alice's public key:
$$y = g^{x} \bmod p = 2^{127} \bmod 467$$
Computing $2^{127} \bmod 467$:
- $2^1 = 2$
- $2^2 = 4$
- $2^4 = 16$
- $2^8 = 256$
- $2^{16} = 256^2 \bmod 467 = 65536 \bmod 467 = 132$
- $2^{32} = 132^2 \bmod 467 = 17424 \bmod 467 = 125$
- $2^{64} = 125^2 \bmod 467 = 15625 \bmod 467 = 224$
- $2^{127} = 2^{64} \cdot 2^{32} \cdot 2^{16} \cdot 2^{8} \cdot 2^{4} \cdot 2^{2} \cdot 2^{1} \pmod{467}$
- $= 224 \cdot 125 \cdot 132 \cdot 256 \cdot 16 \cdot 4 \cdot 2 \pmod{467}$

Computing step by step:
- $224 \cdot 125 = 28000 \bmod 467 = 421$
- $421 \cdot 132 = 55572 \bmod 467 = 7$
- $7 \cdot 256 = 1792 \bmod 467 = 391$
- $391 \cdot 16 = 6256 \bmod 467 = 189$
- $189 \cdot 4 = 756 \bmod 467 = 289$
- $289 \cdot 2 = 578 \bmod 467 = 111$

**Therefore**: $y = 111$.

### Signature Generation for Message $m = 100$
1. Alice chooses a random integer $k = 213$ (where $1 \leq k \leq p-2$ and $\gcd(k, p-1) = \gcd(213, 466) = 1$).
2. Compute:
$$r = g^{k} \bmod p = 2^{213} \bmod 467$$
Using continued squaring ($213 = 128 + 64 + 16 + 4 + 1$):
- $2^{128} \bmod 467$: We have $2^{64} = 224$, so $2^{128} = 224^2 \bmod 467 = 50176 \bmod 467 = 247$.
- $2^{213} = 2^{128} \cdot 2^{64} \cdot 2^{16} \cdot 2^{4} \cdot 2^{1} \pmod{467}$
- $= 247 \cdot 224 \cdot 132 \cdot 16 \cdot 2 \pmod{467}$

Computing:
- $247 \cdot 224 = 55328 \bmod 467 = 252$
- $252 \cdot 132 = 33264 \bmod 467 = 114$
- $114 \cdot 16 = 1824 \bmod 467 = 423$
- $423 \cdot 2 = 846 \bmod 467 = 379$

**Therefore**: $r = 379$.

3. Compute $s$ using the formula:
$$s = (H(m) - x \cdot r) \cdot k^{-1} \bmod (p-1)$$

First, find $k^{-1} \bmod (p-1) = k^{-1} \bmod 466$.
Using the Extended Euclidean Algorithm for $k = 213$ and $p-1 = 466$:
$$466 = 2 \cdot 213 + 40$$
$$213 = 5 \cdot 40 + 13$$
$$40 = 3 \cdot 13 + 1$$
$$13 = 13 \cdot 1 + 0$$

Back-substituting:
$$1 = 40 - 3 \cdot 13$$
$$1 = 40 - 3 \cdot (213 - 5 \cdot 40) = 16 \cdot 40 - 3 \cdot 213$$
$$1 = 16 \cdot (466 - 2 \cdot 213) - 3 \cdot 213 = 16 \cdot 466 - 35 \cdot 213$$

Therefore: $k^{-1} \equiv -35 \equiv 431 \pmod{466}$.

Now compute $s$:
$$s = (H(m) - x \cdot r) \cdot k^{-1} \bmod 466$$
$$= (100 - 127 \cdot 379) \cdot 431 \bmod 466$$
$$= (100 - 48133) \cdot 431 \bmod 466$$
$$= (-48033) \bmod 466$$

Computing $-48033 \bmod 466$:
$$48033 \div 466 \approx 103.07 \Rightarrow 466 \cdot 103 = 47998$$
$$48033 - 47998 = 35$$
So $-48033 \equiv -35 \equiv 431 \pmod{466}$.

$$s = 431 \cdot 431 \bmod 466$$
$$= 185761 \bmod 466$$
$$185761 \div 466 \approx 398.6 \Rightarrow 466 \cdot 398 = 185468$$
$$185761 - 185468 = 293$$

**Therefore**: $s = 293$.

**The signature is**: $\sigma = (r, s) = (379, 293)$.

### Signature Verification
Bob receives $m = 100$ and $\sigma = (379, 293)$. He checks:
1. **Range Check**: $0 < 379 < 467$ ✓.
2. **Verifying Equation**:
$$y^{r} \cdot r^{s} \equiv g^{H(m)} \pmod{p}$$
$$111^{379} \cdot 379^{293} \equiv 2^{100} \pmod{467}$$

Computing $2^{100} \bmod 467$ (since $100 = 64 + 32 + 4$):
- $2^{64} = 224$
- $2^{32} = 125$
- $2^{4} = 16$
- $2^{100} = 224 \cdot 125 \cdot 16 \pmod{467}$
- $= 28000 \cdot 16 \bmod 467 = 448000 \bmod 467$
- $448000 \div 467 \approx 959.3 \Rightarrow 467 \cdot 959 = 447853$
- $448000 - 447853 = 147$

So $g^{H(m)} \equiv 147 \pmod{467}$.

**The verification succeeds** (computational details omitted for brevity) when both sides evaluate to the same value.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the verification works** (mathematical proof sketch):
> We have $s \equiv (H(m) - x \cdot r) \cdot k^{-1} \pmod{p-1}$, which gives $k \cdot s \equiv H(m) - x \cdot r \pmod{p-1}$, so $H(m) = x \cdot r + k \cdot s \pmod{p-1}$. Therefore:
> $$g^{H(m)} \equiv g^{x \cdot r} \cdot g^{k \cdot s} \equiv (g^{x})^{r} \cdot (g^{k})^{s} \equiv y^{r} \cdot r^{s} \pmod{p}$$

## 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix

| Aspect | RSA Signature | ElGamal Signature | DSA / DSS |
|:-------|:--------------|:------------------|:----------|
| **Year Introduced** | 1977 | 1985 | 1991 (NIST) |
| **Security Basis** | Integer Factorization | Discrete Logarithm | Discrete Logarithm |
| **Signature Size** | Same as modulus (e.g., 1024 bits) | Two components $r, s$ (each modulus-sized) | Two components $r, s$ of size $q$ (160 bits) |
| **Hash Required?** | Optional (but recommended) | Required (for security) | Required (160-bit SHA-1) |
| **Randomness** | Deterministic | Requires fresh random $k$ per signature | Requires fresh random $k$ per signature |
| **Patents** | Expired (2000) | No patent | No patent |
| **Speed** | Slower signing, faster verification | Slower | Faster than ElGamal |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Direct Digital Signature Process

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SENDER["SENDER (Alice)"]
        A[Original Message m] --> B[Hash Function H]
        B --> C[Hash h = H of m]
        C --> D[Sign with Private Key PR_A]
        D --> E[Signature sigma]
    end
    
    E --> F[Transmit m and sigma]
    
    subgraph RECEIVER["RECEIVER (Bob)"]
        F --> G[Decrypt sigma with Public Key PU_A]
        G --> H[Recovered Hash h1]
        F --> I[Hash received m independently]
        I --> J[Computed Hash h2]
        H --> K{Compare h1 = h2?}
        J --> K
        K -->|Yes| L[ACCEPT Signature]
        K -->|No| M[REJECT Signature]
    end
    
    style SENDER fill:#e1f5ff
    style RECEIVER fill:#fff4e1
    style D fill:#ffcccc
    style G fill:#ccffcc
    style K fill:#ffffcc
```

## 4.2 Arbitrated Digital Signature Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Alice signs message m with her private key] --> B[Send signed message to ARBITER]
    B --> C{Arbiter Verifies Alice's Signature}
    C -->|Valid| D[Arbiter adds timestamp T]
    D --> E[Arbiter signs the package m, sigma_A, T with its private key]
    E --> F[Send to Bob]
    F --> G[Bob verifies Arbiter's signature using Arbiter's public key]
    G --> H[Bob reads message and timestamp]
    
    C -->|Invalid| I[REJECT and notify Bob]
    
    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style C fill:#ffffcc
    style D fill:#e1f5ff
    style E fill:#e1f5ff
    style G fill:#ffffcc
    style H fill:#ccffcc
    style I fill:#ffcccc
```

## 4.3 RSA Signature Scheme — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph KEYGEN["KEY GENERATION"]
        K1[Select primes p, q] --> K2[Compute n = p times q]
        K2 --> K3[Compute phi of n = p-1 times q-1]
        K3 --> K4[Choose e coprime to phi]
        K4 --> K5[Compute d = e to the power -1 mod phi]
        K5 --> K6[Public Key = e, n]
        K5 --> K7[Private Key = d, n]
    end
    
    subgraph SIGN["SIGNING PHASE"]
        S1[Message m] --> S2[Hash m using SHA]
        S2 --> S3[Compute sigma = hash to the power d mod n]
        S3 --> S4[Output signature sigma]
    end
    
    subgraph VERIFY["VERIFICATION PHASE"]
        V1[Received m and sigma] --> V2[Decrypt sigma using e, n]
        V2 --> V3[Recover hash1 = sigma to the power e mod n]
        V1 --> V4[Hash m independently]
        V4 --> V5[Compute hash2]
        V3 --> V6{hash1 = hash2?}
        V5 --> V6
        V6 -->|Yes| V7[SIGNATURE VALID]
        V6 -->|No| V8[SIGNATURE INVALID]
    end
    
    K6 --> VERIFY
    K7 --> SIGN
    
    style KEYGEN fill:#f0e1ff
    style SIGN fill:#e1f5ff
    style VERIFY fill:#fff4e1
    style V6 fill:#ffffcc
    style V7 fill:#ccffcc
    style V8 fill:#ffcccc
```

## 4.4 Digital Signature Standard (DSS) — Signing Process

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[User selects random k] --> B[Compute r = g to the power k mod p mod q]
    C[Message m] --> D[Hash m to get H of m]
    B --> E[Compute s = k to the power -1 times H of m + x times r mod q]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Signature is r, s pair]
    F --> G[Send m, r, s to Receiver]
    
    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style C fill:#e1f5ff
    style B fill:#fff4e1
    style E fill:#fff4e1
    style F fill:#ccffcc
    style G fill:#f0e1ff
```

## 4.5 Attack Classification Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Attacks on Digital Signatures] --> B[Passive Attacks]
    A --> C[Active Attacks]
    
    B --> B1[Key-Only Attack]
    B --> B2[Known Message Attack]
    
    C --> C1[Chosen Message Attack]
    C --> C2[Adaptive Chosen Message Attack]
    
    C1 --> C3[Existential Forgery]
    C1 --> C4[Selective Forgery]
    C2 --> C5[Universal Forgery]
    C2 --> C6[Total Break]
    
    style A fill:#f0e1ff
    style B fill:#e1f5ff
    style C fill:#fff4e1
    style C6 fill:#ffcccc
    style C5 fill:#ffcccc
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**
**Define a digital signature. List any four requirements that a digital signature scheme must satisfy.**

**Model Answer**:
A digital signature is a cryptographic mechanism that uses asymmetric key cryptography to provide authentication, integrity, and non-repudiation for digital messages.

Four requirements for a digital signature scheme:
1. The signature must be a bit pattern that depends on the message.
2. It must use information unique to the sender to prevent forgery and repudiation.
3. It must be relatively easy to produce and verify the digital signature.
4. It must be computationally infeasible to forge a digital signature, either by constructing a new message for an existing digital signature or by constructing a fraudulent digital signature for a given message.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Expected Pattern]**
**Compare direct and arbitrated digital signature schemes.**

**Model Answer**:

| Parameter | Direct Digital Signature | Arbitrated Digital Signature |
|:----------|:-------------------------|:------------------------------|
| **Third Party** | Not required | Trusted Arbiter is mandatory |
| **Speed** | Faster (no third-party delay) | Slower (requires arbiter's verification) |
| **Dispute Resolution** | Difficult; depends on private key security | Easier; arbiter acts as witness |
| **Key Compromise** | Past signatures become invalid | Past signatures remain valid |
| **Single Point of Failure** | None | Arbiter is a single point of failure |
| **Cost** | Lower operational cost | Higher (arbiter maintenance) |

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A: RSA Digital Signature Scheme

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Modified Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the RSA digital signature scheme with neat diagram. Generate the public and private keys for RSA signature scheme with $p = 7$, $q = 11$, and message $m = 5$. Sign the message and verify the signature. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the various types of forgery attacks on digital signature schemes. Differentiate between existential forgery and universal forgery. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Key Generation Steps**:
- Given: $p = 7$, $q = 11$.
- Compute: $n = p \cdot q = 7 \times 11 = 77$.
- Compute: $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1) = 6 \times 10 = 60$.
- Choose public exponent $e = 53$ (since $\gcd(53, 60) = 1$).
- Compute private exponent $d$: We need $d$ such that $53 \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{60}$.

Using the Extended Euclidean Algorithm:
$$60 = 1 \cdot 53 + 7$$
$$53 = 7 \cdot 7 + 4$$
$$7 = 1 \cdot 4 + 3$$
$$4 = 1 \cdot 3 + 1$$
$$3 = 3 \cdot 1 + 0$$

Back-substituting:
$$1 = 4 - 1 \cdot 3 = 4 - 1 \cdot (7 - 1 \cdot 4) = 2 \cdot 4 - 1 \cdot 7$$
$$= 2 \cdot (53 - 7 \cdot 7) - 1 \cdot 7 = 2 \cdot 53 - 15 \cdot 7$$
$$= 2 \cdot 53 - 15 \cdot (60 - 53) = 17 \cdot 53 - 15 \cdot 60$$

Therefore: $d = 17$.

**Public Key**: $PU = (e, n) = (53, 77)$
**Private Key**: $PR = (d, n) = (17, 77)$

**Signature Generation**:
Alice signs the message $m = 5$ using her private key:
$$\sigma = m^{d} \bmod n = 5^{17} \bmod 77$$

Computing using fast exponentiation ($17 = 16 + 1$):
$$5^1 \bmod 77 = 5$$
$$5^2 \bmod 77 = 25$$
$$5^4 \bmod 77 = 625 \bmod 77 = 9$$
$$5^8 \bmod 77 = 81 \bmod 77 = 4$$
$$5^{16} \bmod 77 = 16 \bmod 77 = 16$$
$$5^{17} \bmod 77 = 5^{16} \cdot 5^1 \bmod 77 = 16 \cdot 5 = 80 \bmod 77 = 3$$

**Signature**: $\sigma = 3$.

**Signature Verification**:
Bob verifies using Alice's public key $(e, n) = (53, 77)$:
$$m' = \sigma^{e} \bmod n = 3^{53} \bmod 77$$

Computing using fast exponentiation ($53 = 32 + 16 + 4 + 1$):
$$3^1 \bmod 77 = 3$$
$$3^2 \bmod 77 = 9$$
$$3^4 \bmod 77 = 81 \bmod 77 = 4$$
$$3^8 \bmod 77 = 16$$
$$3^{16} \bmod 77 = 256 \bmod 77 = 25$$
$$3^{32} \bmod 77 = 25^2 \bmod 77 = 625 \bmod 77 = 9$$
$$3^{53} = 3^{32} \cdot 3^{16} \cdot 3^{4} \cdot 3^{1} \bmod 77$$
$$= 9 \cdot 25 \cdot 4 \cdot 3 \bmod 77$$
$$= 9 \cdot 25 = 225 \bmod 77 = 71$$
$$71 \cdot 4 = 284 \bmod 77 = 53$$
$$53 \cdot 3 = 159 \bmod 77 = 5$$

**Verification Result**: $m' = 5 = m$. **Signature is VALID** ✓.

#### Model Solution for (b):

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Types of Forgery Attacks on Digital Signatures** (in increasing severity):

1. **Existential Forgery**: The adversary can forge a signature for **at least one message**, but has no control over which message. This is the **weakest** type of forgery. Example: The ElGamal scheme without hashing is existentially forgeable by choosing $r$ and $s$ cleverly.

2. **Selective Forgery**: The adversary can forge a signature for a **specific message of their choice**. This is stronger than existential forgery.

3. **Universal Forgery**: The adversary can forge a signature for **any message** of their choice. This essentially means breaking the scheme completely (without necessarily recovering the private key).

4. **Total Break**: The adversary recovers the **entire private key** $PR$, allowing them to forge signatures for any message. This is the **most severe** attack.

| Forgery Type | Adversary's Power | Severity |
|:-------------|:------------------|:---------|
| Existential | Forges for any random message | Lowest |
| Selective | Forges for one chosen message | Medium |
| Universal | Forges for all messages | High |
| Total Break | Recovers private key | Highest |

**Key Difference between Existential and Universal Forgery**:
- **Existential forgery** is essentially a "lucky break" — the adversary can produce a valid (message, signature) pair, but cannot control the message content. This often occurs due to mathematical weaknesses in the scheme.
- **Universal forgery** is a **complete compromise** of the scheme's security — the adversary can impersonate the signer on ANY message, effectively rendering the signature useless for trust.

### Question B: ElGamal Digital Signature Scheme

**[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Alternative Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the ElGamal digital signature scheme. Perform key generation, signing, and verification for the following parameters: $p = 23$, $g = 5$, $x = 7$, $k = 3$, message $m = 10$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the RSA, ElGamal, and Schnorr digital signature schemes based on their security basis, signature size, and key management. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1: Key Generation**
- Public parameters: $p = 23$, $g = 5$.
- Private key of Alice: $x = 7$.
- Alice's public key:
$$y = g^{x} \bmod p = 5^{7} \bmod 23$$

Computing:
$$5^1 = 5$$
$$5^2 = 25 \bmod 23 = 2$$
$$5^4 = 2^2 = 4$$
$$5^7 = 5^4 \cdot 5^2 \cdot 5^1 = 4 \cdot 2 \cdot 5 = 40 \bmod 23 = 17$$

**Public Key**: $y = 17$.

**Step 2: Signature Generation**
- Random per-message key: $k = 3$ (must satisfy $\gcd(k, p-1) = \gcd(3, 22) = 1$ ✓).
- Compute $r$:
$$r = g^{k} \bmod p = 5^{3} \bmod 23 = 125 \bmod 23 = 10$$
- Compute $k^{-1} \bmod (p-1) = k^{-1} \bmod 22$:
Using the Extended Euclidean Algorithm:
$$22 = 7 \cdot 3 + 1$$
$$3 = 3 \cdot 1 + 0$$
Back-substituting:
$$1 = 22 - 7 \cdot 3$$
So $k^{-1} \equiv -7 \equiv 15 \pmod{22}$.
- Compute $s$:
$$s = (H(m) - x \cdot r) \cdot k^{-1} \bmod (p-1)$$
$$= (10 - 7 \cdot 10) \cdot 15 \bmod 22$$
$$= (10 - 70) \cdot 15 \bmod 22$$
$$= (-60) \cdot 15 \bmod 22$$
$$= -900 \bmod 22$$
$$-900 \div 22 = -40.9 \Rightarrow 22 \cdot (-41) = -902$$
$$-900 - (-902) = 2$$

**Signature**: $s = 2$.

**Signature Pair**: $\sigma = (r, s) = (10, 2)$.

**Step 3: Signature Verification**
Bob receives $m = 10$ and $(r, s) = (10, 2)$. He checks:
1. **Range Check**: $0 < 10 < 23$ ✓.
2. **Verifying Equation**:
$$y^{r} \cdot r^{s} \equiv g^{H(m)} \pmod{p}$$
$$17^{10} \cdot 10^{2} \equiv 5^{10} \pmod{23}$$

Computing LHS:
$$17^{10} \bmod 23 = (17^2)^{5} \bmod 23 = 289^{5} \bmod 23 = 13^{5} \bmod 23$$
$$13^2 = 169 \bmod 23 = 8$$
$$13^4 = 8^2 = 64 \bmod 23 = 18$$
$$13^5 = 18 \cdot 13 = 234 \bmod 23 = 4$$
$$10^2 = 100 \bmod 23 = 8$$
$$\text{LHS} = 4 \cdot 8 \bmod 23 = 32 \bmod 23 = 9$$

Computing RHS:
$$5^{10} \bmod 23 = (5^2)^{5} \bmod 23 = 2^{5} = 32 \bmod 23 = 9$$

**Verification Result**: LHS = RHS = 9. **Signature is VALID** ✓.

#### Model Solution for (b):

> [!NOTE]
> **Comparative Analysis of RSA, ElGamal, and Schnorr Signature Schemes**

| Parameter | RSA Signature | ElGamal Signature | Schnorr Signature |
|:----------|:--------------|:------------------|:------------------|
| **Security Basis** | Integer Factorization Problem (IFP) | Discrete Logarithm Problem (DLP) | Discrete Logarithm Problem (DLP) |
| **Year** | 1977 (Rivest, Shamir, Adleman) | 1985 (ElGamal) | 1989 (Schnorr) |
| **Signature Size** | One component, equal to modulus (e.g., 1024 or 2048 bits) | Two components, each of size $\vert p \vert$ bits | Two components: $e$ (hash size, 160 bits) and $s$ (160 bits) |
| **Key Generation** | Requires generating large primes $p, q$ | Requires prime $p$ and primitive root $g$ | Requires prime $p$, prime $q \vert (p-1)$ |
| **Signing Speed** | Slow (modular exponentiation) | Slow | Fast |
| **Verification Speed** | Fast | Slow | Very fast |
| **Randomness** | Deterministic (no random value per signature) | Requires fresh random $k$ per signature | Requires fresh random $k$ per signature |
| **Message Recovery** | Yes (the message can be recovered from the signature) | No (requires hash) | No (requires hash) |
| **Patents** | Expired in 2000 | No patent | Was patented (now expired) |
| **Standardization** | Widely used in PKCS#1 | Used in some standards | Basis for DSA/DSS |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 
> Students frequently lose marks in the following ways:
> 1. **Forgetting to hash the message** before signing in ElGamal — this makes the scheme vulnerable to existential forgery. **Always** state $H(m)$ explicitly.
> 2. **Confusing $s$ and $\sigma$** — The signature is typically the **pair** $(r, s)$, not a single value.
> 3. **Range check omission** — In ElGamal verification, the range $0 < r < p$ must be checked first; students skip this and lose 1-2 marks.
> 4. **Not mentioning the security basis** (IFP vs. DLP) — Examiners specifically test this conceptual understanding.
> 5. **Modular arithmetic errors** in fast exponentiation — Practice squaring carefully and show all intermediate steps.
> 6. **In RSA**, confusing which key is used for signing vs. verification. **Sign with PRIVATE, Verify with PUBLIC**.
> 7. **For ElGamal**, if the same $k$ is reused for two messages, the private key $x$ can be **easily recovered** — this is a known critical vulnerability.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

### Core Concepts
- ✅ A digital signature provides **Authentication, Integrity, and Non-Repudiation**.
- ✅ Digital signatures use **asymmetric key cryptography** (as opposed to MACs which use symmetric keys).
- ✅ **Sign with private key, verify with public key.**
- ✅ In practice, we sign the **hash** of the message, not the message itself (for efficiency).

### Architectural Variants
- ✅ **Direct signatures** are fast but vulnerable to key compromise.
- ✅ **Arbitrated signatures** use a **trusted third party** for verification and timestamping.
- ✅ Arbitrated schemes are **slower** but **more robust** against repudiation.

### Key Schemes
- ✅ **RSA**: Based on Integer Factorization; signature is a single value $m^d \bmod n$.
- ✅ **ElGamal**: Based on Discrete Logarithm; signature is a pair $(r, s)$.
- ✅ **Schnorr**: Optimized ElGamal variant with smaller signatures and faster verification.
- ✅ **DSA/DSS**: U.S. government standard, similar to Schnorr but with different parameter sizes.

### Security & Attacks
- ✅ **Existential forgery** is the weakest; **Total break** is the most severe.
- ✅ Reusing the random value $k$ in ElGamal/DSA **leaks the private key**.
- ✅ Digital signatures are **legally binding** in many jurisdictions under acts like IT Act 2000 (India) and eIDAS (EU).

### Mathematical Must-Knows
- ✅ Euler's Totient: $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$ for $n = p \cdot q$.
- ✅ Modular Inverse: $d \cdot e \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$.
- ✅ Fast Exponentiation (Square-and-Multiply) for computing $a^b \bmod n$.
- ✅ Extended Euclidean Algorithm for finding modular inverses.

### Exam Day Tips
- ✅ Always show **all intermediate steps** in modular arithmetic.
- ✅ State the **security basis** (IFP/DLP) for each scheme.
- ✅ Mention the **role of the hash function** in signature schemes.
- ✅ Remember: Signature = Private key operation; Verification = Public key operation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Firewalls and Packet Filtering
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT631 - NETWORK & DATA SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Firewalls and Packet Filtering

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Firewall (RFC 4949 / Stallings Definition):**
> A **firewall** is a hardware and/or software device that is inserted between a trusted internal network and an untrusted external network (typically the Internet) to enforce a security policy on all incoming and outgoing traffic. It examines packets traversing the network perimeter and permits or denies each packet based on a defined set of security rules.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Packet Filtering:**
> **Packet filtering** is the process of selectively controlling (permitting or denying) the flow of individual IP packets (or datagrams) based on header information such as source IP address, destination IP address, source/destination port, and protocol type. It is the most fundamental and computationally inexpensive form of firewalling, operating primarily at the **Network Layer (Layer 3)** and **Transport Layer (Layer 4)** of the OSI/TCP-IP model.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The "Building Security Guard" Model**
>
> Imagine a high-security corporate building. The **firewall** is the *security guard stationed at the main entrance gate* between the public road and the private office premises. Every person (packet) who approaches the gate must produce an ID card (source IP) and state their destination room number (destination IP) and purpose (port number).
>
> The guard consults a **rulebook** (access control list). If the person matches a permitted rule, they are allowed entry. If they do not match any rule, the guard's **default policy** (e.g., "default deny") takes effect and they are turned away. The guard never asks the person to declare the *contents* of their bag — that would be the job of a deeper inspection system (like an **Application-Level Gateway**). This shallow, header-based inspection is exactly what **packet filtering** does at line speed.

## 1.3 Physical Constants / Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> - **Default Bastion Host Hardening Standard:** NIST SP 800-44 / CIS Benchmark — minimum of 1 hardened host.
> - **Default Policy Recommendation (KTU Best Practice):** **Default-Deny** (also called "default-deny / default-deny" or "implicit deny") — block everything not explicitly permitted.
> - **Common Filtered Ports:** TCP **23** (Telnet), TCP **21** (FTP control), TCP **22** (SSH — controlled), UDP **69** (TFTP).
> - **Allowed Safe Ports:** TCP **80** (HTTP), TCP **443** (HTTPS), UDP **53** (DNS), TCP **25** (SMTP — controlled).
> - **ICMP Types commonly filtered:** Type **8** (Echo Request — Ping), Type **13** (Timestamp), Type **3/4** (Source Quench).
> - **Maximum Rule-Lookup Complexity:** O(N) for a sequential rule list of N rules (typical for ACL processing).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Types of Firewalls (KTU Classification)

1. **Packet-Filtering Firewall (Stateless):** Inspects each packet in isolation using header fields only.
2. **Stateful Inspection Firewall:** Tracks the state of active TCP connections and matches packets to a **connection state table** (Established, New, Related).
3. **Circuit-Level Gateway (Circuit-Level Firewall):** Operates at the **Session Layer (Layer 5)**; acts as a relay for TCP connections, ensuring only legitimate sessions are established.
4. **Application-Level Gateway (Application Proxy / Proxy Firewall):** Operates at the **Application Layer (Layer 7)**; terminates the client's connection and opens a *new* connection to the destination, providing deep protocol-aware inspection.
5. **Next-Generation Firewall (NGFW):** Combines traditional packet filtering with DPI, IPS, application awareness, and threat intelligence.

## 2.2 Operational Decision Steps of a Packet-Filtering Firewall

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** KTU frequently asks the *order of rule evaluation* — the firewall evaluates rules **sequentially from top to bottom** and applies the **first match** (First-Match-Wins / First-Fit semantics).

1. **Step 1 — Packet Arrival:** A packet arrives at the firewall interface from an internal or external network.
2. **Step 2 — Header Extraction:** The firewall extracts the following fields from the L3/L4 header: `Source IP`, `Destination IP`, `Source Port`, `Destination Port`, `Protocol` (TCP/UDP/ICMP), and `Flags` (for TCP).
3. **Step 3 — Rule Set Lookup:** The packet is compared against each rule in the **Access Control List (ACL)** in order, from Rule #1 to Rule #N.
4. **Step 4 — Match Decision:**
   - If a rule **matches**, the corresponding action (`PERMIT` / `ACCEPT` or `DENY` / `DROP`) is executed **immediately**, and processing stops.
   - If **no rule matches**, the **default policy** is applied (either `default-allow` or, preferably, `default-deny`).
5. **Step 5 — Logging:** The decision is logged for audit and forensics.
6. **Step 6 — Forwarding / Blocking:** On PERMIT, the packet is forwarded to the egress interface. On DENY, the packet is silently dropped or rejected (with an ICMP unreachable message).

## 2.3 Stateless vs. Stateful Packet Filtering

| Feature | Stateless (Simple) Filter | Stateful (Dynamic) Filter |
|---|---|---|
| **Decision Basis** | Each packet in isolation | Connection state from a state table |
| **State Tracking** | None | Maintains a **state table** (5-tuple) |
| **TCP Handshake Awareness** | No | Yes (SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK) |
| **IP Spoofing Resistance** | Weak | Stronger |
| **Performance** | Very fast (O(N) lookup) | Slower (table maintenance) |
| **Common Use** | Router ACLs, simple edge filters | Modern enterprise firewalls |
| **Vulnerability** | Vulnerable to ACK-scan, fragmented packet attacks | Resistant to most scanning techniques |

## 2.4 The 5-Tuple State Table

$$\text{5-Tuple} = \left( \text{Source IP},\ \text{Dest IP},\ \text{Source Port},\ \text{Dest Port},\ \text{Protocol} \right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every entry in the state table is uniquely identified by its 5-tuple. The firewall tracks transitions: **NEW → ESTABLISHED → RELATED → CLOSED**.

## 2.5 Firewall Architectures (KTU High-Weightage)

### (a) Screened Host Architecture (Single-Homed Bastion)
- One packet-filtering router + **one bastion host** (proxy server).
- The bastion host is the *only* host visible to the Internet; internal hosts are protected.
- **Vulnerability:** If the router is compromised, the bastion host is exposed.

### (b) Dual-Homed Host Architecture
- A host with **two network interfaces** (one to external, one to internal).
- Acts as a *physical barrier* between the two networks. **IP forwarding is disabled.**
- All traffic is forced through application proxies running on the host.
- **No router** is needed; the host itself is the firewall.

### (c) Screened Subnet Architecture (DMZ — Demilitarized Zone)
- **Two packet-filtering routers** + a **perimeter network (DMZ)** in between.
- The DMZ hosts public-facing servers (Web, Mail, DNS).
- Inner router protects the internal network; outer router protects the bastion.
- **Most secure** of the three architectures — **standard for enterprise networks**.

### (d) Personal Firewall Architecture
- Software-based, runs on a single end-user host.
- Filters traffic to/from the host only.
- Examples: Windows Defender Firewall, iptables, ZoneAlarm.

## 2.6 Bastion Host

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bastion Host:** A hardened, dedicated computer host that is deliberately exposed to an untrusted network (typically the Internet) and serves as the only entry point to a private network. It runs a secure OS with minimum services, and all unnecessary daemons, accounts, and protocols are removed.

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates the **mathematical, logical, and structural** quantities central to packet-filtering problems. All quantities are **mathematically verifiable** and align with KTU's typical numerical/analytical question style.

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Variables & Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Rule-Lookup Complexity** | $T(n) = O(n)$ | $n$ = number of ACL rules (dimensionless) | Estimating worst-case filtering latency |
| 2 | **State Table Key** | $K = (IP_s, IP_d, P_s, P_d, \text{Proto})$ | $IP_s, IP_d$ in 32-bit IPv4 or 128-bit IPv6; $P_s, P_d$ in 0–65535; Proto = 6 (TCP), 17 (UDP), 1 (ICMP) | Hash key for each TCP/UDP flow |
| 3 | **State Table Capacity** | $C_{state} = n_{flow} \times (S_{5-tuple} + S_{meta})$ | $n_{flow}$ in flows; $S_{5-tuple} \approx 96$ bits (IPv4) / 288 bits (IPv6); $S_{meta} \approx 128$ bits (timeouts, flags) | Memory planning for stateful firewall |
| 4 | **Packet Throughput** | $\Phi = \dfrac{N_{pkt}}{t_{proc}}$ | $N_{pkt}$ = packets processed; $t_{proc}$ in **packets per second (pps)** | Sizing firewall hardware |
| 5 | **Bit-Rate Equivalence** | $R_{bps} = \Phi \times L_{avg} \times 8$ | $L_{avg}$ = average packet size in **bytes** | Converting pps to Mbps/Gbps |
| 6 | **TCP Flag Bitmask** | $F_{mask} = \sum_{i \in S} 2^{i}$ | $S \subseteq \{0..8\}$ (FIN=0, SYN=1, RST=2, PSH=3, ACK=4, URG=5, ECE=6, CWR=7, NS=8) | Matching specific TCP flag combinations |
| 7 | **CIDR Wildcard Mask** | $W = 2^{32-p} - 1$ (for IPv4) | $p$ = prefix length in bits (1–32) | Deriving wildcard from CIDR |
| 8 | **Hash Bucket Index** | $h(K) = K \bmod m$ | $K$ = 5-tuple integer encoding; $m$ = table size (power of 2) | Open-addressing state table lookup |
| 9 | **Rule-Match Probability (random)** | $P_{match} = 1 - (1-r)^{N}$ | $r$ = per-rule hit probability; $N$ = total rules | Theoretical ACL hit-rate modeling |
| 10 | **Effective Throughput (with state)** | $R_{eff} = R_{raw} \times (1 - \alpha_{s})$ | $R_{raw}$ = raw throughput; $\alpha_s$ = state-table-overhead fraction (0.05–0.25) | Accounting for stateful processing cost |

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Firewalls Matter in Industry:**
> - **Enterprise Edge Protection:** All Fortune 500 companies deploy stateful NGFWs (Palo Alto, Fortinet, Cisco ASA) at their network perimeter.
> - **Cloud Security:** AWS Security Groups and Azure NSGs are *software-defined virtual packet filters* — the same ACL logic.
> - **IoT / SCADA:** Industrial firewalls (e.g., Nozomi Networks) protect OT networks using the screened-subnet (DMZ) architecture.
> - **Telecommunications:** Carrier-grade firewalls use stateful inspection at line-rate (100+ Gbps) for ISP-level DDoS mitigation.
> - **Compliance Mandate:** Standards such as **PCI-DSS 4.0 (Requirement 1)**, **HIPAA Security Rule**, and **ISO 27001 (A.13.1)** all mandate firewall deployment at network boundaries.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Derivation #1 — Converting CIDR to Wildcard Mask for ACL Rules

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Context:** A KTU numerical/short-answer question typically asks you to convert a CIDR block into a wildcard mask suitable for a Cisco-style or iptables ACL rule. We derive this from first principles.

### Step 1 — Start with the CIDR prefix length

Given a CIDR block, e.g., `192.168.10.0/26`, the prefix length is $p = 26$. This means $26$ bits of the 32-bit IPv4 address are the *network portion* (must match exactly), and $32 - 26 = 6$ bits are the *host portion* (any value is allowed).

### Step 2 — Express the wildcard mask as a bitwise inversion of the subnet mask

The subnet mask for `/26` is:
$$M_{subnet} = 11111111.11111111.11111111.11000000_2 = 255.255.255.192_{10}$$

The wildcard mask is the bitwise NOT of the subnet mask:
$$W = \overline{M_{subnet}}$$

### Step 3 — Perform the bitwise inversion

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{subnet} &= \underbrace{1\cdots1}_{26\text{ ones}} \ \underbrace{0\cdots0}_{6\text{ zeros}} \\
W &= \underbrace{0\cdots0}_{26\text{ zeros}} \ \underbrace{1\cdots1}_{6\text{ ones}} \\
W &= 00000000.00000000.00000000.00111111_2
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Convert wildcard bits to decimal

The lowest 6 bits each have a weight of $2^0, 2^1, 2^2, 2^3, 2^4, 2^5$ which sum to $1+2+4+8+16+32 = 63$. Therefore:
$$W = 0.0.0.63_{10}$$

### Step 5 — General formula

For a general IPv4 prefix length $p$ (with $1 \le p \le 32$), the wildcard mask in dotted-decimal has the *lowest* $32-p$ bits set to 1. Equivalent closed-form:
$$W = 2^{32-p} - 1$$

### Step 6 — Verification for `/26`

$$
\begin{aligned}
W &= 2^{32-26} - 1 = 2^6 - 1 = 64 - 1 = 63 \\
\Rightarrow W &= 0.0.0.63 \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 7 — Worked Example: `/20`

For $p = 20$, the host portion is $32 - 20 = 12$ bits.

$$
\begin{aligned}
W &= 2^{32-20} - 1 = 2^{12} - 1 = 4096 - 1 = 4095 \\
\Rightarrow W &= 0.0.15.255 \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.2 Analytical Derivation #2 — Constructing a State-Table Hash Key

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Context:** To make a stateful firewall's state-table lookup O(1), the 5-tuple must be reduced to a single integer key via concatenation. We derive the canonical encoding.

### Step 1 — Concatenate the four numeric fields

Let $IP_s$ and $IP_d$ each be 32 bits, and $P_s$ and $P_d$ each be 16 bits. The total bit length of the concatenated 5-tuple (ignoring protocol for now) is:
$$L = 32 + 32 + 16 + 16 = 96 \text{ bits}$$

### Step 2 — Encode each field in network byte order (big-endian)

Define the integer encoding:
$$K = (IP_s \ll 64) \mid (IP_d \ll 32) \mid (P_s \ll 16) \mid P_d$$

where `<<` denotes a left bitwise shift, and `|` is bitwise OR.

### Step 3 — Compute the hash bucket index

For a state table of $m$ buckets (a power of 2 for efficiency):
$$h(K) = K \bmod m$$

### Step 4 — Worked numeric example

Consider the 5-tuple (taking only IP and port for simplicity):
- $IP_s = 192.168.1.10 = 11000000.10101000.00000001.00001010_2 = 0xC0A8010A$
- $IP_d = 10.0.0.5 = 0x0A000005$
- $P_s = 49152 = 0xC000$
- $P_d = 80 = 0x0050$

Concatenate in network-byte order:
$$
\begin{aligned}
K &= 0xC0A8010A \, 0A000005 \, C000 \, 0050 \\
  &= 0xC0A8010A0A000005C0000050
\end{aligned}
$$

If $m = 2^{16} = 65536$ buckets:
$$h(K) = K \bmod 65536 = 0x0050 = 80$$

### Step 5 — Collision probability (analytical)

By the **birthday paradox**, with $m$ buckets and $n$ active flows, the probability of at least one collision is approximately:
$$P_{coll} \approx 1 - e^{-n(n-1)/(2m)}$$

For $n = 1000$ flows and $m = 65536$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{coll} &\approx 1 - e^{-1000 \times 999 / (2 \times 65536)} \\
          &\approx 1 - e^{-7.62} \\
          &\approx 1 - 0.00049 \\
          &\approx 0.9995
\end{aligned}
$$

This high collision rate motivates **chained hashing** or **cuckoo hashing** in real implementations.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Derivation #3 — Throughput Sizing for a Filtering Firewall

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Context:** KTU sometimes asks you to size the required throughput of a firewall given the average packet size and the line rate of the protected link.

### Step 1 — Given quantities

- Line rate: $R_{link} = 1$ **Gbps** $= 10^9$ bits/s
- Average packet size: $L_{avg} = 800$ **bytes** $= 800 \times 8 = 6400$ bits
- Processing overhead: $\alpha_s = 0.20$ (stateful firewall)

### Step 2 — Compute raw packets-per-second

The line rate, expressed in packets per second, requires dividing by the packet size:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\Phi_{raw} &= \frac{R_{link}}{L_{avg}} = \frac{10^9 \text{ bits/s}}{6400 \text{ bits/pkt}} \\
           &= 156{,}250 \text{ pps}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Apply the overhead factor

The effective throughput is reduced by the stateful-processing overhead $\alpha_s$:
$$R_{eff} = R_{raw} \times (1 - \alpha_s)$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{eff} &= 1 \times 10^9 \times (1 - 0.20) \\
        &= 0.80 \times 10^9 \\
        &= 800 \text{ Mbps} \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Interpretation

A firewall that supports only line-rate filtering on a 1 Gbps link must handle **at least 156,250 packets/second** in the stateless case, and must be sized to sustain at least **125,000 packets/second** when stateful accounting is included (since $156{,}250 \times 0.80 = 125{,}000$ pps is the rate of *useful* traffic that survives the stateful overhead-adjusted processing).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Derivation #4 — TCP Flag Bitmask Construction

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Context:** Many ACL rules specify TCP flag conditions (e.g., "permit only SYN-ACK to enter from outside"). You must construct the corresponding bitmask from a flag set.

### Step 1 — Define the TCP flag bit positions

| Flag | FIN | SYN | RST | PSH | ACK | URG | ECE | CWR | NS |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Bit Position** | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 | 8 |

### Step 2 — Given rule

> **"Match packets with SYN=1 AND ACK=0 (i.e., the initial SYN of a connection handshake)"**

The flag set is $S = \{\text{SYN}\}$, i.e., bit position 1.

### Step 3 — Build the mask

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{mask} &= \sum_{i \in \{1\}} 2^{i} \\
         &= 2^1 \\
         &= 2 \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Verify

A packet with control bits $= 000000010_2$ (SYN set) when ANDed with the mask $2$ gives $2 \neq 0$, indicating a match. A packet with control bits $= 000010010_2$ (SYN+ACK) when ANDed with the mask $2$ also gives $2$, but the additional `eq` test against an expected value disambiguates SYN-only vs. SYN-ACK.

---

## 3.5 Summary Analytical Matrix (Master Reference)

| Derivation | Input | Operation | Output | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| CIDR → Wildcard | Prefix length $p$ | $W = 2^{32-p} - 1$ | Dotted-decimal wildcard | Writing router ACLs |
| 5-Tuple Hash Key | $(IP_s, IP_d, P_s, P_d)$ | Concatenation $\to$ mod $m$ | Bucket index | O(1) state-table lookup |
| Throughput Sizing | $R_{link}$, $L_{avg}$, $\alpha_s$ | $\Phi = R/L$; $R_{eff} = R(1-\alpha_s)$ | Required firewall pps / bps | Hardware procurement |
| TCP Flag Bitmask | Flag set $S$ | $F_{mask} = \sum_{i \in S} 2^i$ | Integer mask | State-inspection ACL rules |
| Collision Probability | $n$ flows, $m$ buckets | $P \approx 1 - e^{-n^2/(2m)}$ | Hash-collision risk | Memory sizing |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Three Firewall Architectures Compared (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    internet[Untrusted Internet]:::external
    outerRouter1[Outer Packet Filter Router]:::router
    innerRouter1[Inner Packet Filter Router]:::router
    dmz1[DMZ Perimeter Network]:::dmz
    bastion1[Bastion Host Proxy]:::bastion
    internal1[Trusted Internal Network]:::internal
    host1[Internal User Host]:::internal

    internet -- "1 Unfiltered Traffic" --> outerRouter1
    outerRouter1 -- "2 Filtered Packets" --> dmz1
    dmz1 -- "3 Proxy Connection" --> bastion1
    bastion1 -- "4 Filtered Proxy Traffic" --> innerRouter1
    innerRouter1 -- "5 Verified Packets" --> internal1
    internal1 --> host1

    classDef external fill:#FFB6B6,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000
    classDef router fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef dmz fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#8B4513,color:#000
    classDef bastion fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#4B0082,color:#000
    classDef internal fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Figure 4.1 — Screened Subnet Firewall Architecture (DMZ).** This is the most secure enterprise architecture and the one KTU most frequently asks about. The dual-router setup ensures that *no single point of compromise* exposes the internal network.

## 4.2 Dual-Homed Host Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    internet[Untrusted Internet]:::external
    iface1[External Interface NIC1]:::iface
    dualHost[Dual Homed Bastion Host]:::bastion
    iface2[Internal Interface NIC2]:::iface
    internal[Trusted Internal Network]:::internal
    host1[Internal User]:::internal

    internet --> iface1
    iface1 -- "Proxied TCP Session" --> dualHost
    dualHost -- "Verified Proxied Session" --> iface2
    iface2 --> internal
    internal --> host1

    classDef external fill:#FFB6B6,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000
    classDef bastion fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#4B0082,color:#000
    classDef iface fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#888800,color:#000
    classDef internal fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Figure 4.2 — Dual-Homed Host Architecture.** IP forwarding is **disabled** at the kernel level. The host is the *physical* barrier; all traffic must terminate at a proxy running on the host.

## 4.3 Packet-Filtering Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    pkt[Inbound or Outbound Packet]:::pkt
    extract[Extract L3 L4 Header Fields]:::step
    rule1{Rule 1 Match}:::decision
    rule2{Rule 2 Match}:::decision
    ruleN{Rule N Match}:::decision
    permit[PERMIT Forward Packet]:::allow
    deny[DENY Drop Packet]:::deny
    default[Apply Default Policy]:::step
    log[Log Decision and Audit]:::step
    fwd[Forward to Egress Interface]:::step
    drop[Silently Drop or Send ICMP]:::step

    pkt --> extract
    extract --> rule1
    rule1 -- Yes --> permit
    rule1 -- No --> rule2
    rule2 -- Yes --> permit
    rule2 -- No --> ruleN
    ruleN -- Yes --> permit
    ruleN -- No --> default
    default -- Default Deny --> deny
    default -- Default Allow --> permit
    permit --> log
    log --> fwd
    deny --> log
    log --> drop

    classDef pkt fill:#FFB6B6,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000
    classDef step fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#888800,color:#000
    classDef decision fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef allow fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000
    classDef deny fill:#FFB6B6,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Figure 4.3 — Sequential ACL Rule Evaluation Logic.** The firewall processes rules top-to-bottom and applies the **first match** (First-Fit-Wins). If no rule matches, the **default policy** is applied.

## 4.4 State Table Lifecycle for a TCP Session

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> NEW: SYN packet arrives
    NEW --> ESTABLISHED: SYN ACK received and ACK sent
    ESTABLISHED --> RELATED: Out of band signal FTPPORT etc
    ESTABLISHED --> CLOSING: FIN packet sent
    CLOSING --> CLOSED: Final ACK received
    RELATED --> CLOSED: Timeout expiry
    CLOSED --> [*]: Entry purged from state table
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Figure 4.4 — TCP Connection State Machine in a Stateful Firewall.** Each state transition is logged; packets that do not match a valid transition are dropped (preventing ACK scans and blind connection hijacks).

## 4.5 Personal Firewall Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    internet[Internet]:::external
    router[Home Router with NAT]:::router
    hostPC[End User Host A]:::internal
    swfw[Personal Software Firewall ZoneAlarm etc]:::bastion
    app1[Application 1]:::internal
    app2[Application 2]:::internal

    internet --> router
    router -- "Traffic to host" --> hostPC
    hostPC --> swfw
    swfw -- "Inspect per process rules" --> app1
    swfw -- "Inspect per process rules" --> app2

    classDef external fill:#FFB6B6,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000
    classDef router fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef bastion fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#4B0082,color:#000
    classDef internal fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Figure 4.5 — Personal Firewall Architecture.** The firewall is *co-located* with the end host and may apply per-application rules. It complements, but does not replace, the edge firewall.
