# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define a firewall. Differentiate between a packet-filtering firewall and a stateful inspection firewall.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Firewall:** A firewall is a hardware/software device placed between a trusted internal network and an untrusted external network that enforces a security policy by permitting or denying traffic based on a defined rule set.

> **Packet-Filtering Firewall:** Operates at Layers 3–4. Examines each packet *individually* in isolation, using header fields (Source IP, Destination IP, Source Port, Destination Port, Protocol). It has **no awareness** of the connection state. It is fast, simple, and vulnerable to IP spoofing and ACK-scans.

> **Stateful Inspection Firewall:** Maintains a **state table** keyed on the 5-tuple. Tracks TCP connection states (NEW, ESTABLISHED, RELATED, CLOSING). A packet is allowed only if it **belongs to an existing or legitimate new connection**. It is slower but offers stronger security.

**(1 mark each for: Definition, Packet-Filtering characteristics, Stateful characteristics.)**

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**What is a Bastion Host? Mention any two security measures to harden a Bastion Host.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Bastion Host:** A bastion host is a computer system deliberately exposed to an untrusted network (typically the Internet) and serves as the *only gateway* between the external network and the internal private network. All inbound traffic to the internal network is forced through the bastion host, which then proxies and filters the traffic.

> **Two Hardening Measures:**
> 1. **Minimal OS Installation:** Install only the operating system with the **absolute minimum set of services, daemons, and protocols** required for its role. Remove or disable all others.
> 2. **Continuous Logging and Auditing:** Enable comprehensive logging (syslog, auditd) to monitor every access attempt, and ship logs to a secure, off-host SIEM.
> 3. *(Any one of the following may also be accepted)*: Disable IP forwarding at the kernel; apply kernel hardening (SELinux/AppArmor); use a dedicated, single-purpose hardened distribution (e.g., OpenBSD); enforce multi-factor authentication; run on isolated VLAN/DMZ; patch religiously.

**(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for hardening measures.)**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Students describe a *single* firewall architecture instead of *comparing* the three (screened host, dual-homed, screened subnet). Examiners expect a **comparative table** — lose 2–3 marks for missing the comparison.
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Confusing *default-deny* with *default-allow*. KTU expects the academically correct answer: **default-deny** is the recommended posture.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Forgetting the **5-tuple** when explaining stateful inspection — only mentioning "IP address" or "port" is incomplete. Always list the 5 fields.
> - **Common Mistake #4:** Writing ACL rules without stating the **rule ordering** and **first-match** semantics — lose 1 mark.
> - **Common Mistake #5:** Spelling "DMZ" as "DMC" or "DMZ" without explaining the abbreviation.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`**

**(a) Explain the three common firewall architectures: Screened Host, Dual-Homed Host, and Screened Subnet (DMZ). Use diagrams. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

> **1. Screened Host Architecture (3 Marks):**
> In the screened host architecture, a single packet-filtering router is placed between the external untrusted network and the internal trusted network. The router is configured to permit only certain traffic (e.g., to a specific bastion host) to pass through. A **bastion host** is the only system on the internal network visible to the Internet. It runs proxy services for inbound and outbound traffic. **Disadvantage:** if the packet-filtering router is compromised, traffic can flow directly to internal hosts bypassing the bastion host.

> **2. Dual-Homed Host Architecture (2 Marks):**
> This architecture uses a single host (the bastion) equipped with **two network interface cards (NICs)** — one connected to the external network and one to the internal network. The host runs application-level proxy software. Critically, **IP forwarding is disabled** in the operating system kernel. Thus, the only way traffic can pass between the two networks is through an application-level proxy running on the host. This provides a *physical* barrier. **Disadvantage:** if the host fails or is compromised, the internal network is fully exposed.

> **3. Screened Subnet (DMZ) Architecture (2 Marks):**
> This is the **most secure** architecture. It uses *two* packet-filtering routers — an outer router and an inner router — with a **perimeter network (DMZ)** between them. The bastion host(s) and public servers (Web, Mail, DNS) reside in the DMZ. The outer router filters traffic between the Internet and the DMZ; the inner router filters traffic between the DMZ and the internal network. **Advantage:** even if one router is compromised, the internal network remains protected by the second router. The bastion is also isolated from the internal LAN.

> **Diagram (essential):** Show a horizontal arrangement: `Internet | Outer Router | DMZ [Bastion, Web Server] | Inner Router | Internal LAN` — see Section 4.1.

---

**(b) Explain the working of a packet-filtering firewall. Discuss the limitations of simple packet filtering. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

> **Working (4 Marks):**
> A packet-filtering firewall operates at the **Network Layer (Layer 3)** and **Transport Layer (Layer 4)** of the OSI model. For each packet that arrives at the firewall:
> 1. The firewall extracts the header fields: `Source IP`, `Destination IP`, `Source Port`, `Destination Port`, `Protocol` (TCP/UDP/ICMP), and `TCP Flags` (if TCP).
> 2. The packet is compared against the **Access Control List (ACL)** — a sequential list of filtering rules. Each rule specifies a *pattern* (which fields must match) and an *action* (`PERMIT` / `DENY`).
> 3. The **first matching rule** is applied, and processing stops. The packet is either forwarded (on `PERMIT`) or silently dropped (on `DENY`).
> 4. If no rule matches, the **default policy** is invoked. KTU-best-practice is **default-deny** — block all traffic not explicitly allowed.
> 5. The decision is logged for audit purposes.

> **Two Modes:**
> - **Stateless mode:** Each packet is treated independently with no connection memory. Fast but weak.
> - **Stateful mode:** The firewall maintains a state table indexed by the 5-tuple. Packets are permitted only if they correspond to a valid, tracked connection. Provides better security at slightly higher processing cost.

> **Limitations of Simple (Stateless) Packet Filtering (3 Marks):**
> 1. **No Stateful Context:** The firewall cannot tell whether an inbound TCP packet with the ACK flag set is part of a legitimate established connection or a malicious ACK-scan probe. Thus, it cannot block scans without also blocking legitimate return traffic.
> 2. **IP Address Spoofing:** Because the firewall has no state, an attacker can craft a packet with a *spoofed* internal source IP, and the firewall may permit it (if rules allow that "internal" source).
> 3. **No Application-Layer Inspection:** The firewall cannot see inside the payload. Malicious content in HTTP, SMTP, or DNS payloads passes through unchecked. For example, a packet containing an SQL-injection string in its HTTP body is allowed.
> 4. **Vulnerability to Fragmentation Attacks:** The firewall inspects only the *first* fragment of an IP-fragmented packet (which contains the L4 header). Subsequent fragments with no L4 header are typically permitted blindly, allowing attackers to sneak data through.
> 5. **Difficult Rule Management:** As the rule base grows, ordering conflicts and unintended permissions become a serious management problem.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`**

**(a) With a suitable diagram, explain the stateful inspection firewall. Discuss how it is superior to stateless packet filtering. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

> **Stateful Inspection Firewall — Definition (2 Marks):**
> A stateful inspection firewall (also called a *dynamic packet filter*) operates at Layers 3–4 but maintains **state information** about every active network connection. The state is stored in a **state table** whose entries are keyed by the **5-tuple**: `(Source IP, Destination IP, Source Port, Destination Port, Protocol)`. Each entry tracks the connection's lifecycle state: `NEW`, `ESTABLISHED`, `RELATED`, `INVALID`, or `CLOSING`.

> **Working with Diagram (3 Marks):**
> 1. When the *first* packet of a new outbound TCP connection (SYN) arrives, the firewall creates a new entry in the state table with state `NEW`.
> 2. Upon receiving the corresponding SYN-ACK from the destination, the firewall transitions the entry to `ESTABLISHED` and begins allowing return traffic that matches this 5-tuple.
> 3. Any subsequent packet matching an `ESTABLISHED` entry is permitted without re-checking all ACL rules — a fast path.
> 4. Packets not matching any state-table entry are evaluated against the ACL (and may be denied if default-deny is in effect).
> 5. Entries are aged out via timeouts (e.g., 60 seconds for TCP, 30 seconds for UDP) when the connection becomes idle.

> **Diagram:** Show a state-transition diagram (see Section 4.4) and a block diagram with: `Inbound Packet → State-Table Lookup → Match Found? → Forward; No Match → ACL Check → Decision`.

> **Superiority over Stateless Filtering (2 Marks):**
> 1. **Resistance to ACK-scans and port-scans:** A stateless filter may permit ACK packets from the outside (since the ACK flag suggests a return packet of an established connection). A stateful filter rejects such packets because no corresponding `ESTABLISHED` state entry exists.
> 2. **Resistance to IP spoofing:** Since the firewall tracks full 5-tuple state, injecting a packet with a forged source IP cannot match any legitimate outbound connection, and the packet is dropped.
> 3. **Resistance to fragmentation-based bypass:** Many stateful firewalls reassemble fragments before applying rules, eliminating the fragmentation-attack loophole.
> 4. **Better logging and forensics:** The state table provides a complete record of active and recent connections, invaluable for incident response.

---

**(b) Define a packet filter. Construct a sample rule set (ACL) for a corporate network with a web server in the DMZ. Justify the ordering of the rules. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

> **Definition (1 Mark):**
> A packet filter is a network device or software that inspects the header of each packet passing through it and permits or denies the packet based on a defined list of filtering rules (an Access Control List or ACL).

> **Sample Rule Set for: Corporate Network with Web Server in DMZ (4 Marks)**

> Assumed network topology: Internet ↔ Outer Router ↔ DMZ (Web Server 203.0.113.10) ↔ Inner Router ↔ Internal LAN (10.0.0.0/24) ↔ Employees (10.0.0.0/24).

| # | Source | Destination | Protocol | Dest Port | Action | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 10.0.0.0/24 | Any | TCP | 80, 443 | PERMIT | Employees browse web |
| 2 | Any | 203.0.113.10 | TCP | 80 | PERMIT | Internet users reach web server |
| 3 | Any | 203.0.113.10 | TCP | 443 | PERMIT | Secure web access to DMZ server |
| 4 | 10.0.0.0/24 | 203.0.113.10 | TCP | 22 | DENY | Block employees SSHing to web server |
| 5 | Any | 10.0.0.0/24 | TCP | 22 | DENY | Block external SSH to internal LAN |
| 6 | Any | Any | ICMP | – | DENY | Block all ping (reconnaissance) |
| 7 | Any | Any | Any | Any | DENY | **Default-deny** — drop everything else |

> **Justification of Rule Ordering (2 Marks):**
> - Rules are ordered **most specific → most general**. The first matching rule is applied (First-Match-Wins), so the placement of each rule is critical.
> - **Rule 1** is placed first because it permits employee egress traffic to external web services — this is the most frequent traffic and must not be blocked by a more general later rule.
> - **Rule 4** is placed *before* the broader internal-outbound rule (Rule 1) to ensure that internal employees cannot SSH into the web server. If Rule 1 came first, it would match this packet's port 22 traffic (if Rule 1 were overly broad) — so we must be specific first.
> - **Rule 6 and Rule 7** are placed last to enforce the **default-deny** posture.
> - **Pitfall:** If Rule 7 (default-deny) were placed first, *all* traffic would be blocked, making the firewall useless. This shows the importance of ordering.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Rapid-Revision Summary — KTU PEMRT631 / Module 4 / Firewalls & Packet Filtering**

### Core Concepts
- ☐ **Firewall** = trusted ↔ untrusted network policy-enforcement device.
- ☐ **Packet filtering** operates at **OSI Layers 3–4** (IP header + TCP/UDP header).
- ☐ **Rule order matters:** First-Match-Wins (First-Fit semantics).
- ☐ **Default-deny** is the academically correct default policy.

### Key Numbers to Memorize
- ☐ **5-tuple** = (Source IP, Dest IP, Source Port, Dest Port, Protocol) — *always list all five*.
- ☐ **TCP flag bit positions:** FIN=0, SYN=1, RST=2, PSH=3, ACK=4, URG=5.
- ☐ **Wildcard mask formula:** $W = 2^{32-p} - 1$ for IPv4 prefix length $p$.

### Three Firewall Architectures
- ☐ **Screened Host:** 1 router + 1 bastion.
- ☐ **Dual-Homed Host:** 1 host, 2 NICs, IP forwarding OFF, proxies only.
- ☐ **Screened Subnet (DMZ):** 2 routers + 1 perimeter network → **most secure** and most asked.

### Stateless vs. Stateful
- ☐ **Stateless:** No connection memory — fast but weak.
- ☐ **Stateful:** Maintains state table — resistant to scans/spoofing.
- ☐ **State transitions:** NEW → ESTABLISHED → CLOSING → CLOSED.

### Common Pitfalls to Avoid in Exam
- ☐ Don't confuse "default-deny" with "default-allow."
- ☐ Always state the **5-tuple** explicitly.
- ☐ Always include a **diagram** in 14-mark architecture questions.
- ☐ Mention **both pros and cons** in comparison questions.
- ☐ Use "**DMZ**" — not "DMC" or "DMS."

### Real-World Mapping
- ☐ **PCI-DSS, HIPAA, ISO 27001** all mandate firewalls.
- ☐ **NGFWs** (Palo Alto, Fortinet) are the modern enterprise standard.
- ☐ **iptables / nftables** (Linux) and **Windows Filtering Platform (WFP)** are software-defined packet filters.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Proxy Server
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT631 - NETWORK & DATA SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Proxy Server

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Proxy Server** is a dedicated network intermediary (a computer system or an application program) that acts as a **gateway** between end-user clients and the destination web/internet servers. It evaluates and forwards client requests on behalf of the client, while mediating responses from origin servers back to the requesting client, effectively providing **request isolation, access control, caching, anonymity, and policy enforcement** at the application layer of the OSI/TCP-IP model.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (Network & Data Security, Module 4)**, a proxy is formally classified as an **Application-Layer Firewall / Intermediary Node** that implements the **Bump-in-the-Stack / Bump-in-the-Wire** architecture for enforcing perimeter security, content filtering, and traffic anonymization.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> A proxy is *not* merely a "relay." It is a **policy-decision point (PDP)** and a **policy-enforcement point (PEP)** that terminates, inspects, regenerates, and forwards traffic — thereby breaking the direct end-to-end TCP connection between client and server.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a corporate office where employees (clients) want to order food (data) from restaurants (web servers), but the office has a **Reception Desk (Proxy Server)**.

- Every food order (HTTP request) must be placed **through** the Reception Desk.
- The Reception Desk checks: *"Is this restaurant approved by policy? Did someone already order this same food today (cache)? Is the order valid?"*
- If approved, the Reception Desk **places the order on behalf of the employee**, picks up the food, and hands it to the employee.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition:** The restaurant never knows the *real* employee's name — it only knows the Reception Desk. This is the essence of **anonymity and request mediation**.

## 1.3 Core Operational Metrics & Standard Values

The following industry-standard metrics are evaluated for any proxy deployment:

| Metric | Standard Industry Value | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Cache Hit Ratio (CHR)** | **40% – 70%** | Measures cache effectiveness |
| **Mean Response Latency Reduction** | **30% – 60%** | Speed gain over direct access |
| **Connection Pool Size** | **256 – 4096** | Concurrent client support |
| **Throughput per Core** | **~ 1 Gbps** | Forwarding capacity |
| **SSL Inspection Throughput** | **~ 200 Mbps** | With TLS termination |

> [!TIP]
> **Why these matter for KTU:** Numeric metrics such as CHR and latency are the *most expected* answer points in Part B numerical/short-note questions.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic Flow (Step-by-Step)

A proxy server operates through a deterministic pipeline. Every step must be memorized as it is a frequent **3-mark short answer** and **14-mark descriptive** topic.

1. **Client Request Initiation** — The client (browser/app) sends an HTTP/HTTPS/SOCKS request destined for the origin server. However, instead of resolving the origin server's IP, the request is configured to be directed to the **proxy's IP and listening port** (commonly **TCP port 3128, 8080, or 1080**).
2. **Connection Termination at Proxy** — The proxy **accepts the client TCP connection** and **creates a separate, second TCP connection** to the origin server. This is the **"Two-Connection Model"**, which is the defining architectural feature of any application-layer proxy.
3. **Policy Evaluation (PEP / PDP Layer)** — The proxy parses the request headers (URL, Host, User-Agent, Method) and consults:
   - Access Control Lists (ACL)
   - URL filtering database (e.g., URLhaus, Squid-blacklist)
   - Content-type / MIME filters
   - Authentication modules (LDAP, RADIUS)
4. **Cache Lookup (Hash-Based)** — A SHA-1 or MD5 hash of the URL is computed and compared against the proxy's local cache (RAM $\rightarrow$ SSD $\rightarrow$ HDD tiered storage).
5. **Cache Hit Path** — If valid cached content exists and is **not expired** (per HTTP `Cache-Control: max-age` or `Expires` headers), the cached object is returned directly. The origin server is **never contacted**.
6. **Cache Miss Path** — The proxy **forwards the request** to the origin server, receives the response, **optionally stores it** in cache, and returns it to the client.
7. **Logging & Telemetry** — Every transaction is logged in the **Common Log Format (CLF)** with fields: `host, ident, authuser, date, request, status, bytes`.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following equations are the **high-yield quantitative tools** for Part A/B questions on this topic.

| # | Formula / Quantity | Equation | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Cache Hit Ratio (CHR)** | $CHR = \dfrac{H_c}{H_c + M_c}$ | $H_c$ = cache hits, $M_c$ = cache misses (dimensionless, $0 \le CHR \le 1$) |
| 2 | **Byte Hit Ratio (BHR)** | $BHR = \dfrac{B_{served\_from\_cache}}{B_{total\_served}}$ | Better measure than CHR; weighs by *bytes*, not requests |
| 3 | **Average Latency with Proxy** | $T_{avg} = p \cdot T_{cache} + (1-p) \cdot T_{origin}$ | $p$ = CHR; $T_{cache} \ll T_{origin}$ |
| 4 | **Throughput (Little's Law)** | $X = \dfrac{\lambda}{\mu - \lambda}$ | $X$ = concurrent connections, $\lambda$ = arrival rate, $\mu$ = service rate |
| 5 | **Connection Establishment Time** | $T_{conn} = 2 \cdot RTT + S_{proxy}$ | $S_{proxy}$ = proxy processing delay (RTT = Round-Trip Time) |
| 6 | **Effective Latency Reduction** | $L_{gain} = \dfrac{T_{direct} - T_{proxy}}{T_{direct}} \times 100\%$ | Expressed in **%** |
| 7 | **TTL-Based Expiry** | $t_{expired} = t_{cached} + TTL$ | Used in freshness validation |
| 8 | **Anonymity Level** | $AL = n_{headers\_stripped}$ | Count of identifying headers removed (e.g., `X-Forwarded-For`) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** These equations govern **CDN design, enterprise proxy tuning (Squid, HAProxy, Nginx), and SRE capacity-planning** decisions in industry.

## 2.3 Why a Proxy Is Used (Engineering Motivation)

- **Performance:** Reduces bandwidth consumption and origin-server load via caching.
- **Security:** Acts as a single **choke-point** for threat inspection, DLP (Data Loss Prevention), and malware scanning.
- **Privacy:** Masks the originating client's IP address from the destination server.
- **Policy Enforcement:** Centralized URL/keyword/content filtering for organizations.
- **Audit & Compliance:** Single logging point for **GDPR, HIPAA, RBI, and IT-Act** compliance.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Average Response Latency with Proxy Cache

Let us derive the **mean response time** that a client experiences when a proxy is in the path. This is a **favourite 14-mark derivation** at the KTU level.

### Step 1: Define the Two Outcomes
For a given object requested by the client, only two mutually exclusive outcomes exist:
- **Outcome A** — A *cache hit* occurs. The proxy serves the object from local storage.
- **Outcome B** — A *cache miss* occurs. The proxy must contact the origin server.

### Step 2: Assign Probabilities
Let the probability of a cache hit be $p$ and the probability of a cache miss be $(1 - p)$. By definition of the **Cache Hit Ratio**:

$$
p = CHR = \frac{H_c}{H_c + M_c}
$$

### Step 3: Assign Time Costs
- Cache-hit latency: $T_{cache} = T_{proxy\_disk} + T_{proxy\_RAM}$ (sub-millisecond range, $\approx 1$–$5$ ms)
- Origin-fetch latency: $T_{origin} = T_{proxy\_proc} + RTT_{proxy \rightarrow origin} + T_{origin\_server}$

For a typical Internet request, $T_{origin} \approx 100$–$500$ ms.

### Step 4: Apply the Law of Total Expectation
The mean (expected) latency is the weighted sum:

$$
E[T] = p \cdot T_{cache} + (1 - p) \cdot T_{origin}
$$

### Step 5: Substitute the Symbolic Definition

$$
\begin{aligned}
E[T] &= p \cdot T_{cache} + (1 - p) \cdot T_{origin} \\
E[T] &= p \cdot T_{cache} + T_{origin} - p \cdot T_{origin} \\
E[T] &= T_{origin} - p \cdot (T_{origin} - T_{cache}) \\
E[T] &= T_{origin} - p \cdot \Delta T
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\Delta T = T_{origin} - T_{cache}$ is the **latency differential** between the two paths.

### Step 6: Interpretation
The proxy **reduces** mean latency by an amount $p \cdot \Delta T$, where the reduction is **directly proportional to the cache hit ratio $p$**.

### Step 7: Worked Numerical Example
A proxy has a CHR of $p = 0.6$. Cache-hit latency is $T_{cache} = 5$ ms, and origin latency is $T_{origin} = 200$ ms.

$$
\begin{aligned}
E[T] &= (0.6)(5) + (0.4)(200) \\
E[T] &= 3 + 80 \\
E[T] &= 83 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Latency gain over direct access ($200$ ms):**

$$
L_{gain} = \frac{200 - 83}{200} \times 100\% = 58.5\%
$$

## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Forward vs. Reverse Proxy

This matrix is a **high-yield 7-mark comparison question** in the KTU Part B pattern.

| Parameter | **Forward Proxy** | **Reverse Proxy** |
|---|---|---|
| **Sits in front of** | *Clients* (user machines) | *Servers* (web farms) |
| **Primary User** | Enterprise / ISP / End-user | Website / Cloud operator |
| **Purpose** | Anonymity, filtering, caching for *outgoing* requests | Load balancing, SSL termination, caching for *incoming* requests |
| **Knows the real client?** | Yes | No (masks server identity) |
| **Knows the real server?** | No (intercepts outbound) | Yes |
| **Typical Examples** | Squid, CCProxy, Privoxy | Nginx, HAProxy, AWS ELB, Varnish |
| **Topology Position** | Near the *client* (LAN edge) | Near the *server* (DMZ / cloud edge) |
| **Security Role** | Outbound threat prevention, DLP | Inbound DDoS mitigation, WAF |

## 3.3 Types of Proxy Servers (Full Classification)

1. **Forward Proxy (Web Proxy)** — Most common; serves client-side requests.
2. **Reverse Proxy** — Sits in front of web servers; load-balances inbound traffic.
3. **Open Proxy** — Publicly accessible; often abused by attackers (high anonymity, but a security risk).
4. **Transparent Proxy** — Client is **unaware** of the proxy; traffic is intercepted at the network layer (e.g., at a gateway router).
5. **Anonymous Proxy** — Strips identifying headers (`X-Forwarded-For`, `Via`) to hide the client IP.
6. **High Anonymity (Elite) Proxy** — Pretends to be the origin client; no proxy-specific headers are added.
7. **SOCKS Proxy (SOCKS4 / SOCKS5)** — Operates at **Layer 5 (Session Layer)**; protocol-agnostic (handles HTTP, FTP, P2P, torrents).
8. **CGI Proxy** — Web-based proxy accessed through a browser form; often used to bypass firewalls.
9. **SSL / TLS Proxy** — Decrypts, inspects, and re-encrypts HTTPS traffic (requires CA trust on clients).
10. **DNS Proxy** — Forwards DNS queries to upstream resolvers with caching.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

The following Mermaid diagram depicts the **end-to-end functional flow** of a generic forward proxy deployment, including the policy decision, cache lookup, and response paths.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Client A] -->|1 HTTP GET Request| B[Proxy Listener Port 3128]
    C[Client B] -->|1 HTTP GET Request| B
    D[Client C] -->|1 HTTP GET Request| B
    B --> E{Policy Engine ACL Filter}
    E -->|Blocked| F[403 Forbidden Response]
    E -->|Allowed| G{Cache Lookup}
    G -->|Cache Hit and Fresh| H[Serve Cached Object]
    G -->|Cache Miss or Stale| I[Open TCP Connection to Origin]
    I --> J[Origin Web Server]
    J -->|2 HTTP Response| I
    I --> K[Store in Cache Tier RAM SSD HDD]
    K --> L[Forward Response to Client]
    H --> L
    L --> A
    L --> C
    L --> D
    F --> A
    F --> C
    F --> D
    M[Syslog and CLF Logs] -.-> B
    N[Admin Console] -.-> E
    O[Threat Intel Feeds] -.-> E
```

    end

## 4.3 Request Mediation Sequence Diagram

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Client
    participant Proxy
    participant Origin
    Client->>Proxy: GET http://example.com/index.html
    Note over Proxy: Step 1 Parse Headers
    Proxy->>Proxy: Step 2 ACL Check
    Proxy->>Proxy: Step 3 Cache Hash Lookup
    alt Cache Hit
        Proxy-->>Client: 200 OK with Cached Body
    else Cache Miss
        Proxy->>Origin: GET /index.html
        Origin-->>Proxy: 200 OK with Body
        Note over Proxy: Step 4 Store in Cache
        Proxy-->>Client: 200 OK with Body
    end
    Note over Proxy: Step 5 Write to Access Log
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define a proxy server. List any four functions it performs.**

**Model Answer:**

A proxy server is an **application-layer intermediary** that sits between a client and an origin server, forwarding client requests and returning server responses on the client's behalf while applying inspection, caching, and policy logic.

**Four functions:**
1. **Caching** — Stores frequently accessed objects to reduce latency and bandwidth.
2. **Access Control / Filtering** — Blocks URLs or content per ACL rules.
3. **Anonymity** — Strips or rewrites client-identifying headers.
4. **Logging and Auditing** — Records every transaction for compliance.

> [!NOTE]
> Examiner expects *definition + minimum 4 distinct functions* for full 3 marks.

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between a forward proxy and a reverse proxy.**

**Model Answer (Tabular Form Expected):**

| Aspect | Forward Proxy | Reverse Proxy |
|---|---|---|
| Placement | In front of *clients* | In front of *servers* |
| User | End-user / organization | Website / cloud operator |
| Purpose | Outbound filtering, anonymity | Inbound load balancing, caching |
| Example | Squid | Nginx, HAProxy |
| Hides | Client identity from server | Server identity from client |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a **forward proxy server**. Describe the request handling pipeline in detail. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the **average response latency** when a proxy cache is in the path. A proxy has a CHR of $0.7$, $T_{cache} = 4$ ms, and $T_{origin} = 250$ ms. Compute the mean latency and the percentage latency gain. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)
1. **Diagram (3 marks):** Use the **Two-Connection Model**: Client $\rightarrow$ Proxy $\rightarrow$ Origin. The proxy terminates both TCP connections.
2. **Pipeline steps (4 marks):**
   - **Step 1:** Client sends HTTP request to proxy IP:port (e.g., `192.168.1.1:3128`).
   - **Step 2:** Proxy parses request line and headers.
   - **Step 3:** Policy engine evaluates ACL rules.
   - **Step 4:** Cache lookup is performed.
   - **Step 5:** On miss, the proxy opens a new TCP connection to the origin server, forwards the request, and receives the response.
   - **Step 6:** Response is optionally cached and returned to the client.
   - **Step 7:** The transaction is written to the access log in CLF format.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Derivation (4 marks):** As derived in Section 3.1:

$$
E[T] = p \cdot T_{cache} + (1 - p) \cdot T_{origin}
$$

**Numerical Computation (3 marks):** Substituting $p = 0.7$, $T_{cache} = 4$ ms, $T_{origin} = 250$ ms:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E[T] &= (0.7)(4) + (0.3)(250) \\
E[T] &= 2.8 + 75 \\
E[T] &= 77.8 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Latency gain:**

$$
L_{gain} = \frac{250 - 77.8}{250} \times 100\% = 68.88\%
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain the **types of proxy servers** in detail with suitable use cases. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **security benefits and risks** of deploying a proxy server in an enterprise network. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)
1. **Forward Proxy (2 marks):** Client-side; used for outbound filtering, anonymity, corporate compliance.
2. **Reverse Proxy (2 marks):** Server-side; used for load balancing, SSL offloading, and DDoS protection.
3. **Transparent Proxy (1 mark):** Non-configured on client; used by ISPs for content caching.
4. **Anonymous and Elite Proxies (1 mark):** Strip identifying headers; used for privacy.
5. **SOCKS Proxy (1 mark):** Protocol-agnostic; used for P2P and bypassing firewalls.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)
**Security Benefits (3.5 marks):**
- Centralized **policy enforcement** at one choke point.
- **DLP and malware scanning** before traffic reaches the LAN.
- **Anonymity** shields internal IP topology.
- **Logging** enables forensic investigation.

**Security Risks (3.5 marks):**
- **Open proxies** can be hijacked and abused for launching attacks.
- **Single Point of Failure** — proxy outage breaks all external access.
- **SSL inspection** requires trusting a private CA, weakening endpoint security.
- **Cache poisoning** can serve malicious content to multiple users.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> 1. Do **not** write a proxy and a NAT together — they are *different layers* (NAT = Layer 3, Proxy = Layer 7).
> 2. Students often confuse **forward and reverse proxies** — remember the mnemonic: *"Forward protects the user, Reverse protects the server."*
> 3. In numerical problems, forgetting to **convert percentages to decimals** (e.g., $70\% = 0.7$) is the most common arithmetic error.
> 4. Avoid writing "proxy is just a router" — it is an **application-layer intermediary** with policy decision logic.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] A proxy is an **application-layer gateway** sitting between client and server.
- [x] It uses the **Two-TCP-Connection Model** — terminates both ends.
- [x] Main functions: **caching, filtering, anonymity, logging, policy enforcement**.
- [x] **Forward Proxy** sits in front of *clients*; **Reverse Proxy** sits in front of *servers*.
- [x] **Transparent Proxy** is invisible to the client (configured at the network gateway).
- [x] **SOCKS Proxy** operates at the Session Layer and is protocol-agnostic.
- [x] Key equation: $E[T] = p \cdot T_{cache} + (1 - p) \cdot T_{origin}$.
- [x] **CHR (Cache Hit Ratio)** is the most important performance metric.
- [x] Common ports: **3128 (Squid), 8080 (HTTP alternate), 1080 (SOCKS)**.
- [x] Tools: **Squid, Nginx, HAProxy, Varnish, Privoxy, CCProxy**.
- [x] **Open proxies** are a major security risk; never expose them to the Internet.
- [x] The proxy is a **choke point** for **DLP, WAF, and SSL inspection** deployments.
- [x] Memorize the **five-step pipeline**: Parse $\rightarrow$ Policy $\rightarrow$ Cache $\rightarrow$ Forward $\rightarrow$ Log.
